WILEY SERIES IN PROBABILITY AND STATISTICS

<!-- PageBreak -->


# MATRIX DIFFERENTIAL CALCULUS WITH APPLICATIONS IN STATISTICS AND ECONOMETRICS


## Revised Edition

Jan R. Magnus, CentER, Tilburg University, The Netherlands
and
Heinz Neudecker, Cesaro, Schagen, The Netherlands

" ... deals rigorously with many of the problems that have bedevilled
the subject up to the present time ... " - Stephen Pollock, Econometric
Theory

" I continued to be pleasantly surprised by the variety and usefulness
of its contents " - Isabella Verdinelli, Journal of the American
Statistical Association

Continuing the success of their first edition, Magnus and Neudecker
present an exhaustive and self-contained revised text on matrix theory
and matrix differential calculus.

Matrix calculus has become an essential tool for quantitative methods
in a large number of applications, ranging from social and behavioural
sciences to econometrics. While the structure and successful
elements of the first edition remain, this revised and updated edition
contains many new examples and exercises.

· Contains the essentials of multivariable calculus with an emphasis
on the use of differentials

· Many new examples and exercises

· Fulfils the need for a unified and self-contained treatment of matrix
differential calculus

· Includes new developments in this field

Part I presents a concise, yet thorough overview of matrix algebra,
while the second part develops the theory of differentials. The
remaining Parts III to VI combine the theory and application of matrix
differential calculus providing the practitioner
and researcher with both a quick review and a
ISBN 0-471-98633-X
detailed reference.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## Preface

There has been a long-felt need for a book that gives a self-contained and
unified treatment of matrix differential calculus, specifically written for econo-
metricians and statisticians. The present book is meant to satisfy this need.
It can serve as a textbook for advanced undergraduates and postgraduates in
econometrics and as a reference book for practicing econometricians. Math-
ematical statisticians and psychometricians may also find something to their
liking in the book.

When used as a textbook it can provide a full-semester course. Reason-
able proficiency in basic matrix theory is assumed, especially with use of
partitioned matrices. The basics of matrix algebra, as deemed necessary for
a proper understanding of the main subject of the book, are summarized in
the first of the book's six parts. The book also contains the essentials of mul-
tivariable calculus but geared to and often phrased in terms of differentials.

The sequence in which the chapters are being read is not of great conse-
quence. It is fully conceivable that practitioners start with Part Three (Differ-
entials: the practice) and, dependent on their predilections, carry on to Parts
Five or Six, which deal with applications. Those who want a full understand-
ing of the underlying theory should read the whole book, although even then
they could go through the necessary matrix algebra only when the specific
need arises.

Matrix differential calculus as presented in this book is based on differen-
tials, and this sets the book apart from other books in this area. The approach
via differentials is, in our opinion, superior to any other existing approach.
Our principal idea is that differentials are more congenial to multivariable
functions as they crop up in econometrics, mathematical statistics or psycho-
metrics than derivatives, although from a theoretical point of view the two
concepts are equivalent. When there is a specific need for derivatives they will
be obtained from differentials.

The book falls into six parts. Part One deals with matrix algebra. It lists
\- and also often proves - items like the Schur, Jordan and singular-value
decompositions, concepts like the Hadamard and Kronecker products, the vec
operator, the commutation and duplication matrices, and the Moore-Penrose
inverse. Results on bordered matrices (and their determinants) and (linearly
restricted) quadratic forms are also presented here.

<!-- PageNumber="xiii" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="xiv" -->

$$P r e f a c e$$

Part Two, which forms the theoretical heart of the book, is entirely de-
voted to a thorough treatment of the theory of differentials, and presents
the essentials of calculus but geared to and phrased in terms of differentials.
First and second differentials are defined, 'identification' rules for Jacobian
and Hessian matrices are given, and chain rules derived. A separate chapter
on the theory of (constrained) optimization in terms of differentials concludes
this part.

Part Three is the practical core of the book. It contains the rules for
working with differentials, lists the differentials of important scalar, vector
and matrix functions (inter alia eigenvalues, eigenvectors and the Moore-
Penrose inverse) and supplies 'identification' tables for Jacobian and Hessian
matrices.

Part Four, treating inequalities, owes its existence to our feeling that econo-
metricians should be conversant with inequalities, such as the Cauchy-Schwarz
and Minkowski inequalities (and extensions thereof), and that they should
also master a powerful result like Poincaré's separation theorem. This part is
to some extent also the case history of a disappointment. When we started
writing this book we had the ambition to derive all inequalities by means of
matrix differential calculus. After all, every inequality can be rephrased as the
solution of an optimization problem. This proved to be an illusion, due to the
fact that the Hessian matrix in most cases is singular at the optimum point.

Part Five is entirely devoted to applications of matrix differential calculus
to the linear regression model. There is an exhaustive treatment of estimation
problems related to the fixed part of the model under various assumptions
concerning ranks and (other) constraints. Moreover, it contains topics relat-
ing to the stochastic part of the model, viz. estimation of the error variance
and prediction of the error term. There is also a small section on sensitivity
analysis. An introductory chapter deals with the necessary statistical prelim-
inaries.

Part Six deals with maximum likelihood estimation, which is of course an
ideal source for demonstrating the power of the propagated techniques. In the
first of three chapters, several models are analysed, inter alia the multivariate
normal distribution, the errors-in-variables model and the nonlinear regression
model. There is a discussion on how to deal with symmetry and positive defi-
niteness, and special attention is given to the information matrix. The second
chapter in this part deals with simultaneous equations under normality con-
ditions. It investigates both identification and estimation problems, subject
to various (non)linear constraints on the parameters. This part also discusses
full-information maximum likelihood (FIML) and limited- information maxi-
mum likelihood (LIML) with special attention to the derivation of asymptotic
variance matrices. The final chapter addresses itself to various psychometric
problems, inter alia principal components, multimode component analysis,
factor analysis, and canonical correlation.

All chapters contain many exercises. These are frequently meant to be
complementary to the main text.

A large number of books and papers have been published on the theory and
applications of matrix differential calculus. Without attempting to describe

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="XV" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Preface" -->

their relative virtues and particularities, the interested reader may wish to con-
sult Dwyer and McPhail (1948), Bodewig (1959), Wilkinson (1965), Dwyer
(1967), Neudecker (1967, 1969), Tracy and Dwyer (1969), Tracy and Singh
(1972), McDonald and Swaminathan (1973), MacRae (1974), Balestra (1976),
Bentler and Lee (1978), Henderson and Searle (1979), Wong and Wong (1979,
1980), Nel (1980), Rogers (1980), Wong (1980, 1985), Graham (1981), Mc-
Culloch (1982), Schönemann (1985), Magnus and Neudecker (1985), Pollock
(1985), Don (1986), and Kollo (1991). The papers by Henderson and Searle
(1979) and Nel (1980) and Rogers' (1980) book contain extensive bibliogra-
phies.

The two authors share the responsibility for Parts One, Three, Five and
Six, although any new results in Part One are due to Magnus. Parts Two and
Four are due to Magnus, although Neudecker contributed some results to Part
Four. Magnus is also responsible for the writing and organization of the final
text.

We wish to thank our colleagues F. J. H. Don, R. D. H. Heijmans, D. S. G.
Pollock and $\mathrm { R } .$ Ramer for their critical remarks and contributions. The great-
est obligation is owed to Sue Kirkbride at the London School of Economics
who patiently and cheerfully typed and retyped the various versions of the
book. Partial financial support was provided by the Netherlands Organization
for the Advancement of Pure Research (Z. W. O.) and the Suntory Toyota
International Centre for Economics and Related Disciplines at the London
School of Economics.

Cross-References. References to equations, theorems and sections are given
as follows: Equation (1) refers to an equation within the same section; (2.1)
refers to Equation (1) in Section 2 within the same chapter; and (3.2.1) refers
to Equation (1) in Section 2 of Chapter 3. Similarly, we refer to theorems
and sections within the same chapter by a single serial number (Theorem 2,
Section 5), and to theorems and sections in other chapters by double numbers
(Theorem 3.2, Section 3.5).

Notation. The notation is mostly standard, except that matrices and vec-
tors are printed in italic, not in bold face. Special symbols are used to denote
the derivative (matrix) D and the Hessian (matrix) H. The differential opera-
tor is denoted by $d .$ A complete list of all symbols used in the text is presented
in the 'Index of Symbols' at the end of the book.

London/ Amsterdam
April 1987

Jan R. Magnus
Heinz Neudecker

Preface to the first revised printing

Since this book first appeared - now almost four years ago - many of our
colleagues, students and other readers have pointed out typographical errors
and have made suggestions for improving the text. We are particularly grate-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="xvi" -->

$$P r e f a c e$$

ful to $\mathrm { R } .$ D. H. Heijmans, J. F. Kiviet, I. J. Steyn and G. Trenkler. We owe
the greatest debt to $F .$ Gerrish, formerly of the School of Mathematics in the
Polytechnic, Kingston-upon-Thames, who read Chapters 1-11 with awesome
precision and care and made numerous insightful suggestions and constructive
remarks. We hope that this printing will continue to trigger comments from
our readers.

London/Tilburg/Amsterdam
February 1991

Jan R. Magnus
Heinz Neudecker

Preface to the 1999 revised edition

A further seven years have passed since our first revision in 1991. We are
happy to see that our book is still being used by colleagues and students.
In this revision we attempted to reach three goals. First, we made a serious
attempt to keep the book up-to-date by adding many recent references and
new exercises. Secondly, we made numerous small changes throughout the
text, improving the clarity of exposition. Finally, we corrected a number of
typographical and other errors.

The structure of the book and its philosophy are unchanged. Apart from
a large number of small changes, there are two major changes. First, we in-
terchanged Sections 12 and 13 of Chapter 1, since complex numbers need to
be discussed before eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and we corrected an error in
Theorem 1.7. Secondly, in Chapter 17 on psychometrics, we rewrote Sections
8-10 relating to the Eckart-Young theorem.

We are grateful to Karim Abadir, Paul Bekker, Hamparsum Bozdogan,
Michael Browne, Frank Gerrish, Kaddour Hadri, Tonu Kollo, Shuangzhe Liu,
Daan Nel, Albert Satorra, Kazuo Shigemasu, Jos ten Berge, Peter ter Berg,
Götz Trenkler, Haruo Yanai and many others for their thoughtful and con-
structive comments. Of course, we welcome further comments from our read-
ers.

Tilburg/Amsterdam
March 1998

Jan R. Magnus
Heinz Neudecker


### Preface to the 2007 third edition

After the appearance of the second (revised) edition in 1999, the complete
text has been completely retyped in LATEX by Josette Janssen with expert
advice from Jozef Pijnenburg, both at Tilburg University. In the process of
retyping the manuscript, many small changes were made to improve the read-
ability and consistency of the text, but the structure of the book was not

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="xvii" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Preface" -->

changed. The English LATEX version was then used as the basis for the Rus-
sian translation:

Matrichnoe Differenzial'noe Ischislenie s Prilozhenijami
k Statistike i Ekonometrike,

published by Fizmatlit Publishing House, Moscow, 2002.

The current third edition is based on the same LATEX text. A number of
small further corrections have been made. The numbering of chapters, sec-
tions, and theorems corresponds to the second (revised) edition of 1999. But
the page numbers do not correspond.

This edition appears only as a electronic version, and can be downloaded
without charge from Jan Magnus's website:

$$\mathrm { h t t p : } / / \mathrm { c e n t e r } . \mathrm { u v t } . \mathrm { n l } / \mathrm { s t a f f } / \mathrm { m a g n u s } .$$

Comments are, as always, welcome.

Notation. The LATEX edition follows the notation of the 1999 Revised Edi-
tion, with the following three exceptions. First, the symbol for the sum vector
$\left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime }$ has been altered from a calligraphic $s$ to 2 (dotless i); secondly,
the symbol $\mathrm { i }$ for imaginary root, has been replaced by the more common i;
and thirdly, $\mathrm { v } \left( A \right) ,$ the vector indicating the essentially distinct components of
a symmetric matrix $A ,$ has been replaced by $v \left( A \right) .$

Tilburg/Schagen
January 2007

Jan R. Magnus
Heinz Neudecker

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->

Part One -

Matrices

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 1 Basic properties of vectors and matrices


### 1 INTRODUCTION

In this chapter we summarize some of the well-known definitions and theorems
of matrix algebra. Most of the theorems will be proved.


### 2 SETS

A set is a collection of objects, called the elements (or members) of the set.
We write $x \in S$ to mean $x$ is an element of $S ^ { : } ,$ or ${ } ^ { * } x$ belongs to $S ^ { : } .$ If $x$ does
not belong to $S$ we write $x \notin S .$ The set that contains no elements is called the
$e m p t y \quad s e t ,$ denoted $\emptyset .$ If a set has at least one element, it is called non-empty.

Sometimes a set can be defined by displaying the elements in braces. For
example $A = 0 , 1$ or

$$\mathbb{N} = \left\{ 1 , 2 , 3 , \ldots \right\} .$$
(1)

Notice that $A$ is a finite set (contains a finite number of elements), whereas
$\mathbb{N}$ is an infinite set. If $P$ is a property that any element of $S$ has or does not
have, then

$$\left\{ x : x \in S , x \text { satisfies } P \right\}$$
(2)

denotes the set of all the elements of $S$ that have property $P .$

$A$ set $A$ is called a $s u b s e t$ of $B ,$ written $A \subset B ,$ whenever every element
of $A$ also belongs to $B .$ The notation $A \subset B$ does not rule out the possibility
that $A = B .$ If $A \subset B$ and $A \neq B ,$ then we $\mathrm { S a v }$ that $A$ is a proper subset of
$B .$

If $A$ and $B$ are two subsets of $S ,$ we define

$$A \cup B ,$$
(3)

<!-- PageNumber="3" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="4" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

the union of $A$ and $B ,$ as the set of elements of $S$ that belong to $A$ or to $B$
(or to both), and

$$A \cap B ,$$
(4)

the intersection of $A$ and $B ,$ as the set of elements of $S$ that belong to both $A$
and $B .$ We say that $A$ and $B$ are (mutually) disjoint if they have no common
elements. That is, if

$$A \cap B = \emptyset .$$
(5)

The complement of $A$ relative to $B ,$ denoted by $B \quad - \quad A ,$ is the set $\left\{ x : x \in B \right. ,$
but $\left. x \notin A \right\} .$ The complement of $A$ (relative to $\left. S \right)$ is sometimes denoted $A ^ { c } .$

The Cartesian product of two sets $A$ and $B ,$ written $A \times B ,$ is the set of all
ordered pairs $\left( a , b \right)$ such that $a \in A$ and $b \in B .$ More generally, the Cartesian
product of $n$ sets $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { n } ,$ written

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } ,$$
(6)

is the set of all ordered n-tuples $\left( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n } \right)$ such that $a _ { i } \in A _ { i }$ $\left( i = \right.$
$\left. 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$

The set of (finite) real numbers (the one-dimensional Euclidean space)
is denoted by $\mathrm { R } .$ The n-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ is the Cartesian
product of $n$ sets equal to $\mathrm { R } ,$ i.e.

$$\mathrm { R } ^ { n } = \mathrm { R } \times \mathrm { R } \times \cdots \times \mathrm { R } \quad \left( n \mathrm { t i m e s } \right) .$$
(7)

The elements of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ are thus the ordered n-tuples $\left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ of real
numbers $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$

$A$ set $S$ of real numbers is said to be bounded if there exists a number $M$
such that $| x | \leq M$ for all $x \in S .$


### 3 MATRICES: ADDITION AND MULTIPLICATION

An $m \times n$ matrix $A$ is a rectangular array of real numbers

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} a _ { 1 1 } & a _ { 1 2 } & \ldots & a _ { 1 n } \\ a _ { 2 1 } & a _ { 2 2 } & \ldots & a _ { 2 n } \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ a _ { m 1 } & a _ { m 2 } & \ldots & a _ { m n } \end{array} \right) .$$
(1)

We sometimes write $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right) .$ An $m \times n$ matrix can be regarded as a point
in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m \times n } .$ The real numbers $a _ { i j }$ are called the elements of $A .$

An $m \times 1$ matrix is a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m \times 1 }$ (that is, in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and is called a
(column) vector of order $m \times 1 .$ A $1 \times n$ matrix is called a row vector (of order

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="5" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Matrices: addition and multiplication" -->

$1 \times n .$ The elements of a vector are usually called its components. Matrices
are always denoted by capital letters, vectors by lower-case letters.

The sum of two matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order is defined as

$$A + B = \left( a _ { i j } \right) + \left( b _ { i j } \right) = \left( a _ { i j } + b _ { i j } \right) .$$
(2)

The product of a matrix by a scalar $\lambda$ is

$$\lambda A = A \lambda = \left( \lambda a _ { i j } \right) .$$
(3)

The following properties are now easily proved:

$$A + B = B + A ,$$
$$\left( A + B \right) + C = A + \left( B + C \right) ,$$
$$\left( \lambda + \mu \right) A = \lambda A + \mu A ,$$
$$\lambda \left( A + B \right) = \lambda A + \lambda B ,$$
$$\lambda \left( \mu A \right) = \left( \lambda \mu \right) A .$$
(4)
(5)
(6)
(7)
(8)

A matrix whose elements are all zero is called a null matrix and denoted 0.
We have, of course,

$$A + \left( - 1 \right) A = 0 .$$
(9)

If $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix and $B$ an $n \times p$ matrix (so that $A$ has the same
number of columns as $B$ has rows), then we define the product of $A$ and $B$ as

$$A B = \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } b _ { j k } \right) .$$
(10)

Thus, $A B$ is an $m \times p$ matrix and its ik-th element is $\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } b _ { j k } .$ The
following properties of the matrix product can be established:

$$\left( A B \right) C = A \left( B C \right) ,$$
(11)

$$A \left( B + C \right) = A B + A C ,$$
$$\left( A + B \right) C = A C + B C .$$
(12)
(13)

These relations hold provided the matrix products exist.

We note that the existence of $A B$ does not imply the existence of $B A ;$ and
even when both products exist they are not generally equal. (Two matrices $A$
and $B$ for which

$$A B = B A$$
(14)

are said to commute.) We therefore distinguish between pre-multiplication
and post-multiplication: a given $m \times n$ matrix $A$ can be pre-multiplied by a
$p \times m$ matrix $B$ to form the product $B A ;$ it can also be post-multiplied by an
$n \times q$ matrix $C$ to form $A C .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="6" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->


### 4 THE TRANSPOSE OF $A$ MATRIX

The transpose of an $m \times n$ matrix $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ is the $n \times m$ matrix, denoted $A ^ { \prime } ,$
whose ij-th element is $a _ { j i } .$

We have

$$\left( A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } = A ,$$
(1)

$$\left( A + B \right) ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } + B ^ { \prime } ,$$
$$\left( A B \right) ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } .$$
(2)
(3)
.

If $x$ is an $n \times 1$ vector then $x ^ { \prime }$ is a $1 \times n$ row vector and

$$x ^ { \prime } x = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } .$$
(4)

The (Euclidean) norm of $x$ is defined as

$$\| x \| = \left( x ^ { \prime } x \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$
(5)


### 5 SQUARE MATRICES

A matrix is said to be square if it has as many rows as it has columns. A
square matrix $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ is said to be


<table>
<tr>
<td>lower triangular</td>
<td>if</td>
<td>(</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>strictly lower triangular</td>
<td>if</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>unit lower triangular</td>
<td>if</td>
<td>and</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>upper triangular</td>
<td>if</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>strictly upper triangular</td>
<td>if</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>unit upper triangular</td>
<td>if</td>
<td>and (all i),</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>idempotent</td>
<td>if</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


A square matrix $A$ is triangular if it is either triangular or upper triangu-
lar (or both).

A real square matrix $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ is said to be


<table>
<tr>
<td>symmetric</td>
<td>if $A ^ { \prime } = A ,$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>skew symmetric</td>
<td>if $A ^ { \prime } = - A .$</td>
</tr>
</table>


For any square $n \times n$ matrix $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ we define $\deg A$ or $\deg \left( A \right)$ as

$$\mathrm { d g } A = \left( \begin{array}{} a _ { 1 1 } & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\ 0 & a _ { 2 2 } & \ldots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \ldots & a _ { n n } \end{array} \right)$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="7" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] Linear forms and quadratic forms" -->

or, alternatively,

$$\deg A = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( a _ { 1 1 } , a _ { 2 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n n } \right) .$$
(2)

If $A = \deg \left( A \right) ,$ we say that $A$ is diagonal. A particular diagonal matrix is the
identity matrix,

$$I = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & \ldots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \ldots & 1 \end{array} \right) = \left( \delta _ { i j } \right) ,$$ ☒
(3)

where $\delta _ { i j } = 1$ if $i = j$ and $\delta _ { i j } = 0$ if $i \neq j$ $\delta _ { i j }$ is called the Kronecker delta).
We have

$$I A = A I = A$$
(4)

if $A$ and $I$ have the same order.

$A$ real square matrix $A$ is said to be orthogonal if

$$A A ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } A = I$$
(5)

and its columns are orthonormal. $A$ rectangular (not square) matrix can still
have the property that $A A ^ { \prime } = I$ or $A ^ { \prime } A = I ,$ but not both. Such a matrix is
called semi-orthogonal.

Any matrix $B$ satisfying

$$B ^ { 2 } = A$$
(6)

is called a square root of $A ,$ denoted $A ^ { 1 / 2 } .$ Such a matrix need not be unique.


### 6 LINEAR FORMS AND QUADRATIC FORMS

Let $a$ be an $n \times 1$ vector, $A$ an $n \times n$ matrix and $B$ an $n \times m$ matrix. The
expression $a ^ { \prime } x$ is called a linear form in x, the expression $x ^ { \prime }$ Ax is a quadratic
form in x, and the expression $x ^ { \prime } B y$ a bilinear form in $x$ and $y .$ In quadratic
forms we may, without loss of generality, assume that $A$ is symmetric, because
if not then we can replace $A$ by $\left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) / 2 :$

$$x ^ { \prime } A x = x ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { A + A ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \right) x$$
(1)

Thus, let $A$ be a symmetric matrix. We say that $A$ is


<table>
<tr>
<td>positive definite</td>
<td>if $x ^ { \prime } A x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0 ,$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>positive semidefinite</td>
<td>if $x ^ { \prime } A x \geq 0$ for all x,</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>negative definite</td>
<td>if $x ^ { \prime } A x < 0$ for all $x \neq 0 ,$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>negative semidefinite</td>
<td>if $x ^ { \prime } A x \leq 0$ for all x,</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>indefinite</td>
<td>if $x ^ { \prime } A x > 0$ for some $x$ and $x ^ { \prime } A x < 0$ for some x.</td>
</tr>
</table>


<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="8" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

It is clear that the matrices $B B ^ { \prime }$ and $B ^ { \prime } B$ are positive semidefinite, and that
$A$ is negative (semi)definite if and only if $- A$ is positive (semi)definite. A
square null matrix is both positive and negative semidefinite.

The following two theorems are often useful.


#### Theorem 1

Let $A \left( m \times n \right) ,$ $B \left( n \times p \right)$ and $C \left( n \times p \right)$ be matrices and let $x \left( n \times 1 \right)$ be a
vector. Then

(a) $A x = 0 \Leftrightarrow A ^ { \prime } A x = 0 ,$

(b) $A B = 0 \Leftrightarrow A ^ { \prime } A B = 0 ,$

(c) $A ^ { \prime } A B = A ^ { \prime } A C \Leftrightarrow A B = A C .$

Proof. (a) Clearly $A x = 0 \Longrightarrow A ^ { \prime } A x = 0 .$ Conversely, if $A ^ { \prime } A x = 0 ,$ then
$\left( A x \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A x \right) = x ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } A x = 0$ and hence $A x = 0 .$ (b) This follows from (a). (c)
follows from (b) by substituting $B \quad - \quad C$ for $B$ in (b).
✷
☐


#### Theorem 2

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix, $B$ and $C n \times n$ matrices, $B$ symmetric. Then

(a) $A x = 0$ for all $n \times 1$ vectors $x$ if and only if $A =$ 0,

(b) $x ^ { \prime } B x = 0$ for all $n \times 1$ vectors $x$ if and only if $B = 0 ,$

(c) $x ^ { \prime } C x = 0$ for all $n \times 1$ vectors $x$ if and only if $C ^ { \prime } = - C .$

Proof. The proof is easy and is left to the reader.

✷
☐


### 7 THE RANK OF A MATRIX

A set of vectors $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ is said to be linearly independent if $\sum \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } = 0$
implies that all $\alpha _ { i } = 0 .$ If $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are not linearly independent, they are
said to be linearly dependent.

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix. The column rank of $A$ is the maximum number of
linearly independent columns it contains. The row rank of $A$ is the maximum
number of linearly independent rows it contains. It may be shown that the
column rank of $A$ is equal to its row rank. Hence the concept of rank is
unambiguous. We denote the rank of $A$ by

$$r \left( A \right) .$$
(1)

It is clear that

$$r \left( A \right) \leq \min \left( m , n \right) .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="9" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] The inverse" -->

If $r \left( A \right) = m ,$ we say that $A$ has full row rank. If $r \left( A \right) = n ,$ we say that $A$ has
full column rank. If $r \left( A \right) = 0 ,$ then $A$ is the null matrix, and conversely, if $A$
is the null matrix, then $r \left( A \right) = 0 .$

We have the following important results concerning ranks:

$$r \left( A \right) = r \left( A ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) = r \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$
(3)

$$r \left( A B \right) \leq \min \left( r \left( A \right) , r \left( B \right) \right) ,$$
$$r \left( A B \right) = r \left( A \right) \quad \mathrm { i f } B \text { is square and }$$
$$r \left( A + B \right) \leq r \left( A \right) + r \left( B \right) ,$$
(4)
of full rank,
(5)
(6)

and finally, if $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix and $A x = 0$ for some $x \neq 0 ,$ then

$$r \left( A \right) \leq n - 1 .$$
(7)

The column space of $A \left( m \times n \right) ,$ denoted $\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) ,$ is the set of vectors

$$\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) = \left\{ y : y = A x \text { for some } x \text { in } R ^ { n } \right\} .$$
(8)

Thus, $\mathcal{M} \left( A \right)$ is the vector space generated by the columns of $A .$ The dimension
of this vector space is $r \left( A \right) .$ We have

$$\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(9)

for any matrix $A .$


### 8 THE INVERSE

Let $A$ be a square matrix of order $n \times n .$ We say that $A \quad i s$ non-singular if
$r \left( A \right) = n ,$ and that $A$ is singular if $r \left( A \right) < n .$

If $A$ is non-singular, there exists a non-singular matrix $B$ such that

$$A B = B A = I _ { n } .$$
(1)

The matrix $B ,$ denoted $A ^ { - 1 } ,$ is unique and is called the inverse of $A .$ We have

$$\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } = \left( A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(2)

$$\left( A B \right) ^ { - 1 } = B ^ { - 1 } A ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(3)

if the inverses exist.

A square matrix $P$ is said to be a permutation matrix if each row and each
column of $P$ contains a single element 1, and the remaining elements are zero.
An $n \times n$ permutation matrix thus contains $n$ ones and $n \left( n - \quad 1 \right)$ zeros. It can
be proved that any permutation matrix is non-singular. In fact, it is even true
that $P$ is orthogonal, that is,

$$P ^ { - 1 } = P ^ { \prime }$$
(4)

for any permutation matrix $P .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="10" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->


### 9 THE DETERMINANT

Associated with any $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is the determinant | $A |$ defined by

$$| A | = \sum \left( - 1 \right) ^ { \phi \left( j _ { 1 } , \ldots , j _ { n } \right) } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j _ { i } }$$
(1)

where the summation is taken over all permutations $\left( j _ { 1 } , \ldots , j _ { n } \right)$ of the set of
integers $\left( 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$ and $\phi \left( j _ { 1 } , \ldots , j _ { n } \right)$ is the number of transpositions required
to change $\left( 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ into $\left( j _ { 1 } , \ldots , j _ { n } \right) .$ (A transposition consists of interchang-
ing two numbers. It can be shown that the number of transpositions required
to transform $\left( 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ into $\left( j _ { 1 } , \ldots , j _ { n } \right)$ is always even or always odd, so that
$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { \phi \left( j _ { 1 } , \ldots , j _ { n } \right) }$ is consistently defined.)

We have

$$A B | = | A | B | ,$$
$$| A ^ { \prime } | = | A | . ,$$
$$| \alpha A | = \alpha ^ { n } | A | \quad \text { for any scalar } \alpha ,$$
$$| A ^ { - 1 } | = | A | ^ { - 1 } \quad \text { if } A \text { is non-singular } ,$$
$$| I _ { n } | = 1 .$$
(2)
(3)
(4)
(5)
(6)

A submatrix of $A$ is the rectangular array obtained from $A$ by deleting rows
and columns. A minor is the determinant of a square submatrix of $A .$ The
minor of an element $a _ { i j }$ is the determinant of the submatrix of $A$ obtained by
deleting the i-th row and j-th column. The cofactor of $a _ { i j } ,$ say $c _ { i j } ,$ is $\left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j }$
times the minor of $a _ { i j } .$ The matrix $C = \left( c _ { i j } \right)$ is called the cofactor matrix of
$A .$ The transpose of $C$ is called the adjoint of $A$ and will be denoted as $A ^ { \# } .$

We have

$$| A | = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } c _ { i j } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { j k } c _ { j k } \quad \left( i , k = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
$$A A ^ { \# } = A ^ { \# } A = | A | I ,$$
(7)
(8)

$$\left( A B \right) ^ { \# } = B ^ { \# } A ^ { \# } .$$
(9)

For any square matrix $A ,$ a principal submatrix of $A$ is obtained by deleting
corresponding rows and columns. The determinant of a principal submatrix
is called a principal minor.


#### Exercises

1\. If $A$ is non-singular, show that $A ^ { \# } = | A | A ^ { - 1 } .$

2\. Prove that the determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of its
diagonal elements.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="11" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] The trace" -->


### 10 THE TRACE

The trace of a square $n \times n$ matrix $A ,$ denoted tr $A$ or $\mathrm { t r } \left( A \right) ,$ is the sum of its
diagonal elements:

$$\mathrm { t r } A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i i } .$$
(1)

We have

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) = \mathrm { t r } A + \mathrm { t r } B ,$$
$$\mathrm { t r } \left( \lambda A \right) = \lambda \mathrm { t r } A \quad \mathrm { i f } \lambda \text { is a scalar } ,$$
$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { t r } A ,$$
$$A B = \mathrm { t r } B A .$$
(2)
(3)
(4)
tr
(5)

We note in (5) that $A B$ and $B A ,$ though both square, need not be of the same
order.

Corresponding to the vector (Euclidean) norm

$$\| x \| = \left( x ^ { \prime } x \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }$$
(6)

given in (4.5), we now define the matrix (Euclidean) norm as

$$\| A \| = \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$
(7)

We have

tr
$$A ^ { \prime } A \geq 0$$
(8)

with equality if and only if $A = 0 .$


### 11 PARTITIONED MATRICES

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix. We can partition $A$ as

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } & A _ { 1 2 } \\ A _ { 2 1 } & A _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(1)

where $A _ { 1 1 }$ is $m _ { 1 } \times n _ { 1 } ,$ $A _ { 1 2 }$ is $m _ { 1 } \times n _ { 2 } ,$ $A _ { 2 1 }$ is $m _ { 2 } \times n _ { 1 } ,$ $A _ { 2 2 }$ is $m _ { 2 } \times n _ { 2 } ,$ and
$m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } = m ,$ and $n _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } = n .$

Let $B$ $\left( m \times n \right.$ be similarly partitioned into submatrices $B _ { i j } \left( i , j = 1 , 2 \right) .$
Then

$$A + B = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } + B _ { 1 1 } & A _ { 1 2 } + B _ { 1 2 } \\ A _ { 2 1 } + B _ { 2 1 } & A _ { 2 2 } + B _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="12" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

Now let $C \left( n \times p \right)$ be partitioned into submatrices $C _ { i j }$ $\left( i , j = 1 , 2 \right)$ such that
$C _ { 1 1 }$ has $n _ { 1 }$ rows (and hence $C _ { 1 2 }$ also has $n _ { 1 }$ rows and $C _ { 2 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 2 }$ have $n _ { 2 }$
rows). Then we may post-multiply $A$ by $C$ yielding

$$A C = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } C _ { 1 1 } + A _ { 1 2 } C _ { 2 1 } & A _ { 1 1 } C _ { 1 2 } + A _ { 1 2 } C _ { 2 2 } \\ A _ { 2 1 } C _ { 1 1 } + A _ { 2 2 } C _ { 2 1 } & A _ { 2 1 } C _ { 1 2 } + A _ { 2 2 } C _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

The transpose of the matrix $A$ given in (1) is

$$A ^ { \prime } = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } ^ { \prime } & A _ { 2 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ A _ { 1 2 } ^ { \prime } & A _ { 2 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$
(4)

If the off-diagonal blocks $A _ { 1 2 }$ and $A _ { 2 1 }$ are both zero, and $A _ { 1 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 2 }$ are
square and non-singular, then $A$ is also non-singular and its inverse is

$$A ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

More generally, if $A$ as given in (1) is non-singular and $D = A _ { 2 2 } - A _ { 2 1 } A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 1 2 }$
is also non-singular, then

$$A ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 1 2 } D ^ { - 1 } A _ { 2 1 } A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 1 2 } D ^ { - 1 } \\ - D ^ { - 1 } A _ { 2 1 } A _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & D ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(6)

Alternatively, if $A$ is non-singular and $E = A _ { 1 1 } - A _ { 1 2 } A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 2 1 }$ is non-singular,
then

$$A ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} E ^ { - 1 } & - E ^ { - 1 } & A _ { 1 2 } A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \\ - A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 2 1 } E ^ { - 1 } & A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } + A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 2 1 } E ^ { - 1 } A _ { 1 2 } A _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(7)

Of course, if both $D$ and $E$ are non-singular, blocks in (6) and (7) can be
interchanged. The results (6) and (7) can be easily extended to a $3 \times 3$
matrix partition. We only consider the following symmetric case where two of
the off-diagonal blocks are null matrices.


#### Theorem 3

If the matrix

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { A } & { B } & { C } \\ { B ^ { \prime } } & { D } & { 0 } \\ { C ^ { \prime } } & { 0 } & { E } \end{array} \right)$$
(8)

is symmetric and non-singular, its inverse is given by

$$\left( \begin{array}{} Q ^ { - 1 } & - Q ^ { - 1 } B D ^ { - 1 } & - Q ^ { - 1 } C E ^ { - 1 } \\ - D ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } Q ^ { - 1 } & D ^ { - 1 } + D ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } Q ^ { - 1 } B D ^ { - 1 } & D ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } Q ^ { - 1 } C E ^ { - 1 } \\ - E ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } Q ^ { - 1 } & E ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } Q ^ { - 1 } B D ^ { - 1 } & E ^ { - 1 } + E ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } Q ^ { - 1 } C E ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right)$$
(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="13" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Complex matrices" -->

where

$$Q = A - B D ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } - C E ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } .$$
(10)

Proof. The proof is left to the reader.
✷
☐

As to the determinants of partitioned matrices, we note that

$$| \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } & A _ { 1 2 } \\ 0 & A _ { 2 2 } \end{array} | = | A _ { 1 1 } | | A _ { 2 2 } | = | \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } & 0 \\ A _ { 2 1 } & A _ { 2 2 } \end{array} |$$
(11)

if both $A _ { 1 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 2 }$ are square matrices.


#### Exercises

1\. Find the determinant and inverse (if it exists) of

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} A & 0 \\ a ^ { \prime } & 1 \end{array} \right) .$$

2\. If $| A | \neq 0 ,$ prove that

$$| \begin{array}{} A & b \\ a ^ { \prime } & \alpha \end{array} | = \left( \alpha - a ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } b \right) | A | .$$

3\. If $\alpha \neq 0 ,$ prove that

$$| \begin{array}{} A & b \\ a ^ { \prime } & \alpha \end{array} | = \alpha | A - \left( 1 / \alpha \right) b a ^ { \prime } | .$$


### 12 COMPLEX MATRICES

If $X$ and $Y$ are real matrices of the same order, a complex matrix $Z$ can be
defined as

$$Z = Z + i Y ,$$
(1)

where $i$ denotes the imaginary unit with the property $i ^ { 2 } = - 1 .$ The complex
conjugate of $Z ,$ denoted $Z ^ { * } ,$ is defined as

$$Z ^ { * } = X ^ { \prime } - i Y ^ { \prime } .$$
(2)
.

If $Z$ is real, then $Z ^ { * } = Z ^ { \prime } .$ If $Z$ is a scalar, say $\zeta ,$ we usually write $\bar { \zeta }$ instead
of $\zeta ^ { * } .$

A square complex matrix $Z$ is said to be Hermitian if $Z ^ { * } = Z$ (the complex
equivalent to a symmetric matrix) and unitary if $Z ^ { * } Z = I$ (the complex
equivalent to an orthogonal matrix).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="14" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

We shall see in Theorem 4 that the eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix
are real. In general, however, eigenvalues (and hence eigenvectors) are com-
plex. In this book, complex numbers appear only in connection with eigen-
values and eigenvectors of non-symmetric matrices (Chapter 8). A detailed
treatment is therefore omitted. Matrices and vectors are assumed to be real,
unless it is explicitly specified that they are complex.


### 13 EIGENVALUES AND EIGENVECTORS

Let $A$ be a square matrix, say $n \times n$ n. The eigenvalues of $A$ are defined as the
roots of the characteristic equation

$$| \lambda I _ { n } - A | = 0 .$$
(1)

Equation (1) has $n$ roots, in general complex. Let $\lambda$ be an eigenvalue of $A .$
Then there exist vectors x and $y \left( x \neq 0 , y \neq 0 \right)$ such that

$$\left( \lambda I - A \right) x = 0 , \quad y ^ { \prime } \left( \lambda I - A \right) = 0 .$$
(2)

That is,

$$A x = \lambda x \quad y ^ { \prime } A = \lambda y ^ { \prime } .$$
(3)

The vectors $x$ and $y$ are called $a$ (column) eigenvector and row eigenvector
of $A$ associated with the eigenvalue $\lambda .$ Eigenvectors are usually normalized in
some way to make them unique, for example by $x ^ { \prime } x = y ^ { \prime } y = 1$ (when $x$ and
$y$ are real).

Not all roots of the characteristic equation need to be different. Each root is
counted a number of times equal to its multiplicity. When a root (eigenvalue)
appears more than once it is called a multiple eigenvalue; if it appears only
once it is called a simple eigenvalue.

Although eigenvalues are in general complex, the eigenvalues of a real
symmetric matrix are always real.


#### Theorem 4

A real symmetric matrix has only real eigenvalues.

$P r o o f .$ Let $\lambda$ be an eigenvalue of a real symmetric matrix $A$ and let $x = u + i v$
be an associated eigenvector. Then

$$A \left( u + i v \right) = \lambda \left( u + i v \right)$$
(4)

and hence

$$\left( u - i v \right) ^ { \prime } A \left( u + i v \right) = \lambda \left( u - i v \right) ^ { \prime } \left( u + i v \right) ,$$
(5)

which leads to

$$u ^ { \prime } A u + v ^ { \prime } A v = \lambda \left( u ^ { \prime } u + v ^ { \prime } v \right)$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="15" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Eigenvalues and eigenvectors" -->

because of the symmetry of $A .$ This implies that $\lambda$ is real.
✷
☐

Let us prove the following three results, which will be useful to us later.


### Theorem 5

If $A$ is an $n \times n$ matrix and $G$ is a non-singular $n \times n$ matrix, then $A$ and
$G ^ { - 1 } A G$ have the same set of eigenvalues (with the same multiplicities).

Proof. From

$$\lambda I _ { n } - G ^ { - 1 } A G = G ^ { - 1 } \left( \lambda I _ { n } - A \right) G$$
(7)

we obtain

$$| \lambda I _ { n } - G ^ { - 1 } A G | = | G ^ { - 1 } | | \lambda I _ { n } - A | | G | = | \lambda I _ { n } - A |$$
(8)

and the result follows.

✷
☐


### Theorem 6

A singular matrix has at least one zero eigenvalue.

Proof. If $A$ is singular then $| A | = 0$ and hence $| \lambda I - A | = 0$ for $\lambda = 0 .$
✷
☐


### Theorem 7

An idempotent matrix has only eigenvalues 0 or 1. All eigenvalues of a unitary
matrix have unit modulus.

Proof. Let $A$ be idempotent. Then $A ^ { 2 } = A .$ Thus, if $A x = \lambda x ,$ then

$$\lambda x = A x = A ^ { 2 } x = \lambda A x = \lambda ^ { 2 } x$$
(9)

and hence $\lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } ,$ which implies $\lambda = 0$ or $\lambda = 1$ 1.

If $A$ is unitary, then $A ^ { * } A = I .$ Thus, if $A x = \lambda x ,$ then

$$x ^ { * } A ^ { * } = \bar { \lambda } x ^ { * } ,$$
(10)
,

using the notation of Section 12. Hence

$$x ^ { * } x = x ^ { * } A ^ { * } A x = \bar { \lambda } \lambda x ^ { * } x .$$
(11)

Since $x ^ { * } x \neq 0 ,$ we obtain $\bar { \lambda } \lambda = 1$ and hence $| \lambda | = 1 .$
☐
✷

An important theorem regarding positive definite matrices is stated below.


### Theorem 8

A symmetric matrix is positive definite (positive semidefinite) if and only if
all its eigenvalues are positive (non-negative).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="16" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

Proof. If $A$ is positive definite and $A x = \lambda x ,$ then $x ^ { \prime } A x = \lambda x ^ { \prime } x .$ Now, $x ^ { \prime } A x > 0$
and $x ^ { \prime } x > 0$ imply $\lambda > 0 .$ The converse will not be proved here. (It follows
from Theorem 13.)
☐
✷

Next, let us prove Theorem 9.


#### Theorem 9

Let $A$ be $m \times n$ and let $B$ be $n \times m$ $\left( n \geq m \right) .$ Then the non-zero eigenvalues
of $B A$ and $A B$ are identical, and | $| I _ { m } - A B | = | I _ { n } - B A | .$

$P r o o f .$ Taking determinants on both sides of the equality

$$\left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } - A B & A \\ 0 & I _ { n } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } & 0 \\ B & I _ { n } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } & 0 \\ B & I _ { n } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } & A \\ 0 & I _ { n } - B A \end{array} \right) ,$$
(12)

we obtain

$$| I _ { m } - A B | = | I _ { n } - B A | .$$
(13)

Now, let $\lambda \neq 0 .$ Then

$$| \lambda I _ { n } - B A | = \lambda ^ { n } | I _ { n } - B \left( \lambda ^ { - 1 } A \right) |$$
$$= \lambda ^ { n } | I _ { m } - \left( \lambda ^ { - 1 } A \right) B |$$
$$= \lambda ^ { n - m } | \lambda I _ { m } - A B | .$$

(14)

Hence the non-zero eigenvalues of $B A$ are the same as the non-zero eigenval-
ues of $A B ,$ and this is equivalent to the statement in the theorem.
☐
✷

Without proof we state the following famous result.


##### Theorem 10 (Cayley-Hamilton)

Let $A$ be an n $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } .$ Then

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \lambda _ { i } I _ { n } - A \right) = 0 .$$
(15)

Finally, we present the following result on eigenvectors.


#### Theorem 11

Eigenvectors associated with distinct eigenvalues are linearly independent.

Proof. Let $A x _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } ,$ $A x _ { 2 } = \lambda _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ,$ and $\lambda _ { 1 } \neq \lambda _ { 2 } .$ Assume that $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$
are linearly dependent. Then there is an $\alpha \neq 0$ such that $x _ { 2 } = \alpha x _ { 1 } ,$ and hence

$$\alpha \lambda _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } = \alpha A x _ { 1 } = A x _ { 2 } = \lambda _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } = \alpha \lambda _ { 2 } x _ { 1 } .$$
(16)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="17" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] Schur's decomposition theorem" -->

That is

$$\alpha \left( \lambda _ { 1 } - \lambda _ { 2 } \right) x _ { 1 } = 0 .$$
(17)

Since $\alpha \neq 0$ and $\lambda _ { 1 } \neq \lambda _ { 2 } ,$ (17) implies $x _ { 1 } = 0 ,$ a contradiction.
☐
✷


### Exercise

1\. Show that

$$| \begin{array}{} 0 & I _ { m } \\ I _ { m } & 0 \end{array} | = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } .$$


### 14 SCHUR'S DECOMPOSITION THEOREM

In the next few sections we present three decomposition theorems: Schur's
theorem, Jordan's theorem and the singular-value decomposition. Each of
these theorems will prove useful later in this book. We first state Schur's
theorem.


#### Theorem 12 (Schur decomposition)

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Then there exist a unitary $n \times n$ matrix $S$ (that
is, $S ^ { * } S = I _ { n }$ and an upper triangular matrix $M$ whose diagonal elements are
the eigenvalues of $A ,$ such that

$$S ^ { * } A S = M .$$
(1)

The most important special case of Schur's decomposition theorem is the
case where $A$ is symmetric.


#### Theorem 13

Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix. Then there exist an orthogonal
$n \times n$ matrix $S$ (that is $S ^ { \prime } S = I _ { n }$ whose columns are eigenvectors of $A \mathrm { a n d }$
a diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ whose diagonal elements are the eigenvalues of $A ,$ such
that

$$S ^ { \prime } A S = \Lambda .$$
(2)

Proof. Using Theorem 12, there exists a unitary matrix $S = R + i T$ with real
$R$ and $T$ and an upper triangular matrix $M$ such that $S ^ { * } A S = M .$ Then,

$$M = S ^ { * } A S = \left( R - i T \right) ^ { \prime } A \left( R + i T \right)$$
$$= \left( R ^ { \prime } A R + T ^ { \prime } A T \right) + i \left( R ^ { \prime } A T - T ^ { \prime } A R \right)$$

(3)

and hence, using the symmetry of $A ,$

$$M + M ^ { \prime } = 2 \left( R ^ { \prime } A R + T ^ { \prime } A T \right) .$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="18" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

It follows that $M + M ^ { \prime }$ is a real matrix and hence, since $M$ is triangular, that
$M$ is a real matrix. We thus obtain, from (3),

$$M = R ^ { \prime } A R + T ^ { \prime } A T .$$
(5)

Since $A$ is symmetric, $M$ is symmetric. But, since $M$ is also triangular, $M$
must be diagonal. The columns of $S$ are then eigenvectors of $A$ and, since the
diagonal elements of $M$ are real, $S$ can be chosen to be real as well.
✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq$
$\cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$ Use Theorem 13 to prove that

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \leq \lambda _ { n } .$$

2\. Hence show that, for any $m \times n$ matrix $A ,$

$$\| A x \| \leq \mu \| x \| ,$$

where $\mu ^ { 2 }$ denotes the largest eigenvalue of $A ^ { \prime } A .$

3\. Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix of $\mathrm { r a n k } r .$ Show that there exists an $n \times \left( n - r \right)$
matrix $S$ such that

$$A S = 0 , \quad S ^ { \prime } S = I _ { n - r } .$$

4\. Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix of rank r. Let $S$ be a matrix such that $A S = 0 .$
Show that $r \left( S \right) \leq n - r .$


### 15 THE JORDAN DECOMPOSITION

Schur's theorem tells us that there exists, for every square matrix $A ,$ a unitary
(possibly orthogonal) matrix $S$ which 'transforms' $A$ into an upper triangular
matrix $M ,$ whose diagonal elements are the eigenvalues of $A .$

Jordan's theorem similarly states that there exists a non-singular matrix,
$\mathrm { s a y } T ,$ which transforms $A$ into an upper triangular matrix $M ,$ whose diagonal
elements are the eigenvalues of $A .$ The difference between the two decomposi-
tion theorems is that in Jordan's theorem less structure is put on the matrix $T$
(non-singular, but not necessarily unitary) and more structure on the matrix
$M .$


#### Theorem 14 (Jordan decomposition)

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix and denote by $J _ { k } \left( \lambda \right)$ a $k \times k$ matrix of the form

$$J _ { k } \left( \lambda \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \lambda & 1 & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda & 1 & \ldots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & \lambda \end{array} \right)$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="19" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 16 ] The singular-value decomposition" -->

where $J _ { 1 } \left( \lambda \right) = \lambda$ a so-called Jordan block. Then there exists a non-singular
☒
$n \times n$ matrix $T$ such that

$$T ^ { - 1 } A T = \left( \begin{array}{} J _ { k _ { 1 } } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } \right) & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\ 0 & J _ { k _ { 2 } } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } \right) & \ldots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \ldots & J _ { k _ { r } } \left( \lambda _ { r } \right) \end{array} \right)$$
(2)

with $k _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } + \cdots + k _ { r } = n .$ The $\lambda _ { i }$ are the eigenvalues of $A ,$ not necessarily
distinct.

The most important special case of Theorem 14 is Theorem 15.


#### Theorem 15

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix with distinct eigenvalues. Then there exist a non-
singular $n \times n$ matrix $T$ and a diagonal $n \times n$ matrix $\Lambda$ whose diagonal elements
are the eigenvalues of $A ,$ such that

$$T ^ { - 1 } A T = \Lambda .$$
(3)

Proof. Immediate from Theorem 14 (or Theorem 11).
✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Show that $\left( \lambda I _ { k } - J _ { k } \left( \lambda \right) \right) ^ { k } = 0 ,$ and use this fact to prove Theorem 10.

2\. Show that Theorem 15 remains valid when $A$ is complex.


### 16 THE SINGULAR-VALUE DECOMPOSITION

The third important decomposition theorem is the singular-value decomposi-
tion.


#### Theorem 16 (singular-value decomposition)

Let $A$ be a real $m \times n$ matrix with $r \left( A \right) = r > 0 .$ Then there exist an $m \times r$
matrix $S$ such that $S ^ { \prime } S = I _ { r } ,$ an $n \times r$ matrix $T$ such that $T ^ { \prime } T = I _ { r } ,$ and an
$r \times r$ diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ with positive diagonal elements, such that

$$A = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)
.

Proof. Since $A A ^ { \prime }$ is a real $m \times m$ symmetric (in fact, positive semidefinite)
matrix of rank $r$ (by (7.3)), its non-zero eigenvalues are all positive (Theorem

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="20" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

8). From Theorem 13 we know that there exists an orthogonal $m \times m$ matrix
$\left( S : S _ { * } \right)$ such that

$$A A ^ { \prime } S = S \Lambda , \quad A A ^ { \prime } S _ { * } = 0 , \quad S S ^ { \prime } + S _ { * } S _ { * } ^ { \prime } = I _ { m } ,$$
(2)

where $\Lambda$ is an $r \times r$ diagonal matrix having these $r$ positive eigenvalues as
diagonal elements. Define $T = A ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$ Then we see that

$$A ^ { \prime } A T = T \Lambda , \quad T ^ { \prime } T = I _ { r } . ,$$
(3)

Thus, since (2) implies $A ^ { \prime } S _ { * } = 0$ by Theorem 1(b), we have

$$A = \left( S S ^ { \prime } + S _ { * } S _ { * } ^ { \prime } \right) A = S S ^ { \prime } A = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( A ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) ^ { \prime } = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } ,$$
(4)

which concludes the proof.
☐
✷

We see from (2) and (3) that the semi-orthogonal matrices $S$ and $T$ satisfy

$$A A ^ { \prime } S = S \Lambda , \quad A ^ { \prime } A T = T \Lambda .$$
(5)

Hence, $\Lambda$ contains the $r$ non-zero eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime }$ (and of $A ^ { \prime } A$ and $S$ (by
construction) and $T$ contain corresponding eigenvectors. $\mathrm { A }$ common mistake
in applying the singular-value decomposition is to find $S ,$ $T$ and $\Lambda$ from (5).
This is incorrect because, given $S ,$ $T$ is not unique! The correct procedure is to
find $S$ and $\Lambda$ from $A A ^ { \prime } S = S \Lambda$ and then define $T = A ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$ Alternatively,
we can find $T$ and $\Lambda$ from $A ^ { \prime } A T = T \Lambda$ and define $S = A T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$


### 17 FURTHER RESULTS CONCERNING EIGENVALUES

Let us now prove the following theorems, all of which concern eigenvalues.


#### Theorem 17

Let $A$ be a square $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } .$ Then

$$\mathrm { t r } A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i }$$
(1)

and

$$| A | = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } .$$
(2)

Proof. We write, using Theorem 12, $S ^ { * } A S = M .$ Then

$$\mathrm { t r } A = \mathrm { t r } S M S ^ { * } = \mathrm { t r } M S ^ { * } S = \mathrm { t r } M = \sum _ { i } \lambda _ { i }$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="21" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 17 ] Further results concerning eigenvalues" -->

and

$$| A | = | S M S ^ { * } | = | S | | M | | S ^ { * } | = | M | = \prod _ { i } \lambda _ { i } ,$$
(4)

thus completing the proof.


## Theorem 18

If $A$ has $r$ non-zero eigenvalues, then $r \left( A \right) \geq r .$

Proof. We write again, using Theorem 12, $S ^ { * } A S = M .$ We partition

$$M = \left( \begin{array}{} M _ { 1 } & M _ { 2 } \\ 0 & M _ { 3 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(5)

where $M _ { 1 }$ is a non-singular upper triangular $r \times r$ matrix and $M _ { 3 }$ is strictly
upper triangular. Since $r \left( A \right) = r \left( M \right) \geq r \left( M _ { 1 } \right) = r ,$ the result follows.
☐
✷

The following example shows that it is indeed possible that $r \left( A \right) > r .$ Let

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & - 1 \\ 1 & - 1 \end{array} \right) .$$
(6)

Then $r \left( A \right) = 1$ and both eigenvalues of $A$ are zero.


### Theorem 19

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. If $\lambda$ is a simple eigenvalue of $A ,$ then $r \left( \lambda I - A \right) =$
$n - \quad 1 .$ Conversely, if $r \left( \lambda I - A \right) = n - 1 ,$ then $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of $A ,$ but not
necessarily a simple eigenvalue.

Proof. Let $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ be the eigenvalues of $A .$ Then $B = \lambda I - A$ has eigen-
values $\lambda - \lambda _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and, since $\lambda$ is a simple eigenvalue of $A ,$ $B$ has a
simple eigenvalue zero. Hence $r \left( B \right) \leq n - 1 .$ Also, since $B$ has $n \quad - \quad 1$ non-zero
eigenvalues, $r \left( B \right) \geq n - 1$ (Theorem 18). Hence $r \left( B \right) = n \quad - 1 .$ Conversely, if
$r \left( B \right) = n \quad - \quad 1 ,$ then $B$ has at least one zero eigenvalue and hence $\lambda = \lambda _ { i }$ for
at least one $i .$
☐
✷


### Corollary

An $n \times n$ matrix with a simple zero eigenvalue has rank $n \quad -$ 1.


### Theorem 20

If $A$ is symmetric and has $r$ non-zero eigenvalues, then $r \left( A \right) = r .$

Proof. Using Theorem 13, we have $S ^ { \prime } A S = \Lambda$ and hence

$$r \left( A \right) = r \left( S \Lambda S ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( \Lambda \right) = r$$
(7)

☐
✷

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="22" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

and the result follows.

✷

☐


### Theorem 21

If $A$ is an idempotent matrix with $r$ eigenvalues equal to one, then $r \left( A \right) =$
tr $A = r$

$P r o o f .$ By Theorem 12, $S ^ { * } A S = M$ (upper triangular), where

$$M = \left( \begin{array}{} M _ { 1 } & M _ { 2 } \\ 0 & M _ { 3 } \end{array} \right)$$
(8)

with $M _ { 1 }$ a unit upper triangular $r \times r$ matrix and $M _ { 3 }$ a strictly upper trian-
gular matrix. Since $A$ is idempotent, so is $M$ and hence

$$\left( \begin{array}{} M _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } & M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } + M _ { 2 } M _ { 3 } \\ 0 & M _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} M _ { 1 } & M _ { 2 } \\ 0 & M _ { 3 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(9)

This implies that $M _ { 1 }$ is idempotent; it is non-singular, hence $M _ { 1 } = I _ { r } ,$ (see
Exercise 1). Also, $M _ { 3 }$ is idempotent and all its eigenvalues are zero, hence
$M _ { 3 } = 0$ (see Exercise 2), so that

$$M = \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { r } & M _ { 2 } \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$
(10)

Hence,

$$r \left( A \right) = r \left( M \right) = r \left( I _ { r } : M _ { 2 } \right) = r .$$
(11)

Also, by Theorem 17,

$$\mathrm { t r } A = \left( \text { sum of eigenvalues of } A \right) = r ,$$
(12)

thus completing the proof.

✷
☐

We note that in Theorem 21 the matrix $A$ is not required to be symmetric.
If $A$ is idempotent and symmetric, then it is positive semidefinite. Since its
eigenvalues are only 0 and 1, it then follows from Theorem 13 that $A$ can be
written as

$$A = G G ^ { \prime } , \quad G ^ { \prime } G = I _ { r } ,$$
(13)

where $r$ denotes the rank of $A .$


### Exercises

1\. The only non-singular idempotent matrix is the identity matrix.

2\. The only idempotent matrix whose eigenvalues are all zero is the null
matrix.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="23" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 18 ] Positive $\left( s e m i \right) d e f i n i t e$ matrices" -->

3\. If $A$ is a positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix with $r \left( A \right) = r ,$ then there
exists an $n \times r$ matrix $G$ such that

$$A = G G ^ { \prime } , \quad G ^ { \prime } G = \Lambda$$
(14)

where $\Lambda$ is an $r \times r$ diagonal matrix containing the positive eigenvalues
of $A .$


## 18 POSITIVE (SEMI)DEFINITE MATRICES

Positive (semi)definite matrices were introduced in Section 6. We have already
seen that $A A ^ { \prime }$ and $A ^ { \prime } A$ are both positive semidefinite and that the eigenvalues
of $a$ positive (semi)definite matrix are all positive (non-negative) (Theorem
8). We now present some more properties of positive (semi)definite matrices.


### Theorem 22

Let $A$ be positive definite and $B$ positive semidefinite. Then

$$| A + B | \geq | A |$$
(1)

with equality if and only if $B = 0 .$

$P r o o f .$ Let $\Lambda$ be a positive definite diagonal matrix such that

$$S ^ { \prime } A S = \Lambda , \quad S ^ { \prime } S = I .$$
(2)

Then, $S S ^ { \prime } = I$ and

$$A + B = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( I + \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } B S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime }$$
(3)

and, hence, using (9.2),

$$| A + B | = | S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } | | I + \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } B S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } | | \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } |$$
$$= | S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } | | I + \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } B S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } |$$
$$= | A | | I + \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } B S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } | .$$

(4)

If $B = 0$ then $| A + B | = | A | .$ If $B \neq 0 ,$ then the matrix $\Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } B S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ will
be positive semidefinite with at least one positive eigenvalue. Hence we have
$| I + \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } B S \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } | > 1$ and $| A + B | > | A | .$
✷
☐


### Theorem 23

Let $A$ be positive definite and $B$ symmetric of the same order. Then there
exist a non-singular matrix $P$ and a diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ such that

$$A = P P ^ { \prime } , \quad B = P \Lambda P ^ { \prime } .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="24" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

Proof. Let $C = A ^ { - 1 / 2 } B A ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$ Since $C$ is symmetric, there exist by Theorem
13 an orthogonal matrix $S$ and a diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ such that

$$S ^ { \prime } C S = \Lambda , \quad S ^ { \prime } S = I .$$
(6)

Now define

$$P = A ^ { 1 / 2 } S .$$
(7)

Then,

$$P P ^ { \prime } = A ^ { 1 / 2 } S S ^ { \prime } A ^ { 1 / 2 } = A ^ { 1 / 2 } A ^ { 1 / 2 } = A$$
(8)

and

$$P \Lambda P ^ { \prime } = A ^ { 1 / 2 } S \Lambda S ^ { \prime } A ^ { 1 / 2 } = A ^ { 1 / 2 } C A ^ { 1 / 2 } = A ^ { 1 / 2 } A ^ { - 1 / 2 } B A ^ { - 1 / 2 } A ^ { 1 / 2 } = B .$$
(9)

(If $B$ is positive semidefinite, so is A.)
✷
☐

For two symmetric matrices $A$ and $B$ we shall write $A \geq B$ (or $B \leq A$
if $A \quad - \quad B$ is positive semidefinite, and $A > B$ (or $\left. B < A \right)$ if $A \quad - \quad B$ is positive
definite.


### Theorem 24

Let $A$ and $B$ be positive definite $n \times n$ matrices. Then $A > B$ if and only if
$B ^ { - 1 } > A ^ { - 1 } .$

Proof. By Theorem 23 there exist a non-singular matrix $P$ and a positive
definite diagonal matrix $\Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } \right)$ such that

$$A = P P ^ { \prime } , \quad B = P \Lambda P ^ { \prime } .$$
(10)

Then

$$A - B = P \left( I - \Lambda \right) P ^ { \prime } , \quad B ^ { - 1 } - A ^ { - 1 } = P ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 1 } \left( \Lambda ^ { - 1 } - I \right) P ^ { - 1 } .$$
(11)

If $A \quad - \quad B$ is positive definite, then $I - \Lambda$ is positive definite and hence $0 < \lambda _ { i } <$
1 $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$ This implies that $\Lambda ^ { - 1 } - I$ is positive definite and hence that
$B ^ { - 1 } - A ^ { - 1 }$ is positive definite.
✷
☐


### Theorem 25

Let $A$ and $B$ be positive definite matrices such that $A \quad - \quad B$ is positive semidef-
inite. Then $| A | \geq | B |$ with equality if and only if $A = B .$

Proof. Let $C = A \quad - \quad B .$ Then $B$ is positive definite and $C$ is positive semidefi-
nite. Thus, by Theorem 22, $| B + C | \geq | B |$ with equality $\mathrm { i f }$ and only if $C = 0 ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="25" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 19 ] Three further results for positive definite matrices" -->

that is, $| A | \geq | B$ with equality if and only if $A = B .$
☐

✷

A useful special case of Theorem 25 is Theorem 26.


### Theorem 26

Let $A$ be positive definite with $| A | = 1 .$ If $I \quad - \quad A$ is also positive semidefinite,
then $A = I .$

Proof. This follows immediately from Theorem 25.
✷
☐


## 19 THREE FURTHER RESULTS FOR POSITIVE DEFINITE MATRICES

Let us now prove Theorem 27.


### Theorem 27

Let $A$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix, and let $B$ be the $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$
matrix

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} A & b \\ b ^ { \prime } & \alpha \end{array} \right) .$$
(1)

Then, (i)

$$| B | \leq \alpha | A |$$
(2)

with equality if and only if $b = 0 ;$ and (ii) $B$ is positive definite if and only if
$| B | > 0 .$

Proof. Define the $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrix

$$P = \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { n } & - A ^ { - 1 } b \\ 0 ^ { \prime } & 1 \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

Then

$$P ^ { \prime } B P = \left( \begin{array}{} A & 0 \\ 0 ^ { \prime } & \alpha - b ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } b \end{array} \right) ,$$
(4)

so that

$$| B | = | P ^ { \prime } B P | = | A | \left( \alpha - b ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } b \right) .$$
(5)

(Compare Exercise 11.2.) Statement (i) of the theorem is an immediate conse-
quence of (5). To prove (ii) we note that $| B | > 0$ if and only if $\alpha - b ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } b > 0$
(from (5)), which is the case if and only if $P ^ { \prime } B P$ is positive definite (from

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="26" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

(4)). This in turn is true if and only if $B$ is positive definite.
✷
☐

An immediate consequence of Theorem 27, proved by induction, is the
following.


### Theorem 28

If $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ is a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix, then

$$| A | \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i i }$$
(6)

with equality if and only if $A \quad i s \quad d i a g o n a l .$

Another consequence of Theorem 27 is Theorem 29.


### Theorem 29

A symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is positive definite if and only if all principal
minors $| A _ { k } | \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ are positive.

Note. The $k \times k$ matrix $A _ { k }$ is obtained from $A$ by deleting the last $n \quad - \quad k$ rows
and columns of $A .$ Notice that $A _ { n } = A .$

$P r o o f .$ Let $E _ { k } = \left( I _ { k } : 0 \right)$ be a $k \times n$ matrix, so that $A _ { k } = E _ { k } A E _ { k } ^ { \prime } .$ Let $y$ be an
arbitrary $k \times 1$ vector, $y \neq 0 .$ Then

$$y ^ { \prime } A _ { k } y = \left( E _ { k } ^ { \prime } y \right) ^ { \prime } A \left( E _ { k } ^ { \prime } y \right) > 0$$
(7)

since $E _ { k } ^ { \prime } y \neq 0$ and $A$ is positive definite. Hence $A _ { k }$ is positive definite, and,
in particular, $| A _ { k } | > 0 .$

The converse follows by repeated application of Theorem 27 (ii).
☐

✷


### Exercises

1\. If $A$ is positive definite show that the matrix

$$\left( \begin{array}{} A & b \\ b ^ { \prime } & b ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } b \end{array} \right)$$

is positive semidefinite and singular, and find the eigenvector associated
with the zero eigenvalue.

2\. Hence show that, for positive definite $A ,$

$$x ^ { \prime } A x - 2 b ^ { \prime } x \geq - b ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } b$$

for every x, with equality if and only if $x = A ^ { - 1 } b .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="27" -->
<!-- PageHeader="20 ] A useful result" -->


## 20 A USEFUL RESULT

If $A$ is a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix, then, in accordance with Theorem 28,

$$| A | = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i i }$$
(1)

if and only if $A$ is diagonal. If $A$ is merely symmetric, then Equation (1),
while obviously necessary, is no longer sufficient for the diagonality of $A .$ For
example, the matrix

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 3 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right)$$
(2)

has determinant $| A | = 8$ (its eigenvalues are -1, -1 and 8), thus satisfying
(1), but $A$ is not diagonal.

Theorem 30 gives a necessary and sufficient condition for the diagonality
of a symmetric matrix.


### Theorem 30

A real symmetric matrix is diagonal if and only if its eigenvalues and its
diagonal elements coincide.

Proof. Let $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ be a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix. The 'only if' part of the
theorem is trivial. To prove the 'if' part, assume that $\lambda _ { i } \left( A \right) = a _ { i i } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$
and consider the matrix

$$B = A + k I ,$$
(3)

where $k > 0$ is such that $B$ is positive definite. Then

$$\lambda _ { i } \left( B \right) = \lambda _ { i } \left( A \right) + k = a _ { i i } + k = b _ { i i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(4)

and hence

$$| B | = \prod _ { 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } \left( B \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { i i } .$$
(5)

It then follows from Theorem 28 that $B$ is diagonal, and hence that $A$ is
diagonal.
✷
☐


## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. If $A$ and $B$ are square matrices such that $A B = 0 ,$ $A \neq 0 ,$ $B \neq 0 ,$ then
prove that $| A | = | B | = 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="28" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Basic properties of vectors and matrices [Ch. 1" -->

2\. If $x$ and $y$ are vectors of the same order, prove that $x ^ { \prime } y = \mathrm { t r } y x ^ { \prime } .$

3\. Let $P$ and $Q$ be square matrices and $| Q | \neq 0 .$ Show that

$$| \begin{array}{} P & R \\ S & Q \end{array} | = | Q | | P - R Q ^ { - 1 } S | .$$

4\. Show that $\left( I - A B \right) ^ { - 1 } = I + A \left( I - B A \right) ^ { - 1 } B ,$ if the inverses exist.

5\. Show that

$$\left( \alpha I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } - \left( \beta I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } = \left( \beta - \alpha \right) \left( \beta I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } \left( \alpha I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$

.

6\. If $A$ is positive definite, show that $A + A ^ { - 1 } - 2 I$ is positive semidefinite.

7\. For any symmetric matrices $A$ and $B ,$ show that $A B \quad - \quad B A$ is skew
symmetric.

8\. Prove that the eigenvalues $\lambda _ { i }$ of $\left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } A ,$ where $A$ is positive semidef-
inite and $B$ is positive definite, satisfy $0 \leq \lambda _ { i } < 1 .$

9\. Let $x$ and $y$ be $n \times 1$ vectors. Prove that $x y ^ { \prime }$ has $n \quad - \quad 1$ zero eigenvalues
and one eigenvalue $x ^ { \prime } y .$

10\. Show that $| I + x y ^ { \prime } | = 1 + x ^ { \prime } y .$

11\. Let $\mu = 1 + x ^ { \prime } y .$ If $\mu \neq 0 ,$ show that $\left( I + x y ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } = I - \left( 1 / \mu \right) x y ^ { \prime } .$

12\. Show that $\left( I + A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } A = A \left( I + A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } .$

13\. Show that $A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } = \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } A .$

14\. (Monotonicity of the entropic complexity.) Let $A _ { n }$ be a positive definite
$n \times n$ matrix and define

$$\varphi \left( n \right) = \frac { n } { 2 } \log \mathrm { t r } \left( A _ { n } / n \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | A _ { n } | .$$

Let $A _ { n + 1 }$ be a positive definite $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrix such that

$$A _ { n + 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { n } & a _ { n } \\ a _ { n } ^ { \prime } & \alpha _ { n } \end{array} \right) .$$

Then,

$$\varphi \left( n + 1 \right) \geq \varphi \left( n \right)$$

with equality if and only if

$$a _ { n } = 0 , \quad \alpha _ { n } = \mathrm { t r } A _ { n } / n$$

(Bozdogan 1990, 1994).

15\. Let $A$ be positive definite, $X ^ { \prime } X = I ,$ and $B = X X ^ { \prime } A - A X X ^ { \prime } .$ Show
that

$$| X ^ { \prime } A X | | X ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } X | = | A + B | / | A |$$

(Bloomfield and Watson 1975).
