## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. There are many excellent introductory texts on matrix algebra. We men-
tion in particular Hadley (1961), Bellman (1970) and Rao (1973, Chapter 1).
More advanced are Gantmacher (1959) and Mirsky (1961).

§8. For a proof that each permutation matrix is orthogonal, and some exam-
ples, see Marcus and Minc (1964, Section 4.8.2).

§9. Aitken (1939, Chapter 5) contains a useful discussion of adjoint matrices.
§12. See Bellman (1970, Chapter 11, Theorem 8). The Cayley-Hamilton the-
orem is quite easily proved using the Jordan decomposition (Theorem 14).
§14. Schur's theorem is proved in Bellman (1970, Chapter 11, Theorem 4).
§15. Jordan's theorem is not usually proved in introductory texts. For a full
proof see Gantmacher (1959, Volume I, p. 201).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 2 Kronecker products, the vec

$$o p e r a t o r \quad a n d \quad t h e$$


## Moore-Penrose inverse


## 1 INTRODUCTION

This chapter develops some matrix tools that will prove useful to us later. The
first of these is the Kronecker product, which transforms two matrices $A =$
$\left( a _ { i j } \right)$ and $B = \left( b _ { s t } \right)$ into a matrix $C = \left( a _ { i j } b _ { s t } \right) .$ The vec operator transforms
a matrix into a vector by stacking $i t s$ columns one underneath the other.
We shall see that the Kronecker product and the vec operator are intimately
connected. Finally we discuss the Moore-Penrose inverse, which generalizes
the concept of the inverse of a non-singular matrix to singular square matrices
and rectangular matrices.


## 2 THE KRONECKER PRODUCT

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix and $B$ a $p \times q$ matrix. The $m p \times n q$ matrix defined
by

$$\left( \begin{array}{} a _ { 1 1 } B & \ldots & a _ { 1 n } B \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ a _ { m 1 } B & \ldots & a _ { m n } B \end{array} \right)$$
(1)

is called the Kronecker product of $A$ and $B$ and is written $A \otimes B .$

Observe that, while the matrix product $A B$ only exists if the number of
columns in $A$ equals the number of rows in $B$ or if either $A$ or $B$ is a scalar,
the Kronecker product $A \otimes B$ is defined for any pair of matrices $A$ and $B .$

<!-- PageNumber="31" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

32 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse $\left\lceil C h \right.$ 2

The following three properties justify the name Kronecker product:

$$A \otimes B \otimes C = \left( A \otimes B \right) \otimes C = A \otimes \left( B \otimes C \right) ,$$
(2)

$$\left( A + B \right) \otimes \left( C + D \right) = A \otimes C + A \otimes D + B \otimes C + B \otimes D ,$$
(3)

if $A + B$ and $C + D$ exist, and

$$\left( A \otimes B \right) \left( C \otimes D \right) = A C \otimes B D ,$$
(4)

if $A C$ and $B D$ exist.

If $\alpha$ is a scalar, then

$$\alpha \otimes A = \alpha A = A \alpha = A \otimes \alpha .$$
(5)

(This property can be used, for example, to prove that $\left( A \otimes b \right) B = \left( A B \right) \otimes b ,$
by writing $B = B \otimes 1$ Another useful property concerns two column vectors
$a$ and $b$ (not necessarily of the same order):

$$a ^ { \prime } \otimes b = b a ^ { \prime } = b \otimes a ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)

The transpose of a Kronecker product is

$$\left( A \otimes B \right) ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } \otimes B ^ { \prime } .$$
(7)

If $A$ and $B$ are square matrices (not necessarily of the same order), then

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( A \otimes B \right) = \left( \mathrm { t r } A \right) \left( \mathrm { t r } B \right) .$$
(8)

If $A$ and $B$ are non-singular, then

$$\left( A \otimes B \right) ^ { - 1 } = A ^ { - 1 } \otimes B ^ { - 1 } .$$
(9)


### Exercises

1\. Prove properties (2)-(9) above.

2\. If $A$ is a partitioned matrix,

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } & A _ { 1 2 } \\ A _ { 2 1 } & A _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) ,$$

then $A \otimes B$ takes the form

$$A \otimes B = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 1 } \otimes B & A _ { 1 2 } \otimes B \\ A _ { 2 1 } \otimes B & A _ { 2 2 } \otimes B \end{array} \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="33" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Eigenvalues of a Kronecker product" -->


## 3 EIGENVALUES OF A KRONECKER PRODUCT

Let us now demonstrate the following result.


### Theorem 1

Let $A$ be an $m \times m$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { m } ,$ and let $B$ be a
$p \times p$ matrix with eigenvalues $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { p } .$ Then the $m p$ eigenvalues of $A \otimes B$
are $\lambda _ { i } \mu _ { j } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m ; j = 1 , \ldots , p \right) .$

Proof. By Schur's theorem (Theorem 1.12) there exist non-singular (in fact,
unitary) matrices $S$ and $T$ such that

$$S ^ { - 1 } A S = L , \quad T ^ { - 1 } B T = M ,$$
(1)

where L and $M$ are upper triangular matrices whose diagonal elements are
the eigenvalues of $A$ and $B$ respectively. Thus

$$\left( S ^ { - 1 } \otimes T ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( A \otimes B \right) \left( S \otimes T \right) = L \otimes M .$$
(2)

Since $S ^ { - 1 } \otimes T ^ { - 1 }$ is the inverse of $S \otimes T ,$ it follows from Theorem 1.5 that
$A \otimes B$ and $\left( S ^ { - 1 } \otimes T ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( A \otimes B \right) \left( S \otimes T \right)$ have the same set of eigenvalues, and
hence that $A \otimes B$ and $L \otimes M$ have the same set of eigenvalues. But $L \otimes M$
is an upper triangular matrix since both $L$ and $M$ are upper triangular; its
eigenvalues are therefore its diagonal elements $\lambda _ { i } \mu _ { j } .$
✷
☐

Remark. If $x$ is an eigenvector of $A$ and $y$ is an eigenvector of $B ,$ then $x \otimes y$ is
clearly an eigenvector of $A \otimes B .$ It is not generally true, however, that every
eigenvector of $A \otimes B$ is the Kronecker product of an eigenvector of $A$ and an
eigenvector of $B .$ For example, let

$$A = B = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) , \quad e _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 \\ 0 \end{array} \right) , \quad e _ { 2 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 \\ 1 \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

Both eigenvalues of $A$ (and $\left. B \right)$ are zero and the only eigenvector is $e _ { 1 } .$ $\mathrm { T h e }$
four eigenvalues of $A \otimes B$ are all zero (in concordance with Theorem $\left. 1 \right) ,$ but
the eigenvectors of $A \otimes B$ are not just $e _ { 1 } \otimes e _ { 1 } ,$ but also $e _ { 1 } \otimes e _ { 2 }$ and $e _ { 2 } \otimes e _ { 1 } .$

Theorem 1 has several important corollaries. First, if $A$ and $B$ are posi-
tive (semi)definite, then $A \otimes B$ is positive (semi)definite. Secondly, since the
determinant of $A \otimes B$ is equal to the product of its eigenvalues, we obtain

$$| A \otimes B | = | A | ^ { p } | B | ^ { m } ,$$
(4)

where $A$ is an $m \times m$ matrix and $B$ is a $p \times p$ matrix. Thirdly, we can obtain the
rank of $A \otimes B$ from Theorem 1 as follows. The rank of $A \otimes B$ is equal to the rank
of $A A ^ { \prime } \otimes B B ^ { \prime } .$ The rank of the latter (symmetric, in fact positive semidefinite)
matrix equals the number of non-zero (in this case, positive) eigenvalues it

<!-- PageBreak -->

34 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse $\left[ C h . \right.$ 2

possesses. According to Theorem 1, the eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime } \otimes B B ^ { \prime }$ are $\lambda _ { i } \mu _ { j } ,$
where $\lambda _ { i }$ are the eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime }$ and $\mu _ { j }$ are the eigenvalues of $B B ^ { \prime } .$ Now,
$\lambda _ { i } \mu _ { j }$ is non-zero if and only if both $\lambda _ { i }$ and $\mu _ { j }$ are non-zero. Hence, the number
of non-zero eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime } \otimes B B ^ { \prime }$ is the product of the number of non-zero
eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime }$ and the number of non-zero eigenvalues of $B B ^ { \prime } .$ Thus the
rank of $A \otimes B$ is

$$r \left( A \otimes B \right) = r \left( A \right) r \left( B \right) .$$
(5)


### Exercise

1\. Show that $A \otimes B$ is non-singular if and only if $A$ and $B$ are non-singular,
and relate this result to (2.9).


## 4 THE VEC OPERATOR

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix and $a _ { i }$ its j-th column. Then vec $A$ is the $m n \times 1$
vector

$$\mathrm { v e c } A = \left( \begin{array}{} { a _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { a _ { n } } \end{array} \right)$$
(1)
·

Thus the vec operator transforms a matrix into a vector by stacking the
columns of the matrix one underneath the other. Notice that vec $A$ is defined
for any matrix $A ,$ not just for square matrices. Also notice that vec $A = \mathrm { v e c } B$
does not imply $A = B ,$ unless $A$ and $B$ are matrices of the same order.

$\mathrm { A }$ very simple but often useful property is

$$\mathrm { v e c } a ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { v e c } a = a$$
(2)

for any column vector a.

The basic connection between the vec operator and the Kronecker product
is

vec
$$a b ^ { \prime } = b \otimes a$$
(3)

for any two column vectors $a$ and $b$ (not necessarily of the same order). This
follows because the j-th column of $a b ^ { \prime }$ is $b _ { j } a .$ Stacking the columns of $a b ^ { \prime }$ thus
yields $b \otimes a .$

The basic connection between the vec operator and the trace is

$$\left( \mathrm { v e c } A \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } B = \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } B ,$$
(4)

where $A$ and $B$ are matrices of the same order. This is easy to verify since
both the left side and the right side of Equation (4) are equal to

$$\sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } a _ { i j } b _ { i j } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="35" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The vec operator" -->

Let us now generalize the basic properties (3) and (4). The generalization
of (3) is the following well-known result.


### Theorem 2

Let $A ,$ $B$ and $C$ be three matrices such that the matrix product $A B C$ is
defined. Then,

$$\mathrm { v e c } A B C = \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } B .$$
(5)

Proof. Assume that $B$ has $q$ columns denoted $b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots , b _ { q } .$ Similarly let
$e _ { 1 } , e _ { 2 } , \ldots , e _ { q }$ denote the columns of the $q \times q$ identity matrix $I _ { q } ,$ so that

$$B = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } b _ { j } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } .$$

Then, using (3),

$$\mathrm { v e c } A B C = \mathrm { v e c } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } A b _ { j } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } C = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \mathrm { v e c } \left( A b _ { j } \right) \left( C ^ { \prime } e _ { j } \right) ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \left( C ^ { \prime } e _ { j } \otimes A b _ { j } \right) = \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \left( e _ { j } \otimes b _ { j } \right)$$

$$= \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \mathrm { v e c } b _ { j } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } = \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } B ,$$
(6)
☐
✷
(7)

which completes the proof.

One special case of Theorem 2 is

vec $A B = \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes I _ { m } \right)$ vec A = (B' & A) vec In = (Iq & A) vec B,

where $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix and $B$ is an $n \times q$ matrix. Another special case
arises when the matrix $C$ in $\left( 5 \right)$ is replaced by a vector. Then we obtain, using
(2),

$$A B d = \left( d ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } B = \left( A \otimes d ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { v e c } B ^ { \prime } ,$$
(8)

where $d$ is a $q \times 1$ vector.

The equality (4) can be generalized as follows.


### Theorem 3

Let $A ,$ $B ,$ $C$ and $D$ be four matrices such that the matrix product $A B C D$ is
defined and square. Then,

$$\mathrm { t r } A B C D = \left( \mathrm { v e c } D ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } B = \left( \mathrm { v e c } D \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { v e c } B ^ { \prime } .$$
(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

36 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse $\left[ C h . \right.$ 2

Proof. We have, using (4) and (5),

$$\mathrm { t r } A B C D = \mathrm { t r } D \left( A B C \right) = \left( \mathrm { v e c } D ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A B C$$

$$= \left( \mathrm { v e c } D ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } B .$$
(10)

The second equality is proved in the same way starting from tr $A B C D =$
☐
✷

tr $D ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \right) .$


### Exercises

1\. For any $m \times n$ matrix $A ,$ prove that

$$\mathrm { v e c } A = \left( I _ { n } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } = \left( A ^ { \prime } \otimes I _ { m } \right) \mathrm { v e c } I _ { m } .$$

2\. If $A ,$ $B$ and $V$ are square matrices of the same order and $V = V ^ { \prime } ,$ prove
that

$$\left( \mathrm { v e c } V \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes B \right) \mathrm { v e c } V = \left( \mathrm { v e c } V \right) ^ { \prime } \left( B \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } V .$$


## 5 THE MOORE-PENROSE (MP) INVERSE

The inverse of a matrix is defined when the matrix is square and non-singular.
For many purposes it is useful to generalize the concept of invertibility to
singular matrices and, indeed, to non-square matrices. One such generalization
that is particularly useful because of its uniqueness is the Moore-Penrose $\left( M P \right)$
inverse.


### Definition

An $n \times m$ matrix $X$ is the MP inverse of a real $m \times n$ matrix $A$ if

$$A X A = A ,$$
(1)

$$X A X = X ,$$
(2)

$$\left( A X \right) ^ { \prime } = A X ,$$
$$\left( X A \right) ^ { \prime } = X A .$$
(3)
(4)

We shall denote the MP inverse of $A$ as $A ^ { + } .$


## Exercises

1\. What is the $\mathrm { M P }$ inverse of a non-singular matrix?

2\. What is the $\mathrm { M P }$ inverse of a scalar?

3\. What is the MP inverse of a null matrix?

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="37" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] Existence and uniqueness of the $M P$ inverse" -->


## 6 EXISTENCE AND UNIQUENESS OF THE MP INVERSE

Let us now demonstrate the following theorem.


### Theorem 4

For each $A ,$ $A ^ { + }$ exists and is unique.

Proof (uniqueness). Assume that two matrices $B$ and $C$ both satisfy the four
defining conditions. Then

$$A B = \left( A B \right) ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime } \left( A C A \right) ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \left( A B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A C \right) ^ { \prime } = A B A C = A C .$$

(1)

Similarly,

$$B A = \left( B A \right) ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } = \left( A C A \right) ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime }$$

$$= \left( C A \right) ^ { \prime } \left( B A \right) ^ { \prime } = C A B A = C A .$$
(2)

Hence,

$$B = B A B = B A C = C A C = C .$$
(3)

Proof (existence). Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix with $r \left( A \right) = r .$ If $r = 0 ,$ then
$A = 0$ and $A ^ { + } = 0$ satisfies the four defining equations. Assume therefore
$r > 0 .$ According to Theorem 1.16 there exist semi-orthogonal matrices $S$ and
$T$ and a positive definite diagonal $r \times r$ matrix $\Lambda$ such that

$$A = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } , \quad S ^ { \prime } S = T ^ { \prime } T = I _ { r } .$$
(4)

Now define

$$B = T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } .$$
(5)

Then,

$$A B A = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } = A ,$$
$$B A B = T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } = T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } = B . ,$$
$$A B = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } = S S ^ { \prime } \quad \text { is symmetric } ,$$
$$B A = T \Lambda ^ { - 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime } = T T ^ { \prime } \quad \text { is symmetric } .$$
(6)
(7)
(8)
(9)

Hence $B$ is the unique MP inverse of $A .$
✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="38 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse $\left[ C h . \right.$ 2" -->


## 7 SOME PROPERTIES OF THE MP INVERSE

Having established that for any matrix $A$ there exists one, and only one, MP
inverse $A ^ { + } ,$ let us now derive some of its properties.


## Theorem 5

(i) $A ^ { + } = A ^ { - 1 }$ for non-singular $A ,$

(ii) $\left( A ^ { + } \right) ^ { + } = A ,$

(iii) $\left( A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } = \left( A ^ { + } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$

(iv) $A ^ { + } = A$ if $A$ is symmetric and idempotent,

(v) $A A ^ { + }$ and $A ^ { + } A$ are idempotent,

(vi) $A ,$ $A ^ { + } ,$ $A A ^ { + }$ and $A ^ { + } A$ have the same rank,

(vii) $A ^ { \prime } A A ^ { + } = A ^ { \prime } = A ^ { + } A A ^ { \prime } ,$

(viii) $A ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } = A ^ { + } = A ^ { + } A ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } ,$

(ix) $\left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } = A ^ { + } A ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } , \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } = A ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } ,$

(x) $A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } A = A = A A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A ,$

(xi) $A ^ { + } = \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } ,$

(xii) $A ^ { + } = \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime }$ if $A$ has full column rank,

(xiii) $A ^ { + } = A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 }$ if $A$ has full row rank,

(xiv) $A = 0 \Leftrightarrow A ^ { + } = 0 ,$

(xv) $A B = 0 \Leftrightarrow B ^ { + } A ^ { + } = 0 ,$

(xvi) $A ^ { + } B = 0 \Leftrightarrow A ^ { \prime } B = 0 ,$

(xvii) $\left( A \otimes B \right) ^ { + } = A ^ { + } \otimes B ^ { + } .$

Proof. (i)-(v), (xiv) and (xvii) are established by direct substitution in the
defining equations. To prove (vi), notice that each $A ,$ $A ^ { + } ,$ $A A ^ { + }$ and $A ^ { + } A$
can be obtained from the others by pre- and post-multiplication by suitable
matrices. Thus their ranks must all be equal. (vii) and (viii) follow from the
symmetry of $A A ^ { + }$ and $A ^ { + } A .$ (ix) is established by substitution in the defining
equations using (vii) and (viii). (x) follows from (ix) and (vii); (xi) follows from
(ix) and (viii); (xii) and (xiii) follow from (xi) and (i). To prove (xv), note that
$\dot { B } ^ { + } A ^ { + } = \left( B ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \left( \dot { A } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } ,$ using (xi). Finally, to prove (xvi) we use $\left( \mathrm { x i } \right)$
and $\left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ and write $\left. A ^ { + } \dot { B } = \dot { 0 } \Leftrightarrow \widetilde { \left( \right. } A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B = 0 \stackrel { - } { \Longleftrightarrow } A ^ { \prime } A \left( \dot { A } ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B \stackrel { - } { }$
$0 \Leftrightarrow A ^ { \prime } B = 0 .$
✷
☐

<!-- PageFooter="Exercises" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="39" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] Further properties" -->

1\. Determine $a ^ { + } ,$ where $a$ is a column vector.

2\. If $r \left( A \right) = 1 ,$ show that $A ^ { + } = \left( \mathrm { t r } A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } .$

3\. Show that

$$\left( A A ^ { + } \right) ^ { + } = A A ^ { + }$$
$$\left( A ^ { + } A \right) ^ { + } = A ^ { + } A .$$
and

4\. If $A$ is block diagonal, then $A ^ { + }$ is also block diagonal. For example,

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & A _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) \quad \text { if and only if } \quad A ^ { + } = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 } ^ { + } & 0 \\ 0 & A _ { 2 } ^ { + } \end{array} \right) .$$

5\. Show that the converse of (iv) does not hold. [Hint: Consider $A = -$ I.]

6\. Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix. If $A$ has full row rank, show that $A A ^ { + } = I _ { m }$
if $A$ has full column rank, show that $A ^ { + } A = I _ { n } .$

7\. If $A$ is symmetric, then $A ^ { + }$ is also symmetric and $A A ^ { + } = A ^ { + } A .$

8\. Show that $\left( A T ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } = T A ^ { + }$ for any matrix $T$ satisfying $T ^ { \prime } T = I .$

9\. Prove the results of Theorem 5 using the singular-value decomposition.

10\. If | $| A | \neq 0 ,$ then $\left( A B \right) ^ { + } = B ^ { + } \left( A B B ^ { + } \right) ^ { + } .$


## 8 FURTHER PROPERTIES

In this section we discuss some further properties of the Moore-Penrose in-
verse. We first prove Theorem 6, which is related to Theorem 1.1.


## Theorem 6

$A ^ { \prime } A B = A ^ { \prime } C \Leftrightarrow A B = A A ^ { + } C .$

Proof. If $A B = A A ^ { + } C ,$ then

$$A ^ { \prime } A B = A ^ { \prime } A A ^ { + } C = A ^ { \prime } C ,$$
(1)

using Theorem 5(vii). Conversely, if $A ^ { \prime } A B = A ^ { \prime } C ,$ then

$$A A ^ { + } C = A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } C = A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } A B = A B ,$$
(2)

using Theorem $5 \left( x i \right)$ and (x).

✷
☐

Next, let us prove Theorem 7.


## Theorem 7

If $| B B ^ { \prime } | \neq 0 ,$ then $\left( A B \right) \left( A B \right) ^ { + } = A A ^ { + } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="40 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse $\left[ C h . \right.$ 2" -->

Proof. Since $| B B ^ { \prime } | \neq 0 ,$ $B$ has full row rank and $B B ^ { + } = I$ (Exercise 7.6).
Then,

$$A B \left( A B \right) ^ { + } = \left( A B \right) ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } \left( A B \right) ^ { \prime } = \left( A B \right) ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } = \left( A B \right) ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } A A ^ { + }$$

$$= \left( A B \right) ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \left( A B \right) ^ { \prime } A A ^ { + } = A B \left( A B \right) ^ { + } A A ^ { + } = A B \left( A B \right) ^ { + } A B B ^ { + } A ^ { + }$$
$$= A B B ^ { + } A ^ { + } = A A ^ { + } ,$$
(3)

using the fact that $A ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } A A ^ { + } .$
✷
☐

To complete this section we present the following two theorems on idem-
potent matrices.


## Theorem 8

Let $A = A ^ { \prime } = A ^ { 2 }$ and $A B = B .$ Then $A - B B ^ { + }$ is symmetric idempotent
with rank $r \left( A \right) - \quad r \left( B \right) .$ In particular, if $r \left( A \right) = r \left( B \right) ,$ then $A = B B ^ { + } .$

Proof. Let $C = A - B B ^ { + } .$ Then $C = C ^ { \prime } ,$ $C B = 0$ and $C ^ { 2 } = C .$ Hence $C$ is
idempotent. Its rank is

$$r \left( C \right) = \mathrm { t r } C = \mathrm { t r } A - \mathrm { t r } B B ^ { + } = r \left( A \right) - r \left( B \right) .$$
(4)

Clearly, if $r \left( A \right) = r \left( B \right) ,$ then $C = 0 .$
✷
☐


## Theorem 9

Let $A$ be a symmetric idempotent $n \times n$ matrix and let $A B = 0 .$ If $r \left( A \right) +$
$r \left( B \right) = n ,$ then $A = I _ { n } - B B ^ { + } .$

Proof. Let $C = I _ { n } - A .$ Then $C$ is symmetric idempotent and $C B = B .$
Further $r \left( C \right) = n \quad - \quad r \left( A \right) = r \left( B \right) .$ Hence, by Theorem 8, $C = B B ^ { + } ,$ that is,
$A = I _ { n } - B B ^ { + } .$
☐
✷


## Exercises

1\. Show that

$$X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } = X ^ { \prime }$$

for any positive definite matrix $V .$

2\. Hence show that if $\mathcal{M} \left( R ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ then

$$R \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } R = R$$

for any positive definite matrix $V .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="41" -->

3\. Let $V$ be a positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix of rank r. Let $\Lambda$ be an
$r \times r$ diagonal matrix with positive diagonal elements and let $S$ be a
semi-orthogonal $n \times r$ matrix such that

$$V S = S \Lambda , \quad S ^ { \prime } S = I _ { r } . ,$$

Then

$$V = S \Lambda S ^ { \prime } , \quad V ^ { + } = S \Lambda ^ { - 1 } S ^ { \prime } .$$

4\. Show that the condition, in Theorem 7, that $B B ^ { \prime }$ is non-singular is not
necessary. [Hint: Take $B = A ^ { + }$

5\. Prove Theorem 6 using the singular-value decomposition.

6\. Show that $A B B ^ { + } \left( A B B ^ { + } \right) ^ { + } = A B \left( A B \right) ^ { + } .$


## 9 THE SOLUTION OF LINEAR EQUATION SYSTEMS

An important property of the Moore-Penrose inverse is that it enables us to
find explicit solutions of a system of linear equations. We shall first prove
Theorem 10.


## Theorem 10

The general solution of the homogeneous equation $A x = 0$ is

$$x = \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q ,$$
(1)

where $q$ is an arbitrary vector of appropriate order.

Proof. Clearly, $x = \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q$ is a solution of $A x =$ 0. Also, any arbitrary
solution $x$ of the equation $A x = 0$ satisfies

$$x = \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) x ,$$
(2)

which demonstrates that there exists a vector $q$ (namely x) such that $x =$
$\left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q .$
✷
☐

The solution of $A x = 0$ is unique if, and only if, $A$ has full column rank,
since this means that $A ^ { \prime } A$ is non-singular and hence that $A ^ { + } A = I .$ The
unique solution is, of course, $x = 0 .$ If the solution is not unique, then there
exist an infinite number of solutions given by (1).

The homogeneous equation $A x = 0$ always has at least one solution,
namely $x = 0 .$ The inhomogeneous equation

$$A x = b$$
(3)

does not necessarily have any solution for x. If there exists at least one solu-
tion, we say that the Equation (3) is consistent.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="42 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse $\left[ C h . \right.$ 2" -->


## Theorem 11

Let $A$ be a given $m \times n$ matrix and $b$ a given $m \times 1$ vector. The following four
statements are equivalent:

(a) the vector equation $A x = b$ has a solution for x,

(b) $b \in \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) ,$

(c) $r \left( A : b \right) = r \left( A \right) ,$

(d) $A A ^ { + } b = b .$

Proof. It is easy to show that (a), (b) and (c) are equivalent. Let us show that
(a) and (d) are equivalent, $\mathrm { t o o } .$ Suppose $A x = b$ is consistent. Then there ex-
ists an $\widetilde { x }$ such that $A \widetilde { x } = b .$ Hence, $b = A \widetilde { x } = A A ^ { + } A \widetilde { x } = A A ^ { + } b .$ Now suppose
that $A A ^ { + } b = b$ and let $\widetilde { x } = A ^ { + } b .$ Then $A \widetilde { x } = A A ^ { + } b = b .$
✷
☐

Having established conditions for the existence of a solution of the in-
homogeneous vector equation $A x = b ,$ we now proceed to give the general
solution.


## Theorem 12

A necessary and sufficient condition for the vector equation $A x = b$ to have a
solution is that

$$A A ^ { + } b = b ,$$
(4)

in which case the general solution is

$$x = A ^ { + } b + \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q ,$$
(5)

where $q \quad i s \quad a n$ arbitrary vector of appropriate order.

Proof. That (4) is necessary and sufficient for the consistency of $A x = b$
follows from Theorem 11. Let us show that the general solution is given by
(5). Assume $A A ^ { + } b = b$ and define

$$x ^ { o } = x - A ^ { + } b .$$
(6)

Then, by Theorem 10,

$$A x = b \Leftrightarrow A x = A A ^ { + } b \Leftrightarrow A \left( x - A ^ { + } b \right) = 0 \Leftrightarrow A x ^ { o } = 0$$

$$\Leftrightarrow x ^ { o } = \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q \Leftrightarrow x = A ^ { + } b + \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q$$
(7)

and the result follows.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="43" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous exercises" -->

The system $A x = b$ is consistent for every $b$ if and only if $A$ has full row
rank (since $A A ^ { + } = I$ in that case). If the system is consistent, its solution is
unique if and only if $A$ has full column rank. Clearly if $A$ has full row rank
and full column rank then $A$ is non-singular and the unique solution is $A ^ { - 1 } b .$

We now apply Theorem 12 to the matrix equation $A X \quad B = C .$ This yields
the following theorem.


## Theorem 13

A necessary and sufficient condition for the matrix equation $A X \quad B = C$ to
have a solution is that

$$A A ^ { + } C B ^ { + } B = C . ,$$
(8)

in which case the general solution is

$$X = A ^ { + } C B ^ { + } + Q - A ^ { + } A Q B B ^ { + } ,$$
(9)

where $Q$ is an arbitrary matrix of appropriate order.

Proof. Write the matrix equation $A X B = C$ as a vector equation $\left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes \right.$
$\left. A \right)$ vec $X = \mathrm { v e c } C ,$ and apply Theorem 12, remembering that $\left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes \dot { \left. A \right) } ^ { + } = \right.$
$B ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \otimes A ^ { + } .$
✷
☐


## Exercises

1\. The matrix equation $A X \quad B = C$ is consistent for every $C$ if and only if
$A$ has full row rank and $B$ has full column rank.

2\. The solution of $A X \quad B = C ,$ if it exists, is unique if and only if $A$ has full
column rank and $B$ has full row rank.

3\. The general solution of $A X = 0$ is $X = \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) Q .$

4\. The general solution of $X \quad A = 0$ is $X = Q \left( I - A A ^ { + } \right) .$


## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. (Alternative proof of the uniqueness of the MP inverse.) Let $B$ and $C$
be two $\mathrm { M F }$ inverses of $A .$ Let $Z = C \quad - \quad B ,$ and show that

(i) $A Z A = 0 ,$

(ii) $Z = Z A Z + B A Z + Z A B ,$

(iii) $\left( A Z \right) ^ { \prime } = A Z ,$

(iv) $\left( Z A \right) ^ { \prime } = Z A .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

44 Kronecker products, the vec operator and the Moore-Penrose inverse [Ch. 2

Now show that (i) and (iii) imply $A Z = 0$ and that (i) and (iv) imply
$Z A = 0 .$ [Hint: If $P = P ^ { \prime }$ and $\dot { P } ^ { 2 } = 0 ,$ then $P =$ 0.] Conclude that
$Z = 0 .$

2\. Any matrix $X$ that satisfies $A X \quad A = A$ is called a generalized inverse of
$A$ and denoted $A ^ { - } .$ Show that $A ^ { - }$ exists and that

$$A ^ { - } = A ^ { + } + Q - A ^ { + } A Q A A ^ { + } , \quad Q \mathrm { a r b i t r a r y } . ,$$

3\. Show that $A ^ { - } A$ is idempotent, but not, in general, symmetric. However,
if $A ^ { - } A$ is symmetric, then $A ^ { - } A = A ^ { + } A$ and hence unique. A similar
result holds, of course, for $A A ^ { - } .$

4\. Show that $A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - } A ^ { \prime } = A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime }$ and hence is symmetric and idem-
potent.

5\. Show that a necessary and sufficient condition for the equation $A x = b$
to have a solution is that $A A ^ { - } b = b ,$ in which case the general solution
is $x = A ^ { - } b + \left( I - A ^ { - } A \right) q$ where $q$ is an arbitrary vector of appropriate
order. (Compare Theorem 12.)

6\. Show that $\left( A B \right) ^ { + } = B ^ { + } A ^ { + }$ if $A$ has full column rank and $B$ has full
row rank.

7\. Show that $\left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { 2 } B = A ^ { \prime } A$ if and only if $A ^ { + } = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } .$

8\. If $A$ and $B$ are positive semidefinite and $A B = B A ,$ show that
$\left( B ^ { 1 / 2 } A ^ { + } B ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) ^ { + ^ { 2 } } = B ^ { + 1 / 2 } A B ^ { + 1 / 2 }$ (Liu 1995).

9\. Let $b$ be an $n \times 1$ vector with only positive elements $b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n } .$ Let
$B = \mathrm { d g } \left( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n } \right)$ and $M = I _ { n } - \left( 1 / n \right) v l ^ { \prime } ,$ where $\imath$ denotes the $n \times 1$
sum vector $\left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime } .$ Then, $\left( B - b b ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } = M B ^ { - 1 } M$ (Tanabe and
Sagae 1992, Neudecker 1995).

10\. If $A$ and $B$ are positive semidefinite, then $A \otimes A - B \otimes B$ is positive
semidefinite if and only if $A \quad - \quad B$ is positive semidefinite (Neudecker and
Satorra 1993).

11\. If $A$ and $B$ are positive semidefinite, show that tr $A B \geq 0$ (see also
Exercise 11.5.1).

12\. Let $A$ be a symmetric $m \times m$ matrix, $B$ an $m \times n$ matrix, $C = A B$ and
$M = I _ { m } - C C ^ { + } .$ Prove that

$$\left( A C \right) ^ { + } = C ^ { + } A ^ { + } \left( I _ { m } - \left( M A ^ { + } \right) ^ { + } M A ^ { + } \right)$$

(Abdullah, Neudecker and Liu 1992).

13\. Let $A ,$ $B$ and $A - \quad B$ be positive semidefinite matrices. Necessary and suf-
ficient for $B ^ { + } - A ^ { + }$ to be positive semidefinite is $r \left( A \right) = r \left( B \right)$ (Milliken
and Akdeniz 1977, Neudecker 1989b).

<!-- PageNumber="45" -->

14\. For complex matrices we replace the transpose sign (') by the complex
conjugate sign $\left( { } ^ { * } \right)$ in the definition and the properties of the MP inverse.

Show that these properties, thus amended, remain valid for complex
matrices.


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§2-§3. See MacDuffee (1933, pp. 81-84) for some early references on the Kro-
necker product. The original interest in the Kronecker product focused on the
determinantal result (3.4).

§4. The 'vec' notation was introduced by Koopmans, Rubin and Leipnik
(1950). Theorem 2 is due to Roth (1934).

§5-88. The Moore-Penrose inverse was introduced by Moore $\left( 1 9 2 0 , 1 9 3 5 \right)$ and
rediscovered by Penrose (1955). There exists a large amount of literature on
generalized inverses, of which the Moore-Penrose inverse is one example. The
interested reader may wish to consult Rao and Mitra (1971), Pringle and
Rayner (1971), Boullion and Odell (1971), or Ben-Israel and Greville (1974).
§9. The results in this section are due to Penrose (1956).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 3 Miscellaneous matrix results


### 1 INTRODUCTION

In this final chapter of Part One we shall discuss some more specialized topics
which will be applied later in this book. These include some further results
on adjoint matrices (Sections 2 and 3), Hadamard products (Section 6), the
commutation and the duplication matrix (Sections 7-10) and some results
on the bordered Gramian matrix with applications to the solution of certain
matrix equations (Sections 13 and 14).


### 2 THE ADJOINT MATRIX

We recall from Section 1.9 that the cofactor $c _ { i j }$ of the element $a _ { i j }$ of any
square matrix $A$ is $\left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j }$ times the determinant of the submatrix obtained
from $A$ by deleting row $i$ and column $j .$ The matrix $C = \left( c _ { i j } \right)$ is called the
cofactor matrix of $A .$ The transpose of $C$ is called the adjoint matrix of $A$ and
we use the notation

$$A ^ { \# } = C ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)
.

We also recall the following two properties:

$$A A ^ { \# } = A ^ { \# } A = | A | I ,$$
(2)

$$\left( A B \right) ^ { \# } = B ^ { \# } A ^ { \# } .$$
(3)

Let us now prove some further properties of the adjoint matrix.


#### Theorem 1

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix $\left( n \geq 2 \right) ,$ and let $A ^ { \# }$ be the adjoint matrix of $A .$
Then

<!-- PageNumber="47" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="48" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

(a) if $r \left( A \right) = n ,$ then

$$A ^ { \# } = | A | A ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(4)

(b) if $r \left( A \right) = n - 1 ,$ then

$$A ^ { \# } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { k + 1 } \mu \left( A \right) \frac { x y ^ { \prime } } { y ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { k - 1 } \right) ^ { + } x }$$
(5)

where $k$ denotes the multiplicity of the zero eigenvalue of $A$ $\left( 1 \leq k \leq n \right) ,$ $\mu \left( A \right)$
is the product of the $n \quad - \quad k$ non-zero eigenvalues of $A$ (if $k = n ,$ we put
$\mu \left( A \right) = 1 ,$ and $x$ and $y$ are $n \times 1$ vectors satisfying $A x = A ^ { \prime } y = 0 ,$ and

(c) if $r \left( A \right) \leq n - 2 ,$ then

$$A ^ { \# } = 0 .$$
(6)

Before giving the proof of Theorem 1 we formulate the following two im-
portant corollaries.


#### Theorem 2

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix $\left( n \geq 2 \right) .$ Then

$$r \left( A ^ { \# } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} n & \mathrm { i f } r \left( A \right) = n \\ 1 & \mathrm { i f } r \left( A \right) = n - 1 \\ 0 & \mathrm { i f } r \left( A \right) \leq n - 2 . \end{array} \right.$$
(7)


#### Theorem 3

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix $\left( n \geq 2 \right.$ possessing a simple eigenvalue 0. Then
$r \left( A \right) = n \quad - \quad 1 ,$ and

$$A ^ { \# } = \mu \left( A \right) \frac { x y ^ { \prime } } { y ^ { \prime } x }$$
(8)

where $\mu \left( A \right)$ is the product of the $n \quad - \quad 1$ non-zero eigenvalues of $A ,$ and $x$ and
$y$ satisfy $A x = A ^ { \prime } y = 0 .$

$A$ direct proof of Theorem 3 is given in the Miscellaneous Exercises 4 and
5 at the end of Chapter 8.


#### Exercises

1\. Why is $y ^ { \prime } x \neq 0$ in (8)?

2\. Show that $y ^ { \prime } x = 0$ in (5) if $k \geq 2 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="49" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Proof of Theorem 1" -->

3\. Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Show that

(i) $| A ^ { \# } | = | A | ^ { n - 1 } \left( n \geq 2 \right) ,$

(ii) $\left( \alpha A \right) ^ { \# } = \alpha ^ { n - 1 } A ^ { \# } \left( n \geq 2 \right) ,$

(iii) $\left( A ^ { \# } \right) ^ { \# } = | A | ^ { n - 2 } A \left( n \geq 3 \right) .$


### 3 PROOF OF THEOREM 1

If $r \left( A \right) = n ,$ the result follows immediately from (2.2). To prove that $A ^ { \# } = 0$
if $r \left( A \right) \leq n - 2 ,$ we express the cofactor $c _ { i j }$ as

$$c _ { i j } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } | E _ { i } ^ { \prime } A E _ { j } | ,$$
(1)

where $E _ { j }$ is the $n \times \left( n - 1 \right)$ matrix obtained from $I _ { n }$ by deleting column $j .$
Now, $E _ { i } ^ { \prime } A E _ { j }$ is an $\left( n - 1 \right) \times \left( n - 1 \right)$ matrix whose rank satisfies

$$r \left( E _ { i } ^ { \prime } A E _ { j } \right) \leq r \left( A \right) \leq n - 2 .$$
(2)

It follows that $E _ { i } ^ { \prime } A E _ { j }$ is singular and hence that $c _ { i j } = 0 .$ Since this holds for
arbitrary $i$ and $j ,$ we have $C = 0$ and thus $A ^ { \# } = 0 .$

Finally, assume $r \left( A \right) = n \quad - \quad 1 .$ Let $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ be the eigenvalues of $A ,$
and assume

$$\lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 2 } = \cdots = \lambda _ { k } = 0 ,$$
(3)

while the remaining $n - k$ eigenvalues are non-zero. By Jordan's decomposition
theorem (Theorem 1.14), there exists a non-singular matrix $T$ such that

$$T ^ { - 1 } A T = J ,$$
(4)

where

$$J = \left( \begin{array}{} J _ { 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & J _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

Here $J _ { 1 }$ is the $k \times k$ matrix

$$J _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & 1 & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \ldots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & 0 \end{array} \right)$$ ☒
(6)

and $J _ { 2 }$ is the $\left( n - k \right) \times \left( n - k \right)$ matrix

$$J _ { 2 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \lambda _ { k + 1 } & \delta _ { k + 1 } & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda _ { k + 2 } & \delta _ { k + 2 } & \ldots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & \delta _ { n - 1 } \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & \lambda _ { n } \end{array} \right) .$$
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="50" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

where $\delta _ { j } \left( k + 1 \leq j \leq n - 1 \right)$ can take the values zero or one only.

It is easy to see that every cofactor of $J$ vanishes, with the exception of
the cofactor of the element in the $\left( k , 1 \right)$ position. Hence

$$J ^ { \# } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { k + 1 } \mu \left( A \right) e _ { 1 } e _ { k } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(8)

where $e _ { 1 }$ and $e _ { k }$ are the first and k-th unit vectors of order $n \times 1$ 1, and

$$\mu \left( A \right) = \prod _ { j = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } .$$
(9)

Using (2.3), (4) and (8), we obtain

$$A ^ { \# } = \left( T J T ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \# } = \left( T ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \# } J ^ { \# } T ^ { \# }$$
$$= T J ^ { \# } T ^ { - 1 } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { k + 1 } \mu \left( A \right) \left( T e _ { 1 } \right) \left( e _ { k } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$

(10)

From (5)-(7) we have $J e _ { 1 } = 0$ and $e _ { k } ^ { \prime } J = 0 ^ { \prime } .$ Hence, using (4),

$$A T e _ { 1 } = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad e _ { k } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } A = 0 ^ { \prime } .$$
(11)

Further, since $r \left( A \right) = n \quad - \quad 1 ,$ the vectors $x$ and $y$ satisfying $A x = A ^ { \prime } y = 0$ are
unique up to a factor of proportionality. Hence

$$x = \alpha T e _ { 1 } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad y ^ { \prime } = \beta e _ { k } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 }$$
(12)

for some real $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ Now,

$$A ^ { k - 1 } T e _ { k } = T J ^ { k - 1 } T ^ { - 1 } T e _ { k } = T J ^ { k - 1 } e _ { k } = T e _ { 1 } ,$$
(13)

and

$$e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } A ^ { k - 1 } = e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } T J ^ { k - 1 } T ^ { - 1 } = e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } J ^ { k - 1 } T ^ { - 1 } = e _ { k } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } .$$
(14)

It follows that

$$y ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { k - 1 } \right) ^ { + } x = \alpha \beta e _ { k } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } \left( A ^ { k - 1 } \right) ^ { + } T e _ { 1 }$$
$$= \alpha \beta e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } A ^ { k - 1 } \left( A ^ { k - 1 } \right) ^ { + } A ^ { k - 1 } T e _ { k }$$
$$= \alpha \beta e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } A ^ { k - 1 } T e _ { k } = \alpha \beta e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } J ^ { k - 1 } e _ { k } = \alpha \beta .$$

(15)

Hence, from (12) and (15),

$$\frac { x y ^ { \prime } } { y ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { k - 1 } \right) ^ { + } x } = \left( T e _ { 1 } \right) \left( e _ { k } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$
(16)

Inserting (16) in (10) concludes the proof.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="51" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] Bordered determinants" -->


### 4 BORDERED DETERMINANTS

The adjoint matrix also appears in the evaluation of the determinant of a
bordered matrix, as the following theorem demonstrates.


#### Theorem 4

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix, and let $x$ and $y$ be $n \times 1$ vectors. Then

$$| \begin{array}{} { A } & { x } \\ { y ^ { \prime } } & { 0 } \end{array} | = - y ^ { \prime } A ^ { \# } x .$$
(1)

Proof. Let $A _ { i }$ be the $\left( n - 1 \right) \times n$ matrix obtained from $A$ by deleting row i,
and let $A _ { i j }$ be the $\left( n - 1 \right) \times \left( n - 1 \right)$ matrix obtained from $A$ by deleting row
$i$ and column $j .$ Then,

$$| \begin{array}{} A & x & x \\ y ^ { \prime } & 0 & 0 \end{array} | = \sum _ { i } x _ { i } \left( - 1 \right) ^ { n + i + 1 } | \begin{array}{} A _ { i } \\ y ^ { \prime } \end{array} | = \sum _ { i , j } x _ { i } \left( - 1 \right) ^ { n + i + 1 } y _ { j } \left( - 1 \right) ^ { n + j } | A _ { i j }$$

$$= - \sum _ { i , j } \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } x _ { i } y _ { j } | A _ { i j } | = - \sum _ { i , j } x _ { i } y _ { j } A _ { j i } ^ { \# } = - y ^ { \prime } A ^ { \# } x ,$$
(2)

✷
using (1.9.7).
☐

As one of many special cases of Theorem 4, we mention Theorem 5.


#### Theorem 5

Let $A$ be a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $\left( n \geq 2 \right)$ of rank $r \left( A \right) = n - \quad 1 .$ Let $u$ be an
eigenvector of $A$ associated with the (simple) zero eigenvalue, so that $A u = 0 .$
Then,

$$| \begin{array}{} A & u \\ u ^ { \prime } & \alpha \end{array} | = - \left( \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \lambda _ { i } \right) u ^ { \prime } u ,$$
(3)

where $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n - 1 }$ are the non-zero eigenvalues of $A .$

Proof. Without loss of generality we may take $\alpha = 0 .$ The result then follows
immediately from Theorems 3 and 4.
✷
☐


#### Exercise

1\. Prove that $| A + \alpha \imath \imath ^ { \prime } | = | A | + \alpha \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { \# } \imath$ (Rao and Bhimasankaram 1992).


### 5 THE MATRIX EQUATION $A X = 0$

In this section we will be concerned in finding the general solutions of the
matrix equation $A X = 0 ,$ where $A$ is an $n \times n$ matrix with rank $n \quad -$ 1.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="52" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->


#### Theorem 6

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix (possibly complex) with rank $n \quad - \quad 1 .$ Let $u$ and $v$ be
eigenvectors of $A$ associated with the eigenvalue zero (not necessarily simple),
such that

$$A u = 0 , \quad v ^ { * } A = 0 ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)

The general solution of the equation

$$A X = 0$$
(2)

is

$$X = u q ^ { \prime }$$
(3)

where $q$ is an arbitrary vector of appropriate order. Moreover, the general
solution of the equations

$$A X = 0 ,$$
(4)

is

$$X = \mu u v ^ { * }$$
(5)

where $\mu \text { is an }$ arbitrary scalar.

Proof. If $A X = 0 ,$ then it follows from the complex analogue of Exercise 1.14.4
that $X = 0$ or $r \left( X \right) = 1 .$ Since $A u = 0$ and $r \left( X \right) \leq 1 ,$ each column of $X$ must
be a multiple of u, that is

$$X = u q ^ { \prime }$$
(6)

for some vector $q$ of appropriate order. Similarly, if $X A = 0 ,$ then

$$X = p v ^ { * }$$
(7)

for some vector $p$ of appropriate order. If $A X = X A = 0 ,$ we obtain by
combining (6) and (7),

$$X = \mu u v ^ { * }$$
(8)

for some scalar $\mu .$

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="53" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] The Hadamard product" -->


### 6 THE HADAMARD PRODUCT

If $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ and $B = \left( b _ { i j } \right)$ are matrices of the same order, say $m \times n ,$ then
we define the Hadamard product of $A$ and $B$ as

$$A \odot B = \left( a _ { i j } b _ { i j } \right) .$$
(1)

Thus, the Hadamard product $A$ O $B$ is also an $m \times n$ matrix and its ij-th
element is $a _ { i j } b _ { i j } .$

The following properties are immediate consequences of the definition:

$$A \odot B = B \odot A . ,$$
(2)

$$\left( A \odot B \right) ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } \odot B ^ { \prime } ,$$
$$\left( A \odot B \right) \odot C = A \odot \left( B \odot C \right) ,$$
(3)
(4)

so that the brackets in (4) can be deleted without ambiguity. Further

$$\left( A + B \right) \odot \left( C + D \right) = A \odot C + A \odot D + B \odot C + B \odot D ,$$
(5)

$$A \odot I = \mathrm { d g } A ,$$
(6)

$$A \odot J = A = J \odot A ,$$
(7)

where $J$ is a matrix consisting of ones only.

The following two theorems are of importance.


#### Theorem 7

Let $A ,$ $B$ and $C$ be $m \times n$ matrices, let $\imath = \left( 1 , 1 \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime }$ be the $n \times 1$ sum vector
and let $\Gamma = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \gamma _ { 1 } , \gamma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \gamma _ { m } \right)$ with $\gamma _ { i } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } .$ Then

(a)

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } \left( B \odot C \right) = \mathrm { t r } \left( A ^ { \prime } \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) C ,$$
$$\imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \left( B \odot C \right) \imath = \mathrm { t r } B ^ { \prime } \Gamma C .$$
(8)
(9)

(b)

Proof. To prove (a) we note that $A ^ { \prime } \left( B \odot C \right)$ and $\left( A ^ { \prime } \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) C$ have the same
diagonal elements, namely

$$\left[ A ^ { \prime } \left( B \odot C \right) \right] _ { i i } = \sum _ { h } a _ { h i } b _ { h i } c _ { h i } = \left[ \left( A ^ { \prime } \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) C \right] _ { i i } .$$
(10)

To prove (b) we write

$$\imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \left( B \odot C \right) \imath = \sum _ { i , j , h } a _ { h i } b _ { h j } c _ { h j } = \sum _ { j , h } \gamma _ { h } b _ { h j } c _ { h j } = \mathrm { t r } B ^ { \prime } \Gamma C .$$
(11)

This completes the proof.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="54" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->


#### Theorem 8

Let $A$ and $B$ be square $n \times n$ matrices, let $M$ be a diagonal $n \times n$ matrix, and
let $m$ be an $n \times 1$ vector such that

$$M = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { n } \right) , \quad m = M \imath . ,$$
(12)


<table>
<tr>
<th>Then</th>
<th colspan="2"></th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(a)</td>
<td>$$\mathrm { t r } A M B ^ { \prime } M = m ^ { \prime } \left( A \odot B \right) m ,$$</td>
<td>(13)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(b)</td>
<td>tr $$A B ^ { \prime } = \imath ^ { \prime } \left( A \odot B \right) \imath ,$$</td>
<td>(14)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(c)</td>
<td>$$M A \odot B ^ { \prime } M = M \left( A \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) M .$$</td>
<td>(15)</td>
</tr>
</table>


Proof. To prove (a) we write

$$\mathrm { t r } A M B ^ { \prime } M = \sum _ { i } \left( A M B ^ { \prime } M \right) _ { i i } = \sum _ { i , j } \mu _ { i } \mu _ { j } a _ { i j } b _ { i j } = m ^ { \prime } \left( A \odot B \right) m .$$
(16)

Taking $M = I _ { n } ,$ we obtain (b) as a special case of (a). Finally, we write

$$\left( M A \odot B ^ { \prime } M \right) _ { i j } = \left( M A \right) _ { i j } \left( B ^ { \prime } M \right) _ { i j } = \left( \mu _ { i } a _ { i j } \right) \left( \mu _ { j } b _ { j i } \right)$$

$$= \mu _ { i } \mu _ { j } \left( A \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) _ { i j } = \left( M \left( A \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) M \right) _ { i j } . ,$$
(17)

and this proves (c).

✷

☐


### 7 THE COMMUTATION MATRIX $K _ { m n }$

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix. The vectors vec $A$ and vec $A ^ { \prime }$ clearly contain the
same mn components, but in a different order. Hence there exists a unique
$m n \times m n$ permutation matrix which transforms vec $A$ into vec $A ^ { \prime } .$ This matrix
is called the commutation matrix and is denoted $K _ { m n }$ or $K _ { m , n } .$ (If $m = n ,$ we
often write $K _ { n }$ instead of $K _ { n n }$ Thus

$$K _ { m n } \mathrm { v e c } A = \mathrm { v e c } A ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)

Since $K _ { m n }$ is a permutation matrix it is orthogonal, i.e. $K _ { m n } ^ { \prime } = K _ { m n } ^ { - 1 } ,$ see
(1.8.4). Also, pre-multiplying (1) by $K _ { n m }$ gives $K _ { n m } K _ { m n }$ Vec $A = \mathrm { v e c } A ,$ so
that $K _ { n m } K _ { m n } = I _ { m n } .$ Hence,

$$K _ { m n } ^ { \prime } = K _ { m n } ^ { - 1 } = K _ { n m } .$$
(2)

Further, using (2.4.2),

$$K _ { n 1 } = K _ { 1 n } = I _ { n } .$$
(3)

The key property of the commutation matrix (and the one from which it
derives its name) enables us to interchange ('commute') the two matrices of
a Kronecker product.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="55" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7] The commutation matrix $K _ { m n }$" -->


#### Theorem 9

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix, $B$ a $p \times q$ matrix and $b$ a $p \times 1$ vector. Then

$$K _ { p m } \left( A \otimes B \right) = \left( B \otimes A \right) K _ { q n } ,$$
$$K _ { p m } \left( A \otimes B \right) K _ { n q } = B \otimes A ,$$
(4)
(5)


<table>
<tr>
<td>(a)</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(b)</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(c)</td>
<td>$$K _ { p m } \left( A \otimes b \right) = b \otimes A ,$$ $$K _ { m p } \left( b \otimes A \right) = A \otimes b .$$</td>
<td>(6)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(d)</td>
<td></td>
<td>(7)</td>
</tr>
</table>


Proof. Let $X$ be an arbitrary $q \times n$ matrix. Then, by repeated application of
(1) and Theorem 2.2,

Kpm (A & B) vec X = Kpm Vec $B X A ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { v e c } A X ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime }$

$$= \left( B \otimes A \right) \mathrm { v e c } X ^ { \prime } = \left( B \otimes A \right) K _ { q n } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$
(8)

Since $X$ is arbitrary, (a) follows. The remaining results are immediate conse-
quences of $\left( \mathrm { a } \right) .$
✷
☐

An important application of the commutation matrix is that it allows us
to transform the vec of a Kronecker product into the Kronecker product of
the vecs, a crucial property in the differentiation of Kronecker products.


#### Theorem 10

Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix and $B$ a $p \times q$ matrix. Then

$$\mathrm { v e c } \left( A \otimes B \right) = \left( I _ { n } \otimes K _ { q m } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } A \otimes \mathrm { v e c } B \right) .$$
(9)

Proof. Let $a _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and $b _ { j } \left( j = 1 , \ldots , q \right)$ denote the columns of $A$ and
$B ,$ respectively. Also, let $e _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and $u _ { j } \left( j = 1 , \ldots , q \right)$ denote the
columns of $I _ { n }$ and $I _ { q } ,$ respectively. Then we can write $A$ and $B$ as

$$A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \quad B = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } b _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(10)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="56" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

and we obtain

$$\mathrm { v e c } \left( A \otimes B \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \mathrm { v e c } \left( a _ { i } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \otimes b _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { i , j } \mathrm { v e c } \left( a _ { i } \otimes b _ { j } \right) \left( e _ { i } \otimes u _ { j } \right) ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i , j } \left( e _ { i } \otimes u _ { j } \otimes a _ { i } \otimes b _ { j } \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { i , j } \left( I _ { n } \otimes K _ { q m } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( e _ { i } \otimes a _ { i } \otimes u _ { j } \otimes b _ { j } \right)$$
$$= \left( I _ { n } \otimes K _ { q m } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( \left( \sum _ { i } \mathrm { v e c } a _ { i } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \otimes \left( \sum _ { j } \mathrm { v e c } b _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( I _ { n } \otimes K _ { q m } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } A \otimes \mathrm { v e c } B \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(11)

which completes the proof.

✷
☐

Closely related to the matrix $K _ { n }$ is the matrix $\frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } \right) ,$ denote $N _ { n } .$
Some properties of $N _ { n }$ are given in Theorem 11.


#### Theorem 11

Let $N _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } \right) .$ Then

(a)

$$N _ { n } = N _ { n } ^ { \prime } = N _ { n } ^ { 2 } ,$$
(12)

(b)

$$r \left( N _ { n } \right) = \mathrm { t r } N _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right) ,$$
$$N _ { n } K _ { n } = N _ { n } = K _ { n } N _ { n } .$$
(13)
(14)

(c)

Proof. The proof is easy and is left to the reader.
✷

#### Exercise

1\. Let $A \left( m \times n \right)$ and $B \left( p \times q \right)$ be two matrices. Show that

vec (A & B) = (In & G) vec $A = \left( H \otimes I _ { p } \right)$ vec $B ,$
☒

where

$$G = \left( K _ { q m } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( I _ { m } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } B \right) , \quad H = \left( I _ { n } \otimes K _ { q m } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } A \otimes I _ { q } \right) . ,$$ ☒ ☒


### 8 THE DUPLICATION MATRIX $D _ { n }$

Let $A$ be a square $n \times n$ matrix. Then $v \left( A \right)$ will denote the $\frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right) \times 1$
vector that is obtained from vec $A$ by eliminating all supradiagonal elements
of $A .$ For example, if $n =$ 3,

$$A = \left( a _ { 1 1 } , a _ { 2 1 } , a _ { 3 1 } , a _ { 1 2 } , a _ { 2 2 } , a _ { 3 2 } , a _ { 1 3 } , a _ { 2 3 } , a _ { 3 3 } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
vec
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="57" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] The duplication matrix $D _ { n }$" -->

and

$$v \left( A \right) = \left( a _ { 1 1 } , a _ { 2 1 } , a _ { 3 1 } , a _ { 2 2 } , a _ { 3 2 } , a _ { 3 3 } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(2)

In this way, for symmetric $A ,$ $v \left( A \right)$ contains only the generically distinct $\mathrm { e l }$
ements of $A .$ Since the elements of vec $A$ are those of $v \left( A \right)$ with some rep-
etitions, there exists a unique $n ^ { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrix which transforms, for
symmetric $A , v \left( A \right)$ into vec $A .$ This matrix is called the duplication matrix
and is denoted $D _ { n } .$ Thus,

$$D _ { n } v \left( A \right) = \mathrm { v e c } A \quad \left( A = A ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(3)

Let $A = A ^ { \prime }$ and $D _ { n } v \left( A \right) = 0 .$ Then vec $A = 0 ,$ and so $v \left( A \right) = 0 .$ Since
the symmetry of $A$ does not restrict $v \left( A \right) ,$ it follows that the columns of $D _ { n }$
are linearly independent. Hence $D _ { n }$ has full column rank $\frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right) ,$ $D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n }$ is
non-singular, and $D _ { n } ^ { + } ,$ the Moore-Penrose inverse of $D _ { n } ,$ equals

$$D _ { n } ^ { + } = \left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } D _ { n } ^ { \prime } .$$
(4)

Since $D _ { n }$ has full column rank, $v \left( A \right)$ can be uniquely solved from (3) and
we have

$$v \left( A \right) = D _ { n } ^ { + } \mathrm { v e c } A \quad \left( A = A ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(5)

Some further properties of $D _ { n }$ are easily derived from its definition (3).


#### Theorem 12

(a)

$$K _ { n } D _ { n } = D _ { n } ,$$
(6)

(b)

(c)

$$D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } \right) ,$$
$$D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( b \otimes A \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( b \otimes A + A \otimes b \right) ,$$
(7)
(8)

for any $n \times 1$ vector $b$ and $n \times n$ matrix $A .$

Proof. Let $X$ be a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix. Then

$$K _ { n } D _ { n } v \left( X \right) = K _ { n } \mathrm { v e c } X = \mathrm { v e c } X = D _ { n } v \left( X \right) .$$
(9)

Since the symmetry of $X$ does not restrict $v \left( X \right) ,$ we obtain (a). To prove (b),
let $N _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + \check { K } _ { n } \right) .$ Then, from (a), $N _ { n } D _ { n } = D _ { n } .$ Now, $N _ { n }$ is symmetric
idempotent with $r \left( N _ { n } \right) = r \left( D _ { n } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right)$ (Theorem 11(b)). Then, by
Theorem 2.8, $N _ { n } = D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } .$ Finally, (c) follows from (b) and the fact that
✷
☐

$K _ { n } \left( b \otimes A \right) = A \otimes b .$

Much of the interest in the duplication matrix is due to the importance
of the matrices $D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n }$ and $D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } ,$ some of whose properties
follow below.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="58" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->


#### Theorem 13

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Then

(a)

$$D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } = \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } ,$$
(10)

(b)

$$D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } = \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } ,$$
(11)

and if $A$ is non-singular,

(c)

(d)

$$\left( D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } = D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A ^ { - 1 } \otimes A ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { n } ,$$
$$\left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } = D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A ^ { - 1 } \otimes A ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { n } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } .$$
(12)
(13)

Proof. Let $N _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I + K _ { n } \right) .$ Then, since

$$D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } = N _ { n } , \quad N _ { n } \left( A \otimes A \right) = \left( A \otimes A \right) N _ { n } ,$$
$$N _ { n } D _ { n } = D _ { n } , \quad N _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } = D _ { n } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(14)
(15)

we obtain (a) and (b). To prove (c) we write

$$D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A ^ { - 1 } \otimes A ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { n } = D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) N _ { n } \left( A ^ { - 1 } \otimes A ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { n }$$

$$= D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) \left( A ^ { - 1 } \otimes A ^ { - 1 } \right) N _ { n } D _ { n } = D _ { n } ^ { + } D _ { n } = I _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right) } .$$
(16)

Finally, to prove (d), we use (c) and $D _ { n } ^ { + } = \left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } D _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ and write

$$\left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } = \left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \left( D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } \left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } = D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A ^ { - 1 } \otimes A ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { n } \left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
(17)

and the result follows.

✷
☐

Finally, we state, without proof, two further properties of the duplication
matrix which we shall need later.


#### Theorem 14

Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Then

(a)

$$D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A = v \left( A + A ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { d g } A \right) ,$$
$$| D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } | = 2 ^ { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n - 1 \right) } | A | ^ { n + 1 } .$$
(18)
(19)

(b)


### 9 RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN $D _ { n + 1 }$ AND $D _ { n } ,$ $I$

Let $A _ { 1 }$ be a symmetric $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrix. We wish to express $D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes \right.$
$\left. A _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 }$ and $D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes A _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + }$ as partitioned matrices. In particular, we

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="59" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Relationship between $D _ { n + 1 }$ and $D _ { n } ,$ $I$" -->

wish to know whether $D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n }$ is a submatrix of $D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes A _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 }$
and whether $D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( A \otimes A \right) D _ { n } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$ is a submatrix of $D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes A _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + }$ when $A$
is the appropriate submatrix of $A .$ The next theorem answers a slightly more
general question in the affirmative.


### Theorem 15

Let

$$A _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha & a ^ { \prime } \\ a & A \end{array} \right) , \quad B _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \beta & b ^ { \prime } \\ b & B \end{array} \right) , .$$

where $A$ and $B$ are symmetric $n \times n$ matrices, $a$ and $b$ are $n \times 1$ vectors and
$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are scalars. Then

(i) $D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes B _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 } =$

$$\left( \begin{array}{} \alpha \beta & \alpha b ^ { \prime } + \beta a ^ { \prime } & \left( a ^ { \prime } \otimes b ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { n } \\ \alpha b + \beta a & \alpha B + \beta A + a b ^ { \prime } + b a ^ { \prime } & \left( a ^ { \prime } \otimes B + b ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) D _ { n } \\ D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( a \otimes b \right) & D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( a \otimes B + b \otimes A \right) & D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes B \right) D _ { n } \end{array} \right) , .$$

(ii) $D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes B _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } =$

(iv)

Proof. Let $X _ { 1 }$ be an arbitrary symmetric $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrix partitioned
conformably with $A _ { 1 }$ and $B _ { 1 }$ as

$$X _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \xi & x ^ { \prime } \\ x & X \end{array} \right) .$$
(1)

Then,

$$A _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } B _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } = \left( \mathrm { v e c } X _ { 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes B _ { 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } X _ { 1 } \right)$$
tr

$$= \left( v \left( X _ { 1 } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes B _ { 1 } \right) D _ { n + 1 } v \left( X _ { 1 } \right)$$
(2)

and $a l s o$

$$\mathrm { t r } A _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } B _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } = \alpha \beta \xi ^ { 2 } + 2 \xi \left( \alpha b ^ { \prime } x + \beta a ^ { \prime } x \right) + \alpha x ^ { \prime } B x + \beta x ^ { \prime } A x$$

$$+ 2 \left( a ^ { \prime } x \right) \left( b ^ { \prime } x \right) + 2 \xi a ^ { \prime } X b + 2 \left( x ^ { \prime } B X a + x ^ { \prime } A X b \right) + \mathrm { t r } A X B X ,$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="60" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

which can be written as a quadratic form in $v \left( X _ { 1 } \right) ,$ since

$$\left( v \left( X _ { 1 } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } = \left( \xi , x ^ { \prime } , \left( v \left( X \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(4)

The first result now follows from $\left( 2 \right)$ and (3), and the symmetry of all matrices
concerned. By letting $A _ { 1 } = B _ { 1 } = I _ { n + 1 } ,$ we obtain (iii) as a special case of
(i). (iv) follows from (iii). Pre- and post-multiplying (i) by $\left( D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } D _ { n + 1 } \right) ^ { - 1 }$ as
given in (iv) yields (ii).

### 10 RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN $D _ { n + 1 }$ AND $D _ { n } ,$ $I I$

Related to Theorem 15 is the following result.


### Theorem 16

Let

$$A _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha & b ^ { \prime } \\ a & A \end{array} \right) , .$$
(1)

where $A$ is an $n \times n$ matrix (not necessarily symmetric), $a$ and $b$ are $n \times 1$
vectors and $\alpha$ is a scalar. Then

$$D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha \\ a + b \\ D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A \end{array} \right) , \quad D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \mathrm { v e c } A _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha \\ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a + b \right) \\ D _ { n } ^ { + } \mathrm { v e c } A \end{array} \right) .$$
(2)

$P r o o f .$ We have, using Theorem $1 4 \left( a \right) ,$

$$D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A _ { 1 } = v \left( A _ { 1 } + A _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { d g } A _ { 1 } \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha & \alpha \\ a & \\ v \left( A \right) & \end{array} \right) + \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha & \alpha & \alpha \\ b & \\ v \left( A ^ { \prime } \right) & \end{array} \right) - \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha & \\ 0 & \\ v \left( \mathrm { d g } A \right) & \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha & \\ a + b & \\ D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A & \end{array} \right) .$$

(3)

Also, using Theorem 15(iv),

$$D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \mathrm { v e c } A _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { n } & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \left( D _ { n } ^ { \prime } D _ { n } \right) ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha \\ a + b \\ D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } A \end{array} \right)$$

$$= \left( \begin{array}{} \alpha \\ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a + b \right) \\ D _ { n } ^ { + } \mathrm { v e c } A \end{array} \right)$$
(4)

and the resul follows.

✷
☐

As a corollary of Theorem 16 we obtain Theorem 17.

<!-- PageBreak -->

Sec. 11 ] Quadratic forms positive (negative) subject to linear constraints 61


### Theorem 17

Let $A$ be an $n \times p$ matrix and $b$ a $p \times 1$ vector. Then

$$D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \begin{array}{} b ^ { \prime } \\ A \\ 0 _ { 1 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} b ^ { \prime } \\ A \\ 0 _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) , \quad D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \left( \begin{array}{} b ^ { \prime } \\ A \\ 0 _ { 1 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} b ^ { \prime } \\ \frac { 1 } { 2 } A \\ 0 _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(5)

where $0 _ { 1 }$ and $0 _ { 2 }$ denote null matrices of orders $n \left( n + 1 \right) \times p$ and $\frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right) \times p$
respectively.

Proof. Let $\beta _ { i }$ be the i-th component of $b$ and let $a _ { i }$ be the i-th column of
$A \left( i = 1 , \ldots , p \right) .$ Define the $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrices

$$C _ { i } = \left( \begin{array}{} \beta _ { i } & 0 ^ { \prime } \\ a _ { i } & 0 \end{array} \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , p \right) .$$
(6)

Then, using Theorem 16,

$$C _ { i } = \left( \begin{array}{} \beta _ { i } \\ a _ { i } \\ 0 \end{array} \right) , \quad D _ { n + 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } C _ { i } = \left( \begin{array}{} \beta _ { i } \\ a _ { i } \\ 0 \end{array} \right) , \quad D _ { n + 1 } ^ { + } \mathrm { v e c } C _ { i } = \left( \begin{array}{} \beta _ { i } \\ \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { i } \\ 0 \end{array} \right)$$
vec
(7)

for $i = 1 , \ldots , p .$ Now, noting that

$$\left( \begin{array}{} b ^ { \prime } \\ A \\ 0 _ { 1 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \mathrm { v e c } C _ { 1 } , \mathrm { v e c } C _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { v e c } C _ { p } \right) ,$$
(8)

the result follows.

✷
☐


### 11 CONDITIONS FOR A QUADRATIC FORM TO BE POSI- TIVE (NEGATIVE) SUBJECT TO LINEAR CONSTRAINTS

Many optimization problems take the form

$$\mathrm { m a x i m i z e } \quad x ^ { \prime } A x$$
(1)

$$s u b j e c t \quad t o$$
(2)


<table>
