</table>


and, as we shall see later (Theorem 7.12), this problem also arises when we
try to establish second-order conditions for Lagrange minimization (maxi-
mization). The following theorem is then of importance.


## Theorem 18

Let $A$ be a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix and $B$ an $m \times n$ matrix with full row

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="62" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

rank $m .$ Let $A _ { r r }$ denote the $r \times r$ matrix in the top left corner of $A ,$ and $B _ { n }$
the $m \times r$ matrix whose columns are the first $r$ columns of $B \left( r = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$
Assume that $| B _ { m } | \neq 0 .$ Define the $\left( m + r \right) \times \left( m + r \right)$ matrices

$$\Delta _ { r } = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & B _ { r } \\ B _ { r } ^ { \prime } & A _ { r r } \end{array} \right) \quad \left( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(3)

and let $\Gamma = \left( x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } : x \neq 0 , B x = 0 \right) .$ Then

(i) $x ^ { \prime } A x > 0$ for all $x \in \Gamma$ if and only $\mathrm { i f }$

$$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } | \Delta _ { r } | > 0 \quad \left( r = m + 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(4)

(ii) $x ^ { \prime } A x < 0$ for all $x \in \Gamma$ if and only if

$$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { r } | \Delta _ { r } | > 0 \quad \left( r = m + 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(5)

$P r o o f .$ We partition $B$ and $x$ conformably as

$$B = \left( B _ { m } : B _ { * } \right) , \quad x = \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(6)

where $B _ { * }$ is an $m \times \left( n - m \right)$ matrix and $x _ { 1 } \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ $x _ { 2 } \in \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { n - m } .$ The constraint
$B x = 0$ can then be written as

$$B _ { m } x _ { 1 } + B _ { * } x _ { 2 } = 0 .$$
(7)

That is,

$$x _ { 1 } + B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * } x _ { 2 } = 0 ,$$
(8)

or equivalently,

$$x = Q x _ { 2 } , \quad Q = \left( \begin{array}{} - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * } \\ I _ { n - m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(9)

Hence we can write the constraint $\mathrm { s e t } \Gamma$ as

$$\Gamma = \left\{ x \in \mathrm { R } ^ { n } : x = Q y , y \neq 0 , y \in \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { n - m } \right\} ,$$
(10)

and we see that $x ^ { \prime } A x > 0 \left( < 0 \right)$ for all $x \in \Gamma$ if and only if the $\left( n - m \right) \times \left( n - m \right)$
matrix $Q ^ { \prime } A Q$ is positive definite (negative definite).

Next we investigate the signs of the $n \quad - \quad m$ principal minors of $Q ^ { \prime } A Q .$ For
$k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n - m ,$ let $E _ { k }$ be the $k \times \left( n - m \right)$ selection matrix

$$E _ { k } = \left( I _ { k } : 0 \right)$$
(11)

and let $C _ { k }$ be the $k \times k$ matrix in the top left corner of $Q ^ { \prime } A Q .$ Then

$$C _ { k } = E _ { k } Q ^ { \prime } A Q E _ { k } ^ { \prime } .$$
(12)

<!-- PageBreak -->

Sec. 11 ] Quadratic forms positive (negative) subject to linear constraints 63

We partition $B _ { * } = \left( B _ { * 1 } : B _ { * 2 } \right) ,$ where $B _ { * 1 }$ is an $m \times k$ matrix and $B _ { * 2 }$ an
$m \times \left( n - m - k \right)$ matrix, and define the $\left( m + k \right) \times k$ matrix

$$Q _ { k } = \left( \begin{array}{} - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * 1 } \\ I _ { k } \end{array} \right) .$$
(13)

We then have

$$Q E _ { k } ^ { \prime } = \left( \begin{array}{} - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * 1 } & - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * 2 } \\ I _ { k } & 0 \\ 0 & I _ { n - m - k } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { k } \\ 0 \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * 1 } \\ I _ { k } \\ 0 \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { k } \\ 0 \end{array} \right)$$

(14)

and hence

$$C _ { k } = \left( Q _ { k } ^ { \prime } : 0 \right) \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { m + k , m + k } & * \\ * & * \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { k } \\ 0 \end{array} \right) = Q _ { k } ^ { \prime } A _ { m + k , m + k } Q _ { k } ,$$
(15)

where *'s indicate matrices the precise form of which is of no relevance. Now,
let $T _ { k }$ be the non-singular $\left( m + k \right) \times \left( m + k \right)$ matrix

$$T _ { k } = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { m } & B _ { * 1 } \\ 0 & I _ { k } \end{array} \right) .$$
(16)

Its inverse is

$$T _ { k } ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } & - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * 1 } \\ 0 & I _ { k } \end{array} \right)$$
(17)

and one verifies easily that

$$B _ { m + k } T _ { k } ^ { - 1 } = \left( B _ { m } : B _ { * 1 } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } & - B _ { m } ^ { - 1 } B _ { * 1 } \\ 0 & I _ { k } \end{array} \right) = \left( I _ { m } : 0 \right) .$$
(18)

Hence,

$$\left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } & 0 \\ 0 & T _ { k } ^ { - 1 ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & B _ { m + k } \\ B _ { m + k } ^ { \prime } & A _ { m + k , m + k } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } & 0 \\ 0 & T _ { k } ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & B _ { m + k } T _ { k } ^ { - 1 } \\ T _ { k } ^ { - 1 ^ { \prime } } B _ { m + k } ^ { \prime } & T _ { k } ^ { - 1 ^ { \prime } } A _ { m + k , m + k } T _ { k } ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & I _ { m } & 0 \\ I _ { m } & * & * \\ 0 & * & C _ { k } \end{array} \right)$$

$$= \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } & 0 & 0 \\ * & I _ { m } & * \\ * & 0 & C _ { k } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & I _ { m } & 0 \\ I _ { m } & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & I _ { k } \end{array} \right) .$$
(19)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="64" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

Taking determinants on both sides of (19) we obtain

$$| T _ { k } ^ { - 1 } | ^ { 2 } | \Delta _ { m + k } | = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } | C _ { k } |$$
(20)

(see Exercise 1.13.1), and hence

$$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } | \Delta _ { m + k } | = | T _ { k } | ^ { 2 } | C _ { k } | \quad \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n - m \right) .$$
(21)

Thus, $x ^ { \prime } A x > 0$ for all $x \in \Gamma ,$ if and $o n l y$ if $Q ^ { \prime } A Q$ is positive definite, if and
only if $| C _ { k } | > 0 \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n - m \right) ,$ if and only if $\left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } | \Delta _ { m + k } | > 0$ $\left( k = \right.$
$\left. 1 , \ldots , n - m \right) .$

Similarly, $x ^ { \prime } A x < 0$ for all $x \in \Gamma ,$ if and only if $Q ^ { \prime } A Q$ is negative definite, if
and only if $\left( - 1 \right) ^ { k } | C _ { k } | > 0 \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n - m \right) ,$ if and only if $\left( - 1 \right) ^ { m + k } | \Delta _ { m + k } | >$

0 $\left( k = 1 , \ldots , n - m \right) .$

✷

☐


## 12 NECESSARY AND SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS FOR

$r \left( A : B \right) = r \left( A \right) + r \left( B \right)$

Let us now prove Theorem 19.


### Theorem 19

Let $A$ and $B$ be two matrices with the same number of rows. Then the fol-
lowing seven statements are equivalent.

(i) $\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) \cap \mathcal{M} \left( B \right) = \left\{ 0 \right\} ,$

(ii) $r \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( A \right) + r \left( B \right) ,$

(iii) $A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A$ is idempotent,

(iv) $A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A = A ^ { + } A . ,$

(v) $B ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } B$ is idempotent,

(vi) $B ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } B = B ^ { + } B ,$

(vii) $A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } B = 0 .$

Proof. (ii) => (i): Since $r \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( A : B \right) ,$ (ii) implies $r \left( A : B \right) =$
$r \left( A \right) + r \left( B \right) .$ Hence the linear space spanned by the columns of $A$ and the linear
space spanned by the columns of $B$ are disjoint, that is, $\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) \cap \mathcal{M} \left( B \right) = \left\{ 0 \right\} .$

(i) => (iii): We shall show that (i) implies that the eigenvalues of the ma-
trix $\left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A A ^ { \prime }$ are either zero or one. Then, by Theorem 1.9, the same
is true for the symmetric matrix $A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A ,$ thus proving its idem-
potency. Let $\lambda$ be an eigenvalue of $\left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A A ^ { \prime } ,$ and $x$ a corresponding
eigenvector, so that

$$\left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A A ^ { \prime } x = \lambda x .$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

Sec. 12 ] Necessary and sufficient conditions for $r \left( A : B \right) = r \left( A \right) + r \left( B \right)$ 65

Since

$$\left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A = A ,$$
(2)

we have

$$A A ^ { \prime } x = \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A A ^ { \prime } x$$
$$= \lambda \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) x ,$$

(3)

and hence

$$\left( 1 - \lambda \right) A A ^ { \prime } x = \lambda B B ^ { \prime } x .$$
(4)

Now, since $\mathcal{M} \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) \cap \mathcal{M} \left( B B ^ { \prime } \right) = \left\{ 0 \right\} ,$ (4) implies

$$\left( 1 - \lambda \right) A A ^ { \prime } x = 0 .$$
(5)

Thus, $A A ^ { \prime } x = 0$ implies $\lambda = 0$ by (1) and $A A ^ { \prime } x \neq 0$ implies $\lambda = 1$ by (5).
Hence $\lambda = 0$ or $\lambda = 1 .$

(iii) => (vii): If (iii) holds, then

$$A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A = A ^ { \prime } \left( A A + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A$$
$$= A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A$$
$$- A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } B B ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A$$

(6)
$$= A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A - A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } B B ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A .$$

Hence

$$A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } B B ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A = 0 ,$$
(7)

which implies (vii).

(v) => (vii): This is proved similarly.

(vii) => (iv): If (vii) holds, then, using (2),

$$A = \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A = A A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } A .$$
(8)

Pre-multiplication with $A ^ { + }$ gives (iv).

$\left( \mathrm { v i i } \right) \Longrightarrow \left( \mathrm { v i } \right) :$ This is proved similarly.

(iv) => (iii) and $\left( \mathrm { v i } \right) \Longrightarrow \left( \mathrm { v } \right) :$ Trivial.

(vii) => (ii): We already know that (vii) implies (iv) and (vi). Hence

$$\left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { \prime } \\ B ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( A : B \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { + } A & 0 \\ 0 & B ^ { + } B \end{array} \right) .$$
(9)

The rank of the matrix on the left side of $\left( 9 \right)$ is $r \left( A : B \right) ;$ the rank of the
matrix on the right hand side is $r \left( A ^ { + } A \right) + r \left( B ^ { + } B \right) .$ It follows that

$$r \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( A : B \right) = r \left( A ^ { + } A \right) + r \left( B ^ { + } B \right) = r \left( A \right) + r \left( B \right) .$$
(10)

This completes the proof.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="66" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->


## 13 THE BORDERED GRAMIAN MATRIX

Let $A$ be a positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix and $B$ an $n \times k$ matrix. The
symmetric $\left( n + k \right) \times \left( n + k \right)$ matrix

$$Z = \left( \begin{array}{} A & B \\ B ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} \right) , .$$
(1)

called a bordered Gramian matrix, is of great interest in optimization theory.
We first prove Theorem 20.


### Theorem 20

Let $N = A + B B ^ { \prime }$ and $C = B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } B .$ Then

(i) $\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( N \right) ,$ $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( N \right) ,$ $\mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( C \right) ,$

(ii) $N N ^ { + } A = A ,$ $N N ^ { + } B = B ,$

(iii) $C ^ { + } C = B ^ { + } B ,$ $r \left( C \right) = r \left( B \right) .$

$P r o o f .$ Let $A = T T ^ { \prime }$ and recall from (1.7.9) that $\mathcal{M} \left( Q \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( Q Q ^ { \prime } \right)$ for any $Q .$
Then

$$\mathcal{M} \left( A \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( T \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( T : B \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( T T ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( N \right) ,$$
(2)

and similarly $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( N \right) .$ Hence $N N ^ { + } A = A$ and $N N ^ { + } B = B .$ Next,
let $N ^ { + } = F F ^ { \prime }$ and define $G = B ^ { \prime } F .$ Then $C = G G ^ { \prime } .$ Using (ii) and the fact
that $G ^ { \prime } \left( G G ^ { \prime } \right) \left( G G ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } = G ^ { \prime }$ for any $G ,$ we obtain

$$B \left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) = N N ^ { + } B \left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) = N F G ^ { \prime } \left( I - G G ^ { \prime } \left( G G ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \right) = 0 ,$$
(3)

and hence $\mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( C \right) .$ Since obviously $\mathcal{M} \left( C \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ we find that
$\mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( C \right) .$

Finally, to prove (iii), we note that $\mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( C \right)$ implies that the ranks
of $B ^ { \prime }$ and $C$ must be equal and hence that $r \left( B \right) = r \left( C \right) .$ We also have

$$\left( B ^ { \prime } B ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \right) C = \left( B ^ { \prime } B ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \right) \left( B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } B \right) = B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } B = C .$$
(4)

As $B ^ { \prime } B ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$ is symmetric idempotent and $r \left( B ^ { \prime } B ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \right) = r \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( C \right) ,$ it follows
(by Theorem 2.8) that $B ^ { \prime } B ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } = C C ^ { + }$ and hence that $B ^ { + } B = C ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } = C ^ { + } C$
(Exercise 2.7.7).
✷
☐

Next we obtain the Moore-Penrose inverse of $Z .$


### Theorem 21

The Moore-Penrose inverse of $Z$ is

$$Z ^ { + } = \left( \begin{array}{} D & E \\ E ^ { \prime } & - F \end{array} \right)$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="67" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] The bordered Gramian matrix" -->

where

$$D = N ^ { + } - N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } ,$$
$$E = N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } ,$$
$$F = C ^ { + } - C C ^ { + } ,$$
(6)
(7)
(8)

and

$$N = A + B B ^ { \prime } , \quad C = B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } B . ,$$
(9)

Moreover,

$$Z Z ^ { + } = Z ^ { + } Z = \left( \begin{array}{} N N ^ { + } & 0 \\ 0 & C C ^ { + } \end{array} \right) .$$
(10)

Proof. Let $G$ be defined by

$$G = \left( \begin{array}{} N ^ { + } - N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } & N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } \\ C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } & - C ^ { + } + C C ^ { + } \end{array} \right) .$$
(11)

Then $Z G$ is equal to

$$\left( A N ^ { + } - A N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } + B C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } \quad A N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } - B C ^ { + } + B C C ^ { + } \right)$$
)
B'N+-B'N+BC+B'N+
B'N+BC+
(12)

which in turn is equal to the block-diagonal matrix in (10). We obtain this
by replacing $A$ by $N - B B ^ { \prime } ,$ and using the definition of $C$ and the results
$N N ^ { + } B = B$ and $C C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime }$ (see Theorem 20). Since $Z$ and $G$ are both
symmetric and $Z G$ is also symmetric, it follows that $Z G = G Z$ and so $G Z$
is also symmetric. To show that $Z G Z = Z$ and $G Z G = G$ is straightforward.
This concludes the proof.
✷
☐

In the special case where $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) ,$ the results can be simplified.
This case is worth stating as a separate theorem.


### Theorem 22

In the special case where $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) ,$ we have

$$A A ^ { + } B = B , \quad \Gamma \Gamma ^ { + } = B ^ { + } B ,$$
(13)

where $\Gamma = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } B .$ Furthermore,

$$Z ^ { + } = \left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { + } - A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } & A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } \\ \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } & - \Gamma ^ { + } \end{array} \right)$$
(14)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="68" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

and

$$Z Z ^ { + } = Z ^ { + } Z = \left( \begin{array}{} A A ^ { + } & 0 \\ 0 & B ^ { + } B \end{array} \right) .$$
(15)

Proof. We could prove the theorem as a special case of the previous results.
Below, however, we present a simple direct proof. The first statement of (13)
follows from $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) .$ To prove the second statement of (13) we write
$A = T T ^ { \prime }$ with $| T ^ { \prime } T | \neq 0$ and $B = T S ,$ so that

$$\Gamma = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } B = S ^ { \prime } T ^ { \prime } \left( T T ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } T S = S ^ { \prime } S .$$
(16)

Then, using Theorem 2.7,

$$B ^ { + } B = \left( T S \right) ^ { + } \left( T S \right) = S ^ { + } S = \left( S ^ { \prime } S \right) ^ { + } S ^ { \prime } S = \Gamma ^ { + } \Gamma = \Gamma \Gamma ^ { + } .$$
(17)

As a consequence we also have $\Gamma \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime } .$ Now, let $G$ be defined by

$$G = \left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { + } - A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } & A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } \\ \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } & - \Gamma ^ { + } \end{array} \right) .$$
(18)

Then,

$$Z G = \left( \begin{array}{} A A ^ { + } - A A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } + B \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } & A A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } - B \Gamma ^ { + } \\ B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } - B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } & B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } B \Gamma ^ { + } \end{array} \right)$$

$$= \left( \begin{array}{} A A ^ { + } & 0 \\ 0 & \Gamma \Gamma ^ { + } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} A A ^ { + } & 0 \\ 0 & B ^ { + } B \end{array} \right) , .$$
(19)

using the facts $A A ^ { + } B = B , \Gamma \Gamma ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime }$ and $\Gamma \Gamma ^ { + } = B ^ { + } B .$ To show that
$G = Z ^ { + }$ is then straightforward.
✷
☐


## 14 THE EQUATIONS $X _ { 1 } A + X _ { 2 } B ^ { \prime } = G _ { 1 } , X _ { 1 } B = G _ { 2 }$

The two matrix equations in $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 } ,$

$$X _ { 1 } A + X _ { 2 } B ^ { \prime } = G _ { 1 } ,$$
$$X _ { 1 } B = G _ { 2 } ,$$
(1)
(2)

where $A$ is positive semidefinite, can be written equivalently as

$$\left( \begin{array}{} A & B \\ B ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ X _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

The properties of the matrix $Z$ studied in the previous section enable us to
solve these equations.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="69" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] The equations $X _ { 1 } A + X _ { 2 } B ^ { \prime } = G _ { 1 } , X _ { 1 } B = G _ { 2 }$" -->


## Theorem 23

The matrix equation in $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 } ,$

$$\left( \begin{array}{} A & B \\ B ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ X _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) , .$$
(4)

where $A ,$ $B ,$ $G _ { 1 }$ and $G _ { 2 }$ are given matrices (of appropriate orders) and $A$ is
positive semidefinite, has a solution if and only if

$$\mathcal{M} \left( G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A : B \right) \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathcal{M} \left( G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(5)

in which case the general solution is

$$X _ { 1 } = G _ { 1 } \left( N ^ { + } - N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } \right) + G _ { 2 } C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } + Q _ { 1 } \left( I - N N ^ { + } \right)$$
(6)

and

$$X _ { 2 } = G _ { 1 } N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } + G _ { 2 } \left( I - C ^ { + } \right) + Q _ { 2 } \left( I - B ^ { + } B \right) ,$$
(7)

where

$$N = A + B B ^ { \prime } , \quad C = B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } B ,$$
(8)

and $Q _ { 1 }$ and $Q _ { 2 }$ are arbitrary matrices of appropriate orders.

Moreover, if $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) ,$ then we may take $N = A .$

Proof. Let $X = \left( X _ { 1 } : X _ { 2 } \right) ,$ $G = \left( G _ { 1 } : G _ { 2 } \right)$ and

$$Z = \left( \begin{array}{} A & B \\ B ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$
(9)

Then Equation (4) can be written as

$$Z X ^ { \prime } = G ^ { \prime } .$$
(10)
.

A solution of (10) exists if and only if

$$Z Z ^ { + } G ^ { \prime } = G ^ { \prime } . ,$$
(11)

and if a solution exists it takes the form

$$X ^ { \prime } = Z ^ { + } G ^ { \prime } + \left( I - Z ^ { + } Z \right) Q ^ { \prime }$$
(12)

where $Q$ is an arbitrary matrix of appropriate order (Theorem 2.13).

Now, (11) is equivalent, by Theorem 21, to the two equations

$$N N ^ { + } G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \quad C C ^ { + } G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } .$$
(13)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="70" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous matrix results [Ch. 3" -->

The two equations in (13) in their turn are equivalent to

$$\mathcal{M} \left( G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( N \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( A : B \right)$$
(14)

and

$$\mathcal{M} \left( G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( C \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$
(15)

using Theorems 2.11 and 20. This proves (5).

Using (12) and the expression for $Z ^ { + }$ in Theorem 21, we obtain the general
solutions

$$X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \left( N ^ { + } - N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } \right) G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + N ^ { + } B C ^ { + } G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \left( I - N N ^ { + } \right) Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$$
(16)

and

$$X _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \left( C C ^ { + } - C ^ { + } \right) G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) P ^ { \prime }$$
$$= C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \left( I - C ^ { + } \right) G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) \left( P ^ { \prime } - G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right)$$

$$= C ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } G _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \left( I - C ^ { + } \right) G _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \left( I - B ^ { + } B \right) Q _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(17)

using Theorem 20 (iii) and letting $Q = \left( Q _ { 1 } : P \right)$ and $Q _ { 2 } = P - G _ { 2 } .$

The special case where $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A \right)$ follows from Theorem 22.
✷
☐

An important special case of Theorem 23 arises when we take $G _ { 1 } = 0 .$


### Theorem 24

The matrix equation in $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 } ,$

$$\left( \begin{array}{} A & B \\ B ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ X _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 \\ G ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(18)

where $A ,$ $B$ and $G$ are given matrices (of appropriate orders) and $A$ is positive
semidefinite, has a solution if and only if

$$\mathcal{M} \left( G ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( B ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(19)

in which case the general solution for $X _ { 1 }$ is

$$X _ { 1 } = G \left( B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } B \right) ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } N ^ { + } + Q \left( I - N N ^ { + } \right)$$
(20)

where $N = A + B B ^ { \prime }$ and $Q$ is arbitrary (of appropriate order).

Moreover, if $\mathcal{M} \left( B \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) ,$ then the general solution can be written as

$$X _ { 1 } = G \left( B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } B \right) ^ { + } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } + Q \left( I - A A ^ { + } \right) .$$
(21)

Proof. This follows immediately from Theorem 23.
✷
☐


## Exercise

1\. Give the general solution for $X _ { 2 }$ in Theorem 24.

## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. $D _ { n } ^ { \prime } = D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } - \mathrm { d g } K _ { n } \right) = D _ { n } ^ { + } \left( 2 I _ { n ^ { 2 } } - \mathrm { d g } K _ { n } \right) .$

2\. $D _ { n } ^ { + } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { n } ^ { \prime } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + \mathrm { d g } K _ { n } \right) .$

3\. $D _ { n } D _ { n } ^ { \prime } = I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } - \deg K _ { n } .$

4\. Let $e _ { i }$ denote a unit vector of order m, that is, $e _ { i }$ has unity in its i-th
position and zeros elsewhere. Let $u _ { j }$ be a unit vector of order n. Define
the $m ^ { 2 } \times m$ and $n ^ { 2 } \times n$ matrices

$$W _ { m } = \left( \mathrm { v e c } e _ { 1 } e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \mathrm { v e c } e _ { m } e _ { m } ^ { \prime } \right) , \quad W _ { n } = \left( \mathrm { v e c } u _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \mathrm { v e c } u _ { n } u _ { n } ^ { \prime } \right) .$$

Let $A$ and $B$ be $m \times n$ matrices. Prove that

$$A \odot B = W _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( A \otimes B \right) W _ { n } .$$


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§2. A good discussion on adjoint matrices can be found in Aitken (1939,
Chapter 5). Theorem 1(b) appears to be new.

§6. For a review of the properties of the Hadamard product, see Styan (1973).
Browne (1974) was the first to present the relation between the Hadamard
and Kronecker products (square case). Faliva (1983) and Liu (1995) treated
the rectangular case. See also Neudecker, Liu and Polasek (1995) and Neu-
decker, Polasek and Liu (1995) for a survey and applications.

§7. The commutation matrix was systematically studied by Magnus and Neu-
decker (1979). See also Magnus and Neudecker (1986). Theorem 10 is due to
Neudecker and Wansbeek (1983). The matrix $N _ { n }$ was introduced by Browne
(1974). For a rigorous and extensive treatment see Magnus (1988).

§8. See Browne (1974) and Magnus and Neudecker (1980, 1986) for further
properties of the duplication matrix. Theorem 14 follows from equations (60),
(62) and (64) in Magnus and Neudecker (1986). A systematic treatment of
linear structures (of which symmetry is one example) is given in Magnus
(1988).

§9-§10. See Holly and Magnus (1988).

§11. See also Debreu (1952), Black and Morimoto (1968) and Farebrother
(1977).

§12. See also Chipman (1964).

§13. See Pringle and Rayner (1971, Chapter 3), Rao (1973, Section 4i.1) and
Magnus (1990).

Part Two -

$$D i f f e r e n t i a l s : t h e \quad t h e o r y$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 4 Mathematical preliminaries


### 1 INTRODUCTION

Chapters 4-7, which constitute Part Two of this monograph, consist of two
principal parts. The first part discusses differentials; the second part deals
with extremum problems.

The use of differentials in both applied and theoretical work is widespread,
but satisfactory treatment of differentials $\mathrm { i s }$ not so widespread in textbooks
on economics and mathematics for economists. Indeed, some authors still
claim that $\mathrm { d } x$ and dy stand for 'infinitesimally small changes in $x$ and $y ^ { \prime } .$ The
purpose of Chapters 5 and 6 is therefore to provide a systematic theoretical
discussion of differentials.

We begin, however, by reviewing some basic concepts which will be used
throughout.


### 2 INTERIOR POINTS AND ACCUMULATION POINTS

$\mathrm { L e t } c$ be a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ and $r$ a positive number. The set of all points $x$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$
whose distance from c is less than $r$ is called an n-ball of radius $r$ and centre
c, and is denoted by $B \left( c \right)$ or $B \left( c ; r \right) .$ Thus,

$$B \left( c ; r \right) = \left\{ x : x \in \mathrm { R } ^ { n } , \| x - c \| < r \right\} .$$
(1)

An n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ is sometimes called a neighbourhood of c, denoted $N \left( c \right) .$ The
two words are used interchangeably.

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and assume that $c \in S$ and $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ not necessarily
in $S .$ Then

(a) if there is an n-ball $B \left( c \right) ,$ all of whose points belong to $S ,$ then $c$ is called
an interior point of $S ;$

(b) if every n-ball $B \left( x \right)$ contains at least one point of $S$ distinct from x,
then $x$ is called an accumulation point of $S ;$

<!-- PageNumber="75" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="76" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Mathematical preliminaries [Ch. 4" -->

(c) if $c \in S$ is not an accumulation point of $S ,$ then $c$ is called an isolated
point of $S ;$

(d) if every n-ball $B \left( x \right)$ contains at least one point of $S$ and at least one
point of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } - S ,$ then $x$ is called a boundary point of $S .$

We further define:

(e) the interior of $S ,$ denoted $S ,$ as the set of all interior points of $S ;$

(f) the derived set $S ,$ denoted $S ^ { \prime } ,$ as the set of all accumulation points of $S ;$

(g) the closure of $S ,$ denoted $\bar { S } ,$ as $S \cup S ^ { \prime }$ (that is, to obtain $\bar { S } ,$ we adjoin
all accumulation points of $S$ to S);

(h) the boundary of $S ,$ denoted $\partial S ,$ as the set of all boundary points of $S .$


#### Theorem 1

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ is an accumulation point of $S ,$ then every
n-ball $B \left( x \right)$ contains infinitely many points of $S .$

Proof. Suppose there is an n-ball $B \left( x \right)$ which contains only a finite number of
points of $S$ distinct from x, say $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { p } .$ Let

$$r = \min _ { 1 < i < p } \| x - a _ { i } \| .$$
(2)

Then $r > 0 ,$ and the n-ball $B \left( x ; r \right)$ contains no point of $S$ distinct from x.
This contradiction completes the proof.
✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Show that $x$ is a boundary point of a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ if and only if $x$ is a
boundary point of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S .$

2\. Show that $x$ is a boundary point of a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ if and only if

(a) $x \in S$ and $x$ is an accumulation point of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S ,$ or

(b) $x \notin S$ and $x$ is an accumulation point of $S .$


### 3 OPEN AND CLOSED SETS

A set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ is said to be

(a) open, if all its points are interior points;

(b) closed, if it contains all its accumulation points;

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="77" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Open and closed $s e t s$" -->

(c) bounded, if there is a real number $r > 0$ and a point $c$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ such that
$S$ lies entirely within the n-ball $B \left( c ; r \right) ;$ and

(d) compact, if it is closed and bounded.

For example, let $A$ be an interval in $\mathrm { I R } ,$ that is, a set with the property that,
if $a \in A ,$ $b \in A$ and $a < b ,$ then $a < c < b$ implies $c \in A .$ For $a < b \in \mathbb{R}$ the
open intervals in $\mathrm { R }$ are

$$\left( a , b \right) , \quad \left( a , \infty \right) , \quad \left( - \infty , b \right) , \quad \mathrm { R } ; ,$$
(1)

the closed intervals are

$$\left[ a , b \right] , \quad \left[ a , \infty \right) , \quad \left( - \infty , b \right] , \quad \mathrm { I R } ; ,$$
(2)

the bounded intervals are

$$\left( a , b \right) ,$$
(3)

and the only type of compact interval is

$$\left[ a , b \right] .$$
(4)

This example shows that a set can be both open and closed. In fact, the only
sets in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ which are both open and closed are $\emptyset$ and $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ It is also possible
that a set is neither open nor closed as the 'half-open' interval $\left( a , b \right]$ shows.

It is clear that $S$ is open if and only if $S = S ,$ and that $S$ is closed if and
only if $S = \bar { S } .$ An important example of an open $s e t$ is the n-ball.


#### Theorem 2

Every n-ball is an open set in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$

Proof. Let $B \left( c ; r \right)$ be a given n-ball with radius $r$ and centre c, and let $x$ be
an arbitrary point of $B \left( c ; r \right) .$ We have to prove that $x$ is an interior point of
$B \left( c ; r \right) ,$ i.e. that there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that $B \left( x ; \delta \right) \subset B \left( c ; r \right) .$ Now, let

$$\delta = r - \| x - c \| .$$
(5)

Then $\delta > 0 ,$ and, for any $y \in B \left( x ; \delta \right) ,$

$$\| y - c \| \leq \| y - x \| + \| x - c \| < \delta + r - \delta = r ,$$
(6)

so that $y \in B \left( c ; r \right) .$ Thus $B \left( x ; \delta \right) \subset B \left( c ; r \right) ,$ and $x$ is an interior point of
$B \left( c ; r \right) .$
☐
✷

The next theorem characterizes a closed set as the complement of an open
set.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="78" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Mathematical preliminaries [Ch. 4" -->


#### Theorem 3

A set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ is closed if and only if its complement $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S$ is open.

Proof. Assume first that $S$ is closed. Let $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } - S .$ Then $x \notin S$ and, since $S$
contains all its accumulation points, $x$ is not an accumulation point of $S .$ Hence
there exists an n-ball $B \left( x \right)$ which does not intersect $S ,$ i.e. $B \left( x \right) \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } - S .$ It
follows that $x$ is an interior point of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S ,$ and hence that $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S$ is open.

To prove the converse, assume that $\mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S$ is open. Let $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ be an
accumulation point of $S .$ We must show that $x \in S .$ Assume that $x \notin S .$
Then $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } - S ,$ and since every point of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S$ is an interior point, there
exists an n-ball $B \left( x \right) \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } - S .$ Hence $B \left( x \right)$ contains no points of $S$ thereby
contradicting the fact that $x$ is an accumulation point of $S .$ It follows that
$x \in S ,$ and hence that $S$ is closed.
✷
☐

The next two theorems show how to construct further open and closed
sets from given ones.


#### Theorem 4

The union of any collection of open sets is open, and the intersection of a
finite collection of open sets is open.

Proof. Let $F$ be a collection of open sets and let $S$ denote their union,

$$S = \bigcup _ { A \in F } A .$$
(7)

Assume $x \in S .$ Then there is at least one set of $F ,$ say $A ,$ such that $x \in A .$
Since $A$ is open, $x$ is an interior point of $A ,$ and hence of $S .$ It follows that $S$
is open.

Next let $F$ be $\mathrm { a }$ finite collection of open sets, $F = \left\{ A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { k } \right\} ,$ and
let

$$T = \bigcap _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } A _ { j } .$$
(8)

Assume $x \in T .$ (If $T$ is empty, there is nothing to prove.) Then $x$ belongs to
every set in $F .$ Since each set in $F$ is open, there exist kn-balls $B \left( x ; r _ { j } \right) \subset$
$A _ { j } ,$ $j = 1 , \ldots , k .$ Let

$$r = \min _ { 1 \leq j \leq k } r _ { j } .$$
(9)

Then $x \in B \left( x ; r \right) \subset T .$ Hence $x$ is an interior point of $T .$ It follows that $T$ is
open.
☐
✷

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="79" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem" -->

Note. The intersection of an infinite collection of open sets need not be open.
For example,

$$\bigcap _ { n \in \mathrm { I } \mathrm { N } } \left( - \frac { 1 } { n } , \frac { 1 } { n } \right) = \left\{ 0 \right\} .$$
(10)


#### Theorem 5

The union of a finite collection of closed sets is closed, and the intersection of
any collection of closed sets is closed.

Proof. Let $F$ be a finite collection of closed sets, $F = \left\{ A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { k } \right\} ,$ and
let

$$S = \bigcup _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } A _ { j } .$$
(11)

Then,

$$\mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { n } - S = \bigcap _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \left( \mathrm { R } ^ { n } - A _ { j } \right) .$$
(12)

Since each $A _ { j }$ is closed, $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } - A _ { j }$ is open (Theorem 3), and by Theorem 4, so
is their (finite) intersection

$$\bigcap _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \left( \mathbb{R} ^ { n } - A _ { j } \right) .$$
(13)

Hence $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } - S$ is open, and $S$ is closed. The second statement is proved simi-
larly.
✷
☐

Finally, we present the following simple relation between open and closed
sets.


#### Theorem 6

If $A$ is open and $B$ is closed, then $A \quad - \quad B$ is open and $B \quad - \quad A$ is closed.

Proof. It is easy to see that $A - B = A \cap \left( \mathrm { R } ^ { n } - B \right) ,$ the intersection of two open
sets. Hence, by Theorem 4, $A - B$ is open. Similarly, since $B - A = B \cap \left( \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { n } - A \right) ,$
the intersection of two closed sets, it is closed by Theorem 5.
✷
☐


### 4 THE BOLZANO-WEIERSTRASS THEOREM

Theorem 1 implies that a set cannot have an accumulation point unless it
contains infinitely many points to begin with. The converse, however, is not

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="80" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Mathematical preliminaries [Ch. 4" -->

true. For example $\mathbb{N}$ is an infinite set without accumulation points. We shall
now show that infinite sets which are bounded always have an accumulation
point.


#### Theorem 7 (Bolzano-Weierstrass)

Every bounded infinite subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ has an accumulation point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$

Proof. Let us prove the theorem for $n =$ 1. The case $n > 1$ is proved similarly.
Since $S$ is bounded, it lies in some interval $\left[ - a , a \right] .$ Since $S$ contains infinitely
many points, either $\left[ - a , 0 \right]$ or $\left[ 0 , a \right]$ (or both) contain infinitely many points
of $S .$ Call this interval $\left[ a _ { 1 } , b _ { 1 } \right] .$ Bisect $\left[ a _ { 1 } , b _ { 1 } \right]$ and obtain an interval $\left[ a _ { 2 } , b _ { 2 } \right]$
containing infinitely many points of $S .$ Continuing this process we find a
countable sequence of intervals $\left[ a _ { n } , b _ { n } \right] ,$ $n = 1 , 2 , \ldots .$ The intersection

$$\bigcap _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \left[ a _ { n } , b _ { n } \right]$$

of these intervals is a set consisting of only one point, say $c$ (which may or
may not belong to $\left. S \right) .$ We shall show that $c$ is an accumulation point of $S .$
Let $\epsilon > 0 ,$ and consider the neighbourhood $\left( c - \epsilon , c + \epsilon \right)$ of c. Then we can
find an $n _ { 0 } = n _ { 0 } \left( \epsilon \right)$ such that $\left[ a _ { n _ { 0 } } , b _ { n _ { 0 } } \right] \subset \left( c - \epsilon , c + \epsilon \right) .$ Since $\left[ a _ { n _ { 0 } } , b _ { n _ { 0 } } \right]$ contains
infinitely many points of $S ,$ so does $\left( c - \epsilon , c + \epsilon \right) .$ Hence $c$ is an accumulation

☐
point of $S .$
✷


### 5 FUNCTIONS

Let $S$ and $T$ be two sets. If with each element $x \in S$ there is associated exactly
one element $y \in T ,$ denoted $f \left( x \right) ,$ then $f$ is said to be a function from $S$ to
$T .$ We write

$$f : S \rightarrow T ,$$
(1)

and $s a y \quad t h a t \quad f$ is defined on $S$ with values in $T .$ The set $S$ is called the domain
of $f .$ The set of all values of $f ,$

$$\left\{ y : y = f \left( x \right) , x \in S \right\} ,$$
(2)

is called the range of $f ,$ and is a subset of $T .$

$A$ function $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined on a set $S$ with values in $\mathrm { R }$ is called real-
valued. $A$ function $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } \left( m > 1 \right)$ whose values are points in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is
called a vector function.

A real-valued function $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} , S \subset \mathbb{R} ,$ is said to be increasing on $S$ if
for every pair of points $x$ and $y$ in $S ,$

$$\phi \left( x \right) \leq \phi \left( y \right) \quad \mathrm { w h e n e v e r } x < y .$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="81" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] The limit of a function" -->

We say that $\phi$ is strictly increasing on $S$ if

$$\phi \left( x \right) < \phi \left( y \right) \quad \mathrm { w h e n e v e r } x < y .$$
(4)

(Strictly) decreasing functions are similarly defined. $A$ function is (strictly)
monotonic on $S$ if it is either (strictly) increasing or (strictly) decreasing on
$S .$

$A$ vector function $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ $S \subset \mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ is said to be bounded if there is a
real number $M$ such that

$$\| f \left( x \right) \| \leq M \quad \text { for all } x \mathrm { i n } S .$$
(5)

A function $f : \mathbb{R} ^ { n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is said to be affine if there exist an $m \times n$ matrix
$A$ and an $m \times 1$ vector $b$ such that $f \left( x \right) = A x + b$ for every $x$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ If $b = 0 ,$
the function $f$ is said to be linear.


### 6 THE LIMIT OF A FUNCTION


#### Definition 1

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ with values in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m } .$ Let $c$ be
an accumulation point of $S .$ Suppose there exists a point $b$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ with the
property that for every $\epsilon > 0$ there is a $\delta > 0$ such that

$$\| f \left( x \right) - b \| < \epsilon$$
(1)

for all points x in $S ,$ $x \neq c ,$ for which

$$\| x - c \| < \delta .$$
(2)

Then we say that the limit of $f \left( x \right)$ is $b ,$ as $x$ tends to c, and we write

$$\lim _ { x \rightarrow c } f \left( x \right) = b .$$
(3)

Note. The requirement that $c$ is an accumulation point of $S$ guarantees that
there will be points $x \neq c$ in $S$ sufficiently close to c. However, $c$ need not be
a point of $S .$ Moreover, even if $c \in S ,$ we may have

$$f \left( c \right) \neq \lim _ { x \rightarrow c } f \left( x \right) .$$

We have the following rules for calculating with limits of vector functions.


### Theorem 8

Let $f$ and $g$ be two vector functions defined on $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ with values in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$
Let $c$ be an accumulation point of $S ,$ and assume that

$$\lim _ { x \rightarrow c } f \left( x \right) = a , \quad \lim _ { x \rightarrow c } g \left( x \right) = b ,$$
(4)

Then,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="82" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Mathematical preliminaries [Ch. 4" -->

(a) $\lim _ { x \rightarrow c } \left( f + g \right) \left( x \right) = a + b ,$

(b) $\lim _ { x \rightarrow c } \left( \lambda f \right) \left( x \right) = \lambda a$ for every scalar $\lambda ,$

(c) $\lim _ { x \rightarrow c } f \left( x \right) ^ { \prime } g \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } b ,$

(d) $\lim _ { x \rightarrow c } \| f \left( x \right) \| = \| a \| .$

Proof. The proof is left to the reader.
✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Let $\phi : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be defined by $\phi \left( x \right) = x$ if $x \neq 0$ $\phi \left( 0 \right) = 1 .$ Show that
$\phi \left( x \right) \rightarrow 0$ $x \rightarrow 0 .$

2\. Let $\phi : \mathrm { R } - \left\{ 0 \right\} \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be defined by $\phi \left( x \right) = x \sin \left( 1 / x \right)$ if $x \neq 0 .$ Show
that $\phi \left( x \right) \rightarrow 0$ as $x \rightarrow 0 .$


### 7 CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS AND COMPACTNESS

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Let $c$ be a
point of $S .$ Then we say that $\phi$ is continuous at $c$ if for every $\epsilon > 0$ there is a
$\delta > 0$ such that

$$| \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) | < \epsilon$$
(1)

for all points of $c + u$ in $S$ for which $\| u \| < \delta .$ If $\phi$ is continuous at every point
of $S ,$ we say that $\phi$ is continuous on $S .$

Continuity is discussed in more detail in Section 5.2. Here we only prove
the following important theorem.


#### Theorem 9

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a compact set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ If
$\phi$ is continuous on $S ,$ then $\phi$ is bounded on $S .$

Proof. Suppose that $\phi$ is not bounded on $S .$ Then there exists, for every $k \in \mathbb{N} ,$
an $x _ { k } \in S$ such that $| \phi \left( x _ { k } \right) | \geq k .$ The set

$$A = \left\{ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots \right\}$$
(2)

contains infinitely many points, and $A \subset S .$ Since $S$ is a bounded set, so
is $A .$ Hence, by the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem (Theorem 7), $A$ has an
accumulation point, say $x _ { 0 } .$ Then $x _ { 0 }$ is also an accumulation point of $S$ and
hence $x _ { 0 } \in S ,$ since $S$ is closed.

Now choose an integer $p$ such that

$$p > 1 + | \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) | ,$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="83" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] Convex sets" -->

and define the set $A _ { p } \subset A$ by

$$A _ { p } = \left\{ x _ { p } , x _ { p + 1 } , \ldots \right\} ,$$
(4)

so that

$$| \phi \left( x \right) | \geq p \quad \text { for all } x \in A _ { p } .$$
(5)

Since $\phi$ is continuous at $x _ { 0 } ,$ there exists an n-ball $B \left( x _ { 0 } \right)$ such that

$$| \phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) | < 1 \quad \text { for all } x \in S \cap B \left( x _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(6)

In particular,

$$| \phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) | < 1 \quad \text { for all } x \in A _ { p } \cap B \left( x _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(7)

The set $A _ { p } \cap B \left( x _ { 0 } \right)$ is not empty. (In fact, it contains infinitely many points
because $A \cap B \left( x _ { 0 } \right)$ contains infinitely many points, see Theorem 1.) For any
$x \in A _ { p } \cap B \left( x _ { 0 } \right)$ we have

$$| \phi \left( x \right) | < 1 + | \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) | < p ,$$
(8)

using (7) and (3), and also, from (5),

$$| \phi \left( x \right) | \geq p .$$
(9)

This contradiction shows that $\phi$ must be bounded on $S .$
✷
☐


### 8 CONVEX SETS


#### Definition 2

A subset $S$ of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ is called a convex set if, for every pair of points $x$ and $y$ in
$S$ and every real $\theta$ satisfying $0 < \theta < 1 ,$ we have

$$\theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \in S .$$
(1)

In other words, $S \quad i s \quad c o n v e x \quad i f$ the line segment joining any two points of $S$
lies entirely inside $S$ (see Figure 1).

Convex sets need not be closed, open, or compact. A single point and the
whole space $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ are trivial examples of convex sets. Another example of a
convex set is the n-ball.


### Theorem 10

Every n-ball in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ is convex.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="84" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Mathematical preliminaries [Ch. 4" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 1 Convex and non-convex sets in $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 }$</figcaption>

$b$

b

a

a

convex

non-convex

</figure>


Proof. Let $B \left( c ; r \right)$ be an n-ball with radius $r > 0$ and centre c. Let $x$ and $y$ be
points in $B \left( c ; r \right)$ and let $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Then

$$\| \theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y - c \| = \| \theta \left( x - c \right) + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \left( y - c \right) \|$$
$$\leq \theta \| x - c \| + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \| y - c \| < \theta r + \left( 1 - \theta \right) r = r .$$

Hence the point $\theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y$ lies in $B \left( c ; r \right) .$
✷
☐

(2)

Another important property of convex sets is the following.


#### Theorem 11

The intersection of any collection of convex sets is convex.

Proof. Let $F$ be a collection of convex sets and let $S$ denote their intersection,

$$S = \bigcap _ { A \in F } A .$$

Assume $x$ and $y \in S .$ (If $S$ is empty, or consists of only one point, there is
nothing to prove.) Then $x$ and $y$ belong to every set in $F .$ Since each set in
$F$ is convex, the point $\theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y ,$ $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ also belongs to every set in $F ,$
and hence to $S .$ It follows that $S$ is convex.
✷
☐

Note. The union of convex sets is usually not convex.


#### Definition 3

Let $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { k }$ be $k$ points in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ A point $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ is called a convex com-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="85" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Convex and concave functions" -->

bination of these points if there exist $k$ real numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { k }$ such that

$$x = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } x _ { i } , \quad \lambda _ { i } \geq 0 \left( i = 1 , \ldots , k \right) , \quad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } = 1 . ,$$
(3)


### Theorem 12

Let $S$ be a convex set in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Then every convex combination of a finite number
of points in $S$ lies in $S .$

Proof (by induction). The theorem is clearly true for each pair of points in
$S .$ Suppose it is true for all collections of $k$ points in $S .$ Let $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k + 1 }$ be
$k + 1$ arbitrary points in $S ,$ and let $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { k + 1 }$ be arbitrary real numbers
satisfying $\lambda _ { i } \geq 0 \left( i = 1 , \ldots , k + 1 \right)$ and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k + 1 } \lambda _ { i } = 1 .$ Define $x = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k + 1 } \lambda _ { i } x _ { i }$
and assume that $\lambda _ { k + 1 } \neq 1 .$ (If $\lambda _ { k + 1 } = 1 ,$ then $x = x _ { k + 1 } \in S$ Then we can
write $x$ as

$$x = \lambda _ { 0 } y + \lambda _ { k + 1 } x _ { k + 1 }$$
(4)

with

$$\lambda _ { 0 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } , \quad y = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \left( \lambda _ { i } / \lambda _ { 0 } \right) x _ { i } .$$
(5)

By the induction hypothesis, $y$ lies in $S .$ Hence, by the definition of a convex
set, $x \in S .$
✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Consider a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ with the property that, for any pair of points x
and $y$ in $S ,$ their midpoint $\frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( x + y \right)$ also belongs to $S .$ Show, by means
of a counter-example, that $\stackrel { _ { _ { * } } } { S }$ need not be convex.

2\. Show, by means of a counter-example, that the union of two convex sets
need not be convex.

3\. Let $S$ be a convex set in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Show that $\bar { S }$ and $S$ are convex.


### 9 CONVEX AND CONCAVE FUNCTIONS

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$
Then

(a) $\phi$ is said to be convex on $S ,$ if

$$\phi \left( \theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \right) \leq \theta \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \phi \left( y \right)$$
(1)

for every pair of points x, $y$ in $S$ and every $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ (see Figure 2);

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="86" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Mathematical preliminaries [Ch. 4" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 2 A convex function</figcaption>

$\Phi$

$X$

y

</figure>


(b) $\phi$ is said to be strictly convex on $S ,$ if

$$\phi \left( \theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \right) < \theta \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \phi \left( y \right)$$
(2)

for every pair of points x, $y$ in $S ,$ $x \neq y ,$ and every $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$

(c) $\phi$ is said to be (strictly) concave if $\psi \equiv - \phi$ is (strictly) convex.

Note. It is essential in the definition that $S$ is a convex set, since we require
that $\theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \in S$ if x, $y \in S .$

It is clear that a strictly convex (concave) function is convex (concave). Ex-
amples of strictly convex functions in one dimension are $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \acute { 2 } }$ and
$\phi \left( x \right) = e ^ { x } \left( x > 0 \right)$ the function $\phi \left( x \right) = \log x \left( x > 0 \right)$ is strictly concave.
These functions are continuous (and even differentiable) on their respective
domains. That these properties are not necessary is shown by the functions

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 2 } , & \text { if } x > 0 \\ 1 , & \text { if } x = 0 \end{array} \right.$$
(3)

(strictly convex on $\left[ 0 , \infty \right)$ but discontinuous at the boundary point $\left. x = 0 \right)$
and

$$\phi \left( x \right) = | x |$$
(4)

(convex on $\mathbb{R}$ but not differentiable at the interior point $x =$ 0). Thus, a
convex function may have a discontinuity at a boundary point and may not
be differentiable at an interior point. However, every convex (and concave)
function is continuous on its interior.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="87" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Convex and concave functions" -->

The following three theorems give further properties of convex functions.


#### Theorem 13

An affine function is convex as well as concave, but not strictly so.

$P r o o f .$ Since $\phi$ is an affine function, we have

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \alpha + a ^ { \prime } x$$
(5)

for some scalar $\alpha$ and vector a. Hence

$$\phi \left( \theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \right) = \theta \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \phi \left( y \right)$$
(6)

for every $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$
✷
☐


#### Theorem 14

Let $\phi$ and $\psi$ be two convex functions on a convex set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ Then

$$\alpha \phi + \beta \psi$$
(7)

is convex (concave) on $S ,$ if $\alpha \geq 0 \left( \leq 0 \right)$ and $\beta \geq 0 \left( \leq 0 \right) .$

Moreover, if $\phi$ is convex and $\psi$ strictly convex on $S ,$ then $\alpha \phi + \beta \psi$ is strictly
convex (concave) on $S$ if $\alpha \geq 0 \left( \leq 0 \right)$ and $\beta > 0 \left( < 0 \right) .$

Proof. The proof is a direct consequence of the definition and is left to the
reader.
☐
✷


#### Theorem 15

Every increasing convex (concave) function of a convex $\left( \mathrm { c o n c a v e } \right)$ function
is convex (concave). Every strictly increasing convex (concave) function of a
strictly convex (concave) function is strictly convex (concave).

Proof. Let $\phi$ be a convex function defined on a convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ let $\psi$ be
an increasing convex function of one variable defined on the range of $\phi$ and
let $\eta \left( x \right) = \psi \left[ \phi \left( x \right) \right] .$ Then

$$\eta \left( \theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \right) = \psi \left[ \phi \left( \theta x + \left( 1 - \theta \right) y \right) \right] \leq \psi \left[ \theta \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \phi \left( y \right) \right]$$

$$\leq \theta \psi \left[ \phi \left( x \right) \right] + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \psi \left[ \phi \left( y \right) \right] = \theta \eta \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - \theta \right) \eta \left( y \right) ,$$
(8)

for every x, $y \in S$ and $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ (The first inequality follows from the con-
vexity of $\phi$ and the fact that $\psi$ is increasing; the second inequality follows
from the convexity of $\psi$ Hence $\eta$ is convex. The other statements are proved
similarly.
✷
☐

convex on $\left( 0 , \infty \right) .$ (Compare Exercise 7.8.1.)

2\. Show that the quadratic form $x ^ { \prime } A x$ $\left( A = A ^ { \prime } \right)$ is convex if and only
if $A$ is positive semidefinite, and concave if and only if $A$ is negative

is convex.

4\. An increasing function of a convex function is not necessarily convex.
Give an example.

5\. Prove the following statements by providing an example.

(a) A strictly increasing, convex function of a convex function is con-
vex, but not necessarily strictly so.

(b) An increasing convex function of a strictly convex function is con-

(c) An increasing, strictly convex function of a convex function is con-
vex, but not necessarily strictly so.

6\. Show that $\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } X$ is both convex and concave on $\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times n } .$
☒

$7 .$ If $\phi$ is convex on $S \subset \mathbb{R} ,$ $x _ { i } \in S \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$ $\alpha _ { i } \geq 0 \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$ and
$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } = 1 ,$ then

$$\phi \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } \right) \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) .$$


### BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. For a list of frequently occurring errors in the economic literature con-
cerning problems of maxima and minima, see Sydsæter (1974). A careful
development of mathematical analysis at the intermediate level is given in
Rudin (1964) and Apostol (1974). More advanced, but highly recommended,
is Dieudonné (1969).

§9. The fact that convex and concave functions are continuous on their inte-
rior is discussed, for example, in Luenberger (1969, Section 7.9) and Fleming
(1977, Theorem 3.5).

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 5 Differentials and differentiability


## 1 INTRODUCTION

Let us consider a function $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ with values
in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$ If $m =$ 1, the function is called real-valued (and we shall use $\phi$ instead
of $f$ to emphasize this); if $m \geq 2 ,$ $f$ is called a vector function. Examples of
vector functions are

$$f \left( x \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} x ^ { 2 } \\ x ^ { 3 } \end{array} \right) , \quad f \left( x , y \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} x y \\ x \\ y \end{array} \right) , \quad f \left( x , y , z \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} x + y + z \\ x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } \end{array} \right)$$
(1)

Note that $m$ may be larger or smaller than $n$ or equal to n. In the first example
$n =$ $1 , m = 2 ,$ in the second example $n = 2 ,$ $m = 3 ,$ and in the third example
$n = 3 ,$ $m = 2 .$

In this chapter, we extend the one-dimensional theory of differential cal-
culus (concerning real-valued functions $\phi : \mathrm { R } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ to functions from $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ to
$\mathrm { R } ^ { m } .$ The extension from real-valued functions of one variable to real-valued
functions of several variables is far more significant than the extension from
real-valued functions to vector functions. Indeed, for most purposes $a$ vector
function can be viewed as a vector of $m$ real-valued functions. Yet, as we
shall see shortly, there are good reasons to study vector functions rather than
merely real-valued functions.

Throughout this chapter, and indeed, throughout this book, we shall em-
phasize the fundamental idea of a differential rather than that of a derivative.


## 2 CONTINUITY

We first review the concept of continuity. Intuitively a function $f$ is continuous
at a point $c$ if $f \left( x \right)$ can be made arbitrarily close to $f \left( c \right)$ by taking $x$ sufficiently

<!-- PageNumber="89" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="90" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

close to c; in other words, if points close to $c$ are mapped by $f$ into points
close to $f \left( c \right) .$


### Definition 1

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ with values in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m } .$
Let $c$ be a point of $S .$ Then we say that $f$ is continuous at $c$ if for every $\epsilon > 0$
there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that

$$\| f \left( c + u \right) - f \left( c \right) \| < \epsilon$$
(1)

for all points $c + u$ in $S$ for which $\| u \| < \delta .$ If $f$ is continuous at every point
of $S ,$ we say $f$ is continuous on $S .$

Definition 1 is a straightforward generalization of the definition in Section
4.7 concerning continuity of real-valued functions $\left( m = 1 \right) .$ Note that $f$ has to
be defined at the point $c$ in order to be continuous at c. Some authors require
that $c$ is an accumulation point of $S ,$ but this is not assumed here. $I f \quad c$ is an
isolated point of $S$ (a point of $S$ which is not an accumulation point of $\left. S \right) ,$
then every $f$ defined at $c$ will be continuous at $c$ because for sufficiently small
$\delta$ there is only one point $c + u$ in $S$ satisfying $\| u \| < \delta ,$ namely the point $c$
itself; then

$$\| f \left( c + u \right) - f \left( c \right) \| = 0 < \epsilon .$$
(2)

If $c$ is an accumulation point of $S ,$ the definition of continuity implies that

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) .$$
(3)

Geometrical intuition suggests that if $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ is continuous at c,
it must also be continuous near c. This intuition is wrong for two reasons.
First, the point c may be an isolated point of $S ,$ in which case there exists
a neighbourhood of c where $f$ is not even defined. Secondly, even if $c$ is an
accumulation point of $S ,$ it may be that every neighbourhood of $c$ contains
points of $S$ at which $f$ is not continuous. For example, the real-valued function
$\phi : \mathrm { R } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ defined by

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x & \left( x \text { rational\right), } \right. \\ 0 & \left( x \text { irrational\right) } , \right. \end{array} \right. ,$$
(4)

is continuous at $x = 0 ,$ but at no other point.

If $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m } ,$ the formula

$$f \left( x \right) = \left( f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) , \ldots , f _ { m } \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime }$$
(5)

defines $m$ real-valued functions $f _ { i } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$ These functions
are called the component functions of $f$ and we write

$$f = \left( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { m } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="91" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Differentiability and linear approximation" -->


### Theorem 1

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ A function $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is continuous at a point $c$
in $S$ if and only $\mathrm { i f }$ each of its component functions is continuous at c.

If $c$ is an accumulation point of a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ and $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is continuous
at c, then we can write (3) as

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + R _ { c } \left( u \right) ,$$
(7)

where

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } R _ { c } \left( u \right) = 0 .$$
(8)

We may call Equation (7) the Taylor formula $o f$ order zero. It says that
continuity at an accumulation point of $S$ and 'zero-order approximation' (ap-
proximation of $f \left( c + u \right)$ by a polynomial of degree zero, that is a constant)
are equivalent properties. In the next section we discuss the equivalence of
differentiability and first-order (that is linear) approximation.


### Exercises

1\. Prove Theorem 1.

2\. Let $S$ be a set in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ If $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ are continuous
on $S ,$ then so is the function $f + g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$

3\. Let $S$ be a set in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ and $T$ a set in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$ Suppose that $g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$
and $f : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p }$ are continuous on $S$ and $T$ respectively, and that
$g \left( x \right) \in T$ when $x \in S .$ Then the composite function $h : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { p }$ defined
by $h \left( x \right) = f \left( g \left( x \right) \right)$ is continuous on $S .$

4\. Let $S$ be a set in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ If the real-valued functions $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ,$ $\psi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
and $\chi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} - \left\{ 0 \right\}$ are continuous on $S ,$ then so are the real-valued
functions $\phi \psi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ and $\phi / \chi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } .$

5\. Let $\phi : \left( 0 , 1 \right) \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be defined by

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 1 / q & \left( x \text { rational, } x = p / q \right) , \\ 0 & \left( x \text { irrational } \right) , \end{array} \right. ,$$

where $p ,$ $q \in \mathbb{N}$ have no common factor. Show that $\phi$ is continuous at
every irrational point, and discontinuous at every rational point.


## 3 DIFFERENTIABILITY AND LINEAR APPROXIMATION

In the one-dimensional case, the equation

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) } { u } = \phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) ,$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="92" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

defining the derivative at c, is equivalent to the equation

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + u \phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) + r _ { c } \left( u \right) ,$$
(2)

where the remainder $r _ { c } \left( u \right)$ is of smaller order than $u$ as $u \rightarrow 0 ,$ that is

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r _ { c } \left( u \right) } { u } = 0 .$$
(3)

Equation $\left( 2 \right)$ is called the first-order Taylor formula. If for the moment we
think of the point $c$ as fixed and the increment $u$ as variable, then the incre-
ment of the function, that is the quantity $\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) ,$ consists of two terms,
namely a part $u \phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right)$ which is proportional to $u$ and an 'error' which can be
made as small as we please relative to $u$ by making $u$ itself small enough.
Thus the smaller the interval about the point $c$ which we consider, the more
accurately is the function $\phi \left( c + u \right)$ - which $\mathrm { i s } \mathrm { a }$ function of $u - \mathrm { r e p r e s e n t e d }$ by
its affine part $\phi \left( c \right) + u \phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) .$ We now define the expression

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = u \phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right)$$
(4)

as the $\left( \mathrm { f i r s t } \right)$ differential of $\phi$ at $c$ with increment u.

The notation $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right)$ rather than $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c , u \right)$ emphasizes the different roles
of $c$ and u. The first point, c, must be a point where $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right)$ exists, whereas the
second point, u, is an arbitrary point in $\mathbb{R} .$

Although the concept of differential is as a rule only used when $u$ is small,
there is in principle no need to restrict $u$ in any way. In particular, the dif-
ferential $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right)$ is a number which $h a s$ nothing $t o$ do with infinitely small
quantities.

The differential $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right)$ is thus the linear part of the increment $\phi \left( c + u \right) -$
$\phi \left( c \right) .$ This is expressed geometrically by replacing the curve at point $c$ by its
tangent.

Conversely, if there exists a quantity a, depending on $c$ but not on u, such
that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + u \alpha + r \left( u \right) ,$$
(5)

where $r \left( u \right) / u$ tends to 0 with u, that is $\mathrm { i f }$ we can approximate $\phi \left( c + u \right)$ by an
affine function (in $\left. u \right)$ such that the difference between the function and the
approximation function vanishes to a higher order than the increment u, then
$\phi$ is differentiable at c. The quantity $\alpha$ must then be the derivative $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) .$ We
see this immediately if we rewrite Equation (5) in the form

$$\frac { \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) } { u } = \alpha + \frac { r \left( u \right) } { u }$$
(6)

and then let $u$ tend to 0. Differentiability of a function and the possibility of
approximating a function by means of an affine function are therefore equiv-
alent properties.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="93" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The differential of a vector function" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 1 Geometric interpretation of the differential</figcaption>

$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right)$

## 4 THE DIFFERENTIAL OF A VECTOR FUNCTION

These ideas can be extended in a perfectly natural way to vector functions of
two or more variables.


### Definition 2

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Let $c$ be an interior
point of $S ,$ and let $B \left( c ; r \right)$ be an n-ball lying in $S .$ Let $u$ be a point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$
with $\| u \| < r ,$ so that $c + u \in B \left( c ; r \right) .$ If there exists a real $m \times n$ matrix $A ,$
depending on $c$ but not on u, such that

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A \left( c \right) u + r _ { c } \left( u \right)$$
(1)

for all $u \in \mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ with $\| u \| < r$ and

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r _ { c } \left( u \right) } { \| u \| } = 0 ,$$
(2)

then the function $f$ is said to be differentiable at c. The $m \times n$ matrix $A \left( c \right)$ is
then called the (first) derivative of $f$ at c, and the $m \times 1$ vector

$$\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) = A \left( c \right) u ,$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="94" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

which is a linear function of u, is called the (first) differential of $f \quad a t \quad c \left( w i t h \right.$
increment u). If $f$ is differentiable at every point of an open subset $E$ of $S ,$
we say $f$ is differentiable on (or $\left. i n \right)$ $E .$

In other words, $f$ is differentiable at the point $c$ if $f \left( c + u \right)$ can be ap-
proximated by an affine function of u. Note that a function $f$ can only be
differentiated at an interior point or on an open set.


### Example 1

Let $\phi : \mathrm { R } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined by $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x y ^ { 2 } .$ Then

$$\phi \left( x + u , y + v \right) = \left( x + u \right) \left( y + v \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= x y ^ { 2 } + \left( y ^ { 2 } u + 2 x y v \right) + \left( x v ^ { 2 } + 2 y u v + u v ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \phi \left( x , y \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x , y ; u , v \right) + r \left( u , v \right)$$

(4)

with

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x , y ; u , v \right) = \left( y ^ { 2 } , 2 x y \right) \left( \begin{array}{} u \\ v \end{array} \right)$$
(5)

and

$$r \left( u , v \right) = x v ^ { 2 } + 2 y u v + u v ^ { 2 } .$$
(6)

Since $r \left( u , v \right) / \left( u ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \rightarrow 0$ as $\left( u , v \right) \rightarrow \left( 0 , 0 \right) ,$ $\phi$ is differentiable at every
point of $\mathrm { R } ^ { 2 }$ and its derivative is $\left( y ^ { 2 } , 2 x y \right) ,$ a row vector.

We have seen before (Section 2) that a function can be continuous at a
point c, but fails to be continuous at points near $c ;$ indeed, the function may
not even exist near c. If $\mathrm { f } _ { \mathrm { s } }$ a function is differentiable at c, then $i t$ must exist in $a$
neighbourhood of c, but the function need not be differentiable or continuous
in that neighbourhood. For example, the real-valued function $\phi : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
defined by

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 2 } & \left( x \text { rational } \right) , \\ 0 & \left( x \text { irrational } \right) , \end{array} \right. ,$$
(7)

is differentiable (and continuous) at $x = 0 ,$ but neither differentiable nor
continuous at any other point.

Let us return to Equation (1). It consists of $m$ equations,

$$f _ { i } \left( c + u \right) = f _ { i } \left( c \right) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } \left( c \right) u _ { j } + r _ { c } ^ { i } \left( u \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$$
(8)

with

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r _ { c } ^ { i } \left( u \right) } { \| u \| } = 0 \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$$
(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="95" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Uniqueness of the differential" -->

Hence we obtain our next theorem.


### Theorem 2

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ A function $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ is differentiable at an interior
point $c$ of $S$ if and only if each of its component functions $f _ { i }$ is differentiable
at c. In that case, the i-th component of $\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right)$ is $\mathrm { d } f _ { i } \left( c ; u \right) \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$

In view of Theorems 1 and 2, it is not surprising to find that many of
the theorems on continuity and differentiation that are valid for real-valued
functions remain valid for vector functions. It appears therefore that we need
only study real-valued functions. This is not so, however, because in practi-
cal applications real-valued functions are often expressed in terms of vector
functions (and indeed, matrix functions). Another reason for studying vector
functions, rather than merely real-valued functions, is to obtain a meaningful
chain rule (Section 12).

If $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ is differentiable on an open subset $E$ of $S ,$ there
must exist real-valued functions $a _ { i j } : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right.$ $j = 1 , \ldots , n$ such
that (1) holds for every point of $E .$ We have, however, no guarantee that, for
given $f ,$ any such function $a _ { i j }$ exists. We shall prove later (Section 10) that,
when $f$ is suitably restricted, the functions $a _ { i j }$ exist. But first we prove that,
if such functions exist, they are unique.


### Exercise

1\. Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be differentiable at a point $c \in S \subset$
$\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Then the function $h = f + g$ is differentiable at $c$ with $\mathrm { d } h \left( c ; u \right) =$
$\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) + \mathrm { d } g \left( c ; u \right) .$


## 5 UNIQUENESS OF THE DIFFERENTIAL


### Theorem 3

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ be differentiable at a point $c \in S$ with differential
$\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) = A \left( c \right) u .$ Suppose a second matrix $A ^ { * } \left( c \right)$ exists such that $\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) =$
$A ^ { * } \left( c \right) u .$ Then $A \left( c \right) = A ^ { * } \left( c \right) .$

Proof. From the definition of differentiability we have

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A \left( c \right) u + r _ { c } \left( u \right)$$
(1)

and $a l s o$

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A ^ { * } \left( c \right) u + r _ { c } ^ { * } \left( u \right) ,$$
(2)

where $r _ { c } \left( u \right) / \| u \|$ and $r _ { c } ^ { * } \left( u \right) / \| u \|$ both tend to 0 with u. Let $B \left( c \right) = A \left( c \right) -$
$A ^ { * } \left( c \right) .$ Subtracting (2) from (1) gives

$$B \left( c \right) u = r _ { c } ^ { * } \left( u \right) - r _ { c } \left( u \right) .$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="96" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

Hence,

$$\frac { B \left( c \right) u } { \| u \| } \rightarrow 0 \quad \mathrm { a s } u \rightarrow 0 .$$
(4)

For fixed $u \neq 0$ it follows that

$$\frac { B \left( c \right) \left( t u \right) } { \| t u \| } \rightarrow 0 \quad \mathrm { a s } t \rightarrow 0 .$$
(5)

The left side of (5) is independent of t. Thus $B \left( c \right) u = 0$ for all $u \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ The
theorem follows.
☐
✷


## 6 CONTINUITY OF DIFFERENTIABLE FUNCTIONS

Next we prove that the existence of the differential $\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right)$ implies conti-
nuity of $f$ at c. In other words, that continuity is a necessary condition for
differentiability.


### Theorem 4

If $f$ is differentiable at c, then $f$ is continuous at c.

$P r o o f .$ Since $f$ is differentiable, we write

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A \left( c \right) u + r _ { c } \left( u \right) .$$
(1)

Now, both $A \left( c \right) u$ and $r _ { c } \left( u \right)$ tend to 0 with u. Hence

$$f \left( c + u \right) \rightarrow f \left( c \right) \quad \mathrm { a s } u \rightarrow 0$$
(2)

and the result follows.

The converse of Theorem 4 is, of course, false. For example, the function $\phi$ :
$\mathrm { R } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ defined by the equation $\phi \left( x \right) = | x |$ is continuous but not differentiable
at 0.


### Exercise

1\. Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$
and differentiable at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Show that (a) there exists
a non-negative number $M ,$ depending on $c$ but not on $u ,$ such that
$| \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) | \leq M \| u \|$ (b) there exists a positive number $\eta ,$ again depending
on $c$ but not on $u ,$ such that $| r _ { c } \left( u \right) | < \| u \|$ for all $u \neq 0$ with $\| u \| < \eta .$
Conclude that (c) $| \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) | < \left( 1 + M \right) \| u \|$ for all $u \neq 0$ with
$\| u \| < \eta .$ A function with this property is said to satisfy a Lipschitz
condition at c. Of course, if $\phi$ satisfies a Lipschitz condition at $c ,$ then
it must be continuous at c.

✷

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="97" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7 ] Partial derivatives" -->


## 7 PARTIAL DERIVATIVES

Before we develop the theory of differentials any further, we introduce an
important concept in multivariable calculus, the partial derivative.

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ with values in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$
and let $f _ { i } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$ be the $i - \mathrm { t h }$ component function of $f .$ Let
c be an interior point of $S ,$ and let $e _ { j }$ be the j-th unit vector in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ that is
the vector whose j-th component is one and whose remaining components are
zero. Consider another point $c + t e _ { j }$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ all of whose components except
the j-th are the same as those of c. Since $c$ is an interior point of $S ,$ $c + t e _ { j }$
is, for small enough $t ,$ also a point of $S .$ Now consider the limit

$$\lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f _ { i } \left( c + t e _ { j } \right) - f _ { i } \left( c \right) } { t }$$
(1)

When this limit exists, it is called the partial derivative of $f _ { i }$ with respect to
the j-th coordinate (or the j-th partial derivative of $f _ { i }$ at c and is denoted by
