$\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right) .$ (Other notations include $\left[ \partial f _ { i } \left( x \right) / \partial x _ { j } \right] _ { x = c }$ or even $\partial f _ { i } \left( c \right) / \partial x _ { j }$ Partial
differentiation thus produces, from a given function $f _ { i } ,$ $n$ further functions
$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } f _ { i } , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { n } f _ { i }$ defined at those points in $S$ where the corresponding limits
exist.

In fact, the concept of partial differentiation reduces the discussion of
real-valued functions of several variables to the one-dimensional case. We are
merely treating $f _ { i }$ as a function of one variable at a time. Thus $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i }$ is the
derivative of $f _ { i }$ with respect to the j-th variable, holding the other variables
fixed.


### Theorem 5

If $f$ is differentiable at c, then all partial derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right)$ exist.

Proof. Since $f$ is differentiable at c, there exists a real matrix $A \left( c \right)$ with ele-
ments $a _ { i j } \left( c \right)$ such that, for all $\| u \| < r ,$

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A \left( c \right) u + r _ { c } \left( u \right) ,$$
(2)

where

$$r _ { c } \left( u \right) / \| u \| \rightarrow 0 \quad \mathrm { a s } u \rightarrow 0 .$$
(3)

Since (2) is true for all $\| u \| < r ,$ it is true in particular if we choose $u = t e _ { j }$
with $| t | < r .$ This gives

$$f \left( c + t e _ { j } \right) = f \left( c \right) + t A \left( c \right) e _ { j } + r _ { c } \left( t e _ { j } \right)$$
(4)

where

$$r _ { c } \left( t e _ { j } \right) / t \rightarrow 0 \quad \mathrm { a s } t \rightarrow 0 .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="98" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

If we divide both sides of (4) by $t$ and let $t$ tend to 0, we find that

$$a _ { i j } \left( c \right) = \lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f _ { i } \left( c + t e _ { j } \right) - f _ { i } \left( c \right) } { t } .$$
(6)

Since $a _ { i j } \left( c \right)$ exists, so does the limit on the right-hand side of (6). But, by $\left( 1 \right) ,$
this is precisely the partial derivative $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right) .$
☐
✷

The converse of Theorem 5 is false. Indeed, the existence of the partial
derivatives with respect to each variable separately does not even imply con-
tinuity in all the variables simultaneously (although it does imply continuity
in each variable separately, by Theorem 4). Consider the following example
of a function of two variables:

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x + y , & \mathrm { i f } x = 0 \mathrm { o r } y = 0 \text { or both, } \\ 1 , & \mathrm { o t h e r w i s e . } \end{array} \right. ,$$
(7)

This function is clearly not continuous at $\left( 0 , 0 \right) ,$ but the partial derivatives
$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( 0 , 0 \right)$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( 0 , 0 \right)$ both exist. In fact,

$$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( 0 , 0 \right) = \lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \phi \left( t , 0 \right) - \phi \left( 0 , 0 \right) } { t - 0 } = \lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { t } { t } = 1$$
(8)

and, similarly, $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( 0 , 0 \right) = 1 .$

$A$ partial converse of Theorem 5 exists, however (Theorem 7).


### Exercise

1\. Show in the example given by (7) that $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi ,$ while existing at
$\left( 0 , 0 \right) ,$ are not continuous there, and that every disc $B \left( 0 \right)$ contains points
where the partials both exist and points where the partials both do not
exist.


## 8 THE FIRST IDENTIFICATION THEOREM

If $f$ is differentiable at c, then a matrix $A \left( c \right)$ exists such that for all $\| u \| < r ,$

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A \left( c \right) u + r _ { c } \left( u \right) ,$$
(1)

where $r _ { c } \left( u \right) / \| u \| \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0 .$ The proof of Theorem 5 reveals that the
elements $a _ { i j } \left( c \right)$ of the matrix $A \left( c \right)$ are, in fact, precisely the partial deriva-
tives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right) .$ This, in conjunction with the uniqueness theorem (Theorem 3),
establishes the following central result.


### Theorem 6 (first identification theorem)

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a vector function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and differen-
tiable at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Let $u$ be a real $n \times 1$ vector. Then

$$\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) u ,$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="99" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Existence of the differential, $I$" -->

where $D f \left( c \right)$ is an $m \times n$ matrix whose elements $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right)$ are the partial deriva-
tives of $f$ evaluated at c. Conversely, if $A \left( c \right)$ is a matrix such that

$$\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) = A \left( c \right) u$$
(3)

for all real $n \times 1$ vectors u, then $A \left( c \right) = \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) .$

The $m \times n$ matrix $D f \left( c \right)$ in $\left( 2 \right) ,$ whose ij-th element is $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right) ,$ is called
the $J a c o b i a n$ matrix of $f$ at c. It is defined at each point where the partials
$\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m ; j = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ exist. (Hence the Jacobian matrix $D f \left( c \right)$
may exist even when the function $f$ is not differentiable at c.) When $m =$ n,
the determinant of the Jacobian matrix of $f$ is called the $J a c o b i a n$ of $f .$ The
transpose of the $m \times n$ Jacobian matrix $D f \left( c \right)$ is an $n \times m$ matrix called the
gradient of $f$ at c; it is denoted by $\nabla f \left( c \right) .$ (The symbol $\nabla$ is pronounced 'del'.)
Thus

$$\nabla f \left( c \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(4)

In particular, when $m =$ 1, the vector function $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ specializes to a
real-valued function $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ,$ the Jacobian matrix specializes to a $1 \times n$ row
vector $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right)$ and the gradient specializes to an $n \times 1$ column vector $\nabla \phi \left( c \right) .$

The first identification theorem will be used throughout this book. Its
great practical value lies in the fact that if $f$ is differentiable at c and we
have found a differential $\mathrm { d } f$ at c, then the value of the partials at $c$ can be
immediately determined.

Some caution is required when interpreting Equation (2). The right side
of (2) exists $\mathrm { i f }$ (and only if) all the partial derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( c \right)$ exist. But this
does not mean that the differential $\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right)$ exists if all partials exist. We know
that $\mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right)$ exists if and only if $f$ is differentiable at $c$ (Section 4). We also
know from Theorem 5 that the existence of all the partials is a necessary but
not a sufficient condition for differentiability. Hence, Equation (2) is only valid
when $f$ is differentiable at c.


## 9 EXISTENCE OF THE DIFFERENTIAL, I

So far we have derived some theorems concerning differentials on the assump-
tion that the differential exists, or, what is the same, that the function is
differentiable. We have seen (Section 7) that the existence of all partial deriva-
tives at a point is necessary but not sufficient for differentiability (in fact, it
is not even sufficient for continuity).

What, then, is a sufficient condition for differentiability at a point? Before
we answer this question, we pose four preliminary questions in order to gain
further insight into the properties of differentiable functions.

(i) If $f$ is differentiable at c, does it follow that each of the partials is
continuous at c?

(ii) If each of the partials is continuous at c, does it follow that $f$ is differ-
entiable at $c ^ { * }$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="100" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

(iii) If $f$ is differentiable at c, does it follow that each of the partials exists
in some n-ball $B \left( c \right) ?$

(iv) If each of the partials exists in some n-ball $B \left( c \right) ,$ does it follow that $f$
is differentiable at $c ?$

The answer to all four questions is, in general, 'No'. Let us see why.


### Example 2

Let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined by

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 2 } \left[ y + \sin \left( 1 / x \right) \right] , & \mathrm { i f } x \neq 0 , \\ 0 , & \mathrm { i f } x = 0 . \end{array} \right. ,$$
(1)

Then $\phi$ is differentiable at every point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { 2 }$ with partial derivatives

$$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 2 x \left[ y + \sin \left( 1 / x \right) \right] - \cos \left( 1 / x \right) , & \mathrm { i f } x \neq 0 , \\ 0 , & \mathrm { i f } x = 0 , \end{array} \right. ,$$
(2)

and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 2 } .$ We see that $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi$ is not continuous at any point on the
y-axis, since $\cos \left( 1 / x \right)$ in (2) does not tend to a limit as $x \rightarrow 0 .$


### Example 3

Let $A = \left( x , y \right) : x = y , x > 0$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } ,$ and let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be
defined by

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 2 / 3 } , & \mathrm { i f } \left( x , y \right) \in A , \\ 0 , & \mathrm { i f } \left( x , y \right) \notin A . \end{array} \right.$$
(3)

Then $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi$ and $D _ { 2 } \phi$ are both zero everywhere except on $A ,$ where they are
not defined. Thus both partials are continuous at the origin. But $\phi$ is not
differentiable at the origin.


### Example 4

Let $\phi : \mathrm { R } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be defined by

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } , & \mathrm { i f } x \text { and } y \text { are rational } \\ 0 , & \text { otherwise } . \end{array} \right. ,$$
(4)

Here $\phi$ is differentiable at only one point, namely the origin. The partial
derivative $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi$ is zero at the origin and at every point $\left( x , y \right) \in \mathrm { R } ^ { 2 }$ where $y$ is
irrational; it is undefined elsewhere. Similarly, $D _ { 2 } \phi$ is zero at the origin and
every point $\left( x , y \right) \in \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { 2 }$ where $x$ is irrational; it is undefined elsewhere. Hence
every disc with centre 0 contains points where the partials do not exist.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="101" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Existence of the differential, $I I$" -->


### Example 5

Let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be defined by the equation

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 3 } / \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) , & \mathrm { i f } \left( x , y \right) \neq \left( 0 , 0 \right) , \\ 0 , & \mathrm { i f } \left( x , y \right) = \left( 0 , 0 \right) . \end{array} \right. ,$$
(5)

Here $\phi$ is continuous everywhere, both partials exist everywhere, but $\phi$ is not
differentiable at the origin.


## 10 EXISTENCE OF THE DIFFERENTIAL, II

Examples 2-5 show that neither the continuity of all partial derivatives at
a point $c$ nor the existence of all partial derivatives in some n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ is,
in general, a sufficient condition for differentiability. With this knowledge the
reader can now appreciate the following theorem.


### Theorem 7

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and let $c$ be an
interior point of $S .$ If each of the partial derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i }$ exists in some $n - \mathrm { b a l l }$
$B \left( c \right)$ and is continuous at c, then $f$ is differentiable at c.

Proof. In view of Theorem 2, it suffices to consider the case $m =$ 1. The vector
function $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ then specializes to a real-valued function $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} .$

Let $r > 0$ be the radius of the ball $B \left( c \right) ,$ and let $u$ be a point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ with
$\| u \| < r ,$ so that $c + u \in B \left( c \right) .$ Expressing $u$ in terms of its components we
have

$$u = u _ { 1 } e _ { 1 } + \cdots + u _ { n } e _ { n } ,$$
(1)

where $e _ { j }$ is the j-th unit vector in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Let $v _ { 0 } = 0 ,$ and define the partial sums

$$v _ { k } = u _ { 1 } e _ { 1 } + \cdots + u _ { k } e _ { k } \quad \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(2)

Thus $v _ { k }$ is a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ whose first $k$ components are the same as those of $u$
and whose last $n \quad - \quad k$ components are zero. Since $\| u \| < r ,$ we have $\| v _ { k } \| < r ,$
so that $c + v _ { k } \in B \left( c \right)$ for $k = 1 , \ldots , n .$

We now write the difference $\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right)$ as a sum of $n$ terms as follows:

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \phi \left( c + v _ { j } \right) - \phi \left( c + v _ { j - 1 } \right) \right) .$$
(3)

The k-th term in the sum is $\phi \left( c + v _ { k } \right) - \phi \left( c + v _ { k - 1 } \right) .$ Since $B \left( c \right)$ is convex, the
line segment with endpoints $c + v _ { k - 1 }$ and $c + v _ { k }$ lies in $B \left( c \right) .$ Further, since
$v _ { k } = v _ { k - 1 } + u _ { k } e _ { k } ,$ the two points $c + v _ { k - 1 }$ and $c + v _ { k }$ differ only in their k-th

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="102" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

component, and we can apply the one-dimensional mean-value theorem. This
gives

$$\phi \left( c + v _ { k } \right) - \phi \left( c + v _ { k - 1 } \right) = u _ { k } \mathrm { D } _ { k } \phi \left( c + v _ { k - 1 } + \theta _ { k } u _ { k } e _ { k } \right)$$
(4)

for some $\theta _ { k } \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Now, each partial derivative $\mathrm { D } _ { k } \phi$ is continuous at c, so
that

$$\mathrm { D } _ { k } \phi \left( c + v _ { k - 1 } + \theta _ { k } u _ { k } e _ { k } \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { k } \phi \left( c \right) + R _ { k } \left( v _ { k } , \theta _ { k } \right) ,$$
(5)

where $R _ { k } \left( v _ { k } , \theta _ { k } \right) \rightarrow 0$ as $v _ { k } \rightarrow 0 .$ Substituting (5) in (4) and then (4) in (3)
gives, after some rearrangement,

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } R _ { j } \left( v _ { j } , \theta _ { j } \right) .$$
(6)

It follows that

$$| \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c \right) | \leq \| u \| \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } | R _ { j } | ,$$
(7)

where $R _ { k } \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0 ,$ $k = 1 , \ldots , n .$
✷
☐

Note. Examples 2 and 4 in the previous section show that neither the ex-
istence of all partials in an n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ nor the continuity of all partials at c
is a necessary condition for differentiability of $f$ at c.


### Exercises

1\. Prove Equation (5).

2\. Show that, in fact, only the existence of all the partials and continuity
of all but one of them is sufficient for differentiability.

3\. The condition that the $n$ partials be continuous at c, although sufficient,
is by no means a necessary condition for the existence of the differential
at c. Consider, for example, the case where $\phi$ can be expressed as a sum
of $n$ functions,

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \phi _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) + \cdots + \phi _ { n } \left( x _ { n } \right) . ,$$

where $\phi _ { j }$ is a function of the one-dimensional variable $x _ { j }$ alone. Prove
that the mere existence of the partials $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { n } \phi$ is sufficient for the
existence of the differential at c.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="103" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] Continuous differentiability" -->


## 11 CONTINUOUS DIFFERENTIABILITY

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on an open set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ If all the
first-order partial derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i } \left( x \right)$ exist and are continuous at every point
$x$ in $S ,$ then the function $f$ is said to be continuously differentiable on $S .$

Notice that while we defined continuity and differentiability of a function
at a point, continuous differentiability is only defined on an open set. In view
of Theorem 7, continuous differentiability implies differentiability.


## 12 THE CHAIN RULE

A very important $\mathrm { r e s u l t }$ is the so-called chain $r u l e .$ In one dimension, the
chain rule gives a formula for differentiating a composite function $h = g \circ f$
defined by the equation

$$\left( g \circ f \right) \left( x \right) = g \left( f \left( x \right) \right) .$$
(1)

The formula states that

$$h ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) = g ^ { \prime } \left( f \left( c \right) \right) \cdot f ^ { \prime } \left( c \right)$$
(2)

and thus expresses the derivative of $h$ in terms of the derivatives of g and $f .$
Its extension to the multivariable case $\mathrm { i s }$ as follows.


### Theorem 8 (chain rule)

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and assume that $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is differentiable at
an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Let $T$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that $f \left( x \right) \in T$ for
all $x \in S ,$ and assume that $g : T \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { p }$ is differentiable at an interior point
$b = f \left( c \right)$ of T. Then the composite function $h : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p }$ defined by

$$h \left( x \right) = g \left( f \left( x \right) \right)$$
(3)

is differentiable at c, and

$$\mathrm { D } h \left( c \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) .$$
(4)

Proof. We put $A = \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) ,$ $B = \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right)$ and define the set $E _ { r } ^ { n } = \left\{ x : x \in \right.$
$\left. \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { n } , \| x \| < r \right\} .$ Since $c \in S$ and $b \in T$ are interior points, there is an $r > 0$
such that $c + u \in S$ for all $u \in E _ { r } ^ { n } ,$ and $b + v \in T$ for all $v \in E _ { r } ^ { m } .$ We
may therefore define vector functions $r _ { 1 } : E _ { r } ^ { n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ $r _ { 2 } : E _ { r } ^ { m } \rightarrow \check { \mathbb{R} } ^ { p }$ and
$R : E _ { r } ^ { n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p }$ by

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + A u + r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) ,$$
$$g \left( b + v \right) = g \left( b \right) + B v + r _ { 2 } \left( v \right) ,$$
$$h \left( c + u \right) = h \left( c \right) + B A u + R \left( u \right) .$$
(5)
(6)
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="104" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

Since $f$ is differentiable at c, and $g$ is differentiable at $b ,$ we have

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) / \| u \| = 0$$

and

$$\lim _ { v \rightarrow 0 } r _ { 2 } \left( v \right) / \| v \| = 0 .$$
(8)

We have to prove that

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } R \left( u \right) / \| u \| = 0 .$$
(9)

Defining a new vector function $z : E _ { r } ^ { n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ by

$$z \left( u \right) = f \left( c + u \right) - f \left( c \right) ,$$
(10)

and using the definitions of $R$ and $h ,$ we obtain

$$R \left( u \right) = g \left( b + z \left( u \right) \right) - \quad g \left( b \right) - \quad B z \left( u \right) + B \left[ f \left( c + u \right) - f \left( c \right) - \quad A u \right] ,$$
(11)

so that, in view of (5) and (6),

$$R \left( u \right) = r _ { 2 } \left( z \left( u \right) \right) + B r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) .$$
(12)

Now, let $\mu _ { A }$ and $\mu _ { B }$ be constants such that

$$\| A x \| \leq \mu _ { A } \| x \| \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \| B y \| \leq \mu _ { B } \| y \|$$
(13)

for every $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ and $y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ (see Exercise 2), and observe from (5) and (10)
that $z \left( u \right) = A u + r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) .$ Repeated application of the triangle inequality then
shows that

$$\| R \left( u \right) \| \leq \| r _ { 2 } \left( z \left( u \right) \right) \| + \| B r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \|$$
$$= \frac { \| r _ { 2 } \left( z \left( u \right) \right) \| } { \| z \left( u \right) \| } \cdot \| A u + r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \| + \| B r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \|$$
$$\leq \frac { \| r _ { 2 } \left( z \right) \| } { \| z \| } \left( \mu _ { A } \| u \| + \| r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \| \right) + \mu _ { B } \| r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \| .$$

(14)

Dividing both sides of (14) by $\| u \|$ yields

$$\frac { \| R \left( u \right) \| } { \| u \| } \leq \mu _ { A } \frac { \| r _ { 2 } \left( z \right) \| } { \| z \| } + \mu _ { B } \frac { \| r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \| } { \| u \| } + \frac { \| r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) \| } { \| u \| } \cdot \frac { \| r _ { 2 } \left( z \right) \| } { \| z \| } .$$
(15)

Now, $r _ { 2 } \left( z \right) / \| z \| \rightarrow 0$ as $z \rightarrow 0$ by (8), and since $z \left( u \right)$ tends to 0 with u, it
follows that $r _ { 2 } \left( z \right) / \| z \| \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0$ 0. Also by (8), $r _ { 1 } \left( u \right) / \| u \| \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0$ 0.
This shows that (9) holds.
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. What is the order of the matrices $A$ and $B ?$ Is the matrix product $B A$
defined?

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="105" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Cauchy invariance" -->

2\. Show that the constants $\mu _ { A }$ and $\mu _ { B }$ in (13) exist. [Hint: Use Exercise
1.14.2.]

3\. Write out the chain rule as a system of $n p$ equations

$$\mathrm { D } _ { j } h _ { i } \left( c \right) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { m } \mathrm { D } _ { k } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { k } \left( c \right)$$

where $j = 1 , \ldots , n$ and $i = 1 , \ldots , p .$


## 13 CAUCHY INVARIANCE

The chain rule relates the partial derivatives of a composite function $h = g \circ f$
to the partial derivatives of $g \quad a n d \quad f .$ We shall now discuss an immediate conse-
quence of the chain rule, which relates the differential of $h$ to the differentials
of $g$ and $f .$ This result (known as Cauchy's rule of invariance) is particularly
useful in performing computations with differentials.

Let $h = g \circ f$ be a composite function, as before, such that

$$h \left( x \right) = g \left( f \left( x \right) \right) , \quad x \in S . ,$$
(1)

If $f$ is differentiable at c and g is differentiable at $b = f \left( c \right) ,$ then $h$ is differen-
tiable at $c$ with

$$\mathrm { d } h \left( c ; u \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } \left( h \left( c \right) \right) u . \right.$$
(2)

Using the chain rule, (2) becomes

$$\mathrm { d } h \left( c ; u \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \right) \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) .$$

(3)

We have thus proved the following.


### Theorem 9 (Cauchy's rule of invariance)

If $f$ is differentiable at $c \quad a n d \quad g$ is differentiable at $b = f \left( c \right) ,$ then the differential
of the composite function $h = g \circ f$ is

$$\mathrm { d } h \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right)$$
(4)

for every $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$

Cauchy's rule of invariance justifies the use of a simpler notation for differ-
entials in practical applications, which adds greatly to the ease and elegance
of performing computations with differentials. We shall discuss notational
matters in more detail in Section 16.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="106" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->


## 14 THE MEAN-VALUE THEOREM FOR REAL-VALUED FUNCTIONS

The mean-value theorem for functions from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$ states that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \left( \mathrm { D } \phi \left( c + \theta u \right) \right) u$$
(1)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ This equation is, in general, false for vector functions.
Consider for example the vector function $f : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 }$ defined by

$$f \left( t \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { t ^ { 2 } } \\ { t ^ { 3 } } \end{array} \right) .$$
(2)

Then no value of $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ exists such that

$$f \left( 1 \right) = f \left( 0 \right) + \mathrm { D } f \left( \theta \right) ,$$
(3)

as can be easily verified. Several modified versions of the mean-value theorem
exist for vector functions, but here we only need the (straightforward) gener-
alization of the one-dimensional mean-value theorem to real-valued functions
of two or more variables.


### Theorem 10 (mean-value theorem)

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function, defined and differentiable on an open
set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ Let c be a point of $S ,$ and $u$ a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ such that $c + t u \in S$
for all $t \in \left[ 0 , 1 \right] .$ Then

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right)$$
(4)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$

$P r o o f .$ Consider the real-valued function $\psi : \left[ 0 , 1 \right] \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ defined by the equation

$$\psi \left( t \right) = \phi \left( c + t u \right) .$$
(5)

Then $\psi$ is differentiable at each point of $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$ and its derivative is given by

$$\mathrm { D } \psi \left( t \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } \phi \left( c + t u \right) \right) u = \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + t u ; u \right) .$$
(6)

By the one-dimensional mean-value theorem we have

$$\frac { \psi \left( 1 \right) - \psi \left( 0 \right) } { 1 - 0 } = \mathrm { D } \psi \left( \theta \right)$$
(7)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Thus

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) = \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right) ,$$
(8)

thus completing the proof.


### Exercise

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="107" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 15 ] Matrix functions" -->

1\. Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function, defined and differentiable on
an open interval $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) = 0$ for each $c \in S ,$ then $\phi$ is constant
on $S .$


## 15 MATRIX FUNCTIONS

Hitherto we have only considered vector functions. The following are examples
of matrix functions:

$$F \left( \xi \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \xi & 0 \\ 0 & \xi ^ { 2 } \end{array} \right) , \quad F \left( x \right) = x x ^ { \prime } , \quad F \left( X \right) = X ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)

The first example maps a scalar $\xi$ into a matrix, the second example maps a
vector $x$ into a matrix $x x ^ { \prime } ,$ and the third example maps a matrix $X$ into its
transpose matrix $X ^ { \prime } .$

To extend the calculus of vector functions to matrix functions is $\mathrm { s t r a i g h t }$
forward. Let us consider a matrix function $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ defined on a set
$S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } .$ That is, $F$ maps an $n \times q$ matrix $X$ in $S$ into an $m \times p$ matrix
$F \left( X \right) .$


### Definition 3

Let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be a matrix function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } .$ Let $C$ be
an interior point of $S ,$ and let $B \left( C ; r \right) \subset S$ be a ball with centre $C$ and radius
$r \left( \mathrm { a l s o } \right.$ called a neighbourhood of $C$ and denoted $N \left( C \right) .$ Let $U$ be a point in
$\mathrm { I R } ^ { n \times q }$ with $\| U \| < r ,$ so that $C + U \in B \left( C ; r \right) .$ If there exists a real $m p \times n q$
matrix $A ,$ depending on $C$ but not on $U ,$ such that

$$\mathrm { v e c } F \left( C + U \right) = \mathrm { v e c } F \left( C \right) + A \left( C \right) \mathrm { v e c } U + \mathrm { v e c } R _ { C } \left( U \right)$$
(2)

for all $U \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q }$ with $\| U \| < r$ and

$$\lim _ { U \rightarrow 0 } \frac { R _ { C } \left( U \right) } { \| U \| } = 0 ,$$
(3)

then the function $F$ is said to be differentiable at $C .$ The $m \times p$ matrix
$\mathrm { d } F \left( C ; U \right)$ defined by

$$\mathrm { v e c d } F \left( C ; U \right) = A \left( C \right) \mathrm { v e c } U$$
(4)

is then called the (first) differential of $F$ at $C$ with increment $U$ and the
$m p \times n q$ matrix $A \left( C \right)$ is called the (first) derivative of $F$ at $C .$

Note. Recall that the norm of a real matrix $X$ is defined by

$$\| X \| = \left( \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }$$
(5)

and a ball in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q }$ by

$$B \left( C ; r \right) = \left\{ X : X \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } , \| X - C \| < r \right\} .$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="108" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

In view of Definition 3, all calculus properties of matrix functions follow imme-
diately from the corresponding properties of vector functions because, instead
of the matrix function $F ,$ we can consider the vector function $f : \mathrm { v e c } S \rightarrow \mathrm { I R } ^ { m p }$
defined by

$$f \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) = \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) .$$
(7)

It is easy to see from (2) and (3) that the differentials of $F$ and $f$ are related
by

$$\mathrm { v e c d } F \left( C ; U \right) = \mathrm { d } f \left( \mathrm { v e c } C ; \mathrm { v e c } U \right) .$$
(8)

We then define the Jacobian matrix of $F$ at $C$ as

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( C \right) = \mathrm { D } f \left( \mathrm { v e c } C \right) .$$
(9)

This is an $m p \times n q$ matrix, whose ij-th element is the partial derivative of
the i-th component of vec $F \left( X \right)$ with respect to the j-th element of vec $X ,$
☒
evaluated at $X = C .$

The following three theorems are now straightforward generalizations of
Theorems 6, 8 and 9.


### Theorem 11 (first identification theorem for matrix functions)

Let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be a matrix function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ and
differentiable at an interior point $C$ of $S .$ Then

$$\mathrm { v e c d } F \left( C ; U \right) = A \left( C \right) \mathrm { v e c } U$$
(10)

for all $U \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q }$ if and only if

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( C \right) = A \left( C \right) .$$
(11)


### Theorem 12 (chain rule)

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ and assume that $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ is differentiable
at an interior point $C$ of $S .$ Let $T$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ such that $F \left( X \right) \in T$
for all $X \in S ,$ and assume that $G : T \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { r \times s }$ is differentiable at an interior
point $B = F \left( C \right)$ of $T .$ Then the composite function $H : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { r \times s }$ defined by

$$H \left( X \right) = G \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$$ ☒
(12)

is differentiable at $C ,$ and

$$\mathrm { D } H \left( C \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } G \left( B \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } F \left( C \right) \right) .$$
(13)


### Theorem 13 (Cauchy's rule of invariance)

If $F$ is differentiable at $C$ and $G$ is differentiable at $B = F \left( C \right) ,$ then the
differential of the composite function $H = G \circ F$ is

$$\mathrm { d } H \left( C ; U \right) = \mathrm { d } G \left( B ; \mathrm { d } F \left( C ; U \right) \right)$$
(14)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="109" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 16 ] Some remarks on notation" -->

for every $U$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } .$


### Exercise

1\. Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ and assume that $F : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m \times p }$ is continuous
at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Assume also that $F \left( c \right)$ has full rank (that is,
$F \left( c \right)$ has either full column rank $p \quad o r$ full row rank $\left. m \right) .$ Prove that $F \left( x \right)$
has locally constant rank that is, $F \left( x \right)$ has full rank for all $x$ in some
neighbourhood of $x =$ c.


## 16 SOME REMARKS ON NOTATION

We remarked in Section 13 that Cauchy's rule of invariance justifies the use of
a simpler notation for differentials in practical applications. (In the theoretical
Chapters 4-7 we shall not use this simplified notation.) Let us now see what
this simplification involves and how it is justified.

Let $g : \mathbb{R} ^ { m } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p }$ be a given differentiable vector function and consider
the equation

$$y = g \left( t \right) .$$
(1)

We shall now use the symbol $\mathrm { d } y$ to denote the differential

$$\mathrm { d } y = \mathrm { d } g \left( t ; \mathrm { d } t \right) .$$
(2)

In this expression, $\mathrm { d } t$ (previously $\left. u \right)$ denotes an arbitrary vector in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ and
$\mathrm { d } y$ denotes the corresponding vector in $\mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { p } .$ Thus $\mathrm { d } t$ and $\mathrm { d } y$ are vectors of
variables.

Suppose now that the variables $t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } , \ldots , t _ { m }$ depend on certain other vari-
ables, say $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } :$

$$t = f \left( x \right)$$
(3)

Substituting $f \left( x \right)$ for $t$ in (1), we obtain

$$y = g \left( f \left( x \right) \right) \equiv h \left( x \right) ,$$
(4)

and therefore

$$\mathrm { d } y = \mathrm { d } h \left( x ; \mathrm { d } x \right) .$$
(5)

The double use of the symbol $\mathrm { d } y$ in (2) and (5) is justified by Cauchy's rule
of invariance. This is easy to see: from (3) we have

$$d t = d f \left( x ; d x \right) ,$$
(6)

where $\mathrm { d } x$ is an arbitrary vector in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Then (5) gives (by Theorem 9)

$$\mathrm { d } y = \mathrm { d } g \left( f \left( x \right) ; \mathrm { d } f \left( x ; \mathrm { d } x \right) \right) = \mathrm { d } g \left( t ; \mathrm { d } t \right)$$
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="110" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Differentials and differentiability [Ch. 5" -->

using (3) and (6). We conclude that Equation (2) is valid even when $t _ { 1 } , \ldots , t _ { m }$
depend on other variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ although (6) shows that $\mathrm { d } t$ is then no
longer an arbitrary vector in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$

We can economize still further with notation by replacing $y$ in (1) with $g$
itself, thus writing (2) as

$$\mathrm { d } g = \mathrm { d } g \left( t ; \mathrm { d } t \right)$$
(8)

and calling $\mathrm { d } g$ the $d i f f e r e n t i a l$ of $g$ at t. This type of conceptually ambiguous
usage (of $g$ as both function symbol and variable) will assist practical work
with differentials in Part 3.


### Example 6

Let

$$y = \phi \left( x \right) = e ^ { x ^ { \prime } x } .$$
(9)

Then

$$\mathrm { d } y = \mathrm { d } e ^ { x ^ { \prime } x } = e ^ { x ^ { \prime } x } \left( \mathrm { d } x ^ { \prime } x \right) = e ^ { x ^ { \prime } x } \left( \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } x + x ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } x \right)$$
$$= \left( 2 e ^ { x ^ { \prime } x } x ^ { \prime } \right) d x .$$

(10)


### Example 7

Let

$$z = \phi \left( \beta \right) = \left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } \left( y - X \beta \right) .$$
(11)

Then, letting $e = y - X \beta ,$ we have

$$\mathrm { d } z = \mathrm { d } e ^ { \prime } e = 2 e ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } e = 2 e ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } \left( y - X \beta \right)$$
$$= - 2 e ^ { \prime } X d \beta = - 2 \left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } X d \beta .$$

(12)


## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. Consider a vector-valued function $f \left( t \right) = \left( \cos t , \sin t \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ $t \in \mathrm { R } .$ Show that
$f \left( 2 \pi \right) - f \left( 0 \right) = 0 ,$ and that $\| \mathrm { D } f \left( t \right) \| = 1$ for all t. Conclude that the
mean-value theorem does not hold for vector-valued functions.

2\. Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ and assume that $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is differ-
entiable at each point of $S .$ Let $c$ be a point of $S ,$ and $u$ a point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$
such that $c + t u \in S$ for all $t \in \left[ 0 , 1 \right] .$ Then for every vector a in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$
there exists a $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ such that

$$a ^ { \prime } \left[ f \left( c + u \right) - f \left( c \right) \right] = a ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c + \theta u \right) \right) u ,$$

where $\mathrm { D } f$ denotes the $m \times n$ matrix of partial derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i }$ $\left( i = \right.$
$1 , \ldots , m$ $\left. j = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$ This is the mean-value theorem for vector func-
tions.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="111" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

3\. Now formulate the correct mean-value theorem for the example in Ex-
ercise 1, and determine $\theta$ as a function of a.


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. See also Dieudonné (1969), Apostol (1974) and Binmore (1982). For a
discussion of the origins of the differential calculus, see Baron (1969).

§6. There even exist functions which are continuous everywhere without being
differentiable at any point. See Rudin (1964, p. 141) for an example of such a
function.

§14. For modified versions of the mean-value theorem, see Dieudonné (1969,
Section 8.5). Dieudonné regards the mean-value theorem as the most useful
theorem in analysis and argues (p. 148) that its real nature is exhibited by
writing it as an inequality, and not as an equality.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 6 The second differential


### 1 INTRODUCTION

In this chapter we discuss second-order partial derivatives, twice differentiabil-
ity and the second differential. Special attention is given to the relationship
between twice differentiability and second-order approximation. We define
the Hessian matrix (for vector functions) and find conditions for its (column)
symmetry. We also obtain a chain rule for Hessian matrices, and its analogue
for second differentials. Taylor's theorem for real-valued functions is proved.
Finally, we discuss very briefly higher-order differentials, and show how the
calculus of vector functions can be extended to matrix functions.


### 2 SECOND-ORDER PARTIAL DERIVATIVES

Consider a vector function $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ with values
in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$ Let $f _ { i } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$ be the i-th component function of $f ,$
and assume that $f _ { i }$ has partial derivatives not only at an interior point $c$ of
$S ,$ but also at each point of an open neighbourhood of c. Then we can also
consider their partial derivatives, i.e. we can consider the limit

$$\lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( D _ { j } f _ { i } \right) \left( c + t e _ { k } \right) - \left( D _ { j } f _ { i } \right) \left( c \right) } { t }$$
(1)

where $e _ { k }$ is the k-th unit vector in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ When this limit exists, it is called
the $\left( k , j \right) - t h$ second-order partial derivative of $f _ { i }$ at $c$ and is denoted $\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( c \right) .$
(Other notations include $\left[ \partial ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( x \right) / \partial x _ { k } \partial x _ { j } \right] _ { x = c }$ or even $\partial ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( c \right) / \partial x _ { k } \partial x _ { j }$ j.) Thus
$\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } f _ { i }$ is obtained by first partially differentiating $f _ { i }$ with respect to the j-th
variable, and then partially differentiating the resulting function $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i }$ with
respect to the k-th variable.


#### Example 1

<!-- PageNumber="113" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="114" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->

Let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined by the equation

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = x y ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y \right) .$$
(2)

The two (first-order) partial derivatives are given by the derivative

$$D \phi \left( x , y \right) = \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 3 } , 2 x ^ { 3 } y + 3 x y ^ { 2 } \right)$$
(3)

and so the four second-order partial derivatives are

$$\begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } _ { 1 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x , y \right) = 6 x y ^ { 2 } , \quad \mathrm { D } _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x , y \right) = 6 x ^ { 2 } y + 3 y ^ { 2 } \\ \mathrm { D } _ { 2 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x , y \right) = 6 x ^ { 2 } y + 3 y ^ { 2 } , \quad \mathrm { D } _ { 2 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x , y \right) = 2 x ^ { 3 } + 6 x y . \end{array} ,$$
,
(4)

Notice that in this example $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi = \mathrm { D } _ { 2 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi ,$ but this is not always the case.
The standard counter-example follows.


#### Example 2

Let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined by

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x y \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) , & \mathrm { i f } \left( x , y \right) \neq \left( 0 , 0 \right) , \\ 0 , & \mathrm { i f } \left( x , y \right) = \left( 0 , 0 \right) . \end{array} \right. ,$$
(5)

Here the function $\phi$ is differentiable on $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } ,$ the first-order partial deriva-
tives are continuous on $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 }$ (even differentiable, except at the origin), and the
second-order partial derivatives exist at every point of $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 }$ (and are continuous
except at the origin). But

$$\left( \mathrm { D } _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi \right) \left( 0 , 0 \right) = 1 , \quad \left( \mathrm { D } _ { 2 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi \right) \left( 0 , 0 \right) = - 1 .$$
(6)


### 3 THE HESSIAN MATRIX

Earlier we defined a matrix which contains all the first-order partial deriva-
tives. This is the Jacobian matrix. We now define a matrix (called the Hessian
matrix) which contains all second-order partial derivatives. We define this ma-
trix first for real-valued functions, then for vector functions.


#### Definition 1

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ be a real-valued function, and let c be a point of $S$
where the $n ^ { 2 }$ second-order partials $\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right)$ exist. Then we define the $n \times n$
Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ by

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } _ { 1 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) & \mathrm { D } _ { 2 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) & \ldots & \mathrm { D } _ { n 1 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) \\ \mathrm { D } _ { 1 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) & \mathrm { D } _ { 2 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) & \ldots & \mathrm { D } _ { n 2 } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \mathrm { D } _ { 1 n } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) & \mathrm { D } _ { 2 n } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) & \ldots & \mathrm { D } _ { n n } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) \end{array} \right) .$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="115" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] Twice differentiability and second-order approximation, $I$" -->

Note that the ij-th element of $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ is $\mathrm { D } _ { j i } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right)$ and not $\mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) .$


#### Definition 2

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ be a vector function, and let $c$ be a point of $S$
where the $m n ^ { 2 }$ second-order partials $\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( c \right)$ exist. Then we define the $m n \times n$
Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } f \left( c \right)$ by

$$\mathrm { H f } \left( c \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { H } f _ { 1 } \left( c \right) \\ \mathrm { H } f _ { 2 } \left( c \right) \\ \vdots \\ \mathrm { H } f _ { m } \left( c \right) \end{array} \right) .$$
(2)

Referring to the examples in the previous section, we have for the function
in Example 1:

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x , y \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 6 x y ^ { 2 } & 6 x ^ { 2 } y + 3 y ^ { 2 } \\ 6 x ^ { 2 } y + 3 y ^ { 2 } & 2 x ^ { 3 } + 6 x y \end{array} \right) ,$$
(3)

and for the function in Example 2:

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( 0 , 0 \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & - 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$
(4)

The first matrix is symmetric; the second is not. Sufficient conditions for
the symmetry of the Hessian matrix of a real-valued function are derived
in Section 7. The Hessian matrix of a vector function $f$ cannot, of course,
be symmetric if $m \geq 2 .$ We shall say that $\mathrm { H } f \left( c \right)$ is column symmetric if
the Hessian matrix of each of its component functions $f _ { i }$ $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$ is
symmetric at c.


### 4 TWICE DIFFERENTIABILITY AND SECOND-ORDER APPROXIMATION, $\mathrm { I }$

Consider a real-valued function $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ which is differentiable at a point
$c$ in $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ i.e. there exists a vector a, depending on $c$ but not on u, such
that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + a ^ { \prime } u + r \left( u \right) ,$$
(1)

where

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r \left( u \right) } { \| u \| } = 0 .$$
(2)

The vector $a ^ { \prime } ,$ if it exists, is of course the derivative $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) .$ Thus, differentia-
bility is defined by means of a first-order Taylor formula.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="116" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->

Suppose now that there exists a symmetric matrix $B ,$ depending on c but
not on $u ,$ such that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \left( \mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) \right) u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } B u + r \left( u \right) ,$$
(3)

where

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r \left( u \right) } { \| u \| ^ { 2 } } = 0 .$$
(4)

Equation (3) is called the second-order Taylor formula. The question naturally
arises whether it is appropriate to define twice differentiability as the existence
of a second-order Taylor formula. This question must be answered in the
negative. To see why, we consider the function $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by the
equation

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x ^ { 3 } + y ^ { 3 } & \left( x \text { and } y \text { rational } \right) , \\ 0 & \text { \left(otherwise\right) } . \end{array} \right. ,$$
(5)

The function $\phi$ is differentiable at $\left( 0 , 0 \right) ,$ but at no other point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { 2 } .$ The
partial derivative $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi$ is zero at the origin and at every point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { 2 }$ where $y$
is irrational; it is undefined elsewhere. Similarly, $D _ { 2 } \phi$ is zero at the origin and
at every point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 }$ where $x$ is irrational; it is undefined elsewhere. Hence,
neither of the partial derivatives is differentiable at any point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } .$ In spite
of this, a unique matrix $B$ exists (the null matrix), such that the second-order
Taylor formula (3) holds at $c = 0 .$ Surely we do not want to say that $\phi$ is twice
differentiable at a point, when its partial derivatives are not differentiable $\mathrm { a t }$
that point!


### 5 DEFINITION OF TWICE DIFFERENTIABILITY

So, the existence of a second-order Taylor formula at a point c is not sufficient,
in general, for all partial derivatives to be differentiable at c. Neither is it
necessary. That is, the fact that all partials are differentiable at c does not, in
general, imply a second-order Taylor formula at that point. We shall return
to this issue in Section 9.

Motivated by these facts, we define twice differentiability in such a way
that it implies both the existence of a second-order Taylor formula and dif-
ferentiability of all the partials.


### Definition 3

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and let $c$ be an interior
point of $S .$ If $f$ is differentiable in some n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ and each of the partial
derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { i }$ is differentiable at c, then we say that $f$ is twice differentiable
at c. If $f$ is twice differentiable at every point of an open subset $E$ of $S ,$ we

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="117" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Definition of twice differentiability" -->

say $f$ is twice differentiable on $E .$

In the one-dimensional case $\left( n = 1 \right) ,$ the requirement that the derivatives
$\mathrm { D } f _ { i }$ are differentiable at $c$ necessitates the existence of $\mathrm { D } f _ { i } \left( x \right)$ in a neighbour-
$h o o d$ of c, and hence the differentiability of $f$ itself in that neighbourhood.
But for $n \geq 2 ,$ the mere fact that each of the partials is differentiable at c,
necessitating as it does the continuity of each of the partials at c, involves
the differentiability of $f$ at c, but not necessarily in the neighbourhood of
that point. Hence the differentiability of each of the partials at c is necessary
but not sufficient, in general, for $f$ to be twice differentiable at c. However, if
the partials are differentiable not only at c, but also at each point of an open
neighbourhood of c, then $f$ is twice differentiable in that neighbourhood. This
follows from Theorems 5.4 and 5.7. In fact, we have the following theorem.


### Theorem 1

Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ Then $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is twice differentiable on
$S$ if and only if all partial derivatives are differentiable on $S .$

The non-trivial fact that twice differentiability implies (but is not implied
by) the existence of a second-order Taylor formula $w i l l$ be proved in Section
9.

Without difficulty we can prove the analogue of Theorems 5.1 and 5.2.


### Theorem 2

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ A function $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I R } ^ { m }$ is twice differentiable at
an interior point $c$ of $S$ if and only if each of its component functions is twice
differentiable at c.

Let us summarize. If $f$ is twice differentiable at c, then

(a) $f$ is differentiable (and continuous) at c, and in a suitable neighbourhood
$B \left( c \right) ,$

(b) the first-order partials exist in $B \left( c \right)$ and are differentiable (and contin-
uous) at $c ,$ and

(c) the second-order partials exist at c.

But

(d) the first-order partials need not be continuous at any point of $B \left( c \right) ,$
other than $c$ itself,

(e) the second-order partials need not be continuous at c, and

(f) the second-order partials need not exist at any point of $B \left( c \right) ,$ other than
$c$ itself.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="118" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->


### Exercise

1\. Show that the real-valued function $\phi : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $\phi \left( x \right) = | x | x$
is differentiable everywhere, but not twice differentiable at the origin.


### 6 THE SECOND DIFFERENTIAL

The second differential is simply the differential of the (first) differential,

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f = \mathrm { d } \left( \mathrm { d } f \right) .$$
(1)

Since $\mathrm { d } f$ is by definition a function of two sets of variables, say $x$ and u, the
expression $d \left( d f \right) ,$ with whose help the second differential $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f$ is determined,
requires some explanation. While performing the operation $\mathrm { d } \left( \mathrm { d } f \right)$ we always
consider $\mathrm { d } f$ as a function of $x$ alone by assuming $u$ to be constant; further-
more, the same value of $u$ is assumed for the first and second differential.

More formally, we propose the following definition.


#### Definition 4

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be twice differentiable at an interior point $c$ of $S \subset \mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ Let
$B \left( c \right)$ be an n-ball lying in $S$ such that $f$ is differentiable at every point in
$B \left( c \right) ,$ and let $g : B \left( c \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be defined by the equation

$$g \left( x \right) = \mathrm { d } f \left( x ; u \right) .$$
(2)

Then the differential of $g$ at $c$ with increment u, i.e. $\mathrm { d } g \left( c ; u \right) ,$ is called the
second differential of $f$ at $c$ (with increment u), and is denoted by $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) .$

We first settle the existence question.


### Theorem 3

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and let $c$ be an
interior point of $S .$ If each of the first-order partial derivatives is continuous
in some n-ball $B \left( c \right) ,$ and if each of the second-order partial derivatives exists
in $B \left( c \right)$ and is continuous at c, then $f$ is twice differentiable at $c$ and the
second differential of $f$ at $c$ exists.

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Theorem 5.7.
✷
☐

Let us now evaluate the second differential of a real-valued function $\phi :$
$S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ,$ where $S$ is a subset of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ On the assumption that $\phi$ is twice differ-
entiable at a point $c \in S ,$ we can define

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; u \right)$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="119" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] The second differential" -->

for every $x$ in a suitable n-ball $B \left( c \right) .$ Hence

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( x \right)$$
(4)

with partial derivatives

$$\mathrm { D } _ { i } \psi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(5)

and first differential (at u)

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( x ; u \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { i } \mathrm { D } _ { i } \psi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i , j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { i } u _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) .$$
(6)

By definition, the second differential of $\phi$ equals the first differential of $\psi ,$ so
that

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right) \right) u ,$$
(7)

where $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right)$ is the $n \times n$ Hessian matrix of $\phi$ at x.

Equation $\left( 7 \right)$ shows that, while the first differential of a real-valued function
$\phi$ is $a$ linear function of u, the second differential is a quadratic form in u.

We now consider the uniqueness question. We are given a real-valued func-
tion $\phi ,$ twice differentiable at c, and we evaluate its first and second differential
at c. We find

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = a ^ { \prime } u , \quad \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } B u .$$
(8)

Suppose that another vector $a ^ { * }$ and another matrix $B ^ { * }$ exist such that also

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = a ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } u , \quad \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } B ^ { * } u .$$
(9)

Then the uniqueness theorem for first differentials (Theorem 5.3) tells us that
$a = a ^ { * } .$ But a similar uniqueness result does not hold, in general, for second
differentials. We can only conclude that

$$B + B ^ { \prime } = B ^ { * } + B ^ { * \prime } . ,$$
(10)

because, putting $A = B - B ^ { * } ,$ the fact that $u ^ { \prime } A u = 0$ for every u does not
imply that $A$ is the null matrix, but only that $A$ is skew symmetric $\left( A ^ { \prime } = - A \right)$
see Theorem 1.2(c).

The symmetry of the Hessian matrix, which we will discuss in the next
section, is therefore of fundamental importance, because without it we could
not extract the Hessian matrix from the second differential.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="120" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->

Before we turn to proving this result, we note that the second differential
of a vector function $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { m } ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ is easily obtained from (7). In fact,
we have

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f _ { 1 } \left( c ; u \right) \\ \vdots \\ \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f _ { m } \left( c ; u \right) \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } f _ { 1 } \left( c \right) \right) u \\ \vdots \\ u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } f _ { m } \left( c \right) \right) u \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \left( I _ { m } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { H } f _ { 1 } \left( c \right) \\ \vdots \\ \mathrm { H } f _ { m } \left( c \right) \end{array} \right) u ,$$

(11)

so that, in view of the definition of the Hessian matrix of a vector function
(Definition 2 in Section 3),

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) = \left( I _ { m } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) u .$$
(12)


### 7 (COLUMN) SYMMETRY OF THE HESSIAN MATRIX

We have already seen (Section 3) that a Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi$ is not, in general,
symmetric. The next theorem gives us a sufficient condition for symmetry of
the Hessian matrix.


### Theorem 4

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $\phi$ is twice
differentiable at an interior point c of $S ,$ then the $n \times n$ Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi$ is
symmetric at $c ,$ i.e.

$$\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j k } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) \quad \left( k , j = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(1)

Proof. Let $B \left( c ; r \right)$ be an n-ball such that for any point $x$ in $B \left( c ; r \right)$ all partial
derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( x \right)$ exist. Let $A \left( r \right)$ be the open interval $\left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } r \sqrt { 2 } , \frac { 1 } { 2 } r \sqrt { 2 } \right) ,$ and
$t$ a point in $A \left( r \right) .$ We consider real-valued functions $\tau _ { i j } : \widetilde { A } \left( r \right) \stackrel { - } { \rightarrow } \mathrm { R }$ defined
by

$$\tau _ { i j } \left( \zeta \right) = \phi \left( c + t e _ { i } + \zeta e _ { j } \right) - \phi \left( c + \zeta e _ { j } \right) . ,$$
(2)

where $e _ { i }$ and $e _ { j }$ are unit vectors in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ The functions $\tau _ { i j }$ are differentiable at
each point of $A \left( r \right)$ with derivative

$$\left( \mathrm { D } \tau _ { i j } \right) \left( \zeta \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c + t e _ { i } + \zeta e _ { j } \right) - \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c + \zeta e _ { j } \right) .$$
(3)

Since $\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi$ is differentiable at c, we have the first-order Taylor formulae

$$\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c + t e _ { i } + \zeta e _ { j } \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c \right) + t \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) + \zeta \mathrm { D } _ { j j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) + R _ { i j } \left( t , \zeta \right)$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="121" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7] (Column) symmetry of the Hessian matrix" -->

and

$$\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c + \zeta e _ { j } \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c \right) + \zeta \mathrm { D } _ { j j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) + r _ { j } \left( \zeta \right) ,$$
(5)

where

$$\lim _ { \left( t , \zeta \right) \rightarrow \left( 0 , 0 \right) } \frac { R _ { i j } \left( t , \zeta \right) } { \left( t ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } = 0 \quad \lim _ { \zeta \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r _ { j } \left( \zeta \right) } { \zeta } = 0 .$$
(6)

Hence (3) becomes

$$\left( \mathrm { D } \tau _ { i j } \right) \left( \zeta \right) = t \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) + R _ { i j } \left( t , \zeta \right) - r _ { j } \left( \zeta \right) .$$
(7)

We now consider real-valued functions $\delta _ { i j } : A \left( r \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by

$$\delta _ { i j } \left( \zeta \right) = \tau _ { i j } \left( \zeta \right) - \tau _ { i j } \left( 0 \right) .$$
(8)

By the one-dimensional mean-value theorem we have

$$\delta _ { i j } \left( \zeta \right) = \zeta \left( \mathrm { D } \tau _ { i j } \right) \left( \theta _ { i j } \zeta \right)$$
(9)

for some $\theta _ { i j } \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ (Of course, the point $\theta _ { i j }$ depends on the value of $\zeta$ and
on the function $\delta _ { i j }$ Using (7) we thus obtain

$$\delta _ { i j } \left( \zeta \right) = \zeta t \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) + \zeta \left[ R _ { i j } \left( t , \theta _ { i j } \zeta \right) - r _ { j } \left( \theta _ { i j } \zeta \right) \right] .$$
(10)

Now, since $\delta _ { i j } \left( t \right) = \delta _ { j i } \left( t \right) ,$ it follows that

$$\mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) - \mathrm { D } _ { j i } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) = \frac { R _ { j i } \left( t , \theta _ { j i } t \right) - R _ { i j } \left( t , \theta _ { i j } t \right) + r _ { j } \left( \theta _ { i j } t \right) - r _ { i } \left( \theta _ { j i } t \right) } { t }$$
(11)

for some $\theta _ { i j }$ and $\theta _ { j i }$ in the interval $\left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ The left side of (11) is independent
of t; the right side tends to 0 with t, by (6). Hence $\mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j i } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) .$
✷
☐

Note. The requirement in Theorem 4 that $\phi$ is twice differentiable at $c$ is
in fact stronger than necessary. The reader may verify that in the proof we
have merely used the fact that each of the partial derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi$ is differen-
tiable at c.

The generalization of Theorem 4 to vector functions is simple.


### Theorem 5

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $f$ is twice
differentiable at an interior point c of $S ,$ then the $m n \times n$ Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } f$
is column symmetric at c, i.e.

$$\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( c \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j k } ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( c \right) \quad \left( k , j = 1 , \ldots , n ; i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$$
(12)

The column symmetry of $\mathrm { H } f \left( c \right)$ is, as we recall from Section 3, equivalent to
the symmetry of each of the matrices $\mathrm { H } f _ { i } \left( c \right) ,$ i.e. of the Hessian matrices of
the component functions $f _ { i } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="122" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->


### 8 THE SECOND IDENTIFICATION THEOREM

We now have all the ingredients for the following theorem which states that
once we know the second differential, the Hessian matrix is uniquely deter-
mined (and vice versa).


#### Theorem 6 (second identification theorem for real-valued functions)

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and twice
differentiable at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Let $u$ be a real $n \times 1$ vector. Then

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) \right) u ,$$
(1)

where $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ is the $n \times n$ symmetric Hessian matrix of $\phi$ with elements $\mathrm { D } _ { j i } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) .$
Furthermore, if $B \left( c \right)$ is a matrix such that

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } B \left( c \right) u$$
(2)

for a real $n \times 1$ vector $u ,$ then

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ B \left( c \right) + B \left( c \right) ^ { \prime } \right] .$$
(3)

In order to state the second identification theorem for vector functions, of
which Theorem 6 is a special case, we require some more notation.


#### Definition 5

Let $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { m }$ be square $n \times n$ matrices, and let

$$A = \left( A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { m } \right) .$$
(4)

Then we define the block-vec of $A$ as the $m n \times n$ matrix

$$A _ { v } = \left( \begin{array}{} A _ { 1 } \\ A _ { 2 } \\ \vdots \\ A _ { m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

As a result of Definition 5, if $B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots , B _ { m }$ are square matrices, then

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { 1 } \\ B _ { 2 } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m } \end{array} \right) \Leftrightarrow \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) v = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ B _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$
(6)


#### Theorem 7 (second identification theorem)

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a vector function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and twice

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="123" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Twice differentiability and second-order approximation, $I I$" -->

differentiable at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Let $u$ be a real $n \times 1$ vector. Then

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) = \left( I _ { m } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) u ,$$
(7)

where $\mathrm { H } f \left( c \right)$ is the $m n \times n$ column symmetric Hessian matrix of $f$ with ele-
ments $\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } f _ { i } \left( c \right) .$ Furthermore, if $B \left( c \right)$ is a matrix such that

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) = \left( I _ { m } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) B \left( c \right) u$$
(8)

for all real $n \times 1$ vectors $u ,$ then

$$\mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ B \left( c \right) + \left( B \left( c \right) ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } \right] .$$
(9)


### 9 TWICE DIFFERENTIABILITY AND SECOND-ORDER APPROXIMATION, $I I$

In Section 5 the definition of twice differentiability was motivated, in part, by
the claim that it implies the existence of a second-order Taylor formula. Let
us now prove this assertion.


### Theorem 8

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ Let $c$ be an interior
point of $S ,$ and let $B \left( c ; r \right)$ be an n-ball lying in $S .$ Let $u$ be a point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$
with $\| u \| < r ,$ so that $c + u \in B \left( c ; r \right) .$ If $f$ is twice differentiable at c, then

$$f \left( c + u \right) = f \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) + r _ { c } \left( u \right) ,$$
(1)

where

$$\lim _ { u \rightarrow 0 } \frac { r _ { c } \left( u \right) } { \| u \| ^ { 2 } } = 0 .$$
(2)

Proof. It suffices to consider the case $m = 1$ $\left( \mathrm { w h y ? } \right) ,$ in which case the vec-
tor function $f$ specializes to a real-valued function $\phi .$ Let $M = \left( m _ { i j } \right)$ be a
symmetric $n \times n$ matrix, depending on $c$ and $u ,$ such that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } M u .$$
(3)

Since $\phi$ is twice differentiable at c, there exists an n-ball $B \left( c ; \rho \right) \subset B \left( c ; r \right)$ such
that $\phi$ is differentiable at each point of $B \left( c ; \rho \right) .$ Let $A \left( \rho \right) = \left\{ x : x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } , \| x \| < \right.$
$\left. \rho \right\} ,$ and define a real-valued function $\psi : A \left( \rho \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ by the equation

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \phi \left( c + x \right) - \phi \left( c \right) - \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; x \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x ^ { \prime } M x .$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="124" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->

Note that $M$ depends on $u$ (and c), but not on x. Then

$$\psi \left( 0 \right) = \psi \left( u \right) = 0 .$$
(5)

Also, since $\phi$ is differentiable in $B \left( c ; \rho \right) ,$ $\psi$ is differentiable in $A \left( \rho \right) ,$ so that, by
the mean-value theorem (Theorem 5.10),

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( \theta u ; u \right) = 0$$
(6)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Now, since each $\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi$ is differentiable at c, we have the
first-order Taylor formula

$$\mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c + x \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c \right) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) + R _ { j } \left( x \right) ,$$
(7)

where

$$R _ { j } \left( x \right) / \| x \| \rightarrow 0 \quad \mathrm { a s } x \rightarrow 0 .$$
(8)

The partial derivatives of $\psi$ are thus given by

$$\mathrm { D } _ { j } \psi \left( x \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c + x \right) - \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( c \right) - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } m _ { i j }$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) - m _ { i j } \right) + R _ { j } \left( x \right) ,$$

(9)

using (4) and (7). Hence, by (6),

$$0 = \mathrm { d } \psi \left( \theta u ; u \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } \psi \left( \theta u \right)$$
$$= \theta \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { i } u _ { j } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) - m _ { i j } \right) + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } R _ { j } \left( \theta u \right)$$
$$= \theta \left( \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) - u ^ { \prime } M u \right) + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } R _ { j } \left( \theta u \right) ,$$

(10)

so that

$$u ^ { \prime } M u = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \left( 1 / \theta \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { j } R _ { j } \left( \theta u \right) .$$
(11)

Substituting (11) in (3) and noting that

$$\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { u _ { j } R _ { j } \left( \theta u \right) } { \theta \| u \| ^ { 2 } } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { u _ { j } } { \| u \| } \cdot \frac { R _ { j } \left( \theta u \right) } { \| \theta u \| } \rightarrow 0 \quad \mathrm { a s } u \rightarrow 0 ,$$
(12)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="125" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Chain rule for Hessian matrices" -->

using (8), completes the proof.
☐
✷

The example in Section 4 shows that the existence of a second-order Taylor
formula at a point does not imply, in general, twice differentiability there (in
fact, not even differentiability of all partial derivatives).

It is worth remarking that, if in Theorem 8 we replace the requirement
that $f$ is twice differentiable at $c$ by the weaker condition that all first-order
partials of $f$ are differentiable at c, the theorem remains valid for $n = 1$
(trivially) and $n =$ 2, but not, in general, for $n > 3 .$


#### Exercise

1\. Prove Theorem 8 for $n = 2 ,$ assuming that all first-order partials of $f$ are
differentiable at c, but without assuming that $f$ is twice differentiable
at $c .$


### 10 CHAIN RULE FOR HESSIAN MATRICES

In one dimension the first and second derivatives of the composite function
$h = g \circ f ,$ defined by the equation

$$\left( g \circ f \right) \left( x \right) = g \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ,$$
(1)

can be expressed in terms of the first and second derivatives of $g$ and $f$ as
follows:

$$h ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) = g ^ { \prime } \left( f \left( c \right) \right) \cdot f ^ { \prime } \left( c \right)$$
(2)

and

$$h ^ { \prime \prime } \left( c \right) = g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( f \left( c \right) \right) \cdot \left( f ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) \right) ^ { 2 } + g ^ { \prime } \left( f \left( c \right) \right) \cdot f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( c \right) .$$
(3)

The following theorem generalizes Equation (3) to vector functions of several
variables.


#### Theorem 9 (chain rule for Hessian matrices)

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and assume that $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is twice differentiable
at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ Let $T$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that $f \left( x \right) \in T$ for
all $x \in S ,$ and assume that $g : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p }$ is twice differentiable at an interior
point $b = f \left( c \right)$ of $T .$ Then the composite function $h : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p }$ defined by

$$h \left( x \right) = g \left( f \left( x \right) \right)$$
(4)

is twice differentiable at c, and

$$\mathrm { H } h \left( c \right) = \left( I _ { p } \otimes \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } g \left( b \right) \right) \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) + \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \otimes I _ { n } \right) \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) .$$
(5)

Proof. Since $g$ is twice differentiable at $b ,$ it is differentiable in some m-ball

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="126" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->

$B _ { m } \left( b \right) .$ Also, since $f$ is twice differentiable at c, we can choose an n-ball $B _ { n } \left( c \right)$
such that $f$ is differentiable in $B _ { n } \left( c \right) ,$ and $f \left( x \right) \in B _ { m } \left( b \right)$ for all $x \in B _ { n } \left( c \right) .$
Hence, by Theorem 5.8, $h$ is differentiable in $B _ { n } \left( c \right) .$ Further, since the partials
$\mathrm { D } _ { j } h _ { i }$ given by

$$\mathrm { D } _ { j } h _ { i } \left( x \right) = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \left( \mathrm { D } _ { s } g _ { i } \right) \left( f \left( x \right) \right) \right) \left( \left( \mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { s } \right) \left( x \right) \right)$$
(6)

are differentiable at $c$ (because the partials $D _ { s } g _ { i }$ are differentiable at $b$ and
the partials $\mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { s }$ are differentiable at c), the composite function $h$ $i \mathrm { i s }$ twice
differentiable at c.

The second-order partials of $h _ { i }$ evaluated at c are then given by

$$\mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } h _ { i } \left( c \right) = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { t s } ^ { 2 } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { k } f _ { t } \left( c \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { j } f _ { s } \left( c \right) \right)$$
$$+ \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { s } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { k j } ^ { 2 } f _ { s } \left( c \right) \right) .$$

Thus, the Hessian matrix of the i-th component function $h _ { i }$ is

$$\mathrm { H } h _ { i } \left( c \right) = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { t s } ^ { 2 } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f _ { t } \left( c \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { D } f _ { s } \left( c \right) \right)$$
$$+ \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { s } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f _ { s } \left( c \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) + \left( \left( \mathrm { D } g _ { i } \left( b \right) \right) \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) ,$$

(7)

(8)

and the result follows.

✷
☐


### 11 THE ANALOGUE FOR SECOND DIFFERENTIALS

The chain rule for Hessian matrices expresses the second-order partial deriva-
tives of the composite function $h = g \circ f$ in terms of the first-order and
second-order partial derivatives of g and $f .$ The next theorem expresses the
second differential of $h$ in terms of the first and second differentials of $g$ and
$f .$


#### Theorem 10

If $f$ is twice differentiable at c and $g$ is twice differentiable at $b = f \left( c \right) ,$ then
the second differential of the composite function $h = g \circ f$ is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } h \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) + \mathrm { d } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) \right)$$
(1)

for every $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="127" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] The analogue for second differentials" -->

Proof. By Theorems 7 and 9, we have

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } h \left( c ; u \right) = \left( I _ { p } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } h \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \left( I _ { p } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( I _ { p } \otimes \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } g \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$+ \left( I _ { p } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) u .$$

The first term at the right hand side of (2) is

$$\left( I _ { p } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( I _ { p } \otimes \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } g \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \quad \left( I _ { p } \otimes \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) u \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } g \left( b \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \left( I _ { p } \otimes \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } g \left( b \right) \right) \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) .$$

The second term is

$$\left( I _ { p } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \quad \left( \mathrm { D } g \left( b \right) \right) \left( I _ { m } \otimes u ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } f \left( c \right) \right) u$$
$$= \mathrm { d } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) .$$

The result follows.

✷
☐
(4)
