The most important lesson to be learned from Theorem 10 is that the
second differential does $n o t ,$ in general, satisfy Cauchy's rule of invariance. By
this we mean that, while the first differential of a composite function satisfies

$$\mathrm { d } h \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) ,$$
(5)

by Theorem 5.9, it is not true, in general, that

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } h \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right) ,$$
(6)

unless $f$ is an affine function. (A function $f$ is called affine if $f \left( x \right) = A x + b$
for some matrix $A$ and vector b.) This case is of sufficient importance to state
as a separate theorem.


#### Theorem 11

If $f$ is an affine function and $g$ is twice differentiable at $b = f \left( c \right) ,$ then the
second differential of the composite function $h = g \circ f$ is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } h \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } g \left( b ; \mathrm { d } f \left( c ; u \right) \right)$$
(7)

for every $u$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$

Proof. Since $f$ is affine, $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( c ; u \right) = 0 .$ The result then follows from Theorem

✷
☐
10.

(2)

(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="128" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->


### 12 TAYLOR'S THEOREM FOR REAL-VALUED FUNCTIONS

Let $\phi$ be a real-valued function defined on a subset $S$ of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and let $c$ be an
interior point of $S .$ If $\phi$ is continuous at c, then

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + R \left( u \right) ,$$
(1)

and the error $R \left( u \right)$ made in this approximation will tend to zero as $u \rightarrow 0 .$

If we make the stronger assumption that $\phi$ is differentiable in a neighbour-
$h o o d$ of c, we obtain, by the mean-value theorem,

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right)$$
(2)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ This provides an explicit and very useful expression for
the error $R \left( u \right)$ in (1).

If $\phi$ is differentiable at c, we also have the first-order Taylor formula

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( u \right) ,$$
(3)

where $r \left( u \right) / \| u \|$ tends to zero as $u \rightarrow 0 .$ Naturally the question arises whether
it is possible to obtain an explicit form for the error $r \left( u \right) .$ The following result
(known as Taylor's theorem) answers this question.


#### Theorem 12 (Taylor)

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined and twice differentiable on
an open set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Let $c$ be a point of $S ,$ and $u$ a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ such that
$c + t u \in S$ for all $t \in \left[ 0 , 1 \right] .$ Then

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right)$$
(4)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$

Proof. As in the proof of the mean-value theorem (Theorem 5.10), we consider
a real-valued function $\psi : \left[ 0 , 1 \right] \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ defined by

$$\psi \left( t \right) = \phi \left( c + t u \right) .$$
(5)

The hypothesis of the theorem implies that $\psi$ is twice differentiable at each
point in $\left( 0 , 1 \right)$ with

$$\mathrm { D } \psi \left( t \right) = \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + t u ; u \right)$$
(6)

and

$$\mathrm { D } ^ { 2 } \psi \left( t \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c + t u ; u \right) .$$
(7)

By the one-dimensional Taylor theorem we have

$$\psi \left( 1 \right) = \psi \left( 0 \right) + \mathrm { D } \psi \left( 0 \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { D } ^ { 2 } \psi \left( \theta \right)$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="129" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Higher-order differentials" -->

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Hence

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right) ,$$
(9)

thus completing the proof.

✷

☐


### 13 HIGHER-ORDER DIFFERENTIALS

Higher-order differentials are defined recursively. Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a func-
tion defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and $\det$ be an interior point of $S .$ If $f$ is $n \quad - \quad 1$
times differentiable in some n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ and each of the $\left( n - 1 \right) t h - o r d e r$ partial
derivatives is differentiable at c, then we say that $f$ is $n$ times differentiable
at $c .$

Now consider the function $g : B \left( c \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ defined by the equation

$$g \left( x \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { n - 1 } f \left( x ; u \right) .$$
(1)

Then we define the nth-order differential of $f$ at $c$ as

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { n } f \left( c ; u \right) = \mathrm { d } g \left( c ; u \right) .$$
(2)

We note from this definition that if $f$ has an nth-order differential at c, then
$f$ itself has all the differentials up to the $\left( n \quad - \quad 1 \right) t h$ inclusive, not only at c,
but also in a neighbourhood of c.

Third- and higher-order differentials will play no role of significance in this
book.


### 14 MATRIX FUNCTIONS

As in the previous chapter, the extension to matrix functions is straightfor-
ward. Consider a matrix function $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ defined on a set $S$ in
$\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times q } .$ Corresponding to the matrix function $F$ we define a vector function
$f : \mathrm { v e c } S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m p }$ by

$$f \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) = \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) .$$
(1)

In Section 5.15 we defined the Jacobian matrix of $F$ at $C$ as the $m p \times n q$
matrix

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( C \right) = \mathrm { D } f \left( \mathrm { v e c } C \right) .$$
(2)

We now define the Hessian matrix of $F$ at $C$ as

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( C \right) = \mathrm { H } f \left( \mathrm { v e c } C \right) .$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="130" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The second differential [Ch. 6" -->

This is an $m n p q \times n q$ matrix stacking the Hessian matrices of the mp compo-
nent functions $F _ { s t }$ as follows:

HF(C) =

.
HFm1(C)
.
HF1p(C)
HF11(C)

.
(4)

.

HFmp(C)

The matrices $\mathrm { H } F _ { s t } \left( C \right)$ are $n q \times n q ,$ and the ij-th element of $\mathrm { H } F _ { s t } \left( C \right)$ is
the second-order partial derivative of $F _ { s t } \left( X \right)$ with respect to the elements
☒
of vec $X ,$ evaluated at $X = C .$ That is, $\left( \mathrm { H } \dot { F } _ { s t } \left( C \right) \right) _ { i j } = \mathrm { D } _ { j i } ^ { 2 } F _ { s t } \left( C \right) .$

The second differential of $F \quad i s \quad t h e$ differential of the first differential:

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F = \mathrm { d } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) .$$
(5)

More precisely, if we let

$$G \left( X \right) = \mathrm { d } F \left( X ; U \right)$$ ☒
(6)

for all $X$ in some ball $B \left( C \right) ,$ then

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F \left( C ; U \right) = \mathrm { d } G \left( C ; U \right) .$$
(7)

Since the differentials of $F$ and $f$ are related by

$$\mathrm { v e c d } F \left( C ; U \right) = \mathrm { d } f \left( \mathrm { v e c } C ; \mathrm { v e c } U \right) ,$$
(8)

the second differentials are related by

$$\mathrm { v e c } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F \left( C ; U \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } f \left( \mathrm { v e c } C ; \mathrm { v e c } U \right) .$$
(9)

The following two theorems are now straightforward generalizations of
Theorems 7 and 10.


#### Theorem 13 (second identification theorem for matrix functions)

Let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be a matrix function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times q } ,$ and
twice differentiable at an interior point $C$ of $S .$ Then

$$\mathrm { v e c d } ^ { 2 } F \left( C ; U \right) = \left( I _ { m p } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } U \right) ^ { \prime } B \left( C \right) \mathrm { v e c } U$$
(10)

for all $U \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q }$ if and only if

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( C \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ B \left( C \right) + \left( B \left( C \right) ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } \right] .$$
(11)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="131" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

Note. Recall the notation $\left( B \left( C \right) ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v }$ from Definition 5 in Section 8.


#### Theorem 14

If $F$ is twice differentiable at $C$ and $G$ is twice differentiable at $B = F \left( C \right) ,$
then the second differential of the composite function $H = G \circ F$ is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } H \left( C ; U \right) = \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } G \left( B ; \mathrm { d } F \left( C ; U \right) \right) + \mathrm { d } G \left( B ; \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F \left( C ; U \right) \right)$$
(12)

for every $U$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times q } .$


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§9. The fact that, for $n = 2 ,$ the requirement that $f$ is twice differentiable at $c$
can be replaced by the weaker condition that all first-order partial derivatives
are differentiable at c, is proved in Young (1910, Section 23).

## CHAPTER 7 Static optimization


### 1 INTRODUCTION

Static optimization theory is concerned with finding those points (if any) at
which a real-valued function $\phi ,$ defined on a subset $S$ of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ has a minimum
or a maximum.

Two types of problems will be investigated in this chapter:

i) Unconstrained optimization (Sections 2-10) is concerned with the prob-
lem

$$\min \left( \max \right) \phi \left( x \right) ,$$
(1)
xES

where the point at which the extremum occurs is an interior point of
$S .$

(ii) Optimization subject $t o$ constraints (Sections $\left. 1 1 - 1 6 \right)$ is concerned with
the problem of optimizing $\phi$ subject to $m$ non-linear equality constraints,
say $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = 0 , \ldots , g _ { m } \left( x \right) = 0 .$ Letting $g = \left( g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , \ldots , g _ { m } \right) ^ { \prime }$ and

$$\Gamma = \left\{ x : x \in S , g \left( x \right) = 0 \right\} ,$$
(2)

the problem can be written as

$$\min \left( \max \right) \phi \left( x \right) ,$$
(3)

xET

or, equivalently, as

$$\min _ { x \in S } \left( \max \right) \quad \phi \left( x \right)$$
$$s u b j e c t \quad t o$$
(4)
(5)

We shall not deal with inequality constraints.

### 2 UNCONSTRAINED OPTIMIZATION

In Sections 2-10 we wish to show how the one-dimensional theory of maxima
and minima of differentiable functions generalizes to functions of more than
one variable. We start with some definitions.

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and let
c be a point of $S .$ We say that $\phi$ has a local minimum at $c$ if there exists an
n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ such that

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in S \cap B \left( c \right) .$$
(1)

$\phi$ has a strict local minimum at c if we can choose $B \left( c \right)$ such that

$$\phi \left( x \right) > \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in S \cap B \left( c \right) , x \neq c .$$
(2)

$\phi$ has an absolute minimum at $c$ if

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in S .$$
(3)

$\phi$ has a strict absolute minimum at $c$ if

$$\phi \left( x \right) > \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in S , x \neq c .$$
(4)

The point $c$ at which the minimum is attained is called a (strict) local mini-
mum point for $\phi ,$ or a (strict) absolute minimum point for $\phi$ on $S ,$ depending
on the nature of the minimum.

If $\phi$ has a minimum at c, then the function $\psi \equiv - \phi$ has a maximum
at c. Each maximization problem can thus be converted to a minimization
problem (and vice versa). For this reason we lose no generality by treating
minimization problems only.

If c is an interior point of $S ,$ and $\phi$ is differentiable at c, then we say that
$c$ is a critical point (stationary point) of $\phi$ if

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } u \mathrm { i n } \mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$$
(5)

The function value $\phi \left( c \right)$ is then called the critical value of $\phi$ at c.

$A$ critical point is called a saddle point if every n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ contains points
$x$ such that $\phi \left( x \right) > \phi \left( c \right)$ and other points such that $\phi \left( x \right) < \phi \left( c \right) .$ In other
words, a saddle point is a critical point which is neither a local minimum
point nor a local maximum point. Figure 1 illustrates some of these concepts.
The function $\phi$ is defined and continuous at $\left[ 0 , 5 \right] .$ It has a strict absolute
minimum at $x = 0 ,$ and a (not strict) absolute maximum at $x =$ 1. There are
strict local minima at $x = 2$ and $x = 5 ,$ and a strict local maximum at $x =$ 3.
At $x = 4$ the derivative $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is zero, but this is not an extremum point of $\phi$ it
is a saddle point.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="135" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] The existence of absolute extrema" -->

### 3 THE EXISTENCE OF ABSOLUTE EXTREMA

In the example of Figure 1 the function $\phi$ is continuous on the compact interval
$\left[ 0 , 5 \right] ,$ and has an absolute minimum (at $\left. x = 0 \right)$ and an absolute maximum (at
$x =$ 1). That this is typical for continuous functions on compact sets is shown
by the following fundamental result.


#### Theorem 1 (Weierstrass)

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a compact set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$
If $\phi$ is continuous on $S ,$ then $\phi$ attains its maximum and minimum values on
$S .$ Thus, there exist points $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 }$ in $S$ such that

$$\phi \left( c _ { 1 } \right) \leq \phi \left( x \right) \leq \phi \left( c _ { 2 } \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in S .$$
(1)

Note. The Weierstrass theorem is an existence theorem. It tells us that certain
conditions are sufficient to ensure the existence of extrema. The theorem does
not tell us how to find these extrema.

Proof. By Theorem 4.9, $\phi$ is bounded on $S .$ Hence the set

$$M = \left\{ m \in \mathrm { R } , \phi \left( x \right) > m \text { for all } x \in S \right\}$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="136" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

is not empty; indeed, $M$ contains infinitely many points. Let

$$m _ { 0 } = \sup M .$$
(3)

Then,

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq m _ { 0 } \quad \text { for all } x \in S .$$
(4)

Now suppose that $\phi$ does not attain its infimum on $S .$ Then

$$\phi \left( x \right) > m _ { 0 } \quad \text { for all } x \in S$$
(5)

and the real-valued function $\psi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \left( \phi \left( x \right) - m _ { 0 } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
(6)

is continuous (and positive) on $S .$ Again by Theorem 4.9, $\psi$ is bounded on $S ,$
say by $\mu .$ Thus

$$\psi \left( x \right) \leq \mu \quad \text { for all } x \in S ,$$
(7)

that is,

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq m _ { 0 } + 1 / \mu \quad \text { for all } x \in S .$$
(8)

It follows that $m _ { 0 } + 1 / \left( 2 \mu \right)$ is an element of $M .$ But this is impossible, because
no element of $M$ can exceed $m _ { 0 } ,$ the supremum of $M .$ Hence, $\phi$ attains its
minimum (and similarly its maximum).
✷
☐


## Exercises

1\. The Weierstrass theorem is not, in general, correct if we drop any of the
conditions, as the following three counter-examples demonstrate.

(a) $\phi \left( x \right) = x , x \in \left( - 1 , 1 \right) , \phi \left( - 1 \right) = \phi \left( 1 \right) = 0 ,$

(b) $\phi \left( x \right) = x$ $x \in \left( - \infty , \infty \right) ,$

(c) $\phi \left( x \right) = x / \left( 1 - | x | \right) , x \in \left( - 1 , 1 \right) .$

2\. Consider the real-valued function $\phi : \left( 0 , \infty \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} x , & x \in \left( 0 , 2 \right] \\ 1 , & x \in \left( 2 , \infty \right) . \end{array} \right.$$

The set $\left( 0 , \infty \right)$ is neither bounded nor closed, and the function $\phi$ is not
continuous on $\left( 0 , \infty \right) .$ Nevertheless, $\phi$ attains its maximum on $\left( 0 , \infty \right) .$
This shows that none of the conditions of the Weierstrass theorem are
necessary.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="137" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] Necessary conditions for a local minimum" -->


## 4 NECESSARY CONDITIONS FOR A LOCAL MINIMUM

In the one-dimensional case, if a real-valued function $\phi ,$ defined on an interval
$\left( a , b \right) ,$ has a local minimum at an interior point $c$ of $\left( a , b \right) ,$ and if $\phi$ has a deriva-
tive at c, then $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right)$ must be zero. This result, which relates zero derivatives
and local extrema at interior points, can be generalized to the multivariable
case as follows.


### Theorem 2

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and assume
that $\phi$ has a local minimum at an interior point $c$ of $S .$ If $\phi$ is differentiable
at c, then

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = 0$$
(1)

for every $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $\phi$ is twice differentiable at c, then also

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) \geq 0$$
(2)

for every $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$

Note 1. If $\phi$ has a local maximum (rather than a minimum) at c, then condi-
tion (2) is replaced by $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) \leq 0$ for every $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$

Note 2. The necessary conditions (1) and (2) are of course equivalent to the
conditions

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { 2 } } = \cdots = \frac { \partial \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { n } } = 0$$
(3)

and

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) = \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \right) \quad \text { is positive semidefinite } .$$
(4)

Note 3. The example $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { 3 }$ shows (at $\left. x = 0 \right)$ that the converse of Theorem
2 is not true. The example $\phi \left( x \right) = | x |$ shows (again at $\left. x = 0 \right)$ that $\phi$ can have
a local extremum without the derivative being zero.

Proof. Since $\phi$ has a local minimum at an interior point c, there exists an
n-ball $B \left( c ; \delta \right) \subset S$ such that

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in B \left( c ; \delta \right) .$$
(5)

Let u be $a$ point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ $u \neq 0$ and choose $\epsilon > 0$ such that $c + \epsilon u \in B \left( c , \delta \right) .$
From the definition of differentiability, we have for every $| t | < \epsilon ,$

$$\phi \left( c + t u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + t d \phi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( t \right) ,$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="138" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

where $r \left( t \right) / t \rightarrow 0$ ast $t \rightarrow 0$ 0. Therefore

$$t d \phi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( t \right) \geq 0 .$$
(7)

Replacing $t$ by $- t$ in (7), we obtain

$$- r \left( t \right) \leq t d \phi \left( c ; u \right) \leq r \left( - t \right) .$$
(8)

Dividing by $t \neq 0 ,$ and letting $t \rightarrow 0 ,$ we find $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = 0$ for all $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$
This establishes the first part of the theorem.

To prove the second part, assume that $\phi$ is twice differentiable at c. Then,
by the second-order Taylor formula (Theorem 6.8),

$$\phi \left( c + t u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + t \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + R \left( t \right) ,$$
(9)

where $R \left( t \right) / t ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 0 .$ Therefore

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + R \left( t \right) \geq 0 .$$
(10)

Dividing by $t ^ { 2 } \neq 0 ,$ and letting $t \rightarrow 0 ,$ we find $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) \geq 0$ for all $u$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$
☐
✷


## Exercises

1\. Find the extreme $\mathrm { v a l u e } \left( \mathrm { s } \right)$ of the following real-valued functions defined
on $\mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } ,$ and determine whether they are minima or maxima:

(i) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 2 } + x y + 2 y ^ { 2 } + 3 ,$

(ii) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = - x ^ { 2 } + x y - y ^ { 2 } + 2 x + y ,$

(iii) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left( x - y + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } .$

2\. Answer the same questions as above for the following real-valued func-
tions defined for $0 \leq x \leq 2 ,$ $0 \leq y \leq 1 :$

(i) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 3 } + 8 y ^ { 3 } - 9 x y + 1 ,$

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left( x - 2 \right) \left( y - 1 \right) \exp \left( x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } y ^ { 2 } - x - y + 1 \right) .$$
(ii)


## 5 SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS FOR A LOCAL MINIMUM: FIRST-DERIVATIVE TEST

In the one-dimensional case, a sufficient condition for a differentiable function
$\phi$ to have a minimum at an interior point $c$ is that $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( c \right) = 0$ and that there
exists an interval $\left( a , b \right)$ containing $c$ such that $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) < 0$ in $\left( a , c \right)$ and $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) > 0$
in $\left( c , b \right) .$ (These conditions are not necessary, see Exercise 1.)

The multivariable generalization is as follows.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="139" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Sufficient conditions for a local minimum: first-derivative $t e s t$" -->


### Theorem 3 (the first-derivative test)

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$ and let $c$
be an interior point of $S .$ If $\phi$ is differentiable in some n-ball $B \left( c \right) ,$ and

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; x - c \right) \geq 0$$
(1)

for every $x$ in $B \left( c \right) ,$ then $\phi$ has a local minimum at c. Moreover, if the in-
equality in (1) is strict for every $x$ in $B \left( c \right) ,$ $x \neq c ,$ then $\phi$ has a strict local
minimum at c.

Proof. Let $u \neq 0$ be a point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ such that $c + u \in B \left( c \right) .$ Then, by the
mean-value theorem for real-valued functions,

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right)$$
(2)

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Hence

$$\theta \left( \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) \right) = \theta \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; \theta u \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; c + \theta u - c \right) \geq 0 .$$

(3)

Since $\theta > 0 ,$ it follows that $\phi \left( c + u \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) .$ This proves the first part of the
theorem; the second part is proved in the same way.
☐
✷


### Example 1

Let $A$ be a positive definite (hence symmetric) $n \times n$ matrix, and let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { n } \rightarrow$
$\mathrm { R }$ be defined by $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } A x .$ We find

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; u \right) = 2 x ^ { \prime } A u ,$$
(4)

and since $A \quad i s$ non-singular, the only critical point is the origin $x =$ 0. To
prove that this is a local minimum point, we compute

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; x - 0 \right) = 2 x ^ { \prime } A x > 0$$
(5)

for all $x \neq 0 .$ Hence $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at $x = 0 .$ (In fact, $\phi$ has a
strict absolute minimum at $x =$ 0.) In this example the function $\phi$ is strictly
convex on $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ so that the condition of Theorem 3 is automatically fulfilled.
We shall explore this in more detail in Section 7.


### Exercises

1\. Consider the function $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 } \left[ 2 + \sin \left( 1 / x \right) \right]$ when $x \neq 0 ,$ and $\phi \left( 0 \right) = 0 .$
The function $\phi$ clearly has an absolute minimum at $x =$ 0. Show that
the derivative is $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) = 4 x + 2 x \sin \left( 1 / x \right) - \cos \left( 1 / x \right)$ when $x \neq 0 ,$ and
$\phi ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) = 0 .$ Show further that we can find values of $x$ arbitrarily close to
the origin such that $x \phi ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) < 0 .$ Conclude that the converse of Theorem
3 is, in general, not true.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="140" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

2\. Consider the function $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ given by $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 2 } + \left( 1 + x \right) ^ { 3 } y ^ { 2 } .$
Prove that it has one local minimum (at the origin), no other critical
points and no absolute minimum.


## 6 SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS FOR A LOCAL MINIMUM: SECOND-DERIVATIVE TEST

Another test for local extrema is based on the Hessian matrix.


### Theorem 4 (the second-derivative test)

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on $\mathrm { a }$ set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ Assume
that $\phi$ is twice differentiable at an interior point c of $S .$ If

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } u \mathrm { i n } \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$$
(1)

and

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) > 0 \quad \text { for all } u \neq 0 \mathrm { i n } \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$$
(2)

then $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at c.

Proof. Since $\phi$ is twice differentiable at c, we have the second-order Taylor
formula (Theorem 6.8)

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( u \right) ,$$
(3)

where $r \left( u \right) / \| u \| ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0 .$ Now, $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = 0 .$ Further, since the Hessian
matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ is positive definite by assumption, all its eigenvalues are positive
(Theorem 1.8). In particular, if $\lambda$ denotes the smallest eigenvalue of $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) ,$
then $\lambda > 0$ and (by Exercise 1.14.1)

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) \right) u \geq \lambda \| u \| ^ { 2 } .$$
(4)

It follows that, for $u \neq 0 ,$

$$\frac { \phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) } { \| u \| ^ { 2 } } \geq \frac { \lambda } { 2 } + \frac { r \left( u \right) } { \| u \| ^ { 2 } } .$$
(5)

Choose $\delta > 0$ such that $| r \left( u \right) | / \| u \| ^ { 2 } \leq \lambda / 4$ for every $u \neq 0$ with $\| u \| < \delta .$ Then

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) \geq \left( \lambda / 4 \right) \| u \| ^ { 2 } > 0$$
(6)

for every $u \neq 0$ with $\| u \| < \delta .$ Hence $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at c.
✷
☐

In other words, Theorem 4 tells us that the conditions

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { 2 } } = \cdots = \frac { \partial \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { n } } = 0$$
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

Sec. 6 ] Sufficient conditions for a local minimum: second-derivative test 141

and

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) = \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \right) \quad \text { is positive definite }$$
(8)

together are sufficient for $\phi$ to have a strict local minimum at c. If we replace
(8) by the condition that $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ is negative definite, then we obtain sufficient
conditions for a strict local maximum.

If the Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ is neither positive definite nor negative defi-
nite, but is non-singular, then $c$ cannot be a local extremum point (see The-
orem 2); thus $c$ is a saddle point.

In the case where $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right)$ is singular, we cannot tell whether $c$ is a maximum
point, a minimum point, or a saddle point (see Exercise 3). This shows that
the converse of Theorem 4 is not true.


### Example 2

Let $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be twice differentiable at a critical point $c$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { 2 }$ of $\phi .$ Denote
the second-order partial derivatives by $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 1 } \phi \left( c \right) , \mathrm { D } _ { 1 2 } \phi \left( c \right)$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 2 } \phi \left( c \right) ,$ and let
A be the determinant of the Hessian matrix, i.e. $\Delta = \mathrm { D } _ { 1 1 } \phi \left( c \right) \cdot \mathrm { D } _ { 2 2 } \phi \left( c \right) -$
$\left( \mathrm { D } _ { 1 2 } \phi \left( c \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$ Then Theorem 4 implies that

(i) if $\Delta > 0$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 1 } \phi \left( c \right) > 0 ,$ $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at c,

(ii) if $\Delta > 0$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 1 } \phi \left( c \right) < 0 ,$ $\phi$ has a strict local maximum at c,

(iii) if $\Delta < 0 ,$ $\phi$ has a saddle point at c,

$\left( \mathrm { i v } \right)$ if $\Delta = 0 ,$ $\phi$ may have a local minimum, maximum, or saddle point at c.

y)2 has strict local minima at $\left( \sqrt { 2 } , - \sqrt { 2 } \right)$ and $\left( - \sqrt { 2 } , \sqrt { 2 } \right) ,$ and a saddle
☒
point at $\left( 0 , 0 \right) .$

2\. Show that the function $\phi : \mathrm { R } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ defined by $\phi \left( x , y \right) = \left( y - x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( y - 2 x ^ { 2 } \right)$
has a local minimum along each straight line through the origin, but
that $\phi$ has no local minimum at the origin. In fact, the origin is a saddle
point.

3\. Consider the functions (i) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 4 } + y ^ { 4 } ,$ (ii) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = - x ^ { 4 } - y ^ { 4 }$ and
(iii) $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 3 } + y ^ { 3 } .$ For each of these functions show that the origin
is a critical point and that the Hessian matrix is singular at the origin.
Then prove that the origin is a minimum point, a maximum point and
a saddle point, respectively.

4\. Show that the function $\phi : \mathbb{R} ^ { 3 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $\phi \left( x , y , z \right) = x y + y z + z x$
has a saddle point at the origin, and no other critical points.

## 7 CHARACTERIZATION OF DIFFERENTIABLE CONVEX FUNCTIONS

So far we have dealt only with local extrema. However, in the optimization
problems that arise in economics (among other disciplines) we are usually
interested in finding absolute extrema. The importance of convex (and con-
cave) functions in optimization comes from the fact that every local minimum
(maximum) of such a function is an absolute minimum (maximum). Before
we prove this statement (Theorem 8), let us study convex (concave) functions
in some more detail.

Recall that a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ is convex if for all x, $y$ in $S$ and all $\lambda \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$

$$\lambda x + \left( 1 - \lambda \right) y \in S ,$$
(1)

and a real-valued function $\phi ,$ defined on a convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ is convex if for
all x, $y \in S$ and all $\lambda \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$

$$\phi \left( \lambda x + \left( 1 - \lambda \right) y \right) \leq \lambda \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - \lambda \right) \phi \left( y \right) .$$
(2)

$I f \left( 2 \right)$ is satisfied with strict inequality for $x \neq y ,$ then we call $\phi$ strictly convex.
If $\phi \text { is \left(strictly\right) }$ convex, then $\psi \equiv - \phi$ is (strictly) concave.

In this section we consider (strictly) convex functions that are differen-
tiable, but not necessarily twice differentiable. In the next section we consider
twice differentiable convex functions.

We first show that $\phi$ is convex if and only if at any point the tangent
hyperplane is below the graph of $\phi$ (or coincides with it).


### Theorem 5

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function, defined and differentiable on an
open convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Then $\phi$ is convex on $S$ if and only if

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( y \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( y ; x - y \right) \quad \text { for every } x , y \in S .$$
(3)

Furthermore, $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S$ if and only if the inequality in (3) is
strict for every $x \neq y \in S .$

Proof. Assume that $\phi$ is convex on $S .$ Let $x$ be a point of $S ,$ and let $u$ be a
point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ such that $x + u \in S .$ Then the point $x + t u ,$ $t \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ lies on the
line segment joining $x$ and $x + u .$ Since $\phi$ is differentiable at x, we have

$$\phi \left( x + t u \right) = \phi \left( x \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; t u \right) + r \left( t \right) ,$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="143" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7 ] Characterization of differentiable conver functions" -->

where $r \left( t \right) / t \rightarrow 0$ as $t \rightarrow 0 .$ Also, since $\phi$ is convex on $S ,$ we have

$$\phi \left( x + t u \right) = \phi \left( \left( 1 - t \right) x + t \left( x + u \right) \right) \leq \left( 1 - t \right) \phi \left( x \right) + t \phi \left( x + u \right)$$

$$= \phi \left( x \right) + t \left( \phi \left( x + u \right) - \phi \left( x \right) \right) .$$
(5)

Combining (4) and (5) and dividing by t, we obtain

$$\phi \left( x + u \right) \geq \phi \left( x \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; u \right) + r \left( t \right) / t .$$
(6)

Let $t \rightarrow 0$ and (3) follows.

To prove the converse, assume that (3) holds. Let $x$ and $y$ be two points
in $S ,$ and $\mathrm { l e t } z$ be a point on the line segment joining $x$ and y, that is,
$z = t x + \left( 1 - t \right) y$ for some $t \in \left[ 0 , 1 \right] .$ Using our assumption (3), we have

$$\phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( z \right) \geq \mathrm { d } \phi \left( z ; x - z \right) , \quad \phi \left( y \right) - \phi \left( z \right) \geq \mathrm { d } \phi \left( z ; y - z \right) . ,$$
(7)

Multiply the first inequality in (7) with $t$ and the second with $\left( 1 \quad - \quad t \right) ,$ and
add the resulting inequalities. This gives

$$t \left[ \phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( z \right) \right] + \left( 1 - t \right) \left[ \phi \left( y \right) - \phi \left( z \right) \right]$$
$$\geq \mathrm { d } \phi \left( z ; t \left( x - z \right) + \left( 1 - t \right) \left( y - z \right) \right) = 0 ,$$

(8)

because

$$t \left( x - z \right) + \left( 1 - t \right) \left( y - z \right) = t x + \left( 1 - t \right) y - z = 0 .$$
(9)

By rearranging, (8) simplifies to

$$\phi \left( z \right) \leq t \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - t \right) \phi \left( y \right) ,$$
(10)

which shows that $\phi$ is convex.

Next assume that $\phi$ is strictly convex. Let $x$ be a point of $S ,$ and let $u$ be
a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ such that $x + u \in S .$ Since $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S ,$ $\phi$ is convex
on $S .$ Thus,

$$\phi \left( x + t u \right) \geq \phi \left( x \right) + t \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; u \right)$$
(11)

for every $t \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Also, using the definition of strict convexity,

$$\phi \left( x + t u \right) < \phi \left( x \right) + t \left[ \phi \left( x + u \right) - \phi \left( x \right) \right] .$$
(12)

(This is (5) with strict inequality.) Combining (11) and (12) and dividing by
$t ,$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; u \right) \leq \frac { \phi \left( x + t u \right) - \phi \left( x \right) } { t } < \phi \left( x + u \right) - \phi \left( x \right) ,$$
(13)

and the strict version of inequality (3) follows.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="144" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

Finally, the proof that the strict inequality (3) implies that $\phi$ is strictly
convex is the same as the proof that (3) implies that $\phi$ is convex, all inequal-
ities now being strict.
☐
✷

Another characterization of differentiable functions exploits the fact that,
in the one-dimensional case, the first derivative of a convex function is mono-
tonically non-decreasing. The generalization of this property to the multivari-
able case is contained in Theorem 6.


### Theorem 6

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function, defined and differentiable on an
open convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Then $\phi$ is convex on $S$ if and only if

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; x - y \right) - \mathrm { d } \phi \left( y ; x - y \right) \geq 0 \quad \text { for every } x , y \in S .$$
(14)

Furthermore, $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S$ if and only if the inequality in (14) is
strict for every $x \neq y \in S .$

Proof. Assume that $\phi$ is convex on $S .$ Let $x$ and $y$ be two points in $S .$ Then,
using Theorem 5,

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; x - y \right) = - \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; y - x \right) \geq \phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( y \right)$$
$$\geq \mathrm { d } \phi \left( y ; x - y \right) .$$

(15)

To prove the converse, assume that (14) holds. Let $x$ and $y$ be two distinct
points in $S .$ Let $L \left( x , y \right)$ denote the line segment joining $x$ and $y ,$ that is,

$$L \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ t x + \left( 1 - t \right) y : 0 \leq t \leq 1 \right\} ,$$
(16)

and let z be a point in $L \left( x , y \right) .$ By the mean-value theorem there exists a point
$\xi = \alpha x + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) z , 0 < \alpha < 1 ,$ on the line segment joining $x$ and $z$ (hence in
$\left. L \left( x , y \right) \right) ,$ such that

$$\phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( z \right) = \mathrm { d } \phi \left( \xi ; x - z \right) .$$
(17)

Noting that $\xi - z = \alpha \left( x - z \right)$ and assuming (14), we have

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( \xi , x - z \right) = \left( 1 / \alpha \right) \mathrm { d } \phi \left( \xi ; \xi - z \right)$$
$$\geq \left( 1 / \alpha \right) d \phi \left( z ; \xi - z \right) = \mathrm { d } \phi \left( z ; x - z \right) .$$

(18)

Further, if $z = t x + \left( 1 - t \right) y ,$ then $x \quad - \quad z = \left( 1 - t \right) \left( x - y \right) .$ It follows that

$$\phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( z \right) \geq \left( 1 - t \right) \mathrm { d } \phi \left( z ; x - y \right) .$$
(19)

In precisely the same way we can show that

$$\phi \left( z \right) - \phi \left( y \right) \leq t d \phi \left( z ; x - y \right) .$$
(20)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="145" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] Characterization of twice differentiable convex functions" -->

From (19) and (20) we obtain

$$t \left[ \phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( z \right) \right] - \left( 1 - t \right) \left[ \phi \left( z \right) - \phi \left( y \right) \right] \geq 0 .$$
(21)

By rearranging, (21) simplifies $\mathrm { t o }$

$$\phi \left( z \right) \leq t \phi \left( x \right) + \left( 1 - t \right) \phi \left( y \right) ,$$
(22)

which shows that $\phi$ is convex.

The corresponding result for $\phi$ strictly convex is obtained in precisely the
same way, all inequalities now being strict.
☐
✷


### Exercises

1\. Show that the function $\phi \left( x , y \right) = x + y \left( y - 1 \right)$ is convex. Is $\phi$ strictly
convex?

2\. Prove that $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { 4 }$ is strictly convex.


## 8 CHARACTERIZATION OF TWICE DIFFERENTIABLE CONVEX FUNCTIONS

Both characterizations of differentiable convex functions (Theorems 5 and 6)
involved conditions on two points. For twice differentiable functions there is
a characterization that involves only one point.


## Theorem 7

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function, defined and twice differentiable on
an open convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Then $\phi$ is convex on $S$ if and only $\mathrm { i f }$

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x ; u \right) \geq 0 \quad \text { for all } x \in S \mathrm { a n d } u \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$$
(1)

Furthermore, if the inequality in (1) is strict for all $x \in S$ and $u \neq 0$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$
then $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S .$

Note 1. The 'strict' part of Theorem 7 is a one-way implication, and not
an equivalence, i.e. if $\phi$ is twice differentiable and strictly convex, then by $\left( 1 \right)$
the Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right)$ is positive semidefinite, but not necessarily positive
definite for every x. For example, the function $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { 4 }$ is strictly convex
but its second derivative $\phi ^ { \prime \prime } \left( x \right) = 1 2 x ^ { 2 }$ vanishes at $x = 0 .$

Note 2. Theorem 7 tells us that $\phi$ is convex (strictly convex, concave, strictly
concave) on $S$ if the Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right)$ is positive semidefinite (positive
definite, negative semidefinite, negative definite) for all $x$ in $S .$

Proof. Let c be a point of $S ,$ and let $u \neq 0$ be a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ such that $c + u \in S .$
By Taylor's theorem, we have

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right)$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="146" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

for some $\theta \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ If $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x ; u \right) \geq 0$ for every $x \in S ,$ then in particular
$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c + \theta u ; u \right) \geq 0 ,$ so that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) .$$
(3)

Then, by Theorem 5, $\phi$ is convex on $S .$

If $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x ; u \right) > 0$ for every $x \in S ,$ then

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) > \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) ,$$
(4)

which shows, by Theorem 5, that $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S .$

To prove the 'only if' part of $\left( 1 \right) ,$ assume that $\phi$ is convex on $S .$ Let
$t \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Then, by Theorem 5,

$$\phi \left( c + t u \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) + t \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) .$$
(5)

Also, by the second-order Taylor formula (Theorem 6.8),

$$\phi \left( c + t u \right) = \phi \left( c \right) + t \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( t \right) ,$$
(6)

where $r \left( t \right) / t ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 0$ as $t \rightarrow 0$ 0. Combining (5) and (6) and dividing by $t ^ { 2 }$ we
obtain

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) \geq - r \left( t \right) / t ^ { 2 } .$$
(7)

The left side of $\left( 7 \right)$ is independent of t; the right side tends to zero as $t \rightarrow 0 .$
✷
☐

Hence $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( c ; u \right) \geq 0 .$


## Exercises

1\. Repeat Exercise 4.9.1 using Theorem 7.

2\. Show that the function $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { p } ,$ $p > 1$ is strictly convex on $\left[ 0 , \infty \right) .$

3\. Show that the function $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } x ,$ defined on $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ is strictly convex.

4\. Consider the CES (constant elasticity of substitution) production func-
tion

$$\phi \left( x , y \right) = A \left[ \delta x ^ { - \rho } + \left( 1 - \delta \right) y ^ { - \rho } \right] ^ { - 1 / \rho } \quad \left( A > 0 , 0 \leq \delta \leq 1 , \rho \neq 0 \right)$$

defined for $x > 0$ and $y > 0 .$ Show that $\phi$ is convex if $\rho \leq - 1 ,$ and
concave if $\rho \geq - 1$ (and $\rho \neq 0 .$ What happens if $\rho = - 1$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="147" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Sufficient conditions for an absolute minimum" -->


## 9 SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS FOR AN ABSOLUTE MINIMUM

The convexity (concavity) of a function enables us to find the absolute min-
imum (maximum) of the function, since every local minimum (maximum) of
such a function is an absolute minimum (maximum).


## Theorem 8

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined and differentiable on an open
convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and let $c$ be a point of $S$ where

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; u \right) = 0$$
(1)

for every $u \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $i s \left( s t r i c t l y \right)$ convex on $S ,$ then $\phi$ has a (strict) absolute
minimum at c.

Proof. If $\phi$ is convex on $S ,$ then by Theorem 5,

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \phi \left( c ; x - c \right) = \phi \left( c \right)$$
(2)

for all $x$ in $S .$ If $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S ,$ then the inequality (2) is strict for
all $x \neq c$ in $S .$
☐
✷

To check whether a given differentiable function is (strictly) convex, we
have four criteria at our disposal: the definition in Section 4.9, Theorems 5
and 6, and, if the function is twice differentiable, Theorem 7.


## Exercises

1\. Let $a$ be an $n \times 1$ vector and $A$ a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix. Prove
that

$$a ^ { \prime } x + x ^ { \prime } A x \geq - \frac { 1 } { 4 } a ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } a$$

for every $x$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n } .$ For which value of $x$ does the function $\phi \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } x +$
$x ^ { \prime } A x$ attain its minimum value?

2\. (More difficult.) If $A$ is positive semidefinite, under what condition is it
true that

$$a ^ { \prime } x + x ^ { \prime } A x \geq - \frac { 1 } { 4 } a ^ { \prime } A ^ { + } a$$

for every $x$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$


## 10 MONOTONIC TRANSFORMATIONS

To complete our discussion of unconstrained optimization we shall prove the
useful, if simple, fact that minimizing a function is equivalent to minimizing
a monotonically increasing transformation of that function.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="148" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->


## Theorem 9

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined
on $S .$ Let $T \subset \mathrm { R }$ be the range of $\phi$ (the set of all elements $\phi \left( x \right) ,$ for $x \in S ,$ and
let $\eta : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on $T .$ Define the composite
function $\psi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ by

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \eta \left( \phi \left( x \right) \right) .$$
(1)

If $\eta$ is increasing on $T ,$ and if $\phi$ has an absolute minimum (maximum) at a
point $c$ of $S ,$ then $\psi$ has an absolute minimum (maximum) at c.

If $\eta$ in addition is strictly increasing on $T ,$ then $\phi$ has an absolute minimum
(maximum) at $c$ if and $\text { only if } \psi$ has an absolute minimum (maximum) at c.

Proof. Let $\eta$ be an increasing function on $T ,$ and suppose that $\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right)$
for all $x$ in $S .$ Then

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \eta \left( \phi \left( x \right) \right) \geq \eta \left( \phi \left( c \right) \right) = \psi \left( c \right)$$
(2)

for all $x$ in $S .$ Next, let $\eta$ be strictly increasing on $T ,$ and suppose that $\phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) <$
$\phi \left( c \right)$ for some $x _ { 0 }$ in $S .$ Then

$$\psi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) = \eta \left( \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) \right) < \eta \left( \phi \left( c \right) \right) = \psi \left( c \right) .$$
(3)

Hence, if $\psi \left( x \right) \geq \psi \left( c \right)$ for all $x$ in $S ,$ then $\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right)$ for all $x$ in $S .$

The case where $\phi$ has an absolute maximum is proved in the same way.
☐

Note. Theorem 9 is clearly not affected by the presence of constraints. Thus,
minimizing a function subject to certain constraints is equivalent to minimiz-
ing a monotonically increasing transformation of that function subject to the
same constraints.


## Exercise

1\. Consider the likelihood function

$$L \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

Use Theorem 9 to maximize $L$ with respect to $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$


## 11 OPTIMIZATION SUBJECT TO CONSTRAINTS

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ Hitherto
we have considered optimization problems of the type

$$\min _ { x \in S } \mathrm { i z e } \phi \left( x \right) .$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="149" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Necessary conditions for a local minimum under constraints" -->

It may happen, however, that the variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are subject to certain
constraints, say $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = 0 , \ldots , g _ { m } \left( x \right) = 0 .$ Our problem is now

$$\phi \left( x \right)$$
$$g \left( x \right) = 0 ,$$
(2)
subject to
(3)

where $g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ is the vector function $g = \left( g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , \ldots , g _ { m } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ This is known
as a constrained minimization problem (or a minimization problem subject to
equality constraints), and the most convenient way of solving it is, in general,
to use the Lagrange multiplier theory. In the remainder of this chapter we
shall study that important theory in some detail.

We start our discussion with some definitions. The subset of $S$ on which
$g$ vanishes, that is,

$$\Gamma = \left\{ x : x \in S , g \left( x \right) = 0 \right\} ,$$
(4)

is known as the opportunity set (constraint set). Let $c$ be a point of $\Gamma .$ We say
that $\phi$ has a local minimum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0$ if there exists
an n-ball $B \left( c \right)$ such that

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in \Gamma \cap B \left( c \right) .$$
(5)

$\phi$ has a strict local minimum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0$ if we can
choose $B \left( c \right)$ such that

$$\phi \left( x \right) > \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in \Gamma \cap B \left( c \right) , x \neq c .$$
(6)

$\phi$ has an absolute minimum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0$ if

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in \Gamma .$$
(7)

$\phi$ has a strict absolute minimum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0$ if

$$\phi \left( x \right) > \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \in \Gamma , x \neq c .$$
(8)


## 12 NECESSARY CONDITIONS FOR A LOCAL MINIMUM UNDER CONSTRAINTS

The next theorem gives a necessary condition for a constrained minimum to
occur at a given point.


### Theorem 10 (Lagrange)

Let $g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } \left( n > m \right) ,$ and let $c$ be
an interior point of $S .$ Assume that

(i) $g \left( c \right) = 0 ,$

(ii) $g$ is differentiable in some n-ball $B \left( c \right) ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="150" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

(iii) the $m \times n$ Jacobian matrix $\mathrm { D } g$ is continuous at c, and

$\left( \mathrm { i v } \right)$ $\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right)$ has full row rank m.

Further, let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on $S ,$ and assume
that

(v) $\phi$ is differentiable at c, and

(vi) $\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right)$ for every $x \in B \left( c \right)$ satisfying $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$

Then there exists a unique vector $l$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ satisfying the $n$ equations

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) - l ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) = 0 .$$
(1)

Note. If condition (vi) is replaced by

(vi)' $\phi \left( x \right) \leq \phi \left( c \right)$ for every $x \in B \left( c \right)$ satisfying $g \left( x \right) = 0 ,$

then the conclusion of the theorem remains valid.

Lagrange's theorem establishes the validity of the following formal method
('Lagrange's multiplier method') for obtaining necessary conditions for an ex-
tremum subject to equality constraints. We first define the Lagrangian func-
tion $\psi$ by

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \phi \left( x \right) - l ^ { \prime } g \left( x \right) ,$$
(2)

where I is an $m \times 1$ vector of constants $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { m } ,$ called the Lagrange mul-
tipliers. (One multiplier is introduced for each constraint. Notice that $\psi \left( x \right)$
equals $\phi \left( x \right)$ for every $x$ that satisfies the constraint.) Next we differentiate $\psi$
with respect to $x$ and set the result equal to 0. Together with the $m$ con-
straints we obtain the following system of $n + m$ equations (the $f i r s t - o r d e r$
conditions)

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( x ; u \right) = 0 \quad \text { for every } u \mathrm { i n } \mathrm { R } ^ { n } ,$$
$$g \left( x \right) = 0 .$$
(3)

We then try to solve this system of $n + m$ equations in $n + m$ unknowns:
$\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { m }$ and $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$ The points $x = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ obtained in this way
are called critical points, and among them are any points of $S$ at which con-
strained minima or maxima occur. (A critical point of the constrained prob-
lem is thus defined as 'a critical point of the function $\phi \left( x \right)$ defined on the
surface $g \left( x \right) = 0 ,$ and not as 'a critical point of $\phi \left( x \right)$ whose coordinates sat-
isfy $g \left( x \right) = 0 ^ { , } .$ Any critical point in the latter sense is also a critical point in
the former, but not conversely.)

Of course, the question remains whether a given critical point actually
yields a minimum, maximum, or neither.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="151" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Necessary conditions for a local minimum under constraints" -->

Proof. Let us partition the $m \times n$ matrix $\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right)$ as

$$\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } g \left( c \right) , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } g \left( c \right) \right) ,$$
(4)

where $D _ { 1 } g \left( c \right)$ is an $m \times m$ matrix, and $D _ { 2 } g \left( c \right)$ is an $m \times \left( n - m \right)$ matrix. By
renumbering the variables (if necessary), we may assume that

$$| \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } g \left( c \right) | \neq 0 .$$
(5)

We shall denote points $x$ in $S$ by $\left( z ; t \right) ,$ where $z \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $t \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n - m } ,$ so that
$z = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m } \right) ^ { \bar { \prime } }$ and $t = \left( x _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ Also, we write $c = \left( z _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) .$

By the implicit function theorem (Theorem A.1 in the appendix to this
chapter) there exists an open set $T$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n - m }$ containing $t _ { 0 } ,$ and a unique
function $h : T \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ such that

(i) $h \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = z _ { 0 } ,$

(ii) $g \left( h \left( t \right) ; t \right) = 0$ for all $t \in T ,$ and

(iii) $h$ is differentiable at $t _ { 0 } .$

Since $h$ is continuous at $t _ { 0 }$ we can choose an $\left( n \quad - \right.$ m)-ball $T _ { 0 } \subset T$ with
centre $t _ { 0 }$ such that

$$\left( h \left( t \right) ; t \right) \in B \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } t \in T _ { 0 } .$$
(6)

Then the real-valued function $\psi : T _ { 0 } \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by

$$\psi \left( t \right) = \phi \left( h \left( t \right) ; t \right)$$
(7)

has the property

$$\psi \left( t \right) \geq \psi \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \quad \text { for all } t \in T _ { 0 } ,$$
(8)

that is, $\psi$ has a local (unconstrained) minimum at $t _ { 0 } .$ Since $h$ is differentiable
at $t _ { 0 }$ and $\phi$ is differentiable at $\left( z _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) ,$ it follows that $\psi$ is differentiable at $t _ { 0 } .$
Hence, by Theorem 2, its derivative vanishes at $t _ { 0 } ,$ and, using the chain rule,
we find

$$0 = \mathrm { D } \psi \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = \mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } h \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \\ I _ { n - m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(9)

Next, consider the vector function $\kappa : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ defined by

$$\kappa \left( t \right) = g \left( h \left( t \right) ; t \right) .$$
(10)

The function $\kappa$ is identically zero on the set $T .$ Therefore, all its partial deriva-
tives are zero on $T .$ In particular, $\mathrm { D } \kappa \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$ Further, since $h$ is differentiable
at $t _ { 0 }$ and g is differentiable at $\left( z _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) ,$ the chain rule yields

$$0 = \mathrm { D } \kappa \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = \mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } h \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \\ I _ { n - m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(11)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="152" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

Combining (10) and (12), we obtain

$$E \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } h \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \\ I _ { n - m } \end{array} \right) = 0 ,$$
(12)

where $E$ is the $\left( m + 1 \right) \times n$ matrix

$$E = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) \\ \mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( c \right) & \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( c \right) \\ \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } g \left( c \right) & \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } g \left( c \right) \end{array} \right) .$$
(13)

Equation (13) shows that the last $n - \quad m$ columns of $E$ are linear combinations
of the first $m$ columns. Hence $r \left( E \right) \leq m .$ But since $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } g \left( c \right)$ is a submatrix of
$E$ with rank $m ,$ the rank of $E$ cannot be smaller than $m .$ It follows that

$$r \left( E \right) = m .$$
(14)

The $m + 1$ rows of $E$ are therefore linearly dependent. By assumption, the $m$
rows of $\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right)$ are linearly independent. Hence $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right)$ is a linear combination
of the $m$ rows of $\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) ,$ that $\mathrm { i s } ,$

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( c \right) - l ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) = 0$$
(15)

for some $l \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$ This proves the existence of $l ;$ its uniqueness follows imme-
diately from the fact that $\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right)$ has full row rank.
✷
☐


### Example 3

To solve the problem

minimize
subject to
x' Ax = 1
$x ^ { \prime } x$
(16)

(A positive definite)
(17)

by Lagrange's method, we introduce one multiplier $\lambda$ and define the $\mathrm { L a }$
grangian function

$$\psi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } x - \lambda \left( x ^ { \prime } A x - 1 \right) .$$
(18)

Differentiating $\psi$ with respect to $x$ and setting the result equal to $\mathrm { Z e r o }$ yields

$$x = \lambda A x .$$
(19)

To this we add the constraint

$$x ^ { \prime } A x = 1 .$$
(20)

Equations (20) and (21) are the first-order conditions, from which we shall
solve for $x$ and $\lambda .$ Pre-multiplying both sides of (20) by $x ^ { \prime }$ gives

$$x ^ { \prime } x = \lambda x ^ { \prime } A x = \lambda ,$$
(21)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="153" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Necessary conditions for a local minimum under constraints" -->

using (21), and since $x \neq 0$ we obtain from (20)

$$A x = \left( 1 / x ^ { \prime } x \right) x .$$
(22)

This shows that $\left( 1 / x ^ { \prime } x \right)$ is an eigenvalue of $A .$ Let $\mu \left( A \right)$ be the largest eigen-
value of $A .$ Then the minimum value of $x ^ { \prime } x$ under the constraint $x ^ { \prime } A x = 1$ is
$1 / \mu \left( A \right) .$ The value of $x$ for which the minimum is attained is the eigenvector
of $A$ associated with the eigenvalue $\mu \left( A \right) .$


### Exercises

1\. Consider the problem

$$\left( x - 1 \right) \left( y + 1 \right)$$
$$s u b j e c t \quad t o$$

By using Lagrange's method, show that the minimum point is $\left( 0 , 0 \right)$ with
$\lambda = 1 .$ Next consider the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( x , y \right) = \left( x - 1 \right) \left( y + 1 \right) - 1 \left( x - y \right) ,$$

and show that $\psi$ has a saddle point at $\left( 0 , 0 \right) .$ That is, the point $\left( 0 , 0 \right)$
does not minimize $\psi .$ (This shows that it is not correct to say that
minimizing a function subject to constraints is equivalent to minimizing
the Lagrangian function.)

2\. Solve the following problems by using the Lagrange multiplier method:

(i) $\min \left( \max \right)$ $x y$ subject to $x ^ { 2 } + x y + y ^ { 2 } = 1 ,$

(ii) min(max) $\left( y \quad - \quad z \right) \left( z \quad - \quad x \right) \left( x - \quad y \right)$ subject to $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 2 ,$

(iii) min(max) $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } - y z - z x - x y$
subject to $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } - 2 x + 2 y + 6 z + 9 = 0 .$

3\. Prove the inequality

$$\left( x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } \ldots x _ { n } \right) ^ { 1 / n } \leq \frac { x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { n } } { n }$$

for all positive real numbers $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$ (Compare Section 11.4.)

4\. Solve the problem

$$\mathrm { m i n i m i z e } \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 }$$
$$4 x + 3 y + z = 2 5 .$$
subject to

5\. Solve the following utility maximization problem:

maximize
subject to

$$x _ { 1 } ^ { \alpha } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 - \alpha } \quad \left( 0 < \alpha < 1 \right)$$
$$p _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + p _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } = y \quad \left( p _ { 1 } > 0 , p _ { 2 } > 0 , y > 0 \right)$$

with respect to $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 } \left( x _ { 1 } > 0 , x _ { 2 } > 0 \right) .$

## 13 SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS FOR A LOCAL MINIMUM UNDER CONSTRAINTS

In the previous section we obtained conditions that are necessary for a func-
tion to achieve a local minimum or maximum subject to equality constraints.
To investigate whether a given critical point actually yields a minimum, maxi-
mum, or neither, it is often practical to proceed on an ad hoc basis. If this fails,
the following theorem provides sufficient conditions to ensure the existence of
a constrained minimum or maximum at a critical point.


### Theorem 11

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and
$g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } \left( m < n \right)$ a vector function defined on $S .$ Let $c$ be an interior point
of $S$ and let $l$ be a point in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } .$ Define the Lagrangian function $\psi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
by the equation

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \phi \left( x \right) - l ^ { \prime } g \left( x \right) ,$$
(1)

and assume that

(i) $\phi$ is differentiable at c,

(ii) g is twice differentiable at c,

(iii) the $m \times n$ Jacobian matrix $\mathrm { D } g \left( c \right)$ has full row rank $m ,$

(iv) (first-order conditions)

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( c ; u \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } u \mathrm { i n } \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$$
$$g \left( c \right) = 0 ,$$
(2)

(v) (second-order condition)

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \psi \left( c ; u \right) > 0 \quad \text { for all } u \neq 0 \text { satisfying dg } \left( c ; u \right) = 0 .$$
(3)

Then $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at c under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$

The difficulty in applying Theorem 11 lies, of course, in the verification of
the second-order condition. This condition requires that

$$u ^ { \prime } A u > 0 \quad \text { for every } u \neq 0 \text { such that } B u = 0 ,$$
(4)

where

$$A = \mathrm { H } \phi \left( c \right) - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \lambda _ { i } \mathrm { H } g _ { i } \left( c \right) , \quad B = \mathrm { D } g \left( c \right) .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="155" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Sufficient conditions for a local minimum under constraints" -->

Several sets of necessary and sufficient conditions exist for a quadratic form to
be positive definite under linear constraints, and one of these (the 'bordered
determinantal criterion') is discussed in Section 3.11. The following theorem
is therefore easily proved.


### Theorem 12 (bordered determinantal criterion)

Assume that conditions (i)-(iv) of Theorem 11 are satisfied, and let $\Delta _ { r }$ be
the symmetric $\left( m + r \right) \times \left( m + r \right)$ matrix

$$\Delta _ { r } = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & B _ { r } \\ B _ { r } ^ { \prime } & A _ { r r } \end{array} \right) \quad \left( r = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(6)

where $A _ { r r }$ is the $r \times r$ matrix in the top left corner of $A ,$ and $B _ { r }$ is the $m \times r$
matrix whose columns are the first $r$ columns of $B .$ Assume that | $| B _ { m } | \neq 0 .$
(This can always be achieved by renumbering the variables, if necessary.) If

$$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } | \Delta _ { r } | > 0 \quad \left( r = m + 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(7)

then $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$ If

$$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { r } | \Delta _ { r } | > 0 \quad \left( r = m + 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(8)

then $\phi$ has a strict local maximum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$

Proof of Theorem 11. Let us define the sets

$$U \left( \delta \right) = \left\{ u \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } : \| u \| < \delta \right\} , \quad \delta > 0 ,$$
(9)

and

$$T = \left\{ u \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } : u \neq 0 , c + u \in S , g \left( c + u \right) = 0 \right\} .$$
(10)

We need to show that a $\delta > 0$ exists such that

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) > 0 \quad \text { for all } u \in T \cap U \left( \delta \right) .$$
(11)

By assumption, $\phi$ and $g$ are twice differentiable at c, and therefore differen-
tiable at each point of an n-ball $B \left( c \right) \subset S .$ Let $\delta _ { 0 }$ be the radius of $B \left( c \right) .$ Since
$\psi$ is twice differentiable at c, we have for every $u \in U \left( \delta _ { 0 } \right)$ the second-order
Taylor formula (Theorem 6.8)

$$\psi \left( c + u \right) = \psi \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } \psi \left( c ; u \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \psi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( u \right) ,$$
(12)

where $r \left( u \right) / \| u \| ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0 .$ Now, $g \left( c \right) = 0$ and $\mathrm { d } \psi \left( c ; u \right) = 0$ (first-order
conditions). Further, $g \left( c + u \right) = 0$ for $u \in T .$ Hence (12) reduces to

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \psi \left( c ; u \right) + r \left( u \right) \quad \text { for all } u \in T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(13)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="156" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

Next, since g is differentiable at each point of $B \left( c \right) ,$ we may apply the mean-
value theorem to each of its components $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { m } .$ This yields, for every
$u \in U \left( \delta _ { 0 } \right) ,$

$$g _ { i } \left( c + u \right) = g _ { i } \left( c \right) + \mathrm { d } g _ { i } \left( c + \theta _ { i } u ; u \right) ,$$
(14)

where $\theta _ { i } \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , m .$ Again, $g _ { i } \left( c \right) = 0$ and, for $u \in T ,$ $g _ { i } \left( c + u \right) = 0 .$
Hence

(15)
$$\mathrm { d } g _ { i } \left( c + \theta _ { i } u ; u \right) = 0 \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) \quad \text { for all } u \in T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 0 } \right) .$$

Let us denote by $\Delta \left( u \right) ,$ $u \in U \left( \delta _ { 0 } \right) ,$ the $m \times n$ matrix whose ij-th element is
the j-th first-order partial derivative of $g _ { i }$ evaluated at $c + \theta _ { i } u ,$ that is,

$$\Delta _ { i j } \left( u \right) = \mathrm { D } _ { i } g _ { i } \left( c + \theta _ { i } u \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m ; j = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(16)

(Notice that the rows of $\Delta$ are evaluated as possibly different points.) Then
the $m$ equations in (15) can be written as one vector equation

$$\Delta \left( u \right) u = 0 \quad \text { for all } u \in T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(17)

Since the functions $\mathrm { D } _ { j } g _ { i }$ are continuous at $u = 0 ,$ the Jacobian matrix $\Delta$ is
continuous at $u =$ 0. By assumption $\Delta \left( 0 \right)$ has maximum rank $m ,$ and therefore
its rank is locally constant. That is, there exists a $\delta _ { 1 } \in \left( 0 , \delta _ { 0 } \right]$ such that

$$\mathrm { r a n k } \left( \Delta \left( u \right) \right) = m \quad \text { for all } u \in U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right)$$
(18)

(see Exercise 5.15.1). Now, $\Delta \left( u \right)$ has $n$ columns of which only $m$ are linearly
independent. Hence by Exercise 1.14.3 there exists an $n \times \left( n - m \right)$ matrix $\Gamma \left( u \right)$
such that

$$\Delta \left( u \right) \Gamma \left( u \right) = 0 , \quad \Gamma ^ { \prime } \left( u \right) \Gamma \left( u \right) = I _ { n - m } \quad \text { for all } u \in U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right)$$
(19)

(The columns of $\Gamma$ are of course $n \quad - \quad m$ normalized eigenvectors associated
with the $n \quad - \quad m$ zero eigenvalues of $\Delta ^ { \prime } \Delta$ Further, since $\Delta$ is continuous at
$u = 0 ,$ so is $\Gamma .$

From (17)-(19) it follows that $u$ must be a linear combination of the
columns of $\Gamma \left( u \right) ,$ that is, there exists, for every $u$ in $T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right) ,$ a vector
$q \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n - m }$ such that

$$u = \Gamma \left( u \right) q .$$
(20)

If we denote by $K \left( u \right)$ the symmetric $\left( n - m \right) \times \left( n - m \right)$ matrix

$$K \left( u \right) = \Gamma ^ { \prime } \left( u \right) \left( \mathrm { H } \psi \left( c \right) \right) \Gamma \left( u \right) , \quad u \in U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right) ,$$
(21)

and by $\lambda \left( u \right)$ its smallest eigenvalue, then

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \psi \left( c ; u \right) = u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } \psi \left( c \right) \right) u = q ^ { \prime } \Gamma ^ { \prime } \left( u \right) \left( \mathrm { H } \psi \left( c \right) \right) \Gamma \left( u \right) q$$
$$= q ^ { \prime } K \left( u \right) q \geq \lambda \left( u \right) q ^ { \prime } q \quad \text { \left(Exercise 1.14.1\right) }$$
$$= \lambda \left( u \right) q ^ { \prime } \Gamma ^ { \prime } \left( u \right) \Gamma \left( u \right) q = \lambda \left( u \right) \| u \| ^ { 2 }$$

(22)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="157" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Sufficient conditions for a local minimum under constraints" -->

for every $u$ in $T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right) .$ Now, since $\Gamma$ is continuous at $u = 0 ,$ so is $K$ and so
is $\lambda .$ Hence we may write, for $u$ in $U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right) ,$

$$\lambda \left( u \right) = \lambda \left( 0 \right) + R \left( u \right) ,$$
(23)

where $R \left( u \right) \rightarrow 0$ as $u \rightarrow 0 .$ Combining (13), (22) and (23), we obtain

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) \geq \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \lambda \left( 0 \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } R \left( u \right) + r \left( u \right) / \| u \| ^ { 2 } \right) \| u \| ^ { 2 }$$
(24)

for every $u$ in $T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right) .$

Let us now prove that $\lambda \left( 0 \right) > 0 .$ By assumption,

$$u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } \psi \left( c \right) \right) u > 0 \quad \text { for all } u \neq 0 \mathrm { s a t i s f y i n g } \Delta \left( 0 \right) u = 0 .$$
(25)

For $u \in U \left( \delta _ { 1 } \right) ,$ the condition $\Delta \left( 0 \right) u = 0$ is equivalent to $u = \Gamma \left( 0 \right) q$ for some
$q \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n - m } .$ Hence (25) is equivalent $t o$

$$q ^ { \prime } \Gamma ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) \left( \mathrm { H } \psi \left( c \right) \right) \Gamma \left( 0 \right) q > 0 \quad \text { for all } q \neq 0 .$$
(26)

This shows that $K \left( 0 \right)$ is positive definite, and hence that its smallest eigen-
value $\lambda \left( 0 \right)$ is positive.

Finally, choose $\delta _ { 2 } \in \left( 0 , \delta _ { 1 } \right]$ such that

$$| \frac { 1 } { 2 } R \left( u \right) + r \left( u \right) / \| u \| ^ { 2 } | \leq \lambda \left( 0 \right) / 4$$
(27)

for every $u \neq 0$ with $\| u \| < \delta _ { 2 } .$ Then (24) and (27) imply

$$\phi \left( c + u \right) - \phi \left( c \right) \geq \left( \lambda \left( 0 \right) / 4 \right) \| u \| ^ { 2 } > 0$$
(28)

for every $u$ in $T \cap U \left( \delta _ { 2 } \right) .$ Hence $\phi$ has a strict local minimum at $c$ under the
constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$
✷
☐

Example 4 $\left( n = 2 , m = 1 \right)$

Solve the problem

$$\max \left( \min \right) \quad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 }$$
$$x ^ { 2 } + x y + y ^ { 2 } = 3 .$$

Let $\lambda$ be a constant, and define the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( x , y \right) = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } + x y + y ^ { 2 } - 3 \right) .$$

The first-order conditions are

$$2 x - \lambda \left( 2 x + y \right) = 0$$
$$2 y - \lambda \left( x + 2 y \right) = 0$$
$$x ^ { 2 } + x y + y ^ { 2 } = 3 ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="158" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

from which we find the following four solutions: (1,1) and $\left( - 1 , - 1 \right)$ with $\lambda = \frac { 2 } { 3 }$
and $\left( \sqrt { 3 } , - \sqrt { 3 } \right)$ and $\left( - \sqrt { 3 } , \sqrt { 3 } \right)$ with $\lambda = 2 .$ We now compute the bordered
Hessian matrix

$$\Delta \left( x , y \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & 2 x + y & x + 2 y \\ 2 x + y & 2 - 2 \lambda & - \lambda \\ x + 2 y & - \lambda & 2 - 2 \lambda \end{array} \right)$$

whose determinant equals

$$| \Delta \left( x , y \right) | = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 3 \lambda - 2 \right) \left( x - y \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 9 } { 2 } \left( 2 - \lambda \right) \left( x + y \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

For $\lambda = \frac { 2 } { 3 }$ we find $| \Delta \left( 1 , 1 \right) | = | \Delta \left( - 1 , - 1 \right) | = - 2 4 ,$ and for $\lambda = 2$ we find
$| \Delta \left( \sqrt { 3 } , - \sqrt { 3 } \right) | = | \Delta \left( - \sqrt { 3 } , \sqrt { 3 } \right) | = 2 4 .$ We thus conclude, using Theorem 12,
that $\left( 1 , 1 \right)$ and $\left( - 1 , - 1 \right)$ are strict local minimum points, and that $\left( \sqrt { 3 } , - \sqrt { 3 } \right)$
and $\left( - \sqrt { 3 } , \sqrt { 3 } \right)$ are strict local maximum points. (These points are, in fact,
absolute extreme points, as is evident geometrically.)
