problems in Exercise 12.2.

2\. Answer the same question as above for Exercises 12.4 and 12.5.

3\. Compare Example 4 and solution method of Section 13 with Example
3 and the solution method of Section 12.


## 14 SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS FOR AN ABSOLUTE MINI- MUM UNDER CONSTRAINTS

The Lagrange theorem (Theorem 10) gives necessary conditions for a local
(and hence also for an absolute) constrained extremum to occur at a given
point. In Theorem 11 we obtained sufficient conditions for a local constrained
extremum. To find sufficient conditions for an absolute constrained extremum,
we proceed as in the unconstrained case (Section 9), and impose appropriate
convexity (concavity) conditions.


## Theorem 13

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined and differentiable on an open
convex set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ and let $g : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m } \left( m < n \right)$ be a vector function defined
and differentiable on $S .$ Let $c$ be a point of $S$ and let $l$ be a point in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m } .$
Define the Lagrangian function $\psi : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ by the equation

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \phi \left( x \right) - l ^ { \prime } g \left( x \right) ,$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="159" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 15 ] A note on constraints in matrix form" -->

and assume that the first-order conditions are satisfied, that is,

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( c ; u \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } u \mathrm { i n } \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$$
(2)

and

$$g \left( c \right) = 0 .$$
(3)

If $\psi$ is (strictly) convex on $S ,$ then $\phi$ has a (strict) absolute minimum at c
under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$

Note. Under the same conditions, if $\psi$ is (strictly) concave on $S ,$ then $\phi$ has
a (strict) absolute maximum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$

Proof. If $\psi$ is convex on $S$ and $\mathrm { d } \psi \left( c ; u \right) = 0$ for every $u \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ then $\psi$ has an
absolute minimum at $c$ (Theorem 8), that is,

$$\psi \left( x \right) \geq \psi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \mathrm { i n } S .$$
(4)

Since $\psi \left( x \right) = \phi \left( x \right) - l ^ { \prime } g \left( x \right) ,$ it follows that

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) + l ^ { \prime } \left[ g \left( x \right) - g \left( c \right) \right] \quad \text { for all } x \mathrm { i n } S .$$
(5)

But $g \left( c \right) = 0$ by assumption. Hence,

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq \phi \left( c \right) \quad \text { for all } x \text { in S satisfying } g \left( x \right) = 0 ,$$
(6)

that is, $\phi$ has an absolute minimum at $c$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = 0 .$ The
case in which $\psi$ is strictly convex is treated similarly.
✷
☐

Note. To prove that the Lagrangian function $\psi$ is (strictly) convex or (strictly)
concave, we can use the definition in Section 4.9, Theorem 5 or Theorem 6,
or (if $\psi$ is twice differentiable) Theorem $7$ 7. In addition we observe that

(a) if the constraints $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) , \ldots , g _ { m } \left( x \right)$ are all linear, and $\phi \left( x \right)$ is (strictly)
convex, then $\psi \left( x \right)$ is (strictly) convex.

In fact, (a) is a special case of

(b) if the functions $\lambda _ { 1 } g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) , \ldots , \lambda _ { m } g _ { m } \left( x \right)$ are all concave (that is, for $i =$
$1 , 2 , \ldots , m ,$ either $g _ { i } \left( x \right)$ is concave and $\lambda _ { i } \geq 0 ,$ or $g _ { i } \left( x \right)$ is convex and
$\lambda _ { i } \leq 0$ and if $\phi \left( x \right)$ is convex, then $\psi \left( x \right)$ is convex; furthermore, if at
least one of these $m + 1$ conditions is strict, then $\psi \left( x \right)$ is strictly convex.


## 15 A NOTE ON CONSTRAINTS IN MATRIX FORM

Let $\phi : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ be a real-valued function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times q } ,$ and
let $G : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be a matrix function defined on $S .$ We shall frequently
encounter the problem

minimize
subject to

$$\phi \left( X \right)$$
(1)

$$G \left( X \right) = 0 .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="160" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

This problem is, of course, mathematically equivalent to the case where $X$
and $G$ are vectors rather than matrices, so all theorems remain valid. We
now introduce $m p$ multipliers $\lambda _ { i j }$ (one for each constraint $g _ { i j } \left( X \right) = 0 ,$ $i =$
☒
$1 , \ldots , m$ $j = 1 , \ldots , p ,$ and define the $m \times p$ matrix of Lagrange multipliers
$L = \left( \lambda _ { i j } \right) .$ The Lagrangian function then takes the convenient form

$$\psi \left( X \right) = \phi \left( X \right) - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } G \left( X \right) .$$
(3)


## 16 ECONOMIC INTERPRETATION OF LAGRANGE MULTIPLIERS

Consider the constrained minimization problem


<table>
<tr>
<td>minimize</td>
<td>+(x)</td>
<td>(1)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>subject to</td>
<td>$$g \left( x \right) = b ,$$</td>
<td>(2)</td>
</tr>
</table>


where $\phi$ is a real-valued function defined on an open set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ $g$ is a vector
function defined on $S$ with values in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } \left( m < n \right)$ and $b = \left( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { m } \right) ^ { \prime }$ is a
given $m \times 1$ vector of constants (parameters). In this section we shall examine
how the optimal solution of this constrained minimization problem changes
when the parameters change.

We shall assume that

(i) $\phi$ and g are twice continuously differentiable on $S ,$

(ii) (first-order conditions) there exist points $x _ { 0 } = \left( x _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 0 n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ in $S$ and
$l _ { 0 } = \left( \lambda _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { 0 m } \right) ^ { \prime }$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) = l _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } g \left( x _ { 0 } \right)$$
(3)

$$g \left( x _ { 0 } \right) = b .$$
(4)

Now let

$$B _ { n } = \mathrm { D } g \left( x _ { 0 } \right) , \quad A _ { n n } = \mathrm { H } \phi \left( x _ { 0 } \right) - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \lambda _ { 0 i } \mathrm { H } g _ { i } \left( x _ { 0 } \right) ,$$
(5)

and define, for $r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ,$ $B _ { \eta }$ as the $m \times r$ matrix whose columns are the
first $r$ columns of $B _ { n } ,$ and $A _ { r r }$ as the $r \times r$ matrix in the top left corner of
$A _ { n n } .$ In addition to $\left( \mathrm { i } \right)$ and (ii) we assume that

(iii) $| B _ { m } | \neq 0 ,$
(6)

(iv) (second-order conditions)

$$\left( - 1 \right) ^ { m } | \begin{array}{} 0 & B _ { r } \\ B _ { r } ^ { \prime } & A _ { r r } \end{array} | > 0 \quad \left( r = m + 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="161" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 16 ] Economic interpretation of Lagrange multipliers" -->

These assumptions are sufficient (in fact, more than sufficient) for the function
$\phi$ to have a strict local minimum at $x _ { 0 }$ under the constraint $g \left( x \right) = b$ (see
Theorem 12).

The vectors $x _ { 0 }$ and $l _ { 0 }$ for which the first-order conditions (3) and (4) are
satisfied will, in general, depend on the parameter vector b. The question is
whether $x _ { 0 }$ and $l _ { 0 }$ are differentiable functions of $b .$ Given assumptions (i)-(iv),
this question can be answered in the affirmative. By using the implicit function
theorem (Theorem A.2 in the appendix to this chapter), we can show that
there exists an m-ball $B \left( 0 \right)$ with the origin as its centre, and unique functions
$x ^ { * }$ and $l ^ { * }$ defined on $B \left( 0 \right)$ with values in $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ and $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ respectively, such that

(a) $x ^ { * } \left( 0 \right) = x _ { 0 } ,$ $l ^ { * } \left( 0 \right) = l _ { 0 } ,$

(b) $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) = \left( l ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } g \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right)$ for all $y$ in $B \left( 0 \right) ,$

(c) $g \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) = b$ for all $y$ in $B \left( 0 \right) ,$

(d) the functions $x ^ { * }$ and $l ^ { * }$ are continuously differentiable on $B \left( 0 \right) .$

Now consider the real-valued function $\phi ^ { * }$ defined on $B \left( 0 \right)$ by the equation

$$\phi ^ { * } \left( y \right) = \phi \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) .$$
(8)

We first differentiate both sides of (c). This gives

$$\mathrm { D } g \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) \mathrm { D } x ^ { * } \left( y \right) = I _ { m } ,$$
(9)

using the chain rule. Next we differentiate $\phi ^ { * } .$ Using (again) the chain rule,
(b) and (9), we obtain

$$\mathrm { D } \phi ^ { * } \left( y \right) = \mathrm { D } \phi \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) \mathrm { D } x ^ { * } \left( y \right)$$
$$= \left( l ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } g \left( x ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) \mathrm { D } x ^ { * } \left( y \right)$$
$$= \left( l ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) ^ { \prime } I _ { m } = \left( l ^ { * } \left( y \right) \right) ^ { \prime } .$$

(10)

In particular, at $y = 0 ,$

$$\frac { \partial \phi ^ { * } \left( 0 \right) } { \partial b _ { j } } = \lambda _ { 0 j } \quad \left( j = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$$
(11)

Thus the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda _ { 0 j }$ measures the rate at which the optimal value
of the objective function changes with respect to a small change in the right-
hand side of the j-th constraint. For example, suppose we are maximizing a
firm's profit subject to one resource limitation, then the Lagrange multiplier
$\lambda _ { 0 }$ is the extra profit that could be earned if the firm had one more unit of
the resource, and therefore represents the maximum price the firm is willing
to pay for this additional unit. For this reason $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is often referred to as a
shadow price.

<!-- PageNumber="162" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

1\. In Exercise 12.2, find whether a small relaxation of the constraint will
increase or decrease the optimal function value. At what rate?


## APPENDIX: THE IMPLICIT FUNCTION THEOREM

Let $f : \mathbb{R} ^ { m + k } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a linear function defined by

$$f \left( x ; t \right) = A x + B t ,$$
(1)

where, as the notation indicates, points in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m + k }$ are denoted by $\left( x ; t \right)$ with
$x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $t \in \mathbb{R} ^ { k } .$ If the $m \times m$ matrix $A$ is non-singular, then there exists
a unique function $g : \mathbb{R} ^ { k } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that

(a) $g \left( 0 \right) = 0 ,$

(b) $f \left( g \left( t \right) ; t \right) = 0$ for all $t \in \mathrm { R } ^ { k } ,$

(c) $g$ is infinitely times differentiable on $\mathbb{R} ^ { k } .$

This unique function is, of course,

$$g \left( t \right) = - A ^ { - 1 } B t .$$
(2)

The implicit function theorem asserts that a similar conclusion holds for cer-
tain differentiable transformations $w h i c h$ are not linear. In this appendix we
present, without proof, three versions of the implicit function theorem, each
one being useful in slightly different circumstances.


### Theorem A.1

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ be a vector function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m + k } .$ Denote
points in $S$ by $\left( x ; t \right)$ where $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $t \in \mathbb{R} ^ { k } ,$ and let $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right)$ be an interior
point of $S .$ Assume that

(i) $f \left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) = 0 ,$

(ii) $f$ is differentiable at $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) ,$

(iii) $f$ is differentiable with respect to $x$ in some $\left( m + \right.$ k)-ball $B \left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) ,$

(iv) the $m \times m$ matrix $J \left( x ; t \right) = \partial f \left( x ; t \right) / \partial x ^ { \prime }$ is continuous at $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) ,$

(v) $| J \left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) | \neq 0 .$

Then there exists an open set $T$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { k }$ containing $t _ { 0 } ,$ and a unique function
$g : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that

(a) $g \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = x _ { 0 } ,$

(b) $f \left( g \left( t \right) ; t \right) = 0$ for all $t \in T ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="163" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

(c) $g$ is differentiable at $t _ { 0 } .$


### Theorem A.2

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ be a vector function defined on an open set $S$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m + k } ,$ and
let $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right)$ be a point of $S .$ Assume that

(i) $f \left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) = 0 ,$

(ii) $f$ is continuously differentiable on $S ,$

(iii) the $m \times m$ matrix $J \left( x ; t \right) = \partial f \left( x ; t \right) / \partial x ^ { \prime }$ is non-singular at $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) .$

Then there exists an open set $T$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { k }$ containing $t _ { 0 } ,$ and a unique function
$g : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that

(a) $g \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = x _ { 0 } ,$

(b) $f \left( g \left( t \right) ; t \right) = 0$ for all $t \in T ,$

(c) $g$ is continuously differentiable on $T .$


### Theorem A.3

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ be a vector function defined on a set $S$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m + k } ,$ and let
$\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right)$ be an interior point of $S .$ Assume that

(i) $f \left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) = 0 ,$

(ii) $f$ is $p \geq 2$ times differentiable at $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) ,$

(iii) the $m \times m$ matrix $J \left( x ; t \right) = \partial f \left( x ; t \right) / \partial x ^ { \prime }$ is non-singular at $\left( x _ { 0 } ; t _ { 0 } \right) .$

Then there exists an open set $T$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { k }$ containing $t _ { 0 } ,$ and a unique function
$g : T \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ such that

(a) $g \left( t _ { 0 } \right) = x _ { 0 } ,$

(b) $f \left( g \left( t \right) ; t \right) = 0$ for all $t \in T ,$

(c) g is $p \quad - \quad 1$ times differentiable on $T$ and $p$ times differentiable at $t _ { 0 } .$


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. Apostol (1974, Chapter 13) has a good discussion of implicit functions and
extremum problems. See also Luenberger (1969) and Sydsæter (1981, Chapter
5).

§9 and §14. For an interesting approach to absolute minima with applications

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="164" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Static optimization [Ch. 7" -->

in statistics, see Rolle (1996).

Appendix. There are many versions of the implicit function theorem, but The-
orem A.2 is what most authors would call 'the' implicit function theorem. See
Dieudonné (1969, Theorem 10.2.1) or Apostol (1974, Theorem 13.7). Theo-
rems A.1 and A.3 are less often presented. See, however, Young (1910, Section
38).

<!-- PageBreak -->

Part Three -
Differentials: the practice

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 8 Some important differentials


### 1 INTRODUCTION

Now that we know what differentials are, and have adopted a convenient and
simple notation for them, our next step is to determine the differentials of
some important functions.

In this chapter, $X$ always denotes a matrix (usually square) of real vari-
ables, and $Z$ a matrix of complex variables. We shall discuss the differentials
of some scalar functions of $X$ (eigenvalue, determinant), a vector function
of $X$ (eigenvector) and some matrix functions of $X$ (inverse, Moore-Penrose
inverse, adjoint matrix).

But first we must list the basic rules.


### 2 FUNDAMENTAL RULES OF DIFFERENTIAL CALCULUS

The following rules are easily verified. If $u$ and $v$ are real-valued differentiable
functions and $\alpha$ is a real constant, then we have

$$\mathrm { d } \alpha = 0 ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \left( \alpha u \right) = \alpha \mathrm { d } u ,$$
$$d \left( u + v \right) = d u + d v ,$$
$$d \left( u \quad - \quad v \right) = d u \quad - \quad d v ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \left( u v \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } u \right) v + u \mathrm { d } v ,$$
$$d \left( \frac { u } { v } \right) = \frac { v d u - u d v } { v ^ { 2 } } \quad \left( v \neq 0 \right)$$
(1)
(2)
(3)
(4)
(5)
(6)

<!-- PageNumber="167" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="168" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

The differentials of the power function, logarithmic function and exponential
function are

$$\mathrm { d } u ^ { \alpha } = \alpha u ^ { \alpha - 1 } \mathrm { d } u ,$$
$$d \log u = u ^ { - 1 } d u \quad \left( u > 0 \right)$$
$$\mathrm { d } e ^ { u } = e ^ { u } \mathrm { d } u . ,$$
(7)
(8)
(9)

$$\mathrm { d } \alpha ^ { u } = \alpha ^ { u } \log \alpha \mathrm { d } u \quad \left( \alpha > 0 \right) .$$
(10)

Note. The domain of definition of the power function $u ^ { \alpha }$ depends on the arith-
metical nature of $\alpha .$ If $\alpha$ is a positive integer then $u ^ { \alpha }$ is defined for all real
u; but if $\alpha$ is a negative integer or zero, the point $u = 0$ must be excluded. If
$\alpha$ is a rational fraction, e.g. $\alpha = p / q$ (where $p$ and $q$ are integers and we can
always assume that $\left. q > 0 \right) ,$ then $u ^ { \alpha } = \sqrt [ q ] { u ^ { p } } ,$ so that the function is determined
for all values of $u$ when $q$ is odd, and only for $u \geq 0$ when $q$ is even. In cases
where $\alpha$ is irrational, the function is defined for $u > 0 .$

Similar results hold if $U$ and $V$ are matrix functions, and $A$ is a matrix of
real constants:

$$\mathrm { d } A = 0 ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \left( \alpha U \right) = \alpha \mathrm { d } U ,$$
(11)
(12)

$$\mathrm { d } \left( U + V \right) = \mathrm { d } U + \mathrm { d } V ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \left( U - V \right) = \mathrm { d } U - \mathrm { d } V ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \left( U V \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } U \right) V + U \mathrm { d } V .$$
(13)
(14)
(15)

For the Kronecker product and Hadamard product the analogue of (15) holds:

$$\mathrm { d } \left( U \otimes V \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } U \right) \otimes V + U \otimes \mathrm { d } V ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \left( U \odot V \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } U \right) \odot V + U \odot \mathrm { d } V .$$
(16)
(17)

Finally we have

$$\mathrm { d } U ^ { \prime } = \left( \mathrm { d } U \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(18)

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } U = \mathrm { v e c } \mathrm { d } U ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { t r } U = \mathrm { t r } \mathrm { d } U .$$
(19)
(20)

For example, to prove (3), let $\phi \left( x \right) = u \left( x \right) + v \left( x \right) .$ Then,

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x ; h \right) = \sum _ { j } h _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } \phi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { j } h _ { j } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { j } u \left( x \right) + \mathrm { D } _ { j } v \left( x \right) \right)$$

$$= \sum _ { j } h _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } u \left( x \right) + \sum _ { j } h _ { j } \mathrm { D } _ { j } v \left( x \right) = \mathrm { d } u \left( x ; h \right) + \mathrm { d } v \left( x ; h \right) .$$
(21)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="169" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] The differential of a determinant" -->

As a second example, let us prove (15). Using only (3) and (5), we have

$$\left( \mathrm { d } \left( U V \right) \right) _ { i j } = \mathrm { d } \left( U V \right) _ { i j } = \mathrm { d } \sum _ { k } u _ { i k } v _ { k j } = \sum _ { k } \mathrm { d } \left( u _ { i k } v _ { k j } \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { k } \left[ \left( \mathrm { d } u _ { i k } \right) v _ { k j } + u _ { i k } \mathrm { d } v _ { k j } \right]$$
$$= \sum _ { k } \left( \mathrm { d } u _ { i k } \right) v _ { k j } + \sum _ { k } u _ { i k } \mathrm { d } v _ { k j }$$
$$= \left( \left( \mathrm { d } U \right) V \right) _ { i j } + \left( U \mathrm { d } V \right) _ { i j } .$$

(22)

Hence (15) follows.


#### Exercises

1\. Prove (16).

2\. Show that $\mathrm { d } \left( U V W \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } U \right) V W + U \left( \mathrm { d } V \right) W + U V \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) .$

3\. Show that $\mathrm { d } \left( A X B \right) = A \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) B ,$ $A$ and $B$ constant.

4\. Show that $\mathrm { d } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X = 2 \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X$ .

5\. Let $u : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on an open subset $S$ of
$\mathbb{R} ^ { n } .$ If $u ^ { \prime } u = 1$ on $S ,$ then $u ^ { \prime } d u = 0$ on $S .$


### 3 THE DIFFERENTIAL OF $\mathrm { A }$ DETERMINANT

Let us now apply these rules to obtain a number of useful results. The first
of these is the differential of the determinant.


#### Theorem 1

Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathrm { I R } ^ { n \times q } .$ If the matrix function $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } \left( m \geq \right.$
2) is $k$ times (continuously) differentiable on $S ,$ then so is the real-valued
function $| F | : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ given by | $F | \left( X \right) = | F \left( X \right) | .$ Moreover,
☒

$$\mathrm { d } | F | = \mathrm { t r } F ^ { \# } \mathrm { d } F ,$$
(1)

where $F ^ { \# } \left( X \right) = \left( F \left( X \right) \right) ^ { \# }$ denotes the adjoint matrix of $F \left( X \right) .$ In particular,
☒

$$\mathrm { d } | F | = | F | \mathrm { t r } F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } F$$
(2)

at points $X$ with $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m .$ Also,

$$\mathrm { d } | F | = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { p + 1 } \mu \left( F \right) \frac { v ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) u } { v ^ { \prime } \left( F ^ { p - 1 } \right) ^ { + } u }$$
(3)

at points $X$ with $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m \quad - \quad 1 .$ Here $p$ denotes the multiplicity of the
☒
zero eigenvalue of $F \left( X \right) ,$ $1 \leq p \leq m ,$ $\mu \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$ is the product of the $m \quad - \quad p$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="170" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

non-zero eigenvalues of $F \left( X \right)$ if $p < m$ and $\mu \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = 1$ if $p = m ,$ and the
☒ ☒
$m \times 1$ vectors $u$ and $v$ satisfy $F \left( X \right) u = F ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) v = 0 .$ And finally,
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { d } | F | = 0$$
(4)

at points $X$ with $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) \leq m - 2 .$
☒

Proof. Consider the real-valued function $\phi : \mathrm { I R } ^ { m \times m } \rightarrow \mathrm { I R }$ defined by $\phi \left( Y \right) =$
$| Y | .$ Clearly, $\phi$ is $\infty$ times differentiable at every point of $\mathrm { R } ^ { m \times m } .$ If $Y = \left( y _ { i j } \right)$
and $c _ { i j }$ is the cofactor of $y _ { i j } ,$ then by (1.9.7),

$$\phi \left( Y \right) = | Y | = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } c _ { i j } y _ { i j } ,$$
(5)

and since $c _ { 1 j } , \ldots , c _ { m j }$ do not depend on $y _ { i j } ,$ we have

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( Y \right) } { \partial y _ { i j } } = c _ { i j } .$$
(6)

From these partial derivatives we obtain the differential

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( Y \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } c _ { i j } \mathrm { d } y _ { i j } = \mathrm { t r } Y ^ { \# } \mathrm { d } Y .$$
(7)

Now, since the function $| F |$ is the composite of $\phi$ and $F ,$ Cauchy's rule of
invariance (Theorem 5.9) applies, and

$$\mathrm { d } | F | = \mathrm { t r } F ^ { \# } \mathrm { d } F .$$
(8)

The remainder of the theorem follows from Theorem 3.1.

☐
✷

It is worth stressing that at points where $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m \quad - \quad 1 ,$ $F \left( X \right)$ must
☒ ☒
have $a t$ least one zero eigenvalue. At points where $F \left( X \right)$ has a simple zero
☒
eigenvalue (and where, consequently, $\left. r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m - 1 \right) ,$ (3) simplifies to
☒

$$\mathrm { d } | F | = \mu \left( F \right) \frac { v ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) u } { v ^ { \prime } u } ,$$
(9)

where $\mu \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$ is the product of the $m \quad - \quad 1$ non-zero eigenvalues of $F \left( X \right) .$
☒ ☒

We do not, at this point, derive the second- and higher-order differentials
of the determinant function. In Section 4 (Exercises 1 and 2) we obtain the
differentials of $\log | F |$ assuming that $F \left( X \right)$ is non-singular. To obtain the
☒
general result we need the differential of the adjoint matrix. A formula for the
first differential of the adjoint matrix will be obtained in Section 6.

Result (2), the case where $F \left( X \right)$ is non-singular, is of great practical in-
☒
terest. At points where $| F \left( X \right) |$ is positive, its logarithm exists and we arrive
☒
at the following theorem.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="171" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The differential of an inverse" -->


#### Theorem 2

Let $T _ { + }$ denote the set

$$T _ { + } = \left\{ Y : Y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } , | Y | > 0 \right\} .$$
(10)

Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } .$ If the matrix function $F : S \rightarrow T _ { + }$ is $k$
times (continuously) differentiable on $S ,$ then so is the real-valued function
$\log | F | : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ given by $\left( \log | F | \right) \left( X \right) = \log | F \left( X \right) | .$ Moreover
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { d } \log | F | = \mathrm { t r } F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } F .$$
(11)

Proof. Immediate from (2) in Theorem 1.

☐
✷


#### Exercises

1\. Give an intuitive explanation of the fact that $\mathrm { d } | X | = 0$ at points $X \in$
$\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times n }$ where $r \left( X \right) \leq n - 2 .$
☒ ☒

2\. Show that, if $F \left( X \right) \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m }$ and $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m \quad - \quad 1$ for every $X$ in some
☒ ☒

neighbourhood of $X _ { 0 } ,$ then $\mathrm { d } | F \left( X \right) | = 0$ at $X _ { 0 } .$
☒

3\. Show that $\mathrm { d } \log | X ^ { \prime } X | = 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X$ at every point where $X$ has
☒ ☒ ☒
full column rank.


### 4 THE DIFFERENTIAL OF AN INVERSE

The next theorem deals with the differential of the inverse function.


### Theorem 3

Let $T$ be the set of non-singular real $m \times m$ matrices, i.e. $T = \left\{ Y : Y \in \right.$
$\mathrm { R } ^ { m \times m } ,$ $\left. | Y | \neq 0 \right] .$ Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } .$ If the matrix function
$F : S \rightarrow T$ is $k$ times (continuously) differentiable on $S ,$ then so is the matrix
function $F ^ { - 1 } : S \rightarrow T$ defined by $\dot { F } ^ { - 1 } \left( X \right) = \left( F \left( X \right) \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$ and
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { - 1 } = - F ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { - 1 } .$$
(1)

Proof. Let $A _ { i j } \left( X \right)$ be the $\left( m - 1 \right) \times \left( m - 1 \right)$ submatrix of $F \left( X \right)$ obtained by
☒ ☒
deleting row $i$ and column $j$ of $F \left( X \right) .$ The typical element of $F ^ { - 1 } \left( X \right)$ can
☒ ☒
then be expressed as

$$\left[ F ^ { - 1 } \left( X \right) \right] _ { i j } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } | A _ { j i } \left( X \right) | / | F \left( X \right) | .$$ ☒ ☒
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="172" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

Since both determinants | $| A _ { j i }$ and | $F |$ are $k$ times (continuously) differentiable
on $S ,$ the same is true for their ratio and hence for the matrix function $F ^ { - 1 } .$
To prove (1) we then write

$$0 = \mathrm { d } I = \mathrm { d } F ^ { - 1 } F = \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { - 1 } \right) F + F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } F ,$$
(3)

and post-multiply with $F ^ { - 1 } .$

✷
☐

Let us consider $\mathrm { t h e } \mathrm { s e t } T$ of non-singular real $m \times m$ matrices. $T$ is an open
subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } ,$ so that for every $Y _ { 0 } \in T$ there exists an open neighbourhood
$N \left( Y _ { 0 } \right)$ all of whose points are non-singular. This follows from the continuity of
☒
the determinant function $| Y | .$ Put differently, if $Y _ { 0 }$ is non-singular and $\left\{ E _ { j } \right\}$
☒
is a sequence of real $m \times m$ matrices such that $E _ { j } \rightarrow 0$ as $j \rightarrow \infty ,$ then

$$r \left( Y _ { 0 } + E _ { j } \right) = r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right)$$ ☒
(4)

for every greater $j$ than some fixed $j _ { 0 } ,$ and

$$\lim _ { j \rightarrow \infty } \left( Y _ { 0 } + E _ { j } \right) ^ { - 1 } = Y _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } .$$
(5)


### Exercises

1\. Let $T _ { + } = \left\{ Y : Y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } , | Y | > 0 \right\}$ . If $F : S \rightarrow T _ { + } ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ is twice
differentiable on $S ,$ then show that

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \log | F | = - \mathrm { t r } \left( F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } F \right) ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { t r } F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F .$$

2\. Show that, for $X \in T _ { + } , \log | X | \text { is } \infty$ on $T _ { + } ,$ and
☒

$$d ^ { r } \log | X | = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { r - 1 } \left( r - 1 \right) ! \mathrm { t r } \left( X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { r } \quad \left( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

3\. Let $T = \left\{ Y : Y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } , | Y | \neq 0 \right\} .$ If $F : S \rightarrow T ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ is twice
☒ ☒
differentiable on $S ,$ then show

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F ^ { - 1 } = 2 \left[ F ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) \right] ^ { 2 } F ^ { - 1 } - F ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F \right) F ^ { - 1 } .$$

4\. Show that, for $X \in T ,$ $X ^ { - 1 }$ is $\infty$ times differentiable on $T ,$ and
☒

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { r } X ^ { - 1 } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { r } r ! \left( X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { r } X ^ { - 1 } \quad \left( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒


## 5 DIFFERENTIAL OF THE MOORE-PENROSE INVERSE

Equation (4.4) above and Exercise 5.15.1 tell us that non-singular matrices
have locally constant rank. Singular matrices (more precisely matrices of less
than full row or column rank) do not share this property. Consider, for exam-
ple, the matrices

$$Y _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right)$$

and
$$E _ { j } = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 / j \end{array} \right) ,$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="173" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Differential of the Moore-Penrose inverse" -->

and let $Y = Y \left( j \right) = Y _ { 0 } + E _ { j } .$ Then $r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right) = 1 ,$ but $r \left( Y \right) = 2$ for all $j .$ Moreover,
☒ ☒
$Y \rightarrow Y _ { 0 }$ as $j \rightarrow \infty$ , but

$$Y ^ { + } = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & j \end{array} \right)$$
(2)

does certainly not converge to $Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } ,$ because it does not converge to anything.
It follows that (i) $r \left( Y \right)$ is not constant in any neighbourhood of $Y _ { 0 } ,$ and (ii)
☒
$Y ^ { + }$ is not continuous at $Y _ { 0 } .$ The following lemma shows that the conjoint
occurrence of (i) and (ii) is typical.


### Lemma 1

Let $Y _ { 0 } \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ and let $\left\{ E _ { j } \right\}$ be a sequence of real $m \times p$ matrices such that
$E _ { j } \rightarrow 0$ as $j \rightarrow \infty .$ Then

$$r \left( Y _ { 0 } + E _ { j } \right) = r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right) \quad \text { for every } j \geq j _ { 0 }$$ ☒
(3)

if and only if

$$\lim _ { j \rightarrow \infty } \left( Y _ { 0 } + E _ { j } \right) ^ { + } = Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } .$$
(4)

Lemma 1 tells us that if $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p } ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ is a matrix function
defined and continuous on $S ,$ then $F ^ { + } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p \times m }$ is continuous on $S$ if and
only if $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$ is constant on $S .$ If $F ^ { + }$ is to be differentiable at $X _ { 0 } \in S$ it must
☒
be continuous at $X _ { 0 } ,$ hence of constant rank in some neighbourhood $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right)$
of $X _ { 0 } .$ Provided that $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$ is constant in $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) ,$ the differentiability of
☒ ☒ ☒
$F$ at $X _ { 0 }$ implies the differentiability of $F ^ { + }$ at $X _ { 0 } .$ In fact, we have the next
lemma.


### Lemma 2

Let $X _ { 0 }$ be an interior point of a subset $S$ of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } .$ Let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be
a matrix function defined on $S$ and $k \geq 1$ times (continuously) differentiable
at each point of some neighbourhood $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \subset S$ of $X _ { 0 } .$ Then the following
☒
three statements are equivalent:

(i) the rank of $F \left( X \right)$ is constant on $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) ,$
☒ ☒

(ii) $F ^ { + }$ is continuous on $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) ,$
☒

(iii) $F ^ { + }$ is $k$ times (continuously) differentiable on $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) .$
☒

Having established the existence of differentiable Moore-Penrose (MP) in-
verses, we now want to find the relationship between $\mathrm { d } F ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { d } F .$ First, we
find $\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F$ and $\mathrm { d } F F ^ { + }$ then we use these results to obtain $\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } .$


### Theorem 4

Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times q } ,$ and let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be a matrix function

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="174" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

defined and $k \geq 1$ times (continuously) differentiable on $S .$ If $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right.$ is
constant on $S ,$ then $F ^ { + } F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p \times p }$ and $F F ^ { + } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m }$ are $k$ times
(continuously) differentiable on $S ,$ and

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F = F ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) \left( I _ { p } - F ^ { + } F \right) + \left( F ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) \left( I _ { p } - F ^ { + } F \right) \right) ^ { \prime }$$
(5)

and

$$\mathrm { d } F F ^ { + } = \left( I _ { m } - F F ^ { + } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { + } + \left( \left( I _ { m } - F F ^ { + } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { + } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)

Proof. Let us demonstrate the first result, leaving the second as an exercise
for the reader.

Since the matrix $F ^ { + } F$ is idempotent and symmetric, we have

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F = \mathrm { d } \left( F ^ { + } F F ^ { + } F \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) F ^ { + } F + F ^ { + } F \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right)$$

$$= F ^ { + } F \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) + \left( F ^ { + } F \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(7)

To find $\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F$ it suffices therefore to find $F \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) .$ But this is easy, since
the equality

$$\mathrm { d } F = \mathrm { d } \left( F F ^ { + } F \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) \left( F ^ { + } F \right) + F \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right)$$
(8)

can be rearranged as

$$F \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) \left( I - F ^ { + } F \right) .$$
(9)

The result follows by inserting (9) into (7).

✷
☐

We now have all the ingredients for the main result.


### Theorem 5

Let $S$ be an open subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ and let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times p }$ be a matrix function
defined and $k \geq 1$ times (continuously) differentiable on $S .$ If $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$ is
constant on $S ,$ then $F ^ { + } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { p \times m }$ is $k$ times (continuously) differentiable
on $S ,$ and

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } = - F ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { + } + F ^ { + } F ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \prime } \right) \left( I _ { m } - F F ^ { + } \right)$$
$$+ \left( I _ { p } - F ^ { + } F \right) \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \prime } \right) F ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } F ^ { + } .$$

(10)

Proof. The strategy of the proof is to express $\mathrm { d } F ^ { + }$ in $\mathrm { d } F F ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F ,$ and
apply Theorem 4. We have

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } = \mathrm { d } \left( F ^ { + } F F ^ { + } \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) F ^ { + } + F ^ { + } F \mathrm { d } F ^ { + }$$
(11)

and also

$$\mathrm { d } F F ^ { + } = \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { + } + F \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } .$$
(12)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="175" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] The differential of the adjoint matrix" -->

Inserting the expression for $F \mathrm { d } F ^ { + }$ from (12) into the last term of (11), we
obtain

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { + } = \left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } F \right) F ^ { + } + F ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } F F ^ { + } \right) - F ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { + } .$$
(13)

Application of Theorem 4 gives the desired result.
☐
✷


### Exercises

1\. Prove (6).

2\. If $F \left( X \right)$ is idempotent for every $X$ in some neighbourhood of a point $X _ { 0 } ,$
then $F$ is said to be locally idempotent at $X _ { 0 } .$ Show that $F \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F = 0$
at points where $F$ is differentiable and locally idempotent.

3\. If $F \quad i s \quad l o c a l l y$ idempotent at $X _ { 0 }$ and continuous in a neighbourhood of
$X _ { 0 } ,$ then $\mathrm { t r } F$ is differentiable at $X _ { 0 }$ with $\mathrm { d } \left( \mathrm { t r } F \right) \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$

4\. If $F$ has locally constant rank at $X _ { 0 }$ and is continuous in a neighbour-
hood of $X _ { 0 } ,$ then tr $F ^ { + } F$ and $\mathrm { t r } F F ^ { + }$ are differentiable at $X _ { 0 }$ with
$\mathrm { d } \left( \mathrm { t r } F ^ { + } F \right) \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = \mathrm { d } \left( \mathrm { t r } F F ^ { + } \right) \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$

5\. If $F$ has locally constant rank at $X _ { 0 }$ and is differentiable in a neighbour-
hood of $X _ { 0 } ,$ then $\mathrm { t r } F \mathrm { d } F ^ { + } = - \mathrm { t r } F ^ { + } \mathrm { d } F .$


## 6 THE DIFFERENTIAL OF THE ADJOINT MATRIX

If $Y$ is a real $m \times m$ matrix, then by $Y ^ { \# }$ we denote the $m \times m$ adjoint matrix
of $Y .$ Given an $m \times m$ matrix function F we now define an $m \times m$ matrix
function $F ^ { \# }$ by $F ^ { \# } \left( X \right) = \left( F \left( X \right) \right) ^ { \# } .$ The purpose of this section is to find the
differential on $F ^ { \# } .$ We first prove Theorem 6.


## Theorem 6

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ and let $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } \left( m \geq 2 \right)$ be a matrix
function defined on $S .$ If $F$ is $k$ times (continuously) differentiable at a point
$X _ { 0 }$ of $S ,$ then so is the matrix function $F ^ { \# } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m }$ and at $X _ { 0 } ,$

$$\left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \# } \right) _ { i j } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } \mathrm { t r } E _ { i } \left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# } E _ { j } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } F \quad \left( i , j = 1 , \ldots , m \right) ,$$
(1)

where $E _ { i }$ denotes the $m \times \left( m - 1 \right)$ matrix obtained from $I _ { m }$ by deleting column
$i .$

Note. The matrix $E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i }$ is obtained from $F \left( X \right)$ by deleting row $j$ and
column i; the matrix $E _ { i } \left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# } E _ { j } ^ { \prime }$ is obtained from $\left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# }$ by
inserting a row of zeros between rows $\ddot { i } - 1$ and i, and a column of zeros
between columns $j \quad - \quad 1$ and $j .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="176" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

Proof. Since, by definition (see Section 1.9),

$$\left( F ^ { \# } \left( X \right) \right) _ { i j } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } | E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } | ,$$ ☒ ☒
(2)

we have from Theorem 1,

$$\left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \# } \left( X \right) \right) _ { i j } = \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } \mathrm { t r } \left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# } \mathrm { d } \left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } \right)$$
$$= \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } \mathrm { t r } \left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# } E _ { j } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) \right) E _ { i }$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$= \left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } \mathrm { t r } E _ { i } \left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X \right) E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# } E _ { j } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒
(3)

and the result follows.

☐

✷

Recall from Theorem 3.2 that if $Y = F \left( X \right)$ is an $m \times m$ matrix and $m \geq 2 ,$
☒
then the rank of $Y ^ { \# } = F ^ { \# } \left( X \right)$ is given by
☒

$$r \left( Y ^ { \# } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} m , & \mathrm { i f } r \left( Y \right) = m , \\ 1 , & \mathrm { i f } r \left( Y \right) = m - 1 , \\ 0 , & \mathrm { i f } r \left( Y \right) \leq m - 2 . \end{array} \right. ,$$
(4)

As a result, two special cases of Theorem 6 can be proved. The first relates
to the situation where $F \left( X _ { 0 } \right)$ is non-singular.
☒


### Corollary 1

If $F : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m } \left( m \geq 2 \right) ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ is $k$ times (continuously) differentiable
at a point $X _ { 0 } \in S$ where $F \left( X _ { 0 } \right)$ is non-singular, then $F ^ { \# } : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m }$ is also
☒
$k$ times (continuously) differentiable at $X _ { 0 } ,$ and the differential at that point
is given by

$$\mathrm { d } F ^ { \# } = | F | \left[ \left( \mathrm { t r } F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { - 1 } - F ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { - 1 } \right]$$
(5)

or equivalently,

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F ^ { \# } = | F | \left[ \left( \mathrm { v e c } F ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( F ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } - \left( F ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \otimes F ^ { - 1 } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F .$$
(6)

Proof. To demonstrate this result as a special case of Theorem 6 is somewhat
involved, and is left to the reader. Much simpler is to write $F ^ { \# } = | F | F ^ { - 1 }$ and
use the facts, established in Theorems 1 and 3, that $\mathrm { d } | F | = | F | \mathrm { t r } F ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } F$ and
$\mathrm { d } F ^ { - 1 } = - F ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } F \right) F ^ { - 1 } .$ Details of the proof are left to the reader.
☐
✷

The second special case of Theorem 6 concerns points where the rank of
$F \left( X _ { 0 } \right)$ does not exceed $m \quad - \quad 3 .$
☒


## Corollary 2

Let $F : S \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m \times m } \left( m \geq 3 \right) ,$ $S \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times q } ,$ be differentiable at a point $X _ { 0 } \in S .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="177" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7] On differentiating eigenvalues and eigenvectors" -->

If $r \left( F \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \right) \leq m - 3 ,$ then

$$\left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \# } \right) \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$$
(7)

Proof. Since the rank of the $\left( m - 1 \right) \times \left( m - 1 \right)$ matrix $E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X _ { 0 } \right) E _ { i }$ in Theorem
6 cannot exceed $m \quad - \quad 3 ,$ it follows by (4) that its adjoint matrix is the null
matrix. Inserting $\left( E _ { j } ^ { \prime } F \left( X _ { 0 } \right) E _ { i } \right) ^ { \# } = 0$ in (1) gives $\left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \# } \right) \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$
☒ ☐
✷

There is another, more illuminating, proof of Corollary 2 - one which
does not depend on Theorem 6. Let $Y _ { 0 } \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m \times m }$ and assume $Y _ { 0 }$ is singular.
Then $r \left( Y \right)$ is not locally constant at $Y _ { 0 } .$ In fact, if $r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right) = r \left( 1 \leq r \leq m - 1 \right)$
☒ ☒
and we perturb one element of $Y _ { 0 } ,$ then the rank of $\widetilde { Y } _ { 0 }$ (the perturbed matrix)
will be $r -$ 1,r, or $r + 1 .$ An immediate consequence of this simple observation
is that if $r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right)$ does not exceed $m \quad - \quad 3 ,$ then $r \left( \widetilde { Y } _ { 0 } \right)$ will not exceed $m \quad - \quad 2 .$ But
☒ ☒

this means that at points $Y _ { 0 }$ with $r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right) \leq m - 3 ,$
☒

$$\left( \widetilde { Y } _ { 0 } \right) ^ { \# } = Y _ { 0 } ^ { \# } = 0 ,$$
(8)

implying that the differential of $Y ^ { \# }$ at $Y _ { 0 }$ must be the null matrix.

These two corollaries provide expressions for $\mathrm { d } F ^ { \# }$ at every point $X$ where
$r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m o r$ $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) \leq m - 3 .$ The remaining points to consider are those
☒ ☒
where $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right)$ is either $m - \quad 1$ or $m - 2 .$ At such points we must unfortunately
☒

use Theorem 6, which holds irrespective of rank considerations.

Only if we know that the rank of $F \left( X \right)$ is locally constant can we say more.
If $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m \quad - \quad 2$ for every $X$ in some neighbourhood $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right)$ of $X _ { 0 } ,$ then
☒

☒ ☒ ☒
$F ^ { \# } \left( X \right)$ vanishes in that neighbourhood, and hence $\left( \mathrm { d } F ^ { \# } \right) \left( X \right) = 0$ for every
☒ ☒
$X \in N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) .$ More complicated is the situation where $r \left( F \left( X \right) \right) = m - 1$ in some
☒
neighbourhood of $X _ { 0 } .$ A discussion of this case is postponed to Miscellaneous
Exercise 6 at the end of this chapter.


## Exercise

1\. The matrix function $F : \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n }$ defined by $F \left( X \right) = X ^ { \# }$ is $\infty$
☒
times differentiable on $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n } ,$ and $\left( \mathrm { d } ^ { j } F \right) \left( X \right) = 0$ for every $j \leq n - 2 -$
☒
$r \left( X \right) .$
☒


## 7 ON DIFFERENTIATING EIGENVALUES AND EIGEN- VECTORS

There are two problems involved in differentiating eigenvalues and eigenvec-
tors. The first problem is that the eigenvalues of a real matrix $A$ need not, in
general, be real numbers - they may be complex. The second problem is the
possible occurrence of multiple eigenvalues.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="178" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

To appreciate the first point, consider the real $2 \times 2$ matrix function

$$A \left( \epsilon \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & \epsilon \\ - \epsilon & 1 \end{array} \right) , \quad \epsilon \neq 0 . ,$$
(1)

The matrix $A$ is not symmetric, and its eigenvalues are $1 \pm i \epsilon .$ Since both
eigenvalues are complex, the corresponding eigenvectors must be complex as
well; in fact, they can be chosen as

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { i } \end{array} \right)$$

and

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - i } \end{array} \right)$$
(2)

We know however (Theorem 1.4), that if $A$ is a real symmetric matrix, then
its eigenvalues are real and its eigenvectors can always be taken to be real.
Since the derivations in the real symmetric case are somewhat simpler, we
consider this case first.

Thus, let $X _ { 0 }$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix, and let $u _ { 0 }$ be a (normal-
ized) eigenvector associated with an eigenvalue $\lambda _ { 0 }$ of $X _ { 0 } ,$ so that the triple
$\left( X _ { 0 } , u _ { 0 } , \lambda _ { 0 } \right)$ satisfies the equations
☒

$$X u = \lambda u , \quad u ^ { \prime } u = 1 .$$
(3)

Since the $n + 1$ equations in (3) are implicit relations rather than explicit
functions, we must first show that there exist explicit unique functions $\lambda =$
$\lambda \left( X \right)$ and $u = u \left( X \right)$ satisfying (3) in a neighbourhood of $X _ { 0 }$ and such that
☒ ☒
$\lambda \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { 0 }$ and $u \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = u _ { 0 } .$ Here the second (and more serious) problem
☒ ☒
arises - the possible occurrence of multiple eigenvalues.

We shall see that the implicit function theorem (given in the appendix
to Chapter $\left. 7 \right)$ implies the existence of a neighbourhood $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \subset \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n }$ of
$X _ { 0 }$ where the functions $\lambda$ and $u$ both exist and are $\infty$ times (continuously)
☒
differentiable, provided $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is a simple eigenvalue of $X _ { 0 } .$ If, however, $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is a
multiple eigenvalue of $X _ { 0 } ,$ then the conditions of the implicit function theo-
rem are not satisfied. The difficulty is illustrated by the following example.
Consider the real $2 \times 2$ matrix function

$$A \left( \epsilon , \delta \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 + \epsilon & \delta \\ \delta & 1 - \epsilon \end{array} \right) .$$
(4)

The matrix $A$ is symmetric for every value of $\epsilon$ and $\delta$ its eigenvalues are
$\lambda _ { 1 } = 1 + \sqrt { \left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } + \delta ^ { 2 } \right) }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 } = 1 - \sqrt { \left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } + \delta ^ { 2 } \right) } .$ Both eigenvalue functions
☒ ☒
are continuous in $\epsilon$ and $\delta ,$ but clearly not differentiable at $\left( 0 , 0 \right) .$ (Strictly
speaking we should also prove that $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ are the only two continuous
eigenvalue functions.) The conical surface formed by the eigenvalues of $A \left( \epsilon , \delta \right)$
has a singularity at $\epsilon = \delta = 0$ (Figure 1). For a fixed ratio $\epsilon / \delta$ however, we can
pass from one side of the surface to the other going through $\left( 0 , 0 \right)$ without
noticing the singularity. This phenomenon is quite general and it indicates
the need to restrict our study of differentiability of multiple eigenvalues to
one-dimensional perturbations only. We shall delay a further discussion of
multiple eigenvalues to Section 12.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] The differential of eigenvalues and eigenvectors: symmetric case 179" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 1 The eigenvalue functions $\lambda _ { 1 , 2 } = 1 \pm \sqrt { \left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } + \delta ^ { 2 } \right) }$</figcaption>

δ

</figure>


## 8 THE DIFFERENTIAL OF EIGENVALUES AND EIGEN- VECTORS: SYMMETRIC CASE

Let us now demonstrate the following theorem.


### Theorem 7

Let $X _ { 0 }$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix. Let $u _ { 0 }$ be a normalized eigenvector
associated with a simple eigenvalue $\lambda _ { 0 }$ of $X _ { 0 } .$ Then a real-valued function $\lambda$
and a vector function $u$ are defined for all $X$ in some neighbourhood $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \subset$
$\mathrm { R } ^ { n \times n }$ of $X _ { 0 } ,$ such that

$$\lambda \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { 0 } , \quad u \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = u _ { 0 } ,$$
(1)

and

$$X u = \lambda u , \quad u ^ { \prime } u = 1 \quad \left( X \in N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \right) .$$
(2)

Moreover, the functions $\lambda$ and $u$ are $\infty$ times differentiable on $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) ,$ and
the differentials at $X _ { 0 }$ are

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 }$$
(3)

and

$$\mathrm { d } u = \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } .$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="180" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

Note. In order for $\lambda$ (and $\left. u \right)$ to be differentiable at $X _ { 0 }$ we require $\lambda _ { 0 }$ to be
simple, but this does not, of course, exclude the possibility of multiplicities
among the remaining $n \quad - \quad 1$ eigenvalues of $X _ { 0 } .$

Proof. Consider the vector function $f : \mathbb{R} ^ { n + 1 } \times \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { n + 1 }$ defined by the
equation

$$f \left( u , \lambda ; X \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \left( \lambda I _ { n } - X \right) u & \\ u ^ { \prime } u - 1 & \end{array} \right) ,$$
(5)

and observe that $f$ is $\infty$ times differentiable on $\mathbb{R} ^ { n + 1 } \times \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n } .$ The point
$\left( u _ { 0 } , \lambda _ { 0 } ; X _ { 0 } \right)$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n + 1 } \times \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n }$ satisfies

$$f \left( u _ { 0 } , \lambda _ { 0 } ; X _ { 0 } \right) = 0$$
(6)

and

$$| \begin{array}{} \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - X _ { 0 } & u _ { 0 } \\ 2 u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} | \neq 0 .$$
(7)

We note that the determinant in (7) is non-zero if and only if the eigenvalue
$\lambda _ { 0 }$ is simple, in which case it takes the value of $- 2$ times the product of the
$n \quad - \quad 1$ non-zero eigenvalues of $\lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - X _ { 0 }$ (see Theorem 3.5).

The conditions of the implicit function theorem (Theorem A.3 in the
appendix to Chapter 7) thus being satisfied, there exist a neighbourhood
$N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \subset \mathrm { R } ^ { n \times n }$ of $X _ { 0 } ,$ a unique real-valued function $\lambda : N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ,$ and a
unique (apart from its sign) vector function $u : N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ such that

(a) $\lambda$ and $u$ are $\infty$ times differentiable on $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) ,$

(b) $\lambda \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { 0 } ,$ $u \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = u _ { 0 } ,$

(c) $X u = \lambda u ,$ $u ^ { \prime } u = 1$ for every $X \in N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) .$

This completes the first part of our proof.

Let us now derive an explicit expression for $\mathrm { d } \lambda .$ From $X u = \lambda u$ we obtain

$$\left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } + X _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = \left( \mathrm { d } \lambda \right) u _ { 0 } + \lambda _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u ,$$
(8)

where the differentials $\mathrm { d } \lambda$ and $\mathrm { d } u$ are defined at $X _ { 0 } .$ Pre-multiplying by $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$
gives

$$u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } X _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = \left( \mathrm { d } \lambda \right) u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } u _ { 0 } + \lambda _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } u .$$
(9)

Since $X _ { 0 }$ is symmetric we have $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } X _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .$ Hence

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } ,$$
(10)

because the eigenvector $u _ { 0 }$ is normalized by $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } u _ { 0 } = 1 .$ The normalization
of $u$ is not important here; it is important however, in order to obtain an

<!-- PageBreak -->

Sec. 8 ] The differential of eigenvalues and eigenvectors: symmetric case 181

expression for $\mathrm { d } u .$ To this we now turn. Let $Y _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - X _ { 0 }$ and rewrite (8)
as

$$Y _ { 0 } d u = \left( d X \right) u _ { 0 } - \left( d \lambda \right) u _ { 0 } .$$
(11)

Pre-multiplying by $Y _ { 0 } ^ { + }$ we obtain

$$Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } ,$$
(12)

because $Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } u _ { 0 } = 0$ (Exercise 1). To complete the proof we need only show
that

$$Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } d u = d u .$$
(13)

To prove (13), let

$$C _ { 0 } = Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .$$
(14)

The matrix $C _ { 0 }$ is symmetric idempotent (because $\left. Y _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } = Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } u _ { 0 } = 0 \right) ,$ so that
$r \left( C _ { 0 } \right) = r \left( Y _ { 0 } \right) + 1 = n .$ Hence, $C _ { 0 } = I _ { n }$ and

$$\mathrm { d } u = C _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = \left( Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } u = Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u ,$$
(15)

since $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } u = 0$ because of the normalization $u ^ { \prime } u = 1 .$ (See Exercise 2.5.) This
shows that (13) holds, and concludes the proof.
☐
✷

Note 1. We have chosen to normalize the eigenvector $u$ by $u ^ { \prime } u = 1 ,$ which
means that $u$ is a point on the unit ball. This is, however, not the only pos-
sibility. Another normalization,

$$u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } u = 1 ,$$
(16)

though less common, is in many ways more appropriate. The reason for this
will become clear when we discuss the complex case (Section 9). If the eigen-
vectors are normalized according to (16), then $u$ is a point in the hyperplane
tangent (at $u _ { 0 }$ to the unit ball. In either case we obtain $u ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } u = 0$ at $X = X _ { 0 } ,$
which is all that is needed in the proof.

Note 2. It is important to note that, while $X _ { 0 }$ is symmetric, the perturbations
are not assumed to be symmetric. For symmetric perturbations, application
of Theorem 2.2 and the chain rule immediately yields

$$\left. \mathrm { d } \lambda = \left( u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) D \mathrm { d } v \left( X \right) , \quad \mathrm { d } u = \left( u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \right) D \mathrm { d } v \left( X \right) ,$$
(17)

where $D$ is the duplication matrix (see Chapter 3).


### Exercises

1\. If $A = A ^ { \prime } ,$ then $A b = 0$ if and only if $A ^ { + } b = 0 .$

2\. Consider the symmetric $2 \times 2$ matrix

$$X _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & - 1 \end{array} \right) .$$

When $\lambda _ { 0 } = 1$ show that, at $X _ { 0 } ,$
☒

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = \mathrm { d } x _ { 1 1 } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathrm { d } u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } x _ { 2 1 } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} 0 \\ 1 \end{array} \right) ,$$

and derive the corresponding result when $\lambda _ { 0 } = - 1 .$ Interpret these re-
sults.

3\. Now consider the matrix function

$$A \left( \epsilon \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & \epsilon \\ \epsilon & - 1 \end{array} \right) .$$

Plot a graph of the two eigenvalue functions $\lambda _ { 1 } \left( \epsilon \right)$ and $\lambda _ { 2 } \left( \epsilon \right) ,$ and show
that the derivative at $\epsilon = 0$ vanishes. Also obtain this result directly
from the previous exercise.

4\. Consider the symmetric matrix

$$X _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & \sqrt { 3 } \\ 0 & \sqrt { 3 } & 6 \end{array} \right) .$$ ☒

Show that the eigenvalues of $X _ { 0 } \text { are } 3$ and 7, and prove that at $X _ { 0 }$
the differentials of the eigenvalue- and eigenvector-function associated
with the eigenvalue 7 are

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \mathrm { d } x _ { 2 2 } + \left( \mathrm { d } x _ { 2 3 } + \mathrm { d } x _ { 3 2 } \right) \sqrt { 3 } + 3 \mathrm { d } x _ { 3 3 } \right]$$ ☒

and

$$d u = \frac { 1 } { 3 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} 4 & 0 & 0 & 4 \sqrt { 3 } & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & - \sqrt { 3 } & 0 & 3 \sqrt { 3 } & - 3 \\ 0 & - \sqrt { 3 } & 1 & 0 & - 3 & \sqrt { 3 } \end{array} \right) d p \left( X \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

where

$$p \left( X \right) = \left( x _ { 1 2 } , x _ { 2 2 } , x _ { 3 2 } , x _ { 1 3 } , x _ { 2 3 } , x _ { 3 3 } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒


## 9 THE DIFFERENTIAL OF EIGENVALUES AND EIGEN- VECTORS: COMPLEX CASE

Precisely the same techniques as used in establishing Theorem 7 enable us to
establish Theorem 8.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="183" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] The differential of eigenvalues and eigenvectors: complex case" -->


## Theorem 8

Let $\lambda _ { 0 }$ be a simple eigenvalue (possibly complex) of a matrix $Z _ { 0 } \in \mathbb{C} ^ { n \times n } ,$ the
set of complex $n \times n$ matrices, and let $u _ { 0 }$ be an associated eigenvector, $\mathrm { S O }$
that $Z _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } .$ Then a complex-valued function $\lambda$ and a (complex) vector
function $u$ are defined for all $\bar { Z }$ in some neighbourhood $N \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) \in \mathbb{C} ^ { n \times n }$ of $Z _ { 0 } ,$
such that

$$\lambda \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { 0 } , \quad u \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) = u _ { 0 } ,$$
(1)

and

$$Z u = \lambda u , \quad u _ { 0 } ^ { * } u = 1 \quad \left( Z \in N \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) \right) .$$
(2)

Moreover, the functions $\lambda$ and $u$ are $\infty$ times differentiable on $N \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) ,$ and
the differentials at $Z _ { 0 }$ are

$$d \lambda = \frac { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( d Z \right) u _ { 0 } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } }$$
(3)

and

$$\mathrm { d } u = \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - Z _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \left( I _ { n } - \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } ,$$
(4)

where $v _ { 0 }$ is an eigenvector associated with the eigenvalue $\bar { \lambda } _ { 0 }$ of $Z _ { 0 } ^ { * } ,$ so that
$Z _ { 0 } ^ { * } v _ { 0 } = \bar { \lambda } _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } .$

Note. It seems natural to normalize $u$ by $v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u = 1$ instead of $u _ { 0 } ^ { * } u = 1 .$ Such
a normalization does not, however, lead to a Moore-Penrose inverse in (4).
Another possible normalization, $u ^ { * } u = 1 ,$ also leads to trouble, as the proof
shows.

Proof. The fact that the functions $\lambda$ and $u$ exist and are $\infty$ times differentiable
(i.e. analytic) in a neighbourhood of $Z _ { 0 }$ is proved in the same way as in
Theorem 7, using the complex analogue of Theorem 3.3 and Theorem 3.4,
instead of Theorem 3.5. To find $\mathrm { d } \lambda$ we differentiate both sides of $Z u = \lambda u ,$
and obtain

$$\left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } + Z _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = \left( \mathrm { d } \lambda \right) u _ { 0 } + \lambda _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u ,$$
(5)

where $\mathrm { d } u$ and $\mathrm { d } \lambda$ are defined at $Z _ { 0 } .$ We now pre-multiply by $v _ { 0 } ^ { * } ,$ and since
$v _ { 0 } ^ { * } Z _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ and $v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } \neq 0$ (why?), we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = \frac { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } .$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="184" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

To find $\mathrm { d } u$ we again define $Y _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } I - Z _ { 0 } ,$ and rewrite (5) as

$$Y _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } - \left( \mathrm { d } \lambda \right) u _ { 0 }$$
$$= \left( d Z \right) u _ { 0 } - \left( \frac { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( d Z \right) u _ { 0 } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } \right) u _ { 0 }$$
$$= \left( I - \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } .$$

(7)

Pre-multiplying both sides of (7) by $Y _ { 0 } ^ { + }$ we obtain

$$Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } u = Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } \left( I - \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } .$$
(8)

(Note that $Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } u _ { 0 } \neq 0$ in general.) To complete the proof we must again show
that

$$Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } Y _ { 0 } d u = d u .$$
(9)

From $Y _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } = 0$ we have $u _ { 0 } ^ { * } Y _ { 0 } ^ { * } = 0 ^ { \prime }$ and hence $u _ { 0 } ^ { * } Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } = 0 ^ { \prime } .$ Also, since $u$ is
normalized by $u _ { 0 } ^ { * } u = 1 ,$ we have $u _ { 0 } ^ { * } \mathrm { d } u = 0 .$ (Note that $u ^ { * } u = 1$ does $n o t$ imply
$u _ { 0 } ^ { * } \mathrm { d } u = 0$ Hence

$$u _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } : \mathrm { d } u \right) = 0 ^ { \prime } .$$
(10)

It follows that

$$r \left( Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } : \mathrm { d } u \right) = r \left( Y _ { 0 } ^ { + } \right)$$
(11)

which implies (9). From (8) and (9), (4) follows.
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. Show that $v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } \neq 0 .$

2\. Given the conditions of Theorem 8, show that

$$d \bar { \lambda } = \frac { u _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) ^ { * } v _ { 0 } } { u _ { 0 } ^ { * } v _ { 0 } }$$

and

$$\mathrm { d } v ^ { * } = v _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) \left( I - \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } \right) \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - Z _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } .$$

3\. Show that

$$\mathrm { d } u = \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - Z _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 }$$

if and only if $\mathrm { d } \lambda = 0$ or $v _ { 0 }$ is a multiple of $u _ { 0 } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="185" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Two alternative expressions for $\mathrm { d } \lambda$" -->


## 10 TWO ALTERNATIVE EXPRESSIONS FOR $d \lambda$

As we have seen, the differential (9.3) of the eigenvalue function associated
with a simple eigenvalue $\lambda _ { 0 }$ of a (complex) matrix $Z _ { 0 }$ can be expressed as

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = \mathrm { t r } P _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } Z , \quad P _ { 0 } = \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } ,$$
(1)

where $u _ { 0 }$ and $v _ { 0 }$ are (right and left) eigenvectors of $Z _ { 0 }$ associated with $\lambda _ { 0 } :$

$$Z _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } , \quad v _ { 0 } ^ { * } Z _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } , \quad u _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } = v _ { 0 } ^ { * } v _ { 0 } = 1 .$$
(2)

The matrix $P _ { 0 }$ is idempotent with $r \left( P _ { 0 } \right) = 1 .$

Let us now express $P _ { 0 }$ in two other ways: first as a product of $n \quad - \quad 1$
matrices, and then as a weighted sum of the matrices $I ,$ $Z _ { 0 } , \ldots , Z _ { 0 } ^ { n - 1 } .$


## Theorem 9

Let $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ be the eigenvalues of a matrix $Z _ { 0 } \in \mathbb{C} ^ { n \times n } ,$ and assume that
$\lambda _ { i }$ is simple. Then a scalar function $\lambda _ { \left( i \right) }$ exists, defined in a neighbourhood
$N \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) \subset \mathbb{C} ^ { n \times n }$ of $Z _ { 0 } ,$ such that $\lambda _ { \left( i \right) } \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { i }$ and $\lambda _ { \left( i \right) } \left( Z \right)$ is a (simple)
eigenvalue of $Z$ for every $Z \in N \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) .$ Moreover, $\lambda _ { \left( i \right) }$ is $\infty$ times differentiable
on $N \left( Z _ { 0 } \right) ,$ and

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda _ { \left( i \right) } = \mathrm { t r } \left( \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \lambda _ { j } I - Z _ { 0 } } { \lambda _ { j } - \lambda _ { i } } \right) d Z \right) .$$
(3)

If, in addition, we assume that $a l l$ eigenvalues of $Z _ { 0 }$ are simple, then we may
also express $\mathrm { d } \lambda _ { \left( i \right) }$ as

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda _ { \left( i \right) } = \mathrm { t r } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } v ^ { i j } Z _ { 0 } ^ { j - 1 } \mathrm { d } Z \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(4)

where $v ^ { i j }$ is the typical element of the inverse of the Vandermonde matrix

$$V = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 1 & \ldots & 1 \\ \lambda _ { 1 } & \lambda _ { 2 } & \ldots & \lambda _ { n } \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { n - 1 } & \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { n - 1 } & \ldots & \lambda _ { n } ^ { n - 1 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

Note. In expression (3) it is not demanded that the eigenvalues are all distinct,
nor that they are all non-zero. In (4), however, the eigenvalues are assumed
to be distinct. Still, one (but only one) eigenvalue may be zero.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="186" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->

Proof. Consider the following two matrices of order $n \times n$ n:

$$A = \lambda _ { i } I - Z _ { 0 }$$

and

$$B = \prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } I - Z _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(6)

The Cayley-Hamilton theorem (Theorem 1.10) asserts that

$$A B = B A = 0 .$$
(7)

Further, since $\lambda _ { i }$ is a simple eigenvalue of $Z _ { 0 }$ and using the corollary to Theo-
rem 1.19, we find that $r \left( A \right) = n \quad - \quad 1 .$ Hence application of Theorem 3.6 shows
that

$$B = \mu u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } ,$$
(8)

where $u _ { 0 }$ and $v _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ are defined in (2), and $\mu$ is an arbitrary scalar.

To determine the scalar $\mu ,$ we use Schur's decomposition theorem (Theo-
rem 1.12) and write

$$S ^ { * } Z _ { 0 } S = \Lambda + R , \quad S ^ { * } S = I ,$$
(9)

where $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix containing $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ on its diagonal, and $R$
is strictly upper triangular. Then,

$$\mathrm { t r } B = \mathrm { t r } \prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } I - Z _ { 0 } \right) = \mathrm { t r } \prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } I - \Lambda - R \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } I - \Lambda \right) = \prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } - \lambda _ { i } \right) .$$

(10)

From (8) we also have

tr
$$B = \mu v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } ,$$
(11)

and since $v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 }$ is non-zero, we find

$$\mu = \frac { \prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } - \lambda _ { i } \right) } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } .$$
(12)

Hence,

$$\prod _ { j \neq i } \left( \frac { \lambda _ { j } I - Z _ { 0 } } { \lambda _ { j } - \lambda _ { i } } \right) = \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } ,$$
(13)

which by (1) is what we wanted to show.

Let us now prove (4). (See Miscellaneous Exercise 3 for an alternative
proof.) Since all eigenvalues of $Z _ { 0 }$ are now assumed to be distinct, there exists
by Theorem 1.15 a non-singular matrix $T$ such that

$$T ^ { - 1 } Z _ { 0 } T = \Lambda .$$
(14)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="187" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Two alternative expressions for $\mathrm { d } \lambda$" -->

Therefore,

$$\sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } Z _ { 0 } ^ { j - 1 } = T \left( \sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } \Lambda ^ { j - 1 } \right) T ^ { - 1 } .$$
(15)

If we denote by $E _ { i i }$ the $n \times n$ matrix with a one in its i-th diagonal position
and zeros elsewhere, and by $\delta _ { i k }$ the Kronecker delta, then

$$\sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } \Lambda ^ { j - 1 } = \sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } \left( \sum _ { k } \lambda _ { k } ^ { j - 1 } E _ { k k } \right) = \sum _ { k } \left( \sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } \lambda _ { k } ^ { j - 1 } \right) E _ { k k }$$

$$= \sum _ { k } \delta _ { i k } E _ { k k } = E _ { i i } ,$$
(16)

because $\sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } \lambda _ { k } ^ { j - 1 }$ is the inner product of the i-th row of $V ^ { - 1 }$ and the k-th
column of $V ,$ that is

$$\sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } \lambda _ { k } ^ { j - 1 } = \delta _ { i k } .$$
(17)

Inserting (16) in (15) yields

$$\sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } Z _ { 0 } ^ { j - 1 } = T E _ { i i } T ^ { - 1 } = \left( T e _ { i } \right) \left( e _ { i } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } \right) ,$$
(18)

where $e _ { i }$ is the i-th unit vector. Since $\lambda _ { i }$ is a simple eigenvalue of $Z _ { 0 } ,$ we have

$$T e _ { i } = \gamma u _ { 0 } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad e _ { i } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } = \delta v _ { 0 } ^ { * }$$
(19)

for some scalars $\gamma$ and $\delta .$ Further,

$$1 = e _ { i } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } T e _ { i } = \gamma \delta v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } .$$
(20)

Hence,

$$\sum _ { j } v ^ { i j } Z _ { 0 } ^ { j - 1 } = \left( T e _ { i } \right) \left( e _ { i } ^ { \prime } T ^ { - 1 } \right) = \gamma \delta u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } = \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } .$$
(21)

This concludes the proof, using (1).


## Exercise

1\. Show that the elements in the first column of $V ^ { - 1 }$ sum to one, and the
elements in any other column of $V ^ { - 1 }$ sum to zero.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="188" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Some important differentials [Ch. 8" -->


## 11 SECOND DIFFERENTIAL OF THE EIGENVALUE FUNCTION

One application of the differential of the eigenvector $\mathrm { d } u$ is to obtain the second
differential of the eigenvalue: $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda .$ We consider first the case where $X _ { 0 }$ is a
real symmetric matrix.


### Theorem 10

Under the same conditions as in Theorem 7, we have

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda = 2 u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } .$$
(1)

Proof. Twice differentiating both sides of $X u = \lambda u ,$ we obtain

$$2 \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \left( \mathrm { d } u \right) + X _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } u = \left( \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda \right) u _ { 0 } + 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \lambda \right) \left( \mathrm { d } u \right) + \lambda _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } u ,$$
(2)

where all differentials are evaluated at $X _ { 0 } .$ Pre-multiplying by $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ gives

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda = 2 u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \left( \mathrm { d } u \right) ,$$
(3)

since $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } u _ { 0 } = 1 ,$ $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } d u = 0$ and $u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } X _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .$ From Theorem 7 we have
$\mathrm { d } u = \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } .$ Inserting this in (3) gives (1).
✷
☐

The case where $Z _ { 0 }$ is a complex $n \times n$ matrix is proved in a similar way.


### Theorem 11

Under the same conditions as in Theorem 8, we have

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda = \frac { 2 v _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) K _ { 0 } \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - Z _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } K _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) u _ { 0 } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } ,$$
(4)

where

$$K _ { 0 } = I _ { n } - \frac { u _ { 0 } v _ { 0 } ^ { * } } { v _ { 0 } ^ { * } u _ { 0 } } .$$
(5)


### Exercises

1\. Show that (1) can be written as

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda = 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } \left[ \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \otimes u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$

and also as

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda = 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } \left[ u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$

2\. Show that if $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is the largest eigenvalue of $X _ { 0 } ,$ then $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda \geq 0 .$ Relate
this to the fact that the largest eigenvalue is convex on the space of real
symmetric matrices. (Compare Theorem 11.5.)

3\. Similarly, if $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is the smallest eigenvalue of $X _ { 0 } ,$ show that $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda \leq 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="189" -->
<!-- PageHeader="12 ] Multiple eigenvalues" -->


## 12 MULTIPLE EIGENVALUES

The case of multiple eigenvalues is more difficult. In Section 7 we considered
the matrix function

$$A \left( \epsilon , \delta \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 + \epsilon & \delta \\ \delta & 1 - \epsilon \end{array} \right)$$
(1)

whose eigenvalues are not differentiable at $\left( 0 , 0 \right) ,$ and we concluded that it
would be wise to restrict the study of multiple eigenvalues to matrix functions
of one parameter only.

In this section we briefly summarize some of Lancaster's (1964) results.
We consider the eigenvalues of $n \times n$ matrices $A$ whose elements are functions
of one parameter $\zeta ,$ and we assume that (i) the elements of $A \left( \zeta \right)$ are analytic
functions in some neighbourhood of $\zeta _ { 0 } ,$ (ii) the matrix $A _ { 0 } = A \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right)$ has simple
structure (i.e. all eigenvalues of $A _ { 0 }$ have only linear elementary divisors), and
(iii) if $\lambda \left( \zeta \right)$ is an eigenvalue of $A \left( \zeta \right) ,$ then $\lambda \left( \zeta \right) \rightarrow \lambda \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right)$ as $\zeta \rightarrow \zeta _ { 0 } .$

We shall denote by $A ^ { \left( q \right) } \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right)$ the q-th derivative of $A \left( \zeta \right)$ evaluated at $\zeta = \zeta _ { 0 } .$


### Theorem 12

If $A ^ { \left( q \right) } \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right)$ is the first non-vanishing derivative of $A \left( \zeta \right)$ at $\zeta = \zeta _ { 0 } ,$ then the $n$
eigenvalues $\lambda \left( \zeta \right)$ of $A \left( \zeta \right)$ are differentiable at least $q$ times at $\zeta _ { 0 }$ and their first
$q \quad - \quad 1$ derivatives all vanish at $\zeta _ { 0 } .$

Now let $\lambda _ { 0 }$ be an eigenvalue of $A _ { 0 }$ with multiplicity m. Let $U _ { 0 }$ be the
$n \times m$ matrix whose $m$ columns span the subspace of eigenvectors $a s s o c i a t e d$
with $\lambda _ { 0 } ,$ that is $A _ { 0 } U _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } U _ { 0 } .$ Also, let $V _ { 0 }$ be the $n \times m$ matrix whose $m$
columns span the subspace of eigenvectors associated with the eigenvalue $\bar { \lambda } _ { 0 }$
of $A _ { 0 } ^ { * } ,$ that is $A _ { 0 } ^ { * } V _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 0 } V _ { 0 } .$ We can normalize the matrices $U _ { 0 }$ and $V _ { 0 }$ so that
$V _ { 0 } ^ { * } U _ { 0 } = I _ { m }$ -


### Theorem 13

If $A ^ { \left( q \right) } \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right)$ is the first non-vanishing derivative of $A \left( \zeta \right)$ at $\zeta = \zeta _ { 0 } ,$ then the
$m$ derivatives $\lambda ^ { \left( q \right) } \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right)$ (of the $m$ eigenvalues which coincide at $\zeta _ { 0 }$ are the
eigenvalues of the matrix $V _ { 0 } ^ { * } A ^ { \left( q \right) } \left( \zeta _ { 0 } \right) U _ { 0 } .$

Note. Compare Theorem 13 with the expression for $\mathrm { d } \lambda$ in Theorem 8.


### MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. In generalizing the fundamental rule $\mathrm { d } x ^ { k } = k x ^ { k - 1 } \mathrm { d } x$ to matrices, show
that it is not true, in general, that $\mathrm { d } X ^ { k } = k X ^ { k - 1 } \mathrm { d } X .$ It is true, however,
that

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { k } = k \mathrm { t r } X ^ { k - 1 } \mathrm { d } X \quad \left( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots \right) .$$

Prove that this also holds for real $k \geq 1$ when $X$ is positive semidefinite.

that

and in particular,

and in particular,

Evans (1968). See also Stewart (1969). Theorem 5 is due to Golub and Pereyra
(1973).

§7-§11. The development follows Magnus (1985). Figure 1 was suggested to us
by Roald Ramer. See also Lancaster (1964), Sugiura (1973), Bargmann and
