## CHAPTER 9 First-order differentials and

$$J a c o b i a n \quad m a t r i c e s$$


### 1 INTRODUCTION

We begin this chapter with some notational issues. We shall argue very strongly
for a particular way of displaying the partial derivatives $\partial f _ { s t } \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { i j }$ of a ma-

☒ ☒
trix function $F \left( X \right) ,$ one which generalizes the notion of a Jacobian matrix of
a vector function to a Jacobian matrix of a matrix function.

The main tool in this chapter will be the first identification theorem (Theo-
rem 5.11), which tells us how to obtain the derivative (Jacobian matrix) from
the differential. Given a matrix function $F \left( X \right)$ we then proceed as follows:
☒
(i) compute the differential of $F \left( X \right) ,$ (ii) vectorize to obtain $\mathrm { d }$ vec $F \left( X \right) =$
☒ ☒ ☒

☒
$A \left( X \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ,$ and (iii) conclude that $\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = A \left( X \right) .$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

The simplicity and elegance of this approach will be demonstrated by many
examples.


### 2 CLASSIFICATION

We shall consider scalar functions $\phi ,$ vector functions $f$ and matrix functions
$F .$ Each of these may depend on one real variable $\xi ,$ a vector of real variables
x, or a matrix of real variables $X .$ We thus obtain the classification of func-
tions and variables shown in Table 1.


<table>
<caption>Table 1 Classification of functions and variables</caption>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th>Scalar variable</th>
<th>Vector variable</th>
<th>Matrix variable</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Scalar function</td>
<td>$\phi \left( \xi \right)$</td>
<td>☒ $\phi \left( x \right)$</td>
<td>$\bar { \phi \left( X \right) }$ ☒</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Vector function</td>
<td>$f \left( \xi \right)$</td>
<td>☒ $f \left( x \right)$</td>
<td>$f \left( X \right)$ ☒</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Matrix function</td>
<td>$F \left( \xi \right)$</td>
<td>$F \left( x \right)$ ☒</td>
<td>$F \left( X \right)$ ☒</td>
</tr>
</table>

#### Examples

$$\begin{array}{} \phi \left( \xi \right) & : & \xi ^ { 2 } \\ \phi \left( x \right) & : & a ^ { \prime } x , x ^ { \prime } A x \\ \phi \left( X \right) & : & a ^ { \prime } X b , \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X \end{array} ,$$
$$\begin{array}{} { f \left( \xi \right) : \left( \xi , \xi ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \prime } } \\ { f \left( x \right) : A x } \end{array}$$
$\phi \left( X \right)$ $| X | ,$ $\lambda \left( X \right)$ (eigenvalue)
☒
$f \left( X \right)$ : Xa, $u \left( X \right)$ (eigenvector)

☒ ☒

$$\begin{array}{} F \left( \xi \right) & : & \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & \xi \\ \xi & \xi ^ { 2 } \end{array} \right) \\ F \left( x \right) & : & x x ^ { \prime } \end{array}$$

$F \left( X \right) \quad : \quad A X B , X ^ { 2 } ,$ $X ^ { + }$
☒


### 3 BAD NOTATION

If $F$ is a differentiable $m \times p$ matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X$ of variables
then the question naturally arises how to order the mnpq partial derivatives
of $F .$ Obviously, this can be done in many ways. The purpose of this section
is to convince the reader not to use the following notation, which, for reasons
unknown, has earned itself an undeserved popularity.


#### Definition 1

Let $\phi$ be a differentiable real-valued function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X = \left( x _ { i j } \right)$ of
real variables. Then the symbol $\partial \phi \left( X \right) / \partial X$ denotes the $n \times q$ matrix
☒

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = \left( \begin{array}{} \partial \phi / \partial x _ { 1 1 } & \cdots & \partial \phi / \partial x _ { 1 q } \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ \partial \phi / \partial x _ { n 1 } & \ldots & \partial \phi / \partial x _ { n q } \end{array} \right) .$$
(1)


#### Definition 2

Let $F = \left( f _ { s t } \right)$ be a differentiable $m \times p$ real matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix
☒
$X$ of real variables. Then the symbol $\partial F \left( X \right) / \partial X$ denotes the $m n \times p q$ matrix
☒

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = \left( \begin{array}{} \partial f _ { 1 1 } / \partial X & \cdots & \partial f _ { 1 p } / \partial X \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ \partial f _ { m 1 } / \partial X & \ldots & \partial f _ { m p } / \partial X \end{array} \right) .$$
(2)

Before we criticize Definition 2, let us list some of its good points. Two
very pleasant properties are: (i) if F is a matrix function of just one variable $\xi ,$
then $\partial F \left( \xi \right) / \partial \xi$ has the same order as $F \left( \xi \right) ,$ and (ii) if $\phi$ is a scalar function of a

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="195" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Bad notation" -->

matrix of variables $X ,$ then $\partial \phi \left( X \right) / \partial X$ has the same order as $X .$ In particular,
if $\phi$ is a scalar function of a column vector x, then $\partial \phi / \partial x$ is a column vector
and $\partial \phi / \partial x ^ { \prime }$ a row vector. Another consequence of the definition is that it
allows us to order the $m n$ partial derivatives of an $m \times 1$ vector function $f \left( x \right) ,$
where $x$ is an $n \times 1$ vector of variables, in four ways: namely as $\partial f / \partial x ^ { \prime }$ (an
$m \times n$ matrix), as $\partial f ^ { \prime } / \partial x$ (an $n \times m$ matrix), as $\partial f / \partial x$ (an $m n \times 1$ vector),
or as $\partial f ^ { \prime } / \partial x ^ { \prime }$ (a $1 \times m n$ vector).

To see what is wrong with the definition, let us consider the identity func-
tion $F \left( X \right) = X ,$ where $X$ is an $n \times q$ matrix of real variables. We obtain from
Definition 2

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = \left( \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } I _ { q } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(3)

a matrix of rank 1. The Jacobian matrix of the identity function is, of course,
$I _ { n q }$ the $n q \times n q$ identity matrix. Hence Definition 2 does not give us the Jaco-
bian matrix of the function $F ,$ and, indeed, the rank of the Jacobian matrix
is not given by the rank of $\partial F \left( X \right) / \partial X .$ This implies - and this cannot be
stressed enough - that the matrix $\left( 2 \right)$ displays the partial derivatives, but
nothing more. In particular, the determinant of $\partial F \left( X \right) / \partial X$ has no interpre-
tation, and (very important for practical work) a useful chain rule does not
exist.

There exists another definition, equally unsuitable, which is based not on
$\partial \phi \left( X \right) / \partial X ,$ but on

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial x _ { i j } } = \left( \begin{array}{} \partial f _ { 1 1 } \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { i j } & \cdots & \partial f _ { 1 p } \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { i j } \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ \partial f _ { m 1 } \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { i j } & \cdots & \partial f _ { m p } \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { i j } \end{array} \right) .$$
(4)


#### Definition 3

Let $F$ be a differentiable $m \times p$ matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X = \left( x _ { i j } \right)$
of real variables. Then the symbol $\partial F \left( X \right) / / \partial X$ denotes the $m n \times p q$ matrix

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = \left( \begin{array}{} \partial F \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { 1 1 } & \cdots & \partial F \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { 1 q } \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ \partial F \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { n 1 } & \cdots & \partial F \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { n q } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

Definition 3 is equally as bad as Definition 2, except for one point in which
it has an advantage over Definition 2, namely that the expressions $\partial F \left( X \right) / \partial x _ { i j }$
are much easier to evaluate than $\partial f _ { s t } \left( X \right) / \partial X ,$ because the latter expressions
require us to disentangle the matrix function $F \left( X \right) .$

After these critical remarks, let us turn quickly to the only natural and
viable generalization of the notion of a Jacobian matrix of a vector function
to a Jacobian matrix of a matrix function.


#### Exercises

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="196" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->

1\. For the identity function $F \left( X \right) = X ,$ show that
☒ ☒

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = \frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = \left( \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } I _ { q } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

2\. Let $f : \mathrm { R } ^ { n } \rightarrow \mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ be a differentiable vector function. Then show that
☒

$$\frac { \partial f \left( x \right) } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \partial f \left( x \right) } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } = \text { Df } \left( x \right) ,$$

an $m \times n$ matrix of partial derivatives.

3\. Show that $\partial F / \partial X$ and $\partial F / / \partial X$ stand in one-to-one relationship,

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = K _ { n m } \frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } K _ { p q }$$ ☒ ☒

and

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = K _ { m n } \frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } K _ { q p } ,$$ ☒ ☒

where $K$ is the commutation matrix (Neudecker 1982).


### 4 GOOD NOTATION

Let $\phi$ be a scalar function of an $n \times 1$ vector x. We have already encountered
the derivative of $\phi ,$

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( x \right) , \ldots , \mathrm { D } _ { n } \phi \left( x \right) \right) = \frac { \partial \phi \left( x \right) } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } .$$
(1)

If $f$ is an $m \times 1$ vector function of x, then the derivative (or Jacobian matrix)
☒
of $f$ is the $m \times n$ matrix
☒

$$D f \left( x \right) = \frac { \partial f \left( x \right) } { \partial x ^ { \prime } }$$
(2)

Since (1) is just a special case of (2), the double use of the D-symbol is
permitted. Generalizing these concepts to matrix functions of matrices, we
arrive at the following definition.


#### Definition 4

Let $F$ be a differentiable $m \times p$ real matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix of real
variables $X .$ The Jacobian matrix of $F$ at $X$ is the $m p \times n q \mathrm { m a t r i x }$

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } } .$$ ☒ ☒
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="197" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] Good notation" -->

Thus $\mathrm { D } F ,$ $\mathrm { D } f$ and $\mathrm { D } \phi$ are all defined. The reader should compare (3) with the
equivalent expression in (5.15.9).

It is worthwhile noticing that $\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right)$ and $\partial F \left( X \right) / \partial X$ contain the same
☒ ☒
mnpq partial derivatives, but in a different pattern. Indeed, the orders of the
two matrices are different $\left( \mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) \right.$ is of the order $m p \times n q ,$ while $\partial F \left( X \right) / \partial X$
☒ ☒
is of the order $m n \times p q ,$ and, more important, their ranks are in general
different.

Since $\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right)$ is a straightforward matrix generalization of the traditional
☒
definition of the Jacobian matrix $\partial f \left( x \right) / \partial x ^ { \prime } ,$ all properties of Jacobian matri-
ces are preserved. In particular, questions relating to functions with non-zero
Jacobian determinant at certain points remain meaningful.

Definition 4 reduces the study of matrix functions of matrices to the study
of vector functions of vectors, since it allows $F \left( X \right)$ and $X$ only in their vec-
☒
torized forms vec $F$ and vec $X .$ As a result, the unattractive expressions

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial X } , \quad \frac { \partial F \left( x \right) } { \partial x } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \frac { \partial f \left( X \right) } { \partial X }$$ ☒ ☒
(4)

are not needed. The same is, in principle, true for the expressions

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial X }$$

and

$$\frac { \partial F \left( \xi \right) } { \partial \xi } ,$$
(5)

since these can be replaced by

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( X \right) = \frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } }$$ ☒ ☒

and

$$D F \left( \xi \right) = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } F \left( \xi \right) } { \partial \xi }$$
(6)
.

However, the idea of arranging the partial derivatives of $\phi \left( X \right)$ and $F \left( \xi \right)$ into
☒
a matrix (rather than a vector) is rather appealing and sometimes useful, so
we retain the expressions (5).


#### Exercises

1\. Let $F$ be a differentiable matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix of variables
$X = \left( x _ { i j } \right) .$ Then

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial x _ { i j } } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { i j } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$ ☒

where $E _ { i j }$ denotes an $n \times q$ matrix with a one in the ij-th position and
zeros elsewhere.

2\. Show that

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( X \right) = \left( \mathrm { v e c } \frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial X } \right) ^ { \prime }$$

and

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( \xi \right) = \mathrm { v e c } \frac { \partial F \left( \xi \right) } { \partial \xi } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="198" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->


### 5 IDENTIFICATION OF JACOBIAN MATRICES

Our strategy to find the Jacobian matrix of a function will not be $\mathrm { t o }$ evaluate
each of its partial derivatives, but rather to find the differential. In the case
of a differentiable vector function $f \left( x \right) ,$ the first identification theorem (The-
orem 5.6) tells us that there exists a one-to-one correspondence between the
differential of $f$ and its Jacobian matrix. More specifically, it states that

$$d f \left( x \right) = A \left( x \right) d x$$
(1)

implies and is implied by

$$\mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) = A \left( x \right) .$$
(2)

Thus, once we know the differential, the Jacobian matrix is identified.

The extension to matrix functions is straightforward. The identification
theorem for matrix functions (Theorem 5.11) states that

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = A \left( X \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$ ☒ ☒
(3)

implies and is implied by

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = A \left( X \right) .$$
(4)

Since computations $w i t h$ differentials are relatively easy, this identification
result is extremely useful. Given a matrix function $F \left( X \right)$ we may therefore
☒
proceed as follows: (i) compute the differential of $F \left( X \right) ,$ (ii) vectorize to obtain
☒ ☒
d vec $F \left( X \right) = A \left( X \right) d$ vec $X ,$ and (iii) conclude that $\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = A \left( X \right) .$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

Many examples in this chapter will demonstrate the simplicity and $\mathrm { e l e }$
gance of this approach. Let us consider one now. Let $F \left( X \right) = A X B ,$ where $A$
and $B$ are matrices of constants. Then
☒

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) = A \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) B ,$$ ☒
(5)

and

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes A \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ,$$ ☒
(6)

so that

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = B ^ { \prime } \otimes A .$$ ☒
(7)


### 6 THE FIRST IDENTIFICATION TABLE

The identification theorem for matrix functions of matrix variables encom-
passes, of course, identification for matrix, vector and scalar functions of ma-
trix, vector and scalar variables. Table 2 lists these results.

<!-- PageNumber="199" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7 ] Partitioning of the derivative" -->

In the first identification table, $\phi$ is a scalar function, $f$ an $m \times 1$ vector
function and $F$ an $m \times p$ matrix function; $\xi$ is a scalar, $x$ an $n \times 1$ vector and
$X$ an $n \times q$ matrix; $\alpha$ is a scalar, $a$ is a column vector and $A$ is a matrix, each
of which may be a function of $X ,$ $x$ or $\xi .$


### 7 PARTITIONING OF THE DERIVATIVE

Before the workings of the identification table are exemplified, we have to
settle one further question of notation. Let $\phi$ be a differentiable scalar function
of an $n \times 1$ vector x. Suppose that $x$ is partitioned as

$$x ^ { \prime } = \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(1)

Then the derivative $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right)$ is partitioned $\mathrm { i n }$ the same way, and we write

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( \mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( x \right) , \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) \right) ,$$
(2)

where $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( x \right)$ contains the partial derivatives of $\phi$ with respect to x1, and
$\mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( X \right)$ contains the partial derivatives of $\phi$ with respect to x2. As a result,
if

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x \right) = a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) \mathrm { d } x _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) \mathrm { d } x _ { 2 } ,$$
(3)

then

$$\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( x \right) = a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) , \quad \mathrm { D } _ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) = a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) ,$$
(4)

and so

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) , a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) \right) .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="200" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->


### 8 SCALAR FUNCTIONS OF A VECTOR

Let us now give some examples. The two most important cases of a scalar
function of a vector $x$ are the linear form $a ^ { \prime } x$ and the quadratic form $x ^ { \prime } A x .$

Let $\phi \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } x ,$ where $a$ is a vector of constants. Then $\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } x ,$ so
$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } .$ Next, let $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } A x ,$ where $A$ is a square matrix of constants.
Then

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x \right) = \mathrm { d } \left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } A x + x ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { d } x$$
$$= \left( \left( d x \right) ^ { \prime } A x \right) ^ { \prime } + x ^ { \prime } A d x = x ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } d x + x ^ { \prime } A d x$$
$$= x ^ { \prime } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) d x ,$$

(1)

so that $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Thus we obtain Table 3.


<table>
<caption>Table 3</caption>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th></th>
<th>$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right)$</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td>$a ^ { \prime }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


Notice that, if $A$ is symmetric and $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } A x ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = 2 x ^ { \prime } A .$


#### Exercises

1\. If $\phi \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } f \left( x \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = a ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) .$

2\. If $\phi \left( x \right) = \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } g \left( x \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( g \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) + \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } g \left( x \right) .$

3\. If $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } A f \left( x \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } + x ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) .$

4\. If $\phi \left( x \right) = \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } A f \left( x \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) .$

5\. If $\phi \left( x \right) = \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } A g \left( x \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( g \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) + \left( f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { D } g \left( x \right) .$

6\. If $\phi \left( x \right) = x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A x _ { 2 } ,$ where $x = \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ then $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 } \phi \left( x \right) = x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } ,$ $D _ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) =$
$x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A$ and

$$\mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { \prime } \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & A \\ A ^ { \prime } & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$


### 9 SCALAR FUNCTIONS OF A MATRIX, I: TRACE

There is certainly no lack of interesting examples of scalar functions of matri-
ces. In this section we shall investigate differentials of traces of some matrix
functions. Section 10 is devoted to determinants, and Section 11 to eigenval-
ues.

The simplest case is

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { t r } X = \mathrm { t r d } X = \mathrm { t r } I \mathrm { d } X . ,$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="201" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Scalar functions of a matrix, I: trace" -->

implying

$$\frac { \partial \mathrm { t r } X } { \partial X } = I$$
(2)

More interesting is the trace of the (positive semidefinite) matrix function
$X ^ { \prime } X .$ We have

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X = \mathrm { t r d } \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) = \mathrm { t r } \left( \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } X + X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } X + \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X = 2 \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X .$$ ☒ ☒
(3)

Hence,

$$\frac { \partial \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X } { \partial X } = 2 X$$
(4)

Next consider the trace of $X ^ { 2 } ,$ where $X$ is square. This gives
☒

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { t r d } X ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { t r } \left( \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X + X \mathrm { d } X \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } X \mathrm { d } X ,$$ ☒ ☒
(5)

and thus

$$\frac { \partial \mathrm { t r } X ^ { 2 } } { \partial X } = 2 X ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)

In Table 4 we present straightforward generalizations of the three cases
just considered. The proofs are easy and are left to the reader.


<table>
<caption>Table 4</caption>
<tr>
<th>$\phi \left( X \right.$</th>
<th>$\bar { \mathrm { d } \phi \left( X \right. }$</th>
<th>$\bar { \mathrm { D } \phi \left( X \right. }$</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>tr AX</td>
<td>tr $\bar { A \mathrm { d } X }$</td>
<td>$\bar { \left( \mathrm { v e c } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>tr $X A X ^ { \prime } B$</td>
<td>$\mathrm { t r } \left( A X ^ { \prime } B + A ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } X$</td>
<td>$\left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( B ^ { \prime } X A ^ { \prime } + B X A \right) \right) ^ { \prime }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { t r } X A X B$</td>
<td>$\mathrm { t r } \left( A X B + B X A \right) \mathrm { d } X$</td>
<td>$\left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( B ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } + A ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \check { B } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) ^ { \prime }$</td>
</tr>
</table>


#### Exercises

1\. Show that tr $B X ^ { \prime } ,$ tr $X B ,$ tr $X ^ { \prime } B ,$ tr $B X C$ and tr $B X ^ { \prime } C$ can all be
written as tr $A X$ and determine their derivatives.

2\. Show that

$$\partial \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } A X / \partial X = \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) X ,$$
$$\partial \mathrm { t r } X A X ^ { \prime } / \partial X = X \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$
$$\partial \mathrm { t r } X A X / \partial X = X ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } + A ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } .$$

.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="202" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->

3\. Show that

$$\partial \mathrm { t r } A X ^ { - 1 } / \partial X = - \left( X ^ { - 1 } A X ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$

4\. Use the previous results to find the derivatives of $a ^ { \prime } X b ,$ $a ^ { \prime } X X ^ { \prime } a$ and
$a ^ { \prime } X ^ { - 1 } a .$

5\. Show that for square $X ,$

$$\partial \mathrm { t r } X ^ { p } / \partial X = p \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { p - 1 } \quad \left( p = 1 , 2 , \ldots \right) .$$

6\. If $\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } F \left( X \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { D } \phi \left( X \right) = \left( \mathrm { v e c } I \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) .$
☒ ☒

7\. Determine the derivative of $\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } F \left( X \right) A G \left( X \right) B .$
☒ ☒ ☒

8\. Determine the derivative of $\phi \left( X , Z \right) = \mathrm { t r } A X B Z .$


### 10 SCALAR FUNCTIONS OF A MATRIX, $I I :$ DETERMINANT

Recall that the differential of a determinant is given by

$$\mathrm { d } | X | = | X | \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X ,$$
(1)

if $X$ is a square non-singular matrix (Theorem 8.1). As a result, the derivative
is

$$| X | \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( X ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(2)

and

$$\frac { \partial | X | } { \partial X } = | X | \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$
(3)

This is easily verified from the identification table.

Let us now employ Equation (1) to find the differential and derivative of
the determinant of some simple matrix functions of $X .$ The first of these is
$| X X ^ { \prime } ,$ where $X$ is not necessarily a square matrix, but must have full row
rank in order to ensure that the determinant is non-zero (in fact, positive).
The differential is

$$\mathrm { d } | X X ^ { \prime } | = | X X ^ { \prime } | \mathrm { t r } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= | X X ^ { \prime } | \mathrm { t r } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { \prime } + X \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= | X X ^ { \prime } | \left[ \mathrm { t r } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { \prime } + \mathrm { t r } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } X \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } \right]$$
$$= 2 | X X ^ { \prime } | \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X .$$

(4)

As a result,

$$\frac { \partial | X X ^ { \prime } | } { \partial X } = 2 | X X ^ { \prime } | \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } X .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="203" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Scalar functions of a matrix, II: determinant" -->

Similarly we find for $| X ^ { \prime } X | \neq 0 ,$

$$\mathrm { d } | X ^ { \prime } X | = 2 | X ^ { \prime } X | \mathrm { t r } \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒
(6)

so that

$$\frac { \partial | X ^ { \prime } X | } { \partial X } = 2 | X ^ { \prime } X | X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$
(7)

Finally, let us consider the determinant of $X ^ { 2 } ,$ where $X \quad i s \quad n o n - s i n g u l a r .$
☒
Since $| X ^ { 2 } | = | X | ^ { 2 } ,$ we have
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { d } | X ^ { 2 } | = \mathrm { d } | X | ^ { 2 } = 2 | X | \mathrm { d } | X | = 2 | X | ^ { 2 } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(8)

These results are summarized in Table 5, where each determinant is assumed
to be non-zero.

<tr>
<th></th>
<th></th>
<th>$\bar { \mathrm { D } \phi \left( X \right. }$ ☒</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$X$ ☒</td>
<td>$\bar { \left. \right) X \mid \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X }$</td>
<td>$\bar { | X | \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( X ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$| X X ^ { \prime }$</td>
<td>$2 | X X ^ { \prime } | \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X$</td>
<td>$2 | X X ^ { \prime } | \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { \prime }$ ☒</td>
</tr>

<td>$| X ^ { \prime } X$ ☒</td>

</tr>

<td>$| X ^ { 2 } |$ ☒</td>
<td>$2 | X | ^ { 2 } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X$</td>
<td>$2 | X | ^ { 2 } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( X ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime }$</td>
</tr>

#### Exercises

1\. Show that $\partial | A X B | / \partial X = | A X B | A ^ { \prime } \left( B ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } ,$ if the inverse exists.

2\. Let $F \left( X \right)$ be a square non-singular matrix function of $X ,$ and $G \left( X \right) =$
☒ ☒

☒
$C \left( F \left( X \right) \right) ^ { - 1 } A .$ Then
☒

$$\partial | F \left( X \right) | / \partial X = \left\{ \begin{array}{} | F \left( X \right) | \left( G X B + G ^ { \prime } X B ^ { \prime } \right) , & \mathrm { i f } \quad F \left( X \right) = A X B X ^ { \prime } C , \\ | F \left( X \right) | \left( B X G + B ^ { \prime } X G ^ { \prime } \right) , & \mathrm { i f } \quad F \left( X \right) = A X ^ { \prime } B X C , \\ | F \left( X \right) | \left( G X B + B X G \right) ^ { \prime } , & \mathrm { i f } \quad F \left( X \right) = A X B X C . \end{array} \right. ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

3\. Generalize (3) and (8) for non-singular $X$ to

$$\partial | X ^ { p } | / \partial X = p | X | ^ { p } \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$$ ☒ ☒

a formula that holds for positive and negative integers.

4\. Determine the derivative of $\phi \left( X \right) = \log | X ^ { \prime } A X | ,$ where $A$ is positive
definite and $X ^ { \prime } A X$ non-singular.

5\. Determine the derivative of $\phi \left( X \right) = | A F \left( X \right) B G \left( X \right) C | ,$ and verify (3),
☒ ☒ ☒
(5), (7) and (8) as special cases.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="204" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->


### 11 SCALAR FUNCTIONS OF $A$ MATRIX, III: EIGENVALUE

Let $X _ { 0 }$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix, and let $u _ { 0 }$ be a normalized eigenvec-
tor associated with a simple eigenvalue $\lambda _ { 0 }$ of $X _ { 0 } .$ Then we know from Section
8.8 that unique and differentiable functions $\lambda = \lambda \left( X \right)$ and $u = u \left( X \right)$ exist for
☒ ☒ ☒
all $X$ in a neighbourhood $N \left( X _ { 0 } \right)$ of $X _ { 0 }$ satisfying
☒ ☒

$$\lambda \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { 0 } , \quad u \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = u _ { 0 } ,$$ ☒ ☒
(1)

and

$$X u \left( X \right) = \lambda \left( X \right) u \left( X \right) , \quad u \left( X \right) ^ { \prime } u \left( X \right) = 1 \quad \left( X \in N \left( X _ { 0 } \right) \right) . ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(2)

The differential of $\lambda$ at $X _ { 0 }$ is then
☒

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda = u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 } .$$
(3)

Hence we obtain the derivative

$$\mathrm { D } \lambda \left( X \right) = \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } } = u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$$ ☒
(4)

and the gradient (a column vector!)

$$\nabla \lambda \left( X \right) = u _ { 0 } \otimes u _ { 0 } .$$ ☒
(5)

We can also display the partial derivatives in a matrix:

$$\frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial X } = u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)


### 12 TWO EXAMPLES OF VECTOR FUNCTIONS

Let us consider a set of variables $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { m } ,$ and suppose that these are known
linear combinations of another set of variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ so that

$$y _ { i } = \sum _ { j } a _ { i j } x _ { j } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$$
(1)

Then

$$y = f \left( x \right) = A x ,$$
(2)

and since $\mathrm { d } f \left( x \right) = A \mathrm { d } x ,$ we have for the Jacobian matrix

$$D f \left( x \right) = A .$$
(3)

If, on the other hand, the $y _ { i }$ are linearly related to variables $x _ { i j }$ such that

$$y _ { i } = \sum _ { j } a _ { j } x _ { i j } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right) ,$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="205" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Matrix functions" -->

then this defines a vector function

$$y = f \left( X \right) = X a .$$ ☒
(5)

The differential in this case is

$$\mathrm { d } f \left( X \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) a = \mathrm { v e c } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) a = \left( a ^ { \prime } \otimes I \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$ ☒ ☒
(6)

and we find for the Jacobian matrix

$$\mathrm { D } f \left( X \right) = a ^ { \prime } \otimes I .$$ ☒
(7)


#### Exercises

1\. Show that the Jacobian matrix of the vector function $f \left( x \right) = A g \left( x \right)$ is
$\mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) = A \mathrm { D } g \left( x \right) ,$ and generalize this to the case where $A$ is a matrix
function of x.

2\. $\mathrm { S h o w }$ that the Jacobian matrix of the vector function $f \left( x \right) = \left( x ^ { \prime } x \right) a$
is $D f \left( x \right) = 2 a x ^ { \prime } ,$ and generalize this to the case where $a$ is a vector
function of x.

3\. Determine the Jacobian matrix of the vector function $f \left( x \right) = \nabla \phi \left( x \right) .$
This matrix is, of course, the Hessian matrix of $\phi .$

4\. Show that the Jacobian matrix of the vector function $f \left( X \right) = X ^ { \prime } a$ is
☒
$\mathrm { D } f \left( X \right) = I \otimes a ^ { \prime } .$
☒

5\. Under the conditions of Section 11, show that the derivative at $X _ { 0 }$ of
the eigenvector function $u \left( X \right)$ is given by
☒

$$\mathrm { D } u \left( X \right) = \frac { \partial u \left( X \right) } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } } = u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I _ { n } - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒


### 13 MATRIX FUNCTIONS

An example of a matrix function of a vector of variables $x$ is

$$F \left( x \right) = x x ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)
.

The differential is

$$\mathrm { d } x x ^ { \prime } = \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) x ^ { \prime } + x \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(2)

so that

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } x x ^ { \prime } = \left( x \otimes I \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } x + \left( I \otimes x \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } x ^ { \prime }$$

$$= \left( I \otimes x + x \otimes I \right) d x .$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="206" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->

Hence,

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( x \right) = I \otimes x + x \otimes I .$$
(4)

Next we consider four simple examples of matrix functions of a matrix or
variables $X ,$ where the order of $X$ is $n \times q .$ First the identity function
☒ ☒

$$F \left( X \right) = X .$$ ☒
(5)

Clearly, d vec $F \left( X \right) = \mathrm { d }$ vec $X ,$ so that
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = I _ { n q } .$$ ☒
(6)

More interesting is the transpose function

$$F \left( X \right) = X ^ { \prime } .$$
(7)

We obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ^ { \prime } = K _ { n q } \mathrm { d v e c } X .$$ ☒
(8)

Hence,

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = K _ { n q } .$$ ☒
(9)

The commutation matrix $K$ is likely to play a role whenever the transpose of
a matrix of variables occurs. For example, when

$$F \left( X \right) = X X ^ { \prime } ,$$ ☒
(10)

then

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { \prime } + X \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime }$$ ☒
(11)

and

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X + \left( I _ { n } \otimes X \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X + \left( I _ { n } \otimes X \right) K _ { n q } \mathrm { d v e c } X$$
$$= \left( \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right) + K _ { n n } \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right) \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$= \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n n } \right) \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$ ☒ ☒
(12)

Hence,

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = 2 N _ { n } \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒
(13)

where $N _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n n } \right)$ is a symmetric idempotent matrix with rank
$\frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( n + 1 \right)$ (see Theorem 3.11).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="207" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Matrix functions" -->

In a similar fashion we obtain from

$$F \left( X \right) = X ^ { \prime } X _ { 1 } ,$$
$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = \left( I _ { q ^ { 2 } } + K _ { q q } \right) \left( I _ { q } \otimes X ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ,$$ ☒ ☒
(14)
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(15)

so that

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = 2 N _ { q } \left( I _ { q } \otimes X ^ { \prime } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒
(16)

These results are summarized in Table 6, where $X$ is an $n \times q$ matrix of vari-
☒
ables.


<table>
<caption>Table 6</caption>
<tr>
<th>$\bar { F \left( X \right. }$ ☒</th>
<th>$\bar { \mathrm { d } F } \left( \bar { X } \right.$ ☒</th>
<th>$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right.$ ☒</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$X$ ☒</td>
<td>$\mathrm { d } X$ ☒</td>
<td>$I _ { n q }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> ☒</td>
<td> ☒</td>
<td>$K _ { n q }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$X X ^ { \prime }$ ☒</td>
<td>$\left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { \prime } + X \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime }$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒</td>
<td>$2 N _ { n } \left( X \otimes I _ { n } \right)$ ☒</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$X ^ { \prime } X$ ☒</td>
<td>$\left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } X + X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X$ ☒ ☒ ☒</td>
<td>$2 N _ { q } \left( I _ { q } \otimes X ^ { \prime } \right)$ ☒</td>
</tr>
</table>


If $X$ is a non-singular $n \times n$ matrix, then the matrix function
☒

$$F \left( X \right) = X ^ { - 1 }$$ ☒ ☒
(17)

has differential

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) = - X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(18)

Taking vecs we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = - \left( \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \otimes X ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒
(19)

Hence,

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = - \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \otimes X ^ { - 1 } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒
(20)

Finally, if $X$ is a square matrix of variables, then we can consider
☒

$$F \left( X \right) = X ^ { p } \quad \left( p = 1 , 2 , \ldots \right) .$$ ☒ ☒
(21)

We take differentials,

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { p - 1 } + X \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { p - 2 } + \cdots + X ^ { p - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$= \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } X ^ { j - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { p - j } ,$$
(22)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="208" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->

and vecs,

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { p - j } \otimes X ^ { j - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$
(23)

Hence,

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { p - j } \otimes X ^ { j - 1 } .$$
(24)

The last two examples are summarized in Table 7.

<caption>Table 7</caption>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th></th>
<th>$\bar { \mathrm { D } F \left( X \right. }$</th>
<th>Conditions</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td>$\bar { - \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \otimes X ^ { - 1 } }$</td>
<td>$\bar { X \text { non-singular } }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td>$\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { p - j } \otimes X ^ { j - 1 }$</td>
<td>$X$ square, $p \in \mathbb{N}$</td>
</tr>
</table>


#### Exercises

1\. Find the Jacobian matrix of the matrix functions $A X \quad B$ and $A X ^ { - 1 } B .$

2\. Find the Jacobian matrix of the matrix functions $X A X ^ { \prime } ,$ $X ^ { \prime } A X ,$ $X A X$
and $X ^ { \prime } A X ^ { \prime } .$

3\. What is the Jacobian matrix of the Moore-Penrose inverse $F \left( X \right) = X ^ { + }$
☒
(see Section 8.5).

4\. What is the Jacobian matrix of the adjoint matrix $F \left( X \right) = X ^ { \# }$ (see
☒
Section 8.6).

5\. Let $F \left( X \right) = A G \left( X \right) B H \left( X \right) C ,$ where $A ,$ $B$ and $C$ are constant matrices.
☒ ☒ ☒
Find the Jacobian matrix of $F .$


### 14 KRONECKER PRODUCTS

An interesting problem arises in the treatment of Kronecker products. Con-
sider the matrix function

$$F \left( X , Y \right) = X \otimes Y .$$
(1)

The differential is easily found as

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( X , Y \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \otimes Y + X \otimes \mathrm { d } Y ,$$
(2)

and, upon taking vecs, we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X , Y \right) = \mathrm { v e c } \left( \mathrm { d } X \otimes Y \right) + \mathrm { v e c } \left( X \otimes \mathrm { d } Y \right) .$$ ☒
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="209" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] Kronecker products" -->

In order to find the Jacobian of $F$ we must find matrices $A \left( Y \right)$ and $B \left( X \right)$
☒ ☒
such that

$$\mathrm { v e c } \left( \mathrm { d } X \otimes Y \right) = A \left( Y \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$
(4)

and

$$\mathrm { v e c } \left( X \otimes \mathrm { d } Y \right) = B \left( X \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } Y ,$$
(5)

in which case the Jacobian matrix of $F \left( X , Y \right)$ takes the partitioned form
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X , Y \right) = \left( A \left( Y \right) : B \left( X \right) \right) .$$ ☒ ☒
(6)

The crucial step here is to realize that we can express the vec of a Kronecker
product of two matrices in terms of the Kronecker product of their vecs, that
is

$$\mathrm { v e c } \left( X \otimes Y \right) = \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y \right) ,$$
(7)

where it is assumed that $X$ is an $n \times q$ matrix and $Y$ is a $p \times r$ matrix (see
☒

Theorem 3.10).

Using (7) we now write

$$\mathrm { v e c } \left( \mathrm { d } X \otimes Y \right) = \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y \right)$$
$$= \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( I _ { n q } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$

(8)

Hence,

$$A \left( Y \right) = \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( I _ { n q } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y \right)$$
$$= I _ { q } \otimes \left( \left( K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y \right) \right) .$$

(9)

In a similar fashion we find

$$B \left( X \right) = \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \otimes I _ { p r } \right)$$
$$= \left( \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \otimes I _ { r } \right) \right) \otimes I _ { p } .$$ ☒

(10)

We thus obtain the useful formula

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \left( X \otimes Y \right) = \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left[ \left( I _ { n q } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right.$$

$$\left. + \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \otimes I _ { p r } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } Y \right] ,$$
(11)

from which the Jacobian matrix of the matrix function $F \left( X , Y \right) = X \otimes Y$
☒
follows:

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X , Y \right) = \left( I _ { q } \otimes K _ { r n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left( I _ { n q } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y : \mathrm { v e c } X \otimes I _ { p r } \right) .$$
(12)

Exercises

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="210" -->
<!-- PageHeader="First-order differentials and Jacobian matrices [Ch. 9" -->

1\. Let $F \left( X , Y \right) = X X ^ { \prime } \otimes Y Y ^ { \prime } ,$ where $X$ has $n$ rows and $Y$ has $p$ rows (the
number of columns of $X$ and $Y$ is irrelevant). Show that

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X , Y \right) = \left( I _ { n } \otimes K _ { p n } \otimes I _ { p } \right) \left[ \left( G _ { n } \left( X \right) \otimes \mathrm { v e c } Y Y ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right.$$
$$\left. + \left( \mathrm { v e c } X X ^ { \prime } \otimes G _ { p } \left( Y \right) \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } Y \right] ,$$ ☒ ☒

where

$$G _ { m } \left( A \right) = \left( I _ { m ^ { 2 } } + K _ { m m } \right) \left( A \otimes I _ { m } \right)$$

for any matrix $A$ having $m$ rows. Compute $\mathrm { D } F \left( X , Y \right) .$
☒

2\. Find the differential and the derivative of the matrix function $F \left( X , Y \right) =$
☒
$X \odot Y$ (Hadamard product).


### 15 SOME OTHER PROBLEMS

Suppose we want $t o$ find the Jacobian matrix of the real-valued function
$\phi : \mathrm { R } ^ { n \times q } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ given by

$$\phi \left( X \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } x _ { i j } ^ { 2 } .$$
(1)

We can, of course, obtain the Jacobian matrix by first calculating (easy, in
this case) the partial derivatives. More appealing, however, is to note that

$$\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } X X ^ { \prime } ,$$ ☒
(2)

from which we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( X \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X$$ ☒
(3)

and

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = 2 X .$$
(4)

This example is, of course, very simple. But the idea of expressing a func-
tion of $X$ in terms of the matrix $X$ rather than in terms of the elements $x _ { i j }$
☒
is often important. Some more examples should clarify this.

Let $\phi \left( X \right)$ be defined as the sum of the $n ^ { 2 }$ elements of $X ^ { - 1 } .$ Then, let $\imath$ be
☒
the $n \times 1$ sum vector $\left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime }$ and write

$$\phi \left( X \right) = \imath ^ { \prime } X ^ { - 1 } \imath$$
(5)

from which we easily obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( X \right) = - \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \imath \imath ^ { \prime } X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="211" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

and hence

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = - \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \imath \imath ^ { \prime } \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$ ☒ ☒
(7)

Consider another example. Let $F \left( X \right)$ be the $n \times \left( n - 1 \right)$ matrix function
☒
of an $n \times n$ matrix of variables $X$ defined as $X ^ { - 1 }$ without its last column.
☒ ☒
Then let $E _ { n }$ be the $n \times \left( n - 1 \right)$ matrix obtained from the identity matrix $I _ { n }$
by deleting its last column, i.e.

$$E _ { n } = \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { n - 1 } \\ 0 ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$
(8)

With $E _ { n }$ so defined, we can express $F \left( X \right)$ as
☒

$$F \left( X \right) = X ^ { - 1 } E _ { n } .$$ ☒ ☒
(9)

It is then simple to find

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( X \right) = - X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } E _ { n } = - X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) F \left( X \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(10)

and hence

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = - F ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) \otimes X ^ { - 1 } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒
(11)

As a final example, consider the real-valued function $\phi \left( X \right)$ defined as the
☒
ij-th element of $X ^ { 2 } .$ In this case we can write
☒

$$\phi \left( X \right) = e _ { i } ^ { \prime } X ^ { 2 } e _ { j } ,$$ ☒ ☒
(12)

where $e _ { i }$ and $e _ { j }$ are unit vectors. Hence

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( X \right) = e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X e _ { j } + e _ { i } ^ { \prime } X \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) e _ { j }$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( X e _ { j } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } + e _ { j } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } X \right) \mathrm { d } X ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

(13)

so that

$$\frac { \partial \phi \left( X \right) } { \partial X } = e _ { i } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } + X ^ { \prime } e _ { i } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } .$$
(14)


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§3. See also Magnus and Neudecker (1985) and Pollock (1985).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 10 Second-order differentials and

$$H e s s i a n \quad m a t r i c e s$$


### 1 INTRODUCTION

Whilst in Chapter 9 the main tool was the first identification theorem, in
the present chapter it is the second identification theorem (Theorem 6.13)
which plays the central role. The second identification theorem tells us how
to obtain the Hessian matrix from the second differential, and the purpose of
this chapter is to demonstrate its workings in practice.


### 2 THE HESSIAN MATRIX OF A MATRIX FUNCTION

For a scalar function $\phi$ of an $n \times 1$ vector x, the Hessian matrix of $\phi$ at $x$
was introduced in Section 6.3 - it is the $n \times n$ matrix of second-order partial
derivatives $\mathrm { D } _ { j i } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right)$ denoted by

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right)$$

or

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) } { \partial x \partial x ^ { \prime } } .$$
(1)

We note that

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } \left( \frac { \partial \phi \left( x \right) } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { D } \left( \mathrm { D } \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(2)

For a vector function $f : \mathbb{R} ^ { n } \rightarrow \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ we defined the Hessian matrix as the
stacked matrix

$$\mathrm { H } f \left( x \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { H } f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \\ \mathrm { H } f _ { 2 } \left( x \right) \\ \vdots \\ \mathrm { H } f _ { m } \left( x \right) \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

<!-- PageNumber="213" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="214" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Second-order differentials and Hessian matrices [Ch. 10" -->

Without much difficulty one verifies that

$$\mathrm { H } f \left( x \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } \mathrm { v e c } \left( \frac { \partial f \left( x \right) } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { D } \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(4)

This suggests the following definition of the Hessian matrix of a matrix
function (compare Section 6.14).


#### Definition

Let $F$ be a twice differentiable $m \times p$ matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X .$
The Hessian matrix of F at $X$ is the $m n p q \times n q$ matrix

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( X \right) = \mathrm { D } \left( \mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) \right) ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒ ☒
(5)


#### Exercises

1\. Show that

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( X \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } } \mathrm { v e c } \left( \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

2\. Write $\mathrm { H } F \left( X \right)$ in terms of the Hessian matrices $\mathrm { H } F _ { i j } \left( X \right)$ of its component
☒ ☒
functions.

3\. Evaluate $\mathrm { D } ^ { 2 } f \left( x \right) = \mathrm { D } \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) \right) .$ Compare $D ^ { 2 } f \left( x \right)$ with $\mathrm { D } \left( \mathrm { D } f \left( x \right) \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ and
conclude that the latter expression is more practical as a definition for
the Hessian matrix than the former.


### 3 IDENTIFICATION OF HESSIAN MATRICES

The second identification theorem (Theorem 6.6) allows us to identify the Hes-
sian matrix of a scalar function through its second differential. More precisely,
it tells us that

$$d ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) = \left( d x \right) ^ { \prime } B d x$$
(1)

implies and is implied by

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( B + B ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$
(2)

where $B$ may depend on x, but not on $\mathrm { d } x .$

The second identification theorem for vector functions (Theorem 6.7) al-
lows us to identify the Hessian matrix of an $m \times 1$ vector function $f \left( x \right) .$ If
$B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots , B _ { m }$ are square matrices and

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { 1 } \\ B _ { 2 } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="215" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The second identification table" -->

then

$$d ^ { 2 } f \left( x \right) = \left( I _ { m } \otimes d x \right) ^ { \prime } B d x$$ ☒
(4)

implies and is implied by

$$H f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( B + \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } \right) ,$$
(5)

where $B$ may depend on $x ,$ and

$$\left( B ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ B _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$
(6)

The extension to matrix functions is straightforward. The second identifi-
cation theorem for matrix functions (Theorem 6.13) states that

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( X \right) = \left( I _ { m p } \otimes \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } B \mathrm { d v e c } X$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(7)

implies and is implied by

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( B + \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } \right) ,$$ ☒
(8)

where $F \left( X \right)$ is an $m \times p$ matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix $o f \quad v a r i a b l e s \quad X ,$
☒ ☒

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { 1 1 } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m 1 } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { 1 p } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m p } \end{array} \right) , \quad \left( B ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } = \left( \begin{array}{} & \vdots \\ B _ { m 1 } ^ { \prime } & \\ B _ { 1 p } ^ { \prime } & \\ & B _ { 1 p } ^ { \prime } \\ \vdots & \\ B _ { m p } ^ { \prime } & \end{array} \right) ,$$
B11'
(9)

and the $B _ { i j }$ are square $n q \times n q$ matrices.


### 4 THE SECOND IDENTIFICATION TABLE

These considerations lead to Table 1.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="216" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Second-order differentials and Hessian matrices [Ch. 10" -->

</tr>

</tr>

</tr>

<td>$f \left( X \right)$</td>

</tr>

In the second identification table, $\phi$ is a scalar function, $f$ an $m \times 1$ vector
function and $F$ an $m \times p$ matrix function; $\xi$ is a scalar, $x$ an $n \times 1$ vector and
$X$ and $n \times q$ matrix; $\beta$ is a scalar, $b$ is a column vector and $B$ is a matrix,
each of which may be a function of $X ,$ $x$ or $\xi .$ In the case of a vector function
$f ,$ we have

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} { B _ { 1 } } \\ { B _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { B _ { m } } \end{array} \right)$$

and

$$\left( B ^ { \prime } \right) _ { v } = \left( \begin{array}{} B _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \\ B _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \\ \vdots \\ B _ { m } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$
(1)

In the case of a $m a t r i x \quad f u n c t i o n \quad F ,$ we have

The matrices $B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots , B _ { m }$ (respectively, $\left. B _ { 1 1 } , \ldots , B _ { m p } \right)$ are square ma-
trices of order $n \times n$ if $f$ (or $\left. F \right)$ is a function of an $n \times 1$ vector x; the order
of these matrices is $n q \times n q$ if $f$ (or $\left. F \right)$ is a function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X .$
