2\. At every point where the $n \times n$ matrix $X$ is non-singular, show that the
Hessian matrix of the real-valued function $\phi \left( X \right) = | X |$ is
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = | X | K _ { n } \left( X ^ { - 1 } \otimes I _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \left( \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } - I _ { n ^ { 2 } } \right) \left( I _ { n } \otimes X ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

Show that $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right)$ is non-singular for every $n \geq 2 .$
☒


### 5 AN EXPLICIT FORMULA FOR THE HESSIAN MATRIX

It is sometimes difficult to find the Jacobian matrix or Hessian matrix of a
matrix function from the identification tables. In such cases it is convenient
to have an expression which gives the Jacobian matrix or Hessian matrix
explicitly in terms of the partial derivatives.

Let $F$ be an $m \times p$ matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix of variables $X .$
If $q = 1 ,$ we write $x$ instead of $X .$ Let $e _ { i }$ and $e _ { s }$ be $n \times 1$ unit vectors with
a one in the $i$ i-th (s-th) place and zeros elsewhere, and let $E _ { i j }$ and $E _ { s t }$ be
$n \times q$ matrices with a one in the ij-th $\left( s t - t h \right)$ position and zeros elsewhere.
The Jacobian matrix of $F \left( x \right)$ can be expressed as

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) e _ { i } ^ { \prime }$$
(1)

and, as noted in Section 9.4 (Exercise 1), the Jacobian matrix of $F \left( X \right)$ can
☒
be expressed as

$$\mathrm { D } F \left( X \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i j } } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { i j } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒
(2)

Similar expressions can be found for the Hessian matrix of $F \left( x \right)$ and $F \left( X \right) .$
☒
We have in fact

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \mathrm { v e c } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { s } \partial x _ { i } } \right) \otimes E _ { i s }$$
(3)

and

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( X \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { q } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { s t } \partial x _ { i j } } \right) \otimes \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { i j } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { s t } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒
(4)

The verification of these results is left to the reader.


### 6 SCALAR FUNCTIONS

In many cases the second differential of a real-valued function $\phi \left( X \right)$ takes one
☒
of the two forms

$$\mathrm { t r } B \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } C \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad \mathrm { t r } B \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) C \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) .$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="218" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Second-order differentials and Hessian matrices [Ch. 10" -->

The following result will then prove useful.


#### Theorem 1

$\mathrm { L e t } \phi$ be a twice differentiable real-valued function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X .$ Then
the following two relationships hold between the second differential and the
Hessian matrix of $\phi$ at $X :$

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } B \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } C \mathrm { d } X \Leftrightarrow \mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes C + B \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right)$$

and

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } B \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) C \mathrm { d } X \Leftrightarrow \mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } K _ { q n } \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes C + C ^ { \prime } \otimes B \right) .$$

Proof. Using the fact, established in Theorem 2.3, that

tr
$$A B C D = \left( \mathrm { v e c } B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { \prime } \otimes C \right) \mathrm { v e c } D ,$$
(2)

we obtain

$$\mathrm { t r } B \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } C \mathrm { d } X = \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes C \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$
(3)

and

$$\mathrm { t r } B \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) C \mathrm { d } X = \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes C \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } K _ { q n } \left( B ^ { \prime } \otimes C \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X .$$

(4)

The result now follows from the second identification table.
✷
☐

Let us give three examples. First, consider the quadratic function

$$\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } A X .$$
(5)

Twice taking differentials, we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( X \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { d } X ,$$
(6)

so that

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = I \otimes \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(7)

As a second example, consider the real-valued function

$$\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(8)

defined for every non-singular $n \times n$ matrix $X .$ We have

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( X \right) = - \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="219" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7 ] Vector functions" -->

and therefore

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( X \right) = - \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } X ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } - \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \left( \mathrm { d } X ^ { - 1 } \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$= 2 \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } = 2 \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(10)

so that the Hessian matrix becomes

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = K _ { n } \left( X ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 2 } \otimes X ^ { - 1 } + X ^ { \prime } { } ^ { - 1 } \otimes X ^ { - 2 } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(11)

Finally, if $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is a simple eigenvalue of a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $X _ { 0 }$ with
☒ ☒
associated eigenvector $u _ { 0 } ,$ then there exists a twice differentiable 'eigenvalue
function' $\lambda$ such that $\lambda \left( X _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { 0 }$ (see Theorem 8.7). The second differential
at $X _ { 0 }$ is given in Theorem 8.10; it is
☒

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda = 2 u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) u _ { 0 }$$
$$= 2 \mathrm { t r } u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \mathrm { d } X .$$ ☒

(12)

Hence the Hessian matrix is

$$\mathrm { H } \lambda \left( X \right) = K _ { n } \left( u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } + \left( \lambda _ { 0 } I - X _ { 0 } \right) ^ { + } \otimes u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) .$$ ☒
(13)


### Exercises

1\. Show that the Hessian matrix of $\phi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } A X B X ^ { \prime }$ is $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = B ^ { \prime } \otimes$
☒ ☒

$A + B \otimes A ^ { \prime } .$

2\. Show that the Hessian matrix of $\phi \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { 2 }$ is $\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = K _ { n }$ if $X$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
is an $n \times n$ matrix.

3\. Determine the Hessian matrix of $\phi \left( X \right) = a ^ { \prime } X X ^ { \prime } a .$
☒ ☒ ☒

4\. At points where the $n \times n$ matrix $X$ has a positive determinant, show
that the Hessian matrix of $\phi \left( X \right) = \log | X |$ is
☒ ☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { H } \phi \left( X \right) = - K _ { n } \left( \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } \otimes X ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒


## 7 VECTOR FUNCTIONS

Let us consider one example of a vector function, namely

$$f \left( x \right) = \phi \left( x \right) a ,$$
(1)

where $\phi$ is a real-valued function of an $n \times 1$ vector of variables x, and $a$ is an
$m \times 1$ vector of constants. The second differential is

$$d ^ { 2 } f \left( x \right) = d ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) a = \left( \left( d x \right) ^ { \prime } \left( H \phi \left( x \right) \right) \left( d x \right) \right) a$$
$$= a \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right) \right) \mathrm { d } x = \left( a \otimes \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right) \right) \mathrm { d } x$$
$$= \left( I _ { m } \otimes \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \left( a \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( \mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right) \right) \mathrm { d } x ,$$

(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="220" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Second-order differentials and Hessian matrices [Ch. 10" -->

so that

$$\mathrm { H } f \left( x \right) = \left( a \otimes I _ { n } \right) \mathrm { H } \phi \left( x \right)$$
(3)

according to the second identification table.


## 8 MATRIX FUNCTIONS, I

We shall consider two examples of Hessian matrices of a matrix function. The
first is a matrix function of an $n \times 1$ vector $x ,$

$$F \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x x ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)

It is easy to obtain

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } F \left( x \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(2)

from which we find

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( x \right) = \mathrm { v e c } \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } = \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { d } x \right) \mathrm { d } x .$$
(3)

We now use the fact that

$$d x = \left( I _ { n } \otimes \left( d x \right) ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n }$$
(4)

to obtain

$$I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { d } x = I _ { n } \otimes \left( \left( I _ { n } \otimes \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right)$$
$$= \left( I _ { n } \otimes I _ { n } \otimes \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \right) \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒

(5)

Substituting (5) in (3) yields

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } F \left( x \right) = \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } \otimes \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) \mathrm { d } x .$$
(6)

The Hessian matrix then follows from the second identification table; it is

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } + \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) _ { v } ^ { \prime } \right) .$$ ☒
(7)

Alternatively we can use Equation (5.3). We find

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F \left( x \right) } { \partial x _ { s } \partial x _ { i } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="221" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Matrix functions, $I I$" -->

and thus

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \otimes e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( e _ { s } \otimes e _ { i } \otimes e _ { s } ^ { \prime } \otimes e _ { i } + e _ { i } \otimes e _ { s } \otimes e _ { s } ^ { \prime } \otimes e _ { i } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( e _ { s } \otimes e _ { s } ^ { \prime } \otimes e _ { i } \otimes e _ { i } + \left( K _ { n } \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( e _ { s } \otimes e _ { s } ^ { \prime } \otimes e _ { i } \otimes e _ { i } \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } \right) \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) .$$

(9)

In this case, the second derivation is more straightforward than the first;
moreover, it leads to a more appealing (although of course equivalent) expres-
sion, namely (9) rather than $\left( 7 \right) .$


### Exercise

1\. Show that $\left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) _ { v } ^ { \prime } = \left( K _ { n } \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) .$


## 9 MATRIX FUNCTIONS, II

The second example is a matrix function of an $n \times q$ matrix $X$ ,

$$F \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } X X ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)

We find

$$\frac { \partial F \left( X \right) } { \partial x _ { i j } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( E _ { i j } X ^ { \prime } + X E _ { i j } ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(2)

and thus

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F \left( X \right) } { \partial x _ { s t } \partial x _ { i j } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( E _ { i j } E _ { s t } ^ { \prime } + E _ { s t } E _ { i j } ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \delta _ { j t } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$

(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="222" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Second-order differentials and Hessian matrices [Ch. 10" -->

where $\delta _ { j t }$ denotes the Kronecker delta. Using Equation (4) of Section 5 we
obtain the Hessian matrix

$$\mathrm { H } F \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { q } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { q } \delta _ { j t } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \otimes \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { i j } \right) \left( \right.$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \otimes \left( \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { q } \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { i t } \right) \left( \mathrm { v e c } E _ { s t } \right) ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \otimes I _ { q } \otimes e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( K _ { n ^ { 2 } , q } \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( I _ { q } \otimes \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } + e _ { s } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \otimes e _ { i } e _ { s } ^ { \prime } \right)$$ ☒
(vec Est)'
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$= \left( K _ { n ^ { 2 } , q } \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( I _ { q } \otimes A \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒
(4)

where $A$ is the Hessian matrix derived in the previous section,

$$A = \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { n ^ { 2 } } + K _ { n } \right) \otimes I _ { n } \right) \left( I _ { n } \otimes \mathrm { v e c } I _ { n } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->


# Part $F o u r$ -

Inequalities

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


# CHAPTER 11 Inequalities


## 1 INTRODUCTION

Inequalities occur in many disciplines. In economics they occur primarily be-
cause economics is concerned with optimizing behaviour. In other words, we
often want to find an $x ^ { * }$ such that $\phi \left( x ^ { * } \right) \geq \phi \left( x \right)$ for all $x$ in some set. The
equivalence of the inequality

$$\phi \left( x \right) \geq 0 \quad \text { for all } x \mathrm { i n } S$$
(1)

and the minimization problem

$$\min _ { x \in S } \phi \left( x \right) = 0$$
(2)

suggests that inequalities can often be tackled using differential calculus. We
shall see in this chapter that this method does not always lead to success, but
if it does we shall use it.

The chapter falls naturally into several parts. In Sections 1-4 we discuss
(matrix analogues of) the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and the arithmetic-
geometric means inequality. Sections 5-14 are devoted to inequalities con-
cerning eigenvalues and contain inter alia Fischer's min-max theorem and
Poincaré's separation theorem. In Section 15 we prove Hadamard's inequal-
ity. In Sections 16-23 we use Karamata's inequality to prove a representation
theorem for $\left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } ,$ $p > 1 ,$ $A$ positive semidefinite, which in turn is used to
establish matrix analogues of the inequalities of Hölder and Minkowski. Sec-
tions 24 and 25 contain Minkowski's determinant theorem. In Sections 26-28
several inequalities concerning the weighted means of order $p$ are discussed.
Finally, in Sections 29-32, we turn to least-squares inequalities.


## 2 THE CAUCHY-SCHWARZ INEQUALITY

We begin our discussion of inequalities with the following fundamental result.

<!-- PageNumber="225" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="226" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->


### Theorem 1 (Cauchy-Schwarz)

For any two vectors $a$ and $b$ of the same order we have

$$\left( a ^ { \prime } b \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \left( a ^ { \prime } a \right) \left( b ^ { \prime } b \right)$$
(1)

with equality if and only if $a$ and $b$ are linearly dependent.

Let us give two proofs.

First proof. For any matrix $A ,$ tr $A ^ { \prime } A \geq 0$ with equality if and only if $A = 0 ,$
see (1.10.8). Now define

$$A = a b ^ { \prime } - b a ^ { \prime } .$$
(2)
.

Then,

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } A = 2 \left( a ^ { \prime } a \right) \left( b ^ { \prime } b \right) - 2 \left( a ^ { \prime } b \right) ^ { 2 } \geq 0$$
(3)

with equality if and only if $a b ^ { \prime } = b a ^ { \prime } ,$ that is, if and only if $a$ and $b$ are linearly
dependent.
✷
☐

Second proof. If $b = 0$ the result is trivial. Assume therefore that $b \neq 0 ,$
and consider the matrix

$$M = I - \left( 1 / b ^ { \prime } b \right) b b ^ { \prime } .$$
(4)

The matrix $M$ is symmetric idempotent, and therefore positive semidefinite.
Hence,

$$\left( a ^ { \prime } a \right) \left( b ^ { \prime } b \right) - \left( a ^ { \prime } b \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( b ^ { \prime } b \right) a ^ { \prime } M a \geq 0 .$$
(5)

Equality in (5) implies $a ^ { \prime } M a = 0 ,$ and hence $M a = 0 .$ That is, $a = \alpha b$ with
$\alpha = a ^ { \prime } b / b ^ { \prime } b .$ The result follows.
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. If $A$ is positive semidefinite, show that

$$\left( x ^ { \prime } A y \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) \left( y ^ { \prime } A y \right)$$

with equality if and only if $A x$ and $A y$ are linearly dependent.

2\. Hence show that, for $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ positive semidefinite,

$$| a _ { i j } | \leq \max _ { i } | a _ { i i } | .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="227" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Matrix analogues of the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality" -->

3\. Show that

$$\left( x ^ { \prime } y \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) \left( y ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } y \right)$$

for every positive definite matrix $A ,$ with equality if and only if $x$ and
$A ^ { - 1 } y$ are linearly dependent.

4\. Given $x \neq 0 ,$ define $\psi \left( A \right) = \left( x ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { - 1 }$ for $A$ positive definite. Show
that

$$\psi \left( A \right) = \min _ { y } \frac { y ^ { \prime } A y } { \left( y ^ { \prime } x \right) ^ { 2 } } .$$

5\. Prove Bergstrom's inequality,

$$x ^ { \prime } \left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } x \leq \frac { \left( x ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( x ^ { \prime } B ^ { - 1 } x \right) } { x ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { - 1 } + B ^ { - 1 } \right) x }$$

for any positive definite matrices $A$ and $B .$ [Hint: Use the fact that
$\psi \left( A + B \right) \geq \psi \left( A \right) + \psi \left( B \right)$ where $\psi$ is defined in Exercise 4.]

6\. Show that

$$| \left( 1 / n \right) \sum x _ { i } | \leq \left( \left( 1 / n \right) \sum x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }$$

with equality if and only if $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } .$

7\. If all eigenvalues of $A$ are real, show that

$$| \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A | \leq \left( \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }$$

with equality if and only if the eigenvalues of the $n \times n$ matrix $A$ are all
equal.

8\. Prove the triangle inequality: $\| x + y \| \leq \| x \| + \| y \| .$


## 3 MATRIX ANALOGUES OF THE CAUCHY-SCHWARZ INEQUALITY

The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality can be extended to matrices in several $\mathrm { w a y s } .$


### Theorem 2

For any two real matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order, we have

$$\left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } A \right) \left( \mathrm { t r } B ^ { \prime } B \right)$$
(1)

with equality if and only if one of the matrices $A$ and $B$ is a multiple of the
other; also

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( A ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \mathrm { t r } \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) \left( B ^ { \prime } B \right)$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="228" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

with equality if and only if $A B ^ { \prime } = B A ^ { \prime }$ and

$$| A ^ { \prime } B | ^ { 2 } \leq | A ^ { \prime } A | | B ^ { \prime } B |$$
(3)

with equality if and only if $A ^ { \prime } A$ or $B ^ { \prime } B$ is singular, or $B = A Q$ for some
non-singular matrix $Q .$

Proof. The first inequality follows from Theorem 1 by letting $a = \mathrm { v e c } A$ and
$b = \mathrm { v e c } B .$ To prove the second inequality, let $X = A B ^ { \prime }$ and $Y = B A ^ { \prime }$ and
apply (1) to the matrices $X$ and $Y .$ This gives

$$\left( \mathrm { t r } B A ^ { \prime } B A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } B A ^ { \prime } A B ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { t r } A B ^ { \prime } B A ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$
(4)

from which $\left( 2 \right)$ follows. The condition for equality in $\left( 2 \right)$ is easily established.

Finally, to prove (3), assume that $| A ^ { \prime } B | \neq 0 .$ (If $| A ^ { \prime } B | = 0 ,$ the result is
$\left. \mathrm { t r i v i a l } . \right)$ Then both $A$ and $B$ have full column rank, so that $A ^ { \prime } A$ and $B ^ { \prime } B$ are
non-singular. Now define

$$G = B ^ { \prime } A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } B , \quad H = B ^ { \prime } \left( I - A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } \right) B ,$$
(5)

and notice that $G$ is positive definite and $H$ positive semidefinite (because
$I - A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime }$ is idempotent). Since $| G + H | \geq | G |$ by Theorem 1.22, with
equality if and only if $H = 0 ,$ we obtain

$$| B ^ { \prime } B | \geq | B ^ { \prime } A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } B | = | A ^ { \prime } B | ^ { 2 } | A ^ { \prime } A | ^ { - 1 }$$
(6)

with equality if and only if $B ^ { \prime } \left( I - A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } \right) B = 0 ,$ that is, if and only if
$\left( I - A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } \right) B = 0 .$ This concludes the proof.
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. Show that $\mathrm { t r } \left( A ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \mathrm { t r } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( B B ^ { \prime } \right)$ with equality if and only if $A ^ { \prime } B$ is
symmetric.

2\. Prove Schur's inequality tr $A ^ { 2 } \leq \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } A$ with equality if and only if $A$ is
symmetric. [Hint: Use the commutation matrix.]


## 4 THE THEOREM OF THE ARITHMETIC AND GEOMETRIC MEANS

The most famous of all inequalities is the arithmetic-geometric mean inequal-
ity which was first proved (assuming equal weights) by Euclid. In its simplest
form it asserts that

$$x ^ { \alpha } y ^ { 1 - \alpha } \leq \alpha x + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) y \quad \left( 0 < \alpha < 1 \right)$$
(1)

for every non-negative $x$ and $y ,$ with equality if and only if $x = y .$ Let us
demonstrate the general theorem.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="229" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The theorem of the arithmetic and geometric means" -->


### Theorem 3

For any two $n \times 1$ vectors $x = \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ and $a = \left( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$
satisfying $x _ { i } \geq 0 ,$ $\alpha _ { i } > 0$ 0, $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } = 1 ,$ we have

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha _ { i } } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i }$$
(2)

with equality if and only if $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } .$

Proof. Assume that $x _ { i } > 0 ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ (if at least one $x _ { i }$ is zero the result is
trivially true), and define

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } - \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha _ { i } } .$$
(3)

We wish to show that $\phi \left( x \right) \geq 0$ for all positive x. Differentiating $\phi ,$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \phi = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } \mathrm { d } x _ { i } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha _ { i } - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } x _ { i } \right) \prod _ { j \neq i } x _ { j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } }$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \alpha _ { i } - \left( \alpha _ { i } / x _ { i } \right) \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } } \right) \mathrm { d } x _ { i } .$$

(4)

The first-order conditions are therefore

$$\left( \alpha _ { i } / x _ { i } \right) \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } } = \alpha _ { i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(5)

that is,

$$x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } .$$
(6)

At such points $\phi \left( x \right) = 0 .$ Since $\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha _ { i } }$ is concave, $\phi \left( x \right)$ is convex. Hence by
Theorem 7.8, $\phi$ has an absolute minimum (namely zero) at every point where
$x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } .$
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. Prove (1) by using the fact that the log-function is concave on $\left( 0 , \infty \right) .$

2\. Use Theorem 3 to show that

$$| A | ^ { 1 / n } \leq \left( 1 / n \right) \text { tr } A$$
(7)

for every $n \times n$ positive semidefinite $A .$ Also show that equality occurs
if and only if $A = \mu I$ for some $\mu \geq 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="230" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

3\. Prove (7) directly for positive definite $A$ by letting $A = X ^ { \prime } X$ $\left( X \right.$ square)
and defining

$$\phi \left( X \right) = \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X - | X | ^ { 2 / n } .$$
(8)

Show that

$$d \phi = \left( 2 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } \left( X ^ { \prime } - | X | ^ { 2 / n } X ^ { - 1 } \right) d X$$
(9)

and

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi = \left( 2 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right)$$
$$+ \left( 2 / n \right) | X | ^ { 2 / n } \left( \mathrm { t r } \left( X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( 2 / n \right) \left( \mathrm { t r } X ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

(10)


## 5 THE RAYLEIGH QUOTIENT

In the next few sections we shall investigate inequalities concerning eigenvalues
of real symmetric matrices. We shall adopt the convention to arrange the
eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ of a real symmetric matrix $A$ in increasing order,
so that

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$$
(1)

Our first result concerns the bounds of the Rayleigh quotient: $x ^ { \prime } A x / x ^ { \prime } x .$


### Theorem 4

For any real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $A ,$

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \leq \lambda _ { n } .$$
(2)

Proof. Let $S$ be an orthogonal $n \times n$ matrix such that

$$S ^ { \prime } A S = \Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } \right)$$
(3)

and let $y = S ^ { \prime } x .$ Since

$$\lambda _ { 1 } y ^ { \prime } y \leq y ^ { \prime } \Lambda y \leq \lambda _ { n } y ^ { \prime } y ,$$
(4)

we obtain

$$\lambda _ { 1 } x ^ { \prime } x \leq x ^ { \prime } A x \leq \lambda _ { n } x ^ { \prime } x ,$$
(5)

because $x ^ { \prime } A x = y ^ { \prime } \Lambda y$ and $x ^ { \prime } x = y ^ { \prime } y .$
✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="231" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] Concavity of $\lambda _ { 1 } ,$ convexity of $\lambda _ { n }$" -->

Since the extrema of $x ^ { \prime } A x / x ^ { \prime } x$ can be achieved (by choosing $x$ to be an
eigenvector associated with $\lambda _ { 1 }$ or $\lambda _ { n } ,$ Theorem 4 implies that we may define
$\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { n }$ as follows:

$$\lambda _ { 1 } = \min _ { x } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$
$$\lambda _ { n } = \max _ { x } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } .$$
(6)
(7)

The representations $\left( 6 \right)$ and (7) show that we can express $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { n }$ (two non-
linear functions of $\left. A \right)$ as an envelope of linear functions of $A .$ This technique
is called quasilinearization: the right-hand sides of (6) and (7) are quasilinear
representations of $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { n } .$ We shall encounter some useful applications of
this technique in the next few sections.


### Exercises

1\. Use the quasilinear representations (6) and (7) to show that

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \left( A + B \right) \geq \lambda _ { 1 } \left( A \right) , \quad \lambda _ { n } \left( A + B \right) \geq \lambda _ { n } \left( A \right) ,$$
$$\lambda _ { 1 } \left( A \right) \mathrm { t r } B \leq \mathrm { t r } A B \leq \lambda _ { n } \left( A \right)$$
tr $B$

for any $n \times n$ symmetric matrix $A$ and positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix
$B .$

2\. If $A$ is a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix and $A _ { k }$ is a $k \times k$ principal submatrix
of $A ,$ then prove

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \left( A \right) \leq \lambda _ { 1 } \left( A _ { k } \right) \leq \lambda _ { k } \left( A _ { k } \right) \leq \lambda _ { n } \left( A \right) .$$

(A generalization of this result is given in Theorem 12.)

3\. Show that

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \left( A + B \right) \geq \lambda _ { 1 } \left( A \right) + \lambda _ { 1 } \left( B \right) ,$$
$$\lambda _ { n } \left( A + B \right) \leq \lambda _ { n } \left( A \right) + \lambda _ { n } \left( B \right)$$

for any two symmetric $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $B .$ (See also Theorem 5.)


## 6 CONCAVITY OF $\lambda _ { 1 } ,$ CONVEXITY OF $\lambda _ { n }$

As an immediate consequence of the definitions (5.6) and (5.7), let us prove
Theorem 5, thus illustrating the usefulness of quasilinear representations.


## Theorem 5

For any two real symmetric matrices $A$ and $B$ of order $n$ and $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \left( \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B \right) \geq \alpha \lambda _ { 1 } \left( A \right) + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \lambda _ { 1 } \left( B \right) ,$$
$$\lambda _ { n } \left( \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B \right) \leq \alpha \lambda _ { n } \left( A \right) + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \lambda _ { n } \left( B \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="232" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

Hence, $\lambda _ { 1 }$ is concave and $\lambda _ { n }$ convex on the space of real symmetric matrices.

Proof. Using the representation (5.6), we obtain

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \left( \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B \right) = \min _ { x } \frac { x ^ { \prime } \left( \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B \right) x } { x ^ { \prime } x }$$
$$\geq \alpha \min _ { x } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \min _ { x } \frac { x ^ { \prime } B x } { x ^ { \prime } x }$$
$$= \alpha \lambda _ { 1 } \left( A \right) + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \lambda _ { 1 } \left( B \right) .$$

The analogue for $\lambda _ { n }$ is proved similarly.
☐
✷


## 7 VARIATIONAL DESCRIPTION OF EIGENVALUES

The representation of $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { n }$ given in (5.6) and (5.7) can be extended in
the following way.


## Theorem 6

Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$
Let $S = \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { n } \right)$ be an orthogonal $n \times n$ matrix which diagonalizes $A ,$
so that

$$S ^ { \prime } A S = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } \right) .$$
(1)

Then, for $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ,$

$$\lambda _ { k } = \min _ { R _ { k - 1 } ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \max _ { T _ { k + 1 } ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$
(2)

where

$$R _ { k } = \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { k } \right) , \quad T _ { k } = \left( s _ { k } , s _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , s _ { n } \right) .$$
(3)

Moreover, if $\lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 2 } = \cdots = \lambda _ { k } ,$ then

$$\frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \lambda _ { 1 } \quad \text { if and only if } \quad x = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha _ { i } s _ { i }$$
(4)

for some set of real numbers $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { k }$ not all zero. Similarly, if $\lambda _ { l } = \lambda _ { l + 1 } =$
$\cdots = \lambda _ { n } ,$ then

$$\frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \lambda _ { n } \quad \text { if and only if } \quad x = \sum _ { j = l } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } s _ { j }$$
(5)

for some set of real numbers $\alpha _ { l } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ not all zero.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="233" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] Fischer's min-max theorem" -->

Proof. Let us prove the first representation of $\lambda _ { k }$ in (2), the second being
proved in the same way.

As in the proof of Theorem 4, let $y = S ^ { \prime } x .$ Partitioning $S$ and $y$ as

$$S = \left( R _ { k - 1 } , T _ { k } \right) , \quad y = \left( \begin{array}{} y _ { 1 } \\ y _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(6)

we may express $x$ as

$$x = S y = R _ { k - 1 } y _ { 1 } + T _ { k } y _ { 2 } .$$
(7)

Hence,

$$R _ { k - 1 } { } ^ { \prime } x = 0 \Leftrightarrow y _ { 1 } = 0 \Leftrightarrow x = T _ { k } y _ { 2 } .$$
(8)

It follows that

$$\min _ { R _ { k - 1 } { } ^ { \prime } { } ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \min _ { x = T _ { k } y _ { 2 } } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \min _ { y _ { 2 } } \frac { y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \left( T _ { k } ^ { \prime } A T _ { k } \right) y _ { 2 } } { y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 2 } } = \lambda _ { k } ,$$
(9)

using Theorem 4 and the fact that $T _ { k } ^ { \prime } A T _ { k } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { k } , \lambda _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } \right) .$ The case
of equality is easily proved and is left to the reader.
✷
☐

Useful as the representations in (2) may be, there is one problem in using
them, namely that the representations are not quasilinear, because $R _ { k - 1 }$ and
$T _ { k + 1 }$ also depend on $A .$ A quasilinear representation of the eigenvalues was
first obtained by Fischer in 1905.


## 8 FISCHER'S MIN-MAX THEOREM

We shall obtain Fischer's result by using the following theorem, of interest in
itself.


### Theorem 7

Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$
Let $1 \leq k \leq n .$ Then,

$$\min _ { B ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \leq \lambda _ { k }$$
(1)

for every $n \times \left( k - 1 \right)$ matrix $B ,$ and

$$\max _ { C ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \geq \lambda _ { k }$$
(2)

for every $n \times \left( n - k \right)$ matrix $C .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="234" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

Proof. Let $B$ be an arbitrary $n \times \left( k - 1 \right)$ matrix, and denote (normalized)
eigenvectors associated with the eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ of $A$ by $s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { n } .$
Let $R = \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { k } \right) ,$ so that

$$R ^ { \prime } A R = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { k } \right) , \quad R ^ { \prime } R = I _ { k } . ,$$
(3)

Now consider the $\left( k - 1 \right) \times k$ matrix $B ^ { \prime } R .$ Since the rank of $B ^ { \prime } R$ cannot exceed
$k \quad - \quad 1 ,$ its $k$ columns are linearly dependent. Thus

$$B ^ { \prime } R p = 0$$
(4)

for some $k \times 1$ vector $p \neq 0 .$ Then, choosing $x = R p ,$ we obtain

$$\min _ { B ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \leq \frac { p ^ { \prime } \left( R ^ { \prime } A R \right) p } { p ^ { \prime } p } \leq \lambda _ { k } ,$$
(5)

using (3) and Theorem 4. This proves (1). The proof of $\left( 2 \right)$ is similar.
☐
✷

Let us now demonstrate Fischer's famous min-max theorem.


### Theorem 8 (Fischer)

Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$
Then $\lambda _ { k } \left( 1 \leq k \leq n \right)$ may be defined as

$$\lambda _ { k } = \max _ { B ^ { \prime } B = I _ { k - 1 } } \min _ { B ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$
(6)

or equivalently as

$$\lambda _ { k } = \min _ { C ^ { \prime } = I _ { n - k } } \max _ { C ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$
(7)

where, as the notation indicates, $B$ is an $n \times \left( k - 1 \right)$ matrix and $C$ is an
$n \times \left( n - k \right)$ matrix.

$P r o o f .$ Again we shall prove only the first representation, leaving the proof of
(7) as an exercise for the reader.

As in the proof of Theorem $6 ,$ let $R _ { k - 1 }$ be a semi-orthogonal $n \times \left( k - 1 \right)$
matrix satisfying

$$A R _ { k - 1 } = R _ { k - 1 } \Lambda _ { k - 1 } , \quad R _ { k - 1 } ^ { \prime } R _ { k - 1 } = I _ { k - 1 } ,$$
(8)

where

$$\Lambda _ { k - 1 } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { k - 1 } \right) .$$
(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="235" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Monotonicity of the eigenvalues" -->

Then, defining

$$\phi \left( B \right) = \min _ { B ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$
(10)

we obtain

$$\lambda _ { k } = \phi \left( R _ { k - 1 } \right) = \max _ { B = R _ { k - 1 } } \phi \left( B \right) \leq \max _ { B ^ { \prime } B = I _ { k - 1 } } \phi \left( B \right) \leq \lambda _ { k } ,$$
(11)

where the first equality follows from Theorem 6, and the last inequality from
Theorem 7. Hence,

$$\lambda _ { k } = \max _ { B ^ { \prime } B = I _ { k - 1 } } \phi \left( B \right) = \max _ { B ^ { \prime } B = I _ { k - 1 } } \min _ { B ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$
(12)

thus completing the proof.

✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. Let $A$ be a square $n \times n$ matrix (not necessarily symmetric). Show that
for every $n \times 1$ vector $x$

$$\left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \left( x ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } x \right) \left( x ^ { \prime } x \right)$$

and hence

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } | \frac { x ^ { \prime } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) x } { x ^ { \prime } x } | \leq \left( \frac { x ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$

2\. Use Exercise 1 and Theorems 6 and 7 to prove that

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } | \lambda _ { k } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) | \leq \left( \lambda _ { k } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \quad \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$$

for every $n \times n$ matrix $A .$ (This was first proved by Fan and Hoffman
(1955). Related inequalities are given in Amir-Moéz and Fass (1962).)


## 9 MONOTONICITY OF THE EIGENVALUES

The usefulness of the quasilinear representation of the eigenvalues in Theorem
8, as opposed to the representation in Theorem 6, is clearly brought out in
the proof of Theorem 9.


## Theorem 9

For any symmetric matrix $A$ and positive semidefinite matrix $B ,$

$$\lambda _ { k } \left( A + B \right) \geq \lambda _ { k } \left( A \right) \quad \left( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(1)

If $B$ is positive definite, then the inequality is strict.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="236" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

Proof. For any $n \times \left( k - 1 \right)$ matrix $P$ we have

$$\min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } \left( A + B \right) x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \left( \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } + \frac { x ^ { \prime } B x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \right)$$
$$\geq \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } + \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } B x } { x ^ { \prime } x }$$
$$\geq \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } + \min _ { x } \frac { x ^ { \prime } B x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \geq \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } ,$$

and hence, by Theorem 8,

$$\lambda _ { k } \left( A + B \right) = \max _ { P ^ { \prime } = I _ { k - 1 } } \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } \left( A + B \right) x } { x ^ { \prime } x }$$
$$\geq \max _ { P ^ { \prime } = I _ { k - 1 } } \min _ { P ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \lambda _ { k } \left( A \right) .$$

If $B$ is positive definite, the last inequality in (2) is strict, and so the inequality
in (3) is also strict.
☐
✷


### Exercises

1\. Prove Theorem 9 by means of the representation (8.7) rather than (8.6).

2\. Show how an application of Theorem 6 fails to prove Theorem 9.


## 10 THE POINCARÉ SEPARATION THEOREM

In Section 8 we employed Theorems $6$ and 7 to prove Fischer's min-max
theorem. Let us now demonstrate another consequence of Theorems 6 and 7:
Poincaré's separation theorem.


### Theorem 10 (Poincaré)

Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$
and let $G$ be a semi-orthogonal $n \times k$ matrix $\left( 1 \leq k \leq n \right) ,$ so that $G ^ { \prime } G = I _ { k } .$
Then the eigenvalues $\mu _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \mu _ { k }$ of $G ^ { \prime } A G$ satisfy

$$\lambda _ { i } \leq \mu _ { i } \leq \lambda _ { n - k + i } \quad \left( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k \right) .$$
(1)

Note. For $k = 1 ,$ Theorem 10 reduces to Theorem 4. For $k = n ,$ we obtain the
well-known result that the symmetric matrices $A$ and $G ^ { \prime } A G$ have the same
set of eigenvalues, if $G$ is orthogonal (see Theorem 1.5).

Proof. Let $1 < i > k$ and let $R$ be a semi-orthogonal $n \times \left( i - 1 \right)$ matrix whose

(2)

(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="237" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] Two corollaries of Poincaré's theorem" -->

columns are eigenvectors of $A$ associated with $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { i - 1 } .$ Then,

$$\lambda _ { i } = \min _ { R ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \leq \min _ { \begin{array}{} R ^ { \prime } x = 0 \\ x = G y \end{array} } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \min _ { R ^ { \prime } G y = 0 } \frac { y ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } A G y } { y ^ { \prime } y } \leq \mu _ { i } ,$$
(2)

using Theorems 6 and 7.

Next, let $n - k + 1 \leq j \leq n ,$ and let $T$ be a semi-orthogonal $n \times \left( n - j \right)$
matrix whose columns are eigenvectors of $A$ associated with $\lambda _ { j + 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } .$
Then we obtain in the same way

$$\lambda _ { j } = \max _ { T ^ { \prime } x = 0 } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \geq \max _ { \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } x = 0 \\ x = G y \end{array} } \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } = \max _ { T ^ { \prime } G y = 0 } \frac { y ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } A G y } { y ^ { \prime } y } \geq \mu _ { k - n + j } .$$
(3)

Choosing $j = n - k + i \left( 1 \leq i \leq k \right)$ in (3) thus yields $\mu _ { i } \leq \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$
✷
☐


## 11 TWO COROLLARIES OF POINCARÉ'S THEOREM

The Poincaré theorem is of such fundamental importance that we shall present
a number of special cases in this and the next two sections. The first of these
is not merely a special case, but an equivalent formulation of the same result:
see Exercise 2.


### Theorem 11

Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$
and let $M$ be an idempotent symmetric $n \times n$ matrix of rank $k \left( 1 \leq k \leq n \right) .$
Denoting the eigenvalues of the $n \times n$ matrix $M A M ,$ apart from $n \quad - \quad k$ zeros,
by $\mu _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \mu _ { k } ,$ we have

$$\lambda _ { i } \leq \mu _ { i } \leq \lambda _ { n - k + i } \quad \left( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k \right) .$$

Proof. Immediate from Theorem 10 by writing $M = G G ^ { \prime } ,$ $G ^ { \prime } G = I _ { k }$ (see
(1.17.13)), and noting that $G G ^ { \prime } A G G ^ { \prime }$ and $G ^ { \prime } A G$ have the same eigenvalues,
apart from $n \quad - \quad k$ zeros.
✷
☐

Another special case of Theorem 10 is Theorem 12.


### Theorem 12

If $A$ is a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix and $A _ { k }$ is a $k \times k$ principal submatrix
of $A ,$ then

$$\lambda _ { i } \left( A \right) \leq \lambda _ { i } \left( A _ { k } \right) \leq \lambda _ { n - k + i } \left( A \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , k \right) .$$

Proof. Let $G$ be the $n \times k$ matrix

$$G = \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { k } \\ 0 \end{array} \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="238" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

or a row permutation thereof. Then $G ^ { \prime } G = I _ { k }$ and $G ^ { \prime } A G$ is a $k \times k$ principal
submatrix of $A .$ The result now follows from Theorem 10.
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq$
$\cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$ and let $B$ be the real symmetric $\left( n + 1 \right) \times \left( n + 1 \right)$ matrix

$$B = \left( \begin{array}{} A & b \\ b ^ { \prime } & \alpha \end{array} \right) .$$

Then the eigenvalues $\mu _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \mu _ { n + 1 }$ of $B$ satisfy

$$\mu _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } \leq \mu _ { n + 1 } .$$

[Hint: Use Theorem 12.]

2\. Obtain Theorem 10 as a special case of Theorem 11.


## 12 FURTHER CONSEQUENCES OF THE POINCARÉ THEOREM

An immediate consequence of Poincaré's inequality (Theorem 10) is the fol-
lowing theorem.


### Theorem 13

For any real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $A$ with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$

$$\min _ { X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { k } } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } A X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } ,$$
$$\max _ { X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { k } } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } A X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$$
(1)
(2)

Proof. Denoting the $k$ eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } A X$ by $\mu _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \mu _ { k } ,$ we have
from Theorem 10,

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \mu _ { i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$$
(3)

Noting that $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \mu _ { i } = \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } A X ,$ and that the bounds in (3) can be attained
by suitable choices of $X ,$ the result follows.
✷
☐

An important special case of Theorem 13, which we shall use in Section
17, is the following.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="239" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Multiplicative version" -->


### Theorem 14

Let $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq$
$\cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$ Then,

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \leq a _ { i i } \leq \lambda _ { n } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
$$\lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } \leq a _ { i i } + a _ { j j } \leq \lambda _ { n - 1 } + \lambda _ { n }$$
(4)
(i + j = 1, ., n),
(5)

and so on. In particular, for $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ,$

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } a _ { i i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(6)

Proof. Theorem 13 implies that the inequality

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } A X \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i }$$ ☒ ☒
(7)

is valid for every $n \times k$ matrix $X$ satisfying $X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { k } .$ Taking $X = \left( I _ { k } , 0 \right) ^ { \prime }$ or
a row permutation thereof, the result follows.
✷
☐


### Exercise

1\. Prove Theorem 13 directly from Theorem 6 without using Poincaré's
theorem. [Hint: Write tr $X ^ { \prime } A X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } A x _ { i }$ where $X = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right)$


## 13 MULTIPLICATIVE VERSION

Let us now obtain the multiplicative versions of Theorems 13 and 14 for the
positive definite case.


## Theorem 15

For any positive definite $n \times n$ matrix $A$ with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$

$$\min _ { X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { k } } | X ^ { \prime } A X | = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } ,$$
(1)

$$\max _ { X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { k } } | X ^ { \prime } A X | = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="240" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

Proof. As in the proof of Theorem 13, let $\mu _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \mu _ { k }$ be the eigen-
values of $X ^ { \prime } A X .$ Then Theorem 10 implies

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \mu _ { i } \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$$
(3)

Since

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \mu _ { i } = | X ^ { \prime } A X | ,$$
(4)

and the bounds in (3) can be attained by suitable choices of $X ,$ the result
✷
☐
follows.


### Theorem 16

Let $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq$
$\cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$ and define

$$A _ { k } = \left( \begin{array}{} a _ { 1 1 } & \cdots & a _ { 1 k } \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ a _ { k 1 } & \cdots & a _ { k k } \end{array} \right) \quad \left( k = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(5)

Then, for $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ,$

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } \leq | A _ { k } | \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i } .$$
(6)

Proof. Theorem 15 implies that the inequality

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { i } \leq | X ^ { \prime } A X | \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { n - k + i }$$
(7)

is valid for every $n \times k$ matrix $X$ satisfying $X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { k } .$ Taking $X = \left( I _ { k } , 0 \right) ^ { \prime } ,$
✷
☐

the result follows.


### Exercises

1\. Prove Theorem 16 using Theorem 12 rather than Theorem 15.

2\. Use Theorem 16 to show that a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is positive
definite if and only if $| A _ { k } | > 0 ,$ $k = 1 , \ldots , n .$ This gives an alternative
proof of Theorem 1.29.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="241" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] The maximum of a bilinear form" -->


## 14 THE MAXIMUM OF $\mathrm { A }$ BILINEAR FORM

Theorem 6 together with the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality allows a generaliza-
tion from quadratic to bilinear forms.


### Theorem 17

Let $A$ be a $m \times n$ matrix with rank $r \geq 1 .$ Let $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { r }$ denote
the positive eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime }$ and let $S = \left( s _ { 1 } , \ldots , s _ { r } \right)$ be a semi-orthogonal
$m \times r$ matrix such that

$$A A ^ { \prime } S = S \Lambda , \quad S ^ { \prime } S = I _ { r } , \quad \Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { r } \right) .$$
(1)

Then, for $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , r ,$

$$\left( x ^ { \prime } A y \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \lambda _ { k }$$
(2)

for every $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ satisfying

$$x ^ { \prime } x = 1 , \quad y ^ { \prime } y = 1 , \quad s _ { i } ^ { \prime } x = 0 \quad \left( i = k + 1 , \ldots , r \right) .$$
(3)

Moreover, if $\lambda _ { j } = \lambda _ { j + 1 } = \cdots = \lambda _ { k } ,$ and either $\lambda _ { j - 1 } < \lambda _ { j }$ or $j = 1 ,$ then
equality in (2) occurs $\mathrm { i f }$ and only if $x = x _ { * } ,$ and $y = y _ { * } ,$ where

$$x _ { * } = \sum _ { i = j } ^ { k } \alpha _ { i } s _ { i } , \quad y _ { * } = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { - 1 / 2 } A ^ { \prime } x _ { * }$$
(4)

for some set of real numbers $\alpha _ { j } , \ldots , \alpha _ { k }$ satisfying $\sum _ { i = j } ^ { k } \alpha _ { i } ^ { 2 } = 1 .$ (If $\lambda _ { k }$ is a
simple eigenvalue of $A A ^ { \prime } ,$ then $j = k$ and $x _ { * }$ and $y _ { * }$ are unique, apart from
sign.) Moreover, $y _ { * }$ is an eigenvector of $A ^ { \prime } A$ associated with the eigenvalue
$\lambda _ { k } ,$ and

$$x _ { * } = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { - 1 / 2 } A y _ { * } , \quad s _ { i } ^ { \prime } A y _ { * } = 0 \quad \left( i = k + 1 , \ldots , r \right) .$$
(5)

Proof. Let $x$ and $y$ be arbitrary vectors in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m }$ and $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ respectively, satisfying
(3). Then

$$\left( x ^ { \prime } A y \right) ^ { 2 } \leq x ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } x \leq \lambda _ { k } ,$$
(6)

where the first inequality in Cauchy-Schwarz and the second follows from
Theorem 6.

Equality occurs if and only if $y = \gamma A ^ { \prime } x$ for some $\gamma \neq 0$ (to make the first
inequality of (6) into an equality) and

$$x = \sum _ { i = j } ^ { k } \alpha _ { i } s _ { i }$$
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="242" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

for some $\alpha _ { j } , \ldots , \alpha _ { k }$ satisfying $\sum _ { i = j } ^ { k } \alpha _ { i } ^ { 2 } = 1$ (because of the requirement that
$x ^ { \prime } x = 1$ 1). From (7) it follows that $A A ^ { \prime } x = \lambda _ { k } x ,$ so that

$$1 = y ^ { \prime } y = \gamma ^ { 2 } x ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } x = \gamma ^ { 2 } \lambda _ { k } .$$
(8)

Hence $\gamma = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and $y = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { - 1 / 2 } A ^ { \prime } x .$

Furthermore

$$A y = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { - 1 / 2 } A A ^ { \prime } x = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \lambda _ { k } x = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } x ,$$
(9)

implying

$$A ^ { \prime } A y = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } A ^ { \prime } x = \lambda _ { k } y ,$$
(10)

and $a l s o$

$$s _ { i } ^ { \prime } A y = \pm \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } s _ { i } ^ { \prime } x = 0 \quad \left( i = k + 1 , \ldots , r \right) .$$
(11)

This concludes the proof.

✷
☐


## 15 HADAMARD'S INEQUALITY

The following inequality is a very famous one, and is due to Hadamard.


### Theorem 18 (Hadamard)

For any real $n \times n$ matrix $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right) ,$

$$| A | ^ { 2 } \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \right)$$
(1)

with equality if and only if $A A ^ { \prime }$ is a diagonal matrix or $A$ contains a row of
zeros.

Proof. Assume that $A$ is non-singular. Then $A A ^ { \prime }$ is positive definite and hence,
by Theorem 1.28,

$$| A | ^ { 2 } = | A A ^ { \prime } | \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) _ { i i } = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \right)$$
(2)

with equality if and only if $A A ^ { \prime }$ is diagonal. If $A$ is singular, the inequality is
trivial, and equality occurs if and only if $\sum _ { j } a _ { i j } ^ { 2 } = 0$ for some i, that is, if and
only if $A$ contains a row of zeros.
✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="243" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 16 ] An interlude: Karamata's inequality" -->


## 16 AN INTERLUDE: KARAMATA'S INEQUALITY

Let $x = \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ and $y = \left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ be two $n \times 1$ vectors. We
say that $y$ is majorized by $x$ and write

$$\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) \prec \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ,$$
(1)

when the following three conditions are satisfied:

$$x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { n } = y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } + \cdots + y _ { n } ,$$
$$x _ { 1 } \leq x _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq x _ { n } , \quad y _ { 1 } \leq y _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq y _ { n } ,$$
$$x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { k } \leq y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } + \cdots + y _ { k } \quad \left( 1 \leq k \leq n - 1 \right) .$$
(2)
(3)
(4)


### Theorem 19 (Karamata)

Let $\phi$ be a real-valued convex function defined on an interval $S$ in $\mathbb{R} .$ If
$\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) \prec \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ,$ then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) \geq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right) .$$
(5)

If, in addition, $\phi$ is strictly convex on $S ,$ then equality in (5) occurs if and
only if $x _ { i } = y _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$

Proof. The first part of the theorem is a well-known result (see Hardy, Little-
wood and Pólya 1952, Beckenbach and Bellman 1961). Let us prove the sec-
ond part, which investigates when equality in (5) can occur. Clearly, if $x _ { i } = y _ { i }$
for all i, then $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right) .$ To prove the converse, assume that
$\phi$ is strictly convex. We must then demonstrate the truth of the following
statement: if $\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) \prec \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right) ,$ then
$x _ { i } = y _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$

Let us proceed by induction. For $n = 1$ the statement is trivially true.
Assume the statement to be true for $n = 1 , 2 , \ldots , N - 1 .$ Assume also that
$\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { N } \right) \prec \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { N } \right)$ and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right) .$ We shall show that
$x _ { i } = y _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , N \right) .$

Assume first that $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { i } < \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } y _ { i }$ (strict inequality) for $k = 1 , \ldots , N -$
1\. Replace $y _ { i }$ by $z _ { i }$ where

$$z _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } - \epsilon , \quad z _ { i } = y _ { i } \left( i = 2 , \ldots , N - 1 \right) , \quad z _ { N } = y _ { N } + \epsilon .$$
(6)

Then, as we can choose $\epsilon > 0$ arbitrarily small, $\left( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { N } \right) \prec \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { N } \right) .$
Hence,

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) \geq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( z _ { i } \right) .$$
(7)

<!-- PageNumber="244" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

ǫ

<

$\frac { \phi \left( y _ { 1 } \right) - \phi \left( y _ { 1 } - \epsilon \right) } { \epsilon } < \frac { \phi \left( y _ { N } + \epsilon \right) - \phi \left( y _ { N } \right) } { \epsilon }$

ǫ

On the other hand,

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( z _ { i } \right) + \epsilon \left( \frac { \phi \left( y _ { 1 } \right) - \phi \left( y _ { 1 } - \epsilon \right) } { \epsilon } - \frac { \phi \left( y _ { N } + \epsilon \right) - \phi \left( y _ { N } \right) } { \epsilon } \right)$$
$$< \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( z _ { i } \right) ,$$

(8)

which contradicts (7). (See Figure 1 to see how the inequality in (8) is ob-
tained.)

Next assume that $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } x _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } y _ { i }$ for some $m \left( 1 \leq m \leq N - 1 \right) .$ Then
$\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { m } \right) \prec \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , \check { x _ { m } } \right)$ and $\left. \bar { \left( y _ { m + 1 } \right. } , \ldots , y _ { N } \right) \prec \left( x _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { N } \right) .$ The first
part of the theorem implies

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) \geq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right) , \quad \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) \geq \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right) ,$$
(9)

and since $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \phi \left( y _ { i } \right)$ by assumption, it follows that the $\geq \text { signs }$
in (9) can be replaced by $= \mathrm { s i g n s } .$ The induction hypothesis applied to the
sets $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m } \right)$ and $\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { m } \right)$ then yields $x _ { i } = y _ { i }$ $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$ the
induction hypothesis applied to the sets $\left( x _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { N } \right)$ and $\left( y _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , y _ { N } \right)$
✷
yields $x _ { i } = y _ { i } \left( i = m + 1 , \ldots , N \right) .$
☐

## 17 KARAMATA'S INEQUALITY APPLIED TO EIGENVALUES

An important application of Karamata's inequality is the next result, which
provides the basis of the analysis in the next few sections.


### Theorem 20

Let $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } .$
Then for any convex function $\phi$ we have

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( \lambda _ { i } \right) \geq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( a _ { i i } \right) .$$
(1)

Moreover, if $\phi$ is strictly convex, then equality in (1) occurs if and only if $A$
is diagonal.

Proof. Without loss of generality we may assume that

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } , \quad a _ { 1 1 } \leq a _ { 2 2 } \leq \cdots \leq a _ { n n } .$$
(2)

Theorem 14 implies that $\left( a _ { 1 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n n } \right)$ is majorized by $\left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } \right)$ in the
sense of Section 16. Karamata's inequality (Theorem 19) then yields (1). For
strictly convex $\phi ,$ Theorem 19 implies that equality in (1) holds if and only if
$\lambda _ { i } = a _ { i i }$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ and by Theorem 1.30, this is the case if and only if
✷
☐
$A$ is diagonal.

1\. Prove Theorem 1.28 as a special case of Theorem 20. [Hint: Choose
$\phi \left( x \right) = - \log x , x > 0 .$


## 18 AN INEQUALITY CONCERNING POSITIVE SEMIDEFI- NITE MATRICES

If $A$ is positive semidefinite then, by Theorem 1.13, we can write $A = S \Lambda S ^ { \prime } ,$
where $\Lambda$ is diagonal with non-negative diagonal elements. We now define the
p-th power of $A$ as $A ^ { p } = S \Lambda ^ { p } S ^ { \prime } .$ In particular, $A ^ { 1 / 2 }$ (for positive semidefinite
$\left. A \right)$ is the unique positive semidefinite matrix $S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } .$


### Theorem 21

Let $A = \left( a _ { i j } \right)$ be a positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix. Then

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \geq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i i } ^ { p } \quad \left( p > 1 \right)$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="246" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

and

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i i } ^ { p } \quad \left( 0 < p < 1 \right)$$
(2)

with equality if and only if $A \quad i s \quad d i a g o n a l .$

Proof. Let $p > 1$ and define $\phi \left( x \right) = x ^ { p }$ $\left( x \geq 0 \right.$ The function $\phi$ is continuous
and strictly convex. Hence Theorem 20 implies that

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { p } \left( A \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( \lambda _ { i } \left( A \right) \right) \geq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi \left( a _ { i i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i i } ^ { p }$$
(3)

with equality if and only if $A$ is diagonal. Next, let $0 < p < 1 ,$ define $\psi \left( x \right) =$
$- x ^ { p } \left( x \geq 0 \right) ,$ and proceed in the same way to make the proof complete.

✷


## 19 A REPRESENTATION THEOREM FOR $\left( \sum a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$

As a preliminary to Theorem 23, let us prove Theorem 22.


### Theorem 22

Let $p > 1 ,$ $q = p / \left( p - 1 \right)$ and $a _ { i } \geq 0 \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$ Then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } x _ { i } \leq \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(1)

for every set of non-negative numbers $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ satisfying $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { q } = 1 .$
Equality in (1) occurs if and only if $a _ { 1 } = a _ { 2 } = \cdots = a _ { n } = 0$ or

$$x _ { i } ^ { q } = a _ { i } ^ { p } / \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { j } ^ { p } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(2)

Note. We call this theorem a representation theorem because (1) can be al-
ternatively written as

$$\max _ { x \in S } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } x _ { i } = \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(3)

where

$$S = \left\{ x = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) : x _ { i } \geq 0 , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { q } = 1 \right\} .$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="247" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 19 ] A representation theorem for $\left( \sum a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$" -->

Proof. Let us consider the maximization problem

maximize
subject to

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } x _ { i }$$
(5)

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { q } = 1 .$$
(6)

We form the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } x _ { i } - \lambda q ^ { - 1 } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { q } - 1 \right) ,$$
(7)

and differentiate. This yields

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( a _ { i } - \lambda x _ { i } ^ { q - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } x _ { i } .$$
(8)

From (8) we obtain the first-order conditions

$$\lambda x _ { i } ^ { q - 1 } = a _ { i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(9)

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { q } = 1 .$$
(10)

Solving for $x _ { i }$ and $\lambda ,$ we obtain

$$x _ { i } = \left( a _ { i } ^ { p } / \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / q } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
$$\lambda = \left( \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } .$$
(11)
(12)

Since $q > 1 ,$ $\psi \left( x \right)$ is concave; it follows from Theorem 7.13 that $\sum a _ { i } x _ { i }$ has
an absolute maximum under the constraint (6) at every point where (11) is
satisfied. The constrained maximum is

$$\sum _ { i } a _ { i } \left( a _ { i } ^ { p } / \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / q } = \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ^ { p } / \left( \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / q } = \left( \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / i }$$
(13)
.

This completes the proof.

✷
