## 20 A REPRESENTATION THEOREM FOR $\left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$

An important generalization of Theorem 22, which provides the basis for prov-
ing matrix analogues of the fundamental inequalities of Hölder and Minkowski
(Theorems 24 and 26), is given in Theorem 23.


### Theorem 23

Let $p > 1 ,$ $q = p / \left( p - 1 \right)$ and let $A \neq 0$ be a positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix.
Then

$$\mathrm { t r } A X \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(1)

for every positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix $X$ satisfying tr $X ^ { q } = 1$ 1. Equality
in (1) occurs if and only if

$$X ^ { q } = \left( 1 / \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) A ^ { p } .$$
(2)

Proof. Let $X$ be an arbitrary positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix satisfying
tr $X ^ { q } = 1 .$ Let $S$ be an orthogonal matrix such that $S ^ { \prime } X S = \Lambda ,$ where $\Lambda$
is diagonal and has the eigenvalues of $X$ as its diagonal elements. Define
$B = \left( b _ { i j } \right) = S ^ { \prime } A S .$ Then

$$\mathrm { t r } A X = \mathrm { t r } B \Lambda = \sum _ { i } b _ { i i } \lambda _ { i }$$
(3)

and

$$\mathrm { t r } X ^ { q } = \mathrm { t r } \Lambda ^ { q } = \sum _ { i } \lambda _ { i } ^ { q } .$$
(4)

Hence, by Theorem 22,

$$\mathrm { t r } A X \leq \left( \sum _ { i } b _ { i i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } .$$
(5)

Since $A$ is positive semidefinite, so is $B ,$ and Theorem 21 thus implies that

$$\sum _ { i } b _ { i i } ^ { p } \leq \mathrm { t r } B ^ { p } .$$
(6)

Combining (5) and (6) we obtain

$$\mathrm { t r } A X \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } B ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } = \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } .$$
(7)

Equality in (5) occurs if and only if

$$\lambda _ { i } ^ { q } = b _ { i i } ^ { p } / \sum _ { i } b _ { i i } ^ { p } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="249" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 21 ] Hölder's inequality" -->

and equality in (6) occurs if and only if $B$ is diagonal. Hence, equality in (1)
occurs if and only if

$$\Lambda ^ { q } = B ^ { p } / \mathrm { t r } B ^ { p } ,$$
(9)

✷
☐
which is equivalent to (2).


## 21 HOLDER'S INEQUALITY

In its simplest form Hölder's inequality asserts that

$$x _ { 1 } ^ { \alpha } y _ { 1 } ^ { 1 - \alpha } + x _ { 2 } ^ { \alpha } y _ { 2 } ^ { 1 - \alpha } \leq \left( x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } \right) ^ { \alpha } \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 - \alpha }$$
(0 < a <1)
(1)

for every non-negative $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } .$ This inequality can be extended in two
directions. We can show (by simple mathematical induction) that

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha } y _ { i } ^ { 1 - \alpha } \leq \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } x _ { i } \right) ^ { \alpha } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } y _ { i } \right) ^ { 1 - \alpha } \quad \left( 0 < \alpha < 1 \right)$$
(2)

for every $x _ { i } \geq 0 ,$ $y _ { i } \geq 0$ and $a l s o ,$ arranging the induction differently, that

$$\prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } } + \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } } \leq \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { j } + y _ { j } \right) ^ { \alpha _ { j } }$$
(3)

for every $x _ { j } \geq 0 ,$ $y _ { j } \geq 0 ,$ $\alpha _ { j } > 0$ 0, $\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } = 1 .$

Combining (2) and (3), we obtain the following result.


### Hölder's inequality

Let $X = \left( x _ { i j } \right)$ be a non-negative $m \times n$ matrix (that is, a matrix all of whose
elements are non-negative), and let $\alpha _ { j } > 0$ $\left( j = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$ $\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } = 1 .$ Then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } } \leq \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } x _ { i j } \right) ^ { \alpha _ { j } }$$
(4)

with equality if and only if either $r \left( X \right) = 1$ or one of the columns of $X$ is the
null vector.

In this section we want to show how Theorem 23 can be used to obtain
the matrix analogue of (2).


### Theorem 24

For any two positive semidefinite matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order, $A \neq$
0, $B \neq 0 ,$ and $0 < \alpha < 1 ,$ we have

$$A ^ { \alpha } B ^ { 1 - \alpha } \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } A \right) ^ { \alpha } \left( \mathrm { t r } B \right) ^ { 1 - \alpha } ,$$
tr
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="250" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

with equality if and only if $B = \mu A$ for some scalar $\mu > 0 .$

$\mathrm { P r o o f } .$ Let $p = 1 / \alpha ,$ $q = 1 / \left( 1 - \alpha \right)$ and assume $B \neq 0 .$ Now define

$$X = \frac { B ^ { 1 / q } } { \left( \mathrm { t r } B \right) ^ { 1 / q } } .$$
(6)

Then tr $X ^ { q } = 1 ,$ and hence Theorem 23 applied to $A ^ { 1 / p }$ yields

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { 1 / p } B ^ { 1 / q } \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } A \right) ^ { 1 / p } \left( \mathrm { t r } B \right) ^ { 1 / q } ,$$
(7)

which is (5). According to Theorem 23, equality in (5) can occur only if
$X ^ { q } = \left( 1 / \mathrm { t r } A \right) A ,$ that is, if $B = \mu A$ for some $\mu > 0 .$
☐
✷


### Exercises

1\. Let $A$ and $B$ be positive semidefinite and $0 < \alpha < 1 .$ Define the sym-
metric matrix

$$C = \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B - A ^ { \alpha / 2 } B ^ { 1 - \alpha } A ^ { \alpha / 2 } .$$

Show that $\mathrm { t r } C \geq 0$ with equality if and only if $A = B .$

2\. For every $x > 0 ,$

$$x ^ { \alpha } \leq \alpha x + 1 - \alpha \quad \left( 0 < \alpha < 1 \right)$$
$$x ^ { \alpha } \geq \alpha x + 1 - \alpha \quad \left( \alpha > 1 \mathrm { o r } \alpha < 0 \right)$$

with equality if and only if $x = 1 .$

3\. If $A$ and $B$ are positive semidefinite and commute (that is, $\left. A B = B A \right) ,$
then the matrix $C$ of Exercise 1 is positive semidefinite. Moreover, $C$ is
non-singular (hence positive definite) if and only if $A - \quad B$ is non-singular.

4\. Let $p > 1$ and $q = p / \left( p - 1 \right) .$ Show that for any two positive semidefinite
matrices $A \neq 0$ and $B \neq 0$ of the same order,

$$\mathrm { t r } A B \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } \left( \mathrm { t r } B ^ { q } \right) ^ { 1 / q }$$

with equality if and only if $B = \mu A ^ { p - 1 }$ for some $\mu > 0 .$


## 22 CONCAVITY OF $\log | A |$

In Exercise 1 of the previous section we saw that

tr $A ^ { \alpha } B ^ { 1 - \alpha } \leq \mathrm { t r } \left( \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B \right) \quad \left( 0 < \alpha < 1 \right)$
(1)

for any pair of positive semidefinite matrices $A$ and $B .$ Let us now demonstrate
the multiplicative analogue of (1).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="251" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 22 ] Concavity of $\log | A |$" -->


### Theorem 25

For any two positive semidefinite matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order and
$0 < \alpha < 1 ,$ we have

$$| A | ^ { \alpha } | B | ^ { 1 - \alpha } \leq | \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B |$$
(2)

with equality if and only if $A = B$ or $| \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B | = 0 .$

Proof. If either $A$ or $B$ is singular, the result is obvious. Assume therefore that
$A$ and $B$ are both positive definite. Applying Exercise 2 of Section 21 to the
eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ of the positive definite matrix $B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ yields

$$\lambda _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \leq \alpha \lambda _ { i } + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(3)

and hence, multiplying both sides of (3) over $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$

$$| B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 } | ^ { \alpha } \leq | \alpha B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 } + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) I | .$$
(4)

From (4) we obtain

$$| A | ^ { \alpha } | B | ^ { 1 - \alpha } = | B | | B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 } | ^ { \alpha } \leq | B | | \alpha B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 } + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) I |$$

$$= | B ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( \alpha B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 } + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) I \right) B ^ { 1 / 2 } | = | \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B | .$$
(5)

There is equality in (5) if and only if there is equality in (3), which occurs if
and only if every eigenvalue of $B ^ { - 1 / 2 } A B ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ equals one, that is, if and only
if $A = B .$
✷
☐

Another way of expressing the result of Theorem 25 is to say that the real-
valued function $\phi$ defined by $\phi \left( A \right) = \log | A |$ is concave on the set of positive
definite matrices. This is seen by taking logarithms on both sides of (2). We
note, however, that the function $\psi$ given by $\psi \left( A \right) = | A |$ is neither convex nor
concave on the set of positive definite matrices. This is easily seen by taking

$$A = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right) , \quad B = \left( \begin{array}{} 1 + \delta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 + \epsilon \end{array} \right) \quad \left( \delta > - 1 , \epsilon > - 1 \right) .$$
(6)

Then, for $\alpha = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$

$$\alpha | A | + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) | B | - | \alpha A + \left( 1 - \alpha \right) B | = \delta \epsilon / 4 ,$$
(7)

which can take positive or negative values depending on whether $\delta$ and $\epsilon$ have
the same or opposite signs.


### Exercises

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="252" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

1\. Show that, for $A$ positive definite

$$d ^ { 2 } \log | A | = - \mathrm { t r } A ^ { - 1 } \left( d A \right) A ^ { - 1 } \left( d A \right) < 0$$

for all $\mathrm { d } A \neq 0 .$ (Compare Theorem 25.)

2\. Show that the $m a t r i x$ inverse is 'matrix convex' on the set of positive
definite matrices. That is, show that the matrix

$$C \left( \lambda \right) = \lambda A ^ { - 1 } + \left( 1 - \lambda \right) B ^ { - 1 } - \left( \lambda A + \left( 1 - \lambda \right) B \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

is positive semidefinite for all positive definite $A$ and $B$ and $0 < \lambda < 1$
(Moore 1973).

3\. Furthermore, show that $C \left( \lambda \right)$ is positive definite for all $\lambda \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ if and
only if $| A - B | \neq 0$ (Moore 1973).

4\. Show that

$$x ^ { \prime } \left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } x \leq \frac { \left( x ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( x ^ { \prime } B ^ { - 1 } x \right) } { x ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { - 1 } + B ^ { - 1 } \right) x } \leq \frac { x ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } x + x ^ { \prime } B ^ { - 1 } x } { 4 }$$

[Hint: Use Exercise 2 and Bergstrom's inequality, $S e c t i o n$ 11.2.]


## 23 MINKOWSKI'S INEQUALITY

Minkowski's inequality, in its most rudimentary form, states that

$$\left( \left( x _ { 1 } + y _ { 1 } \right) ^ { p } + \left( x _ { 2 } + y _ { 2 } \right) ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } \leq \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { p } + x _ { 2 } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } + \left( y _ { 1 } ^ { p } + y _ { 2 } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(1)

for every non-negative $x _ { 1 } ,$ $x _ { 2 } ,$ $y _ { 1 } ,$ $y _ { 2 }$ and $p > 1 .$ As in Holder's inequality, (1)
can be extended in two directions. We have

$$\left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( x _ { i } + y _ { i } \right) ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } \leq \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } + \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } y _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(2)

for every $x _ { i } \geq 0 ,$ $y _ { i } \geq 0$ and $p > 1 ;$ and also

$$\left[ \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } \right) ^ { p } + \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { j } \right) ^ { p } \right] ^ { 1 / p } \leq \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { j } ^ { p } + y _ { j } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(3)

for every $x _ { j } \geq 0 ,$ $y _ { j } \geq 0$ and $p > 1 .$ Notice that if in (3) we replace $x _ { j }$ by
$x _ { j } ^ { 1 / p } ,$ $y _ { j }$ by $y _ { i } ^ { 1 / p }$
and $p$ by $1 / p ,$ we obtain

$$\left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { j } + y _ { j } \right) ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } \geq \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } + \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { j } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="253" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 23 ] Minkowski's inequality" -->

for every $x _ { j } \geq 0 ,$ $y _ { j } \geq 0$ and $0 < p < 1 .$

All these cases are contained in the following inequality.


## Minkowski's inequality

Let $X = \left( x _ { i j } \right)$ be a non-negative $m \times n$ matrix (that is, $x _ { i j } \geq 0$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , m$
and $\left. j = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and let $p > 1 .$ Then

$$\left[ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i j } \right) ^ { p } \right] ^ { 1 / p } \leq \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } x _ { i j } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(5)

with equality if and only if $r \left( X \right) = 1 .$
☒

Let us now obtain, again by using Theorem 23, the matrix analogue of
(2).


## Theorem 26

For any two positive semidefinite matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order $\left( A \neq \right.$
0, $B \neq 0 ,$ and $p > 1 ,$ we have

$$\left( \mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } + \left( \mathrm { t r } B ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(6)

with equality if and only if $A = \mu B$ for some $\mu > 0 .$

$P r o o f .$ Let $p > 1 ,$ $q = p / \left( p - 1 \right)$ and let

$$R = \left\{ X : X \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times n } , X \text { positive semidefinite, tr } X ^ { q } = 1 \right\} .$$
(7)

An equivalent version of Theorem 23 then states that

$$\max _ { R } \mathrm { t r } A X = \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(8)

for every positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix $A .$ Using this representation, we
obtain

$$\left( \mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } = \max _ { R } \mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) X$$
$$\leq \max _ { R } \mathrm { t r } A X + \max _ { R } \mathrm { t r } B X$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } + \left( \mathrm { t r } B ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p } .$$

(9)

Equality in (9) can occur only if the same $X$ maximizes tr $A X ,$ tr $B X$ and
$\mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) X ,$ which implies, by Theorem 23, that $A ^ { p } ,$ $B ^ { p }$ and $\left( A + B \right) ^ { p }$ are
proportional, and hence that $A$ and $B$ must be proportional.
✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="254" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->


## 24 QUASILINEAR REPRESENTATION OF $| A | ^ { 1 / n }$

In Section 4 we established (Exercise 2) that

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A \geq | A | ^ { 1 / n }$$
(1)

for every positive semidefinite $n \times n \mathrm { m a t r i x } A .$ The following theorem gener-
alizes this result.


### Theorem 27

Let $A \neq 0$ be a positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix. Then

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A X \geq | A | ^ { 1 / n }$$
(2)

for every positive definite $n \times n$ matrix $X$ satisfying $| X | = 1 ,$ with equality if
and only if

$$X = | A | ^ { 1 / n } A ^ { - 1 } .$$
(3)

Let us give two proofs.

First proof. Let $A \neq 0$ be positive semidefinite and $X$ positive definite with
$| X | = 1 .$ Denote the eigenvalues of $X ^ { 1 / 2 } A X ^ { 1 / 2 }$ by $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } .$ Then $\lambda _ { i } \geq$
$\left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$ and Theorem 3 implies that

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { 1 / n } \leq \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i }$$
(4)

with equality if and only if $\lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 2 } = \cdots = \lambda _ { n } .$ Rewriting (4) in terms of the
matrices $A$ and $X$ we obtain

$$| X ^ { 1 / 2 } A X ^ { 1 / 2 } | ^ { 1 / n } \leq \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } X ^ { 1 / 2 } A X ^ { 1 / 2 }$$
(5)

and hence, since $| X | = 1 ,$

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A X \geq | A | ^ { 1 / n } .$$
(6)

Equality in (6) occurs if and only if all eigenvalues of $X ^ { 1 / 2 } A X ^ { 1 / 2 }$ are equal,
that is, $\mathrm { i f }$ and only if

$$X ^ { 1 / 2 } A X ^ { 1 / 2 } = \mu I _ { n }$$
(7)

for some $\mu > 0 .$ (Notice that $\mu = 0$ cannot occur, because it would imply
$A = 0 ,$ which we have excluded.) From (7) we obtain $A = \mu X ^ { - 1 }$ and hence
$X = \mu A ^ { - 1 } .$ Taking determinants on both sides we find $\mu = | A | ^ { 1 / n }$ since
$| X | = 1 .$
✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="255" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 24 ] Quasilinear representation of $| A | ^ { 1 / n }$" -->

Second proof. In this proof we view the inequality (2) as the solution of the
following constrained minimization problem in $X :$


<table>
<tr>
<td>minimize</td>
<td>$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A X$$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>subject to</td>
<td>log |X | = 0, $X$ positive definite,</td>
</tr>
</table>


(8)

(9)

where $A$ is a given positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix. To take into account
the positive definiteness of $X$ we write $X = Y Y ^ { \prime }$ where $Y$ is a square matrix
of order n; the minimization problem then becomes

$$\mathrm { m i n i m i z e } \quad \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } Y ^ { \prime } A Y$$
$$\text { subject to } \quad \log | Y | ^ { 2 } = 0 .$$
(10)
(11)


<table>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


To solve (10)-(11) we form the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( Y \right) = \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } Y ^ { \prime } A Y - \lambda \log | Y | ^ { 2 }$$
(12)

and differentiate. This yields

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( Y \right) = \left( 2 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } Y ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { d } Y - 2 \lambda \mathrm { t r } Y ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } Y$$

$$= 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( \left( 1 / n \right) Y ^ { \prime } A - \lambda Y ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } Y .$$
(13)

From (13) we obtain the first-order conditions

$$\left( 1 / n \right) Y ^ { \prime } A = \lambda Y ^ { - 1 }$$
$$| Y | ^ { 2 } = 1 .$$
(14)
(15)

Pre-multiplying both sides of (14) by $n \left( Y ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 }$ gives

$$A = n \lambda \left( Y Y ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(16)

which shows that $\lambda > 0$ and $A$ is non-singular. (If $\lambda = 0 ,$ then $A$ is the null
matrix; this case we have excluded from the beginning.) Taking determinants
in (16) we obtain, using (15),

$$n \lambda = | A | ^ { 1 / n } .$$
(17)

$$\mathrm { r e a r r a n g i n g } \mathrm { y i e l d s }$$
Inserting this in (16) and

$$Y Y ^ { \prime } = | A | ^ { 1 / n } A ^ { - 1 } .$$
(18)

Since tr $Y ^ { \prime } A Y$ is convex, $\log | Y | ^ { 2 }$ concave (Theorem 25) and $\lambda > 0 ,$ it follows
that $\psi \left( Y \right)$ is convex. Hence Theorem 7.13 implies that $\left( 1 / n \right)$ tr $Y ^ { \prime } A Y$ has
an absolute minimum under the constraint (11) at every point where (18) is
satisfied. The constrained minimum is

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } Y ^ { \prime } A Y = \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } | A | ^ { 1 / n } A ^ { - 1 } A = | A | ^ { 1 / n } .$$
(19)

This completes the second proof of Theorem 27.
✷
☐

Exercises

<!-- PageNumber="256" -->

1\. Use Exercise 4.2 to prove that

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A X \geq | A | ^ { 1 / n } | X | ^ { 1 / n }$$

for every two positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $X ,$ with equality
if and only if $A = 0$ or $X = \mu A ^ { - 1 }$ for some $\mu \geq 0 .$

2\. Hence obtain Theorem 27 as a special case.


## 25 MINKOWSKI'S DETERMINANT THEOREM

Using the quasilinear representation given in Theorem 27, let us establish
Minkowski's determinant theorem.


### Theorem 28

For any two positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrices $A \neq 0$ and $B \neq 0 ,$

$$| A + B | ^ { 1 / n } \geq | A | ^ { 1 / n } + | B | ^ { 1 / n }$$
(1)

with equality if and only if | $A + B | = 0$ or $A = \mu B$ for some $\mu > 0 .$

Proof. Let $A$ and $B$ be two positive semidefinite matrices, and assume that
$A \neq 0 ,$ $B \neq 0 .$ If $| A | = 0 ,$ $| B | > 0 ,$ we clearly have $| A + B | > | B | .$ If
$| A | > 0 ,$ $| B | = 0 ,$ we have $| A + B | > | A | .$ If | $A | = | B | = 0 ,$ we have $| A + B | \geq 0 .$
Hence, if $A$ or $B$ is singular, the inequality (1) holds, and equality occurs if
and only if $| A + B | = 0 .$

Assume next that $A$ and $B$ are positive definite. Using the representation
in Theorem 27, we then have

$$| A + B | ^ { 1 / n } = \min _ { X } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) X$$
$$\geq \min _ { X } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A X + \min _ { X } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } B X$$
$$= | A | ^ { 1 / n } + | B | ^ { 1 / n } ,$$

(2)

where the minimum is taken over all positive definite $X$ satisfying $| X | = 1 .$
Equality occurs only if the same $X$ minimizes $\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A X ,$ $\left( 1 / n \right)$ tr $B X$ and
$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) X ,$ which implies that $A ^ { - 1 } ,$ $B ^ { - 1 }$ and $\left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 }$ must be
proportional, and hence that $A$ and $B$ must be proportional.
✷
☐


## 26 WEIGHTED MEANS OF ORDER $p$


### Definition

Let $x$ be an $n \times 1$ vector with positive components $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ and let $a$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="257" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 26 ] Weighted means of order $p$" -->

be an $n \times 1$ vector of positive weights $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } ,$ so that

$$0 < \alpha _ { i } < 1 , \quad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } = 1 . ,$$
(1)

Then, for any real $p \neq 0 ,$ the expression

$$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { 1 / p }$$
(2)

is called the weighted mean of order $p$ of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ with weights $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } .$

Note. This definition can be extended to non-negative $x$ if we set $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = 0$
in the case where $p < 0$ and one or more of the $x _ { i }$ are zero. We shall, however,
confine ourselves to positive x.

The functional form defined by (2) occurs frequently in the economics
literature. For example, if we multiply $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ by a constant, we obtain the
CES (constant elasticity of substitution) functional form.


### Theorem 29

$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ is (positively) linearly homogeneous in x, that is,

$$M _ { p } \left( \lambda x , a \right) = \lambda M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$$
(3)

for every $\lambda > 0 .$

Proof. Immediate from the definition.
✷
☐

One would expect a mean of $n$ numbers to lie between the smallest and
largest of the $n$ numbers. This is indeed the case here as we shall now demon-
strate.


### Theorem 30

We have

$$\min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } x _ { i } \leq M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \leq \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } x _ { i }$$
(4)

with equality if and only if $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } .$

Proof. We first prove the theorem for $p = 1 .$ Since

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } \left( x _ { i } - M _ { 1 } \left( x , a \right) \right) = 0 ,$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="258" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

we have either $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } ,$ or else $x _ { i } < M _ { 1 } \left( x , a \right)$ for at least one
$x _ { i }$ and $M _ { 1 } \left( x , a \right) < x _ { i }$ for at least one other $x _ { i } .$ This proves the theorem for
$p = 1 .$

For $p \neq 1 ,$ we let $y _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { p } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$ Then, since

$$\min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } y _ { i } \leq M _ { 1 } \left( y , a \right) \leq \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } y _ { i } ,$$
(6)

we obtain

$$\min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } x _ { i } ^ { p } \leq \left( M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \right) ^ { p } \leq \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } x _ { i } ^ { p } .$$
(7)

This implies that $\left( M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \right) ^ { p }$ lies between $\left( \min x _ { i } \right) ^ { p }$ and $\left( \max x _ { i } \right) ^ { p } .$
☐

✷

Let us next investigate the behaviour of $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ when $p$ tends to 0 or to
$\pm \infty .$


### Theorem 31

$$\lim _ { p \rightarrow 0 } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha _ { i } }$$
$$\lim _ { p \rightarrow \infty } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \max x _ { i }$$
(8)
(9)

$$\lim _ { p \rightarrow - \infty } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \min x _ { i }$$
(10)

Proof. To prove (8) we let

$$\phi \left( p \right) = \log \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right)$$
(11)

and $\psi \left( p \right) = p ,$ so that

$$\log M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \phi \left( p \right) / \psi \left( p \right) .$$
(12)

Then $\phi \left( 0 \right) = \psi \left( 0 \right) = 0 ,$ and

$$\phi ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) = \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } x _ { j } ^ { p } \log x _ { j } , \quad \psi ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) = 1 . ,$$
(13)

By l'Hôpital's rule,

$$\lim _ { p \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \phi \left( p \right) } { \psi \left( p \right) } = \lim _ { p \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \phi ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) } { \psi ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) } = \frac { \phi ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) } { \psi ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } \log x _ { j } = \log \left( \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { \alpha _ { j } } \right) ,$$
(14)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="259" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 27 ] Schlömilch's inequality" -->

and (8) follows. To prove $\left( 9 \right) ,$ let

$$x _ { k } = \max _ { 1 < i \leq n } x _ { i }$$
(15)

$\left( k \right.$ is not necessarily unique). Then, for $p > 0 ,$

$$\alpha _ { k } ^ { 1 / p } x _ { k } \leq M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \leq x _ { k }$$
(16)

which implies (9). Finally, to prove (10), let $q = - \quad p$ and $y _ { i } = 1 / x _ { i } .$ Then

$$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \left( M _ { q } \left( y , a \right) \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
(17)

and hence

$$\lim _ { p \rightarrow - \infty } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \lim _ { q \rightarrow \infty } \left( M _ { q } \left( y , a \right) \right) ^ { - 1 } = \left( \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } y _ { i } \right) ^ { - 1 } = \min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } x _ { i } .$$
(18)

This completes the proof.
☐

✷


## 27 SCHLÖMILCH'S INEQUALITY

The limiting result (26.8) in the previous section suggests that it is convenient
to define

$$M _ { 0 } \left( x , a \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { \alpha _ { i } } ,$$
(1)

since in that case $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) ,$ regarded as a function of $p ,$ is continuous for every
$p$ in $\mathbb{R} .$ The arithmetic-geometric mean inequality (Theorem 3) then takes the
form

$$M _ { 0 } \left( x , a \right) \leq M _ { 1 } \left( x , a \right) ,$$
(2)

and is a special case of the following result, due to Schlömilch.


### Theorem 32 (Schlömilch)

If not all $x _ { i }$ are equal, $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ is a monotonically increasing function of $p .$
That is, if $p < q ,$ then

$$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \leq M _ { q } \left( x , a \right)$$
(3)

with equality if and only if $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n } .$

Proof. Assume that not all $x _ { i }$ are equal. (If they are, the result is trivial.) We
show first that

$$\mathrm { d } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) / \mathrm { d } p > 0 \quad \text { for all } p \neq 0 .$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="260" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

Define

$$\phi \left( p \right) = \log \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) .$$
(5)

Then $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \exp \left( \phi \left( p \right) / p \right)$ and

$$\mathrm { d } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) / \mathrm { d } p = p ^ { - 2 } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \left( p \phi ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) - \phi \left( p \right) \right)$$
$$= p ^ { - 2 } M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) \left( \left( \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { j } \alpha _ { j } x _ { j } ^ { p } \log x _ { j } ^ { p } - \log \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right)$$
$$= \frac { M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) } { p ^ { 2 } \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } } \left( \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } g \left( x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) - g \left( \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } \right) \right) ,$$

where the real-valued function $g$ is defined for $z > 0$ by $g \left( z \right) = z \log z .$ Since
$g$ is strictly convex (see Exercise 4.9.1), (4) follows. Hence $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ is strictly
increasing on $\left( - \infty , 0 \right)$ and $\left( 0 , \infty \right) ,$ and since $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ is continuous at $p = 0 ,$
✷

☐
the result follows.


## 28 CURVATURE PROPERTIES OF $M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$

The curvature properties of the weighted means of order $p$ follow from the
sign of the Hessian matrix.


## Theorem 33

$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ is a concave function of $x$ for $p \leq 1$ and a convex function of $x$ for
$p \geq 1 .$ In particular,

$$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) + M _ { p } \left( y , a \right) \leq M _ { p } \left( x + y , a \right) \quad \left( p < 1 \right)$$
(1)

and

$$M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) + M _ { p } \left( y , a \right) \geq M _ { p } \left( x + y , a \right) \quad \left( p > 1 \right)$$
(2)

with equality if and only if $x$ and $y$ are linearly dependent.

Proof. Let $p \neq 0 ,$ $p \neq 1 .$ (If $p = 1 ,$ the result is obvious.) Let

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p } ,$$
(3)

so that $M \left( x \right) \equiv M _ { p } \left( x , a \right) = \left( \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 1 / p } .$ Then

$$\mathrm { d } M \left( x \right) = \frac { M \left( x \right) } { p \phi \left( x \right) } \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x \right) ,$$
(4)

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } M \left( x \right) = \frac { \left( 1 - p \right) M \left( x \right) } { \left( p \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } \left( \left( \mathrm { d } \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( p / \left( 1 - p \right) \right) \phi \left( x \right) \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) \right) .$$
(5)

(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="261" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 29 ] Least squares" -->

Now, since

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( x \right) = p \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p - 1 } \mathrm { d } x _ { i } , \quad \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \phi \left( x \right) = p \left( p - 1 \right) \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p - 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$
(6)

we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } M \left( x \right) = \frac { \left( 1 - p \right) M \left( x \right) } { \left( \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } \left[ \left( \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p - 1 } \mathrm { d } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } - \phi \left( x \right) \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p - 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] . \quad \left( \quad \right)$$

Let $\lambda _ { i } = \alpha _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { p - 2 } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and $\Lambda$ the diagonal $n \times n$ matrix with $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$
on its diagonal. Then

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } M \left( x \right) = \frac { \left( 1 - p \right) M \left( x \right) } { \left( \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } \left( \left( x ^ { \prime } \Lambda \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { 2 } - \phi \left( x \right) \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \Lambda \mathrm { d } x \right)$$

$$= \frac { \left( p - 1 \right) M \left( x \right) } { \left( \phi \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } \left( \mathrm { d } x \right) ^ { \prime } \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( \phi \left( x \right) I _ { n } - \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } x x ^ { \prime } \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } \mathrm { d } x .$$
(8)

The matrix $\phi \left( x \right) I - \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } x x ^ { \prime } \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is positive semidefinite, because all but one
of its eigenvalues equal $\phi \left( x \right) ,$ and the remaining eigenvalue is zero. (Note that
$x ^ { \prime } \Lambda x = \phi \left( x \right)$ Hence $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } M \left( x \right) \geq 0$ for $p > 1$ and $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } M \left( x \right) \leq 0$ for $p < 1 .$ The
result then follows from Theorem 7.7.
☐
✷

Note. The second part of the theorem also follows from Minkowski's inequal-
ity by writing $\alpha _ { i } ^ { 1 / p } x _ { i }$ for $x _ { i }$ and $\alpha _ { i } ^ { 1 / p } y _ { i }$ for $y _ { i }$ in (23.2) and (23.4).


### Exercises

1\. Show that $p \log M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ is a convex function of $p .$

2\. Hence show that the function $M _ { p } \equiv M _ { p } \left( x , a \right)$ satisfies

$$M _ { p } ^ { p } \leq \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } M _ { p _ { i } } ^ { \delta _ { i } p _ { i } }$$

for every $p _ { i } ,$ where

$$p = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \delta _ { i } p _ { i } , \quad 0 < \delta _ { i } < 1 , \quad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \delta _ { i } = 1 . ,$$


## 29 LEAST SQUARES

The last topic in this chapter on inequalities deals with least-squares prob-
lems. In Theorem 34 we wish to approximate a given vector $d$ by a linear
combination of the columns of a given matrix $A .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="262" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->


### Theorem 34 (least squares)

Let $A$ be a given $n \times k$ matrix, and $d$ a given $n \times 1$ vector. Then

$$\left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A x - d \right) \geq d ^ { \prime } \left( I - A A ^ { + } \right) d$$
(1)

for every $x$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { k } ,$ with equality if and only if

$$x = A ^ { + } d + \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q$$
(2)

for some $q$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { k } .$

Note. In the special case where $A$ has full column rank $k ,$ we have $A ^ { + } =$
$\left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime }$ and hence a unique vector $x _ { * }$ exists which minimizes $\left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A x - \right.$
$\left. d \right)$ over all x, namely

$$x _ { * } = \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } d .$$
(3)

Proof. Consider the real-valued functions $\phi : \mathrm { R } ^ { k } \rightarrow \mathrm { R }$ defined by

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A x - d \right) .$$
(4)

Differentiating $\phi$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \phi = 2 \left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } \left( A x - d \right) = 2 \left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { d } x .$$
(5)

Since $\phi$ is convex it has an absolute minimum $\mathrm { a t }$ points $x$ which satisfy $d \phi \left( x \right) =$
0, that is,

$$A ^ { \prime } A x = A ^ { \prime } d .$$
(6)

Using Theorem 2.12 we see that Equation (6) is consistent, and that its general
solution is given by

$$x = \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } d + \left( I - \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } A \right) q$$
$$= A ^ { + } d + \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) q .$$

(7)

Hence $A x = A A ^ { + } d ,$ and the absolute minimum is

$$\left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A x - d \right) = d ^ { \prime } \left( I - A A ^ { + } \right) d .$$
(8)

This completes the proof.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="263" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 30 ] Generalized least squares" -->


## 30 GENERALIZED LEAST SQUARES

As an immediate generalization of Theorem 34, let us prove Theorem 35.


### Theorem 35 (generalized least squares)

Let $A$ be a given $n \times k$ matrix, $d$ a given $n \times 1$ vector and $B$ a given positive
semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix. Then

$$\left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } B \left( A x - d \right) \geq d ^ { \prime } C d$$
(1)

with

$$C = B - B A \left( A ^ { \prime } B A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B$$
(2)

for every $x$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { k } ,$ with equality if and only if

$$x = \left( A ^ { \prime } B A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B d + \left( I - \left( A ^ { \prime } B A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B A \right) q$$
(3)

for some $q$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { k } .$

Proof. Let $d _ { 0 } = B ^ { 1 / 2 } d$ and $A _ { 0 } = B ^ { 1 / 2 } A ,$ and apply Theorem 34.
✷
☐


### Exercises

1\. Consider the matrix $C$ defined in $\left( 2 \right) .$ Show that (i) $C$ is symmetric
and positive semidefinite, (ii) $C A = 0 ,$ and (iii) $C$ is idempotent if $B$ is
idempotent.

2\. Consider the solution for $x$ in (3). Show that (i) $x$ is unique if and only
if $A ^ { \prime } B A$ is non-singular, and (ii) $A x$ is unique if and only if $r \left( A ^ { \prime } B A \right) =$
$r \left( A \right) .$


## 31 RESTRICTED LEAST SQUARES

The next result determines the minimum of a quadratic form when $x$ is subject
to linear restrictions.


### Theorem 36 (restricted least squares)

Let $A$ be a given $n \times k$ matrix, $d$ a given $n \times 1$ vector and $B$ a given positive
semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix. Further, let $R$ be a given $m \times k$ matrix and $r$ a
given $m \times 1$ vector such that $R R ^ { + } r = r .$ Then

$$\left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } B \left( A x - d \right) \geq \left( \begin{array}{} d & C _ { 1 1 } & C _ { 1 2 } \\ r & C _ { 1 2 } ^ { \prime } & C _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} d \\ r \end{array} \right)$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="264" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

for every $x$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { k }$ satisfying $R x = r .$ Here

$$C _ { 1 1 } = B + B A N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } R N ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B - B A N ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B ,$$

$$C _ { 1 2 } = - B A N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } , \quad C _ { 2 2 } = \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } - I ,$$
(2)

and $N = A ^ { \prime } B A + R ^ { \prime } R .$ Equality occurs if and only if

$$x = x _ { 0 } + N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( r - R x _ { 0 } \right) + \left( I - N ^ { + } N \right) q ,$$
(3)

where $x _ { 0 } = N ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B d$ and $q$ is an arbitrary $k \times 1$ vector.

Proof. Define the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } B \left( A x - d \right) - l ^ { \prime } \left( R x - r \right) ,$$
(4)

where $l$ is an $m \times 1$ vector of Langrange multipliers. Differentiating $\psi$ we
obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = x ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } B A \mathrm { d } x - d ^ { \prime } B A \mathrm { d } x - l ^ { \prime } R \mathrm { d } x .$$
(5)

The first-order conditions are therefore

$$A ^ { \prime } B A x - R ^ { \prime } l = A ^ { \prime } B d ,$$
$$R x = r ,$$
(6)
(7)

which we can write as one equation as

$$\left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { \prime } B A & R ^ { \prime } \\ R & 0 \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} x \\ - l \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { \prime } B d \\ r \end{array} \right) .$$
(8)

According to Theorem 3.23, Equation (8) in $x$ and $l$ has a solution if and only
if

$$A ^ { \prime } B d \in \mathcal{M} \left( A ^ { \prime } B A , R ^ { \prime } \right) \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad r \in \mathcal{M} \left( R \right) ,$$
(9)

in which case the general solution for $x$ is

$$x = \left[ N ^ { + } - N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } R N ^ { + } \right] A ^ { \prime } B d$$
$$+ N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } r + \left( I - N N ^ { + } \right) q$$

(10)

where $N = A ^ { \prime } B A + R ^ { \prime } R$ and $q$ is arbitrary.

The consistency conditions (9) being satisfied, the general solution for $x$
is given by (10) which we rewrite as

$$x = x _ { 0 } + N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R N ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( r - R x _ { 0 } \right) + \left( I - N N ^ { + } \right) q ,$$
(11)

where $x _ { 0 } = N ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } B d .$ Since $\psi$ is convex (independent of the signs of the
components of l), constrained absolute minima occur at points $x$ satisfying
(11). The value of the absolute minimum is obtained by inserting (11) in
$\left( A x - d \right) ^ { \prime } B \left( A x - d \right) .$
✷
☐


#### Exercises

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="265" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 32 ] Restricted least squares: matrix version" -->

1\. Let $V$ be a given positive semidefinite matrix, and let $A$ be a given
matrix and $b$ a given vector such that $b \in \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) .$ The class of solutions
to the problem


<table>
<tr>
<td>minimize</td>
<td>$$x ^ { \prime } V x$$ $$A x = b$$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>subject to</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


is given by

$$x = V _ { 0 } ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } \left( A V _ { 0 } ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } b + \left( I - V _ { 0 } ^ { + } V _ { 0 } \right) q ,$$

where $V _ { 0 } = V + A ^ { \prime } A$ and $q$ is an arbitrary vector. Moreover, if $\mathcal{M} \left( A ^ { \prime } \right) \subset$
$\mathcal{M} \left( V \right) ,$ then the solution simplifies to

$$x = V ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } \left( A V ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } b + \left( I - V ^ { + } V \right) q .$$

2\. Hence show that

$$\min _ { A x = b } x ^ { \prime } V x = b ^ { \prime } C b ,$$

where $C = \left( A V _ { 0 } ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } - I .$ Also show that, if $\mathcal{M} \left( A ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right) ,$ the
matrix $C$ simplifies to $C = \left( A V ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } .$


## 32 RESTRICTED LEAST SQUARES: MATRIX VERSION

Finally, let us prove the following matrix version of Theorem 36, which we
shall have opportunity to apply in Section 13.16.


### Theorem 37

Let $B$ be a given positive semidefinite matrix, and let $W$ and $R$ be given
matrices such that $\mathcal{M} \left( W \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( R \right) .$ Then

$$X ^ { \prime } B X \geq \mathrm { t r } W ^ { \prime } C W$$
tr
(1)

for every $X$ satisfying $R X = W .$ Here

$$C = \left( R B _ { 0 } ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } - I , \quad B _ { 0 } = B + R ^ { \prime } R . ,$$
(2)

Equality occurs if and only if

$$X = B _ { 0 } ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R B _ { 0 } ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } W + \left( I - B _ { 0 } B _ { 0 } ^ { + } \right) Q ,$$
(3)

where $Q$ is an arbitrary matrix of appropriate order.

Moreover, if $\mathcal{M} \left( R ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( B \right) ,$ then $C$ simplifies to

$$C = \left( R B ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + }$$
(4)

with equality occurring if and only if

$$X = B ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R B ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } W + \left( I - B B ^ { + } \right) Q .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="266" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

Proof. Consider the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } B X - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } \left( R X - W \right) ,$$
(6)

where $L$ is a matrix of Lagrange multipliers. Differentiating leads to

$$\mathrm { d } \psi \left( X \right) = \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } B \mathrm { d } X - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } R \mathrm { d } X .$$
(7)

Hence we obtain the first-order conditions

$$B X = R ^ { \prime } L . ,$$
$$R X = W ,$$
(8)
(9)

which we write as one matrix equation

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { B } & { R ^ { \prime } } \\ { R } & { 0 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} X \\ - L \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 \\ W \end{array} \right) .$$
(10)

According to Theorem 3.24, Equation (10) is $c o n s i s t e n t ,$ because $\mathcal{M} \left( W \right) \subset$
$\mathcal{M} \left( R \right)$ the solution for $X$ is

$$X = B _ { 0 } ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R B _ { 0 } ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } W + \left( I - B _ { 0 } B _ { 0 } ^ { + } \right) Q$$
(11)

in general, and

$$X = B ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R B ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } W + \left( I - B B ^ { + } \right) Q$$
(12)

if $\mathcal{M} \left( R ^ { \prime } \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( B \right) .$ Since $\psi$ is convex, the constrained absolute minima occur
at points $X$ satisfying (11) or (12). The value of the absolute minimum is
obtained by inserting (11) or (12) in tr $X ^ { \prime } B X .$
✷
☐


### Exercise

1\. Let $X$ be given by (3). Show that $X ^ { \prime } a$ is unique if and only if $a \in \mathcal{M} \left( B : \right. :$
$R ^ { \prime } .$


## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. Show that $\log x \leq x - 1$ for every $x > 0$ with equality if and only if
$x = 1 .$

2\. Hence show that $\log | A | \leq \mathrm { t r } A - n$ for every positive definite $n \times n$
matrix $A ,$ with equality if and only if $A = I _ { n } .$

3\. Show that

$$| A + B | / | A | \leq \exp \left[ \mathrm { t r } \left( A ^ { - 1 } B \right) \right]$$

where $A$ and $A + B$ are positive definite, with equality if and only if
$B = 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="267" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous exercises" -->

4\. For any positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix $A$ and $n \times 1$ vector $b ,$

$$0 \leq b ^ { \prime } \left( A + b b ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } b \leq 1$$

with equality if and only if $b = 0$ or $b \neq \mathcal{M} \left( A \right) .$

5\. Let $A$ be positive definite and $B$ symmetric, both of order n. Then

$$\min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } \lambda _ { i } \left( A ^ { - 1 } B \right) \leq \frac { x ^ { \prime } B x } { x ^ { \prime } A x } \leq \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } \lambda _ { i } \left( A ^ { - 1 } B \right)$$

for every $x \neq 0 .$

6\. Let $A$ be a symmetric $m \times m$ matrix and let $B$ be an $m \times n$ matrix of
rank n. Let $C = \left( B ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } A B .$ Then

$$\min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } \lambda _ { i } \left( C \right) \leq \frac { x ^ { \prime } A x } { x ^ { \prime } x } \leq \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } \lambda _ { i } \left( C \right)$$

for every $x \in \mathcal{M} \left( B \right) .$

7\. Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix with full column rank, and let $\imath$ be the $m \times 1$
vector consisting of ones only. Assume that $\imath$ is the first column of $A .$
Then

$$x ^ { \prime } \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } x \geq 1 / m$$

for every $x$ satisfying $x _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ with equality $\mathrm { i f }$ and only if $x = \left( 1 / m \right) A ^ { \prime } \imath .$

8\. Let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix of rank r. Let $\delta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \delta _ { r }$ be the singular values
of $A$ (that is, the positive square roots of the non-zero eigenvalues of
$A A ^ { \prime } ,$ and let $\delta = \delta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 2 } + \cdots + \delta _ { r } .$ Then

$$- \delta \leq \mathrm { t r } A X \leq \delta$$

for every $n \times m$ matrix $X$ satisfying $X ^ { \prime } X = I _ { m } .$

9\. Let $A$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix and $B$ an $m \times n$ matrix of
rank $m .$ Then

$$x ^ { \prime } A x \geq b ^ { \prime } \left( B A ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } b$$

for every $x$ satisfying $B x = b .$

Equality occurs if and only if $x = A ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } \left( B A ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } b .$

10\. Let $A$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix and $B$ an $m \times n$ matrix. Then

$$X ^ { \prime } A X \geq \mathrm { t r } \left( B A ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
tr

for every $n \times m$ matrix $X$ satisfying $B X = I _ { m } ,$ with equality if and only
if $X = A ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } \left( B A ^ { - 1 } B ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="268" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

11\. Let $A$ and $B$ be matrices of the same order, and assume that $A$ has full
row rank. Define $C = A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } B .$ Then

$$\mathrm { t r } X ^ { 2 } \geq 2 \mathrm { t r } C \left( I - A ^ { + } A \right) C ^ { \prime } + 2 \mathrm { t r } C ^ { 2 }$$ ☒

for every symmetric matrix $X$ satisfying $A X = B ,$ with equality if and
only if $\check { X } = C + C ^ { \prime } - C A ^ { \prime } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } A .$

12\. For any symmetric matrix $S ,$ let $\mu \left( S \right)$ denote its largest eigenvalue in
absolute value. Then, for any positive semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix $V$ and
$m \times n$ matrix $A$ we have

(i) $\mu \left( A V A ^ { \prime } \right) \leq \mu \left( V \right) \mu \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) ,$

(ii) $\mu \left( V \right) A A ^ { \prime } - A V A ^ { \prime }$ is positive semidefinite,
☒

(iii) tr $A V A ^ { \prime } \leq \mu \left( V \right)$ tr $A A ^ { \prime } \leq \left( \mathrm { t r } V \right) \left( \mathrm { t r } A A ^ { \prime } \right) ,$
☒

(iv) tr $V ^ { 2 } \leq \mu \left( V \right) \mathrm { t r } V ,$

(v) $\mathrm { t r } \left( A V A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \mu ^ { 2 } \left( V \right) \mu \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { t r } A A ^ { \prime } .$
☒

13\. Let $A$ and $B$ be positive semidefinite matrices of the same order. Show
that

$$\sqrt { \mathrm { t r } A B } \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { t r } A + \mathrm { t r } B \right)$$

with equality if and only if $A = B$ and $r \left( A \right) \leq 1$ (Yang 1988, Neudecker
1992).

14\. For any two matrices $A$ and $B$ of the same order,

(i) $2 \left( A A ^ { \prime } + B B ^ { \prime } \right) - \left( A + B \right) \left( A + B \right) ^ { \prime }$ is positive semidefinite,

(ii) $\mu \left[ \left( A + B \right) \left( A + B \right) ^ { \prime } \right] \leq 2 \left( \mu \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) + \mu \left( B B ^ { \prime } \right) \right) ,$

(iii) $\mathrm { t r } \left( A + B \right) \left( A + B \right) ^ { \prime } \leq 2 \left( \mathrm { t r } A A ^ { \prime } + \mathrm { t r } B B ^ { \prime } \right) .$

15\. Let $A ,$ $B$ and $C$ be matrices of the same order. Show that

$$\mu \left( A B C + C ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } \right) \leq 2 \left( \mu \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right) \mu \left( B B ^ { \prime } \right) \mu \left( C C ^ { \prime } \right) \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$

.

In particular, if $A ,$ $B$ and $C$ are symmetric,

$$\mu \left( A B C + C B A \right) \leq 2 \mu \left( A \right) \mu \left( B \right) \mu \left( C \right) .$$

16\. Let $A$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $0 < \lambda _ { 1 } \leq$
$\lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$ Show that the matrix

$$\left( \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { n } \right) I _ { n } - A - \left( \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { n } \right) A ^ { - 1 }$$

is positive semidefinite with $\mathrm { r a n k } \leq n - 2 .$ [Hint: Use the fact that
$x ^ { 2 } - \left( a + b \right) x + a b \leq 0$ for all $x \in \left[ a , b \right]$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="269" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous exercises" -->

17\. (Kantorovich inequality) Let $A$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix with
eigenvalues $0 < \lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$ Use the previous exercise to prove
that

$$1 \leq \left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) \left( x ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } x \right) \leq \frac { \left( \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { n } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 4 \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { n } }$$

for every $x \in \mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ satisfying $x ^ { \prime } x = 1$ (Kantorovich 1948, Greub and
Rheinboldt 1959).

18\. For any two matrices $A$ and $B$ satisfying $A ^ { \prime } B = I ,$ we have

$$B ^ { \prime } B \geq \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } , \quad A ^ { \prime } A \geq \left( B ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$

[Hint: Use the fact that $I - A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } \geq 0$

19\. Hence, for any positive definite $n \times n$ matrix $A$ and $n \times k$ matrix $X$ with
$r \left( X \right) = k ,$ we have

$$\left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } A X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } \geq \left( X ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$

.

20\. (Kantorovich inequality, matrix version). Let $A$ be a positive definite
matrix with eigenvalues $0 < \lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$ Show that

$$\left( X ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 } \leq X ^ { \prime } A X \leq \frac { \left( \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { n } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 4 \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { n } } \left( X ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

for every $X$ satisfying $X ^ { \prime } X = I .$

21\. (Bergstrom's inequality, matrix version). Let $A$ and $B$ be positive defi-
nite and $X$ of full column rank. Then

$$\left( X ^ { \prime } \left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 } \geq \left( X ^ { \prime } A ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 } + \left( X ^ { \prime } B ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

(Marshall and Olkin 1979, $p p .$ 469-473; and Neudecker and Liu 1995).

22\. Let $A$ and $B$ be positive definite matrices of the same order. Show that

$$2 \left( A ^ { - 1 } + B ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { - 1 } \leq A ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( A ^ { - 1 / 2 } B A ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } A ^ { 1 / 2 } \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( A + B \right) .$$

This provides a matrix version of the harmonic-geometric-arithmetic
mean inequality (Ando 1979, 1983).

23\. Let $A$ be positive definite and $B$ symmetric such that $| A + B | \neq 0 .$ Prove
that

$$\left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } B \left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } \leq A ^ { - 1 } - \left( A + B \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$

Prove further that the inequality is strict if and only if $B$ is non-singular
(see Olkin 1983).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="270" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Inequalities [Ch. 11" -->

24\. Let $A$ be positive definite and $V _ { 1 } , V _ { 2 } , \ldots , V _ { m }$ positive semidefinite, all of
the same order. Then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( A + V _ { 1 } + \cdots + V _ { i } \right) ^ { - 1 } V _ { i } \left( A + V _ { 1 } + \cdots + V _ { i } \right) ^ { - 1 } \leq A ^ { - 1 }$$

(Olkin 1983).

25\. Let $A$ be a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix and let $B _ { 1 } , \ldots , B _ { m }$ be $n \times r$
matrices. Then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \mathrm { t r } B _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( A + B _ { 1 } B _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \cdots + B _ { i } B _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 2 } B _ { i } < \mathrm { t r } A ^ { - 1 }$$

(Olkin 1983).

26\. Let the $n \times n$ matrix $A$ have real eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } .$ Show
that

(i) $m - s \left( n - 1 \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \leq \lambda _ { 1 } \leq m - s \left( n - 1 \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$

(ii) $m + s \left( n - 1 \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \leq \lambda _ { n } \leq m + s \left( n - 1 \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,$

where

$$m = \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A , \quad s ^ { 2 } = \left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } - m ^ { 2 } .$$

Equality holds on the left (right) of $\left( \mathrm { i } \right)$ if and only if equality holds on
the left (right) of (ii) if and only if the $n - \quad 1$ largest (smallest) eigenvalues
are equal (Wolkowicz and Styan 1980).


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. The classic work on inequalities is Hardy, Littlewood and Pólya (1952),
first published in 1934. Useful sources are also Beckenbach and Bellman
(1961), Marcus and Minc (1964), Bellman (1970, Chapters 7 and 8), and
Wang and Chow (1994).

§5. The idea of expressing a non-linear function as an envelope of linear func-
tions goes back to Minkowski and was used extensively by Bellman and others.
§8. See Fischer (1905). An important extension by Courant can be found in
Courant and Hilbert (1931).

§10. See Poincaré (1890).

§16. The inequality (5) was proved in 1932 by Karamata. Proofs and histori-
cal details can be found in Hardy, Littlewood and Pólya (1952, Theorem 108,
p. 89) or Beckenbach and Bellman (1961, pp. 30-32). Hardy, Littlewood and
Pólya also prove that if $\phi ^ { \prime \prime } \left( x \right)$ exists for all x, and is positive, then there is
strict inequality in (5) unless $x =$ y. Theorem 19 gives a weaker condition.
See also Marshall and Olkin (1979, Propositions 3.C.1 and 3.C.1.a).

§17-§20. See Magnus (1987). An alternative proof of Theorem 23 was given

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="271" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

by Neudecker (1989a).

§21. Hölder's inequality is a very famous one and is discussed extensively by
Hardy, Littlewood and Pólya (1952, pp. 22-24). Theorem 24 is due to Magnus
(1987).

§22. See Fan (1949, 1950).

§23. A detailed discussion of Minkowski's inequality can be found in Hardy,
Littlewood and Pólya (1952, pp. 30-31). Theorem 26 is due to Magnus (1987).

§24. The second proof of Theorem 27 is based on Neudecker (1974).

§26-§28. Weighted means are thoroughly treated in Hardy, Littlewood and
Pólya (1952).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->

Part Five -
The linear model

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 12 Statistical preliminaries


### 1 INTRODUCTION

The purpose of this chapter is to review briefly those statistical concepts and
properties that we shall use in the remainder of this book. No attempt is made
to be either exhaustive or rigorous.

It is assumed that the reader is familiar (however vaguely) with the con-
cepts of probability and random variables and has a rudimentary knowledge
of Riemann integration. Integrals are necessary in this chapter, but they will
not appear in any other chapter of the book.


### 2 THE CUMULATIVE DISTRIBUTION FUNCTION

If $x$ is a real-valued random variable, we define the cumulative distribution
function $F$ by

$$F \left( \xi \right) = \Pr \left( x \leq \xi \right) .$$
(1)

Thus, $F \left( \xi \right)$ specifies the probability that the random variable $x$ is at most
equal to a given number $\xi .$

$I t$ is clear that $F$ is non-decreasing and that

$$\lim _ { \xi \rightarrow - \infty } F \left( \xi \right) = 0 , \quad \lim _ { \xi \rightarrow \infty } F \left( \xi \right) = 1 . ,$$
(2)

Similarly, if $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ is an $n \times 1$ vector of real random variables, we
define the cumulative distribution function $F$ by

$$F \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) = \Pr \left( x _ { 1 } \leq \xi _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \leq \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \leq \xi _ { n } \right) ,$$
(3)

which specifies the probability of the joint occurrence $x _ { i } \leq \xi _ { i }$ for all $i .$

<!-- PageNumber="275" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="276" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Statistical preliminaries [Ch. 12" -->


### 3 THE JOINT DENSITY FUNCTION

Let $F$ be the cumulative distribution function of a real-valued random variable
x. If there exists a non-negative real-valued (in fact, Lebesgue-measurable)
function $f$ such that

$$F \left( \xi \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \xi } f \left( y \right) d y$$
(1)

for all $y \in \mathrm { R } ,$ then we say that $x$ is a continuous random variable and $f$ is
called its density function. In this case the derivative of $F$ exists and we have

$$\mathrm { d } F \left( \xi \right) / \mathrm { d } \xi = f \left( \xi \right) .$$
(2)

(Strictly speaking, (2) is true except for a set of values of $\xi$ of probability
zero.) The density function satisfies

$$f \left( \xi \right) \geq 0 , \quad \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( \xi \right) d \xi = 1 .$$
(3)

In the case of a continuous $n \times 1$ random vector $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ there exists
a non-negative real-valued function $f$ such that

$$F \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \xi _ { 1 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \xi _ { 2 } } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \xi _ { n } } f \left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) d y _ { 1 } d y _ { 2 } \cdots d y _ { n }$$
(4)

for all $\left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ in which case

$$\frac { \partial ^ { n } F \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) } { \partial \xi _ { 1 } \partial \xi _ { 2 } \cdots \partial \xi _ { n } } = f \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right)$$
(5)

at all points in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ (except possibly for a set of probability 0). The function
$f$ defined by (4) is called the joint density function of $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$

In this and subsequent chapters we shall only be concerned with continuous
random variables.


### 4 EXPECTATIONS

The expectation (or expected value) of any function $g$ of a random variable $x$
is defined as

$$\mathcal{E} g \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( \xi \right) f \left( \xi \right) d \xi ,$$
(1)

if the integral exists. More generally, let $x = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ be a random $n \times 1$
vector with joint density function $f .$ Then the expectation of $a n y$ function $g$
of $x$ is defined as

$$\mathcal{E} g \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) f \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) d \xi _ { 1 } \cdots d \xi _ { n }$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="277" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Variance and covariance" -->

if the n-fold integral exists.

If $G = \left( g _ { i j } \right)$ is an $m \times p$ matrix function, then we define the expectation
of the matrix $G$ as the $m \times p$ matrix of the expectations

$$\mathcal{E} G \left( x \right) = \left( \mathcal{E} g _ { i j } \left( x \right) \right) .$$
(3)

Below we list some useful elementary facts about expectations when they
exist. The first of these is

$$\mathcal{E} A = A$$
(4)

where $A$ is a matrix of constants. Next,

$$\mathcal{E} A G \left( x \right) B = A \left( \mathcal{E} G \left( x \right) \right) B$$
(5)

where $A$ and $B$ are matrices of constants and $G$ is a matrix function. Finally,

$$\mathcal{E} \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } G _ { i } \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { i } \mathcal{E} G _ { i } \left( x \right)$$
(6)

where the $\alpha _ { i }$ are constants and the $G _ { i }$ are matrix functions. This last property
characterizes expectation as a linear operator.


### 5 VARIANCE AND COVARIANCE

$I f \quad x$ is a random variable, we define its variance as

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \mathcal{E} \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) ^ { 2 } .$$
(1)

If $x$ and $y$ are two random variables with a joint density function, we define
their covariance as

$$\mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) = \mathcal{E} \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) \left( y - \mathcal{E} y \right) .$$
(2)

If $\mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) = 0 ,$ we say that $x$ and $y$ are uncorrelated.

We note the following facts about two random variables $x$ and $y$ and two
constants $\alpha$ and $\beta :$

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x + \alpha \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) ,$$
(3)

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \alpha x \right) = \alpha ^ { 2 } \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) ,$$
$$\mathcal{V} \left( x + y \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) + \mathcal{V} \left( y \right) + 2 \mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) ,$$
$$\mathcal{C} \left( \alpha x , \beta y \right) = \alpha \beta \mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) .$$
(4)
(5)
(6)

If $x$ and $y$ are uncorrelated, we obtain as a special case of (5):

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x + y \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) + \mathcal{V} \left( y \right) .$$
(7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="278" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Statistical preliminaries [Ch. 12" -->

Let us now consider the multivariate case. We define the variance (matrix)
of an $n \times 1$ random vector $x$ as the $n \times n$ matrix

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \mathcal{E} \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(8)

It is clear that the ij-th $\left( i \neq j \right)$ element of $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right)$ is just the covariance between
$x _ { i }$ and $x _ { j } ,$ and that the $i$ i-th diagonal element of $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right)$ is just the variance of
$x _ { i } .$


#### Theorem 1

Each variance matrix is symmetric and positive semidefinite.

Proof. Symmetry is obvious. To prove that $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right)$ is positive semidefinite, define
a real-valued random variable $y = a ^ { \prime } \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) ,$ where $a$ is an arbitrary $n \times 1$
vector. Then,

(9)
$$a ^ { \prime } \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) a = a ^ { \prime } \mathcal{E} \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) ^ { \prime } a = \mathcal{E} a ^ { \prime } \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) ^ { \prime } a = \mathcal{E} y ^ { 2 } \geq 0 ,$$

and hence $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right)$ is positive semidefinite.

✷
☐

The determinant $| \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) |$ is sometimes called the generalized variance of x.
The variance matrix of an $m \times n$ random matrix $X$ is defined as the $m n \times m n$
variance matrix of vec $X$ .

If $x$ is a random $n \times 1$ vector and $y$ a random $m \times 1$ vector, then we define
the covariance (matrix) between $x$ and $y$ as the $n \times m$ matrix

$$\mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) = \mathcal{E} \left( x - \mathcal{E} x \right) \left( y - \mathcal{E} y \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(10)

If $\mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) = 0$ we say that the two vectors $x$ and $y$ are uncorrelated.

The next two results generalize properties $\left( 3 \right) - \left( 7 \right)$ to the multivariate case.


#### Theorem 2

Let $x$ be a random $n \times 1$ vector and define $y = A x + b ,$ where $A$ is a constant
$m \times n$ matrix and $b$ a constant $m \times 1$ vector. Then

$$\mathcal{E} y = A \mathcal{E} x + b , \quad \mathcal{V} \left( y \right) = A \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) A ^ { \prime } .$$
(11)

Proof. The proof is left as an exercise for the reader.
✷
☐


#### Theorem 3

Let $x$ and $y$ be random $n \times 1$ vectors and let z be a random $m \times 1$ vector. Let
$A \left( p \times n \right)$ and $B \left( q \times m \right)$ be matrices of constants. Then

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x + y \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) + \mathcal{V} \left( y \right) + \mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) + \mathcal{C} \left( y , x \right) ,$$
$$\mathcal{C} \left( A x , B z \right) = A \mathcal{C} \left( x , z \right) B ^ { \prime } ,$$
(12)
(13)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="279" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] Independence of two random variables" -->

and, if $x$ and $y$ are uncorrelated,

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x + y \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) + \mathcal{V} \left( y \right) .$$
(14)

Proof. The proof is easy and again left as an exercise.
✷
☐

Finally, we present the following useful result regarding the expected value
of a quadratic form.


#### Theorem 4

Let $x$ be a random $n \times 1$ vector with $\mathcal{E} x = \mu$ and $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \Omega .$ Let $A$ be an $n \times n$
matrix. Then

$$\mathcal{E} x ^ { \prime } A x = \mathrm { t r } A \Omega + \mu ^ { \prime } A \mu .$$
(15)

Proof. We have

$$\mathcal{E} x ^ { \prime } A x = \mathcal{E} \mathrm { t r } x ^ { \prime } A x = \mathcal{E} \mathrm { t r } A x x ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \mathcal{E} A x x ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { t r } A \left( \mathcal{E} x x ^ { \prime } \right)$$

$$= \mathrm { t r } A \left( \Omega + \mu \mu ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathrm { t r } A \Omega + \mu ^ { \prime } A \mu ,$$
(16)

which is the desired result.

✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Show that $x$ has a degenerate distribution if and only if $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = 0 .$ (A
random vector $x$ is said to have a degenerate distribution if $\Pr \left( x = \xi \right) =$
1 for some $\xi .$ If $x$ has a degenerate distribution we also say that $x = \xi$
almost surely (a.s.) or with probability one.)

2\. Show that $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right)$ is positive definite if and only if the distribution of $a ^ { \prime } x$
is non-degenerate for all $a \neq 0 .$


### 6 INDEPENDENCE OF TWO RANDOM VARIABLES

Let $f \left( x , y \right)$ be the joint density function of two random variables $x$ and $y .$
Suppose we wish to calculate a probability that concerns only x, say the
probability of the event

$$a < x < b ,$$
(1)

where $a < b .$ We then have

$$\Pr \left( a < x < b \right) = \Pr \left( a < x < b , - \infty < y < \infty \right)$$
$$= \int _ { a } ^ { b } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d y d x = \int _ { a } ^ { b } f _ { x } \left( x \right) d x ,$$

(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="280" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Statistical preliminaries [Ch. 12" -->

where

$$f _ { x } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d y .$$
(3)

is called the marginal density function of x. Similarly we define

$$f _ { y } \left( y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d x$$
(4)

as the marginal density function of y. We proceed to define the important
concept of independence.


#### Definition 1

Let $f \left( x , y \right)$ be the joint density function of two random variables $x$ and $y$
and let $f _ { x } \left( x \right)$ and $f _ { y } \left( y \right)$ denote the marginal density functions of $x$ and $y$
respectively. Then we say that $x$ and $y$ are (stochastically) independent if

$$f \left( x , y \right) = f _ { x } \left( x \right) f _ { y } \left( y \right) .$$
(5)

The following result states that functions of independent variables are
uncorrelated.


#### Theorem 5

Let $x$ and $y$ be two independent random variables. Then, for any functions $g$
and $h ,$

$$\mathcal{E} g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) = \left( \mathcal{E} g \left( x \right) \right) \left( \mathcal{E} h \left( y \right) \right)$$
(6)

if the expectations exist.

Proof. We have

$$\mathcal{E} g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) f _ { x } \left( x \right) f _ { y } \left( y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) f _ { x } \left( x \right) d x \right) \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( y \right) f _ { y } \left( y \right) d y \right)$$
$$= \mathcal{E} g \left( x \right) \mathcal{E} h \left( y \right) ,$$

which completes the proof.

✷
☐
(7)

As an immediate consequence of Theorem 5 we obtain Theorem 6.


#### Theorem 6

If two random variables are independent, they are uncorrelated.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="281" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7 ] Independence of $n$ random variables" -->

The converse of Theorem 6 is not, in general, true (see Exercise 1). A
partial converse is given in Theorem 8.

If $x$ and $y$ are random vectors rather than random variables, straightfor-
ward extensions of Definition 1 and Theorems 5 and 6 hold.


#### Exercise

1\. Let $x$ be a random variable with $\mathcal{E} x = \mathcal{E} x ^ { 3 } = 0 .$ Show that $x$ and $x ^ { 2 }$ are
uncorrelated, but not in general independent.


### 7 INDEPENDENCE OF $n$ RANDOM VARIABLES

The notion of independence can be extended in an obvious manner to the case
of three or more random variables (vectors).


#### Definition 2

Let the random variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ have joint density function $f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$
and marginal density functions $f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , f _ { n } \left( x _ { n } \right) ,$ respectively. Then we say
that $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are (mutually) independent if

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) \cdots f _ { n } \left( x _ { n } \right) .$$
(1)

We note that, if $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are independent in the sense of Definition 2, they
are pairwise independent (that is, $x _ { i }$ and $x _ { j }$ are independent for all $i \neq j ,$ but
that the converse is not true. Thus pairwise independence does not necessarily
imply mutual independence.

Again the extension to random vectors is straightforward.


### 8 SAMPLING

Let $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ be independent random variables (vectors), each with the same
density function $f \left( x \right) .$ Then we say that $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are independent and iden-
tically distributed (i.i.d.) or, equivalently, that they constitute a (random)
sample $\left( o f \quad s i z e \quad n \right)$ from a distribution with density function $f \left( x \right) .$

Thus, if we have a sample $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ from a distribution with density $f \left( x \right) ,$
the joint density function of the sample is

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } \right) f \left( x _ { 2 } \right) \cdots f \left( x _ { n } \right) .$$


### 9 THE ONE-DIMENSIONAL NORMAL DISTRIBUTION

The most important of all distributions - and the only one that will play a
role in the subsequent chapters of this book - is the normal distribution. Its
density function is defined as

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } } } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right)$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="282" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Statistical preliminaries [Ch. 12" -->

for $- \infty < x < \infty ,$ where $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are the parameters of the distribution. If
$x$ is distributed as in (1), we write

$$x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$$
(2)

If $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } = 1$ we say that $x$ is standard-normally distributed.

Without proof we present the following theorem.


#### Theorem 7

If $x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ then

$$\mathcal{E} x = \mu , \mathcal{E} x ^ { 2 } = \mu ^ { 2 } + \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$$
(3)

$$\mathcal{E} x ^ { 3 } = \mu \left( \mu ^ { 2 } + 3 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) , \quad \quad \mathcal{E} x ^ { 4 } = \mu ^ { 4 } + 6 \mu ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } + 3 \sigma ^ { 4 } ,$$
(4)

and hence

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$$
$\mathcal{V} \left( x ^ { 2 } \right) = 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } + 4 \mu ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } .$
(5)
,


### 10 THE MULTIVARIATE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION

A random $n \times 1$ vector $x$ is said to be normally distributed if its density
function is given by

$$f \left( x \right) = \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { - n / 2 } | \Omega | ^ { - 1 / 2 } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( x - \mu \right) \right)$$
(1)

for $x \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ where $\mu$ is an $n \times 1$ vector and $\Omega$ a non-singular symmetric $n \times n$
matrix.

It is easily verified that (1) reduces to the one-dimensional normal density
(9.1) in the case $n =$ 1.

If $x$ is distributed as in (1), we write

$$x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \Omega \right)$$
(2)

or, occasionally, if we wish to emphasize the dimension of x,

$$x \sim \mathcal{N} _ { n } \left( \mu , \Omega \right) .$$
(3)

The parameters $\mu$ and $\Omega$ are just the expectation and variance matrix of x:

$$\mathcal{E} x = \mu , \quad \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \Omega .$$
(4)

We shall present (without proof) five theorems concerning the multivariate
normal distribution which we shall need in the following chapters. The first
of these provides a partial converse of Theorem 6.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="283" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] The multivariate normal distribution" -->


### Theorem 8

If $x$ and $y$ are normally distributed with $\mathcal{C} \left( x , y \right) = 0 ,$ then they are indepen-
dent.

Next, let us consider the marginal distributions associated with the mul-
tivariate normal distribution.


### Theorem 9

The marginal distributions associated with a normally distributed vector are
also normal. That is, if $x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \Omega \right)$ is partitioned as

$$\left( \begin{array}{} x _ { 1 } \\ x _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \left( \begin{array}{} \mu _ { 1 } \\ \mu _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) , \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 1 1 } & \Omega _ { 1 2 } \\ \Omega _ { 2 1 } & \Omega _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) \right] ,$$
(5)

then the marginal distribution of $x _ { 1 }$ is $\mathcal{N} \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \Omega _ { 1 1 } \right)$ and the marginal distribu-
tion of $x _ { 2 }$ is $\mathcal{N} \left( \mu _ { 2 } , \Omega _ { 2 2 } \right) .$

$A$ crucial property of the normal distribution is given in Theorem 10.


### Theorem 10

An affine transformation of a normal vector is again normal. That is, if $x \sim$
$\mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \Omega \right)$ and $y = A x + b$ where $A$ has full row rank, then

$$y \sim \mathcal{N} \left( A \mu + b , A \Omega A ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(6)

If $\mu = 0$ and $\Omega = I _ { n }$ we say that $x$ is standard-normally distributed and
we write

$$x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , I _ { n } \right) .$$
(7)


#### Theorem 11

If $x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , I _ { n } \right) ,$ then $x$ and $x \otimes x$ are uncorrelated.

Proof. Noting that

$$\mathcal{E} x _ { i } x _ { j } x _ { k } = 0 \quad \text { for all } i , j , k ,$$
(8)

the result follows.

✷
☐

Let us conclude this section with two results on quadratic forms in normal
variables, the first of which is a special case of Theorem 4.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="284" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Statistical preliminaries [Ch. 12" -->


#### Theorem 12

If $x \sim \mathcal{N} _ { n } \left( \mu , \Omega \right)$ and $A$ is a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix, then

$$\mathcal{E} x ^ { \prime } A x = \mathrm { t r } A \Omega + \mu ^ { \prime } A \mu$$
(9)

and

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( A \Omega \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \mu ^ { \prime } A \Omega A \mu .$$
(10)


### Exercise

1\. (Proof of (10)) Let $x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \Omega \right)$ and $A = A ^ { \prime } .$ Let $T$ be an orthogonal
matrix and $\Lambda$ a diagonal matrix such that

$$T ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { 1 / 2 } A \Omega ^ { 1 / 2 } T = \Lambda$$
(11)

and define

$$y = T ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 / 2 } \left( x - \mu \right) , \quad \omega = T ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { 1 / 2 } A \mu . ,$$
(12)

Prove that

(a) $y \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , I _ { n } \right) ,$

(b) $x ^ { \prime } A x = y ^ { \prime } \Lambda y + 2 \omega ^ { \prime } y + \mu ^ { \prime } A \mu . ,$

(c) $y ^ { \prime } \Lambda y$ and $\omega ^ { \prime } y$ are uncorrelated,

(d) $\mathcal{V} \left( y ^ { \prime } \Lambda y \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } \Lambda ^ { 2 } = 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( A \Omega \right) ^ { 2 } ,$

(e) $\mathcal{V} \left( \omega ^ { \prime } y \right) = \omega ^ { \prime } \omega = \mu ^ { \prime } A \Omega A \mu ,$

$$\mathcal{V} \left( x ^ { \prime } A x \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( y ^ { \prime } \Lambda y \right) + \mathcal{V} \left( 2 \omega ^ { \prime } y \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( A \Omega \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \mu ^ { \prime } A \Omega A \mu .$$
(f)


### 11 ESTIMATION

Statistical inference asks the question: Given a sample, what can be inferred
about the population from which it was drawn? Most textbooks distinguish
between point estimation, interval estimation and hypothesis testing. In the
following we shall only be concerned with point estimation.

In the theory of point estimation we seek to select a function of the ob-
servations that will approximate a parameter of the population in some well-
defined sense. A function of the hypothetical observations used to approximate
