✷
6.


### Exercises

1\. Suppose that $\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } S \right) \cap \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } T \right) = \left\{ 0 \right\}$ in the model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) .$
Show that the best affine unbiased estimator of $A X \beta$ (which always
exists) is $A C y ,$ where

$$C = S S ^ { \prime } X \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } + T T ^ { \prime } X X ^ { \prime } T \left( T ^ { \prime } X X ^ { \prime } T \right) ^ { + } T ^ { \prime } .$$

[Hint: Use Theorem 7.]

2\. Show that the variance matrix of this estimator is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( A C y \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } A S S ^ { \prime } X \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } S S ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } .$$


## 14 EXPLICIT AND IMPLICIT LINEAR CONSTRAINTS

Linear constraints on the parameter vector $\beta$ arise in two ways. First, we
may possess a priori knowledge that the parameters satisfy certain linear
constraints

$$R \beta = r . ,$$
(1)

where the matrix $R$ and vector $r$ are known and non-stochastic. These are
the explicit constraints.

Secondly, if the variance matrix $\sigma ^ { 2 } V$ is singular, then $\beta$ satisfies the linear
constraints

$$T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y \quad \mathrm { a . s . } ,$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="311" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] Explicit and implicit linear constraints" -->

where $T$ is a matrix of maximum column rank such that $V T = 0 .$ These are
the implicit constraints, due to the stochastic structure of the model. Implicit
constraints exist whenever $T ^ { \prime } X \neq 0 ,$ that is, whenever $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \not \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right) .$

Let us combine the two sets of constraints (1) and (2) as

$$R _ { 0 } \beta = r _ { 0 } \quad \mathrm { a . s . , } \quad R _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } X \\ R \end{array} \right) , \quad r _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } y \\ r \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

We do not require the matrix $R _ { 0 }$ to have full row rank; the constraints may
thus be linearly dependent. We must require, however, that the model is
consistent.


### Proposition 5 (consistency of the linear model with constraints)

In order for the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $\beta$ satisfies the
constraints $R \beta = r ,$ to be a consistent model it is necessary and sufficient
that

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} X & V \\ R & 0 \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . }$$
(4)

Proof. We write the model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ together with the constraints $R \beta = r$
as

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} X \\ R \end{array} \right) \beta + u ,$$
(5)

where

$$\mathcal{E} u = 0 , \quad \mathcal{E} u u ^ { \prime } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} V & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$
(6)

Proposition 5 then follows from Proposition 4.
✷
☐

The consistency condition (4) is equivalent (as, of course, it should be) to
the requirement that (3) is a consistent equation, i.e.

$$r _ { 0 } \in \mathcal{M} \left( R _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(7)

Let us see why. If (7) holds, then there exists a vector $c$ such that

$$T ^ { \prime } y = T ^ { \prime } X c , \quad r = R c . ,$$
(8)

This implies that $T ^ { \prime } \left( y - X c \right) = 0$ from which we solve

$$y - X c = \left( I - T ^ { \prime } T ^ { \prime } \right) q ,$$
(9)

where $q$ is arbitrary. Further, since

$$I - T T ^ { \prime } = S S ^ { \prime } = S \Lambda S ^ { \prime } S \Lambda ^ { - 1 } S ^ { \prime } = V V ^ { + } . ,$$
(10)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="312" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The linear regression model [Ch. 13" -->

we obtain

$$y = X c + V V ^ { + } q , \quad r = R c ,$$
(11)

and hence (4). It is easy to see that the converse is also true, that is, (4)
implies $\left( 7 \right) .$

The necessary consistency condition being established, let us now seek to
find the best affine unbiased estimator of $\mathrm { a }$ parametric function $W \beta$ in the
model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $X$ may fail to have full column rank, $V$ may be
singular, and explicit constraints $R \beta = r$ may be present.

We first prove a special case; the general result is discussed in the next
section.


### Theorem 11

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $\beta$ satisfies the con-
sistent linear constraints $R \beta = r ,$ and

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} X & V \\ R & 0 \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . }$$
(12)

Assume that $r \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) = r \left( X \right)$ and $\mathcal{M} \left( R ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Then the best affine
unbiased estimator $\widehat { W \beta }$ of $W \beta$ exists if and only if $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ in
which case

$$\widehat { W \beta } = W \beta ^ { * } + W \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left[ R _ { 0 } \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right] ^ { + } \left( r _ { 0 } - R _ { 0 } \beta ^ { * } \right) ,$$
(13)

where

$$R _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } X \\ R \end{array} \right) , \quad r _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } y \\ r \end{array} \right) , \quad \beta ^ { * } = \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } y , .$$
(14)

and $T$ is a matrix of maximum rank such that $V T =$ 0. The variance matrix
of $W \beta$ is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { W \beta } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } W \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } W ^ { \prime }$$
$$- \sigma ^ { 2 } W \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left[ R _ { 0 } \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right] ^ { + } R _ { 0 } \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } W ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒
(15)

$P r o o f .$ We write the constrained model in its equivalent form

$$S ^ { \prime } y = S ^ { \prime } X \beta + u , \quad \mathcal{E} u = 0 , \quad \mathcal{E} u u ^ { \prime } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \Lambda ,$$
(16)

where $\beta$ satisfies the combined implicit and explicit constraints

$$R _ { 0 } \beta = r _ { 0 } .$$
(17)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="313" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 15 ] The general linear model, $I$" -->

From Section 12 we know that the three conditions

$$r \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) = r \left( X \right) ,$$
$$\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } T \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } S \right)$$
(18)
(19)

and

$$\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } S \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(20)

are equivalent. Hence the two conditions $r \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) = r \left( X \right)$ and $\mathcal{M} \left( R ^ { \prime } \right) \subset$
☒
$\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right)$ are both satisfied if and only if

$$\mathcal{M} \left( R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } S \right) .$$
(21)

The result then follows from Theorem 5.
☐
✷


## 15 THE GENERAL LINEAR MODEL, I

Now we consider the general linear model

$$\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$$
(1)

where $V$ is possibly singular, $X$ may fail to have full column rank, and $\beta$
satisfies certain a priori (explicit) constraints $R \beta = r .$ $A s$ before, we transform
the model into

$$\left( S ^ { \prime } y , S ^ { \prime } X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \Lambda \right) ,$$
(2)

where $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix with positive diagonal elements, and the param-
eter vector $\beta$ satisfies

$$T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y \quad \text { \left(implicit constraints\right) }$$
(3)

and

$$R \beta = r \quad \text { \left(explicit constraints\right) } ,$$
(4)

which we combine as

$$R _ { 0 } \beta = r _ { 0 } , \quad R _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } X \\ R \end{array} \right) , \quad r _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } y \\ r \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

The model is consistent (that is, the implicit and explicit linear constraints
are consistent equations) if and only if

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} X & V \\ R & 0 \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . } ,$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="314" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The linear regression model [Ch. 13" -->

according to Proposition 5.

We want to find the best affine unbiased estimator of a parametric function
$W \beta .$ According to Proposition 3, the class of affine unbiased estimators of $W \beta$
is not empty (that is, $W \beta$ is estimable) if and only if

$$\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(7)

Notice that we can apply Proposition 3 to model (1) subject to the explicit
constraints, or to model (2) subject to the explicit and implicit constraints;
in either case we find (7).

$A$ direct application of Theorem 6 now yields the following theorem.


### Theorem 12

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $\beta$ satisfies the con-
sistent linear constraints $R \beta = r ,$ and

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} X & V \\ R & 0 \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . }$$
(8)

The best affine unbiased estimator $\widehat { W \beta }$ of $W \beta$ exists if and only if $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset$
$\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ in which case

$$\widehat { W \beta } = W \beta ^ { * } + W G ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( R _ { 0 } G ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( r _ { 0 } - R _ { 0 } \beta ^ { * } \right) ,$$
(9)

where

$$R _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } X \\ R \end{array} \right) , \quad r _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} T ^ { \prime } y \\ r \end{array} \right) , .$$
(10)

$$G = X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X + R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } R _ { 0 } , \quad \beta ^ { * } = G ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } y ,$$
(11)

and $T$ is a matrix of maximum rank such that $V T = 0 .$ The variance matrix
of $\bar { W } \widetilde { \beta }$ is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { W \beta } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } W G ^ { + } W ^ { \prime } - \sigma ^ { 2 } W G ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( R _ { 0 } G ^ { + } R _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } R _ { 0 } G ^ { + } W ^ { \prime } .$$
(12)

Note. We give alternative expressions for (9) and (12) in Theorem 14.


## 16 A SINGULAR VARIANCE MATRIX: THE GENERAL CASE, $I I$

We have now discussed every single case and combination of cases. Hence we
could stop here. There is, however, an alternative route that is of interest, and
leads to different (although equivalent) expressions for the estimators.

The route we have followed is this: first we considered the estimation of a
parametric function $W \beta$ with explicit restrictions $R \beta = r ,$ assuming that $V$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="315" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 16 ] A singular variance matrix: the general case, $I I$" -->

is non-singular; then we transformed the model with singular $V$ into a model
with non-singular variance matrix and explicit restrictions, thereby making
the implicit restrictions (due to the singularity of $\left. V \right)$ explicit. Thus we have
treated the singular model as a special case of the constrained model.

An alternative procedure is to reverse this route, and to look first at the
model

$$\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$$
(1)

where $V \quad i s \quad p o s s i b l y$ singular (and $X$ may not have full column rank). In the
case of a priori constraints $R \beta = r$ we then consider

$$y _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) , \quad X _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} X \\ R \end{array} \right) , .$$
(2)

in which case

$$\sigma ^ { 2 } V _ { e } = \mathcal{V} \left( y _ { e } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} V & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
(3)

so that the extended model can be written as

$$\left( y _ { e } , X _ { e } \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V _ { e } \right) ,$$
(4)

which is in the same form as (1). In this set-up the constrained model is a
special case of the singular model.

Thus we consider the model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $V$ is possibly singular, $X$
may have linearly dependent columns, but no explicit constraints are given.
We know, however, that the singularity of $V$ implies certain constraints on $\beta ,$
which we have called implicit constraints,

$$T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y ,$$
(5)

where $T$ is a matrix of maximum column rank such that $V T = 0 .$ In the
present approach, the implicit constraints need not be taken into account (they
are automatically satisfied, see Exercise 5), because we consider the whole $V$
matrix and the constraints are embodied in $V .$

According to Proposition 2, the parametric function $W \beta$ is estimable if
and only $\mathrm { i f }$

$$\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(6)

According to Proposition 4, the model is consistent $\mathrm { i f }$ and only if

$$y \in \mathcal{M} \left( X : V \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . } .$$
(7)

(Recall that (7) is equivalent to the requirement that the implicit constraint
(5) is a consistent equation in $\beta$ Let $A y + c$ be the affine estimator of $W \beta .$
The estimator is unbiased if and only if

$$A X \beta + c = W \beta \quad \text { for all } \beta \mathrm { i n } \mathrm { R } ^ { k } ,$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="316" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The linear regression model [Ch. 13" -->

which implies

$$A X = W ,$$
(9)

Since the variance matrix of $A y$ is $\sigma ^ { 2 } A V A ^ { \prime } ,$ the affine minimum-trace unbiased
estimator of $W \beta$ is found by solving the problem


<table>
<tr>
<td>$$\mathrm { m i n i m i z e } \quad \mathrm { t r } A V A ^ { \prime }$$</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$$A X = W .$$</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


(10)

(11)

Theorem 11.37 provides the solution

$$A ^ { * } = W \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } + Q \left( I - V _ { 0 } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } \right) ,$$
(12)

where $V _ { 0 } = V + X X ^ { \prime }$ and $Q$ is arbitrary. Since $y \in \mathcal{M} \left( V _ { 0 } \right)$ a.s., because of
(7), it follows that

$$A ^ { * } y = W \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } y$$
(13)

is the unique affine minimum-trace unbiased estimator of $W \beta .$ If, in addition,
$\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right) ,$ then $A ^ { * } y$ simplifies to

$$A ^ { * } y = W \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } y .$$
(14)

Summarizing, we have proved our next theorem.


### Theorem 13

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $y \in \mathcal{M} \left( X : V \right)$ a.s.
The best affine unbiased estimator $\widehat { W \beta }$ of $W \beta$ exists if and only if $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset$
$\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ in which case

$$\widehat { W \beta } = W \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } y ,$$
(15)

where $V _ { 0 } = V + X X ^ { \prime } .$ Its variance matrix is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { W \beta } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } W \left[ \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } - I \right] W ^ { \prime } .$$ ☒
(16)

Moreover, if $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right) ,$ then the estimator simplifies to

$$\widehat { W \beta } = W \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } y$$
(17)

with variance matrix

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { W \beta } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } W \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } W ^ { \prime } .$$
(18)

Note. Theorem 13 gives another (but equivalent) expression for the estimator
of Theorem 10. The special case $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right)$ is identical to Theorem 8.


### Exercises

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="317" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 17] The general linear model, $I I$" -->

1\. Show that $V _ { 0 } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X = X .$

2\. Show that $X \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } = X .$

3\. Let $T$ be any matrix such that $V T = 0 .$ Then

$$T ^ { \prime } X \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) = T ^ { \prime } X = T ^ { \prime } X \left( X ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X \right) ^ { + } .$$

4\. Suppose that we replace the unbiasedness condition (8) by

AXB+c=WB
for all $\beta$ satisfying $T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y .$

Show that this yields the same constrained minimization problem (10)
and (11) and hence the same estimator for $W \beta .$

5\. Show that the best affine unbiased estimator of $T ^ { \prime } X \beta$ is $T ^ { \prime } y$ with $\mathcal{V} \left( T ^ { \prime } y \right) =$
0\. Conclude that the implicit constraints $T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y$ are automatically
satisfied and need not be imposed.


## 17 THE GENERAL LINEAR MODEL, $I I$

Let us look at the general linear model

$$\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$$
(1)

where $V \quad i s \quad p o s s i b l y$ singular, $X$ may fail to have full column rank and $\beta$
satisfies explicit a priori constraints $R \beta = r .$ As discussed in the previous
section, we write the constrained model as

$$\left( y _ { e } , X _ { e } \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V _ { e } \right) ,$$
(2)

where

$$y _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) , \quad X _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} X \\ R \end{array} \right) , \quad V _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} V & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$
(3)

Applying Theorem 13 to model (2) we obtain Theorem 14, which provides a
different (though equivalent) expression for the estimator of Theorem 12.


### Theorem 14

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $\beta$ satisfies the con-
sistent linear constraints $R \beta = r ,$ and

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { y } \\ { r } \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} { X } & { V } \\ { R } & { 0 } \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . }$$
(4)

The best affine unbiased estimator $\widehat { W \beta }$ of $W \beta$ exists if and only if $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset$
$\mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ in which case

$$\widehat { W \beta } = W \left( X _ { e } ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X _ { e } \right) ^ { + } X _ { e } ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } y _ { e } ,$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="318" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The linear regression model [Ch. 13" -->

where $y _ { e } ,$ $X _ { e }$ and $V _ { e }$ are defined in (3), and $V _ { 0 } = V _ { e } + X _ { e } X _ { e } ^ { \prime } .$ Its variance
matrix is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { W \beta } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } W \left[ \left( X _ { e } ^ { \prime } V _ { 0 } ^ { + } X _ { e } \right) ^ { + } - I \right] W ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)


## 18 GENERALIZED LEAST SQUARES

Consider the Gauss-Markov set-up $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I \right)$ where $r \left( X \right) = k .$ In Section
3 we obtained the best affine unbiased estimator of $\beta ,$ $\widehat { \beta } = \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } y$ (the
Gauss-Markov estimator), by minimizing a quadratic form (the trace of the
estimator's variance matrix) subject to a linear constraint (unbiasedness). In
Section 4 we showed that the Gauss-Markov estimator can also be obtained
by minimizing $\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } \left( y - X \beta \right)$ over all $\beta$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { k } .$ The fact that the prin-
ciple of least squares (which is not a method of estimation but a method of
approximation) produces best affine estimators is rather surprising and by no
means trivial.

We now ask whether this relationship stands up against the introduction
of more general assumptions such as $| V | = 0 ,$ or $r \left( X \right) < k .$ The answer to this
☒
question is in the affirmative.

To see why, we recall from Theorem 11.35 that for a given positive semidef-
inite matrix $A$ the problem

$$\mathrm { m i n i m i z e } \left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } A \left( y - X \beta \right)$$
(1)

has a unique solution for $W \beta$ if and only if

$$\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) ,$$
(2)

in which case

$$W \beta ^ { * } = W \left( X ^ { \prime } A X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } A y .$$
(3)

Choosing $A = \left( V + X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + }$ and comparing with Theorem 13 yields the fol-
lowing.


### Theorem 15

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $y \in \mathcal{M} \left( X : V \right)$ a.s.
Let $W$ be a matrix such that $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Then the best affine unbiased
estimator of $W \beta$ is $W \widehat { \beta } ,$ where $\widehat { \beta }$ minimizes

$$\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } \left( V + X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( y - X \beta \right) .$$
(4)

In fact we may, instead of (4), minimize the quadratic form

$$\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } \left( V + X E X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( y - X \beta \right) ,$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="319" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 19 ] Restricted least squares" -->

where $E$ is a positive semidefinite matrix such that $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V + X E X ^ { \prime } \right) .$
The estimator $W \widehat { \beta }$ will be independent of the actual choice of $E .$ For $E = I$
the requirement $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V + X X ^ { \prime } \right)$ is obviously satisfied; this leads to
Theorem 15. If $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right) ,$ which includes the case of non-singular $V ,$
we can choose $E = 0$ and minimize, instead of (4),

$$\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } \left( y - X \beta \right) .$$
(6)

In the case of a priori linear constraints, the following corollary applies.


### Corollary 1

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $\beta$ satisfies the con-
sistent linear constraints $R \beta = r ,$ and

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { y } \\ { r } \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} { X } & { V } \\ { R } & { 0 } \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . }$$
(7)

Let $W$ be a matrix such that $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Then the best affine
unbiased estimator of $W \beta$ is $W \widehat { \beta } ,$ where $\widehat { \beta }$ minimizes

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y - X \beta \\ r - R \beta \end{array} \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \begin{array}{} V + X X ^ { \prime } & X R ^ { \prime } \\ R X ^ { \prime } & R R ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) ^ { + } \left( \begin{array}{} y - X \beta \\ r - R \beta \end{array} \right) .$$
(8)

Proof. Define

$$y _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) , \quad X _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} X \\ R \end{array} \right) , \quad V _ { e } = \left( \begin{array}{} V & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) , .$$
(9)

and apply Theorem 15 to the extended model $\left( y _ { e } , X _ { e } \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V _ { e } \right) .$
✷
☐


## 19 RESTRICTED LEAST SQUARES

Alternatively, we can use the method of restricted least squares.


### Theorem 16

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $| V | \neq 0$ and $\beta$ sat-
isfies the consistent linear constraints $R \beta = r .$ Let $W$ be a matrix such that
$\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Then the best affine unbiased estimator of $W \beta$ is $W \widehat { \beta } ,$
where $\widehat { \beta }$ is a solution of the constrained minimization problem

minimize
subject to

$$\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \left( y - X \beta \right)$$
(1)

$$R \beta = r .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="320" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The linear regression model [Ch. 13" -->

Proof. From Theorem 11.36 we know that $\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \left( y - X \beta \right)$ is minimized
over all $\beta$ satisfying $R \beta = r ,$ where $\beta$ takes the value

$$\widehat { \beta } = \beta ^ { * } + G ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R G ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( r - R \beta ^ { * } \right) + \left( I - G ^ { + } G \right) q ,$$
(3)

where

$$G = X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X + R ^ { \prime } R , \quad \beta ^ { * } = G ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } y ,$$
(4)

and $q$ is arbitrary. Since $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathcal{M} \left( G \right) ,$ we obtain the
unique expression

$$W \widehat { \beta } = W \beta ^ { * } + W G ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \left( R G ^ { + } R ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( r - R \beta ^ { * } \right)$$
(5)

which is identical to the best affine unbiased estimator of $W \beta$ see Theorem
6.
☐
✷

The model where $V$ is singular can be treated as a special case of the
non-singular model with constraints.


### Corollary 2

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $\beta$ satisfies the con-
sistent linear constraints $R \beta = r ,$ and

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y \\ r \end{array} \right) \in \mathcal{M} \left( \begin{array}{} X & V \\ R & 0 \end{array} \right) \quad \mathrm { a . s . }$$
(6)

Let $W$ be a matrix such that $\mathcal{M} \left( W ^ { \prime } \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( X ^ { \prime } : R ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Then the best affine
unbiased estimator of $W \beta$ is $W \widehat { \beta } ,$ where $\widehat { \beta }$ is a solution of the constrained
minimization problem

minimize
subject to

$$\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } \left( y - X \beta \right)$$
(7)

$$\left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) X \beta = \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) y \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad R \beta = r .$$
(8)

Proof. As in Section 11 we introduce the orthogonal matrix $\left( S : T \right)$ which
diagonalizes $V :$

$$\left( S : T \right) ^ { \prime } V \left( S : T \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \Lambda & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) ,$$
(9)

where $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix containing the positive eigenvalues of $V . \mathrm { T r a n s }$
forming the model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ by means of the matrix $\left( S : T \right) ^ { \prime }$ yields the
equivalent model $\left( S ^ { \prime } y , S ^ { \prime } X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \Lambda \right) ,$ where $\beta$ now satisfies the (implicit) con-
straints $T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y$ in addition to the (explicit) constraints $R \beta = r .$ Condi-
tion (6) shows that the combined constraints are consistent; see Section 14,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="321" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous exercises" -->

Proposition 5. Applying Theorem 16 to the transformed model shows that
the best affine unbiased estimator of $W \beta$ is $W \widehat { \beta }$ where $\widehat { \beta }$ is a solution of the
constrained minimization problem

minimize
subject to

$$\left( S ^ { \prime } y - S ^ { \prime } X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } \Lambda ^ { - 1 } \left( S ^ { \prime } y - S ^ { \prime } X \beta \right)$$
(10)

$$T ^ { \prime } X \beta = T ^ { \prime } y \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad R \beta = r .$$
(11)

It is easy to see that this constrained minimization problem is equivalent to
the constrained minimization problem (7)-(8).
☐
✷

Theorems 15 and 16 and their corollaries prove the striking and by no
means trivial fact that the principle of (restricted) least squares provides best
affine unbiased estimators.


## Exercises

1\. Show that the unconstrained problem

minimize

$$\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } \left( V + X X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } \left( y - X \beta \right)$$

and the constrained problem

minimize
(y - X B)'V+(y - X3)

subject to

$$\left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) X \beta = \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) y$$

have the same solution for $\beta .$

2\. Show further that if $\mathcal{M} \left( X \right) \subset \mathcal{M} \left( V \right) ,$ both problems reduce to the
unconstrained problem of minimizing $\left( y - X \beta \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { + } \left( y - X \beta \right) .$


## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

Consider the model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) .$ Recall from Section 12.11 that the mean
squared error (MSE) matrix of an estimator $\widehat { \beta }$ of $\beta$ is defined as $\mathrm { M S E } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) =$
$\mathcal{E} \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) ^ { \prime } .$

1\. If $\widehat { \beta }$ is a linear estimator, say $\widehat { \beta } = A y ,$ show that

$$\mathrm { M S E } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) = \left( A X - I \right) \beta \beta ^ { \prime } \left( A X - I \right) ^ { \prime } + \sigma ^ { 2 } A V A ^ { \prime } .$$

2\. Let $\phi \left( A \right) = \mathrm { t r } \mathrm { M S E } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right)$ and consider the problem of minimizing $\phi$ with
respect to $A .$ Show that

$$\mathrm { d } \phi = 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } \left( A X - I \right) ^ { \prime } + 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) V A ^ { \prime } ,$$

and obtain the first-order condition

$$\left( \sigma ^ { 2 } V + X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \right) A ^ { \prime } = X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="322" -->
<!-- PageHeader="The linear regression model [Ch. 13" -->

3\. Conclude that the matrix $A$ which minimizes $\phi \left( A \right)$ is a function of the
unknown parameter vector $\beta ,$ unless $\widehat { \beta }$ is unbiased.

4\. Show that

$$\left( \sigma ^ { 2 } V + X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } V + X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } X \beta = X \beta$$

and conclude that the first-order condition is a consistent equation in
$A .$

5\. The matrices $A$ which minimize $\phi \left( A \right)$ are then given by

$$A = \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } C ^ { + } + Q \left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) ,$$

where $C = \sigma ^ { 2 } V + X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime }$ and $Q$ is an arbitrary matrix.

6\. Show that $C C ^ { + } V = V ,$ and hence that $\left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) \epsilon = 0$ a.s.

7\. Conclude from Exercises 4 and 6 above that $\left( I - C C ^ { + } \right) y = 0$ a.s.

8\. The 'estimator' which, in the class of linear estimators, minimizes the
trace of the MSE matrix is therefore

$$\widehat { \beta } = \lambda \beta$$

where

$$\lambda = \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } V + X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } y .$$

9\. Let $\mu = \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } V + X \beta \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { + } X \beta .$ Show that

$$\mathcal{E} \lambda = \mu , \quad \mathcal{V} \left( \lambda \right) = \mu \left( 1 - \mu \right) .$$

10\. Show that $0 \leq \mu \leq 1 ,$ so that $\widehat { \beta }$ will in general 'underestimate' $\beta .$

11\. Discuss the usefulness of the 'estimator' $\widehat { \beta } = \lambda \beta$ in an iterative proce-
dure.


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. The linear model is treated in every econometrics book and in most statis-
tics books. See e.g. Theil (1971) and Rao (1973). The theory originated with
Gauss (1809) and Markov (1900).

§2. See Schönfeld (1971) for an alternative optimality criterion.

§3-§4. See Gauss (1809) and Markov (1900).

§5. See Aitken (1935).

§8. See also Rao (1945).

§11. See Zyskind and Martin (1969) and Albert (1973).

§14. The special case of Theorem 11 where $X$ has full column rank and $R$ has
full row rank was considered by Kreijger and Neudecker (1977).

§16-§17. Theorems 13 and 14 are due to Rao (1971a, 1973) in the context of
a unified theory of linear estimation.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 14 Further topics in the $\mathrm { l i n e a r }$

$$m o d e l$$


### 1 INTRODUCTION

In the preceding chapter we derived the 'best' affine unbiased estimator of $\beta$
in the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ under various assumptions about
the ranks of $X$ and $V .$ In this chapter we discuss some other topics relating
to the linear model.

Sections 2-7 are devoted to constructing the 'best' quadratic estimator of
$\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ The multivariate analogue is discussed in Section 8. The estimator

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - k } y ^ { \prime } \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) y ,$$
(1)

known as the least squares estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ is the best quadratic unbiased
estimator in the model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I \right) .$ But if $\mathcal{V} \left( y \right) \neq \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } ,$ then $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ in (1) will,
in general, be biased. Bounds for this bias which do not depend on $X$ are
obtained in Sections 9 and 10.

The statistical analysis of the disturbances $\epsilon = y - X \beta$ is taken up in
Sections $1 1 - 1 4 ,$ where predictors that are best linear unbiased with scalar
variance matrix (BLUS) and with fixed variance matrix (BLUF) are derived.

Finally, we show how matrix differential calculus can be useful in sensitiv-
ity analysis. In particular, we study the sensitivities of the posterior moments
of $\beta$ in a Bayesian framework.


### 2 BEST QUADRATIC UNBIASED ESTIMATION OF $\sigma ^ { 2 }$

Let $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ be the linear regression model. In the previous chapter we
considered the estimation of $\beta$ as a linear function of the observation vector $y .$
Since the variance $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is a quadratic concept, we now consider the estimation

<!-- PageNumber="323" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="324" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ as a quadratic function of $y ,$ that is, a function of the form

$$y ^ { \prime } A y$$
(1)

where $A$ is non-stochastic and symmetric. Any estimator satisfying (1) is
called a quadratic estimator.

If, in addition, the matrix $A$ is positive (semi)definite and $A V \neq 0 ,$ and if
$y$ is a continuous random vector, then

$$\Pr \left( y ^ { \prime } A y > 0 \right) = 1 ,$$
(2)

and we say that the estimator is quadratic and positive (almost surely).

An unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is an estimator, say $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } ,$ such that

$$\mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \quad \text { for all } \beta \in \mathbb{R} ^ { k } \mathrm { a n d } \sigma ^ { 2 } > 0 .$$
(3)

In (3) it is implicitly assumed that $\beta$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are not restricted (for example,
by $R \beta = r$ apart from the requirement that $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is positive.

We now propose the following definition.


#### Definition 1

The best quadratic (and positive) unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in the linear re-
gression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ is a quadratic (and positive) unbiased estimator
of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ say $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } ,$ such that

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { \tau } ^ { 2 } \right) \geq \mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } \right)$$
(4)

for all quadratic (and positive) unbiased estimators $\widehat { \tau } ^ { 2 }$ of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

In the following two sections we shall derive the best quadratic unbiased
estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ for the normal linear regression model where

$$y \sim \mathcal{N} \left( X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right) ,$$
(5)

first requiring that the estimator is positive, then dropping this requirement.


### 3 THE BEST QUADRATIC AND POSITIVE UNBIASED ESTIMATOR OF $\sigma ^ { 2 }$

Our first result is the following well-known theorem.


#### Theorem 1

The best quadratic and positive unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in the normal linear
regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right)$ is

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - r } y ^ { \prime } \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) y$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="325" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] The best quadratic and positive unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$" -->

where $r$ denotes the rank of $X$ .

Proof. We consider a quadratic estimator of $y ^ { \prime } A y .$ To ensure that the estimator
is positive we write $A = C ^ { \prime } C .$ The problem is to determine an $n \times n$ matrix
$C$ such that $y ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C y$ is unbiased and has the smallest variance in the class of
unbiased estimators.

Unbiasedness requires

$$\mathcal{E} y ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C y = \sigma ^ { 2 } \quad \text { for all } \beta \mathrm { a n d } \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$$
(2)

that is,

$$\beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C X \beta + \sigma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C = \sigma ^ { 2 } \quad \text { for all } \beta \mathrm { a n d } \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$
(3)

This leads to the conditions

$$C X = 0 , \quad \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C = 1 .$$
(4)

Given the condition $C X = 0$ we can write

$$y ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C y = \epsilon ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C \epsilon$$
(5)

where $\epsilon \sim N \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right) ,$ and hence, by Theorem 12.12,

$$\mathcal{V} \left( y ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C y \right) = 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } .$$
(6)

Our optimization problem thus becomes

minimize
subject to

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 }$$
(7)

and tr C'C = 1.
$$C X = 0$$
(8)

To solve (7) and (8) we form the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( C \right) = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \lambda \left( \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C - 1 \right) - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } C X$$
(9)

where $\lambda$ is a Lagrange multiplier and $L$ is a matrix of Lagrange multipliers.
Differentiating $\psi$ gives

$$d \psi = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } C C ^ { \prime } C \left( \mathrm { d } C \right) ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } C \right)$$
$$- \frac { 1 } { 2 } \lambda \left( \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } C \right) ^ { \prime } C + \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } C \right) - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } C \right) X$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } C - \lambda \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } C - \mathrm { t r } X L ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } C ,$$

(10)

so that we obtain as our first-order conditions

$$C ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } = \lambda C ^ { \prime } + X L ^ { \prime }$$
$$\mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C = 1$$
$$C X = 0 .$$
(11)
(12)
(13)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="326" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

Pre-multiplying (11) with $X X ^ { + }$ and using (13) gives

$$X L ^ { \prime } = 0 .$$
(14)

Inserting (14) in (11) gives

$$C ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } = \lambda C ^ { \prime } .$$
(15)
.

Condition (15) implies that $\lambda > 0 .$ Also, defining

$$B = \left( 1 / \lambda \right) C ^ { \prime } C ,$$
(16)

we obtain from (12), (13) and (15),

$$B ^ { 2 } = B$$
$$\mathrm { t r } B = 1 / \lambda$$
$$B X = 0 .$$
(17)
(18)
(19)

Hence $B$ is an idempotent symmetric matrix. Now, since by (12) and (15)

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ,$$
(20)

it appears that we must choose $\lambda$ as small as possible, that $\mathrm { i s } ,$ we must choose
the rank of $B$ as large as possible. The only constraint on the rank of $B$ is
(19), which implies that

$$r \left( B \right) \leq n - r$$
(21)

where $r$ is the rank of $X .$ Since we want to maximize $r \left( B \right)$ we take

$$1 / \lambda = r \left( B \right) = n - r .$$
(22)

From (17), (19) and (21) we find, using Theorem 2.9,

$$B = I _ { n } - X X ^ { + }$$
(23)

and hence

$$A = C ^ { \prime } C = \lambda B = \frac { 1 } { n - r } \left( I _ { n } - X X ^ { + } \right) .$$
(24)

The result follows.

✷

### 4 THE BEST QUADRATIC UNBIASED ESTIMATOR OF $\sigma ^ { 2 }$

The estimator obtained in the preceding section is, in fact, the best in a wider
class of estimators: the class of quadratic unbiased estimators. In other words,
the constraint that $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ be positive is not binding. We thus obtain the following
generalization of Theorem 1.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="327" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] The best quadratic unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$" -->


#### Theorem 2

The best quadratic unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in the normal linear regression
model $\left( y , \bar { X } \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right)$ is

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - r } y ^ { \prime } \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) y$$
(1)

where $r$ denotes the rank of $X .$

$P r o o f .$ Let $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = y ^ { \prime } A y$ be the quadratic estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ and let $\epsilon = y - X \beta \sim$
$\mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right) .$ Then

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A X \beta + 2 \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A \epsilon + \epsilon ^ { \prime } A \epsilon$$
(2)

so that $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ for all $\beta$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ if and only if

$$X ^ { \prime } A X = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathrm { t r } A = 1 .$$
(3)

The variance of $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } \right) = 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } + 2 \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } X \gamma \right)$$
(4)

where $\gamma = \beta / \sigma .$ Hence the optimization problem becomes


<table>
<tr>
<td>minimize</td>
<td>$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } + 2 \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } X \gamma$$ $$X ^ { \prime } A X = 0$$</td>
<td>(5)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>subject to</td>
<td>and tr A=1.</td>
<td>(6)</td>
</tr>
</table>


We notice that the function to be minimized in (5) depends on $\gamma$ so that we
would expect the optimal value of $A$ to depend on $\gamma$ as well. This, however,
turns out not $\mathrm { t o }$ be the case. We form the Lagrangian (taking into account
the symmetry of $A ,$ see Section 3.8)

$$\psi \left( v \left( A \right) \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } + \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } X \gamma - \lambda \left( \mathrm { t r } A - 1 \right) - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A X ,$$
(7)

where $\lambda$ is a Lagrange multiplier and $L$ is a matrix of Lagrange multipli-
ers. Since the constraint function $X ^ { \prime } A X$ is symmetric, we may take $L$ to be
symmetric too (see Exercise 17.9.2).

Differentiating $\psi$ gives

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = \mathrm { t r } A \mathrm { d } A + 2 \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) X \gamma - \lambda \mathrm { t r } \mathrm { d } A - \mathrm { t r } L X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) X$$

$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( A + X \gamma \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A + A X \gamma \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } - \lambda I - X L X ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } A ,$$
(8)

so that the first-order conditions are

$$A - \lambda I _ { n } + A X \gamma \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } + X \gamma \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A = X L X ^ { \prime }$$
$$X ^ { \prime } A X = 0$$
$$A = 1 .$$
(9)
(10)
tr
(11)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="328" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

Pre- and post-multiplying (9) with $X X ^ { + }$ gives, in view of (10),

$$- \lambda X X ^ { + } = X L X ^ { \prime } .$$
(12)

Inserting (12) in (9) we obtain

$$A = \lambda M - P$$
(13)

where

$$M = I _ { n } - X X ^ { + }$$
and
P = AXYY'X' + XYY' X' A.
(14)

Since $\mathrm { t r } P = 0 ,$ because of (10), we have

$$\mathrm { t r } A = \lambda \mathrm { t r } M$$
(15)

and hence

$$\lambda = 1 / \left( n - r \right) .$$
(16)

Also, since

$$M P + P M = P ,$$
(17)

we obtain

$$A ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } M + P ^ { 2 } - \lambda P$$
(18)

so that

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } \mathrm { t r } M + \mathrm { t r } P ^ { 2 }$$

$$= 1 / \left( n - r \right) + 2 \left( \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X \gamma \right) \left( \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } X \gamma \right) .$$
(19)

The objective function (5) can now be written as

$$A ^ { 2 } + 2 \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } X \gamma = 1 / \left( n - r \right) + 2 \left( \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } X \gamma \right) \left( 1 + \gamma ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X \gamma \right) ,$$
tr
(20)

which is minimized for $A X \gamma = 0 ,$ that is, for $P = 0 .$ Inserting $P = 0$ in (13)
and using (16) gives

$$A = \frac { 1 } { n - r } M ,$$
(21)

thus concluding the proof.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="329" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Best quadratic invariant estimation of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$" -->


### 5 BEST QUADRATIC INVARIANT ESTIMATION OF $\sigma ^ { 2 }$

Unbiasedness, though a useful property for linear estimators in linear models,
is somewhat suspect for non-linear estimators. Another, perhaps more useful,
criterion is invariance. In the context of the linear regression model

$$y = X \beta + \epsilon ,$$
(1)

let us consider, instead of $\beta ,$ a translation $\beta - \beta _ { 0 } .$ Then (1) is equivalent to

$$y - X \beta _ { 0 } = X \left( \beta - \beta _ { 0 } \right) + \epsilon ,$$
(2)

and we say that a quadratic estimator $y ^ { \prime } A y$ is invariant $u n d e r$ translation of
$\beta$ if

$$\left( y - X \beta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { \prime } A \left( y - X \beta _ { 0 } \right) = y ^ { \prime } A y \quad \text { for all } \beta _ { 0 } .$$
(3)

This, clearly, is the case if and only if

$$A X = 0 .$$
(4)

We can obtain (4) in another, though closely related, way if we assume $\mathrm { t h a t }$
the disturbance vector $\epsilon$ is normally distributed, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ $V$ positive
definite. Then, by Theorem 12.12,

$$\mathcal{E} \left( y ^ { \prime } A y \right) = \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A X \beta + \sigma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { t r } A V$$
(5)

and

$$\mathcal{V} \left( y ^ { \prime } A y \right) = 4 \sigma ^ { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } A V A X \beta + 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } \mathrm { t r } \left( A V \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$
(6)

so that, under normality, the distribution of $y ^ { \prime } A y$ is independent of $\beta$ if and
only if $A X = 0 .$

If the estimator is biased we replace the minimum variance criterion by
the minimum mean squared error criterion. Thus we obtain Definition 2.


#### Definition 2

The best quadratic (and positive) invariant estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in the linear re-
gression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right)$ is a quadratic (and positive) estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ say
$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } ,$ which is invariant under translation of $\beta ,$ such that

$$\mathcal{E} \left( \widehat { \tau } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \geq \mathcal{E} \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
(7)

for all quadratic (and positive) invariant estimators $\widehat { \tau } ^ { 2 }$ of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

In Sections 6 and 7 we shall derive the best quadratic invariant estimator
of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ assuming normality, first requiring that $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is positive, then that $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is
merely quadratic.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="330" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->


### 6 THE BEST QUADRATIC AND POSITIVE INVARIANT ESTIMATOR OF $\sigma ^ { 2 }$

Given invariance instead of unbiasedness we obtain Theorem 3 instead of
Theorem 1.


#### Theorem 3

The best quadratic and positive invariant estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in the normal linear
regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right)$ is

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - r + 2 } y ^ { \prime } \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) y$$
(1)

where $r$ denotes the rank of $X$ .

Proof. Again, let $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = y ^ { \prime } A y$ be the quadratic estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ and write $A =$
$C ^ { \prime } C .$ Invariance requires $C ^ { \prime } C X = 0 ,$ that is,

$$C X = 0 .$$
(2)

Letting $\epsilon = y - X \beta \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right) ,$ the estimator for $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ can be written as

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \epsilon ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } C \epsilon$$
(3)

so that the mean squared error becomes

$$\mathcal{E} \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 4 } \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } .$$
(4)

The minimization problem is thus

minimize
subject to

$$\left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$C X = 0 .$$
(5)
(6)

The Lagrangian is

$$\psi \left( C \right) = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } C \right) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } C X ,$$
(7)

where $L$ is a matrix of Lagrange multipliers, leading to the first-order condi-
tions

$$2 C ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } - \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C \right) C ^ { \prime } = X L ^ { \prime }$$
$$C X = 0 .$$
(8)
(9)

Pre-multiplying both sides of (8) with $X X ^ { + }$ gives, in view of (9),

$$X L ^ { \prime } = 0 .$$
(10)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="331" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7] The best quadratic invariant estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$" -->

Inserting (10) in (8) gives

$$2 C ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } = \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C \right) C ^ { \prime } .$$
(11)

Now define

$$B = \left( \frac { 2 } { 1 - \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C } \right) C ^ { \prime } C = \left( \frac { 2 } { 1 - \mathrm { t r } A } \right) A .$$
(12)

(Notice that $\mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C \neq 1$ (why?).) Then, from (9) and (11),

$$B ^ { 2 } = B$$
$$B X = 0 .$$
(13)
(14)

We also obtain from (12),

$$\mathrm { t r } A = \frac { \mathrm { t r } B } { 2 + \mathrm { t r } B } , \quad \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } = \frac { \mathrm { t r } B ^ { 2 } } { \left( 2 + \mathrm { t r } B \right) ^ { 2 } } .$$
(15)

Let $\rho$ denote the rank of $B .$ Then tr $B = \mathrm { t r } B ^ { 2 } = \rho$ and hence

$$\frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } A \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 2 + \rho \right) } .$$
(16)

The left-hand side of (16) is the function we wish to minimize. Therefore we
must choose $\rho$ as large as possible, and hence, in view of (14),

$$\rho = n - r .$$
(17)

From (13), (14) and (17) we find, using Theorem 2.9,

$$B = I _ { n } - X X ^ { + }$$
(18)

and hence

$$A = \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 + \mathrm { t r } B } \right) B = \frac { 1 } { n - r + 2 } \left( I _ { n } - X X ^ { + } \right) .$$
(19)

This concludes the proof.

✷
☐


### 7 THE BEST QUADRATIC INVARIANT ESTIMATOR OF $\sigma ^ { 2 }$

A generalization of Theorem 2 is obtained by dropping the requirement that
the quadratic estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ be positive. In this wider class of estimators we
find that the estimator of Theorem 3 is again the best (smallest mean squared
error), thus showing that the requirement of positiveness is not binding.

Comparing Theorems 2 and 4 we see that the best quadratic invariant
estimator has a larger bias (it underestimates $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ but a smaller variance than

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="332" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

the best quadratic unbiased estimator, and altogether a smaller mean squared
error.


#### Theorem 4

The best quadratic invariant estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in the normal linear regression
model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right)$ is

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - r + 2 } y ^ { \prime } \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) y$$
(1)

where $r$ denotes the rank of $X$ .

Proof. Here we must solve the problem

$$\mathrm { m i n i m i z e } \quad \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } A \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 }$$
$$A X = 0 .$$
(2)
subject to
(3)

This is the same as in the proof of Theorem 3, except that $A$ is only symmetric
and not necessarily positive definite. The Lagrangian is

$$\psi \left( v \left( A \right) \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } A \right) ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } A X$$
(4)

and the first-order conditions are

$$2 A - \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } A \right) I _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( X L ^ { \prime } + L X ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(5)

$$A X = 0 .$$
(6)

Pre-multiplying (5) with $A$ gives, in view of (6),

$$2 A ^ { 2 } - \left( 1 - \mathrm { t r } A \right) A = \frac { 1 } { 2 } A L X ^ { \prime } .$$
(7)

Post-multiplying (7) with $X X ^ { + }$ gives, again using (6), $A L X ^ { \prime } = 0 .$ Inserting
$A L X ^ { \prime } = 0$ in (7) then shows that the matrix

$$B = \left( \frac { 2 } { 1 - \mathrm { t r } A } \right) A$$
(8)

is symmetric idempotent. Furthermore, by (6), $B X = 0 .$ The remainder of
the proof follows in the same way as in the proof of Theorem 3 (from (15)
onwards).
✷
☐


### 8 BEST QUADRATIC UNBIASED ESTIMATION: MULTIVARIATE NORMAL CASE

Extending Definition 1 to the multivariate case we obtain Definition 3.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="333" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] Best quadratic unbiased estimation: multivariate normal case" -->


#### Definition 3

Let $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be a random sample from an m-dimensional distribution
with positive definite variance matrix $\Omega .$ Let $Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ The best
quadratic unbiased estimator of $\Omega ,$ say $\widehat { \Omega } ,$ is a quadratic estimator (that is, an
estimator of the form $Y ^ { \prime } A Y$ where $A$ is symmetric) such that $\widehat { \Omega }$
and

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \mathrm { v e c } \widehat { \Psi } \right) \geq \mathcal{V} \left( \mathrm { v e c } \widehat { \Omega } \right)$$
(1)

for all quadratic unbiased estimators of $\widehat { \Psi }$ of $\Omega .$

We can now generalize Theorem 2 to the multivariate case. We see again
that the estimator is positive semidefinite, even though this was not required.


#### Theorem 5

Let $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be a random sample from the m-dimensional normal dis-
tribution with mean $\mu$ and positive definite variance matrix $\Omega .$ Let $Y =$
$\left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ The best quadratic unbiased estimator of $\Omega$ is

$$\widehat { \Omega } = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } Y ^ { \prime } \left( I _ { n } - \frac { 1 } { n } \imath ^ { \prime } \right) Y$$
(2)

where, as always, $\imath = \left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime } .$

Proof. Consider a quadratic estimator $Y ^ { \prime } A Y .$ From Chapter 12 (Miscellaneous
Exercise 2) we know that

$$\mathcal{E} Y ^ { \prime } A Y = \left( \mathrm { t r } A \right) \Omega + \left( \imath ^ { \prime } A \imath \right) \mu \mu ^ { \prime }$$
(3)

and

$$\mathcal{V} \left( \mathrm { v e c } Y ^ { \prime } A Y \right) = \left( I + K _ { m } \right) \left( \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \Omega \otimes \Omega \right) + \left( \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath \right) \left( \Omega \otimes \mu \mu ^ { \prime } + \mu \mu ^ { \prime } \otimes \Omega \right) \right)$$

$$= \left( I + K _ { m } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \Omega \otimes \Omega \right) + \left( \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath \right) \left( \Omega \otimes \mu \mu ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \left( I + K _ { m } \right) .$$
(4)

The estimator $Y ^ { \prime } A Y$ is unbiased if and only if

$$\left( \mathrm { t r } A \right) \Omega + \left( \imath ^ { \prime } A \imath \right) \mu \mu ^ { \prime } = \Omega \quad \text { for all } \mu \mathrm { a n d } \Omega ,$$
(5)

that is,

$$\mathrm { t r } A = 1 , \quad \imath ^ { \prime } A \imath = 0 .$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="334" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

Let $T \neq 0$ be an arbitrary $m \times m$ matrix and let $\widetilde { T } = T + T ^ { \prime } .$ Then
˜

$$\left( \mathrm { v e c } T \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathcal{V} \left( \mathrm { v e c } Y ^ { \prime } A Y \right) \right) \mathrm { v e c } T$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { v e c } \widetilde { T } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \Omega \otimes \Omega \right) + \left( \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath \right) \left( \Omega \otimes \mu \mu ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \mathrm { v e c } ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { t r } \widetilde { T } \Omega \widetilde { T } \Omega \right) + \left( \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath \right) \mu ^ { \prime } \widetilde { T } \Omega \widetilde { T } \mu$$
$$= \alpha \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } + \beta \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath ,$$

˜

(7)

where

$$\alpha = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \widetilde { T } \Omega \widetilde { T } \Omega$$
and

$$\beta = \mu ^ { \prime } \widetilde { T } \Omega \widetilde { T } \mu .$$
(8)

Consider now the optimization problem

minimize

$$\alpha \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } + \beta \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath$$
(9)

$$\text { subject to } \quad \mathrm { t r } A = 1 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \imath ^ { \prime } A \imath = 0 ,$$
(10)

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are fixed numbers. If the optimal matrix $A ,$ which minimizes
(9) subject to (10), does not depend on $\alpha$ and $\beta$ - and this will turn out
to be the case - then this matrix $A$ must be the $\mathrm { b e s t }$ quadratic unbiased
estimator according to Definition 3.

Define the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( A \right) = \alpha \mathrm { t r } A ^ { 2 } + \beta \imath ^ { \prime } A ^ { 2 } \imath - \lambda _ { 1 } \left( \mathrm { t r } A - 1 \right) - \lambda _ { 2 } \imath ^ { \prime } A \imath ,$$
(11)

where $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ are Lagrange multipliers. Differentiating $\psi$ gives

$$d \psi = 2 \alpha \mathrm { t r } A \mathrm { d } A + 2 \beta \imath ^ { \prime } A \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) \imath - \lambda _ { 1 } \mathrm { t r } \mathrm { d } A - \lambda _ { 2 } \imath ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) \imath$$

$$= \mathrm { t r } \left[ 2 \alpha A + \beta \left( \imath \imath ^ { \prime } A + A \imath \imath ^ { \prime } \right) - \lambda _ { 1 } I - \lambda _ { 2 } \imath ^ { \prime } \right] \mathrm { d } A .$$
(12)

Since the matrix in square brackets in (12) is symmetric, we do not have to
impose the symmetry condition on $A .$ Thus we find the first-order conditions

$$2 \alpha A + \beta \left( \imath \imath ^ { \prime } A + A \imath \imath ^ { \prime } \right) - \lambda _ { 1 } I _ { n } - \lambda _ { 2 } \imath \imath ^ { \prime } = 0$$
$$A = 1$$
(13)
tr
(14)

$$\imath ^ { \prime } A \imath = 0 .$$
(15)

Taking the trace in (13) yields

$$2 \alpha = n \left( \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } \right) .$$
(16)

Pre- and post-multiplying (13) with 2 gives

$$\lambda _ { 1 } + n \lambda _ { 2 } = 0 .$$
(17)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="335" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] Bounds for the bias of the least squares estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $I$" -->

Hence

$$\lambda _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 \alpha } { n - 1 } , \quad \lambda _ { 2 } = \frac { - 2 \alpha } { n \left( n - 1 \right) } .$$
(18)

Post-multiplying (13) with 2 gives, in view of (17),

$$\left( 2 \alpha + n \beta \right) A \imath = 0 .$$
(19)

Since $\alpha > 0$ (why?) and $\beta \geq 0$ we obtain

$$A \imath = 0$$
(20)

and hence

$$A = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \left( I _ { n } - \frac { 1 } { n } u ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(21)

As the objective function (9) is strictly convex, this solution provides the
required minimum.
☐
✷


### 9 BOUNDS FOR THE BIAS OF THE LEAST SQUARES ESTIMATOR OF $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ I

Let us again consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ where $X$ has
full column rank $k$ and $V$ is positive semidefinite.

If $V = I _ { n } ,$ then we know from Theorem 2 that

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - k } y ^ { \prime } \left( I _ { n } - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) y$$
(1)

is the best quadratic unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ also known as the least squares
(LS) estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ If $V \neq I _ { n } ,$ then (1) is no longer an unbiased estimator
of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ because, in general,

$$\mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n - k } \mathrm { t r } \left( I _ { n } - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) V \neq \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$
(2)

If both $V$ and $X$ are known, we can calculate the relative bias

$$\frac { \mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } }$$
(3)

exactly. Here we are concerned with the case where $V$ is known (at least in
structure, say first-order autocorrelation) while $X$ is not known. Of course we
cannot calculate the exact relative bias in this case. We can, however, find a
lower and an upper bound for the relative bias of $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ over all possible values
of $X .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="336" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->


### Theorem 6

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $V$ is a positive
semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$ and $X$ is
a non-stochastic $n \times k$ matrix of rank $k .$ Let $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ be the least squares estimator
of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - k } y ^ { \prime } \left( I _ { n } - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) y .$$
(4)

Then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \frac { \left( n - k \right) \mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \leq \sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } .$$
(5)

$P r o o f .$ Let $M = I - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } .$ Then

$$\mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n - k } \mathrm { t r } M V = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n - k } \mathrm { t r } M V M .$$
(6)

Now, $M$ is an idempotent $n \times n$ matrix of rank $n - \quad k .$ Let us denote
the eigenvalues of $M V M ,$ apart from $k$ zeros, by

$$\mu _ { 1 } \leq \mu _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \mu _ { n - k } .$$
(7)

Then, by Theorem 11.11,

$$\lambda _ { i } \leq \mu _ { i } \leq \lambda _ { k + i } \quad \left( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n - k \right)$$
(8)

and $h e n c e$

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \mu _ { i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { k + i }$$
(9)

and the result follows.

✷
☐


### 10 BOUNDS FOR THE BIAS OF THE LEAST SQUARES ESTIMATOR OF $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $I I$

Suppose now that $X$ is not completely unknown. In particular, suppose that
the regression contains a constant term, so that $X$ contains a column of ones.
Surely this additional information must lead to a tighter interval for the rel-
ative bias of $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } .$ Theorem 7 shows that this is indeed the case. Somewhat
surprisingly perhaps only the upper bound of the relative bias is affected, not
the lower bound.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="337" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Bounds for the bias of the least squares estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $I I$" -->


#### Theorem 7

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $V$ is a positive
semidefinite $n \times n$ matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ,$ and $X$ is
a non-stochastic $n \times k$ matrix of rank $k .$ Assume that $X$ contains a column
$\imath = \left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime } .$ Let $A = I _ { n } - \left( 1 / n \right) v l ^ { \prime }$ and let $0 = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { * } \leq \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { * } \leq \cdots \leq \lambda _ { n } ^ { * }$ be the
eigenvalues of $A V \quad A .$ Let $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ be the least squares estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ that is

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - k } y ^ { \prime } \left( I _ { n } - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) y .$$
(1)

Then

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \frac { \left( n - k \right) \mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \leq \sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { * } .$$
(2)

Proof. Let $M = I _ { n } - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } .$ Since $M A = M$ we have $M V M =$
$M A V \quad A M$ and hence

$$\mathcal{E} \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n - k } \mathrm { t r } M V M = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n - k } \mathrm { t r } M A V A M .$$
(3)

We obtain, just as in the proof of Theorem 6,

$$\sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } ^ { * } \leq \mathrm { t r } M A V A M \leq \sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { * } .$$
(4)

We also have, by Theorem 6,

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \mathrm { t r } M A V A M \leq \sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } .$$
(5)

In order to select the smallest upper bound and largest lower bound we use
the inequality

$$\lambda _ { i } \leq \lambda _ { i + 1 } ^ { * } \leq \lambda _ { i + 1 } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n - 1 \right) ,$$
(6)

which follows from Theorem 11.11. We then find

$$\sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } ^ { * } \leq \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i }$$
(7)

and

$$\sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { * } \leq \sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ,$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="338" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

so that

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \lambda _ { i } \leq \mathrm { t r } M A V A M \leq \sum _ { i = k + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { * } .$$
(9)

The result follows.
☐
✷


### 11 THE PREDICTION OF DISTURBANCES

Let us write the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } \right)$ as

$$y = X \beta + \epsilon , \quad \mathcal{E} \epsilon = 0 , \quad \mathcal{E} \epsilon \epsilon ^ { \prime } = \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } .$$
(1)

We have seen how the unknown parameters $\beta$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ can be optimally es-
timated by linear or quadratic functions of $y .$ We now turn our attention to
the 'estimation' of the disturbance vector e. Since $\epsilon$ (unlike $\beta$ is a random
vector, it cannot, strictly speaking, be estimated. Furthermore, $\epsilon$ (unlike y) is
unobservable.

If we try to find an observable random vector, say e, which approximates
the unobservable $\epsilon$ as closely as possible in some sense, it is appealing to
minimize

$$\mathcal{E} \left( e - \epsilon \right) ^ { \prime } \left( e - \epsilon \right)$$
(2)

subject to the constraints

(i) (linearity)

$$e = A y$$
for some square matrix $A ,$
for all 3.
(4)

(ii) (unbiasedness)
$\mathcal{E} \left( e - \epsilon \right) = 0$
(3)

This leads to the best linear unbiased predictor of €,

$$e = \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) y ,$$
(5)

which we recognize as the least squares residual vector (see Exercises 1 and
2).

A major drawback of the best linear unbiased predictor given in (5) is that
its variance matrix is non-scalar. In fact,

$$\mathcal{V} \left( e \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( I - X X ^ { + } \right) ,$$
(6)

whereas the variance matrix of e, which $\epsilon$ hopes to resemble, is $\sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } .$ This
drawback is especially serious $\mathrm { i f }$ we wish to use $e$ in testing the hypothesis
$\mathcal{V} \left( \epsilon \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { n } .$

For this reason we wish to find a predictor of $\epsilon$ (or more generally, $S \epsilon$
$w h i c h ,$ in addition to being linear and unbiased, has a scalar variance matrix.


#### Exercises

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="339" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Best linear unbiased predictors with scalar variance matrix" -->

1\. Show that the minimization problem (2) subject to (3) and (4) amounts
to

$$\mathrm { m i n i m i z e } \quad \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } A - 2 \mathrm { t r } A$$
$$A X = 0 .$$
subject to

2\. Solve this problem and show that the minimizer $\widehat { A }$ satisfies

$$\widehat { A } = I - X X ^ { + } .$$

3\. Show that, while $\epsilon$ is unobservable, certain linear combinations of $\epsilon$ are
observable. In fact, show that $c ^ { \prime } \epsilon$ is observable if and only if $X ^ { \prime } c = 0 ,$ in
which case $c ^ { \prime } \epsilon = c ^ { \prime } y .$


### 12 BEST LINEAR UNBIASED PREDICTORS WITH SCALAR VARIANCE MATRIX

Thus motivated, we propose the following definition of the predictor of $S \epsilon$
that is best linear unbiased with scalar variance matrix (BLUS).


#### Definition 4

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I \right) .$ Let $S$ be a given $m \times n$
matrix. A random $m \times 1$ vector $w$ will be called a $B L U S$ predictor of $S \epsilon$ if

$$\mathcal{E} \left( w - S \epsilon \right) ^ { \prime } \left( w - S \epsilon \right)$$
(1)

is minimized subject to the constraints

(i) (linearity)

$$w = A y$$

(ii) (unbiasedness)

$$\mathcal{E} \left( w - S \epsilon \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } \beta ,$$

(iii) (scalar variance matrix)
$\mathcal{V} \left( w \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } I _ { m } .$
for some $m \times n$ matrix $A ,$

Our next task, of course, is to find the BLUS predictor of $S \epsilon .$


### Theorem 8

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I \right)$ and let $M = I - X X ^ { + } .$ Let
$S$ be a given $m \times n$ matrix such that

$$r \left( S M S ^ { \prime } \right) = m .$$
(2)

Then the BLUS predictor of $S \epsilon$ is

$$\left( S M S ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } S M y ,$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="340" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

where $\left( S M S ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ is the positive definite square root of $\left( S M S ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$

Proof. We seek a linear predictor $w$ of $S \epsilon ,$ that is a predictor of the form

$$w = A y$$
(4)

where $A$ is a constant $m \times n$ matrix. Unbiasedness of the prediction error
requires

$$0 = \mathcal{E} \left( A y - S \epsilon \right) = A X \beta \quad \text { for all } \beta \mathrm { i n } \mathrm { R } ^ { k } ,$$
(5)

which yields

$$A X = 0 .$$
(6)

The variance matrix of $w$ is

$$\mathcal{E} w w ^ { \prime } = \sigma ^ { 2 } A A ^ { \prime } .$$
(7)

In order to satisfy condition (iii) of Definition 4, we thus require

$$A A ^ { \prime } = I .$$
(8)

Under the constraints (6) and (8), the prediction error variance is

$$\mathcal{V} \left( A y - S \epsilon \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( I + S S ^ { \prime } - A S ^ { \prime } - S A ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(9)

Hence the BLUS predictor of $S \epsilon$ is obtained by minimizing the trace of (9)
with respect to $A$ subject to the constraints (6) and (8). This amounts to
solving the problem

maximize
subject to

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( A S ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$A X = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d }$$
(10)
AA' = I.
(11)

We define the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( A \right) = \mathrm { t r } A S ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A X - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } L _ { 2 } \left( A A ^ { \prime } - I \right)$$
(12)

where $L _ { 1 }$ and $L _ { 2 }$ are matrices of Lagrange multipliers and $L _ { 2 }$ is symmetric.
Differentiating $\psi$ with respect to $A$ yields

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) S ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) X - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } L _ { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) A ^ { \prime } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } L _ { 2 } A \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } S ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } A - \mathrm { t r } X L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } A - \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } \mathrm { d } A .$$
(13)

The first-order conditions are

$$S ^ { \prime } = X L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 }$$
(14)

$$A X = 0$$
$$A A ^ { \prime } = I .$$
(15)
(16)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="341" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Best linear unbiased predictors with fixed variance matrix, $I$" -->

Pre-multiplying (14) with $X X ^ { + }$ yields

$$X L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = X X ^ { + } S ^ { \prime }$$
(17)

because $X ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } = 0$ in view of (15). Inserting (17) in (14) gives

$$M S ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } .$$
(18)

Also, pre-multiplying (14) with $A$ gives

$$A S ^ { \prime } = S A ^ { \prime } = L _ { 2 }$$
(19)

in view of (15) and (16) and the symmetry of $L _ { 2 } .$ Pre-multiplying (18) with
$S$ and using (19) we find

$$S M S ^ { \prime } = L _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$$
(20)

and hence

$$L _ { 2 } = \left( S M S ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$
(21)

It follows from (10) and (19) that our objective is to maximize the trace of $L _ { 2 } .$
Therefore we must choose in (21) the positive definite square root of $S M S ^ { \prime } .$
Inserting (21) in (18) yields

$$A = \left( S M S ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } S M .$$
(22)

The result follows.

✷
