### 13 BEST LINEAR UNBIASED PREDICTORS WITH FIXED VARIANCE MATRIX, I

We can generalize the BLUS approach in two directions. First, we may assume
that the variance matrix of the linear unbiased predictor is not scalar, but
some fixed known positive semidefinite matrix, say $\Omega .$ This is useful, because
for many purposes the requirement that the variance matrix of the predictor is
scalar is unnecessary; it is sufficient that the variance matrix does not depend
on $X .$

Secondly, we may wish to generalize the criterion function to

$$\mathcal{E} \left( w - S \epsilon \right) ^ { \prime } Q \left( w - S \epsilon \right) ,$$
(1)

where $Q$ is some given positive definite matrix.


#### Definition 5

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right)$ where $V$ is a given positive
definite $n \times n$ matrix. Let $S$ be a given $m \times n$ matrix, $\Omega$ a given positive

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="342" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

semidefinite $m \times m$ matrix and $Q$ a given positive definite $m \times m$ matrix. A
random $m \times 1$ vector $w$ will be called a BLUF $\left( \Omega , Q \right)$ predictor of $S \epsilon$ if

$$\mathcal{E} \left( w - S \epsilon \right) ^ { \prime } Q \left( w - S \epsilon \right)$$
(2)

is minimized subject to the constraints

(i) (linearity)

$$w = A y$$

(ii) (unbiasedness)

$$\mathcal{E} \left( w - S \epsilon \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } \beta ,$$

(iii) (fixed variance matrix)
$\mathcal{V} \left( w \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } \Omega .$
for some $m \times n$ matrix $A ,$

In Theorem 9 we consider the first generalization where the criterion func-
tion is unchanged, but where the variance matrix of the predictor is assumed
to be some fixed known positive semidefinite matrix.


#### Theorem 9

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } I \right)$ and let $M = I - X X ^ { + } .$ Let
$S$ be a given $m \times n$ matrix and $\Omega$ a given positive semidefinite $m \times m$ matrix
such that

$$r \left( S M S ^ { \prime } \Omega \right) = r \left( \Omega \right) .$$
(3)

Then the BLUF $\left( \Omega , I _ { m } \right)$ predictor of $S \epsilon$ is

$$P Z ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { \prime } S M y , \quad Z = P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P ,$$
(4)

where $P$ is a matrix with full column rank satisfying $P P ^ { \prime } = \Omega$ and $Z ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ is
the positive definite square root of $Z ^ { - 1 } .$

Proof. Proceeding as in the proof of Theorem 8, we seek a linear predictor $A y$
of $S \epsilon$ such that

$$\mathrm { t r } \mathcal{V} \left( A y - S \epsilon \right)$$
(5)

is minimized subject to the conditions

$$\mathcal{E} \left( A y - S \epsilon \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } \beta \mathrm { i n } \mathrm { R } ^ { k }$$
(6)

and

$$\mathcal{V} \left( A y \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } \Omega .$$
(7)

This leads to the maximization problem

maximize

tr AS'

subject to

(8)
and AA' = 22.
$$A X = 0$$

(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="343" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] Best linear unbiased predictors with fixed variance matrix, $I$" -->

The first-order conditions are

$$S ^ { \prime } = X L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 }$$
$$A X = 0$$
$$A A ^ { \prime } = \Omega ,$$
(10)
(11)
(12)

where $L _ { 1 }$ and $L _ { 2 }$ are matrices of Lagrange multipliers and $L _ { 2 }$ is symmetric.
Pre-multiplying (10) with $X X ^ { + }$ and $A ,$ respectively, yields

$$X L _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = X X ^ { + } S ^ { \prime }$$
(13)

and

$$A S ^ { \prime } = \Omega L _ { 2 } ,$$
(14)

in view of (11) and (12). Inserting (13) in (10) gives

$$M S ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } .$$
(15)

Hence,

$$S M S ^ { \prime } = S A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } = L _ { 2 } \Omega L _ { 2 } = L _ { 2 } P P ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 }$$
(16)

using (15), (14) and the fact that $\Omega = P P ^ { \prime } .$ This gives

$$P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P = \left( P ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } P \right) ^ { 2 }$$
(17)

and hence

$$P ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } P = \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$
(18)

By assumption, the matrix $P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P$ is positive definite. Also, it follows
from (8) and (14) that we must maximize the trace of $P ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } P ,$ so that we
must choose the positive definite square root of $P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P .$

So far the proof is very similar to the proof of Theorem 8. However, con-
trary to that proof we now cannot obtain $A$ directly from (15) and (18).
Instead, we proceed as follows. From (15), (12) and (18) we have

$$A M S ^ { \prime } P = A A ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } P = P P ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } P = P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$
(19)

The general solution for $A$ in (19) is

$$A = P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { + } + Q \left( I - M S ^ { \prime } P \left( M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { + } \right)$$

$$= P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { \prime } S M + Q \left( I - M S ^ { \prime } P \left( P M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { - 1 } P ^ { \prime } S M \right)$$
(20)

where $Q$ is an arbitrary $m \times n$ matrix. From (20) we $\mathrm { o b t a i n }$

$$A A ^ { \prime } = P P ^ { \prime } + Q \left( I - M S ^ { \prime } P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { - 1 } P ^ { \prime } S M \right) Q ^ { \prime }$$
(21)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="344" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

and hence, in view of (12),

$$Q \left( I - M S ^ { \prime } P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { - 1 } P ^ { \prime } S M \right) Q ^ { \prime } = 0 .$$
(22)

Since the matrix in the middle is idempotent, (22) implies

$$Q \left( I - M S ^ { \prime } P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { - 1 } P ^ { \prime } S M \right) = 0$$
(23)

and hence, from (20),

$$A = P \left( P ^ { \prime } S M S ^ { \prime } P \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { \prime } S M .$$
(24)

This concludes the proof.

✷

### 14 BEST LINEAR UNBIASED PREDICTORS WITH FIXED VARIANCE MATRIX, $I I$

Let us now present the full generalization of Theorem 8.


#### Theorem 10

Consider the linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } V \right) ,$ where $V$ is positive defi-
nite, and let

$$R = V - X \left( X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { + } X ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)

Let $S$ be a given $m \times n$ matrix and $\Omega$ a given positive semidefinite $m \times m$
matrix such that

$$r \left( S R S ^ { \prime } \Omega \right) = r \left( \Omega \right) .$$
(2)

Then, for any positive definite $m \times m$ matrix $Q ,$ the $\mathrm { B L U F } \left( \Omega , Q \right)$ predictor
of $S \epsilon$ is

$$P Z ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { \prime } Q S R V ^ { - 1 } y , \quad Z = P ^ { \prime } Q S R S ^ { \prime } Q P ,$$
(3)

where $P$ is a matrix with full column rank satisfying $P P ^ { \prime } = \Omega$ and $Z ^ { - 1 / 2 }$
denotes the positive definite square root of $Z ^ { - 1 } .$

Proof. The maximization problem amounts to

maximize
subject to

$$\mathrm { t r } Q A V S ^ { \prime }$$
(4)

AVA' = 2.
$$A X = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d }$$
(5)

We define

$$A ^ { * } = Q A V ^ { 1 / 2 } , \quad S ^ { * } = S V ^ { 1 / 2 } , \quad X ^ { * } = V ^ { - 1 / 2 } X ,$$
$$\Omega ^ { * } = Q \Omega Q , \quad P ^ { * } = Q P , \quad M ^ { * } = I - X ^ { * } X ^ { * + } .$$
(6)
(7)

<!-- PageNumber="345" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 15 ] Local sensitivity of the posterior mean" -->

Then we rewrite the maximization problem (4) subject to (5) as a maximiza-
tion problem in $A ^ { * } :$

$$\mathrm { t r } A ^ { * } S ^ { * \prime }$$
$$\text { subject to } \quad A ^ { * } X ^ { * } = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad A ^ { * } A ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } = \Omega ^ { * } .$$
maximize
(8)
(9)

We know from Theorem 9 that the solution is

$$A ^ { * } = P ^ { * } \left( P ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } S ^ { * } M ^ { * } S ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } P ^ { * } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } S ^ { * } M ^ { * } .$$
(10)

Hence, writing $M ^ { * } = V ^ { - 1 / 2 } R V ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$ we obtain

$$Q A V ^ { 1 / 2 } = Q P \left( P ^ { \prime } Q S R S ^ { \prime } Q P \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { \prime } Q S R V ^ { - 1 / 2 }$$
(11)

and thus

$$A = P \left( P ^ { \prime } Q S R S ^ { \prime } Q P \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } P ^ { \prime } Q S R V ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(12)

which completes the proof.

### 15 LOCAL SENSITIVITY OF THE POSTERIOR MEAN

Let $\left( y , X \beta , V \right)$ be the normal linear regression model where $V$ is positive
definite. Suppose, however, that there is prior information concerning $\beta :$

$$\beta \sim \mathcal{N} \left( b ^ { * } , H ^ { * } { } ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$
(1)

Then, as Leamer (1978, p. 76) shows, the posterior distribution of $\beta$ is

$$\beta \sim \mathcal{N} \left( b , H ^ { - 1 } \right)$$
(2)

with

$$b = H ^ { - 1 } \left( H ^ { * } b ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } y \right)$$
(3)

and

$$H = H ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X .$$
(4)

We are interested in the effects of small changes in the precision matrix $V ^ { - 1 } ,$
the design matrix $X$ and the prior moments $b ^ { * }$ and $\widetilde { H ^ { * } } ^ { - 1 }$ on the posterior
mean $b$ and the posterior precision $H ^ { - 1 } .$

We first study the effects on the posterior mean.


#### Theorem 11

Consider the normal linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , V \right) ,$ $V$ positive definite,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="346" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Further topics in the linear model [Ch. 14" -->

with prior information $\beta \sim \mathcal{N} \left( b ^ { * } , H ^ { * - 1 } \right) .$ The local sensitivities of the poste-
rior mean $b$ given in (3) with respect to $V ^ { - 1 } ,$ $X ,$ and the prior moments $b ^ { * }$
and $H ^ { * - 1 }$ are

$$\partial b / \partial \left( v \left( V ^ { - 1 } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } = \left[ \left( y - X b \right) ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right] D _ { n }$$
(5)

$$\partial b / \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } = H ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( y - X b \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } - b ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\partial b / \partial b ^ { * \prime } = H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * }$$
$$\partial b / \partial \left( v \left( H ^ { * - 1 } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } = \left[ \left( b - b ^ { * } \right) ^ { \prime } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right] D _ { k } .$$
(6)
(7)
(8)

Note. The matrices $D _ { n }$ and $D _ { k }$ are 'duplication' matrices. See Section 3.8.

Proof. We have, letting $e = y \quad - \quad X b ,$

$$\mathrm { d } b = \left( \mathrm { d } H ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( H ^ { * } b ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } y \right) + H ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \left( H ^ { * } b ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } y \right)$$
$$= - H ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } H \right) b + H ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \left( H ^ { * } b ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } y \right)$$
$$= - H ^ { - 1 } \left[ \mathrm { d } H ^ { * } + \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X + X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } V ^ { - 1 } \right) X \right] b$$
$$+ H ^ { - 1 } \left[ \left( \mathrm { d } H ^ { * } \right) b ^ { * } + H ^ { * } \mathrm { d } b ^ { * } + \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } y + X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } V ^ { - 1 } \right) y \right]$$
$$= H ^ { - 1 } \left[ \left( \mathrm { d } H ^ { * } \right) \left( b ^ { * } - b \right) + H ^ { * } \mathrm { d } b ^ { * } + \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } e \right.$$
$$\left. - X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) b + X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } V ^ { - 1 } \right) e \right]$$
$$= H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } H ^ { * - 1 } \right) H ^ { * } \left( b - b ^ { * } \right) + H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \mathrm { d } b ^ { * }$$
$$+ H ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } e - H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) b + H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } V ^ { - 1 } \right) e$$
$$= \left[ \left( b - b ^ { * } \right) ^ { \prime } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } H ^ { * - 1 } + H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \mathrm { d } b ^ { * }$$
$$+ \mathrm { v e c } e ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) H ^ { - 1 } - \left( b ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$
$$+ \left[ e ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } V ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \left[ \left( b - b ^ { * } \right) ^ { \prime } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right] D _ { k } d v \left( H ^ { * } { } ^ { - 1 } \right) + H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } d b ^ { * }$$
$$+ \left[ H ^ { - 1 } \otimes e ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } - b ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$
$$+ \left[ e ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right] D _ { n } d v \left( V ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$

The results follow.


#### Exercise

1\. Show that the local sensitivity of the least squares estimator $b = \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } y$
with respect to $X$ is given by

$$\frac { \partial b } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } } = \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( y - X b \right) ^ { \prime } - b ^ { \prime } \otimes \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } .$$

### 16 LOCAL SENSITIVITY OF THE POSTERIOR PRECISION

In precisely the same manner we can obtain the local sensitivity of the pos-
terior precision.


#### Theorem 12

Consider the normal linear regression model $\left( y , X \beta , V \right) ,$ $V$ positive definite,
with prior information $\beta \sim \mathcal{N} \left( b ^ { * } , H ^ { * - 1 } \right) .$ The local sensitivities of the poste-
rior precision matrix $H ^ { - 1 }$ given by

$$H ^ { - 1 } = \left( H ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
(1)

with respect to $V ^ { - 1 } ,$ $X$ and the prior moments $b ^ { * }$ and $H ^ { * - 1 }$ are

$$\partial v \left( H ^ { - 1 } \right) / \partial \left( v \left( V ^ { - 1 } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } = D _ { k } ^ { + } \left( H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { n }$$
$$\partial v \left( H ^ { - 1 } / \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } X \right) ^ { \prime } = - 2 D _ { k } ^ { + } \left( H ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right) \right.$$
$$\partial v \left( H ^ { - 1 } \right) / \partial b ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } = 0$$
$$\partial v \left( H ^ { - 1 } \right) / \partial \left( v \left( H ^ { * } { } ^ { - 1 } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } = D _ { k } ^ { + } \left( H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right) D _ { k } .$$
(2)
(3)
(4)
(5)

Proof. From $H = H ^ { * } + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } H ^ { - 1 } = - H ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } H \right) H ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= - H ^ { - 1 } \left[ \mathrm { d } H ^ { * } + \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X + X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } X + X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } V ^ { - 1 } \right) X \right] H ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } H ^ { * } { } ^ { - 1 } \right) H ^ { * } H ^ { - 1 } - H ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } X H ^ { - 1 }$$

$$- H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) H ^ { - 1 } - H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } V ^ { - 1 } \right) X H ^ { - 1 } .$$
(6)

Hence

$$\mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } H ^ { - 1 } = \left( H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } H ^ { * - 1 } - \left( H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X ^ { \prime }$$
$$- \left( H ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X - \left( I \right.$$
(H-1X' & H-1X')d vec V-1

$$= \left( H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } H ^ { * } { } ^ { - 1 }$$
$$- \left[ \left( H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } \right) K _ { n k } + H ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right] \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$

$$- \left( H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } V ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \left( H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } H ^ { * - 1 }$$

$$- \left( I _ { k ^ { 2 } } + K _ { k k } \right) \left( H ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$
$$- \left( H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } V ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(7)

so that

$$\mathrm { d } v \left( H ^ { - 1 } \right) = D _ { k } ^ { + } \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } H ^ { - 1 } = D _ { k } ^ { + } \left( H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } H ^ { * } \right) D _ { k } \mathrm { d } v \left( H ^ { * } { } ^ { - 1 } \right)$$
$$- 2 D _ { k } ^ { + } \left( H ^ { - 1 } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } V ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } X$$

$$- D _ { k } ^ { + } \left( H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \otimes H ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { n } d v \left( V ^ { - 1 } \right)$$
(8)

and the results follow.

### BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§2-§7. See Theil and Schweitzer (1961), Theil (1971), Rao (1971b) and Neudecker
(1980a).

§8. See Balestra (1973), Neudecker (1980b, 1985), Neudecker and Liu (1993),
and Rolle (1994).

§9-§10. See Neudecker (1977b, 1978). Theorem 7 corrects an error in Neudecker
(1978).

§11-§14. See Theil (1965), Koerts and Abrahamse (1969), Abrahamse and
Koerts (1971), Dubbelman, Abrahamse and Koerts (1972), and Neudecker
(1973, 1977a).

§15-§16. See Leamer (1978) and Polasek (1986).

<!-- PageBreak -->

Part Six -
Applications to maximum likelihood
estimation

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


### CHAPTER 15 Maximum likelihood estimation


#### 1 INTRODUCTION

The method of maximum likelihood estimation has great intuitive appeal and
generates estimators with desirable asymptotic properties. The estimators are
obtained by maximization of the likelihood function, and the asymptotic pre-
cision of the estimators is measured by the inverse of the information matrix.
Thus both the first and the second differential of the likelihood function need
to be found and this provides an excellent example of the use of our techniques.


#### 2 THE METHOD OF MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD (ML)

Let $\left\{ y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots \right\}$ be a sequence of random variables, not necessarily indepen-
dent or identically distributed. The joint density function of $y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) \in$
$\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ is denoted $h _ { n } \left( \cdot ; \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ and is known except for $\gamma _ { 0 } ,$ the true value of the param-
eter vector to be estimated. We assume that $\gamma _ { 0 } \in \Gamma ,$ where $\Gamma$ (the parameter
space) is a subset of a finite-dimensional Euclidean space. For every (fixed)
$y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n }$ the real-valued function

$$L _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) = L _ { n } \left( \gamma ; y \right) = h _ { n } \left( y ; \gamma \right) , \quad \gamma \in \Gamma ,$$
(1)

is called the likelihood function, and its logarithm

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) = \log L _ { n } \left( \gamma \right)$$
(2)

is called the loglikelihood function.

For fixed $y \in \mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ every value $\widehat { \gamma } _ { n } \left( y \right) \in \Gamma$ with

$$L _ { n } \left( \widehat { \gamma } _ { n } \left( y \right) ; y \right) = \sup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } L _ { n } \left( \gamma ; y \right)$$
(3)

is called a maximum likelihood (ML) estimate of $\gamma _ { 0 } .$ In general, there is no
guarantee that an ML estimate of $\gamma _ { 0 }$ exists for (almost) every $y \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } ,$ but if

<!-- PageNumber="351" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="352" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

it does and if the function $\widehat { \gamma } _ { n } : \mathrm { I R } ^ { n } \rightarrow \Gamma$ so defined is measurable, then this
function is called an ML estimator of $\gamma _ { 0 } .$

When the supremum in (3) is attained at an interior point of $\Gamma$ and $L _ { n } \left( \gamma \right)$
is a differentiable function of $\gamma ,$ then the score vector

$$s _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) = \partial \Lambda _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) / \partial \gamma$$
(4)

vanishes at that point, so that $\widehat { \gamma } _ { n }$ is a solution of the vector equation $s _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) = 0 .$
If $L _ { n } \left( \gamma \right)$ is a twice differentiable function of $\gamma ,$ then the Hessian matrix is
defined as

$$\mathrm { H } _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) = \partial ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) / \partial \gamma \partial \gamma ^ { \prime }$$
(5)

and the information matrix for $\gamma _ { 0 }$ is

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) = - \mathcal{E} \mathrm { H } _ { n } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) .$$
(6)

Notice that the information matrix is evaluated at the true value $\gamma _ { 0 } .$ The
asymptotic information matrix for $\gamma _ { 0 }$ is defined as

$$\mathcal{F} \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$$
(7)

if the limit exists. If $\mathcal{F} \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ is positive definite, its inverse $\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ is essen-
tially a lower bound for the asymptotic variance matrix of any consistent
estimator of $\gamma _ { 0 }$ (asymptotic Cramér-Rao inequality). Under suitable regular-
ity conditions the ML estimator attains this lower bound asymptotically. As
a consequence we shall refer to $\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ as the asymptotic variance $m a t r i x$
$\mathrm { o f }$ the ML estimator $\widehat { \gamma } _ { n } .$ The precise meaning of this is that, under suitable
conditions, the sequence of random variables

$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \gamma } _ { n } - \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$$
(8)

converges in distribution to a normally distributed random vector with mean
zero and variance matrix $\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) .$ Thus, $\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ is the variance matrix of
the asymptotic distribution, and an estimator of the variance matrix of $\widehat { \gamma } _ { n }$ is
given by

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } \left( \widehat { \gamma } _ { n } \right) \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad \mathcal{F} _ { n } ^ { - 1 } \left( \widehat { \gamma } _ { n } \right) .$$
(9)


#### 3 ML ESTIMATION OF THE MULTIVARIATE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION

Our first theorem is the following well-known result concerning the multivari-
ate normal distribution.


##### Theorem 1

Let the random $m \times 1$ vectors $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be independently and identically
distributed such that

$$y _ { i } \sim \mathcal{N} _ { m } \left( \mu _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="353" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] ML estimation of the multivariate normal distribution" -->

where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is positive definite, and let $n \geq m + 1 .$ The ML estimators of $\mu _ { 0 }$
and $\Omega _ { 0 }$ are

$$\widehat { \mu } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { i } \equiv \bar { y }$$
(2)

$$\widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(3)

Let us give four proofs of this theorem. The first proof ignores the fact that
$\Omega$ is symmetric.

First proof of Theorem 1. The loglikelihood function is

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu , \Omega \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m n \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Omega | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } Z ,$$
(4)

and

$$Z = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \mu \right) \left( y _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(5)

The first differential of $\Lambda _ { n }$ is

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda _ { n } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { d } \log | \Omega | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) Z - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } Z$$
$$= - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Omega + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } Z$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \sum _ { i } \left( y _ { i } - \mu \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } + \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) \sum _ { i } \left( y _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { i } \left( y _ { i } - \mu \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) .$$

(6)

If we ignore the symmetry constraint on $\Omega ,$ we obtain the first-order conditions

$$\Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } = 0 , \quad \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) = 0 ,$$
(7)

from which (2) and (3) follow immediately. To prove that we have in fact
found the maximum of (4), we differentiate (6) again. This yields

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } Z - n \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$

$$- n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu .$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="354" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

At the point $\left( \widehat { \mu } , \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ we have $\widehat { \mu } = \bar { y } ,$ $\widehat { Z } - n \widehat { \Omega } = 0$ and $\mathrm { d } \widehat { Z } = 0$ (see Exercise 1),
and hence

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \widehat { \mu } , \widehat { \Omega } \right) = - \frac { n } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \widehat { \Omega } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \widehat { \Omega } ^ { - 1 } - n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \widehat { \Omega } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu < 0$$
(9)

unless $\mathrm { d } \mu = 0$ and $\mathrm { d } \Omega = 0 .$ It follows that $\Lambda _ { n }$ has a strict local maximum at

$\left( \widehat { \mu } , \widehat { \Omega } \right) .$

✷

☐


##### Exercises

1\. Show that $\mathrm { d } Z = - n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) ^ { \prime } - n \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ and conclude that
$\mathrm { d } \widehat { Z } = 0 .$

2\. Show that $\mathcal{E} \widehat { \Omega } = \left( \left( n - 1 \right) / n \right) \Omega .$

3\. Show that $\widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) Y ^ { \prime } \left( I - \left( 1 / n \right) \imath \imath ^ { \prime } \right) Y ,$ where $Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$

4\. Hence show that $\widehat { \Omega }$ is positive definite (almost surely) if and only if
$n \quad - 1 > m .$


#### 4 SYMMETRY: IMPLICIT VERSUS EXPLICIT TREATMENT

The first proof of Theorem 1 shows that, even if we do not improve symme-
try (or positive definiteness) on $\Omega ,$ the solution $\widehat { \Omega }$ is symmetric and positive
semidefinite (in fact, positive definite with probability 1). Hence there is no
need to impose symmetry at this stage. Nevertheless, we shall give two proofs
of Theorem 1 where the symmetry is properly taken into account. We shall
need these results in any case when we discuss the second-order conditions
(Hessian matrix and information matrix).

Second proof of Theorem 1. Starting from (3.6) we have

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { v e c } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } v \left( \Omega \right) \right) ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { v e c } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) ,$$

(1)

where $D _ { m }$ is the duplication matrix (see Section 3.8). The first-order condi-
tions are

$$\Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) = 0 , \quad D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { v e c } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) = 0 .$$
(2)

The first of these conditions implies $\widehat { \mu } = \bar { y }$ the second can be written as

$$D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } v \left( Z - n \Omega \right) = 0$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="355" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] The treatment of positive definiteness" -->

since $Z - n \Omega$ is symmetric. Now, $D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m }$ is non-singular (see
Theorem 3.13), so (3) implies $v \left( Z - n \Omega \right) = 0 .$ Using again the symmetry of $Z$
and $\Omega ,$ we obtain

$$\widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) \widehat { Z } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i } \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(4)

This concludes the second proof of Theorem 1.
✷
☐

We shall call the above treatment of the symmetry condition (using the
duplication matrix) implicit. In contrast, an explicit treatment of symmetry
involves inclusion of the side condition $\Omega = \Omega ^ { \prime } .$ The next proof of Theorem 1
illustrates this approach.

Third proof of Theorem 1. Our starting point now is the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( \mu , \Omega \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m n \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Omega | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } Z - \mathrm { t r } L ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega - \Omega ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$
(5)

where $L$ is an $m \times m$ matrix of Lagrange multipliers. Differentiating (5) yields

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \mathrm { t r } \left( L - L ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \Omega + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) ,$$
(6)

so that the first-order conditions are

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + L - L ^ { \prime } = 0$$
$$\Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) = 0$$
$$\Omega = \Omega ^ { \prime } .$$
(7)
(8)
(9)
.

From (8) follows $\widehat { \mu } = \bar { y } .$ Adding (7) to its transpose and using (9) yields
$\Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } = 0$ and hence the desired result.
✷
☐


#### 5 THE TREATMENT OF POSITIVE DEFINITENESS

Finally we may impose both symmetry and positive definiteness on $\Omega$ by
writing $\Omega = X ^ { \prime } X ,$ $X$ square. This leads to our final proof of Theorem 1.

Fourth proof of Theorem 1. Again starting from (3.6), we have

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } X ^ { \prime } X \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$
$$\left. = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \left( \mathrm { d } X \right) ^ { \prime } X + X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right)$$
$$+ n \left( d \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$

$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } X \right) + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) .$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="356" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

The first-order conditions are

$$\Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } = 0 , \quad \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) = 0 ,$$
(2)

from which it follows that $\widehat { \mu } = \bar { y }$ and $\widehat { \Omega } = \widehat { X } ^ { \prime } \widehat { X } = \left( 1 / n \right) \widehat { Z } .$
☐
✷


#### 6 THE INFORMATION MATRIX

To obtain the information matrix we need to take the symmetry of $\Omega$ into
account, either implicitly or explicitly. We prefer the implicit treatment using
the duplication matrix.


##### Theorem 2

Let the random $m \times 1$ vectors $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be independently and identically
distributed such that

$$y _ { i } \sim \mathcal{N} _ { m } \left( \mu _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(1)

where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is positive definite, and let $n \geq m + 1 .$ The information matrix for
$\mu _ { 0 }$ and v $v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ is the $\frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( m + 3 \right) \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( m + 3 \right)$ matrix

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } = n \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(2)

The asymptotic variance matrix of the $M L$ estimators $\widehat { \mu }$ and $v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 0 & 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(3)

and the generalized asymptotic variance of $v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ is

$$| 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } | = 2 ^ { m } | \Omega _ { 0 } | ^ { m + 1 } .$$
(4)

Proof. Since $\Omega$ is a linear function of $v \left( \Omega \right) ,$ we have $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Omega = 0$ and hence the
second differential of $\Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu , v \left( \Omega \right) \right)$ is given by (3.8):

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu , v \left( \Omega \right) \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } Z - n \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$

$$- n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu .$$
(5)

Notice that we do not at this stage evaluate $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n }$ completely in terms of $\mathrm { d } \mu$
and $\mathrm { d } v \left( \Omega \right)$ this is unnecessary because, upon taking expectations, we find
immediately

$$- \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu _ { 0 } , v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \right) = \frac { n } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu ,$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="357" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7 ] MLE of the multivariate normal distribution: distinct means" -->

since $\mathcal{E} \bar { y } = \mu _ { 0 } ,$ $\mathcal{E} Z = n \Omega _ { 0 }$ and $\mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } Z = 0$ (compare the passage from (3.8) to
(3.9)). We now use the duplication matrix and obtain

$$- \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu _ { 0 } , v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \right) = \frac { n } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { v e c d } \Omega \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { v e c d } \Omega + :$$
n(du)'S-1 du

$$= \frac { n } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } v \left( \Omega \right) \right) ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Omega \right) + n \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu .$$
(7)

Hence the information matrix for $\mu _ { 0 }$ and $v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ is $\mathcal{F} _ { n } = n \mathcal{F}$ with

$$\mathcal{F} = \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(8)

The asymptotic variance matrix of $\widehat { \mu }$ and $v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 0 & 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(9)

using Theorem 3.13(d). The generalized asymptotic variance of $v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ follows
from (9) and Theorem $3 . 1 4 \left( \mathrm { b } \right) .$

##### Exercises

1\. Taking (5) as your starting point, show that

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu , v \left( \Omega \right) \right) = - \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu - 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right)$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 }$$
$$- \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z / n \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } .$$

2\. Hence show that the Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } _ { n } \left( \mu , v \left( \Omega \right) \right)$ takes the form

$$- n \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega ^ { - 1 } & \left( \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \\ D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes A \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right)$$

with

$$A = \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( 2 / n \right) Z - \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } .$$


#### 7 ML ESTIMATION OF THE MULTIVARIATE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION: DISTINCT MEANS

Suppose now that we have not one but, say, $p$ random samples, and let $\mathrm { t h e } j - \mathrm { t h }$
sample be from the $\mathcal{N} _ { m } \left( \mu _ { 0 j } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ distribution. We wish to estimate $\mu _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { 0 p }$
and the common variance matrix $\Omega _ { 0 } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="358" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->


##### Theorem 3

Let the random $m \times 1$ vectors $y _ { i j } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n _ { j } ; j = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$ be independently
distributed such that

$$y _ { i j } \sim \mathcal{N} _ { m } \left( \mu _ { 0 j } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n _ { j } ; j = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$$
(1)

where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is positive definite, and let $n = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } n _ { j } \geq m + p .$ The ML estimators
of $\mu _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { 0 p }$ and $\Omega _ { 0 }$ are

$$\widehat { \mu } _ { j } = \left( 1 / n _ { j } \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n _ { j } } y _ { i j } \equiv \bar { y } _ { j } \quad \left( j = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$$
(2)

$$\widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n _ { j } } \left( y _ { i j } - \bar { y } _ { j } \right) \left( y _ { i j } - \bar { y } _ { j } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(3)

The information matrix for $\mu _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { 0 p }$ and $v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } = n \left( \begin{array}{} A \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(4)

where $A$ is a diagonal $p \times p$ matrix with diagonal elements $n _ { j } / n \left( j = 1 , \ldots , p \right) .$
The asymptotic variance matrix of the ML estimators $\widehat { \mu } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \widehat { \mu } _ { p }$ and $v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} A ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 0 & 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

Proof. The proof is left as an exercise for the reader.

✷
☐


##### Exercise

1\. Show that $\widehat { \Omega }$ is positive definite (almost surely) if and only if $n - p \geq m .$


#### 8 THE MULTIVARIATE LINEAR REGRESSION MODEL

Let us consider a system of linear regression equations

$$y _ { i j } = x _ { i } ^ { \prime } \beta _ { 0 j } + \epsilon _ { i j } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n ; j = 1 , \ldots , m \right) ,$$
(1)

where $y _ { i j }$ denotes the i-th observation on the j-th dependent variable, $x _ { i } \left( i = \right.$
$\left. 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ are observations on the $k$ regressors, $\beta _ { 0 j } \left( j = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$ are $k \times 1$
parameter vectors to be estimated, and $\epsilon _ { i j }$ is a random disturbance term. We
let $\epsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \left( \epsilon _ { i 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { i m } \right)$ and assume that $\mathcal{E} \epsilon _ { i } = 0$ $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and

$$\begin{array}{} \mathcal{E} \epsilon _ { i } \epsilon _ { h } ^ { \prime } = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 0 \mathrm { i f } i \neq h & \\ \Omega _ { 0 } \mathrm { i f } i = h . & \end{array} \right. \end{array} .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="359" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] The multivariate linear regression model" -->

Let $Y = \left( y _ { i j } \right)$ be the $n \times m$ matrix of the observations on the dependent
variables and let

$$Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } = \left( y _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , y _ { \left( m \right) } \right) .$$
(3)

Similarly we define

$$X = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad B _ { 0 } = \left( \beta _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { 0 m } \right)$$
(4)

of orders $n \times k$ and $k \times m$ respectively, and $\epsilon _ { \left( j \right) } = \left( \epsilon _ { 1 j } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n j } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ We can then
write the system (1) either as

$$y _ { \left( j \right) } = X \beta _ { 0 j } + \epsilon _ { \left( j \right) } \quad \left( j = 1 , \ldots , m \right)$$
(5)

or as

$$y _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } ^ { \prime } B _ { 0 } + \epsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(6)

If the vectors $\epsilon _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { \left( m \right) }$ are uncorrelated, which is the case if $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is diagonal,
we can estimate each $\beta _ { 0 j }$ separately. But in general this will not be the case
and we have to estimate the whole system on efficiency grounds.


##### Theorem 4

Let the random $m \times 1$ vectors $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be independently distributed such
that

$$y _ { i } \sim \mathcal{N} _ { m } \left( B _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } x _ { i } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(7)

where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is positive definite and $X = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ is a given non-random
$n \times k$ matrix of full column rank $k .$ Let $n > m + k .$ The $\mathrm { M L }$ estimators of $B _ { 0 }$
and $\Omega _ { 0 }$ are

$$\widehat { B } = \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } Y , \quad \widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) Y ^ { \prime } M Y ,$$
(8)

where

$$Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad M = I _ { n } - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } .$$
(9)

The information matrix for vec $B _ { 0 }$ and $v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } = n \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X & 0 \\ 0 & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right) .$$
(10)

And, if $\left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X$ converges to a positive definite matrix $Q$ when $n \rightarrow \infty ,$ the
asymptotic variance matrix of vec $\widehat { B }$ and $v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes Q ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right) .$$
(11)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="360" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

Proof. The loglikelihood is

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( B , v \left( \Omega \right) \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m n \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Omega | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } Z$$
(12)

where

$$Z = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - B ^ { \prime } x _ { i } \right) \left( y _ { i } - B ^ { \prime } x _ { i } \right) ^ { \prime } = \left( Y - X B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( Y - X B \right) ,$$
(13)

and its first differential takes the form

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda _ { n } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Omega + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } Z - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } Z$$

$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Y - X B \right) ^ { \prime } X \mathrm { d } B .$$
(14)

The first-order conditions are therefore

$$\Omega = \left( 1 / n \right) Z , \quad \left( Y - X B \right) ^ { \prime } X = 0 .$$
(15)

This leads to $\widehat { B } = \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } Y ,$ so that

$$\widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) \left( Y - X \widehat { B } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( Y - X \widehat { B } \right) = \left( 1 / n \right) \left( M Y \right) ^ { \prime } M Y = \left( 1 / n \right) Y ^ { \prime } M Y .$$
(16)

The second differential is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } = \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( Z - n \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } Z - n \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 }$$

$$+ \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( Y - X B \right) ^ { \prime } X \mathrm { d } B - \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } B \right) ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X \mathrm { d } B ,$$
(17)

and taking expectations we obtain

$$- \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( B _ { 0 } , v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \right) = \frac { n } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + \mathrm { t r } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } B \right) ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \mathrm { X d } B$$
$$= \frac { n } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } v \left( \Omega \right) \right) ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Omega \right)$$

$$+ \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes X ^ { \prime } X \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } B .$$
(18)

The information matrix and the inverse of its limit now follow easily from
✷
☐
(18).


##### Exercises

1\. Use (17) to show that

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( B , v \left( \Omega \right) \right) = - \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( X ^ { \prime } X / n \right) \mathrm { d } B$$
$$- 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( X ^ { \prime } \left( Y - X B \right) / n \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 }$$
$$- \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( Z / n \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } .$$

2\. Hence show that the Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } _ { n } \left( \mathrm { v e c } B , v \left( \Omega \right) \right)$ takes the form

$$- n \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( X ^ { \prime } X / n \right) & \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( X ^ { \prime } V / n \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \\ D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( V ^ { \prime } X / n \right) \right) & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes A \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right)$$

with

$$V = Y - X B , \quad A = \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( 2 / n \right) Z - \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } .$$

Compare this result with the Hessian matrix obtained in Exercise 6.2.


#### 9 THE ERRORS-IN-VARIABLES MODEL

Consider the linear regression model

$$y _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { \prime } \beta _ { 0 } + \epsilon _ { i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(1)

where $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are non-stochastic $k \times 1$ vectors. Assume that both $y _ { i }$ and
$x _ { i }$ are measured with error, so that instead of observing $y _ { i }$ and $x _ { i }$ we observe
$y _ { i } ^ { * }$ and $x _ { i } ^ { * }$ where

$$y _ { i } ^ { * } = y _ { i } + \eta _ { i } , \quad x _ { i } ^ { * } = x _ { i } + \xi _ { i } . ,$$
(2)

Then we have

$$\left( \begin{array}{} y _ { i } ^ { * } \\ x _ { i } ^ { * } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} x _ { i } ^ { \prime } \beta _ { 0 } \\ x _ { i } \end{array} \right) + \left( \begin{array}{} \epsilon _ { i } + \eta _ { i } \\ \xi _ { i } \end{array} \right)$$
(3)

or, for short,

$$z _ { i } = \mu _ { 0 i } + v _ { i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(4)

If we assume that the distribution of $\left( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { n } \right)$ is completely known, then the
problem is to estimate the vectors $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and $\beta _ { 0 } .$ Letting $\alpha _ { 0 } = \left( - 1 , \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$
we see that this is equivalent to estimating $\mu _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { 0 n }$ and $\alpha _ { 0 }$ subject to the
constraints $\mu _ { 0 i } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 0 } = 0 \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$ In this context the following result is of
importance.


##### Theorem 5

Let the random $m \times 1$ vectors $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be independently distributed such
that

$$y _ { i } \sim \mathcal{N} _ { m } \left( \mu _ { 0 i } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(5)

where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is positive definite and known, and the parameter vectors $\mu _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { 0 n }$
are subject to the constraint

$$\mu _ { 0 i } ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 0 } = 0 \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="362" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

for some unknown $\alpha _ { 0 }$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { m } ,$ normalized by $\alpha _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha _ { 0 } = 1$ 1. The ML estimators
of $\mu _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { 0 n }$ and $\alpha _ { 0 }$ are

$$\widehat { \mu } _ { i } = \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( I - u u ^ { \prime } \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } y _ { i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
$$\widehat { \alpha } = \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } u ,$$
(7)
(8)

where $u$ is the normalized eigenvector $\left( u ^ { \prime } u = 1 \right)$ associated with the smallest
eigenvalue of

$$\Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { i } y _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$$
(9)

Proof. Letting

$$Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad M = \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(10)

we write the loglikelihood as

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( M , \alpha \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m n \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | \Omega _ { 0 } | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y - M \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(11)

We wish to maximize $\Lambda _ { n }$ subject to the constraints $M \alpha = 0$ and $\alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha = 1 .$
Since $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is given, the problem becomes

minimize
subject to

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y - M \right) ^ { \prime }$$

a'20a = 1.
$$M \alpha = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d }$$
(12)

The Lagrangian function is

$$\psi \left( M , \alpha \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y - M \right) ^ { \prime } - l ^ { \prime } M \alpha - \lambda \left( \alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha - 1 \right) ,$$
(13)

where $l$ is a vector of Lagrange multipliers and $\lambda$ is a (scalar) Lagrange mul-
tiplier. The first differential is

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = - \mathrm { t r } \left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } M \right) ^ { \prime } - l ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } M \right) \alpha - l ^ { \prime } M \mathrm { d } \alpha - 2 \lambda \alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } \alpha$$

$$= - \mathrm { t r } \left( \left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + l \alpha ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } M \right) ^ { \prime } - \left( l ^ { \prime } M + 2 \lambda \alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \right) d \alpha$$
(14)

and the first-order conditions are thus

$$\left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } = - l \alpha ^ { \prime }$$
$$M ^ { \prime } l = - 2 \lambda \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha$$
$$M \alpha = 0$$
$$\alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha = 1 .$$
(15)
(16)
(17)
(18)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="363" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] The errors-in-variables model" -->

As usual we first solve for the Lagrange multipliers. Post-multiplying (15) by
$\Omega _ { 0 } \alpha$ yields

$$l = - Y \alpha ,$$
(19)

using (17) and (18). Also, pre-multiplying (16) by $\alpha ^ { \prime }$ yields

$$\lambda = 0$$
(20)

in view of (17) and (18). Inserting (19) and (20) into (15)-(18) gives

$$M = Y - Y \alpha \alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 }$$
(21)

$$M ^ { \prime } Y \alpha = 0$$
$$\alpha ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha = 1 .$$
(22)
(23)

(Note that $M \alpha = 0$ is automatically satisfied.) Inserting (21) into (22) gives

$$\left( Y ^ { \prime } Y - \nu \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \alpha = 0 ,$$
(24)

where $\nu = \alpha ^ { \prime } Y ^ { \prime } Y \alpha ,$ which we rewrite as

$$\left( \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } Y ^ { \prime } Y \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } - \nu I \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha = 0 .$$
(25)

Given (21) and (23) we have

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( Y - M \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y - M \right) ^ { \prime } = \alpha ^ { \prime } Y ^ { \prime } Y \alpha = \nu .$$
(26)

But this is the function we wish to minimize! Hence we take $\nu$ as the smallest
eigenvalue of $\Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } Y ^ { \prime } Y \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and $\Omega _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha$ as the associated normalized eigen-
vector. This yields (8), the $\mathrm { M L }$ estimator of a. The $M L$ estimator of $M$ then
follows from (21).
✷

1\. If $\alpha _ { 0 }$ is normalized by $e ^ { \prime } \alpha _ { 0 } = - 1$ (rather than by $\alpha _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \alpha _ { 0 } = 1 ,$ show
that the ML estimators (7) and (8) become

$$\widehat { \mu } _ { i } = \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( I - \frac { u u ^ { \prime } } { u ^ { \prime } u } \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } y _ { i } , \quad \widehat { \alpha } = \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } u ,$$

where $u$ is the eigenvector (normalized by $\left. e ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } u = - 1 \right)$ associated
with the smallest eigenvalue of

$$\Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \left( \sum _ { i } y _ { i } y _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$$
