#### 10 THE NON-LINEAR REGRESSION MODEL WITH NORMAL ERRORS

Let us now consider a system of $n$ non-linear regression equations with normal
errors, which we write as

$$y \sim \mathcal{N} _ { n } \left( \mu \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) , \Omega \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) \right) .$$
(1)

Here $\gamma _ { 0 }$ denotes the true (but unknown) value of the parameter vector to be
estimated. We assume that $\gamma _ { 0 } \in \Gamma ,$ an open subset of $\mathbb{R} ^ { p } ,$ and that $p$ (the
dimension of $\Gamma$ is independent of n. We also assume that $\Omega \left( \gamma \right)$ is positive
definite for every $\gamma \in \Gamma ,$ and that $\mu$ and $\Omega$ are twice differentiable on $\Gamma .$ We
define the $p \times 1$ vector $l \left( \gamma \right) = \left( l _ { j } \left( \gamma \right) \right) ,$

$$l _ { j } \left( \gamma \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { j } } \Omega \left( \gamma \right) \right) + u ^ { \prime } \left( \gamma \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) \frac { \partial \mu \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { j } }$$
$$- \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } \left( \gamma \right) \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { j } } u \left( \gamma \right) ,$$
(2)

where $u \left( \gamma \right) = y - \mu \left( \gamma \right) ,$ and the $p \times p$ matrix $\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) = \left( \mathcal{F} _ { n , i j } \left( \gamma \right) \right) ,$

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n , i j } \left( \gamma \right) = \left( \frac { \partial \mu \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { i } } \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) \frac { \partial \mu \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { j } }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { i } } \Omega \left( \gamma \right) \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) } { \partial \gamma _ { j } } \Omega \left( \gamma \right) \right) .$$

(3)


##### Theorem 6

The ML estimator of $\gamma _ { 0 }$ in the non-linear regression model $\left( 1 \right)$ is obtained as
a solution of the vector equation $l \left( \gamma \right) = 0 .$ The information matrix is $\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$
and the asymptotic variance matrix of the $\mathrm { M L }$ estimator $\widehat { \gamma }$ is

$$\left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right) \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
(4)

if the limit exists.

Proof. The loglikelihood takes the form

$$\Lambda \left( \gamma \right) = - \left( n / 2 \right) \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | \Omega \left( \gamma \right) | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \gamma \right) u ,$$
(5)

where $u = u \left( \gamma \right) = y - \mu \left( \gamma \right) .$ The first differential is

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda \left( \gamma \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Omega - u ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } u - \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) u$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) + u ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu - \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) u .$$

(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="365" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] Functional independence of mean- and variance parameters" -->

Hence $\partial \Lambda \left( \gamma \right) / \partial \gamma = l \left( \gamma \right)$ and the first-order conditions are given by $l \left( \gamma \right) = 0 .$
The second differential is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda \left( \gamma \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega \left( \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) + \left( \mathrm { d } u \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu$$

$$+ u ^ { \prime } d \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } d \mu \right) - u ^ { \prime } \left( d \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) d u - \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } \left( d ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) u .$$
(7)

Equation (7) can be further expanded, but this $\mathrm { i s }$ not necessary here. Notice
that $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 }$ (and $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \mu$ does not vanish unless $\Omega ^ { - 1 }$ (and $\mu$ is a linear (or affine)
function of $\gamma .$ Taking expectations at $\gamma = \gamma _ { 0 } ,$ we obtain (letting $\Omega _ { 0 } = \Omega \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$

$$- \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda \left( \gamma \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \Omega _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) + \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } d ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right)$$

$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \Omega _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) + \left( \mathrm { d } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \mu ,$$
(8)

because $\mathcal{E} u _ { 0 } = 0 ,$ $\mathcal{E} u _ { 0 } u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = \Omega _ { 0 } .$ This shows that the information matrix is
$\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ and concludes the proof.
☐
✷


##### Exercise

1\. Use (7) to obtain the Hessian matrix $\mathrm { H } _ { n } \left( \gamma \right) .$


#### 11 SPECIAL CASE: FUNCTIONAL INDEPENDENCE OF MEAN- AND VARIANCE PARAMETERS

Theorem 6 is rather general in that the same parameters may appear in both
$\mu$ and $\Omega .$ We often encounter the special case where

$$\gamma = \left( \beta ^ { \prime } , \theta ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime }$$
(1)

and $\mu$ only depends on the $\beta$ parameters while $\Omega$ only depends on the $\theta$
parameters.


##### Theorem 7

The ML estimators of $\beta _ { 0 } = \left( \beta _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { 0 k } \right) ^ { \prime }$ and $\theta _ { 0 } = \left( \theta _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { 0 m } \right) ^ { \prime }$ in the
non-linear regression model

$$y \sim \mathcal{N} _ { n } \left( \mu \left( \beta _ { 0 } \right) , \Omega \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right)$$
(2)

are obtained by solving the equations

$$\left( y - \mu \left( \beta \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) \frac { \partial \mu \left( \beta \right) } { \partial \beta _ { h } } = 0 \quad \left( h = 1 , \ldots , k \right)$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="366" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

and

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) } { \partial \theta _ { j } } \Omega \left( \theta \right) \right) = \left( y - \mu \left( \beta \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) } { \partial \theta _ { j } } \left( y - \mu \left( \beta \right) \right) .$$
(4)

The information matrix for $\beta _ { 0 }$ and $\theta _ { 0 }$ is $\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \beta _ { 0 } , \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ where

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \beta , \theta \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu \right) & 0 \\ 0 & \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right) \end{array} \right) ,$$

and

$$\mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu = \frac { \partial \mu \left( \beta \right) } { \partial \beta ^ { \prime } } , \quad \mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \left( \theta \right) } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } .$$

The asymptotic variance matrix of the $M L$ estimators $\widehat { \beta }$ and $\widehat { \theta }$ is

$$\left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \beta _ { 0 } , \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$$

if the limit exists.

Proof. Immediate from Theorem 6 and Equations $\left( 1 0 . 2 \right)$ and (10.3).
☐
✷


##### Exercises

1\. Under the conditions of Theorem 7 show that the asymptotic variance
matrix of $\widehat { \beta } ,$ denoted $\mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) ,$ is

$$\mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) = \left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) S _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) S _ { 0 } \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$$

where $S _ { 0 }$ denotes the $n \times k$ matrix of partial derivatives $\partial \mu \left( \beta \right) / \partial \beta ^ { \prime }$
evaluated at $\beta _ { 0 } .$

2\. In particular, in the linear regression model $y \sim \mathcal{N} _ { n } \left( X \beta _ { 0 } , \Omega \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right) ,$ show
that

$$\mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) = \left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) X \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$


#### 12 GENERALIZATION OF THEOREM 6

In Theorem 6 we assumed that both $\mu$ and $\Omega$ depend on all the parameters in
the system. In Theorem 7 we assumed that $\mu$ depends on some parameters $\beta$
while $\Omega$ depends on some other parameters $\theta ,$ and that $\mu$ does not depend on
$\theta$ or $\Omega$ on $\beta .$ The most general case, which we discuss in this section, assumes
that $\beta$ and $\theta$ may partially overlap. The following two theorems present the
first-order conditions and the information matrix for this case.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="367" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Generalization of Theorem 6" -->


## Theorem 8

The ML estimators of $\beta _ { 0 } = \left( \beta _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { 0 k } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ $\zeta _ { 0 } = \left( \zeta _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \zeta _ { 0 l } \right) ^ { \prime }$ and $\theta _ { 0 } =$
$\left( \theta _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { 0 m } \right) ^ { \prime }$ in the non-linear regression model

$$y \sim \mathcal{N} _ { n } \left( \mu \left( \beta _ { 0 } , \zeta _ { 0 } \right) , \Omega \left( \theta _ { 0 } , \zeta _ { 0 } \right) \right)$$
(1)

are obtained by solving the equations

$$u ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \frac { \partial \mu } { \partial \beta _ { h } } = 0 \quad \left( h = 1 , \ldots , k \right)$$
$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } } { \partial \zeta _ { i } } \Omega \right) + u ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \frac { \partial \mu } { \partial \zeta _ { i } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } u ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } } { \partial \zeta _ { i } } u = 0$$
$$\mathrm { t r } \left( \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } } { \partial \theta _ { j } } \Omega \right) = u ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { - 1 } } { \partial \theta _ { j } } u \quad \left( j = 1 , \ldots , m \right) ,$$
(i=1, ... ,l)

(2)

(3)

(4)

where $u = y - \mu \left( \beta , \zeta \right) .$

Proof. Let $\gamma = \left( \beta ^ { \prime } , \zeta ^ { \prime } , \theta ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ We know from Theorem 6 that we must solve the
vector equation $l \left( \gamma \right) = 0 ,$ where the elements of $l$ are given in (10.2). The
results follow.
✷
☐


### Theorem 9

The information matrix for $\beta _ { 0 } ,$ $\zeta _ { 0 }$ and $\theta _ { 0 }$ in the non-linear regression model
(1) is $\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \beta _ { 0 } , \zeta _ { 0 } , \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$ where

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \beta , \zeta , \theta \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathcal{F} _ { \beta \beta } & \mathcal{F} _ { \beta \zeta } & 0 \\ \mathcal{F} _ { \zeta \beta } & \mathcal{F} _ { \zeta \zeta } & \mathcal{F} _ { \zeta \theta } \\ 0 & \mathcal{F} _ { \theta \zeta } & \mathcal{F} _ { \theta \theta } \end{array} \right)$$
(5)

and

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \beta \beta } = \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu \right)$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \beta \zeta } = \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mu \right)$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \zeta \zeta } = \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mu \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right)$$
(6)
(7)
(8)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \zeta \theta } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right)$$
(9)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \theta \theta } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \right)$$
(10)

and where, as the notation indicates,

$$D _ { \zeta } \mu = \frac { \partial \mu \left( \beta , \zeta \right) } { \partial \zeta ^ { \prime } } ,$$
$$\mathrm { D } _ { \theta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \left( \theta , \zeta \right) } { \partial \theta ^ { \prime } } , \quad \mathrm { D } _ { \zeta } \mathrm { v e c } \Omega = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Omega \left( \theta , \zeta \right) } { \partial \zeta ^ { \prime } } .$$
$$\mathrm { D } _ { \beta } \mu = \frac { \partial \mu \left( \beta , \zeta \right) } { \partial \beta ^ { \prime } } ,$$

(11)
(12)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="368" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->

Moreover, if $\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \beta _ { 0 } , \zeta _ { 0 } , \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ tends to a finite positive definite matrix, say
$G ,$ partitioned as

$$G = \left( \begin{array}{} G _ { \beta \beta } & G _ { \beta \zeta } & 0 \\ G _ { \zeta \beta } & G _ { \zeta \zeta } & G _ { \zeta \theta } \\ 0 & G _ { \theta \zeta } & G _ { \theta \theta } \end{array} \right) , .$$
(13)

then $G ^ { - 1 }$ is the asymptotic variance matrix of the $M L$ estimators $\widehat { \beta } ,$ $\widehat { \zeta }$ and $\widehat { \theta }$
and takes the form

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { G _ { \beta \beta } ^ { - 1 } + Q _ { \beta } Q ^ { - 1 } Q _ { \beta } ^ { \prime } } & { - Q _ { \beta } Q ^ { - 1 } } & { Q _ { \beta } Q ^ { - 1 } Q _ { \theta } ^ { \prime } } \\ { - Q ^ { - 1 } Q _ { \beta } ^ { \prime } } & { Q ^ { - 1 } } & { - Q _ { \theta } ^ { - 1 } } & { Q _ { \theta \theta } ^ { - 1 } } \\ { Q _ { \theta } Q ^ { - 1 } Q _ { \beta } ^ { \prime } } & { - Q _ { \theta } Q ^ { - 1 } } & { G _ { \theta \theta } ^ { - 1 } + Q _ { \theta } Q ^ { - 1 } Q _ { \theta } ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(14)

where

$$Q _ { \beta } = G _ { \beta \beta } ^ { - 1 } G _ { \beta \zeta } , \quad Q _ { \theta } = G _ { \theta \theta } ^ { - 1 } G _ { \theta \zeta } ,$$
(15)

and

$$Q = G _ { \zeta \zeta } - G _ { \zeta \beta } G _ { \beta \beta } ^ { - 1 } G _ { \beta \zeta } - G _ { \zeta \theta } G _ { \theta \theta } ^ { - 1 } G _ { \theta \zeta } .$$
(16)

Proof. The structure of the information matrix follows from Theorem 6 and
(10.3). The inverse of its limit follows from Theorem 1.3.
✷
☐


## MISCELLANEOUS EXERCISES

1\. Consider an m-dimensional system of demand equations

$$y _ { t } = a + \Gamma f _ { t } + v _ { t } \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$

where

$$f _ { t } = \left( 1 / \imath ^ { \prime } \Gamma \imath \right) \left( \imath ^ { \prime } y _ { t } \right) \imath + C z _ { t } , \quad C = I _ { m } - \left( 1 / \imath ^ { \prime } \Gamma \imath \right) \imath \imath ^ { \prime } \Gamma ,$$

and $\Gamma$ is diagonal. Let the $m \times 1$ vectors $v _ { t }$ be independently and iden-
tically distributed as $\mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Omega \right) .$ It is easy to see that $\imath ^ { \prime } \Gamma f _ { t } = \imath ^ { \prime } y _ { t }$ $\left( t = \right.$
$\left. 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ almost surely, and hence that $\imath ^ { \prime } a = 0 ,$ $\imath ^ { \prime } v _ { t } = 0$ $\left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$
almost surely, and $\Omega \imath = 0 .$ Assume that $r \left( \Omega \right) = m - 1$ and denote the
positive eigenvalues of $\Omega$ by $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { m - 1 } .$

(a) Show that the loglikelihood of the sample is

$$\log L = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } - \left( n / 2 \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m - 1 } \log \lambda _ { i } - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { + } V ^ { \prime } V ,$$

where $V = \left( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ (The density of a singular normal distri-
bution is given, e.g. in Mardia, Kent and Bibby 1992, $p .$ 41.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="369" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Miscellaneous exercises" -->

(b) Show that the concentrated (with respect to $\Omega$ loglikelihood is

$$\log L _ { c } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } - \left( n / 2 \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m - 1 } \log \mu _ { i } ,$$

where $\mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { m - 1 }$ are the positive eigenvalues of $\left( 1 / n \right) V ^ { \prime } V .$ [ Hint:
Use Miscellaneous Exercise 8.7 to show that $d \Omega ^ { + } = - \Omega ^ { + } \left( d \widetilde { \Omega } \right) \Omega ^ { + } ,$
since $\Omega$ has locally constant rank.]

(c) Show that $\log L _ { c }$ can be equivalently written as

$$\log L _ { c } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } - \left( n / 2 \right) \log | A | ,$$

where

$$A = \left( 1 / n \right) V ^ { \prime } V + \left( 1 / m \right) v ^ { \prime } .$$

[Hint: Use Exercise 1.11.3 and Theorem 3.5.]

(d) Show that the first-order condition with respect to $a$ is given by

$$\sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { t } - a - \Gamma f _ { t } \right) = 0 ,$$

irrespective of whether we take account of the constraint $\imath ^ { \prime } a = 0 .$

(e) Show that the first-order condition with respect to $\gamma = \Gamma \imath$ is given
by

$$\sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { n } F _ { t } C A ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } \left( y _ { t } - a - \Gamma f _ { t } \right) = 0 ,$$

where $F _ { t }$ is the diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are the
components of $f _ { t }$ (Barten 1969).

2\. Let the random $p \times 1$ vectors $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ be independently distributed
such that

$$y _ { t } \sim \mathcal{N} _ { p } \left( A B _ { 0 } c _ { t } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$$

where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is positive definite, $A$ is a known $p \times q$ matrix and $c _ { t } , \ldots , c _ { n }$
are known $k \times 1$ vectors. The matrices $B _ { 0 }$ and $\Omega _ { 0 }$ are to be estimated.
Let $C = \left( c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n } \right) ,$ $Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ and denote the ML estimators
of $B _ { 0 }$ and $\Omega _ { 0 }$ by $\widehat { B }$ and $\widehat { \Omega } .$ Assume that $r \left( C \right) \leq n - p$ and prove that

$$\widehat { \Omega } = \left( 1 / n \right) \left( Y ^ { \prime } - A \widehat { B } C \right) \left( Y ^ { \prime } - A \widehat { B } C \right) ^ { \prime }$$
$$A \widehat { B } C = A \left( A ^ { \prime } S ^ { - 1 } A \right) ^ { + } A ^ { \prime } S ^ { - 1 } Y ^ { \prime } C ^ { + } C ,$$

where

$$S = \left( 1 / n \right) Y ^ { \prime } \left( I - C ^ { + } C \right) Y$$

(cf. Von Rosen 1985).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="370" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Maximum likelihood estimation [Ch. 15" -->


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§2. The method of maximum likelihood originated with R.A. Fisher in the
1920s. Important contributions were made by H. Cramér, C.R. Rao and A.
Wald. See Norden (1972, 1973) for a survey of maximum likelihood estimation.
See also Cramer (1986).

§4-§6. Implicit versus explicit treatment of symmetry is discussed in more
depth in Magnus (1988). The second proof of Theorem 1 and the proof of
Theorem 2 follow Magnus and Neudecker (1980). The fourth proof of Theorem
1 follows Neudecker (1980a).

§8. See Zellner (1962) and Malinvaud (1966, Chapter 6).

§9. See Madansky (1976), Malinvaud (1966, Chapter 10) and Pollock (1979,
Chapter 8).

§10-§12. See Holly (1985) and Heijmans and Magnus (1986).

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 16 Simultaneous equations


### 1 INTRODUCTION

In Chapter 13 we considered the simple linear regression model

$$y _ { t } = x _ { t } ^ { \prime } \beta _ { 0 } + u _ { t } \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(1)

where $y _ { t }$ and $u _ { t }$ are scalar random variables and $x _ { t }$ and $\beta _ { 0 }$ are $k \times 1$ vectors. In
Section 8 of Chapter 15 we generalized (1) to the multivariate linear regression
model

$$y _ { t } ^ { \prime } = x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B _ { 0 } + u _ { t } ^ { \prime } \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(2)

where $y _ { t }$ and $u _ { t }$ are random $m \times 1$ vectors, $x _ { t }$ is a $k \times 1$ vector and $B _ { 0 }$ a $k \times m$
matrix.

In this chapter we consider a further generalization, where the model is
specified by

$$y _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Gamma _ { 0 } + x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B _ { 0 } = u _ { t } ^ { \prime } \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right) .$$
(3)

This model is known as the simultaneous equations model.


### 2 THE SIMULTANEOUS EQUATIONS MODEL

Thus, let economic theory specify a set of economic relations of the form

$$y _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Gamma _ { 0 } + x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B _ { 0 } = u _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(1)

where $y _ { t }$ is an $m \times 1$ vector of observed endogenous variables, $x _ { t }$ is a $k \times 1$ vector
of observed exogenous (non-random) variables and $u _ { 0 t }$ is an $m \times 1$ vector of
unobserved random disturbances. The $m \times m$ matrix $\Gamma _ { 0 }$ and the $k \times m$ matrix
$B _ { 0 }$ are unknown parameter matrices. We shall make the following assumption.

<!-- PageNumber="371" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="372" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->


## Assumption 1 (normality)

The vectors $\left\{ u _ { 0 t } , t = 1 , \ldots , n \right\}$ are independent and identically distributed as
$\mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ with $\Sigma _ { 0 }$ a positive definite $m \times m$ matrix of unknown parameters.


## Lemma

Given Assumption 1, the $m \times m$ matrix $\Gamma _ { 0 }$ is non-singular.

$P r o o f .$ Assume $\Gamma _ { 0 }$ is singular and let $a$ be an $m \times 1$ vector such that $\Gamma _ { 0 } a = 0$
and $a \neq 0 .$ Post-multiplying (1) by $a$ then yields

$$x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B _ { 0 } a = u _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } a .$$
(2)

Since the left-hand side $\mathrm { o f } \left( 2 \right)$ is non-random, the variance of the random vari-
able on the right-hand side must be zero. Hence $a ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } a = 0 ,$ which contradicts
the non-singularity of $\Sigma _ { 0 } .$
☐
✷

Given the non-singularity of $\Gamma _ { 0 }$ we may post-multiply (1) with $\Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } ,$ thus
obtaining the reduced form

$$y _ { t } ^ { \prime } = x _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Pi _ { 0 } + v _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } \quad \left( t = 1 , \ldots , n \right) ,$$
(3)

where

$$\Pi _ { 0 } = - B _ { 0 } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } , \quad v _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } . ,$$
(4)

Combining the observations we define

$$Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad X = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$$
(5)

and similarly

$$U _ { 0 } = \left( u _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 0 n } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad V _ { 0 } = \left( v _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 0 n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(6)

Then we rewrite the structure (1) as

$$Y \Gamma _ { 0 } + X B _ { 0 } = U _ { 0 }$$
(7)

and the reduced form (3) as

$$Y = X \Pi _ { 0 } + V _ { 0 } .$$
(8)

It is clear that the vectors $\left\{ v _ { 0 t } , t = 1 , \ldots , n \right\}$ are independent and iden-
tically distributed as $\mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ where $\Omega _ { 0 } = \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } { } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } .$ The loglikelihood
function expressed in terms of the reduced-form parameters $\left( \Pi , \Omega \right)$ follows
from (15.8.12):

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( \Pi , \Omega \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m n \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Omega | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } W ,$$
(9)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="373" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] The identification problem" -->

where

$$W = \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { t } ^ { \prime } - x _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } \left( y _ { t } ^ { \prime } - x _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Pi \right) = \left( Y - X \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } \left( Y - X \Pi \right) .$$
(10)

Rewriting (9) in terms of $\left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right) ,$ using $\Pi = - B \Gamma ^ { - 1 }$ and $\Omega = \Gamma ^ { - 1 ^ { \prime } } \Sigma \Gamma ^ { - 1 }$
,
we obtain

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } m n \log 2 \pi + \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Gamma | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Sigma | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } W ^ { * } ,$$
(1)

where

$$W ^ { * } = \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Gamma + x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( y _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Gamma + x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B \right) = \left( Y \Gamma + X B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( Y \Gamma + X B \right) .$$
(12)

The essential feature of (11) is the presence of the Jacobian term $\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Gamma |$
of the transformation from $u _ { t }$ to $y _ { t } .$

There are two problems relating to the simultaneous equations model:
the identification problem and the estimation problem. We shall discuss the
identification problem first.

Exercise

1\. In (11) we write $\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Gamma |$ rather than $\log | \Gamma | .$ Why?


## 3 THE IDENTIFICATION PROBLEM

It is clear that knowledge of the structural parameters $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ implies
knowledge of the reduced-form parameters $\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) ,$ but that the converse is
not true. It is also clear that a non-singular transformation of (2.1), say

$$y _ { t } ^ { \prime } \Gamma _ { 0 } G + x _ { t } ^ { \prime } B _ { 0 } G = u _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } G ,$$
(1)

leads to the same loglikelihood (2.11) and the same reduced-form parameters
$\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) .$ We say that $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ and $\left( B _ { 0 } G , \Gamma _ { 0 } G , G ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } G \right)$ are observation-
ally equivalent, and that therefore $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ is $n o t$ identified. The following
definition makes these concepts precise.


### Definition

Let $z = \left( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ be a vector of random observations with continuous
density function $h \left( z ; \gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ where $\gamma _ { 0 }$ is a p-dimensional parameter vector lying
in an open set $\Gamma \subset \mathrm { R } ^ { p } .$ Let $\Lambda \left( \gamma ; z \right)$ be the loglikelihood function. Then

(i) two parameter points $\gamma$ and $\gamma ^ { * }$ are observationally equivalent if $\Lambda \left( \gamma ; z \right) =$
$\Lambda \left( \gamma ^ { * } ; z \right)$ for all z,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="374" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

(ii) a parameter point $\gamma$ in $\Gamma$ is (globally) identified if there is no other point
in $\Gamma$ which is observationally equivalent,

(iii) a parameter point $\gamma$ in $\Gamma$ is locally identified if there exists an open neigh-
bourhood $N \left( \gamma \right)$ of $\gamma$ such that no other point of $N \left( \gamma \right)$ is observationally
equivalent $\mathrm { t o } \gamma$ y.

The following assumption is essential for the reduced-form parameter $\Pi _ { 0 }$
to be identified.


### Assumption 2 (rank)

The $n \times k$ matrix $X$ has full column rank $k .$


### Theorem 1

Consider the simultaneous equations model (2.1) under the normality assump-
tion (Assumption 1) and rank condition (Assumption 2). Then, (i) the joint
density of $\left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right)$ depends on $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ only through the reduced-form
parameters $\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right)$ and (ii) $\Pi _ { 0 }$ and $\Omega _ { 0 }$ are globally identified.

$P r o o f .$ Since $Y = \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime }$ is normally distributed, its density function
depends only on its first two moments,

$$\mathcal{E} Y = X \Pi _ { 0 } , \quad \mathcal{V} \left( \mathrm { v e c } Y \right) = \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { n } .$$
(2)

Now, $X$ has full column rank, so $X ^ { \prime } X$ is non-singular and hence knowledge
of these two moments is equivalent to knowledge of $\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) .$ Thus the den-
sity of $Y$ depends only on $\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) .$ This proves (i). But it also shows that
if we know the density of $Y ,$ we know the value of $\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) ,$ thus proving (ii).
☐

As a consequence of Theorem 1, a structural parameter of $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$
is identified if and only if its value can be deduced from the reduced-form
parameters $\left( \Pi _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } \right) .$ Since without a priori restrictions on $\left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right)$ none of
the structural parameters are identified (why not?), we introduce constraints

$$\psi _ { i } \left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right) = 0 \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , r \right) .$$
(3)

The identifiability of the structure $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ satisfying (3) then depends on
the uniqueness of solutions of

$$\Pi _ { 0 } \Gamma + B = 0 ,$$
(4)

$$\Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \Gamma - \Sigma = 0 ,$$
$$\psi _ { i } \left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right) = 0 \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , r \right) .$$
(5)
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="375" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 4 ] Identification with linear constraints on $B$ and $\Gamma$ only" -->


## 4 IDENTIFICATION WITH LINEAR CONSTRAINTS ON $B$ AND T ONLY

In this section we shall assume that all prior information is in the form of
linear restrictions on $B$ and $\Gamma ,$ apart from the obvious symmetry constraints
on $\Sigma .$ We shall prove our next theorem.


### Theorem 2

Consider the simultaneous equations model (2.1) under the normality assump-
tion (Assumption 1) and rank condition (Assumption 2). Assume further that
prior information is available in the form of linear restrictions on $B$ and $\Gamma :$

$$R _ { 1 } \mathrm { v e c } B + R _ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma = r .$$
(1)

Then $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ is globally identified if and only if the matrix

$$R _ { 1 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes B _ { 0 } \right) + R _ { 2 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma _ { 0 } \right)$$ ☒
(2)

has full column rank $m ^ { 2 } .$

Proof. The identifiability of the structure $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ depends on the unique-
ness of solutions of

$$\Pi _ { 0 } \Gamma + B = 0$$
$$\Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \Gamma - \Sigma = 0$$
(3)
(4)

$$R _ { 1 } \mathrm { v e c } B + R _ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma - r = 0$$
$$\Sigma = \Sigma ^ { \prime } .$$
(5)
(6)
.

Now, (6) is redundant since it is implied by (4). From (3) we obtain

$$\mathrm { v e c } B = - \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Pi _ { 0 } \right) \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma ,$$
(7)

and from (4), $\Sigma = \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \Gamma .$ Inserting (7) into (5) we see that the identifiability
hinges on the uniqueness of solutions of the linear equation

$$\left( R _ { 2 } - R _ { 1 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Pi _ { 0 } \right) \right) \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma = r .$$ ☒
(8)

By Theorem 2.12, Equation (8) has a unique solution for vec if and only if
the matrix $R _ { 2 } - R _ { 1 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Pi _ { 0 } \right)$ has full column rank $m ^ { 2 } .$ Post-multiplying this
matrix by the non-singular matrix $I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma _ { 0 } ,$ we obtain (2).
☒ ☐
✷


## 5 IDENTIFICATION WITH LINEAR CONSTRAINTS ON $B ,$ $\Gamma$ AND $\Sigma$

In Theorem 2 we obtained a global result, but this is only possible if the
constraint functions are linear in $B$ and $\Gamma$ and independent of $\Sigma .$ The reason

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="376" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

is that, even with linear constraints on $B ,$ $\Gamma$ and $\Sigma ,$ our problem becomes one
of solving a system of non-linear equations, for which in general only local
results can be obtained.


### Theorem 3

Consider the simultaneous equations model (2.1) under the normality assump-
tion (Assumption 1) and rank condition (Assumption 2). Assume further that
prior information is available in the form of linear restrictions on $B ,$ $\Gamma$ and $\Sigma :$

$$R _ { 1 } \mathrm { v e c } B + R _ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma + R _ { 3 } v \left( \Sigma \right) = r .$$
(1)

Then $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ is locally identified if the matrix

$$W = R _ { 1 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes B _ { 0 } \right) + R _ { 2 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma _ { 0 } \right) + 2 R _ { 3 } D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$$
(2)

has full column rank $m ^ { 2 } .$

Remark. If we define the parameter set $P$ as the set of all $\left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right)$ such
that $\Gamma$ is non-singular and $\Sigma$ is positive definite, and the restricted parameter
set $P ^ { \prime }$ as the subset of $P \quad s a t i s f y i n g$ restriction (1), then condition (2), which
is sufficient for the local identification of $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right) ,$ becomes a necessary
condition as well, if it is assumed that there exists an open neighbourhood of
$\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ in the restricted parameter set $P ^ { \prime }$ in which the matrix

$$W \left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right) = R _ { 1 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes B \right) + R _ { 2 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma \right) + 2 R _ { 3 } D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Sigma \right)$$
(3)

has constant rank.

Proof. The identifiability of $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ depends on the uniqueness of solu-
tions of

$$\Pi _ { 0 } \Gamma + B = 0$$
(4)

$$\Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \Gamma - \Sigma = 0$$
$$R _ { 1 } \mathrm { v e c } B + R _ { 2 } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma + R _ { 3 } v \left( \Sigma \right) - r = 0 .$$
(5)
(6)

The symmetry of $\Sigma$ follows again from the symmetry of $\Omega _ { 0 }$ and $\left( 5 \right) .$ Equations
(4)-(6) form a system of non-linear equations (because of (5)) in B,I and
$v \left( \Sigma \right) .$ Differentiating (4)-(6) gives

$$\Pi _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } \Gamma + \mathrm { d } B = 0$$
(7)

$$\left( \mathrm { d } \Gamma \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \Gamma + \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \mathrm { d } \Gamma - \mathrm { d } \Sigma = 0$$
$$R _ { 1 } \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } B + R _ { 2 } \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma + R _ { 3 } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Sigma \right) = 0 ,$$
(8)
(9)

and hence, upon taking vecs in (7) and (8),

$$\left( I _ { m } \otimes \Pi _ { 0 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma + \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } B = 0$$
(10)

$$\left( \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { m } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma ^ { \prime } + \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma - \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Sigma = 0$$
(11)

$$R _ { 1 } \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } B + R _ { 2 } \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma + R _ { 3 } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Sigma \right) = 0 .$$
(12)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="377" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] Non-linear constraints" -->

Writing vec $\Sigma = D _ { m } v \left( \Sigma \right) ,$ vecT' $\Gamma ^ { \prime } = K _ { m }$ vecT and using Theorem 3.9(a), (11)
becomes

$$\left( I _ { m ^ { 2 } } + K _ { m } \right) \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma - D _ { m } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Sigma \right) = 0 .$$
(13)

From (10), (13) and (12) we obtain the Jacobian matrix

$$J \left( \Gamma \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} I _ { m } \otimes \Pi _ { 0 } & I _ { m } \\ \left( I _ { m ^ { 2 } } + K _ { m } \right) \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \right) & 0 & - D _ { m } \\ R _ { 2 } & R _ { 1 } & R _ { 3 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(14)

where we notice that $J$ depends on $\Gamma ,$ but not on $B$ and $\Sigma .$ (This follows of
course from the fact that the only non-linearity in (4)-(6) is in T.) A sufficient
condition for $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ to be locally identifiable is that $J$ evaluated at $\Gamma _ { 0 }$ has
full column rank. (This follows essentially from the implicit function theorem.)

But, when evaluated at $\Gamma _ { 0 } ,$ we can write
$$J \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & I _ { m k } & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & - D _ { m } \\ W & R _ { 1 } & R _ { 3 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma _ { 0 } \right) ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ I _ { m } \otimes \Pi _ { 0 } & I _ { m k } \\ - 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } \right) & 0 \end{array} \right.$$
0
)
0
Im(m+1)/2
(15)

using the fact that $D _ { m } D _ { m } ^ { + } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( I _ { m ^ { 2 } } + K _ { m } \right) ,$ see Theorem 3.12(b). The second
partitioned matrix in (15) is non-singular. Hence $J \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } \right)$ has full column rank
if and only if the first partitioned matrix in (15) has full column rank; this,
in turn, is the case if and only if $W$ has full column rank.
✷

## 6 NON-LINEAR CONSTRAINTS

Exactly the same techniques are used in establishing Theorem 3 (linear con-
straints) enable us to establish Theorem 4 (non-linear constraints).


### Theorem 4

Consider the simultaneous equations model (2.1) under the normality assump-
tion (Assumption 1) and rank condition (Assumption 2). Assume that prior
information is available in the form of non-linear continuously differentiable
restrictions on $B ,$ $\Gamma$ and $\Sigma :$

$$f \left( B , \Gamma , v \left( \Sigma \right) \right) = 0 .$$
(1)

Then $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ is locally identified if the matrix

$$W = R _ { 1 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes B _ { 0 } \right) + R _ { 2 } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Gamma _ { 0 } \right) + 2 R _ { 3 } D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( I _ { m } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$$
(2)

has full column rank $m ^ { 2 } ,$ where the matrices

$$R _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial f } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } B \right) ^ { \prime } } , \quad R _ { 2 } = \frac { \partial f } { \partial \left( \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma \right) ^ { \prime } } , \quad R _ { 3 } = \frac { \partial f } { \partial \left( v \left( \Sigma \right) \right) ^ { \prime } }$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="378" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

are evaluated at $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , v \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } \right) \right) .$

Proof. The proof is left as an exercise for the reader.
☐
✷


## 7 FULL-INFORMATION MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD (FIML): THE INFORMATION MATRIX (GENERAL CASE)

We now turn to the problem of estimating simultaneous equations models,
assuming that sufficient restrictions are present for identification. Maximum
likelihood estimation of the structural parameters $\left( B _ { 0 } , \Gamma _ { 0 } , \Sigma _ { 0 } \right)$ calls for maxi-
mization of the loglikelihood function (2.11) subject to the a priori and iden-
tifying constraints. This method of estimation is known as full-information
maximum likelihood (FIML). Finding the FIML estimates involves non-linear
optimization and can be computationally burdensome. We shall first find the
information matrix for the rather general case where every element of $B ,$ $\Gamma$
and $\Sigma$ can be expressed as a known function of some parameter vector $\theta .$


### Theorem 5

Consider a random sample of size $n$ from the process defined by the simul-
taneous equations model (2.1) under the normality assumption (Assumption
1\) and the rank condition (Assumption 2). Assume that $\left( B , \Gamma , \Sigma \right)$ satisfies
certain a priori (non-linear) twice differentiable constraints

$$B = B \left( \theta \right) , \quad \Gamma = \Gamma \left( \theta \right) , \quad \Sigma = \Sigma \left( \theta \right) ,$$
(1)

where $\theta$ is an unknown parameter vector. The true value of $\theta$ is denoted by
$\theta _ { 0 } ,$ so that $B _ { 0 } = B \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$ $\Gamma _ { 0 } = \Gamma \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ and $\Sigma _ { 0 } = \Sigma \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) .$ Let $\Lambda _ { n } \left( \theta \right)$ be the
loglikelihood, so that

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( \theta \right) = - \left( m n / 2 \right) \log 2 \pi + \left( n / 2 \right) | \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Gamma | - \left( n / 2 \right) \log | \Sigma |$$

$$- \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \left( Y \Gamma + X B \right) ^ { \prime } \left( Y \Gamma + X B \right) .$$
(2)

Then the information matrix $\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$ determined by

$$- \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \left( \mathrm { d } \theta \right) ^ { \prime } \mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \mathrm { d } \theta ,$$
(3)

is given by

$$n \left( \begin{array}{} \Delta _ { 1 } \\ \Delta _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \begin{array}{} K _ { m + k , m } \left( C \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) + P _ { n } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } & - C ^ { \prime } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \\ - C \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } & \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \Delta _ { 1 } \\ \Delta _ { 2 } \end{array} \right)$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="379" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 7] FIML: information matrix (general case)" -->

where

$$\Delta _ { 1 } = \partial \mathrm { v e c } A ^ { \prime } / \partial \theta ^ { \prime } ,$$
$$\Delta _ { 2 } = \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Sigma / \partial \theta ^ { \prime } ,$$
$$P _ { n } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { n } \Pi _ { 0 } + \Omega _ { 0 } & \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { n } \\ Q _ { n } \Pi _ { 0 } & Q _ { n } \end{array} \right) , \quad Q _ { n } = \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X ,$$
$$\begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } = - B _ { 0 } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } , \Omega _ { 0 } = \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } { } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } , \\ A ^ { \prime } = \left( \Gamma ^ { \prime } : B ^ { \prime } \right) , G = \left( \Gamma _ { \circ } ^ { - 1 } : 0 \right) . \end{array} ,$$
$$C = \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } : 0 \right) ,$$
$$A ^ { \prime } = \left( \Gamma ^ { \prime } : B ^ { \prime } \right) ,$$

(5)

(6)

(7)

(8)

and $\Delta _ { 1 }$ and $\Delta _ { 2 }$ are evaluated at $\theta _ { 0 } .$

Proof. We rewrite the $\log$

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( \theta \right) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Gamma ^ { \prime } \Gamma | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Sigma | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z A ,$$
(9)

where $Z = \left( Y : X \right) .$ The first differential is

$$d \Lambda _ { n } = n \mathrm { t r } \Gamma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Gamma - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Sigma - \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z \mathrm { d } A$$

$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) \Sigma ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z A ,$$
(10)

and the second differential is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } = - n \mathrm { t r } \left( \Gamma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Gamma \right) ^ { 2 } + n \mathrm { t r } \Gamma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Gamma + \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \left( \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$- \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Sigma - \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z \mathrm { d } A$$
$$+ 2 \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) \Sigma ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z \mathrm { d } A - \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } A$$

$$- \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z A \left( \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) ^ { 2 } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z A \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Sigma .$$
(11)

It is easily verified that

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \left( Z ^ { \prime } Z \right) = P _ { n } ,$$
$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } A _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z \right) = C$$
(12)
(13)

and

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \left( A _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } Z A _ { 0 } \right) = \Sigma _ { 0 } .$$
(14)

Using these results we obtain

$$- \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Gamma \right) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { t r } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Gamma - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Sigma + \mathrm { t r } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) ^ { \prime } P _ { n } \mathrm { d } A - 2 \mathrm { t r } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) C \mathrm { d } A$$
$$+ \mathrm { t r } C d ^ { 2 } A + \mathrm { t r } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } d \Sigma \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Sigma$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Gamma \right) ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { t r } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) ^ { \prime } P _ { n } \mathrm { d } A$$
$$- 2 \mathrm { t r } \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) C \mathrm { d } A + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Sigma \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( K _ { m + k , m } \left( C \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) + P _ { n } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } A ^ { \prime }$$
$$- 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Sigma \right) ^ { \prime } \left( C \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } A ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Sigma \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Sigma .$$

(15)

Finally, since d vec $A ^ { \prime } = \Delta _ { 1 } \mathrm { d } \theta$ and d vec $\Sigma = \Delta _ { 2 } \mathrm { d } \theta ,$ the result follows.
✷
☐


## 8 FULL-INFORMATION MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD (FIML): THE ASYMPTOTIC VARIANCE MATRIX (SPECIAL CASE)

Theorem 5 provides us with the information matrix of the FIML estimator
$\widehat { \theta } ,$ assuming that $B ,$ $\Gamma$ and $\Sigma$ can all be expressed as (non-linear) functions
of a parameter vector $\theta .$ Our real interest, however, lies not so much in the
information matrix as in the inverse of its limit, known as the asymptotic
variance matrix. But to make further progress we need to assume more about
the functions $B ,$ $\Gamma$ and $\Sigma .$ Therefore we shall assume that $B$ and $\Gamma$ depend
on some parameter, say $\zeta ,$ functionally independent of $v \left( \Sigma \right) .$ If $\Sigma$ is also con-
strained, say $\Sigma = \Sigma \left( \sigma \right) ,$ where $\sigma$ and $\zeta$ are independent, the results are less
appealing (see Exercise 3).


### Theorem 6

Consider a random sample of size $n$ from the process defined by the simulta-
neous equations model (2.1) under the normality assumption (Assumption 1)
and the rank condition (Assumption 2). Assume that $B$ and $\Gamma$ satisfy certain
a priori (non-linear) twice differentiable constraints,

$$B = B \left( \zeta \right) , \quad \Gamma = \Gamma \left( \zeta \right) ,$$
(1)

where $\zeta$ is an unknown parameter vector, functionally independent of $v \left( \Sigma \right) .$
Then the information matrix $\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \zeta _ { 0 } , v \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } \right) \right)$ is given by

$$n \left( \begin{array}{} \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( K _ { m + k , m } \left( C \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) + P _ { n } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta & - \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \\ - D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( C \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta & \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } \end{array} \right)$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="381" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] FIML: asymptotic variance matrix (special case)" -->

where

$$\Delta = \left( \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } : \Delta _ { \beta } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad \Delta _ { \beta } = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } B ^ { \prime } } { \partial \zeta ^ { \prime } } , \quad \Delta _ { \gamma } = \frac { \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma ^ { \prime } } { \partial \zeta ^ { \prime } }$$
(3)

are all evaluated at $\zeta _ { 0 } ,$ and $C$ and $P _ { n }$ are defined in Theorem 5.

Moreover, if $Q _ { n } = \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X$ tends to a positive definite limit $Q$ as $n \rightarrow \infty ,$
so that $P _ { n }$ tends to a positive semidefinite limit, say $P ,$ then the asymptotic
variance matrix of the ML estimators $\widehat { \zeta }$ and $v \left( \widehat { \Sigma } \right)$ is

$$\left( \begin{array}{} V ^ { - 1 } & 2 V ^ { - 1 } & E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { + \prime } \\ 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } E _ { 0 } \Delta _ { \gamma } V ^ { - 1 } & 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } + 2 E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Delta _ { \gamma } V ^ { - 1 } \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } ^ { \prime } & \end{array} \right)$$
(4)

with

$$V = \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( \left( P - C ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } C \right) \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta , \quad E _ { 0 } = \Sigma _ { 0 } \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes I _ { m } . ,$$
(5)

Proof. We apply Theorem 5. Let $\theta = \left( \zeta ^ { \prime } , v \left( \Sigma \right) ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ Then

$$\Delta _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Gamma ^ { \prime } / \partial \theta ^ { \prime } \\ \partial \mathrm { v e c } B ^ { \prime } / \partial \theta ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \Delta _ { \gamma } & 0 \\ \Delta _ { \beta } & 0 \end{array} \right) = \left( \Delta : 0 \right)$$
(6)

and

$$\Delta _ { 2 } = \partial \mathrm { v e c } \Sigma / \partial \theta ^ { \prime } = \left( 0 : D _ { m } \right) .$$
(7)

Thus, (2) follows from (7.4). The asymptotic variance matrix is obtained as
the inverse of

$$\mathcal{F} = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathcal{F} _ { 1 1 } & \mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \\ \mathcal{F} _ { 2 1 } & \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right)$$
(8)

where

$$\mathcal{F} _ { 1 1 } = \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( K _ { m + k , m } \left( C \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) + P \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } = - \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m }$$
(9)
(10)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } .$$
(11)

We have

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} W ^ { - 1 } & - W ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \\ - \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 1 } W ^ { - 1 } & \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } + \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 1 } W ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right)$$
(12)

with

$$W = \mathcal{F} _ { 1 1 } - \mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 1 } .$$
(13)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="382" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

From (10) and (11) we obtain first

$$\mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } = - 2 \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m } D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
$$= - 2 \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { m } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } ,$$

(14)

using Theorem 3.13(b) and (2.2.4). Hence

$$\mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 1 } = 2 \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { m } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { \prime } \left( C \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta$$
$$= \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { m } \right) \left( I _ { m ^ { 2 } } + K _ { m } \right) \left( C \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta$$
$$= \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( C ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } C \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + K _ { m + k , m } \left( C \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \Delta ,$$

(15)

using Theorems 3.12(b) and 3.9(a). Inserting (9) and (15) in (13) yields

$$W = \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( \left( P - C ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } C \right) \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta = V .$$
(16)

To obtain the remaining terms of $\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 }$ we recall that $C = \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } : 0 \right)$ and
rewrite (14) as

$$\mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } = - 2 \left( \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } : \Delta _ { \beta } ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } { } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { m } \\ 0 \end{array} \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
$$= - 2 \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } \left( \Gamma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } { } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes I _ { m } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime }$$
$$= - 2 \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { + } { } ^ { \prime } .$$

(17)

Hence

$$- W ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } = 2 V ^ { - 1 } \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
(18)

and

$$\mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } + \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 1 } W ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { 1 2 } \mathcal{F} _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } + 4 D _ { m } ^ { + } E _ { 0 } \Delta _ { \gamma } V ^ { - 1 } \Delta _ { \gamma } ^ { \prime } E _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } .$$

(19)

This concludes the proof.

✷

### Exercises

1\. In the special case of Theorem 6 where $\Gamma _ { 0 }$ is a known matrix of constants
and $B = B \left( \zeta \right) ,$ show that the asymptotic variance matrix of $\widehat { \zeta }$ and $v \left( \widehat { \Sigma } \right)$
is

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \left( \Delta _ { \beta } ^ { \prime } \left( Q \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \Delta _ { \beta } \right) ^ { - 1 } & 0 \\ 0 & 2 D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Sigma _ { 0 } \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right) .$$

2\. How does this result relate to (8.11) in Theorem 15.4.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="383" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] LIML: first-order conditions" -->

3\. Assume, in addition to the set-up of Theorem 6, that $\Sigma$ is diagonal and
let $\sigma$ be the $m \times 1$ vector of its diagonal elements. Obtain the asymptotic
variance matrix of $\left( \widehat { \zeta } , \widehat { \sigma } \right) .$ In particular, show that $\mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \widehat { \zeta } \right) ,$ the asymptotic
variance matrix of $\widehat { \zeta } ,$ equals

$$\left( \Delta ^ { \prime } \left( K _ { m + k , m } \left( C \otimes C ^ { \prime } \right) + P \otimes \Sigma _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } - 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( C ^ { \prime } E _ { i i } C \otimes E _ { i i } \right) \right) \Delta \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

where $E _ { i i }$ denotes the $m \times m$ matrix with $\mathrm { a }$ one in the $i$ i-th diagonal
position and zeros elsewhere.


## 9 LIMITED-INFORMATION MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD (LIML): THE FIRST-ORDER CONDITIONS

In contrast to the FIML method of estimation, the limited-information max-
imum likelihood (LIML) method estimates the parameters of a single struc-
tural equation, say the first, subject only to those constraints that involve
the coefficients of the equation being estimated. We shall only consider the
standard case where all constraints are of the exclusion type. Then LIML can
be represented as a special case of FIML where every equation (apart from
the first) is just identified. Thus we write

$$y = Y \gamma _ { 0 } + X _ { 1 } \beta _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 }$$
(1)

$$Y = X _ { 1 } \Pi _ { 0 1 } + X _ { 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } + V _ { 0 } .$$
(2)

The matrices $\Pi _ { 0 1 }$ and $\Pi _ { 0 2 }$ are unrestricted. The LIML estimates of $\beta _ { 0 }$ and
$\gamma _ { 0 }$ in Equation (1) are then defined as the $\mathrm { M I }$ estimates of $\beta _ { 0 }$ and $\gamma _ { 0 }$ in the
system (1)-(2).

We shall first obtain the first-order conditions.


### Theorem 7

Consider a single equation from a simultaneous equations system,

$$y = Y \gamma _ { 0 } + X _ { 1 } \beta _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } ,$$
(3)

completed by the reduced form of $Y$ ,

$$Y = X _ { 1 } \Pi _ { 0 1 } + X _ { 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } + V _ { 0 } ,$$
(4)

where $y \left( n \times 1 \right)$ and $Y \left( n \times m \right)$ contain the observations on the endogenous
variables, $X _ { 1 } \left( n \times k _ { 1 } \right)$ and $X _ { 2 } \left( n \times k _ { 2 } \right)$ are exogenous (non-random), and $u _ { 0 } \left( n \times \right.$
1\) and $V _ { 0 } \left( n \times m \right)$ are random disturbances. We assume that the $n$ rows of
$\left( u _ { 0 } : V _ { 0 } \right)$ are independent and identically distributed as $\mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Psi _ { 0 } \right) ,$ where $\Psi _ { 0 }$
is a positive definite $\left( m + 1 \right) \times \left( m + 1 \right)$ matrix partitioned as

$$\Psi _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } & \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \\ \theta _ { 0 } & \Omega _ { 0 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="384" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

There are $m + k _ { 1 } + m \left( k _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( m + 1 \right) \left( m + 2 \right)$ parameters to be estimated,
namely $\gamma _ { 0 } \left( m \times 1 \right) ,$ $\beta _ { 0 } \left( k _ { 1 } \times 1 \right) ,$ $\Pi _ { 0 1 } \left( k _ { 1 } \times m \right) ,$ $\Pi _ { 0 2 } \left( k _ { 2 } \times m \right) ,$ $\sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\theta _ { 0 } \left( m \times 1 \right)$
and $v \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( m + 1 \right) \times 1 \right) .$ We define

$$X = \left( X _ { 1 } : X _ { 2 } \right) , \quad Z = \left( Y : X _ { 1 } \right) ,$$
(6)

$$\Pi = \left( \Pi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } : \Pi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } , \quad \alpha = \left( \gamma ^ { \prime } : \beta ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } . ,$$
(7)

If $\widehat { u }$ and $\widehat { V }$ are solutions of the equations

$$u = \left( I - Z \left( Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) Z \right) ^ { - 1 } Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) \right) y ,$$
(8)

$$V = \left( I - X \left( X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) \right) Y ,$$
(9)

where $\left( X : u \right)$ and $\left( Z : V \right)$ are assumed to have full column rank, then the
$M L$ estimators of $\alpha _ { 0 } ,$ $\Pi _ { 0 }$ and $\Psi _ { 0 }$ are

$$\widehat { \alpha } = \left( Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - \widehat { V } \widehat { V } ^ { + } \right) Z \right) ^ { - 1 } Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - \widehat { V } \widehat { V } ^ { + } \right) y ,$$
(10)

$$\widehat { \Pi } = \left( X ^ { \prime } \left( I - \widehat { u } \widehat { u } ^ { + } \right) X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \left( I - \widehat { u } \widehat { u } ^ { + } \right) Y ,$$
$$\widehat { \Psi } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \begin{array}{} \widehat { u } ^ { \prime } \widehat { u } & \widehat { u } ^ { \prime } \widehat { V } \\ \widehat { V } ^ { \prime } \widehat { u } & \widehat { V } ^ { \prime } \widehat { V } \end{array} \right) .$$
(11)
(12)

Remark. To solve equations (8) and (9) we can use the following iterative
scheme. Choose $u ^ { \left( 0 \right) } = 0$ as the starting value. Then compute

$$V ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = V \left( u ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \right) = \left( I - X \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \right) Y$$
(13)

and $u ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = u \left( V ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) ,$ $V ^ { \left( 2 \right) } = V \left( u ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) ,$ and $\mathrm { S O }$ on. $\mathrm { I f }$ this scheme converges, a
solution has been found.

Proof. Given (6) and (7) we may rewrite (3) and (4) as

$$y = Z \alpha _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } , \quad Y = X \Pi _ { 0 } + V _ { 0 } .$$
(14)

We define $W = \left( u : V \right) ,$ where

$$u = u \left( \alpha \right) = y - Z \alpha , \quad V = V \left( \Pi \right) = Y - X \Pi .$$
(15)

Then we can write the loglikelihood function as

$$\Lambda \left( \alpha , \pi , \psi \right) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Psi | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } W ^ { \prime } ,$$
(16)

where $\pi = \mathrm { v e c } \Pi ^ { \prime }$ and $\psi = v \left( \Psi \right) .$ The first differential is

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Psi ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Psi + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } W ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(17)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="385" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 9 ] LIML: first-order conditions" -->

Since $\mathrm { d } W = - \left( Z \mathrm { d } \alpha , X \mathrm { d } \Pi \right)$ and

$$\Psi ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { \eta ^ { 2 } } \left( \begin{array}{} 1 & - \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \\ - \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta & \eta ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right)$$
(18)

where $\eta ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } - \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta ,$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } = - \left( 1 / \eta ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } u - \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } u - \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } Z ^ { \prime } V \Omega ^ { - 1 } , \right.$$

$$\left. + \mathrm { t r } V \left( \eta ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(19)

and hence

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( \Psi ^ { - 1 } W ^ { \prime } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } - n \Psi ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \Psi + \left( 1 / \eta ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } \left( Z ^ { \prime } u - Z ^ { \prime } V \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta \right)$$

$$- \left( 1 / \eta ^ { 2 } \right) \mathrm { t r } \left( X ^ { \prime } u \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } - X ^ { \prime } V \left( \eta ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(20)

Hence the first-order conditions are

$$\Psi = \left( 1 / n \right) W ^ { \prime } W$$
(21)

$$Z ^ { \prime } u = Z ^ { \prime } V \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta$$
(22)

$$X ^ { \prime } V \left( \eta ^ { 2 } \Omega ^ { - 1 } + \Omega ^ { - 1 } \theta \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) = X ^ { \prime } u \theta ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } .$$
(23)

Post-multiplying (23) by $\Omega ^ { - 1 } - \left( 1 / \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \theta \theta ^ { \prime }$ yields

$$\sigma ^ { 2 } X ^ { \prime } V = X ^ { \prime } u \theta ^ { \prime } .$$
(24)

Inserting $\sigma ^ { 2 } = u ^ { \prime } u / n ,$ $\Omega = V ^ { \prime } V / n$ and $\theta = V ^ { \prime } u / n$ in (22) and (24) gives

$$Z ^ { \prime } u = Z ^ { \prime } V \left( V ^ { \prime } V \right) ^ { - 1 } V ^ { \prime } u$$
$$X ^ { \prime } V = X ^ { \prime } \left( 1 / u ^ { \prime } u \right) u u ^ { \prime } V$$
(25)
(26)

and hence, since $u = y - Z \alpha$ and $V = Y - X \Pi ,$

$$Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) Z \alpha = Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) y$$
$$X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) X \Pi = X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) Y .$$
(27)
(28)

Since $\left( X : u \right)$ and $\left( Z : V \right)$ have full column rank, the matrices $Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) Z$
and $X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) X$ are non-singular. This gives

$$Z \alpha = Z \left( Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) Z \right) ^ { - 1 } Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) y$$
$$X \Pi = X \left( X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) Y .$$
(29)
(30)

Hence, we can express $u$ in terms of $V$ and $V$ in terms of $u$ as follows:

$$u = \left( I - Z \left( Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) Z \right) ^ { - 1 } Z ^ { \prime } \left( I - V V ^ { + } \right) \right) y$$
(31)

$$V = \left( I - X \left( X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { \prime } \left( I - u u ^ { + } \right) \right) Y .$$
(32)

Given a solution $\left( \widehat { u } , \widehat { V } \right)$ of these equations, we obtain $\widehat { \alpha }$ from (29), $\widehat { \Pi }$ from (30)
and $\widehat { \Psi }$ from (21).
✷

<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->


## 10 LIMITED-INFORMATION MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD (LIML): THE INFORMATION MATRIX

Having obtained the first-order conditions for LIML estimation, we proceed
to derive the information matrix.


## Theorem 8

Consider a single equation from a simultaneous equations system,

$$y = Y \gamma _ { 0 } + X _ { 1 } \beta _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } ,$$
(1)

completed by the reduced form of $Y$ ,

$$Y = X _ { 1 } \Pi _ { 0 1 } + X _ { 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } + V _ { 0 } .$$
(2)

Under the conditions of Theorem 7 and letting $\pi = \mathrm { v e c } \Pi ^ { \prime }$ and $\psi = v \left( \Psi \right) ,$ the
information matrix in terms of the parametrization $\left( \alpha , \pi , \psi \right)$ is

$$\mathcal{F} _ { n } \left( \alpha _ { 0 } , \pi _ { 0 } , \psi _ { 0 } \right) = n \left( \begin{array}{} \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \alpha } & \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } & \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \\ \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } & \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } & 0 \\ \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } & 0 & \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } \end{array} \right)$$
(3)

with

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \alpha } = \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) A _ { z z }$$
(4)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } = - \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( A _ { z x } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) = \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } ^ { \prime }$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } = \left( e ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes S ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { m + 1 } = \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } ^ { \prime }$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } = \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X \otimes \left( \eta _ { 0 } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \right)$$
(5)
(6)
(7)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m + 1 }$$
(8)

where $A _ { z z }$ and $A _ { z x }$ are defined as

$$A _ { z z } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X \Pi _ { 0 } + n \Omega _ { 0 } & \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X _ { 1 } \\ X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } X \Pi _ { 0 } & X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } X _ { 1 } \end{array} \right) , \quad A _ { z x } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X \\ X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } X \end{array} \right.$$
)
(9)

of orders $\left( m + k _ { 1 } \right) \times \left( m + k _ { 1 } \right)$ and $\left( m + k _ { 1 } \right) \times \left( k _ { 2 } + k _ { 1 } \right)$ respectively, $e =$
$\left( 1 , 0 , \ldots , 0 \right) ^ { \prime }$ of order $\left( m + 1 \right) \times 1 ,$ $\eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } ,$ and $S$ is the $\left( m + 1 \right) \times$
$\left( m + k _ { 1 } \right)$ selection matrix

$$S = \left( \begin{array}{} 0 & 0 \\ I _ { m } & 0 \end{array} \right) .$$
(10)

Proof. Recall from (9.17) that the first differential of the loglikelihood function
$\Lambda \left( \alpha , \pi , \psi \right)$ is

$$\mathrm { d } \Lambda = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Psi ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Psi + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } W ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(11)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="387" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] LIML: information matrix" -->

where $W = \left( u : V \right) ,$ $u = y - Z \alpha ,$ and $V = Y - X \Pi .$ Hence, the second
differential is

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \mathrm { t r } \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Psi - \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } W ^ { \prime }$$

$$+ 2 \mathrm { t r } W \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) \Psi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(12)

using the (obvious) facts that both $\Psi$ and $W$ are linear in the parameters, so
that $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Psi = 0$ and $\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } W = 0 .$ Let

$$W _ { 0 } = \left( u _ { 0 } : V _ { 0 } \right) = \left( w _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , w _ { 0 n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(13)

Then $\left\{ w _ { 0 t } , t = 1 , \ldots , n \right\}$ are independent and identically distributed as
$\mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Psi _ { 0 } \right) .$ Hence

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} W _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } W _ { 0 } = \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { n } w _ { 0 t } w _ { 0 t } ^ { \prime } = \Psi _ { 0 } ,$$
(14)

and also, since $\mathrm { d } W = - \left( Z \mathrm { d } \alpha : X \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ,$

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } W _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} - \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } & \\ 0 & \end{array} \right)$$
(15)

and

$$\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } W \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z z } \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) & \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z x } \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) \\ \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) & \left( 1 / n \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) \end{array} \right) .$$
(16)

Now, writing the inverse of $\Psi$ as in (9.18), we obtain

$$- \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \Lambda \left( \alpha _ { 0 } , \pi _ { 0 } , \psi _ { 0 } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Psi + 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } S ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Psi \right) \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } e$$
$$+ \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z z } \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) - \left( 2 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right)$$
$$+ \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \mathrm { t r } \left( \left( \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \Pi \right) ^ { \prime } \right.$$
$$\left. \left( \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X \right) \left( d \Pi \right) \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { d } v \left( \Psi \right) \right) ^ { \prime } D _ { m + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Psi \right)$$
$$+ 2 \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } \left( e ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes S ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { m + 1 } \mathrm { d } v \left( \Psi \right)$$
$$+ \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z z } \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right)$$
$$\left. - \left( 2 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \alpha \right) ^ { \prime } A _ { z x } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Pi ^ { \prime }$$
$$+ \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Pi ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \left( \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X \right) \right.$$
$$\left. \otimes \left( \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \right) \mathrm { d } \mathrm { v e c } \Pi ^ { \prime } ,$$
(17)

and the result follows.
