## 11 LIMITED-INFORMATION MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD (LIML): THE ASYMPTOTIC VARIANCE MATRIX

Again, the derivation of the information matrix in Theorem 8 is only an
intermediary result. Our real interest lies in the asymptotic variance matrix,
which we shall now derive.


### Theorem 9

Consider a single equation from a simultaneous equations system,

$$y = Y \gamma _ { 0 } + X _ { 1 } \beta _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 }$$
(1)

completed by the reduced form of $Y$ ,

$$Y = X _ { 1 } \Pi _ { 0 1 } + X _ { 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } + V _ { 0 } .$$
(2)

Assume, in addition to the conditions of Theorem 7, that $\Pi _ { 0 2 }$ has full column
rank $m$ and that $\left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } X$ tends to a positive definite $\left( k _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } \right) \times \left( k _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } \right)$
matrix $Q$ as $n \rightarrow \infty .$ Then, letting $\pi _ { 1 } = \mathrm { v e c } \Pi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ,$ $\pi _ { 2 } = \mathrm { v e c } \Pi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ,$ and $\omega =$
$v \left( \Omega \right) ,$ the asymptotic variance matrix of the $\mathrm { M L }$ estimators $\widehat { \alpha } = \left( \widehat { \beta } ^ { \prime } , \widehat { \gamma } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$
$\widehat { \pi } = \left( \widehat { \pi } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \widehat { \pi } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$ and $\widehat { \psi } = \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } , \widehat { \theta } ^ { \prime } , v \left( \widehat { \Omega } \right) ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime }$ is

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \pi } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \psi } \\ \mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \alpha } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \pi } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \psi } \\ \mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \alpha } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \pi } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \psi } \end{array} \right)$$
(3)

with

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \\ - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \end{array} \right)$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \pi } = \left( \begin{array}{} - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } & P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \\ \left( Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } & - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right)$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \psi } = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} - 2 P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 2 P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
(4)
(5)
(6)

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \pi } = \left( \begin{array}{} Q ^ { 1 1 } & Q ^ { 1 2 } \\ Q ^ { 2 1 } & Q ^ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} H _ { * } ^ { 1 1 } & H _ { * } ^ { 1 2 } \\ H _ { * } ^ { 2 1 } & H _ { * } ^ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) \otimes \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$$
(7)

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \psi } = \left( \begin{array}{} 2 Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & 0 \\ - 2 \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & - \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \psi } =$$

where

$$Q = \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { 1 1 } & Q _ { 1 2 } \\ Q _ { 2 1 } & Q _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) = \left( Q _ { 1 } : Q _ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$Q ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} Q ^ { 1 1 } & Q ^ { 1 2 } \\ Q ^ { 2 1 } & Q ^ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } G ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } G ^ { - 1 } \\ - G ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & G ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
$$P _ { 1 } = \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } G \Pi _ { 0 2 } , \quad P _ { 2 } = \Pi _ { 0 1 } ^ { \prime } + \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(10)
(11)
(12)

$$G = Q _ { 2 2 } - Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } , \quad H = G ^ { - 1 } - \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(13)

and

$$H _ { * } = \left( \begin{array}{} H _ { * } ^ { 1 1 } & H _ { * } ^ { 1 2 } \\ H _ { * } ^ { 2 1 } & H _ { * } ^ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } H Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } H \\ - H Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & H \end{array} \right) .$$
(14)

Proof. Theorem 8 gives the information matrix. The asymptotic information
matrix, denoted as $\mathcal{F} ,$ is obtained as the limit of $\left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{F} _ { n }$ for $n \rightarrow \infty .$ We find

$$\mathcal{F} = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \alpha } & \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } & \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \\ \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } & \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } & 0 \\ \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } & 0 & \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } \end{array} \right)$$
(15)

with

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \alpha } = \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) A _ { z z }$$
(16)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } = - \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( A _ { z x } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) = \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } ^ { \prime }$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } = \left( e ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes S ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { m + 1 } = \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } ^ { \prime }$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } = \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( Q \otimes \left( \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } + \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \right)$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } D _ { m + 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ,$$
(17)
(18)
(19)
(20)

where $A _ { z z }$ and $A _ { z x }$ are now defined as the limits of (10.9):

$$A _ { z z } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q \Pi _ { 0 } + \Omega _ { 0 } & \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 1 } \\ Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Pi _ { 0 } & Q _ { 1 1 } \end{array} \right) , \quad A _ { z x } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q \\ Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(21)

and e, $\eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ and $S$ are defined in Theorem 8.

It follows from Theorem 1.3 that

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \pi } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \psi } \\ \mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \alpha } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \pi } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \psi } \\ \mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \alpha } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \pi } & \mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \psi } \end{array} \right)$$
(22)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="390" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

with

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } = \left( \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \alpha } - \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } - \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \pi } = - \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \psi } = - \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \pi } = \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } + \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \psi } = \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \psi } = \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } + \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } .$$
(23)
(24)
(25)
(26)
(27)
(28)

To evaluate $\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } ,$ which is the asymptotic variance matrix of $\widehat { \alpha } ,$ we need some
intermediary results:

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } = Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( \Omega _ { 0 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right)$$
(29)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } = - A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$$
(30)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \pi } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } = \left( \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \right) A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(31)

and also, using Theorems 3.13(d), 3.13(b), 3.12(b) and $3 . 9 \left( \mathrm { a } \right) ,$

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } = 2 D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
(32)

$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } = 2 \left( e ^ { \prime } \otimes S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
$$\mathcal{F} _ { \alpha \psi } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \psi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \psi \alpha } = \left( e ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } e \right) S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } S = \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } S ,$$
(33)
(34)

since $S ^ { \prime } e = 0 .$ Hence

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } = \left( \left( 1 / \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( A _ { z z } - A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } - S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } S \right) + \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

$$= \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$
(35)

It is not difficult to partition the expression for $\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha }$ in (35). Since

$$A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 1 } ^ { \prime } & \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \\ I _ { k _ { 1 } } & 0 \end{array} \right) ,$$
(36)

we have

$$A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q \Pi _ { 0 } & \Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 1 } \\ Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Pi _ { 0 } & Q _ { 1 1 } \end{array} \right)$$
(37)

and

$$\left( A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \\ - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \end{array} \right)$$
(38)

with $P _ { 1 }$ and $P _ { 2 }$ defined in (12). Hence

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \\ - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(39)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="391" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] LIML: asymptotic variance matrix" -->

We now proceed to obtain expressions for the other blocks of $\mathcal{F} ^ { - 1 } .$ We have

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \pi } = \left( \begin{array}{} P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \\ - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 1 } ^ { \prime } & \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \\ I _ { k _ { 1 } } & 0 \end{array} \right) \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$$

$$= \left( \begin{array}{} - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } & P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \\ \left( Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } \right) \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } & \end{array} \right) ,$$
(40)

and

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \psi } = - 2 \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \left( e ^ { \prime } \otimes S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
$$= - 2 \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \left( \begin{array}{} \theta _ { 0 } & \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
$$= - 2 \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \\ - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } P _ { 2 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \theta _ { 0 } & \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
$$= - 2 \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & \frac { 1 } { 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ - P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & - \frac { 1 } { 2 } P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right) ,$$

(41)

using Theorem 3.17. Further

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \pi } = Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( \Omega _ { 0 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) + Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } \right) \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$$

$$= Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( Q ^ { - 1 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 } \right) \otimes \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .$$
.
(42)

With $Q$ and $G$ as defined in (10) and (13) one easily verifies that

$$Q ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } G ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } G ^ { - 1 } \\ - G ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & G ^ { - 1 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(43)

Also,

$$Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } + Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & - Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \\ - \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } & \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right) .$$

(44)

Hence

$$Q ^ { - 1 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } A _ { z x } Q ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } H Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } } & { - Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } H } \\ { - H Q _ { 2 1 } Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } } & { H } \end{array} \right)$$

(45)

where $H$ is defined in (13). Inserting (43) and (45) in (42) gives (7).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="392" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Simultaneous equations [Ch. 16" -->

Next,

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \pi \psi } = - \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \pi } ^ { - 1 } \mathcal{F} _ { \pi \alpha } \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \psi }$$
$$= \left( Q ^ { - 1 } A _ { z x } ^ { \prime } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} - 2 P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 2 P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} \Pi _ { 0 1 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & I \otimes \theta _ { 0 } \\ \Pi _ { 0 2 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} - 2 P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & - P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 2 P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} 2 Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & Q _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } Q _ { 1 2 } \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & 0 \\ - 2 \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & - \Pi _ { 0 2 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } & 0 \end{array} \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

(46)

and finally

$$\mathcal{F} ^ { \psi \psi } = 2 D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } + 4 D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( e \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } S \right) \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } \left( e ^ { \prime } \otimes S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$ ☒

$$= 2 D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } + 4 D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( e e ^ { \prime } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } S \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$ ☒ ☒
(47)

Using Theorem 3.15 we find

$$D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( \Psi _ { 0 } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } }$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } & \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } & \left( \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + \prime } \\ & \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \theta _ { 0 } & \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \Omega _ { 0 } + \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) & \left( \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } \\ D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \otimes \theta _ { 0 } \right) & D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) & D _ { m } ^ { + } \left( \Omega _ { 0 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m } ^ { + } ^ { \prime } \end{array} \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

(48)

and

$$D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + } \left( e e ^ { \prime } \otimes \Psi _ { 0 } S \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } \right) D _ { m + 1 } ^ { + ^ { \prime } } = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & \frac { 1 } { 2 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { 0 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & \frac { 1 } { 4 } \Omega _ { 0 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right)$$
(49)

because

$$\Psi _ { 0 } S \mathcal{F} ^ { \alpha \alpha } S ^ { \prime } \Psi _ { 0 } = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } \\ \Omega _ { 0 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } & \Omega _ { 0 } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 0 } \end{array} \right) .$$
(50)

This concludes the proof.

✷

Exercises

1\. Show that

$$A _ { z x } = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) \mathcal{E} Z ^ { \prime } X .$$

2\. Hence prove that

$$\mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \widehat { \alpha } \right) = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 / n \right) \left( \mathcal{E} Z ^ { \prime } X \right) \left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathcal{E} X ^ { \prime } Z \right) \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

(see Holly and Magnus 1988).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="393" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

3\. Let $\theta _ { 0 } = \left( \theta _ { 0 1 } ^ { \prime } , \theta _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } .$ What is the interpretation of the hypothesis
$\theta _ { 0 2 } = 0$

4\. Show that

$$\mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \widehat { \theta } _ { 2 } \right) = \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \left( \Omega _ { 2 2 } ^ { 0 } + \Omega _ { 2 } ^ { 0 ^ { \prime } } P _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Omega _ { 2 } ^ { 0 } \right) + \theta _ { 0 2 } \theta _ { 0 2 } ^ { \prime } ,$$

where

$$\Omega _ { 0 } = \left( \begin{array}{} \Omega _ { 1 1 } ^ { 0 } & \Omega _ { 1 2 } ^ { 0 } \\ \Omega _ { 2 1 } ^ { 0 } & \Omega _ { 2 2 } ^ { 0 } \end{array} \right) = \left( \Omega _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } : \Omega _ { 2 } ^ { 0 } \right)$$

is partitioned conformably to $\theta _ { 0 }$ (see Smith 1985). How would you test
the hypothesis $\theta _ { 0 2 } = 0$

5\. Show that $H _ { * } .$ is positive semidefinite.

6\. Hence show that

$$Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \left( \Omega _ { 0 } - \left( 1 / \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) \theta _ { 0 } \theta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) \leq \mathcal{V} _ { a s } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \widehat { \Pi } ^ { \prime } \right) \leq Q ^ { - 1 } \otimes \Omega _ { 0 } .$$


## BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§3-§6. The identification problem is thoroughly discussed by Fisher (1966).
See also Koopmans, Rubin and Leipnik (1950), Malinvaud (1966, Chapter
18), and Rothenberg (1971). The remark in §5 is based on Theorem 5.A.2 in
Fisher (1966). See also Hsiao (1983).

§7-88. See also Koopmans, Rubin and Leipnik (1950) and Rothenberg and
Leenders (1964).

§9. The fact that LIML can be represented as a special case of FIML where
every equation (apart from the first) is just identified is discussed by Godfrey
and Wickens (1982).

§10-§11. See Smith (1985) and Holly and Magnus (1988).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 17 Topics in psychometrics


### 1 INTRODUCTION

In this chapter we shall explore some of the optimization problems that occur
in psychometrics. Most of these are concerned with the eigenstructure of vari-
ance matrices, that is, with their eigenvalues and eigenvectors. The theorems
in this chapter fall into four categories. Thus, Sections 2-7 deal with princi-
pal components analysis. Here, a set of $p$ scalar random variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p }$
is transformed linearly and orthogonally into an equal number of new ran-
dom variables $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { p } .$ The transformation is such that the new variables
are uncorrelated. The first principal component $v _ { 1 }$ is the normalized linear
combination of the $x$ variables with maximum variance; the second principal
component $v _ { 2 }$ is the normalized linear combination having maximum variance
out of all linear combinations uncorrelated with $v _ { 1 }$ and so on. One hopes that
the first few components account for a large proportion of the variance of the
$x$ variables. Another way of looking at principal components analysis is to
approximate the variance matrix of x, say $\Omega ,$ which is assumed known, 'as
well as possible' by another positive semidefinite matrix of lower rank. If $\Omega$
is not known we use an estimate $S$ of $\Omega$ based on a sample of x, and try to
approximate $S$ rather than $\Omega .$

Instead of approximating $S ,$ which depends on the observation matrix $X$
(containing the sample values of x), we can also attempt to approximate $X$
directly. For example, we could approximate $X$ be a lower-rank matrix, say
$\widetilde { X } .$ Employing a singular-value decomposition we can write $\widetilde { X } = Z A ^ { \prime } ,$ where
$A$ is semi-orthogonal. Hence, $X = Z A ^ { \prime } + E ,$ where $Z$ and $A$ have to be deter-
mined subject to $A$ being semi-orthogonal such that tr $E ^ { \prime } E$ is minimized. This
method of approximating $X$ is called one-mode component analysis and is dis-
cussed in Section 8. Generalizations to two-mode and multimode component
analysis are also discussed (Sections 10 and 11).

In contrast to principal components analysis, which is primarily concerned
with explaining the variance structure, factor analysis attempts to explain the
covariances of the variables $x$ in terms of a smaller number of non-observables,

<!-- PageNumber="395" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="396" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

called 'factors'. This typically leads to the model

$$x = A y + \mu + \epsilon ,$$
(1)

where $y$ and $\epsilon$ are unobservable and independent. One usually assumes that
$y \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , I _ { m } \right) ,$ $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Phi \right) ,$ where $\Phi$ is diagonal. The variance matrix of x
is then $A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi ,$ and the problem is to estimate $A$ and $\Phi$ from the data.
Interesting optimization problems arise in this context and are discussed in
Sections 12-15.

A final section deals with canonical correlations. Here, again, the idea is
to reduce the number of variables without sacrificing too much information.
Whereas principal components analysis regards the variables as arising from
a single set, canonical correlation analysis assumes that the variables fall
naturally into two sets. Instead of studying the two complete sets, the aim
is to select only a few uncorrelated linear combinations of the two sets of
variables, which are pairwise highly correlated.


### 2 POPULATION PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS

Let $x$ be a $p \times 1$ random vector with mean $\mu$ and positive definite variance
matrix $\Omega .$ It is assumed that $\Omega$ is known. Let $\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { p } > 0$ be the
eigenvalues of $\Omega$ and let $T = \left( t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } , \ldots , t _ { p } \right)$ be a $p \times p$ orthogonal matrix such
that

$$T ^ { \prime } \Omega T = \Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p } \right) .$$
(1)

If the eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ are distinct, then $T$ is unique apart from possible
sign reversals of its columns. If multiple eigenvalues occur, $T$ is not unique.
The i-th column of $T$ is, of course, an eigenvector of $\Omega$ associated with the
eigenvalue $\lambda _ { i } .$

We now define the $p \times 1$ vector of transformed random variables

$$v = T ^ { \prime } x$$
(2)

as the vector of principal components of x. The i-th element of v, say $v _ { i } ,$ is
called the i-th principal component.


#### Theorem 1

The principal components $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { p }$ are uncorrelated, and $\mathcal{V} \left( v _ { i } \right) = \lambda _ { i } ,$ $i =$
$1 , \ldots , p .$

Proof. We have

$$\mathcal{V} \left( v \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( T ^ { \prime } x \right) = T ^ { \prime } \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) T = T ^ { \prime } \Omega T = \Lambda ,$$
(3)

and the result follows.

✷
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="397" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 3 ] Optimality of principal components" -->


### 3 OPTIMALITY OF PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS

The principal components have the following optimality property.


#### Theorem 2

The first principal component $v _ { 1 }$ is the normalized linear combination of
$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p }$ with maximum variance. That is,

$$\max _ { a ^ { \prime } a = 1 } \mathcal{V} \left( a ^ { \prime } x \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( v _ { 1 } \right) = \lambda _ { 1 } .$$
(1)

The second principal component $v _ { 2 }$ is the normalized linear combination of
$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p }$ with maximum variance subject to being uncorrelated to $v _ { 1 } .$ That
is,

$$\max _ { a ^ { \prime } a = 1 } \mathcal{V} \left( a ^ { \prime } x \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( v _ { 2 } \right) = \lambda _ { 2 } ,$$
(2)

where $t _ { 1 }$ denotes the first column of $T .$ In general, for $i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ,$ the $i - t h$
principal component $v _ { i }$ is the normalized linear combination of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p }$ with
maximum variance subject to being uncorrelated to $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { i - 1 } .$ That is,

$$\max _ { \begin{array}{} a ^ { \prime } a = 1 \\ T _ { i - 1 } ^ { \prime } a = 0 \end{array} } \mathcal{V} \left( a ^ { \prime } x \right) = \mathcal{V} \left( v _ { i } \right) = \lambda _ { i } ,$$
(3)

where $T _ { i - 1 }$ denotes the $p \times \left( i - 1 \right)$ matrix consisting of the first $i \quad - \quad 1$ columns
of $T .$

Proof. We want to find a linear combination of the elements of x, say $a ^ { \prime } x$ such
that $\mathcal{V} \left( a ^ { \prime } x \right)$ is maximal subject to the conditions $a ^ { \prime } a = 1$ (normalization) and
$\mathcal{C} \left( a ^ { \prime } x , v _ { j } \right) = 0 ,$ $j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , i - 1 .$ Noting that

$$\mathcal{V} \left( a ^ { \prime } x \right) = a ^ { \prime } \Omega a$$
(4)

and also that

$$\mathcal{C} \left( a ^ { \prime } x , v _ { j } \right) = \mathcal{C} \left( a ^ { \prime } x , t _ { j } ^ { \prime } x \right) = a ^ { \prime } \Omega t _ { j } = \lambda _ { j } a ^ { \prime } t _ { j } ,$$
(5)

the problem boils down to


<table>
<tr>
<td>maximize</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>subject to</td>
<td>$$a ^ { \prime } \Omega a / a ^ { \prime } a$$ $$t _ { j } ^ { \prime } a = 0 \left( j = 1 , \ldots , i - 1 \right) .$$</td>
</tr>
</table>


(6)
(7)

From Theorem 11.6 we know that the constrained maximum is $\lambda _ { i }$ and is ob-
tained for $a = t _ { i } .$
✷
☐

Notice that the principal components are unique (apart from sign) if and
only if all eigenvalues are distinct. But Theorem 2 holds irrespective of mul-
tiplicities among the eigenvalues.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="398" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

Since principal components analysis attempts to 'explain' the variability
in $x ,$ we need some measure of the amount of total variation in $x$ that has
been explained by the first $r$ principal components. One such measure is

$$\mu _ { r } = \frac { \mathcal{V} \left( v _ { 1 } \right) + \cdots + \mathcal{V} \left( v _ { r } \right) } { \mathcal{V} \left( x _ { 1 } \right) + \cdots + \mathcal{V} \left( x _ { p } \right) } .$$
(8)

It is clear that

$$\mu _ { r } = \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } + \cdots + \lambda _ { r } } { \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } + \cdots + \lambda _ { p } } ,$$
(9)

and hence that $0 < \mu _ { r } \leq 1$ and $\mu _ { p } = 1 .$

Principal components analysis is only useful when, for a relatively small
value of r, $\mu _ { r }$ is close to one; in that case a small number of principal com-
ponents explain most of the variation in x.


### 4 A RELATED RESULT

Another way of looking at the problem of explaining the variation in $x$ is to
try and find a matrix $V$ of specified rank $r \leq p$ which provides the 'best'
approximation of $\Omega .$ It turns out that the optimal $V$ is a matrix whose $r$
non-zero eigenvalues are the $r$ largest eigenvalues of $\Omega .$


### Theorem 3

Let $\Omega$ be a given positive definite $p \times p$ matrix and let $1 \leq r \leq p .$ Let $\phi$ be a
real-valued function defined by

$$\phi \left( V \right) = \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega - V \right) ^ { 2 }$$
(1)

where $V$ is positive semidefinite of rank r. The minimum of $\phi$ is obtained for

$$V = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { r } \lambda _ { i } t _ { i } t _ { i } ^ { \prime }$$
(2)

where $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { r } ,$ are the $r$ largest eigenvalues of $\Omega$ and $t _ { 1 } , \ldots , t _ { r }$ are corre-
sponding orthonormal eigenvectors. The minimum value of $\phi$ is the sum of
the squares of the $p \quad - r$ smallest eigenvalues of $\Omega .$

Proof. In order to force positive semidefiniteness on $V ,$ we write $V = A A ^ { \prime }$
where $A$ is a $p \times r$ matrix of full column rank r. Let

$$\phi \left( A \right) = \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega - A A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$
(3)

Then we must minimize $\phi$ with respect to $A .$ The first differential is

$$\mathrm { d } \phi \left( A \right) = - 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega - A A ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \left( A A ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= - 4 \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } \left( \Omega - A A ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } A .$$

(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="399" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 5 ] Sample principal components" -->

The first-order condition is thus

$$\Omega A = A \left( A ^ { \prime } A \right) .$$
(5)

As $A ^ { \prime } A$ is symmetric it can be diagonalized. Thus, if $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { r }$ denote the
eigenvalues of $A ^ { \prime } A ,$ then there exists an orthogonal $r \times r$ matrix $S$ such that

$$S ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } A S = M = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { r } \right) .$$
(6)

Defining $Q = A S M ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$ we can now rewrite (5) as

$$\Omega Q = Q M , \quad Q ^ { \prime } Q = I _ { r } . ,$$
(7)

Hence, every eigenvalue of $A ^ { \prime } A$ is an eigenvalue of $\Omega ,$ and $Q$ is a corresponding
matrix of orthonormal eigenvectors.

Given (5) and (6) the objective function $\phi$ can be rewritten as

$$\phi \left( A \right) = \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { t r } M ^ { 2 } .$$
(8)
.

For a minimum we thus put $\mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { r }$ equal to $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { r } ,$ the $r$ largest eigen-
values of $\Omega .$ Then,

$$V = A A ^ { \prime } = Q M ^ { 1 / 2 } S ^ { \prime } S M ^ { 1 / 2 } Q ^ { \prime } = Q M Q ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { r } \lambda _ { i } t _ { i } t _ { i } ^ { \prime } .$$
(9)

This concludes the proof.

✷
☐


#### Exercises

1\. Show that the explained variation in $x$ as defined in (3.8) is given by
$\mu _ { r } = \mathrm { t r } V / \mathrm { t r } \Omega .$

2\. Show that if, in Theorem 3, we only require $V$ to be symmetric (rather
than positive semidefinite), we obtain the same result.


### 5 SAMPLE PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS

In applied research the variance matrix $\Omega$ is usually not known and must be
estimated. To this end we consider a random sample $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ of size
$n > p$ from the distribution of a random $p \times 1$ vector x. We let

$$\mathcal{E} x = \mu , \quad \mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \Omega ,$$
(1)

where both $\mu$ and $\Omega$ are unknown (but finite). We assume that $\Omega$
definite and denote its eigenvalues by $\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { p } > 0 .$

The observations in the sample can be combined into the $n \times p$ observation
matrix

$$X = \left( \begin{array}{} x _ { 1 1 } & \cdots & x _ { 1 p } \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ x _ { n 1 } & \cdots & x _ { n p } \end{array} \right) = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="400" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

The sample variance of x, denoted $S ,$ is

$$S = \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } M X = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { \prime } ,$$
(3)

where

$$\bar { x } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } , \quad M = I _ { n } - \left( 1 / n \right) n ^ { \prime } , \quad \imath = \left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(4)

The sample variance is more commonly defined as $S ^ { * } = \left( n / \left( n - 1 \right) \right) S ,$ which
has the advantage of being an unbiased estimator of $\Omega .$ We prefer to work
with $S$ as given in (3) because, given normality, it is the ML estimator of $\Omega .$

We denote the eigenvalues of $S$ by $l _ { 1 } > l _ { 2 } > \cdots > l _ { p } ,$ and notice that these
are distinct with probability one even when the eigenvalues of $\Omega$ are not all
distinct. Let $Q = \left( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { p } \right)$ be a $p \times p$ orthogonal matrix such that

$$Q ^ { \prime } S Q = L = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , \ldots , l _ { p } \right) .$$
(5)

We then define the $p \times 1$ vector

$$\widehat { v } = Q ^ { \prime } x$$
(6)

as the vector of sample principal components of x, and its i-th element $\widehat { v } _ { i }$ as
the i-th sample principal component.

Recall that $T = \left( t _ { 1 } , \ldots , t _ { p } \right)$ denotes a $p \times p$ orthogonal matrix such that

$$T ^ { \prime } \Omega T = \Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p } \right) .$$
(7)

We would expect that the matrices $S ,$ $Q$ and $L$ from the sample provide good
estimates of the corresponding population matrices $\Omega ,$ $T$ and $\Lambda .$ That this is
$i n d e e d$ the case follows from the next theorem.


#### Theorem 4 (Anderson)

$I f \quad x$ follows a p-dimensional normal distribution, then $S$ is the $\mathrm { M L }$ estimator of
$\Omega .$ If, in addition, the eigenvalues of $\Omega$ are all distinct, then the ML estimators
of $\lambda _ { i }$ and $t _ { i }$ are $l _ { i }$ and $q _ { i }$ respectively $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , p \right) .$

$R e m a r k .$ If the eigenvalues of both $\Omega$ and $S$ are distinct (as in the second part
of Theorem 4), then the eigenvectors $t _ { i }$ and $q _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$ are unique apart
from their sign. We can resolve this indeterminacy by requiring that the first
non-zero element in each column of $T$ and $Q$ is positive.


#### Exercise

1\. If $\Omega$ is singular, show that $r \left( X \right) \leq r \left( \Omega \right) + 1$ 1. Conclude that $X$ cannot
have full rank $p$ and $S$ must be singular, if $r \left( \Omega \right) \leq p - 2 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="401" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 6 ] Optimality of sample principal components" -->


### 6 OPTIMALITY OF SAMPLE PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS

In direct analogy with population principal components, the sample principal
components have the following optimality property.


#### Theorem 5

The first sample principal component $\widehat { v } _ { 1 }$ is the normalized linear combination
of x, say $a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } x ,$ with maximum sample variance. That is, the vector $a _ { 1 }$ maxi-
mizes $a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } S \bar { a _ { 1 } }$ subject to the constraint $a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 } = 1 .$ In general, for $i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ,$
the i-th sample principal component $\widehat { v } _ { i }$ is the normalized linear combination
of x, say $a _ { i } ^ { \prime } x ,$ with maximum sample variance subject to having zero sample
correlation with $\widehat { v } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \widehat { v } _ { i - 1 } .$ That is, the vector $a _ { i }$ maximizes $a _ { i } ^ { \prime } S a _ { i }$ subject
to the constraints $a _ { i } ^ { \prime } a _ { i } = 1$ and $q _ { j } ^ { \prime } a _ { i } = 0 ,$ $j = 1 , \ldots , i - 1 .$


### 7 SAMPLE ANALOGUE OF THEOREM 3

Precisely as in Section 4, the problem can also be viewed as one of approxi-
mating the sample variance matrix $S ,$ of rank $p ,$ by a matrix $V$ of given rank
$r \leq p .$


### Theorem 6

The positive semidefinite $p \times p$ matrix $V$ of given rank $r \leq p$ which provides
the best approximation to $S \equiv \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } M X$ in the sense that it minimizes
$\mathrm { t r } \left( S - V \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ is given by

$$V = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { r } l _ { i } q _ { i } q _ { i } ^ { \prime } .$$
(1)


### 8 ONE-MODE COMPONENT ANALYSIS

Let $X$ be the $n \times p$ observation matrix and $M = I _ { n } - \left( 1 / n \right) v \imath ^ { \prime } .$ As in (5.3) we
express the sample variance matrix $S$ as

$$S = \left( 1 / n \right) X ^ { \prime } M X .$$
(1)

In Theorem 6 we found the best approximation to $S$ by a matrix $V$ of given
rank. Of course, instead of approximating $S$ we can also approximate $X$ by a
matrix of given (lower) rank. This is attempted in component analysis.

In the one-mode component model we try to approximate the $p$ columns
of $X = \left( x ^ { 1 } , \ldots , x ^ { p } \right)$ by linear combinations of a smaller number of vectors
$z ^ { 1 } , \ldots , z ^ { r } .$ In other words, we write

$$x ^ { j } = \sum _ { h = 1 } ^ { r } \alpha _ { j h } z ^ { h } + e ^ { j } \quad \left( j = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$$
(2)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="402" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

and try to make the residuals $e ^ { j }$ 'as small as possible' by suitable choices of
$\left\{ z ^ { h } \right\}$ and $\left\{ \alpha _ { j h } \right\} .$ In matrix notation (2) becomes

$$X = Z A ^ { \prime } + E .$$
(3)

The $n \times r$ matrix $Z \quad i s \quad k n o w n$ as the core matrix. Without loss of generality
we may assume $A ^ { \prime } A = I _ { r }$ (see Exercise 1). Even with this constraint on $A$
there is some indeterminacy $\mathrm { i n } \left( 3 \right) .$ We can post-multiply $Z$ with an orthogonal
matrix $R$ and pre-multiply $A ^ { \prime }$ with $R ^ { \prime }$ without changing $Z A ^ { \prime }$ or the constraint
$A ^ { \prime } A = I _ { r } .$

Let us introduce the set of matrices

$$\mathcal{O} _ { p \times r } = \left\{ A : A \in \mathrm { R } ^ { p \times r } , A ^ { \prime } A = I _ { r } \right\} .$$
(4)

This is the set of all semi-orthogonal $p \times r$ matrices, also known as the Stiefel
manifold.

With this notation we can now prove Theorem 7.


#### Theorem 7 (Eckart and Young)

Let $X$ be a given $n \times p$ matrix and let $\phi$ be a real-valued function defined by

$$\phi \left( A , Z \right) = \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime }$$
(5)

where $A \in \mathcal{O} _ { p \times r }$ and $Z \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n \times r } .$ The minimum of $\phi$ is obtained when $A$
is a $p \times r$ matrix of orthonormal eigenvectors associated with the $r$ largest
eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } X$ and $Z = X \quad A .$ The 'best' approximation $\widetilde { X }$ (of rank r) to
$X$ is then $\widetilde { X } = X A A ^ { \prime } .$ The constrained minimum of $\phi$ is the sum of the $p \quad - \quad r$
smallest eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } X .$

Proof. Define the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( A , Z \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } L \left( A ^ { \prime } A - I \right) ,$$
(6)

where L is a symmetric $r \times r$ matrix of Lagrange multipliers. Differentiating
$\psi$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } L \left( \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) ^ { \prime } A + A ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } A \right)$$
$$= - \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) A \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) Z ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } L A ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } A$$

$$= - \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) A \left( \mathrm { d } Z \right) ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { t r } \left( Z ^ { \prime } X - Z ^ { \prime } Z A ^ { \prime } + L A ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } A .$$
(7)

The first-order conditions are

$$\left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) A = 0$$
(8)

$$Z ^ { \prime } X - Z ^ { \prime } Z A ^ { \prime } + L A ^ { \prime } = 0$$
$$A ^ { \prime } A = I .$$
(9)
(10)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="403" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 8 ] One-mode component analysis" -->

From (8) and (10) we find

$$Z = X A .$$
(11)

Post-multiplying both sides of (9) by A gives

$$L = Z ^ { \prime } Z - Z ^ { \prime } X A = 0 ,$$
(12)

in view of (10) and (11). Hence (9) can be rewritten as

$$\left( X ^ { \prime } X \right) A = A \left( A ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X A \right) .$$
(13)

Now, let $P$ be an orthogonal $r \times r$ matrix such that

$$P ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X A P = \Lambda _ { 1 } ,$$
(14)

where $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ is a diagonal $r \times r$ matrix containing the eigenvalues of $A ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } X A$ on
its diagonal. Let $T _ { 1 } = A P .$ Then (13) can be written as

$$X ^ { \prime } X T _ { 1 } = T _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } .$$
(15)

Hence $T _ { 1 }$ is a semi-orthogonal $p \times r$ matrix that diagonalizes $X ^ { \prime } X ,$ and the $r$ r
diagonal elements in $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ are eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } X$ .

Given $Z = X \quad A ,$ $\mathrm { w e }$ have

$$\left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } = X \left( I - A A ^ { \prime } \right) X ^ { \prime }$$
(16)

and thus

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { t r } X ^ { \prime } X - \mathrm { t r } \Lambda _ { 1 } .$$
(17)

To minimize (17), we must maximize tr $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ hence $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ contains the $r$ largest
eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } X ,$ and $T _ { 1 }$ contains eigenvectors associated with these $r$
eigenvalues. The 'best' approximation to $X$ is then

$$Z A ^ { \prime } = X A A ^ { \prime } = X T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(18)

so that an optimal choice is $A = T _ { 1 } ,$ $Z = X T _ { 1 } .$ From (17) it is clear that the
value of the constrained minimum is the sum of the $p - r$ smallest eigenvalues
of $X ^ { \prime } X .$
✷
☐

We notice that the 'best' approximation to $X ,$ say $\widetilde { X } ,$ is given by (18):
$\widetilde { X } = X A A ^ { \prime } .$ It is important to observe that $\widetilde { X }$ is part of a singular-value de-
composition of $X ,$ namely the part corresponding to the $r$ largest eigenvalues
of $X ^ { \prime } X .$ To see this, assume that $r \left( X \right) = p$ and that the eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } X$
are given by $\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { p } > 0 .$ Let $\Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p } \right)$ and let

$$X = S \Lambda ^ { 1 / 2 } T ^ { \prime }$$
(19)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="404" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

be a singular-value decomposition of $X ,$ with $S ^ { \prime } S = T ^ { \prime } T = I _ { p } .$ Let
☒

$$\Lambda _ { 1 } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { r } \right) , \quad \Lambda _ { 2 } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { r + 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p } \right)$$
(20)

and partition $S$ and $T$ accordingly as

$$S = \left( S _ { 1 } : S _ { 2 } \right) , \quad T = \left( T _ { 1 } : T _ { 2 } \right) . ,$$
(21)

Then

$$X = S _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + S _ { 2 } \Lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } .$$
(22)

From (19)-(21) we see that $X ^ { \prime } X T _ { 1 } = T _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ,$ in accordance with (15). $\mathrm { T h e }$
approximation $\widetilde { X }$ can then be written as
☒
˜

$$\widetilde { X } = X A A ^ { \prime } = X T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \left( S _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + S _ { 2 } \Lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = S _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } .$$
(23)
☒

This result will be helpful in the treatment of two-mode component analysis in
Section 10. Notice that when $r \left( Z A ^ { \prime } \right) = r \left( X \right) ,$ then $\widetilde { X } = X$ (see also Exercise
☒
3).
☒ ☒


### Exercises

1\. Suppose $r \left( A \right) = r ^ { \prime } \leq r .$ Use the singular-value decomposition of $A$ to
show that $Z A ^ { \prime } = Z ^ { * } A ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } ,$ where $A ^ { * } { } ^ { \bar { \prime } } A ^ { * } = I _ { r } .$ Conclude that we may
assume $A ^ { \prime } A = I _ { r } ,$ in (3).

2\. Consider the optimization problem

$$\text { minimize } \quad \phi \left( X \right)$$
$$F \left( X \right) = 0 .$$ ☒ ☒

If $F \left( X \right)$ is symmetric for all $X ,$ prove that the Lagrangian function is
☒ ☒

$$\psi \left( X \right) = \phi \left( X \right) - \mathrm { t r } L F \left( X \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

where $L$ is symmetric.

3\. If $X$ has rank $\leq r$ show that

$$\min \mathrm { t r } \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( X - Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } = 0$$ ☒

over all $A$ in $\mathcal{O} _ { p \times r }$ and $Z$ in $\mathbb{R} ^ { n \times r } .$


### 9 ONE-MODE COMPONENT ANALYSIS AND SAMPLE PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS

In the one-mode component model we attempted to approximate the $n \times p$
matrix $X$ by $Z A ^ { \prime }$ satisfying $A ^ { \prime } A = I _ { r } .$ The solution, from Theorem 7, is
☒

$$Z A ^ { \prime } = X T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="405" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 10 ] Two-mode component analysis" -->

where $T _ { 1 }$ is a $p \times r$ matrix of eigenvectors associated with the $r$ largest eigen-
values of $X ^ { \prime } X .$

If, instead of $X ,$ we approximate $M X$ by $Z A ^ { \prime }$ under the constraint $A ^ { \prime } A =$
$I _ { r } ,$ we find in precisely the same way

$$Z A ^ { \prime } = M X T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(2)

but now $T _ { 1 }$ is a $p \times r$ matrix of eigenvectors associated with the $r$ largest eigen-
values of $\left( M X \right) ^ { \prime } \left( M X \right) = X ^ { \prime } M X .$ This suggests that a suitable approximation
to $X ^ { \prime } M X$ is provided by

$$\left( Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } Z A ^ { \prime } = T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } M X T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = T _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$$
(3)

where $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ is an $r \times r$ matrix containing the $r$ largest eigenvalues of $X ^ { \prime } M X .$
Now, (3) is precisely the approximation obtained in Theorem 6. Thus one-
mode component analysis and sample principal components are tightly con-
nected.


### 10 TWO-MODE COMPONENT ANALYSIS

Suppose that our data set consists of a $2 7 \times 6$ matrix $X$ containing the scores
given by $n = 2 7$ individuals to each of $p = 6$ television commercials. A one-
mode component analysis would attempt to reduce $p$ from 6 to 2 (say). There
is no reason, however, why we should not also reduce n, say from 27 to 4.
This is attempted in two-mode component analysis, where the purpose is to
find matrices $A ,$ $B$ and $Z$ such that

$$X = B Z A ^ { \prime } + E$$
(1)

with $A ^ { \prime } A = I _ { r _ { 1 } }$ and $B ^ { \prime } B = I _ { r _ { 2 } } ,$ and 'minimal' residual matrix $E .$ (In our
example $r _ { 1 } = 2 ,$ $r _ { 2 } = 4$ When $r _ { 1 } = r _ { 2 }$ the result follows directly from
Theorem 7 and we obtain Theorem 8.


### Theorem 8

Let $X$ be a given $n \times p$ matrix and let $\phi$ be a real-valued function defined by

$$\phi \left( A , B , Z \right) = \mathrm { t r } \left( X - B Z A ^ { \prime } \right) \left( X - B Z A ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime }$$
(2)

where $A \in \mathcal{O} _ { p \times r } ,$ $B \in \mathcal{O} _ { n \times r }$ and $Z \in \mathbb{R} ^ { r \times r } .$ The minimum of $\phi$ is obtained
when $A ,$ $B$ and $Z$ satisfy

$$A = T _ { 1 } , \quad B = S _ { 1 } , \quad Z = \Lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } ,$$
(3)

where $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ is a diagonal $r \times r$ matrix containing the $r$ largest eigenvalues of
$X X ^ { \prime } \left( \text { and of } X ^ { \prime } X \right) ,$ $S _ { 1 }$ is an $n \times r$ matrix of orthonormal eigenvectors of $X X ^ { \prime }$
associated with these $r$ eigenvalues,

$$X X ^ { \prime } S _ { 1 } = S _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ,$$
(4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="406" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

and $T _ { 1 }$ is a $p \times r$ matrix of orthonormal eigenvectors of $X ^ { \prime } X$ defined by

$$T _ { 1 } = X ^ { \prime } S _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$$
(5)

The constrained minimum of $\phi$ is the sum of the $p - r$ smallest eigenvalues of
$X X ^ { \prime } .$

Proof. Immediate from Theorem 7 and the discussion following its proof.
☐
✷

In the more general case where $r _ { 1 } \neq r _ { 2 }$ the solution is essentially the same.
$A$ better approximation does not exist. Suppose $r _ { 2 } > r _ { 1 } .$ Then we can extend
$B$ with $r _ { 2 } - r _ { 1 }$ additional columns such that $B ^ { \prime } B = I _ { r _ { 2 } } ,$ and we can extend
$Z$ with $r _ { 2 } - r _ { 1 }$ additional rows of zeros. The approximation is still the same:
$B Z A ^ { \prime } = S _ { 1 } \Lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } .$ Adding columns to $B$ turns out to be useless; it does not
lead to a better approximation to $X ,$ since the rank of $B Z A ^ { \prime }$ remains $r _ { 1 } .$


### 11 MULTIMODE COMPONENT ANALYSIS

Continuing our example of Section 10, suppose that we now have an enlarged
data set consisting of a three-dimensional matrix $X$ of order $2 7 \times 6 \times 5$ con-
taining scores by $p _ { 1 } = 2 7$ individuals to each of $p _ { 2 } = 6$ television commer-
cials; each commercial is shown $p _ { 3 } = 5$ times to every individual. A three-
mode component analysis would attempt to reduce $p _ { 1 } ,$ $p _ { 2 }$ and $p _ { 3 }$ to, say,
$r _ { 1 } = 6 ,$ $r _ { 2 } = 2 ,$ $r _ { 3 } = 3 .$ Since, in principle, there is no limit to the number
of modes we might be interested in, let us consider the s-mode model. First,
however, we reconsider the two-mode case

$$X = B Z A ^ { \prime } + E .$$
(1)

We rewrite (1) as

$$x = \left( A \otimes B \right) z + e$$
(2)

where $x = \mathrm { v e c } X ,$ $z = \mathrm { v e c } Z$ and $e = \mathrm { v e c } E .$ This suggests the following
formulation for the s-mode component case:

$$x = \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes A _ { 2 } \otimes \cdots \otimes A _ { s } \right) z + e ,$$
(3)

where $A _ { i }$ is a $p _ { i } \times r _ { i }$ matrix satisfying $A _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { i } = I _ { r _ { i } } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right) .$ The data
vector $x$ and the 'core' vector $z$ can be considered as stacked versions of s-
dimensional matrices $X$ and $Z .$ The elements in $x$ are identified by $s$ indices
with the $i - \mathrm { t h }$ index assuming the values $1 , 2 , \ldots , p _ { i } .$ The elements are arranged
in such a way that the first index runs slowly and the last index runs fast.
The elements in $z$ are also identified by $s$ indices; the $i$ i-th index runs from 1
to $r _ { i } .$

The mathematical problem is to choose $A _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$ and $z$ in such a
way that the residual $e$ is 'as small as possible'.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="407" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] Multimode component analysis" -->


#### Theorem 9

Let $p _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , \ldots , p _ { s }$ and $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { s }$ be given integers $\geq 1 ,$ and put $p = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } p _ { i }$
and $r = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } r _ { i } .$ Let $x$ be a given $p \times 1$ vector and let $\phi$ be a real-valued
function defined by

$$\phi \left( A , z \right) = \left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } \left( x - A z \right)$$
(4)

where $A = A _ { 1 } \otimes A _ { 2 } \otimes \cdots \otimes A _ { s } ,$ $A _ { i } \in \mathcal{O} _ { p _ { i } \times r _ { i } }$ $\left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$ and $z \in \mathbb{R} ^ { r } .$ The
minimum of $\phi$ is obtained when $A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { s }$ and $z$ satisfy

$$A _ { i } = T _ { i } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right) , \quad z = \left( T _ { 1 } \otimes \cdots \otimes T _ { s } \right) ^ { \prime } x ,$$
(5)

where $T _ { i }$ is a $p _ { i } \times r _ { i }$ matrix of orthonormal eigenvectors associated with the
$r _ { i }$ largest eigenvalues of $X _ { i } ^ { \prime } T _ { \left( i \right) } T _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } .$ Here $T _ { \left( i \right) }$ denotes the $\left( p / p _ { i } \right) \times \left( r / r _ { i } \right)$
matrix

$$T _ { \left( i \right) } = T _ { 1 } \otimes \cdots \otimes T _ { i - 1 } \otimes T _ { i + 1 } \otimes \cdots \otimes T _ { s } ,$$
(6)

and $X _ { i }$ is the $\left( p / p _ { i } \right) \times p _ { i }$ matrix defined by

$$\mathrm { v e c } X _ { i } ^ { \prime } = Q _ { i } x \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$$
(7)

where

$$Q _ { i } = I _ { \alpha _ { i - 1 } } \otimes K _ { \beta _ { s - i } , p _ { i } } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$$
(8)

with

$$\alpha _ { 0 } = 1 , \quad \alpha _ { 1 } = p _ { 1 } , \quad \alpha _ { 2 } = p _ { 1 } p _ { 2 } , \quad \ldots , \quad \alpha _ { s } = p ,$$
(9)

and

$$\beta _ { 0 } = 1 , \quad \beta _ { 1 } = p _ { s } , \quad \beta _ { 2 } = p _ { s } p _ { s - 1 } , \quad \ldots , \quad \beta _ { s } = p . ,$$
(10)

The minimum value of $\phi$ is $x ^ { \prime } x - z ^ { \prime } z .$

Remark. The solution has to be obtained iteratively. Take $A _ { 2 } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ldots , A _ { s } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ as
starting values for $A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { s } .$ Compute $A _ { \left( 1 \right) } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } = A _ { 2 } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \otimes \cdots \otimes A _ { s } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } .$ Then form a
first approximate of $A _ { 1 } ,$ say $A _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ as the $p _ { 1 } \times r _ { 1 }$ matrix of orthonormal eigen-
vectors associated with the $r _ { 1 }$ largest eigenvalues of $X _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( 1 \right) } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } A _ { \left( 1 \right) } ^ { \left( 0 \right) ^ { \prime } } X _ { 1 } .$ Next,
use $A _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $A _ { 3 } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ldots , A _ { s } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ to compute $A _ { \left( 2 \right) } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } = A _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \otimes A _ { 3 } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \otimes \cdots \otimes A _ { s } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } ,$ and
form $A _ { 2 } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ the first approximate of $A _ { 2 } ,$ in a similar manner. Having computed
$A _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , A _ { s } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ we form a new approximate of $A _ { 1 } ,$ say $A _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } .$ This process is
continued until convergence.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="408" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

Proof. Analogous to the $p \times p$ matrices $Q _ { i }$ we define the $r \times r$ matrices

$$R _ { i } = I _ { \gamma _ { i - 1 } } \otimes K _ { \delta _ { s - i } , r _ { i } } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$$
(11)

where

$$\gamma _ { 0 } = 1 , \quad \gamma _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 } , \quad \gamma _ { 2 } = r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } , \quad \ldots , \quad \gamma _ { s } = r ,$$
(12)

and

$$\delta _ { 0 } = 1 , \quad \delta _ { 1 } = r _ { s } , \quad \delta _ { 2 } = r _ { s } r _ { s - 1 } , \quad \ldots , \quad \delta _ { s } = r . ,$$
(13)

We also define the $\left( r / r _ { i } \right) \times r _ { i }$ matrices $Z _ { i }$ by

$$\mathrm { v e c } Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } = R _ { i } z \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right) ,$$
(14)

and notice that

$$Q _ { i } \left( A _ { 1 } \otimes A _ { 2 } \otimes \cdots \otimes A _ { s } \right) R _ { i } ^ { \prime } = A _ { \left( i \right) } \otimes A _ { i } ,$$
(15)

where $A _ { \left( i \right) }$ is defined in the same way as $T _ { \left( i \right) } .$

Now, let $\psi$ be the Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( A , Z \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } \left( x - A z \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \mathrm { t r } L _ { i } \left( A _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { i } - I \right) ,$$
(16)

where $L _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$ is a symmetric $r _ { i } \times r _ { i }$ matrix of Lagrange multipliers.
We have

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = - \left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) z - \left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { d } z - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \mathrm { t r } L _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } A _ { i } .$$
(17)

Since $A = Q _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { \left( i \right) } \otimes A _ { i } \right) R _ { i }$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , s ,$ we obtain

$$\mathrm { d } A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } Q _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { \left( i \right) } \otimes \mathrm { d } A _ { i } \right) R _ { i }$$
(18)

and hence

$$\left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) z = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } Q _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { \left( i \right) } \otimes \mathrm { d } A _ { i } \right) R _ { i } z$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \left( \mathrm { v e c } X _ { i } ^ { \prime } - Q _ { i } A R _ { i } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { v e c } Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { \left( i \right) } \otimes \mathrm { d } A _ { i } \right) \mathrm { v e c }$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \left( \mathrm { v e c } \left( X _ { i } ^ { \prime } - A _ { i } Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } \right) \right) ^ { \prime } \left( A _ { \left( i \right) } \otimes d A _ { i } \right) \mathrm { v e c } Z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \mathrm { t r } Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } \left( X _ { i } - A _ { \left( i \right) } Z _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } A _ { i } .$$
$Z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$

(19)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="409" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 11 ] Multimode component analysis" -->

Inserting (19) in (17) we thus find

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = - \left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } A \mathrm { d } z - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } \mathrm { t r } \left( Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } \left( X _ { i } - A _ { \left( i \right) } Z _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) + L _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } A _ { i } ,$$
(20)

from which we obtain the first-order conditions

$$A ^ { \prime } \left( x - A z \right) = 0$$
$$Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } - Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } Z _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } + L _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } = 0 \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$$
(21)
(22)

$$A _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { i } = I _ { r _ { i } } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right) .$$
(23)

We find again

$$z = A ^ { \prime } x ,$$
(24)

from which it follows that $Z _ { i } = A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } A _ { i } .$ Hence $L _ { i } = 0$ and (21) can be
simplified to

$$\left( X _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } \right) A _ { i } = A _ { i } \left( A _ { i } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } A _ { i } \right) .$$
(25)

For $i = 1 , \ldots , s ,$ let $S _ { i }$ be an orthogonal $r _ { i } \times r _ { i }$ matrix such that

$$S _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } A _ { i } S _ { i } = \Lambda _ { i } \quad \left( \mathrm { d i a g o n a l } \right) .$$
(26)

Then (24) can be written as

$$\left( X _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } \right) \left( A _ { i } S _ { i } \right) = \left( A _ { i } S _ { i } \right) \Lambda _ { i } .$$
(27)
.

We notice that

$$\mathrm { t r } \Lambda _ { i } = \mathrm { t r } Z _ { i } ^ { \prime } Z _ { i } = z ^ { \prime } z = \lambda \left( \mathrm { s a y } \right) ,$$
(28)

is the same for $\mathrm { a l l } i$ i. Then

$$\left( x - A z \right) ^ { \prime } \left( x - A z \right) = x ^ { \prime } x - \lambda .$$
(29)

To minimize (28), we must maximize $\lambda$ hence $\Lambda _ { i }$ contains the $r _ { i }$ largest eigen-
values of $X _ { i } ^ { \prime } A _ { \left( i \right) } A _ { \left( i \right) } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } ,$ and $A S _ { i } = T _ { i } .$ Then, by (23),

$$A z = A A ^ { \prime } x = \left( A _ { 1 } A _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \cdots \otimes A _ { s } A _ { s } ^ { \prime } \right) x$$
$$= \left( T _ { 1 } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \cdots \otimes T _ { s } T _ { s } ^ { \prime } \right) x ,$$

(30)

and an optimal choice is $A _ { i } = T _ { i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , s \right)$ and $z = \left( T _ { 1 } \otimes \cdots \otimes T _ { s } \right) ^ { \prime } x .$
✷
☐


#### Exercise

1\. Show that the matrices $Q _ { i }$ and $R _ { i }$ defined in (8) and (11) satisfy

$$Q _ { 1 } = K _ { p / p _ { 1 } , p _ { 1 } } , \quad Q _ { s } = I _ { p } ,$$

and

$$R _ { 1 } = K _ { r / r _ { 1 } , r _ { 1 } } , \quad R _ { s } = I _ { r } .$$
