<!-- PageNumber="410" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->


### 12 FACTOR ANALYSIS

Let $x$ be an observable $p \times 1$ random vector with $\mathcal{E} x = \mu$ and $\mathcal{V} \left( x \right) = \Omega .$
The factor analysis model assumes that the observations are generated by the
structure

$$x = A y + \mu + \epsilon ,$$
(1)

where $y$ is an $m \times 1$ vector of non-observable random variables called 'common
factors', $A$ is a $p \times m$ matrix of unknown parameters called 'factor loadings',
and $\epsilon$ is a $p \times 1$ vector of non-observable random errors. It is assumed that
$y \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , I _ { m } \right) ,$ $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N} \left( 0 , \Phi \right) ,$ where $\Phi$ is diagonal positive definite, and that
$y$ and e are independent. Given these assumptions we find that $x \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \mu , \Omega \right)$
with

$$\Omega = A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi .$$
(2)

There is clearly a problem of identifying $A$ from $A A ^ { \prime } ,$ because if $A ^ { * } = A T$
is an orthogonal transformation of $A ,$ then $A ^ { * } A ^ { * } { } ^ { \prime } = A A ^ { \prime } .$ We shall see later
(Section 15) how this ambiguity can be solved.

Suppose that a random sample of $n > p$ observations $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ of $x$ is
obtained. The loglikelihood is

$$\Lambda _ { n } \left( \mu , A , \Phi \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n p \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log | \Omega | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { \prime } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) .$$
(3)

Maximizing $\Lambda$ with respect to $\mu$ yields $\widehat { \mu } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } .$ Substituting $\widehat { \mu }$ for
$\mu$ in (3) yields the so-called concentrated loglikelihood

$$\Lambda _ { n } ^ { c } \left( A , \Phi \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n p \log 2 \pi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( \log | \Omega | + \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } S \right)$$
(4)

with

$$S = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { \prime } .$$
(5)

Clearly, maximizing (4) is equivalent to minimizing $\log | \Omega | + \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } S$ with
respect to $A$ and $\Phi .$ The following theorem assumes $\Phi$ known, and thus min-
imizes with respect to $A$ only.


#### Theorem 10

Let $S$ and $\Phi$ be two given positive definite $p \times p$ matrices, $\Phi$ diagonal, and let
$1 \leq m \leq p .$ Let $\phi$ be a real-valued function defined by

$$\phi \left( A \right) = \log | A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi | + \mathrm { t r } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi \right) ^ { - 1 } S ,$$
(6)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="411" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 12 ] Factor analysis" -->

where $A \in \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { p \times m } .$ The minimum of $\phi$ is obtained when

$$A = \Phi ^ { 1 / 2 } T \left( \Lambda - I _ { m } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,$$
(7)

where $\Lambda$ is a diagonal $m \times m$ matrix containing the $m$ largest eigenvalues of
$\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and $T$ is a $p \times m$ matrix of corresponding orthonormal eigen-
vectors. The minimum value of $\phi$ is

$$p + \log | S | + \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - \log \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) ,$$
(8)

where $\lambda _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ denote the $p \quad - m$ smallest eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$

Proof. Define

$$\Omega = A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi , \quad C = \Omega ^ { - 1 } - \Omega ^ { - 1 } S \Omega ^ { - 1 } .$$
(9)

Then $\phi = \log | \Omega | + \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } S$ and hence

$$d \phi = \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } d \Omega - \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } S = \mathrm { t r } C \mathrm { d } \Omega$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } C \left( \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) A ^ { \prime } + A \left( \mathrm { d } A \right) ^ { \prime } \right) = 2 \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } C \mathrm { d } A .$$

(10)

The first-order condition is

$$C A = 0 ,$$
(11)

or, equivalently,

$$A = S \Omega ^ { - 1 } A .$$
(12)

From (12) we obtain

$$A A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A = S \Omega ^ { - 1 } A A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A$$
$$= S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \Omega - \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } A$$
$$= S \Phi ^ { - 1 } A - S \Omega ^ { - 1 } A = S \Phi ^ { - 1 } A - A .$$

(13)

Hence

$$S \Phi ^ { - 1 } A = A \left( I _ { m } + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \right) .$$
(14)

Assume that $r \left( A \right) = m ^ { \prime } \leq m$ and let $Q$ be a semi-orthogonal $m \times m ^ { \prime }$ matrix
$\left( Q ^ { \prime } Q = I _ { m ^ { \prime } } \right)$ such that

$$A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A Q = Q M ,$$
(15)

where $M$ is diagonal and contains the $m ^ { \prime }$ eigenvalues of $A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A .$
Then (14) can be written as

$$S \Phi ^ { - 1 } A Q = A Q \left( I + M \right)$$
(16)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="412" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

from which we obtain

$$\left( \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) \widetilde { T } = \widetilde { T } \left( I + M \right) ,$$
(17)

where $\widetilde { T } \equiv \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } A Q M ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ is a semi-orthogonal $p \times m ^ { \prime }$ matrix.

Our next step is to rewrite $\Omega = A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi$ as

$$\Omega = \Phi ^ { 1 / 2 } \left( I + \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } A A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) \Phi ^ { 1 / 2 } ,$$
(18)

so that the determinant and inverse of $\Omega$ can be expressed as

$$| \Omega | = | \Phi | | I + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A |$$
(19)

and

$$\Omega ^ { - 1 } = \Phi ^ { - 1 } - \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \left( I + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } .$$
(20)

Then, using (14),

$$\Omega ^ { - 1 } S = \Phi ^ { - 1 } S - \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \left( I + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \right) ^ { - 1 } \left( I + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \right) A ^ { \prime }$$
$$= \Phi ^ { - 1 } S - \Phi ^ { - 1 } A A ^ { \prime } .$$
(21)

Given the first-order condition, we thus have

$$\phi = \log | \Omega | + \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } S$$
$$= \log | \Phi | + \log | I + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A | + \mathrm { t r } \Phi ^ { - 1 } S - \mathrm { t r } A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A$$
$$= p + \log | S | + \left( \mathrm { t r } \left( \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) - \log | \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } | - p \right)$$
$$- \left( \mathrm { t r } \left( I _ { m } + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A \right) - \log | I _ { m } + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A | - m \right)$$
$$= p + \log | S | + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - \log \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \nu _ { j } - \log \nu _ { j } - 1 \right) ,$$

(22)

where $\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { p }$ are the eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and $\nu _ { 1 } \geq \nu _ { 2 } \geq$
$\cdots \geq \nu _ { m }$ are the eigenvalues of $I _ { m } + A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A .$ From (15) and (17) we see
that $\nu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \nu _ { m ^ { \prime } }$ are also eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and that the remaining
eigenvalues $\nu _ { m ^ { \prime } + 1 } , \ldots , \nu _ { m }$ are all one. Since we wish to minimize $\phi ,$ we make
$\nu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \nu _ { m ^ { \prime } }$ as large as possible, hence equal to the $m ^ { \prime }$ largest eigenvalues of
$\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$ Thus,

$$\nu _ { i } = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \lambda _ { i } & \left( i = 1 , \ldots , m ^ { \prime } \right) \\ 1 & \left( i = m ^ { \prime } + 1 , \ldots , m \right) . \end{array} \right.$$
(23)

Given (23), (22) reduces to

$$\phi = p + \log | S | + \sum _ { i = m ^ { \prime } + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - \log \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) ,$$
(24)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="413" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 13 ] A zigzag routine" -->

which, in turn, is minimized when $m ^ { \prime }$ is taken as large as possible; that is,
$m ^ { \prime } = m .$
Given $m ^ { \prime } = m ,$ $Q$ is orthogonal, $T = \widetilde { T } = \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } A Q M ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and $\Lambda = I + M .$
Hence

$$A A ^ { \prime } = \Phi ^ { 1 / 2 } T \left( \Lambda - I \right) T ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { 1 / 2 }$$
(25)

and $A$ can be chosen as $A = \Phi ^ { 1 / 2 } T \left( \Lambda - I \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$
☐
✷

Notice that the optimal choice for $A$ is such that $A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } A$ is a diagonal
matrix, even though this was not imposed.


### 13 A ZIGZAG ROUTINE

Theorem 10 provides the basis for (at least) two procedures by which $M L$
estimates of $A$ and $\Phi$ in the factor model can be found. The first procedure is
to minimize the concentrated function (12.8) with respect to the $p$ diagonal
elements of $\Phi .$ The second procedure is based on the first-order conditions
obtained from minimizing the function

$$\psi \left( A , \Phi \right) = \log | A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi | + \mathrm { t r } \left( A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi \right) ^ { - 1 } S .$$
(1)

The function $\psi$ is the same as the function $\phi$ defined in (12.6) except that $\phi$
is a function of $A$ given $\Phi ,$ while $\psi$ is a function of $A$ and $\Phi .$

In this section we investigate the second procedure. The first procedure is
discussed in Section 14.

From (12.12) we see that the first-order condition of $\psi$ with respect to $A$
is given by

$$A = S \Omega ^ { - 1 } A ,$$
(2)

where $\Omega = A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi .$ To obtain the first-order condition with respect to $\Phi ,$ we
differentiate $\psi$ holding $A$ constant. This yields

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Omega - \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Omega \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } S$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \Phi - \mathrm { t r } \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Omega ^ { - 1 } S$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } - \Omega ^ { - 1 } S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } \Phi .$$

(3)

Since $\Phi$ is diagonal, the first-order condition with respect to $\Phi$ is

$$\mathrm { d g } \left( \Omega ^ { - 1 } - \Omega ^ { - 1 } S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \right) = 0 .$$
(4)

Pre- and post-multiplying (4) by $\Phi$ we obtain the equivalent condition

$$\mathrm { d g } \left( \Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi \right) = \mathrm { d g } \left( \Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi \right) .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="414" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

(The equivalence follows from the fact that $\Phi$ is diagonal and non-singular.)
Now, given the first-order condition for $A$ in (2), and writing $\Omega - A A ^ { \prime }$ for $\Phi ,$
we have

$$S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi = S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( \Omega - A A ^ { \prime } \right) = S - S \Omega ^ { - 1 } A A ^ { \prime }$$
$$= S - A A ^ { \prime } = S + \Phi - \Omega ,$$

(6)

so that

$$\Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi = \Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } \left( S + \Phi - \Omega \right)$$
$$= \Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } S + \Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi - \Phi$$
$$= \Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi + S - \Omega ,$$

(7)

using the fact that $\Phi \Omega ^ { - 1 } S = S \Omega ^ { - 1 } \Phi .$ Hence, given (2), (5) is equivalent to

$$\deg \Omega = \deg S ,$$
(8)

that is,

$$\Phi = \mathrm { d g } \left( S - A A ^ { \prime } \right) .$$
(9)

Thus, Theorem 10 provides an explicit solution for $A$ as a function of $\Phi ,$ and
(9) gives $\Phi$ as an explicit function of $A .$ A zigzag routine suggests itself: choose
an appropriate starting value for $\Phi ,$ then calculate $A A ^ { \prime }$ from (12.25), then $\Phi$
from (9), etcetera. If convergence occurs (which is not guaranteed), then the
resulting values for $\Phi$ and $A A ^ { \prime }$ correspond to a (local) minimum of $\psi .$

From (12.25) and $\left( 9 \right)$ we summarize this iterative procedure as

$$\phi _ { i } ^ { \left( k + 1 \right) } = s _ { i i } - \phi _ { i } ^ { \left( k \right) } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \lambda _ { j } ^ { \left( k \right) } - 1 \right) \left( t _ { i j } ^ { \left( k \right) } \right) ^ { 2 } \quad \left( i = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$$
(10)

for $k = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots .$ Here $s _ { i i }$ denotes the i-th diagonal element of S, $\lambda _ { j } ^ { \left( k \right) }$ the
j-th largest eigenvalue of $\left( \Phi ^ { \left( k \right) } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \left( \Phi ^ { \left( k \right) } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$ and $\left( t _ { 1 j } ^ { \left( k \right) } , \ldots , t _ { p j } ^ { \left( k \right) } \right) ^ { \prime }$ the cor-
responding eigenvector.

What is an appropriate starting value for $\Phi$ From (9) we see that $0 <$
$\phi _ { i } < s _ { i i } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , p \right) .$ This suggests that we choose our starting value as

$$\Phi ^ { \left( 0 \right) } = \alpha \deg S$$
(11)

for some $\alpha$ satisfying $0 < \alpha < 1 .$ Calculating $A$ from (12.7) given $\Phi = \Phi ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$
leads to

$$A ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \left( \deg S \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } T \left( \Lambda - \alpha I _ { m } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,$$
(12)

where $\Lambda$ is a diagonal $m \times m$ matrix containing the $m$ largest eigenvalues
of $S ^ { * } \equiv \left( \mathrm { d g } S \right) ^ { - \widetilde { 1 / 2 } } S \left( \mathrm { d g } S \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and $T$ is a $p \times m$ matrix of corresponding
orthonormal eigenvectors. This shows that $\alpha$ must be chosen smaller than
each of the $m$ largest eigenvalues of $S ^ { * } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="415" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] A Newton-Raphson routine" -->


### 14 A NEWTON-RAPHSON ROUTINE

Instead of using the first-order conditions to set up a zigzag procedure, we can
also use the Newton-Raphson method in order $t o$ find the values of $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p }$
that minimize the concentrated function (12.8). The Newton-Raphson method
requires knowledge of the first- and second-order derivatives of this function,
and these are provided by the following theorem.


#### Theorem 11

Let $S$ be a given positive definite $p \times p$ matrix and let $1 \leq m \leq p - 1 .$ Let $\gamma$
be a real-valued function defined by

$$\gamma \left( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p } \right) = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - \log \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) ,$$
(1)

where $\lambda _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ denote the $p \quad - \quad m$ smallest eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$
and $\Phi = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p } \right)$ is diagonal positive definite of order $p \times p .$ At points
$\left( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p } \right)$ where $\lambda _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ are all distinct eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$
,
the gradient of $\gamma$ is the $p \times 1$ vector

$$g \left( \phi \right) = - \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) u _ { i } \odot u _ { i } \right)$$
(2)

and the Hessian is the $p \times p$ matrix

$$G \left( \phi \right) = \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } u _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \odot B ^ { i } \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } ,$$
(3)

where

$$B ^ { i } = \left( 2 \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) u _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } + 2 \lambda _ { i } \left( \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) \left( \lambda _ { i } I - \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) ^ { + } ,$$
(4)

and $u _ { i } \left( i = m + 1 , \ldots , p \right)$ denotes the orthonormal eigenvector of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$
associated with $\lambda _ { i } .$

Remark. The symbol O denotes the Hadamard product: $A \odot B = \left( a _ { i j } b _ { i j } \right) ,$ see
Section 3.6.

Proof. Let $\phi = \left( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p } \right)$ and $S ^ { * } \left( \phi \right) = \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } .$ Let $\phi _ { 0 }$ be a given
point in $\mathrm { R } _ { + } ^ { p }$ (the positive orthant of $\mathrm { R } ^ { p }$ and $S _ { 0 } ^ { * } = S ^ { * } \left( \phi _ { 0 } \right) .$ Let

$$\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { m } > \lambda _ { m + 1 } > \cdots > \lambda _ { p }$$
(5)

denote the eigenvalues of $S _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ and let $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { p }$ be corresponding eigenvec-
tors. (Notice that the $p \quad - \quad m$ smallest eigenvalues of $S _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ are assumed distinct.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="416" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

Then, according to Theorem 8.7, there is a neighbourhood, say $N \left( \phi _ { 0 } \right) ,$ where
differentiable eigenvalue functions $\lambda ^ { \left( i \right) }$ and eigenvector functions $u ^ { \left( i \right) }$ $\left( i = \right.$
$\left. m + 1 , \ldots , p \right)$ exist satisfying

$$S ^ { * } u ^ { \left( i \right) } = \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } u ^ { \left( i \right) } , \quad u ^ { \left( i \right) ^ { \prime } } u ^ { \left( i \right) } = 1 ,$$
(6)

and

$$u ^ { \left( i \right) } \left( \phi _ { 0 } \right) = u _ { i } , \quad \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } \left( \phi _ { 0 } \right) = \lambda _ { i } .$$
(7)

Furthermore, at $\phi = \phi _ { 0 } ,$

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } = u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } S ^ { * } \right) u _ { i }$$
(8)

and

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } = 2 u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } S ^ { * } \right) T _ { i } ^ { + } \left( \mathrm { d } S ^ { * } \right) u _ { i } + u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } S ^ { * } \right) u _ { i }$$
(9)

where $T _ { i } = \lambda _ { i } I - S _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ see also Theorem 8.10.

In the present case, $S ^ { * } = \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ and hence

$$\mathrm { d } S ^ { * } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) S ^ { * } + S ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \right)$$
(10)

and

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } S ^ { * } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) S ^ { * } + S ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \right)$$

$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) S ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } .$$
(11)

Inserting (10) into (8) yields

$$\mathrm { d } \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } = - \lambda _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i } .$$
(12)

Similarly, inserting (10) and (11) into (9) yields

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \lambda _ { i } ^ { 2 } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } T _ { i } ^ { + } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i }$$
$$+ \lambda _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) S _ { 0 } ^ { * } T _ { i } ^ { + } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) S _ { 0 } ^ { * } T _ { i } ^ { + } S _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } u _ { i }$$
$$+ \frac { 3 } { 2 } \lambda _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i }$$
$$+ \frac { 1 } { 2 } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( d \Phi \right) S _ { 0 } ^ { * } \left( d \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } u _ { i }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } C _ { i } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i } ,$$

(13)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="417" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 14 ] A Newton-Raphson routine" -->

where

$$C _ { i } = \lambda _ { i } ^ { 2 } T _ { i } ^ { + } + 2 \lambda _ { i } S _ { 0 } ^ { * } T _ { i } ^ { + } + S _ { 0 } ^ { * } T _ { i } ^ { + } S _ { 0 } ^ { * } + 3 \lambda _ { i } I + S _ { 0 } ^ { * } .$$
(14)

Now, since

$$T _ { i } ^ { + } = \sum _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { i } - \lambda _ { j } \right) ^ { - 1 } u _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad S _ { 0 } ^ { * } = \sum _ { j } \lambda _ { j } u _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } ,$$
(15)

we have

$$S _ { 0 } ^ { * } T _ { i } ^ { + } = \sum _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } / \left( \lambda _ { i } - \lambda _ { j } \right) \right) u _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } , \quad S _ { 0 } ^ { * } T _ { i } ^ { + } S _ { 0 } ^ { * } = \sum _ { j \neq i } \left( \lambda _ { j } ^ { 2 } / \left( \lambda _ { i } - \lambda _ { j } \right) \right) u _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } .$$
(16)

Hence we obtain

$$C _ { i } = 4 \lambda _ { i } \left( u _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } + \lambda _ { i } T _ { i } ^ { + } \right) .$$
(17)

We can now take the differentials of

$$\gamma = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - \log \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) .$$
(18)

We have

$$d \gamma = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( 1 - \lambda _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \right) d \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) }$$
(19)

and

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \gamma = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \left( \lambda _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { d } \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( 1 - \lambda _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \right) \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \lambda ^ { \left( i \right) } \right) .$$
(20)

Inserting (12) in (19) gives

$$\mathrm { d } \gamma = - \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i } .$$
(21)

Inserting (12) and (13) in (20) gives

$$\mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \gamma = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( u _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 1 - \lambda _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \right) C _ { i } \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i }$$
$$= \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( 2 \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) u _ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } + 2 \lambda _ { i } \left( \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) T _ { i } ^ { + } \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i }$$

$$= \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) \Phi ^ { - 1 } B ^ { i } \Phi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { d } \Phi \right) u _ { i } ,$$
(22)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="418" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

in view of (17). The first-order partial derivatives are thus

$$\frac { \partial \gamma } { \partial \phi _ { h } } = - \phi _ { h } ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } \left( \lambda _ { i } - 1 \right) u _ { i h } ^ { 2 } \quad \left( h = 1 , \ldots , p \right) ,$$
(23)

where $u _ { i h }$ denotes the h-th component of $u _ { i } .$ The second-order partial deriva-
tives are

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \gamma } { \partial \phi _ { h } \partial \phi _ { k } } = \left( \phi _ { h } \phi _ { k } \right) ^ { - 1 } \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { p } u _ { i h } u _ { i k } B _ { h k } ^ { i } \quad \left( h , k = 1 , \ldots , p \right)$$
(24)

and the result follows.

✷
☐

Given knowledge of the gradient $g \left( \phi \right)$ and the Hessian $G \left( \phi \right)$ from (2) and
(3), the Newton-Raphson method proceeds as follows. First choose a starting
value $\phi ^ { \left( 0 \right) } .$ Then, for $k = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots .$ , compute

$$\phi ^ { \left( k + 1 \right) } = \phi ^ { \left( k \right) } - \left( G \left( \phi ^ { \left( k \right) } \right) \right) ^ { - 1 } g \left( \phi ^ { \left( k \right) } \right) .$$
(25)

This method appears to work well in practice and yields the values $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p }$
which minimize (1). Given these values we can compute $A$ from (12.7), thus
completing the solution.

There is, however, one proviso. In Theorem 11 we require that the $p \quad - \quad m$
smallest eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ are all distinct. But, by rewriting (12.2)
as

$$\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } \Omega \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } = I + \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } A A ^ { \prime } \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$$
(26)

we see that the $p \quad - \quad m$ smallest eigenvalues of $\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } \Omega \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ are all one.
Therefore, if the sample size increases, the $p \quad - \quad m$ smallest eigenvalues of
$\Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 } S \Phi ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ will all converge to one. For large samples an optimization
method based on Theorem 11 may therefore not give reliable results.


### 15 KAISER'S VARIMAX METHOD

The factorization $\Omega = A A ^ { \prime } + \Phi$ of the variance matrix is not unique. If we
transform the 'loading' matrix $A$ by an orthogonal matrix $T ,$ then $\left( A T \right) \left( A T \right) ^ { \prime } =$
$A A ^ { \prime } .$ In this way, we can always rotate $A$ by an orthogonal matrix $T ,$ so that
$A ^ { * } = A T$ yields the same $\Omega .$ Several approaches have been suggested to use
this ambiguity in a factor analysis solution in order to create maximum con-
trast between the columns of $A .$ A well-known method, due to Kaiser, is to
maximize the raw varimax criterion.

Kaiser defined the simplicity of the k-th factor, denoted $s _ { k } ,$ as the sample
variance of its squared factor loadings. Thus

$$s _ { k } = \frac { 1 } { p } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \left( a _ { i k } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { p } \sum _ { h = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { h k } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \quad \left( k = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$$
(1)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="419" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 15 ] Kaiser's varimax method" -->

The total simplicity is $s = s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } + \cdots + s _ { m }$ and the raw varimax method
selects an orthogonal matrix $T$ such that $s$ is maximized.


#### Theorem 12

Let $A$ be a given $p \times m$ matrix of rank $m .$ Let $\phi$ be a real-valued function
defined by

$$\phi \left( T \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \left[ \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } b _ { i j } ^ { 4 } \right) - \frac { 1 } { p } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } b _ { i j } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] ,$$
(2)

where $B = \left( b _ { i j } \right)$ satisfies $B = A T$ and $T \in \mathcal{O} _ { m \times m } .$ The function $\phi$ reaches a
maximum when $B$ satisfies

$$B = A A ^ { \prime } Q \left( Q ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } Q \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$$
(3)

where $Q = \left( q _ { i j } \right)$ is the $p \times m$ matrix with typical element

$$q _ { i j } = b _ { i j } \left( b _ { i j } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { p } \sum _ { h = 1 } ^ { p } b _ { h j } ^ { 2 } \right) .$$
(4)

Proof. Let $C = B \odot B ,$ so that $c _ { i j } = b _ { i j } ^ { 2 }$ . Let $\imath = \left( 1 , 1 , \ldots , 1 \right) ^ { \prime }$ be of order $p \times 1$
and $M = I _ { p } - \left( I / p \right) u ^ { \prime } .$ Let $e _ { i }$ denote the i-th column of $I _ { p }$ and $u _ { j }$ the j-th
column of $I _ { m } .$ Then we can rewrite $\phi$ as

$$\phi \left( T \right) = \sum _ { j } \sum _ { i } c _ { i j } ^ { 2 } - \left( 1 / p \right) \sum _ { j } \left( \sum _ { i } c _ { i j } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C - \left( 1 / p \right) \sum _ { j } \left( \sum _ { i } e _ { i } ^ { \prime } C u _ { j } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C - \left( 1 / p \right) \sum _ { j } \left( \imath ^ { \prime } C u _ { j } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C - \left( 1 / p \right) \sum _ { j } \imath ^ { \prime } C u _ { j } u _ { j } ^ { \prime } C ^ { \prime } \imath$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } C - \left( 1 / p \right) \imath ^ { \prime } C C ^ { \prime } \imath = \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } M C .$$

We wish to maximize $\phi$ with respect to $T$ subject to the orthogonality con-
straint $T ^ { \prime } T = I _ { m } .$ Let $\psi$ be the appropriate Lagrangian function

$$\psi \left( T \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } M C - \mathrm { t r } L \left( T ^ { \prime } T - I \right) ,$$
(6)

(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="420" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

where L is a symmetric $m \times m$ matrix of Lagrange multipliers. Then the
differential of $\psi$ is

$$\mathrm { d } \psi = \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } M \mathrm { d } C - 2 \mathrm { t r } L T ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } T$$
$$= 2 \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } M \left( B \odot \mathrm { d } B \right) - 2 \mathrm { t r } L T ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } T$$
$$= 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } M \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) \mathrm { d } B - 2 \mathrm { t r } L T ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } T$$
$$= 2 \mathrm { t r } \left( C ^ { \prime } M \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) A \mathrm { d } T - 2 \mathrm { t r } L T ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d } T ,$$

(7)

where the third equality follows from Theorem 3.7(a). Hence, the first-order
conditions are

$$\left( C ^ { \prime } M \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) A = L T ^ { \prime }$$
(8)

and

$$T ^ { \prime } T = I .$$
(9)

It is easy to verify that the $p \times m$ matrix $Q$ given in (4) satisfies

$$Q = B \odot M C ,$$
(10)

so that (8) becomes

$$Q ^ { \prime } A = L T ^ { \prime } .$$
(11)
.

Post-multiplying with $T$ and using the symmetry of $L$ we obtain the condition

$$Q ^ { \prime } B = B ^ { \prime } Q .$$
(12)

We see from (11) that $L = Q ^ { \prime } B .$ This is a symmetric matrix and

$$\mathrm { t r } L = \mathrm { t r } B ^ { \prime } Q = \mathrm { t r } B ^ { \prime } \left( B \odot M C \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { t r } \left( B ^ { \prime } \odot B ^ { \prime } \right) M C = \mathrm { t r } C ^ { \prime } M C ,$$

(13)

using Theorem 3.7(a). From (11) follows

$$L ^ { 2 } = Q ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } Q$$
(14)

so that

$$L = \left( Q ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } Q \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$
(15)

It is clear that L must be positive semidefinite. Assuming that $L$ is, in fact,
non-singular, we may write

$$L ^ { - 1 } = \left( Q ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } Q \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 }$$
(16)

and we obtain from (11)

$$T ^ { \prime } = L ^ { - 1 } Q ^ { \prime } A = \left( Q ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } Q \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } Q ^ { \prime } A .$$
(17)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="421" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Sec. 16 ] Canonical correlations and variates in the population" -->

The solution for $B$ is then

$$B = A T = A A ^ { \prime } Q \left( Q ^ { \prime } A A ^ { \prime } Q \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,$$
(18)

which completes the proof.

✷
☐

An iterative zigzag procedure can be based on (3) and (4). In (3) we have
$B = B \left( Q \right)$ and in (4) we have $Q = Q \left( B \right) .$ An obvious starting value for $B$ is
$B ^ { \left( 0 \right) } = A .$ Then calculate $Q ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = Q \left( B ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \right) ,$ $B ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = B \left( Q ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) ,$ $Q ^ { \left( 2 \right) } = Q \left( B ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) ,$
etcetera. If the procedure converges, which is not guaranteed, then a (local)
maximum of (2) has been found.


### 16 CANONICAL CORRELATIONS AND VARIATES IN THE POPULATION

$\mathrm { L e t } z$ be a random vector with zero expectations and positive definite variance
matrix $\Sigma .$ Let $z$ and $\Sigma$ be partitioned as

$$z = \left( \begin{array}{} z ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \\ z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } \end{array} \right) , \quad \Sigma = \left( \begin{array}{} \Sigma _ { 1 1 } & \Sigma _ { 1 2 } \\ \Sigma _ { 2 1 } & \Sigma _ { 2 2 } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(1)

so that $\Sigma _ { 1 1 }$ is the variance matrix of $z ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ $\Sigma _ { 2 2 }$ the variance matrix of $z ^ { \left( 2 \right) }$ and
$\Sigma _ { 1 2 } = \Sigma _ { 2 1 } ^ { \prime }$ the covariance matrix between $z ^ { \bar { \left( 1 \right) } }$ and $z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } .$

The pair of linear combinations $u ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $v ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$ each of unit variance,
with maximum correlation (in absolute value) is called the first pair of canoni-
cal variates and its correlation is called the $f i r s t$ canonical correlation between
$z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } .$

The k-th pair of canonical variates is the pair $u ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $v ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$ each of
unit variance and uncorrelated with the first $k \quad - \quad 1$ pairs of canonical variates,
with maximum correlation (in absolute value). This correlation is the k-th
canonical correlation.


#### Theorem 13

Let $z$ be a random vector with zero expectation and positive definite variance
matrix $\sum .$ Let $z$ and $\sum$ be partitioned as in $\left( 1 \right) ,$ and define

$$B = \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Sigma _ { 1 2 } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \Sigma _ { 2 1 } , \quad C = \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 } \Sigma _ { 2 1 } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 } \Sigma _ { 1 2 } . ,$$
(2)

(a) There are $r$ non-zero canonical correlations between $z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$ where
$r$ is the rank of $\Sigma _ { 1 2 } .$

(b) Let $\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { r } > 0$ denote the non-zero eigenvalues of $B$ (and
of $\left. C \right) .$ Then the k-th canonical correlation between $z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $z ^ { \left( 2 \right) }$ is $\lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } .$

(c) The k-th pair of canonical variates is given by $u ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $v ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$ where
$u$ and $v$ are normalized eigenvectors of $B$ and $C ,$ respectively, associ-
ated with the eigenvalue $\lambda _ { k } .$ Moreover, if $\lambda _ { k }$ is a simple (non-repeated)
eigenvalue of $B$ (and $\left. C \right) ,$ then $u$ and $v$ are unique (apart from sign).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="422" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

(d) If the pair $u ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $v ^ { \prime } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) }$ is the k-th pair of canonical variates, then

$$\Sigma _ { 1 2 } v = \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } u , \quad \Sigma _ { 2 1 } u = \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } v . ,$$
(3)

$P r o o f .$ Let $A = \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 1 2 } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ with rank $r \left( A \right) = r \left( \Sigma _ { 1 2 } \right) = r ,$ and notice that
the $r$ non-zero eigenvalues of $A A ^ { \prime } ,$ $A ^ { \prime } A ,$ $B$ and $C$ are all the same, namely
$\lambda _ { 1 } \geq \lambda _ { 2 } \geq \cdots \geq \lambda _ { r } > 0 .$ Let $S = \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { r } \right)$ and $T = \left( t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } , \ldots , t _ { r } \right)$ be
semi-orthogonal matrices such that

$$A A ^ { \prime } S = S \Lambda , \quad A ^ { \prime } A T = T \Lambda ,$$
$$S ^ { \prime } S = I _ { r } , \quad T ^ { \prime } T = I _ { r } , \quad \Lambda = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { r } \right) .$$
(4)
(5)

We assume first that all $\lambda _ { i } \left( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , r \right)$ are $d i s t i n c t .$

The first pair of canonical variates is obtained from the maximization
problem

$$\mathrm { m a x i m i z e } _ { u , v } \quad \left( u ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 2 } v \right) ^ { 2 }$$

subject to

$$u ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } u = v ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } v = 1 .$$
(6)

Let $x = \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } u ,$ $y = \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } v .$ Then (6) can be equivalently stated as

$$\mathrm { m a x i m i z e } _ { x , y } \quad \left( x ^ { \prime } A y \right) ^ { 2 }$$

subject to

$$x ^ { \prime } x = y ^ { \prime } y = 1 .$$
(7)

According to Theorem 11.17, the maximum $\lambda _ { 1 }$ is obtained for $x = s _ { 1 }$ $1, $y = t _ { 1 }$
(apart from the sign, which is irrelevant). Hence $\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is the first canonical
correlation, and the first pair of canonical variates is $u ^ { \left( 1 \right) ^ { \prime } } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $v ^ { \left( 1 \right) ^ { \prime } } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) }$
with $u ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } s _ { 1 } ,$ $v ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } t _ { 1 } .$ It follows that $B u ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \lambda _ { 1 } u ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ (because
$\left. A A ^ { \prime } s _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } s _ { 1 } \right)$ and $C v ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \lambda _ { 1 } v ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ (because $\left. A ^ { \prime } A t _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } t _ { 1 } \right) .$ Theorem 11.17
also gives $s _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } A t _ { 1 } ,$ $t _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } A ^ { \prime } s _ { 1 }$ from which we obtain $\Sigma _ { 1 2 } v ^ { \left( 1 \right) } =$
$\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } u ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ $\Sigma _ { 2 1 } u ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } v ^ { \left( 1 \right) } .$

Now assume that $\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } , \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { k - 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ are the first $k \quad - \quad 1$ canonical cor-
relations, and that $s _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $t _ { i } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$ $i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k - 1 ,$ are the
corresponding pairs of canonical variates. In order to obtain the k-th pair of
canonical variates we let $S _ { 1 } = \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { k - 1 } \right)$ and $T _ { 1 } = \left( t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } , \ldots , t _ { k - 1 } \right) ,$
and consider the constrained maximization problem

$$\mathrm { m a x i m i z e } _ { u , v } \quad \left( u ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 2 } v \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$u ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } u = v ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } v = 1 ,$$
$$S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } u = 0 , \quad S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 1 2 } v = 0 ,$$
subject to

$$T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } v = 0 , \quad T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 2 1 } u = 0 .$$
(8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="423" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Bibliographical notes" -->

Again, letting $x = \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } u ,$ $y = \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } v ,$ we can rephrase (8) as

maximize
x, y
subject to

$$\left( x ^ { \prime } A y \right) ^ { 2 }$$

$$x ^ { \prime } x = y ^ { \prime } y = 1 ,$$
$$S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } x = S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A y = 0 ,$$

$$T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } y = T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } x = 0 .$$
(9)

It turns out, as we shall see shortly, that we can take any one of the four con-
straints $S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } x = 0 ,$ $S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A y = 0 ,$ $T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } y = 0 ,$ $T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } x = 0 ,$ because the solution will
automatically satisfy the remaining three conditions. The reduced problem is

$$\mathrm { m a x i m i z e } _ { x , y } \quad \left( x ^ { \prime } A y \right) ^ { 2 }$$

subject to

$$x ^ { \prime } x = y ^ { \prime } y = 1 , \quad S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } x = 0 ,$$
(10)

and its solution follows from Theorem 11.17. The constrained maximum is $\lambda _ { k }$
and is achieved by $x _ { * } = s _ { k }$ and $y _ { * } = t _ { k } .$

We see that the three constraints that were dropped in the passage from
(9) to (10) are indeed satisfied: $S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A y _ { * } = 0 ,$ because $A y _ { * } = \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { * }$ $T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { * } = 0$
and $T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime } x _ { * } = 0 ,$ because $A ^ { \prime } x _ { * } = \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } y _ { * } .$ Hence we may conclude that $\lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 }$
is the k-th canonical correlation; that $u ^ { \left( k \right) ^ { \prime } } z ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ $v ^ { \left( k \right) ^ { \prime } } z ^ { \left( 2 \right) }$ with $u ^ { \left( k \right) } = \Sigma _ { 1 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } s _ { k }$
and $v ^ { \left( k \right) } = \Sigma _ { 2 2 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } t _ { k }$ is the k-th pair of canonical variates; that $u ^ { \left( k \right) }$ and $v ^ { \left( k \right) }$ are
the (unique) normalized eigenvectors of $B$ and $C ,$ respectively, associated with
the eigenvalue $\lambda _ { k }$ and that $\Sigma _ { 1 2 } v ^ { \left( k \right) } = \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 1 1 } u ^ { \left( k \right) }$ and $\Sigma _ { 2 1 } u ^ { \left( k \right) } = \lambda _ { k } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Sigma _ { 2 2 } v ^ { \left( k \right) } .$

The theorem (still assuming distinct eigenvalues) now follows by simple
mathematical induction. It is clear that only $r$ pairs of canonical variates
can be found yielding non-zero canonical correlations. (The $\left( r + \right.$ 1)-st pair
would yield zero canonical correlations, since $A A ^ { \prime }$ possesses only $r$ positive
eigenvalues.)

In the case of multiple eigenvalues, the proof remains unchanged, except
that the eigenvectors associated with multiple eigenvalues are not unique, and
therefore the pairs of canonical variates corresponding to these eigenvectors
are not unique either.
✷
☐


### BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTES

§1. There are some excellent texts on multivariate statistics and psychomet-
rics, of which we mention in particular Morrison (1976) and Anderson (1984).
§2-§3. See also Lawley and Maxwell (1971), Muirhead (1982) and Anderson
(1984).

§5-§6. See Morrison (1976) and Muirhead (1982). Theorem 4 is proved in An-
derson (1984). For asymptotic distributional results concerning $l _ { i }$ and $q _ { i } ,$ see
Kollo and Neudecker (1993). For asymptotic distributional results concern-
ing $q _ { i }$ in Hotelling's (1933) model where $t _ { i } ^ { \prime } t _ { i } = \lambda _ { i } ,$ see Kollo and Neudecker

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="424" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Topics in psychometrics [Ch. 17" -->

(1997).

§8-§10. See Eckart and Young (1936), Theil (1971), Ten Berge (1993), Greene
(1993) and Chipman (1996). We are grateful to Jos Ten Berge for pointing
out a redundancy in Theorem 8.

§11. For three-mode component analysis see Tucker (1966). An extension to
four models is given in Lastovička (1981), and to an arbitrary number of
modes in Kapteyn, Neudecker and Wansbeek (1986).

§12-§13. See Rao (1955), Morrison (1976), and Mardia, Kent and Bibby
(1992).

§14. See Clarke (1970), Lawley and Maxwell (1971) and Neudecker (1975).
§15. See Kaiser (1958, 1959), Sherin (1966), Lawley and Maxwell (1971) and
Neudecker (1981).

§16. See Muirhead (1982) and Anderson (1984).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->
